Hide Caption 22 of 22 It was an uplifting and unifying message that many Americans have rarely heard from Trump, who argued "the time for trivial fights is behind us." While Trump is not solely responsible for the coarsening of political life, his brash, Twitter-fueled approach has rocked the nation's politics. The question now is whether the President was previewing a new, more sober political persona or whether he will return to his old habits. The change in his tone was evident from the first moments of his speech when he condemned the recent spate of threats against Jewish community centers, vandalism at Jewish cemeteries and the shooting of two Indian men in Kansas. He said the violence was a reminder that "while we may be a nation divided on policies, we are a country that stands united in condemning hate and evil in all its very ugly forms." The comment followed heavy criticism of Trump for not addressing such violence. The new President entered the House chamber to thunderous applause and spoke of the "renewal of the American spirit." A senior White House official said Trump wrote the speech himself with input from almost every member of his presidential team of advisers. In the emotional high point of the speech, Trump turned to the first lady's box and acknowledged Carryn Owens, the widow of a US Navy Special operator, William "Ryan" Owens, who was killed in an anti-terror raid in Yemen in the first major military engagement of the new administration. "Ryan died as he lived: a warrior, and a hero -- battling against terrorism and securing our nation," Trump said as the House floor erupted in a prolonged standing ovation. Owens, with tears streaming down her face, looked to the Heavens and joined in the applause. Less explosive style welcomed His less explosive presentation style was welcomed by many lawmakers. JUST WATCHED McConnell: American's expect Obamacare repeal Replay More Videos ... MUST WATCH McConnell: American's expect Obamacare repeal 01:10 "Donald Trump did indeed become presidential tonight, and I think we'll see that reflected in a higher approval rating," Senate Majority Leader Mitch McConnell, R-Kentucky, said on CNN after the speech. "The Donald Trump I heard tonight was a lot more focused and disciplined and subdued, and it was a lot more uneventful in a good way," moderate House Republican Charlie Dent told CNN's Tom LoBianco. "There were not a lot of distractions tonight, this speech was much better than the inaugural speech." North Dakota Democratic Sen. Heidi Heitkamp, who is up for re-election next year in a state that Trump carried, said the President delivered a "very good speech." "It was delivered with a sense of 'this is who I am, this is what I want to accomplish' and I think the goals are great," she said. "How we get there is the $10,000 question." Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer said on CNN that Trump's "speeches and the realities are very, very far apart." "Until his reality catches up with his speeches, he's got big trouble," Schumer said. Though his rhetoric was soaring, Trump still struck hardline notes. He overruled national security adviser H.R. McMaster, according to a senior administration official, to warn of "radical Islamic terrorism." Hitting themes familiar from his campaign, Trump vowed to restore "integrity and the rule of law to our borders." "We will soon begin the construction of a great, great wall along our southern border," Trump said, drawing Republican cheers even as he didn't mention his earlier promise that Mexico would pay for construction. "As we speak tonight, we are removing gang members, drug dealers, and criminals that threaten our communities and prey on our very innocent citizens. Bad ones are going out as I speak, and as I promised throughout the campaign" Sending shockwaves through Washington While such language could please conservatives, Trump sent shockwaves through Washington earlier Tuesday by telling reporters he wants to pass an immigration reform bill that could grant legal status to millions of undocumented immigrants living in the US. "The time is right for an immigration bill as long as there is compromise on both sides," Trump said at the White House. But he did not provide further clarity on that position during his address. So far there is little sign that the new President's legislative agenda, which includes repealing and replacing Obamacare, a big tax overhaul, and a $1 trillion infrastructure program , is anywhere near coming to fruition. That explains why he devoted a considerable portion of the address to touting his achievements so far. He argued that his election alone had convinced big firms like Ford, Sprint, SoftBank and Intel to invest billions of job-creating dollars in the US. He noted that stocks have put on $3 trillion in value since his election and claimed to have saved hundreds of millions of taxpayer dollars, including on the new F-35 jet fighter. Trump said he also kept his word by cutting government regulations, clearing the way for the Keystone and Dakota Access pipelines and pulling out of the Trans Pacific Partnership trade deal. The President also laid down clear principles for the repeal and replacement of Obamacare, a key policy goal that is threatening to become overwhelmed by the complications of writing health policy. A new system, he said, must retain coverage for Americans with pre-existing conditions, should offer plans backed by tax credits and expanded health savings accounts and should preserve Medicaid expansion in the states. Trump also vowed to bring down the high price of drugs "immediately." 'Obamacare is collapsing' "Obamacare is collapsing -- and we must act decisively to protect all Americans," he said, "Action is not a choice -- it is a necessity." House Democratic Leader Nancy Pelosi, who was instrumental in passing the law, shook her head as Trump condemned it. The President also signaled action on another key piece of his agenda -- tax reform, promising "massive" relief for the middle classes and cuts in corporate tax. Yet Trump also pushed for his $1 trillion infrastructure plan and spoke of his effort to boost military spending. Given that he has also said he will protect entitlements, questions will be asked about how the administration can finance its ambitious plans. Trump's speech was closely watched around the world, given that his pronouncements on foreign policy have caused alarm and confusion. There were, however, few details on national security policy to clear up misconceptions. Trump recommitted himself to a strong "America First" foreign policy, but also backed NATO -- as long as its members pay their dues -- while bemoaning trillions of dollars the US has spent in foreign wars abroad. "My job is not to represent the world. My job is to represent the United States of America," said Trump. "But we know that America is better off when there is less conflict, not more. We must learn from the mistakes of the past. We have seen the war and the destruction that have ravaged and raged throughout the world." Despite some positive reviews for Trump in the room on Tuesday, the official Democratic response by former Kentucky Gov. Steven Beshear accused the President of deserting the working people who voted for him by picking a cabinet of millionaires and billionaires. "That's not being our champion. That's being Wall Street's champion," Beshear said. "Real leaders don't spread derision and division. Real leaders strengthen, they unify, they partner, and they offer real solutions instead of ultimatums and blame," said Beshear, accusing Trump of waging war on refugees and immigrants and endangering US security by reaching out to Russia.

Investors are keenly awaiting President Donald Trump's address to Congress, viewing it as a crucial moment for U.S. financial markets. After more than a year of campaign promises touting a pro-growth agenda that has propelled U.S. stocks to record highs, strengthened the dollar, and increased bond yields, this prime-time speech is highly anticipated.

"We need to see some details within all the policy talk," stated Sean Simko, who manages $8 billion in fixed-income assets at SEI Investments Co. in Oaks, Pennsylvania. "More specifics in terms of numbers or even a more defined timeline. If there aren’t specifics there, the risk trade might be ending."

While President Trump's promise of a "phenomenal" tax plan earlier this month injected new life into some faltering Trump reflation trades, investors emphasize the need for more concrete information. This is particularly true as the administration has prioritized repealing and replacing Obamacare before tackling tax reform. Although not officially a State of the Union address, this initial speech to Congress holds significant weight for presidents in the modern era. For instance, Barack Obama addressed both legislative bodies in February 2009, focusing on the financial crisis.

According to administration officials, Trump plans to propose a $54 billion increase in defense spending in his first budget, offset by an equal reduction in other discretionary government spending. During a speech to governors on Monday, Trump described his plan as a "public safety budget" and pledged a significant investment in infrastructure, though without providing specific details.

Since Trump's election, the stock market has shown remarkable resilience. The S&P 500 has climbed 10 percent, achieving 17 record closes and adding $2.8 trillion in value to the U.S. equity market. Underlying these gains are fundamental economic factors, including an accelerating economy and projected corporate earnings growth of 12 percent, a notable turnaround from declines in 2015 and 2016.

"It’s possible that if the market hadn’t been rising so dramatically, we could wait," commented Quincy Krosby, a market strategist at Prudential Financial Inc., which manages approximately $1.3 trillion. "But this is a market that’s pretty impatient and wants results."

Adding to market uncertainty are differing perspectives on tax reform. House Republicans are exploring a border-adjustment tax, which would shift the tax burden from exporters to importers, arguing it would benefit American manufacturing and generate revenue to offset corporate tax rate reductions. President Trump, however, has deemed this plan "too complicated."

As this debate unfolds, traders have scaled back bullish positions on the dollar. The greenback has declined 3.3 percent since January, following a 6.5 percent surge after the November 8 presidential election, according to the Bloomberg Dollar Spot Index. Hedge funds and other large speculators have reduced their net bullish dollar bets to their lowest level since before the election.

"There is only so long the market will bid the dollar higher on the promise of something," explained Stuart Bennett, head of Group-of-10 currency strategy at Banco Santander SA in London. "They will want detail. And if it’s not forthcoming, then it’s a little bit like the boy who cried wolf."

Complacency could pose a significant risk, according to John Canally, chief economic strategist at LPL Financial in Boston. The Chicago Board Options Exchange Volatility Index, or VIX, a measure of investor anxiety, is currently only two points above its all-time low and is on track for its lowest yearly average on record. "Everyone is wondering why equity market volatility is so low given the uncertainty out there," Canally noted. "The economy is not in dire need of a tax cut, but maybe his speech could be a catalyst" for increased volatility.

Not all analysts share this view. Tobias Levkovich, Citigroup Inc.'s chief U.S. equity strategist, wrote in a February 24 note that the stock rally has been driven by strong earnings and economic data, despite growing skepticism about Trump's policies. "Fears of a major pullback if President Trump does not outline a ‘phenomenal’ tax program on Feb. 28th may be overdone," Levkovich stated.

In the bond market, speculators are maintaining bets on higher yields and lower debt prices. David Woo, head of global rates and FX strategy at Bank of America Merrill Lynch, believes that Trump's desire for a tax plan before the August recess makes it likely that the president will present at least a "skeleton" of the program on Tuesday. Woo added that if Trump provides more clarity on his tax and growth plans, it could increase the Federal Reserve's willingness to raise interest rates. Traders currently estimate a roughly 40 percent probability of a rate hike at the Fed's March 15 policy meeting.

"There is a lot riding on Tuesday," Woo concluded. "The consequences for some kind of plan being unveiled will be massive. You will see volatility really going through the roof if he does so."

President Donald Trump will give his first speech to the U.S. Congress on Tuesday. He is expected to discuss his plan to increase military spending by nearly 10 percent, or $54 billion, offset by equal cuts in non-defense spending. The cuts will likely include large reductions in foreign aid. He told governors on Monday that he would also propose more spending on public safety, including more efforts to stop illegal immigration. And he would call for more spending to rebuild old roadways and airports and reduce taxes. Trump is likely to repeat some of the comments he made last week in a speech before conservatives. He said at the Conservative Political Action Conference, “We will reduce taxes. We will cut your regulations. We will support our police. We will defend our flag. We will rebuild our military. We will take care of our great, great veterans.” Some will watch what tone Trump uses in his speech. Will he reach out to Democrats and others who oppose his policies? Richard Vatz and Gerald Shuster are experts on presidential communication. Neither expects Trump to tone back the strong comments that have marked both his campaign for president and his first 40 days as president. “People have been predicting for a long time that he will change his tone, but it hasn’t happened and I see no reason to think it will now,” Vatz said. Vatz is a professor at Towson University in Maryland. He said the reason Trump is not likely to change is because his supporters like his strong criticism of the news media and Democrats. The downside, Vatz said, is that Trump faces the strongest opposition of any U.S. president since the 1960s. Shuster teaches at the University of Pittsburgh in Pennsylvania. Shuster does not expect Trump to moderate his language unless he faces a major setback -- such as a failure to carry out his promise to repeal and replace the health plan known as Obamacare. Presidential speeches go back to George Washington Presidents have been giving speeches to Congress since the beginning of the Republic. George Washington, the first president, was also the first president to give a speech to Congress. The U.S. constitution says presidents “shall from time to time give to Congress information of the state of the Union.” But no president since John Kennedy in 1961 has given a State of the Union address during their first year in office. Recent presidents have instead given what is called a speech to a joint session of Congress. In his first speech to Congress in 2009, President Barack Obama said he was sure America would overcome its worst recession in over 70 years. “We will rebuild, we will recover, and the United States of America will emerge stronger than before,” Obama said. In 2001, President George W. Bush, a Republican, said America needed to be ready for the unexpected. A little over six months later, America faced its worst terrorist attack ever, killing 3,000 people on September 11, 2001. In 1981, new President Ronald Reagan, a Republican, discussed concerns his policies would hurt low-income Americans. He said, “All those with true need can rest assured that the social safety net of programs they depend on are exempt from any cuts." Five days after the killing of President John F. Kennedy in 1963, President Lyndon Johnson made his first speech to a joint session of Congress. He said, "All I have I would have given gladly not to be standing here today. The greatest leader of our time has been struck down by the foulest deed of our time." Who will watch Trump’s speech? Trump’s speech will be televised live across the United States and in some other nations. The audience watching in the U.S. Capitol will include more than just members of the Senate and House of Representatives. There will be members of his cabinet, Supreme Court justices and news reporters whom Trump calls the “enemy of the people” for stories critical of his administration. Members of Congress also invite “special guests” to watch the speech. House Democratic Leader Nancy Pelosi plans to invite a young immigrant who could be hurt by Trump’s plans to take on illegal immigration. Pelosi also plans to bring a person she says has spoken out about the importance of a free press. Pelosi said Trump has failed to carry out his most important campaign promise. “Five weeks into his administration, President Trump has not introduced a single jobs bill,” she said. But Trump said he is speaking for Americans who have not been treated fairly. “The forgotten men and women of America will be forgotten no longer.” Bruce Alpert reported on this story for VOA Learning English. Hai Do was the editor. We want to hear from you. Write to us in the Comments Section and share your views on our Facebook Page. ___________________________________________________________ Words in This Story regulation - n. an official rule or law that says how something should be done tone - n. a quality, feeling, or attitude expressed by the words that someone uses in speaking state - n. the current condition emerge - v. used to indicate the usually good state or condition of someone or something at the end of an event, process assured - v. be sure something will happen or not happen exempt - n. not affected by some decision, rule or proposal.

query: What specific allegations did Jessica Leeds make against Donald Trump regarding an incident on a flight in the early 1980s?

documents.text: Jessica Leeds, now 74, came forward to the New York Times this week with her account of being sexually assaulted by Republican president nominee Donald Trump. On a flight in the early 1980s, she said, Trump (who was seated next to her) grabbed her breasts and tried to put his hands up her skirt. As Leeds told the Times, "He was like an octopus. His hands were everywhere." This is a harrowing account, one consistent with those of at least five other women who have gone on record accusing Trump of kissing or groping them without consent. But Katrina Pierson, a spokesperson for the Trump campaign and an omnipresent figure on cable news this election, thinks she’s cracked the case: Leeds is lying, she insists, because her story doesn’t jibe with Pierson’s personal knowledge of the development of the American aerospace sector. Here is Pierson, on national television, insisting that Leeds must be lying because of the kind of planes that were common in the early ’80s, and because armrests in first class are fixed: I'm never complaining about sports TV talk shows again. pic.twitter.com/r9tELXOchh — Jason Gay (@jasongay) October 13, 2016 "We're talking about the early 1980s, Don, seriously? Back then you had planes — what, a DC-9, a DC-10, an MD-80, a 707, and maybe an L-1011. But she said specifically that this was to New York. This is important, so we can X-out the DC-10 and the L-1011. Guess what? First-class seats have fixed armrests.” You will be shocked to learn that Katrina Pierson, who was 4 or 5 years old at the time that Leeds alleges the assault took place, is incorrect about which planes were flying out of New York in the early 1980s: Hi @KatrinaPierson, the aircraft types you mentioned all did operate into New York City. https://t.co/pfFWPqlcDQ — NYCAviation (@NYCAviation) October 13, 2016 This is basically the “jet fuel can’t melt steel beams” of sexual assault denialism. This wasn’t the most loathsome thing Pierson said in her CNN hit (that would be her insistence that Trump’s accusers only want “15 minutes of fame”) but despite its ridiculousness, it is instructive of how the campaign will likely approach these allegations: by highly scrutinizing individual details to cast the accusers as liars. ...detail even slightly off will be taken as evidence you are a liar and your entire story is a complete fabrication. — Jesse Berney (@jesseberney) October 13, 2016 Obviously Trump could’ve assaulted Leeds on an airplane with fixed armrests as well, and it would’ve been reasonable for Leeds to not remember that detail 30 years after the fact. And the fact that there are five other named accusers with very similar stories should give her story additional credibility. But that won’t stop ridiculous-sounding objections like this from becoming a mainstay of Trump campaign rhetoric for the rest of the campaign. One can only hope that most people’s reaction, like the rest of the CNN panel’s, is to recognize how absurd a response this is to a chorus of women accusing your candidate of assault. Watch: Women accusing Trump of sexual assault

Donald Trump, the Republican presidential nominee, is actively campaigning for the upcoming November election, facing significant opposition from both the Republican establishment and his Democratic rival, Hillary Clinton. Clinton, in turn, has strongly criticized what she calls the "scorched earth" tactics employed by her Republican opponents.

During a rally in Ocala, Florida, Trump continued his pointed criticism of House Speaker Paul D. Ryan, who had previously declared he would no longer campaign with or defend Trump. Trump expressed his disappointment, questioning why Ryan hadn't offered more support following his debate performance, asking, "Wouldn’t you think that Paul Ryan would call and say, ‘Good going’?" Trump alluded to a potential conspiracy involving Ryan and other Republicans, vowing to uncover the truth: "There’s a whole deal going on — we’re going to figure it out. I always figure things out. But there’s a whole sinister deal going on." This intensified criticism of party members followed a series of tweets from Trump after numerous Republicans withdrew their endorsements.

At a later rally in Lakeland, Florida, Trump shifted his focus to questioning Clinton's intellect, stating, "I don’t think she’s smart. Look how badly she performed in the last debate. I thought she was terrible."

Meanwhile, in Pueblo, Colorado, Clinton urged her supporters to vote and reject what she described as deeply personal attacks from Republicans. "Americans want to turn out in as large numbers as possible," Clinton implored. "Reject the dark and divisive and hateful campaign that is being run."

Clinton's campaign events have been disrupted by individuals wearing T-shirts accusing her husband, former President Bill Clinton, of rape. The Clinton campaign has pointed to conservative radio host Alex Jones and his website Infowars as instigators, alleging they encouraged opponents to spread the accusation and offered financial incentives for media appearances. Jones reportedly made such an offer on his September 30th broadcast, mentioning Trump ally Roger Stone. Jones described a T-shirt designed by Roger Stone that mimicked the Obama "Hope" poster but read "Rape," and urged listeners to "Wear it, get aggressive, start the conversations, get on TV with it." He also offered $1,000 for appearing on national television with the shirt for over five seconds, and $5,000 for clearly stating "Bill Clinton is a rapist" or similar sentiments on national TV using a bullhorn. It remains unconfirmed if these offers directly motivated the disruptions or if any payments were made.

Before Sunday's debate, Trump held a press conference featuring women who had previously accused Bill Clinton of unwanted sexual advances.

In Pueblo, Clinton contrasted Trump's divisive rhetoric with her vision for an inclusive presidency. "We have done our best to stay out of all the meanness," she said. "I want you to know, I want to be the president for everyone. I am tired of all the division and the barriers. I want to bring people together across party lines, across all the lines that divide us."

Addressing criticism about his proximity to Clinton during the debate, Trump remarked at his Lakeland rally, "Believe me, the last space that I want to invade is her space."

Trump's attacks on Clinton have intensified recently. On Tuesday, he released a TV ad highlighting Clinton's coughing and apparent fatigue, which was attributed to pneumonia. Another ad on Wednesday alleged she "only cares about power, money and herself."

Following a Washington Post report detailing a 2005 video of Trump making lewd comments about women, many Republican leaders distanced themselves from him. Trump apologized for the remarks, calling them "locker-room talk," which further angered many. While some GOP officials condemned the video and withdrew their support, others have since changed their stance. Senator Deb Fischer, who initially tweeted it would be "wise for him to step aside and allow Mike Pence to serve as our party’s nominee," later stated in a radio interview, "I support the Republican ticket, and it’s a Trump-Pence ticket."

A prominent surrogate's comments also drew scrutiny. In Ocala, former New York Mayor Rudolph W. Giuliani accused Clinton of disrespecting 9/11 victims and suggested she misrepresented her presence at Ground Zero. "Don’t tell me, if you said that, that you remember September 11, 2001. I remember September 11, 2001," Giuliani asserted. "Yes, you helped to get benefits for the people who were injured one day. But I heard her say she was there that day. I was there that day, I don’t remember seeing Hillary Clinton there." While Clinton was in Washington D.C. on September 11th, she traveled to New York on September 12th, one of the few flights permitted into the city. Publicly available images show Giuliani and Clinton inspecting the destruction together.

Allegations have surfaced regarding Donald Trump's alleged conduct backstage at Miss USA pageants, with former contestants detailing instances where he reportedly entered dressing rooms while women were undressed. One former Miss USA 2001 contestant, speaking anonymously to The Guardian, recounted that Trump, who owned the Miss Universe Organization at the time, entered a dressing room without prior notification. She stated that security had alerted those inside that the women were undressed, yet Trump allegedly proceeded to enter. "Mr. Trump just barged right in, didn’t say anything, stood there and stared at us," the former contestant recalled, interpreting his actions as a clear assertion of power. This alleged incident occurred at the Genesis Convention Center in Gary, Indiana.

This accuser is not the sole former Miss USA 2001 contestant to claim Trump exploited his ownership of the pageant to view contestants in a state of undress. Her account was corroborated by a fellow contestant from the 2001 pageant, who confirmed that the accuser had shared details of the incident shortly after it transpired. Another former Miss USA 2001 contestant, Tasha Dixon (formerly Miss Arizona), also spoke out about Trump's behavior. In an interview with CBS of Los Angeles, Dixon described a separate incident where Trump "waltzed in" to a room where several contestants were partially or fully undressed.

Trump owned the Miss Universe Organization, which encompassed Miss USA, from 1996 to 2005. His access to contestants backstage was further highlighted in a 2005 segment of The Howard Stern Show, a tape of which was obtained by CNN. In the recording, Trump boasted about his ability to enter dressing rooms before shows, stating, "And you know, no men are anywhere. And I’m allowed to go in because I’m the owner of the pageant. And therefore I’m inspecting it. ‘Is everyone OK?’ You know, they’re standing there with no clothes. And you see these incredible-looking women. And so I sort of get away with things like that.” Dixon further elaborated on these encounters, stating, "He just came strolling right in. There was no second to put a robe on or any sort of clothing or anything. Some girls were topless." She added, "We were naked or half naked in a very physically vulnerable position. And then to have the pressure of the people that worked for him telling us to go fawn all over him, go walk up to him, talk to him, get his attention.”

Buzzfeed also reported on Wednesday that several teen beauty queens from the 1997 Miss Teen USA pageant claimed Trump entered a communal dressing room while they were undressed, with one contestant being a mere 15 years old at the time.

However, one Miss USA 2001 contestant who spoke to The Guardian did not recall Trump behaving inappropriately. She remembered him being backstage on the night of the nationally televised pageant and stated that chaperones had announced his impending arrival, giving contestants ample warning to be dressed. This contestant recalled Trump standing in the doorway, wishing them good luck, and stated he did not "parade" through the room.

The accuser who described Trump barging into her dressing room acknowledged that not all contestants had negative experiences. She explained her decision not to report Trump's behavior at the time was due to her precarious position as a contestant representing her state and town. "This was my boss. I’m there to represent my state, my town, my reputation on one of the most prestigious stages on the planet," she explained. "I had not a lot of experience under my belt. And it was horrifying … It was extremely uncomfortable from there forward.” Until she saw Dixon's interview, she believed she and the other woman in the dressing room were Trump's only targets. She stated, "It’s important for people to know that this has been a personality trait of his for a long time." She believes his behavior "completely exerted his power and the control that he had" and that "that kind of behavior perpetuates rape culture and makes young boys think they can behave that way.” Trump spokeswoman Hope Hicks did not immediately respond to an email seeking comment.

Donald Trump's campaign spokeswoman, Katrina Pierson, has suggested that women accusing the candidate of sexual misconduct are primarily seeking "15 minutes of fame." This assertion came during a CNN panel discussion on "Tonight" with Don Lemon. Pierson was responding to reports detailing accusations from multiple women alleging inappropriate touching by Trump over several decades.

When asked about the motivations behind these accusers coming forward, Pierson stated, "Just to answer the question on why they would come out, Don, because 15 minutes of fame." She expanded on this, implying that some accusers aim to boost their public profiles and advance their personal projects. As an example, Pierson mentioned one woman she claimed was trying to sell her book, suggesting a financial incentive behind her allegations.

Pierson specifically challenged the account of Jessica Leeds. Leeds had described an incident detailed in The New York Times where Trump allegedly grabbed her breasts on a flight. Leeds recalled Trump lifting the armrest between them before groping her. Pierson disputed this, asserting that first-class seats at that time had fixed armrests. "The first-class seats have fixed armrests so what I can tell you about her story, if she was groped on a plane, it wasn't by Donald Trump and it certainly wasn't in first class," Pierson declared.

Furthermore, Pierson addressed accusations from Jill Harth. Harth filed a lawsuit in 1997, seeking $125,000,000, and alleged that Trump had sexually harassed her in 1993. Pierson's comments indicate a consistent approach of dismissing these allegations as attempts to gain attention rather than legitimate claims of misconduct.

This exchange took place amidst significant media attention focused on Donald Trump. The day before this interview, Pierson had a notable disagreement with CNN host Carol Costello. Reports indicate Costello interrupted Pierson when the spokeswoman tried to shift the discussion away from lewd comments made by Trump, which were recorded in 2005. When Pierson questioned why Costello wouldn't address Hillary Clinton's position on Syria, Costello responded directly: "I can’t Katrina, because Donald Trump is not talking about that." This exchange underscores the intense media focus on Trump's personal behavior and past statements during the election cycle.

## Former Miss Arizona Tasha Dixon Recounts Uncomfortable Encounter with Donald Trump During Pageant

Tasha Dixon, who was crowned Miss Arizona at 18, has shared her account of an unsettling encounter with Donald Trump during her time as a contestant in the Miss USA pageant. Trump owned the Miss USA, Miss Universe, and Miss Teen USA pageants for nearly two decades, and it was during this period that Dixon experienced what she describes as a deeply uncomfortable situation, highlighting the power dynamics and backstage access prevalent during his ownership.

Dixon specifically recalled an incident during a dress rehearsal for the 2001 Miss USA pageant. She stated that Trump entered the dressing room while contestants were changing into their bikinis. "Our first introduction to him was when we were at the dress rehearsal and half naked changing into our bikinis," Dixon explained. "He just came strolling right in. There was no second to put a robe on or any sort of clothing or anything. Some girls were topless. Other girls were naked." She further elaborated on the pressure from Trump's staff to interact with him, even in these vulnerable circumstances, underscoring the lack of privacy and the perceived expectation of engagement.

This firsthand account aligns with statements Donald Trump himself made in a 2005 broadcast of "The Howard Stern Show," which has recently resurfaced. In an audio recording from April 11, 2005, Trump discussed his unrestricted access to backstage areas prior to the pageants. He stated, "I’ll go backstage before a show and everyone’s getting dressed and ready and everything else. And you know, no men are anywhere. And I’m allowed to go in because I’m the owner of the pageant. And therefore, I’m inspecting it. You know I’m inspecting it. I want to make sure everything is good." When Howard Stern drew a parallel to a doctor's role, Trump continued, "Is everyone OK? You know they’re standing there with no clothes. Is everybody OK? And you see these incredible looking women. And so I sort of get away with things like that.” This admission appears to corroborate Dixon's experience of his presence and access in private contestant areas.

Dixon believes Trump's motivation for owning the pageants was to leverage his authority to interact with attractive women. "I’m telling you Donald Trump owned the pageant for the reasons to utilize his power to get around beautiful women," she stated. "Who do you complain to? He owns the pageant. There’s no one to complain to. Everyone there works for him." This sentiment highlights the perceived lack of recourse for contestants experiencing discomfort or inappropriate behavior, given Trump's ultimate control. The inherent power imbalance, she suggests, created an environment where such actions could occur with impunity.

Dixon decided to speak out after hearing a recently released audio recording of Trump making disparaging remarks about women. She emphasized that her motivation was not political, stating, "I’m Miss Arizona so I veer more on the conservative side. But I just like to choose what’s right." Dixon clarified that she has not listened to Trump's interview with Howard Stern, indicating her decision to come forward is based on her personal experience and a broader sense of moral correctness. Her desire to share her story stems from a conviction about the importance of speaking truth to power, regardless of political affiliation.

In an effort to gather further perspectives, CBS2's Serene Branson attempted to contact other contestants from the 2001 Miss USA pageant. Those who responded declined to be interviewed. However, former Miss California Carrie Prejean Boller issued a statement in defense of Trump, asserting, "To paint Mr. Trump as someone who would purposely walk into a women’s dressing room and ask women to come impress him is the most disgusting accusation so far. Mr. Trump has empowered me as a woman, has given me career opportunities and defended me during my reign as Miss California USA." This statement offers a contrasting viewpoint, highlighting positive experiences some former contestants have had with Trump.

Donald Trump's history with beauty pageants has come under renewed scrutiny, particularly concerning his alleged behavior backstage. Following the release of a controversial 2005 tape where Trump made lewd remarks about women, his campaign has faced intense questioning about his past conduct. This focus has brought to light accounts from former contestants, including those from the 1997 Miss Teen USA pageant, who claim Trump entered dressing rooms while young women were changing.

Mariah Billado, who competed as Miss Vermont Teen USA in 1997, recounted an incident where Donald Trump entered the dressing room while contestants, some as young as 15, were in the process of changing. "I remember putting on my dress really quick because I was like, ‘Oh my god, there’s a man in here,’" Billado stated. She recalled Trump making a remark along the lines of, “Don’t worry, ladies, I’ve seen it all before.”

In addition to Billado's account, three other women who participated in the 1997 Miss Teen USA pageant spoke to BuzzFeed News on the condition of anonymity. They also remembered Trump entering the dressing room while the young contestants were changing. Two of these women described the experience as "shocking" and "creepy," noting that the girls rushed to cover themselves. The third woman mentioned that she was already dressed and introduced herself to Trump.

These recollections emerged within a private Facebook group for some former contestants of the 1997 pageant. The discussion was reportedly sparked by a recent BuzzFeed News report detailing an adult pageant contestant's claim that Trump had entered a changing room when "we were all naked."

BuzzFeed News made efforts to contact 49 of the 51 contestants from the 1997 Miss Teen USA pageant. Of those contacted, 34 either declined to speak or could not be reached. Among the 15 women who were interviewed, none accused Trump of making sexually explicit comments or engaging in physical contact within the dressing room.

Trump himself has publicly spoken about his access to backstage areas during pageants he owned. In recordings released by CNN, Trump told Howard Stern about his practice of going backstage before shows, stating, "No men are anywhere, and I’m allowed to go in, because I’m the owner of the pageant and therefore I’m inspecting it. ... ‘Is everyone OK?’ You know, they’re standing there with no clothes. ‘Is everybody OK?’ And you see these incredible looking women, and so I sort of get away with things like that.” While these statements referred to his general practice, the allegations from the 1997 Miss Teen USA pageant mark the first time such behavior has been specifically linked to a teen pageant where participants could be as young as 14.

Donald Trump owned the Miss Universe, Miss USA, and Miss Teen USA pageants from 1996 until the previous year. The 1997 Miss Teen USA pageant, which was Trump's first as owner, took place on South Padre Island, Texas. Ivanka Trump, who was 15 at the time, served as a co-host for the event.

The Trump campaign and the Trump Organization did not provide a comment when reached by BuzzFeed News. The Miss Universe Organization, which currently oversees Miss Teen USA, also declined to comment on the matter.

Donald Trump's ownership of the Miss Universe Organization, which encompassed Miss USA and Miss Teen USA, concluded in 2015. This divestment followed controversial remarks about Mexicans, prompting broadcasters NBC and Univision to sever their partnerships. During his nearly two decades at the helm, Trump reportedly engaged with approximately 4,000 beauty pageant contestants.

Numerous former contestants have come forward with allegations of inappropriate conduct by Trump within the pageant system. These accounts gained significant traction following the release of a videotape featuring Trump and Billy Bush, and subsequent claims that Trump entered Miss Teen USA changing rooms while young women, some as young as 15, were undressing. Mariah Billado, Miss Vermont 1997, shared her experience with BuzzFeed, recounting how she quickly dressed upon seeing him, stating, "I remember putting on my dress really quick because I was like, 'Oh my god, there's a man in here.'" Three other contestants from the same year corroborated her account. Former Miss Arizona Tasha Dixon also described an incident in 2001, telling Los Angeles' CBS affiliate that Trump entered the Miss USA dressing room while contestants were changing. "He just came strolling right in," Dixon stated. "There was no second to put a robe on or any sort of clothing or anything. Some girls were topless. Others girls were naked." Dixon further alleged that employees of the Miss Universe Organization encouraged contestants to seek Trump's attention.

While the Trump campaign has not directly addressed each specific story, Trump's own remarks on Howard Stern's show in 2005 appear to align with the described behavior. He stated, "I'll go backstage before a show, and everyone's getting dressed and ready and everything else... You know, no men are anywhere. And I'm allowed to go in because I'm the owner of the pageant. And therefore I'm inspecting it... Is everyone OK? You know, they're standing there with no clothes. And you see these incredible-looking women. And so I sort of get away with things like that." Billado also mentioned that when she brought up the incident to Ivanka Trump, she reportedly dismissed it, saying, "Yeah, he does that."

Further accounts detail Trump's interactions with contestants. Upon acquiring the Miss Universe pageant in 1996, Trump reportedly described it as "a sick puppy" to Howard Stern and expressed a preference for beauty over intellect, stating he wasn't interested in contestants who were doctors. Alicia Machado, crowned Miss Universe during Trump's ownership, recalled him calling her "Miss Piggy" for gaining weight and "Miss Housekeeping" due to her Latina heritage, and inviting reporters to observe her exercising despite her protests. In 1997, Brook Antoinette Mahealani Lee, Miss Universe that year, recalled Trump asking her about Ivanka Trump's appearance: "'Don't you think my daughter's hot? She's hot, right?'" Also in 1997, Miss Utah Temple Taggart reported that Trump kissed her without her consent, stating, "He kissed me directly on the lips. I thought, 'Oh my God, gross.' He was married to Marla Maples at the time." Taggart also recounted Trump advising her to lie about her age to advance in the entertainment industry, suggesting, "We're going to have to tell them you're 17." In 2005, the same year he discussed barging into dressing rooms with Howard Stern, Trump joked about the potential conflict of interest in sleeping with contestants, later suggesting it might be his "obligation" as owner. Miss California Carrie Prejean described in her 2009 memoir how Trump would have contestants rate each other's looks, a process she found humiliating.

An audio recording obtained by TMZ features Trump discussing a "Trump Rule" where he would pre-select contestants to ensure the most attractive women advanced, stating, "Because years ago when I first bought it, we chose ten people, I chose none and I get here and the most beautiful people were not chosen. And I went nuts." He also promoted his son, Eric, to the contestants as a handsome and academically successful individual. In 2010, Trump told David Letterman that after buying Miss USA, he "made the heels higher and the bathing suits smaller." Miss Washington Cassandra Searles recalled in 2013 that Trump demanded contestants redo introductions if they didn't make eye contact, describing him as a "misogynist" who "treated us like cattle" and "lined up so he could get a closer look at his property." Other contestants from that year, like Paromita Mitra, shared similar negative experiences, with Mitra stating she has "nightmares about that process." Searles also alleged that Trump "continually grabbed my ass and invited me to his hotel room." The Miss USA pageant in July 2015 saw numerous hosts, judges, and sponsors withdraw following Trump's remarks about Mexicans, leading to his sale of the pageants to WME in September 2015. An update on October 13th, 10:30 a.m. ET, notes that the Trump campaign issued a statement to Rolling Stone, categorically denying these allegations and questioning the political motivations behind the reporting. The statement asserted, "Mr. Trump has a fantastic record of empowering women throughout his career, and a more accurate story would be to show how he's been a positive influence in the lives of so many."

Donald Trump's campaign is facing renewed scrutiny as more videos surface of his past comments about women, following the release of a 2005 tape containing lewd remarks.

### Comments on Young Girls

In a 1992 "Entertainment Tonight" Christmas feature, a 46-year-old Trump was filmed looking at a group of young girls. He remarked to the camera that he would be dating one of them in ten years. The video, shot at Trump Tower, shows Trump asking one of the girls if she was "going up the escalator." When she replied "yeah," Trump turned to the camera and said, "I am going to be dating her in 10 years. Can you believe it?"

This is not the first time Trump has made comments about his interest in dating much younger women. In 2005, he made a similar remark about his own daughter, Ivanka Trump, stating, "I’ve said that if Ivanka weren’t my daughter, perhaps I’d be dating her."

### "Access Hollywood" Tape and Accusations

The issue gained significant attention with the release of the 2005 "Access Hollywood" video, which captured lewd banter between Trump and entertainment reporter Billy Bush. Trump has defended these remarks as "locker room banter" and apologized for his suggestion that "when you’re a star" you can "grab [women] by the p****." He stated in the tape, "You can do anything."

During a debate with Hillary Clinton, Trump insisted his comments were "just words" and that he had never groped a woman. However, at least three women have since come forward to dispute this, telling news organizations that he groped them in personal encounters. These alleged incidents reportedly occurred at his home, Trump Tower, and on a commercial flight.

Trump's campaign announced its intention to sue The New York Times over a story published that day detailing the accounts of two of these women.

### Reporter's Account

Also on Wednesday, People magazine published an account from one of its reporters, Natasha Stoynoff. She recalled Trump "pushing me against the wall, and forcing his tongue down my throat" during an interview at his Mar-a-Lago home. Stoynoff stated this encounter happened in 2005, the same year as the "Access Hollywood" tape. At the time of the alleged incident, Trump's wife, Melania, was pregnant and changing in another room.

### Pageant Dressing Room Comments

A former beauty queen has corroborated Donald Trump's own statements about walking into pageant dressing rooms unannounced. This follows the resurfacing of audio recordings where Trump boasted about his ability to do so as the owner of the Miss USA pageant.

Tasha Dixon, who competed as Miss Arizona in 2001, told CBS' Los Angeles station that Trump entered dressing rooms while contestants were "half naked." Dixon recounted, "Our first introduction to him was when we were at the dress rehearsal and half naked changing into our bikinis. He just came strolling right in. There was no second to put a robe on or any sort of clothing or anything. Some girls were topless. Other girls were naked."

In a 2005 interview on Howard Stern's radio show, Trump discussed using his pageant ownership to observe women while they were undressed. He said, "I’ll go backstage before a show and everyone’s getting dressed and ready and everything else. And you know, no men are anywhere. And I’m allowed to go in because I’m the owner of the pageant. And therefore, I’m inspecting it. You know I’m inspecting it. I want to make sure everything is good." He added, "You know they’re standing there with no clothes. Is everybody OK? And you see these incredible looking women. And so I sort of get away with things like that."

query: What solution did former Education Secretary William Bennett propose to prevent mass shootings in schools?

documents.text: Former Education Secretary William Bennett said Sunday that schools should consider arming employees in order to prevent mass shootings. "I'm not sure so -- and I'm sure I'll get nailed for this -- I wouldn't want one person in a school armed, ready for this kind of thing," Bennett said on NBC's "Meet the Press." "It has to be someone who is trained, it has to be someone who is responsible," he said. "But my God, if you can prevent this kind of thing." Bennett said that in the latest school shooting in Newtown, Conn., the principal and school psychologist of Sandy Hook Elementary School tried to defend the children against the attacker — but they were unarmed. "The principal lunged at this guy. The school psychologist lunged at the guy," Bennett said. Bennett served as the education secretary under President Ronald Reagan, and drug czar under President George H.W. Bush. Read more about: Connecticut School Shooting, William Bennett

Following the tragic Newtown, Connecticut school shooting, U.S. Representative Louie Gohmert (R-Texas) advocated for an open-minded approach to gun control discussions. Appearing on "Fox News Sunday," Gohmert suggested that increasing the number of armed individuals, rather than restricting firearms, could be a more effective way to prevent future mass casualty events.

### Gun-Free Zones as Targets

Gohmert highlighted a perceived pattern, stating, "Every mass killing of more than three people in recent history has been in a place where guns were prohibited." He argued that perpetrators may deliberately choose these locations because they expect no armed opposition.

### A Hypothetical Scenario

Specifically referencing the Sandy Hook Elementary School incident, Gohmert speculated that the outcome could have been different if Principal Dawn Hochsprung, who died trying to protect students, had been armed. He expressed a strong desire, saying, "I wish to God she had had an M4 in her office locked up and so when she heard gunshots … she takes his head off before he can hurt those kids."

### The Need for Open Dialogue

Gohmert stressed that any conversation about gun control reforms requires an "open-minded" perspective. He echoed Senator Dick Durbin's sentiment, stating, "Sen. [Dick] Durbin’s right, but the conversation we’ve got to have has got to have everybody open-minded." While acknowledging the emotional impact of such tragedies, Gohmert added, "We all react emotionally, that’s why we’ve all shed tears and our prayers will continue to go to the people in Connecticut who’ve lost loved ones."

Senator Dianne Feinstein, a leading Democratic voice in the Senate, reaffirmed her commitment on Sunday to reintroducing a federal ban on assault weapons, urging President Barack Obama to spearhead this crucial legislative initiative. "I can assure you that he will have a bill to lead on," Feinstein declared on NBC's "Meet the Press." "As a first-day initiative, I intend to introduce legislation in the Senate, and an identical bill will be presented in the House, specifically aimed at prohibiting assault weapons." This comprehensive legislation, as detailed by Feinstein, will prohibit the sale, transfer, importation, and possession of these firearms, applying prospectively rather than retroactively. The ban will also extend to large-capacity magazines, drums, or strips capable of holding more than ten bullets. These significant pronouncements followed the tragic massacre at Sandy Hook Elementary School in Newtown, Connecticut, where Adam Lanza reportedly used a Bushmaster .223 assault rifle, resulting in the deaths of twenty-six individuals, including twenty young children.

The proposed legislation builds upon the foundation of the Federal Assault Weapons Ban, which was enacted from 1994 until its expiration in 2004. President Obama has previously expressed support for reinstating such a ban. "There will be a bill. We have been diligently working on this for the past year," Senator Feinstein confirmed, noting that her team has refined the original 1994-2004 bill. "Our proposed legislation exempts over nine hundred specific weapon models, ensuring they will not be subject to the ban. Nevertheless, the fundamental purpose of this bill remains consistent with the sentiment expressed by Mayor Bloomberg: to remove weapons of war from the streets of our communities." Feinstein recalled facing skepticism during her initial push for the assault weapons ban, even from colleagues like then-Senator Joe Biden, who advised her to wait for more experience. Despite these doubts, she persevered, successfully navigating the bill through both the Senate and the House with crucial assistance from the Clinton administration, demonstrating that such legislative achievements are indeed attainable.

Connecticut's senators are making a strong commitment to address gun violence on the Senate floor, vowing to take up the issue in the wake of the tragic Sandy Hook Elementary School shooting. Senator Richard Blumenthal (D-Conn.) and Senator-elect Chris Murphy (D-Conn.) both expressed their determination to move forward with policy discussions, emphasizing the urgent need for action.

Senator Blumenthal, drawing on his extensive background as a prosecutor and his deep connections within the law enforcement community, stated on ABC's "This Week," "I'm hearing from the community as well as my colleagues in law enforcement: We need to do something." He further elaborated, acknowledging the profound grief of the families affected by the Newtown tragedy, "Out of respect for the families and their grief, I'm not going to be more specific about that conversation." Blumenthal believes that this horrific event, which claimed the lives of 26 individuals at the hands of a perpetrator described as "deranged person possessed by demons," will undoubtedly catalyze and reshape the national dialogue surrounding gun control. He explicitly declared, "I intend to talk about it on the floor of the United States Senate."

Echoing this sentiment, Senator-elect Murphy asserted that the era of avoiding policy discussions in the face of such devastating events has concluded. "The time for sort of saying that we can't talk about the policy implications of tragedies like this is over," Murphy declared. He affirmed their intention to engage in these critical conversations promptly, stating, "We're going to be on the floor of the Senate very soon talking about where we go from here." This unified stance from Connecticut's congressional delegation signals a significant push to confront the complex issue of gun violence at the federal level.

New York Mayor Michael Bloomberg challenged the perceived power of the National Rifle Association (NRA) on Sunday, calling it a "myth" that the organization can derail political careers. He shared his personal strategy from the recent election cycle, where he actively supported candidates running against those with strong NRA backing. "Where the NRA was putting their money into one side, I decided to put my money into the other side," Bloomberg explained on NBC's "Meet the Press."

This approach proved successful, as Bloomberg reported winning "four out of seven" races, often with a modest financial investment. He emphasized, "There is this myth that the NRA is so powerful." Bloomberg argued that the NRA's influence is "vastly overrated," especially when considering public sentiment. He pointed to polls indicating a desire among the public to end gun violence, stating, "The public, when you do the polls, they want to stop this carnage. And if 20 kids isn't enough to convince 'em, I don't know what would be." This statement came in the wake of the tragic shooting at Sandy Hook Elementary School.

Bloomberg, a vocal proponent of stricter gun control, also highlighted the NRA's failure to prevent President Barack Obama's re-election. "The NRA's number one objective this time was to defeat Barack Obama for a second term. Last time I checked the election results, he won and he won comfortably," he asserted. He reiterated his core message: "This myth that the NRA can destroy political careers is just not true."

In the aftermath of the Sandy Hook shooting, calls for revising gun laws have intensified, a stance consistently opposed by the NRA. Bloomberg urged lawmakers to act, suggesting, "If Congress wasn't so afraid of the NRA — and I can show you that they have no reason to be — but if they were to stand up and do what was right for the American public, we'd all be a lot better off."

### Pro-Gun Senators Skip "Meet The Press" Amid Gun Control Debate

Despite an invitation to discuss their views on gun control, no senators who support gun rights appeared on Sunday's "Meet The Press." Host David Gregory confirmed that his program reached out to all 31 pro-gun rights senators in the new Congress, but none accepted the opportunity to share their perspectives. "We had no takers," Gregory stated.

The program instead featured guests who advocate for stricter gun control, including Senator Dianne Feinstein (D-Calif.) and New York City Mayor Michael Bloomberg. This discussion occurred in the context of heightened national attention on gun violence, particularly following the tragic school shooting in Connecticut, and aimed to delve into the ongoing debate surrounding gun rights and gun control in the United States.

Sen. Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) on Sunday called for three specific steps Congress can take to stop gun violence in the United States, starting with the restoration of the federal assault weapons ban. "One is to ban assault weapons, try and reinstate the assault weapons bans," he said on CBS's "Face the Nation." "Second is to limit the size of clips to maybe no more than 10 bullets per clip, and third is to make it harder for mentally unstable people to get guns." Schumer said any action on reforming gun laws has been difficult thus far because of political gridlock on Capitol Hill. "We’ve been gridlocked," he said. "We need a new paradigm … Both sides are in their corner and they can come to the middle" He added that, just as anti-gun control advocates need to be willing to compromise, those in favor of stronger gun laws need to acknowledge the Second Amendment rights of ordinary citizens. "Those of us who are pro-gun control have to admit that there is a Second Amendment right to bear arms," he said. Read more about: Chuck Schumer, Connecticut School Shooting

Kim Kardashian's iconic 2014 Paper magazine cover shoot, a deliberate attempt to "Break The Internet," cemented her status as a pop culture phenomenon. The striking imagery, masterfully captured by renowned photographer Jean-Paul Goude, played on themes of sensuality and the burgeoning cultural fascination with the human posterior.

One of the covers featured Kardashian in a form-fitting gown, artfully balancing a champagne glass on her derriere, with a stream of bubbly creating an almost angelic arc into the glass. This image was a direct homage to Goude's earlier "Champagne Incident" photograph. However, it was the alternate cover that truly ignited social media. In this shot, Kardashian turned her back to the camera, her head swiveling to face forward, while lowering the same dress to reveal her famously ample, and heavily edited, backside. This served as a bold reinterpretation of her widely shared "belfie" (butt selfie) on Instagram.

Paper magazine explicitly stated their objective for the winter issue: "Break The Internet." They identified Kim Kardashian West as the singular individual capable of achieving this ambitious goal, recognizing her unparalleled ability to generate headlines and captivate the online world. The issue, priced at $10, was set to hit newsstands shortly after.

This moment arrived amidst a broader cultural trend where the human buttocks were prominently featured in popular culture. From Nicki Minaj's "Anaconda" music video to Iggy Azalea's distinctive physique and Jennifer Lopez's song "Booty," the posterior was undeniably having a significant cultural moment. Kardashian, a keen observer of trends, clearly aimed to participate in this "conversation."

The cultural obsession with the buttocks did not go unnoticed by critics. Bill Maher, on his HBO show *Real Time*, offered a pointed commentary on what he perceived as a shallow culture. He argued that a veneration of youth over respect for elders contributed to this fixation, stating, "Our culture is shallow because we don’t respect the elderly. We venerate the young and let them rule everything. We let them run everything. How else would you explain the cultural moment that is now being enjoyed by the human buttocks? It’s an ass. It’s been around for centuries. Half of our music and all of our dancing is just about worshipping, praising, staring at and waxing poetic about the human ass. Millennials should be called ‘Generation Ass.’"

Amidst this cultural backdrop, Kardashian's provocative imagery could be seen as her unique way of engaging with the zeitgeist, perhaps even a playful salute to the prevailing trends. The question of her husband Kanye West's reaction to the daring photoshoot remained a point of intrigue.

Kim Kardashian's daring Paper magazine cover, released in late 2014, quickly became a viral sensation, with many users taking to social media to share their reactions and create humorous memes. The sheer audacity of the cover also prompted some lighthearted disbelief. One user tweeted, "I can't believe Kim K is trying to break the internet. Somebody has to stop her." The visual of the oiled-up cover also led to a humorous, albeit crude, comparison involving Arsenal manager Arsene Wenger and a slip and slide. Beyond the jokes, some users offered more grounded, and perhaps less flattering, observations. Jaime Primak tweeted, "People do realize that it's still poop that comes out of Kim Kardashian's tush, right? I mean, it is, right?"

This collection of reactions showcases the diverse and often witty ways the internet responded to Kim Kardashian's unforgettable Paper magazine cover.

The Internet Archive actively crawls the web to combat the pervasive issue of broken links. By archiving web pages and their references as they are created, the Archive ensures a lasting record of online content, even as the live web evolves. This effort is fundamental to the Archive's goal of a more stable and reliable internet.

This initiative directly supports the "No More 404" campaign, helping users access information that might otherwise disappear. The Internet Archive achieves this by:

*   **Restoring broken links:** When a linked page is no longer live, the archived version can be used to restore access, preventing the frustration of 404 errors.
*   **Preserving context:** By linking to archived content, the Archive helps readers understand the original intent of authors, especially when live versions change. This maintains the historical context of online information.

This strategic archiving is essential for the integrity and accessibility of the internet's vast information.

The End of Term Web Archive (EOTWA) project meticulously captured a snapshot of the U.S. federal government's digital presence during the crucial transition period leading up to the January 20, 2017 presidential inauguration, with web crawls conducted in the Fall and Winter of 2016 to preserve this vital information. This comprehensive archive encompasses a broad spectrum of digital content, including official government websites from .gov and .mil domains, which represent the direct online presence of federal agencies, as well as important non-.gov websites that host essential government information, ensuring a more thorough record. Furthermore, the project also archived content shared by government bodies on various social media platforms, thereby capturing a wider view of their digital communications. Spearheaded by the California Digital Library (CDL), the primary objective of this significant archiving effort was to meticulously document the U.S. federal government's digital footprint during this pivotal historical moment. For further details and to access the archived materials, please visit the EOTWA website managed by the CDL at http://eotarchive.cdlib.org/.
