query: What specific actions did the U.S. Treasury Department take against Chinese and Russian individuals and firms in relation to North Korea's missile and nuclear program?

documents.text: A photo distributed by the North Korean government shows what was said to be the launch of a Hwasong-14 intercontinental ballistic missile at an undisclosed location in North Korea. (AP) The Treasury Department on Tuesday placed sanctions on Chinese and Russian individuals and firms that it said had conducted business with North Korea in ways that advanced the country’s missile and nuclear weapons program, part of a broad effort by the Trump administration to further isolate the regime. The sanctions against 10 companies and six individuals are designed to disrupt the economic ties that have allowed Pyongyang to continue funding its missile and nuclear program despite strict United Nations sanctions prohibiting it. It was the fifth set of U.S. sanctions related to North Korea this year, and the largest. In a related move, two legal complaints were filed Tuesday by the Justice Department seeking the forfeiture of $11 million from two of the sanctioned companies believed to have been laundering money on behalf of North Korea. The complaints, filed in the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia, would represent two of the largest seizures of North Korean funds. The Trump administration has been trying to strengthen the economic vise on North Korea in an effort to persuade it to negotiate an end to its nuclear weapons development. Last month, the administration pushed a new round of sanctions against North Korea at the U.N. Security Council. In response, North Korea vowed retaliation “a thousand times over,” and Foreign Minister Ri Yong Ho declared that North Korea would never relinquish its ballistic missile and nuclear programs. For three decades, North Korean Ri Jong Ho was one of many men responsible for secretly sending millions of dollars back to Pyongyang. He sat down with The Washington Post’s Anna Fifield to tell his story. (Anna Fifield,Jason Aldag/The Washington Post) But even as two federal agencies were taking stern measures against North Korea, Secretary of State Rex Tillerson made a gesture of appreciation to Pyongyang, welcoming its apparent restraint in not conducting any new weapons tests since the latest U.N. sanctions were adopted Aug. 5. “We hope that this is the beginning of this signal that we’ve been looking for,” Tillerson said at a news conference that otherwise focused on Afghanistan, “that they are ready to restrain their level of tensions, they’re ready to restrain their provocative acts and that, perhaps, we are seeing our pathway to sometime in the near future having some dialogue.” Sanctions have been the main weapon of choice as the United States has grown increasingly alarmed at North Korea’s technological progress in developing weapons capable of reaching the United States and miniaturizing nuclear warheads to fit atop them. Even though they are imposed by just one country, U.S. sanctions have an outsize influence because most international banking is conducted at least partially in U.S. dollars. The measures prohibit U.S. citizens and companies from doing business with the sanctioned companies, and many banks outside the United States also adhere to them so they don’t run afoul of U.S. laws and face stiff penalties. “The sanctions send a strong message to Beijing and Moscow to stop facilitating North Korea’s sanctions evasion,” said Anthony Ruggiero, a fellow with the Foundation for Defense of Democracies, which favors tougher sanctions on Russia and China over North Korea. “The action is one element of a pressure campaign that also includes targeting illicit financial transactions and pressuring U.S. allies to choose between business with the United States or North Korea.” But in a display of the Kremlin’s anger over sanctions placed on four Russian individuals and one Russian company, Russian Deputy Foreign Minister Sergei Ryabkov said the United States had again “stepped on the same rake.” He called the new sanctions the latest example of the United States damaging its relationship with Russia. “In recent years, Washington ‘in theory’ should have learned that for us the language of sanctions is unacceptable,” Ryabkov said in a statement. “The solution of real problems is only hindered by such actions. So far, however, it does not seem that they have come to an understanding of such obvious truths.” Ryabkov promised that Russia was “beginning to work out a response that is inevitable in this situation.” The Chinese Embassy in Washington, in a statement carried by the China Daily newspaper, said, “China opposes unilateral sanctions out of the U.N. Security Council framework, especially the ‘long-arm jurisdiction’ over Chinese entities and individuals exercised by any country in accordance with its domestic laws.” It said that China faithfully implements Security Council resolutions on North Korea in their entirety and fully observes its international obligations. “If there are any Chinese companies or individuals suspected of violating Security Council resolutions, they will be investigated and treated in accordance with China’s domestic laws and regulations,” it said. “We strongly urge the U.S. to immediately correct its mistake, so as not to impact bilateral cooperation on relevant issues.” Although U.S. military officials and President Trump have said that the United States is prepared to take some sort of military action against North Korea if provoked, Tillerson has repeatedly called for Pyongyang to negotiate and said that the United States does not seek regime change. Despite the diplomatic push, China, in particular, has been a sore point in making existing sanctions stick. Beijing has largely gone along with restrictions, at least for a time, and supported an escalating series of U.N. sanctions. But many Chinese companies have continued to do business with the North Korean regime by supplying technology and hardware for its missiles. China is believed to be responsible for 90 percent of North Korea’s international trade. The sanctions announced Tuesday by the Office of Foreign Assets Control were predominantly against Chinese companies that have dealt with North Korea by purchasing and selling coal, oil and mineral resources, or have provided banking services that made the transactions possible. The sanctions also hit two companies that arranged for North Korean laborers to build statues in foreign countries. Tillerson has been urging countries that have relations with North Korea to downsize Pyongyang’s diplomatic presence and refuse to hire North Korean labor. Overseas labor is a source of revenue for the North Korean government, and the Treasury Department contends that some of the laborers’ income helped finance ballistic missile testing. “Treasury will continue to increase pressure on North Korea by targeting those who support the advancement of nuclear and ballistic missile programs, and isolating them from the American financial system,” Treasury Secretary Steven Mnuchin said. He added: “It is unacceptable for individuals and companies in China, Russia and elsewhere to enable North Korea to generate income used to develop weapons of mass destruction and destabilize the region.” The sanctions hit three types of business dealings that provide a window into how North Korea uses companies in other countries to evade sanctions. China-based Dandong Rich Earth Trading Co. was sanctioned for buying vanadium ore from a company tied to North Korea’s atomic energy agency. The Russian firm Gefest-M, which trades in a wide range of consumer goods as well as construction and industrial equipment, allegedly procured metals for a North Korean mining company with a Moscow office. The Chinese company Mingzheng International Trading was accused of facilitating dollar transactions on behalf of North Korea’s proliferation network. In addition, three Chinese coal companies were sanctioned for importing nearly $500 million of North Korean coal between 2013 and 2016. The Treasury Department said coal trade generates more than $1 billion a year for North Korea, the country’s biggest export and an activity that was targeted in U.N. sanctions imposed in November. In the Justice Department complaints filed in federal court Tuesday, one seeks $4 million from Dandong Zhicheng Metallic Material Co., also known as Dandong Chengtai Trading Co. , one of China’s largest importers of North Korean coal. According to the complaint, it and related companies imported North Korean coal and then sent a wide array of products — cellphones, luxury items, rubber and sugar — to North Korea. The other complaint seeks $7 million associated with Velmur Management Pte., a Singapore-based company that was allegedly accepting money from front companies for North Korean banks. Velmur is accused of sending money to a Russian petroleum company alleged to have sent fuel oil to North Korea. “These complaints show our determination to stop North Korean sanctioned banks and their foreign financial facilitators from aiding North Korea in illegally accessing the United States financial system to obtain goods and services in the global market place,” U.S. Attorney Channing Phillips said in a statement. The United States also is targeting North Korea’s revenue from overseas labor. Among the new sanctions, Mansudae Overseas Projects was accused of helping North Korean laborers work abroad, usually in countries with authoritarian rulers, to build statues that immortalize the dictators. According to Treasury, Kim Tong Chol, Mansudae’s managing director, arranged for Qingdao Construction, a Namibia-based subsidiary of a Chinese company, to take over four Namibian government-sponsored construction projects as well as the employees and materials associated with the work. David Filipov in Moscow and Simon Denyer in Beijing contributed to this report.

WASHINGTON—The U.S. on Tuesday targeted a host of Chinese and Russian firms and related individuals it accuses of aiding Pyongyang, expanding its broader efforts to clamp down on financing critical to North Korea’s nuclear-weapons program. The U.S. Treasury’s Office of Foreign Assets Control added 10 firms, including Chinese coal importers and Russian fuel exporters, to its North Korea sanctions list. Federal prosecutors also filed a pair of cases in federal court in Washington seeking tens of millions of dollars in penalties...

The United States has levied new sanctions against Chinese and Russian companies and individuals, accusing them of aiding North Korea's weapons development. In a statement released Tuesday, a Chinese embassy spokesperson in Beijing declared that the U.S. must "immediately correct its mistake" regarding these unilateral sanctions.

This spokesperson underscored China's opposition to sanctions not sanctioned by the U.N. Security Council. They specifically called out the U.S. policy of "long-arm jurisdiction," which involves a nation enforcing its own laws on entities and individuals beyond its territorial limits. China is strongly urging the U.S. to retract this decision to avoid detrimental effects on their cooperative efforts.

North Korea's envoy to U.N. disarmament talks, Ju Yong Chol, declared on Tuesday that the United States' persistent "military threats and pressure" are the direct impetus behind Pyongyang's ongoing efforts to bolster its nuclear deterrence capabilities. Addressing the Conference on Disarmament in Geneva, Ju firmly asserted that any nations opposing North Korea's nuclear and missile testing activities are, in effect, aligning themselves with the United States' "hostile" posture. He further accused Washington of attempting to unfairly "shift the blame for the tense situation on the Korean Peninsula to DPRK."

This declaration from the North Korean envoy emerges amidst escalating concerns voiced by U.S. ambassador Robert Wood, who has unequivocally warned that North Korea's advanced ballistic-missile and nuclear-weapons programs represent "grave threats to the entire world." Ambassador Wood highlighted that recent intercontinental ballistic missile tests conducted by Pyongyang have starkly demonstrated the "dangerous and reckless behavior of the North," behavior he characterized as actively destabilizing the region and extending its negative impact far beyond. Notwithstanding these serious apprehensions, Wood emphasized that the "path to dialogue remains open," signaling a continued willingness for diplomatic engagement.

"If North Korea demonstrates a willingness to relinquish certain assets, which is our primary objective, then exploring this avenue is essential," he stated. "I am confident that our military possesses the capability to maintain troop readiness and ensure seamless interoperability with South Korean forces."

Australia has strongly condemned North Korea's "reckless and dangerous" actions, particularly after Pyongyang directly threatened Australia, posing a significant risk to global peace. This denouncement follows North Korea's prior warning that Australia's support for US and South Korean military forces during an attack would be a "suicidal act." Prime Minister Malcolm Turnbull urged global cooperation to bring North Korea into compliance with international norms, stating, "North Korea has demonstrated a complete disregard for the welfare of its own citizens, for the security and positive relationships with its neighbours, and for international law." He implored, "We implore all nations to intensify their efforts, including the rigorous implementation of established UN Security Council resolutions, to compel North Korea to cease its dangerous and destabilizing threats to regional and world peace." Turnbull also reaffirmed Australia's commitment to its ANZUS treaty obligations, stating, "In matters of defense, our alliance is unbreakable."

North Korea's state news agency, KCNA, reported that Australia's commitment to the US and its participation in a South Korean military exercise constituted a "suicidal act." This statement came as the US and South Korea began their annual 10-day exercise, Ulchi-Freedom Guardian (UFG). The exercise involves tens of thousands of personnel, with a small contingent of British and Australian troops participating in computer simulations. A North Korean Foreign Ministry spokesperson declared, "This is a suicidal act of inviting disaster, illustrating political immaturity and a failure to grasp the gravity of the current situation." Australia's Defence Minister, Marise Payne, clarified that Australia has played a minor role in these exercises since 2010, emphasizing, "These exercises should not be perceived as provocative in any way."

The joint military drill has inflamed North Korea due to heightened animosity between Pyongyang and Washington, intensified by North Korea's recent intercontinental ballistic missile (ICBM) tests. These tests placed much of the US within range, prompting a stern warning from President Donald Trump. Australian Foreign Minister Julie Bishop characterized Trump's threat as "speaking Kim Jong-un's language." North Korea then threatened to launch missiles towards Guam, a plan temporarily postponed by Kim Jong-un. Amidst these threats, North Korea declared the UFG exercise could escalate into "nuclear war." An editorial in North Korea's official Rodong Sinmun newspaper stated, "The joint exercise represents the most overt expression of hostility towards us, and no one can guarantee that it will not devolve into actual combat."

Seoul and Washington confirmed the largely computer-simulated UFG exercise will proceed. Approximately 17,500 US troops are slated to participate, a reduction from the previous year. South Korea's top military official stated the current security environment is "more critical than at any other time" due to North Korea's escalating capabilities. He warned Pyongyang of severe retaliation for any aggression. "Should the enemy provoke us, our military will respond decisively and forcefully, ensuring they deeply regret their actions," declared General Jeong Kyeong-Doo.

China, North Korea's primary ally, has reiterated calls for de-escalation. Beijing expressed dissatisfaction with both Pyongyang's tests and perceived escalatory actions by the US and South Korea. The state-run Global Times editorialized that Beijing is unable to influence either Washington or Pyongyang to back down. The paper stated, "China must clearly articulate its position to all parties, making them understand that any actions jeopardizing China's interests will be met with a firm response." It further clarified China's stance: "Furthermore, China must make it clear that if North Korea initiates missile launches threatening US territory and the US retaliates, China will remain neutral." It also stated, "Should the US and South Korea launch strikes aimed at regime change in North Korea and altering the political landscape of the Korean Peninsula, China will intervene to prevent such an outcome." China fears a conflict could trigger a refugee crisis and result in a unified Korea allied with the US. North Korea serves as a crucial buffer state for China, separating it from US forces in South Korea and Japan.

The U.S. Treasury Department has imposed sanctions on 16 Chinese and Russian entities and individuals accused of supporting North Korea's nuclear and ballistic missile programs. Treasury Secretary Steven Mnuchin stated that these measures are designed to "increase pressure on North Korea by targeting those who support the advancement of nuclear and ballistic missile programs, and isolating them from the American financial system."

Among the sanctioned entities are three Chinese companies identified by the administration for importing nearly half a billion dollars in North Korean coal. Additionally, Russian individuals involved in supplying oil to North Korea have been targeted.

This action by the Treasury Department, as reported by sources like the Associated Press, follows a period of heightened tensions stemming from North Korea's nuclear weapons development and missile tests. The United Nations also agreed earlier this month to enhance sanctions against the nation.

Secretary Mnuchin emphasized the unacceptability of individuals and companies in China, Russia, and other nations enabling North Korea to generate revenue for weapons of mass destruction and regional destabilization. He affirmed that these actions align with UN sanctions, demonstrating consequences for defying international restrictions and providing support to North Korea, with the aim of deterring such activities.

Looking ahead, the Senate Banking Committee is anticipated to review new legislation concerning North Korea sanctions this autumn.

North Korea's art exports have made a significant global impact, with many of their grand creations prominently displayed in public spaces across Africa. At the core of this unique artistic enterprise is the **Mansudae Art Studio**, established in 1959. This massive complex houses around 4,000 artists and craftsmen. Pier Luigi Cecioni, the studio's only external representative, described it as "more of a campus than a factory, the biggest in the world." The studio produces iconic North Korean public art: large propaganda murals, detailed tapestries, and imposing bronze statues. While these works initially served domestic ideological purposes, they have become a crucial international revenue source, generating millions of dollars through commissions, with African nations being a key market.

**Mansudae Overseas Projects (MOP)** is the primary channel for the Mansudae Art Studio's international artistic ventures. MOP exports North Korea's distinctive artistic style—marked by its bold, authoritarian aesthetic—throughout Africa and beyond. This artistic diplomacy, which began in the early 1980s with gifts to socialist and non-aligned countries, is now a vital source of foreign currency for the isolated state. MOP's artists have completed major projects in numerous African nations, including Angola, Benin, Chad, the Democratic Republic of Congo, Equatorial Guinea, Ethiopia, and Togo. For example, reports from Zimbabwe mention the commissioning of two large statues of Robert Mugabe, intended for unveiling after his death, highlighting the long-term nature of these artistic projects.

A remarkable example of MOP's capabilities is the **African Renaissance Monument** in Senegal. This 49-meter (161ft) structure, inaugurated in 2010, was entirely cast on-site by Mansudae craftsmen. This achievement demonstrates their impressive logistical skills and artistic talent in completing such a massive project in a foreign country. The sheer scale and ambition of this monument underscore the significant role North Korean art plays in shaping the visual landscape of African nations.

The enduring appeal of North Korean art in Africa stems from its **affordability and its distinctive, powerful style**. In Senegal, for instance, land grants facilitated the financing for their monumental statue, which North Korea later sold for capital, showing a practical approach to international art commissions. Art critic William Feaver notes that while countries like Russia and China have moved away from overtly declarative art, the "statement of the obvious" and emphasis on monumental scale still resonate. Feaver compares these grand artistic statements to landmarks like Mount Rushmore, suggesting they serve as potent symbols for nation-building and self-assertion. This is particularly relevant for newly independent nations aiming to project a strong national identity and a sense of permanence. North Korean art's ability to meet this need, often at a competitive price, makes it an attractive option for governments.

The presence of Mansudae's art is evident across many African landscapes, leaving a lasting mark on the continent's public spaces. In Namibia, the **Heroes' Acre memorial** near Windhoek features a large obelisk and an 11-meter (36ft) bronze statue of the Unknown Soldier. This statue bears a striking resemblance to Namibia's first President, Sam Nujoma, raising questions about artistic intent. Historian Adrian Tinniswood observes that while these statues effectively symbolize liberation and national pride, they can project a "weirdly North Korean" aesthetic. This stylistic imprint, he suggests, might indicate a "failure of confidence" in developing truly unique indigenous artistic representation. The pervasive Mansudae style can sometimes overshadow the specific cultural narratives the monuments are meant to convey.

Concerns about ethnic representation and stylistic authenticity have also arisen with these commissions. In Dakar, Senegal, President Abdoulaye Wade reportedly requested changes to monument figures to make them appear less distinctly Asian, indicating a desire for local relevance. The 2011 statue of Samora Machel in Maputo, Mozambique, is noted for its weak likeness to the subject, a common criticism of some Mansudae works. Additionally, the 2001 statue of Laurent Kabila in Kinshasa, DRC, is depicted in attire favored by the Kims' tailors, highlighting the pervasive North Korean stylistic influence that can sometimes overshadow the specific historical context or personal characteristics of the commemorated individual. These instances reveal the delicate balance between artistic execution and the cultural and political sensitivities of the commissioning nation.

Despite these criticisms, Mansudae artists possess a unique artistic perspective. Pier Luigi Cecioni highlights their deep understanding of classical art traditions and their view of abstract and conceptual art as merely "amusing," indicating a focus on representational and narrative art. Unlike Western artists navigating market pressures, Mansudae artists benefit from stable salaries, consistent recognition within their studio system, and privileges associated with their state-sponsored roles. Their work, often anonymous and dedicated to a perceived "higher good" or national ideology, contrasts sharply with the profit-driven global art market. Resources like Cecioni's website showcase their preference for subjects like flowers and soldiers, executed with exceptional technical skill and realism. For those seeking monumental artistic statements that project power, national identity, and a lasting legacy, the Mansudae Art Studio remains a singular global provider. They offer a distinct artistic vision that continues to shape public spaces and national narratives across Africa, blending artistic tradition with ideological expression.

query: What was the significance of Earl Spencer's eulogy during Princess Diana's funeral?

documents.text: Earl Spencer, Princess Diana's brother, her two sons Prince Harry and Prince William, and her former husband Prince Charles arrive into Westminster Abbey for her funeral, Sept. 6 1997. (Photo: Associated Press) It may be the most memorable funeral oration of all time: Heartbroken, loving and defiant, it was by turns elegiac and fiery, elegant and over-the-top. When Princess Diana's younger brother Charles, the ninth Earl Spencer, delivered his eulogy at her funeral in Westminster Abbey on Sept. 6, 1997, no one among the 1,900 sitting inside, nor the million listening outside in parks and on The Mall, expected what poured forth. Least of all the royal family sitting right by him. And when he finished, voice trembling with emotion, no one expected the surging applause that erupted outside and soon spread inside the abbey. It was just one more unforgettable moment when the world said goodbye to the Princess of Wales. Here is the text of Spencer's eulogy: "I stand before you today the representative of a family in grief, in a country in mourning before a world in shock. "We are all united not only in our desire to pay our respects to Diana but rather in our need to do so. For such was her extraordinary appeal that the tens of millions of people taking part in this service all over the world via television and radio who never actually met her, feel that they too lost someone close to them in the early hours of Sunday morning. It is a more remarkable tribute to Diana than I can ever hope to offer her today. No Need for Royal Title "Diana was the very essence of compassion, of duty, of style, of beauty. All over the world she was a symbol of selfless humanity. All over the world, a standard bearer for the rights of the truly downtrodden, a very British girl who transcended nationality. Someone with a natural nobility who was classless and who proved in the last year that she needed no royal title to continue to generate her particular brand of magic. "Today is our chance to say thank you for the way you brightened our lives, even though God granted you but half a life. We will all feel cheated always that you were taken from us so young and yet we must learn to be grateful that you came along at all. Only now that you are gone do we truly appreciate what we are now without and we want you to know that life without you is very, very difficult. "We have all despaired at our loss over the past week and only the strength of the message you gave us through your years of giving has afforded us the strength to move forward. Earl Spencer, Princess Diana's brother addresses the congregation in London's Westminster Abbey, Saturday Sept. 6 1997 during the funeral service for his sister, Diana, Princess of Wales. Earl Spencer, scathingly condemned the newspapers and the paparazzi which had followed Diana's every step and pledged himself to protect Diana's son's, Princes' William and Harry from such intrusion. Princess Diana was killed in a car crash in Paris on Aug. 31. (Photo: John Stillwell, pool photo) Great Gift of Intuition "There is a temptation to rush to canonize your memory, there is no need to do so. You stand tall enough as a human being of unique qualities not to need to be seen as a saint. Indeed to sanctify your memory would be to miss out on the very core of your being, your wonderfully mischievous sense of humor with a laugh that bent you double. "Your joy for life transmitted where ever you took your smile and the sparkle in those unforgettable eyes. Your boundless energy which you could barely contain. "But your greatest gift was your intuition and it was a gift you used wisely. This is what underpinned all your other wonderful attributes and if we look to analyze what it was about you that had such a wide appeal we find it in your instinctive feel for what was really important in all our lives. "Without your God-given sensitivity we would be immersed in greater ignorance at the anguish of AIDS and HIV sufferers, the plight of the homeless, the isolation of lepers, the random destruction of landmines. Prince William, right, and Earl Spencer at Westminster Abbey for funeral ceremony of Princess Diana, Sept. 6, 1997. (Photo: JOEL ROBINE, Associated Press) Innermost Feelings of Suffering "Diana explained to me once that it was her innermost feelings of suffering that made it possible for her to connect with her constituency of the rejected. "And here we come to another truth about her. For all the status, the glamour, the applause, Diana remained throughout a very insecure person at heart, almost childlike in her desire to do good for others so she could release herself from deep feelings of unworthiness of which her eating disorders were merely a symptom. "The world sensed this part of her character and cherished her for her vulnerability whilst admiring her for her honesty. "The last time I saw Diana was on July 1, her birthday in London, when typically she was not taking time to celebrate her special day with friends but was guest of honor at a special charity fundraising evening. She sparkled of course, but I would rather cherish the days I spent with her in March when she came to visit me and my children in our home in South Africa. I am proud of the fact apart from when she was on display meeting President Mandela we managed to contrive to stop the ever-present paparazzi from getting a single picture of her — that meant a lot to her. "These were days I will always treasure. It was as if we had been transported back to our childhood when we spent such an enormous amount of time together — the two youngest in the family. "Fundamentally she had not changed at all from the big sister who mothered me as a baby, fought with me at school and endured those long train journeys between our parents' homes with me at weekends. "It is a tribute to her level-headedness and strength that despite the most bizarre-like life imaginable after her childhood, she remained intact, true to herself. Charles, Earl Spencer, the brother of Princess Diana, arrives by car at St James's Palace Sept. 3, 1997. (Photo: JOHN STILLWELL, Associated Press) Diana's Goodness Threatened the Media "There is no doubt that she was looking for a new direction in her life at this time. She talked endlessly of getting away from England, mainly because of the treatment that she received at the hands of the newspapers. I don't think she ever understood why her genuinely good intentions were sneered at by the media, why there appeared to be a permanent quest on their behalf to bring her down. It is baffling. "My own and only explanation is that genuine goodness is threatening to those at the opposite end of the moral spectrum. It is a point to remember that of all the ironies about Diana, perhaps the greatest was this — a girl given the name of the ancient goddess of hunting was, in the end, the most hunted person of the modern age. "She would want us today to pledge ourselves to protecting her beloved boys William and Harry from a similar fate and I do this here Diana on your behalf. We will not allow them to suffer the anguish that used regularly to drive you to tearful despair. Blood Family Will Protect Sons "And beyond that, on behalf of your mother and sisters, I pledge that we, your blood family, will do all we can to continue the imaginative way in which you were steering these two exceptional young men so that their souls are not simply immersed by duty and tradition but can sing openly as you planned. "We fully respect the heritage into which they have both been born and will always respect and encourage them in their royal role but we, like you, recognize the need for them to experience as many different aspects of life as possible to arm them spiritually and emotionally for the years ahead. I know you would have expected nothing less from us. More Timeline of Princess Diana's life: Timeline: Princess Diana's life and the events that made her who she was "William and Harry, we all cared desperately for you today. We are all chewed up with the sadness at the loss of a woman who was not even our mother. How great your suffering is, we cannot even imagine. "I would like to end by thanking God for the small mercies he has shown us at this dreadful time. For taking Diana at her most beautiful and radiant and when she had joy in her private life. Above all we give thanks for the life of a woman I am so proud to be able to call my sister, the unique, the complex, the extraordinary and irreplaceable Diana whose beauty, both internal and external, will never be extinguished from our minds." Source: The BBC Read or Share this story: https://usat.ly/2wTSLTd

The Internet Archive is undertaking a significant effort to preserve the web's content by archiving pages as they are created, along with the pages they reference. This proactive approach ensures that even if linked pages are altered or removed from the live web, a record of their original state will remain accessible.

The Archive's hope is that these preserved versions will be used to replace broken links or serve as companion resources. This allows readers to see the content as it was originally intended by the authors, effectively addressing the pervasive issue of broken links across the internet. This initiative is part of the "No More 404" campaign, aiming to mend the web's fractured connections.

Twenty years after her untimely passing, the world continues to commemorate Princess Diana, affectionately known as “the People’s Princess,” and celebrate her profound and enduring legacy as a humanitarian. Diana was merely 36 years old when she tragically perished in a Paris automobile accident alongside Dodi Fayed. She left behind her two sons, Prince William and Prince Harry, who were 15 and 12, respectively, at the time of her death.

The princes are observing this significant anniversary privately. However, on Wednesday, Prince William, now 35 and a father of two, and Prince Harry, 32, undertook a poignant visit to the Kensington Palace memorial sunken garden. This cherished space has been meticulously transformed with Diana’s favorite white blooms, serving as a heartfelt tribute to her memory. They were accompanied by William's wife, Catherine, Princess of Wales, and representatives from several charities that Diana passionately championed, including the Great Ormond Street Hospital, the English National Ballet, the Leprosy Mission, Centrepoint, the Royal Marsden Hospital, and the National Aids Trust.

Following their visit to the garden, Princes William and Harry made an impromptu stop to interact with well-wishers and observe the numerous tributes and heartfelt messages left at the gates of Kensington Palace, Diana's former residence from 1981 until her death. Kensington Palace shared on Twitter, "The Duke and Prince Harry are grateful for the many flowers, letters, and messages they have received about their mother. They wanted to express their thanks to those who made the journey to Kensington Palace."

This year marks the first time publicly that William and Harry have spoken candidly about their mother and the profound impact of her sudden demise. In a documentary that aired in July, Prince Harry reflected, “There's not a day that William and I don't wish that she was... we don't wish that she was still around, and we wonder what kind of a mother she would be now. And what kind of a public role she would have, and what a difference she would be making.”

Prince William expressed his deep sadness that Diana would never have the opportunity to meet his wife, Catherine, or their children, Prince George and Princess Charlotte. He shared with *GQ* magazine in May, "I would like to have had her advice. I would love her to have met Catherine and to have seen the children grow up. It makes me sad that she won’t, that they will never know her."

During Diana's funeral at Westminster Abbey in September 1997, her close friend Elton John poignantly sang, "Your candle's burned out long before your legend ever will." Today, two decades later, Diana's distinctive style, captivating glamour, infectious smile, and extensive humanitarian work continue to resonate deeply with the public.

Diana's humanitarian efforts are widely considered her most significant and lasting legacy. As patron of over 100 charities, according to The Diana Princess of Wales Memorial Fund, she was a tireless advocate for those marginalized by society. She championed individuals living with HIV/AIDS and leprosy, bringing much-needed attention to their causes. Her groundbreaking actions, such as publicly embracing a 7-year-old boy with AIDS in Harlem, are credited with transforming public perception of the disease. Furthermore, her courageous walk through an Angolan minefield months before her death, wearing protective gear, drew global attention to the devastating impact of landmines, contributing to the eventual signing of the Ottawa Treaty by Britain and 120 other nations.

Prince Harry emphasized his mother's teachings on the importance of quiet philanthropy, stating at an awards ceremony in May, "One of the things our mother taught William and I was the value of doing good when no one is watching. She visited hospitals late at night to comfort patients. She spent hours writing letters to privately support the work of others. She achieved a lot by shining a spotlight, but she worked just as hard when the cameras were gone."

To honor their mother, William and Harry have recreated a semblance of Diana's study at Kensington Palace within the music room at Buckingham Palace, featuring some of her personal possessions. A central piece of this exhibit is the desk where Diana meticulously organized her charitable endeavors and correspondence. The princes have also commissioned a statue to commemorate Diana, which will be erected on the grounds of Kensington Palace.

In a BBC documentary aired this month, Prince Harry spoke about his ongoing efforts to fill the void left by his mother: "Now all I want to do is try and fill the holes that my mother has left, and that's what it's about for us, is trying to make a difference and in making a difference, making her proud. She was the Princess of Wales, and she stood for so many things, but deep down inside for us, she was a mother. And we will miss our mother, and I wonder every single day what it would be like having her around."

Kensington Palace has hosted a series of commemorative events throughout the year, including the creation of the memorial garden adorned with Diana's favorite white roses, tulips, hyacinths, and forget-me-nots. An exhibition titled "Diana: Her Fashion Story," which opened in February, offers a unique glimpse into Diana's evolving personal style through some of her most iconic outfits.

In the months leading up to the anniversary, numerous documentaries have explored Diana's final days and her profound impact during her nearly two decades in the public eye. Lord Charles Spencer, Diana's brother, remarked in a four-hour documentary on ABC this month, "One of the reasons I want to talk now is because I think that after 20 years someone shifts from becoming a contemporary person, to one of history. And Diana deserves a place in history." Spencer, who delivered a memorable eulogy at Diana's funeral, added, "This was a special person, and not just a beautiful one."

Ken Wharfe, who served as Diana's protection officer from 1986 to 1993, affirmed that Diana was precisely as the public perceived her. He told ABC News this month, "What I liked about her was that there was always there was only the one side. What you saw in public was actually how she behaved in private. And I think you know what the public got was the real Diana. This wasn't, you know, a person that dressed up for the occasion and spoke for that occasion."

One of the significant challenges for William and Harry is ensuring that Diana's memory remains vivid for William's children, Prince George and Princess Charlotte. On July 3rd, which would have been Diana's 56th birthday, the princes held a service of re-dedication at Diana's grave on the island at Althorp, the Spencer family estate. George and Charlotte attended this service alongside William, Catherine, and Harry.

Prince William shared in an ITV documentary how he actively keeps Diana's memory alive within their home. He explained, "I think constantly talking about Granny Diana, so we've got more photos up around the house now of her and we talk about her a bit and stuff. It's hard because obviously Catherine didn't know her, so she cannot really provide that level of detail. So I do regularly, putting George or Charlotte to bed, talk about her and just try and remind them that there are two grandmothers, there were two grandmothers in in their lives. It's important that they know who she was and that she existed."

Image copyright PA With her world renowned glamour and her willingness to get close to people through her charity work, Princess Diana fascinated the nation. Here people tell their stories of when they met the 'people's princess'. Once the ceremony was over, you could hear the roar of the crowd as Charles and Diana stepped out of the cathedral." Mrs Philcox recalls seeing the princess again at St Paul's at another event in 1982 marking the end of the Falklands war. That has always stuck in my mind." Mrs Conn says she felt the princess was "definitely a people person". "What struck me was her natural beauty. When she spoke to you she looked directly at you, not over your shoulder. You felt as though she was really very interested in what you did and what you had to say. She was lovely. Mrs Warnock's lasting memory is of a woman who was different from the image portrayed of her by the media at the time. "I remember excitedly phoning my parents afterwards. I was surprised at my own reaction. "I've said to many that when people talk about "the X factor", Diana was the only person I've met who had it." The death of Diana, Princess of Wales, on August 31, 1997, triggered a profound public outpouring of grief. In the early hours of that day, news broke of a car crash in Paris's Pont l'Alma tunnel. By 4:45 am, reports citing sources confirmed Diana's death.

The royal family, on their summer break at Balmoral Castle, issued a brief statement: "The Queen and Prince Philip are deeply shocked and distressed by this terrible news. Other members of the royal family are being informed of the Princess's death." This initial reaction, however, proved to be a significant misstep. It alienated the public, leading to a loss of respect that took nearly two decades to mend.

The world reacted with unprecedented grief, catching the royal family off guard. Politicians struggled to articulate the magnitude of the loss. Diana's brother, Earl Spencer, speaking from South Africa, suggested the press had played a role in her death. Prime Minister Tony Blair famously dubbed her the "People's Princess." Meanwhile, the royals remained at Balmoral, offering no further public comment beyond their initial statement. Their stance was that Diana, having divorced Prince Charles and relinquished her royal highness title, was no longer considered a senior member of the family, and thus the usual protocols for mourning a royal death did not apply.

The Queen's characteristic "keep calm and carry on" approach, a strategy that had served her well through numerous personal and national crises, proved ill-suited for this particular moment. The public perceived the royal family's silence as a sign of disrespect towards Diana. Even staunchly royalist newspapers, like the Daily Express, urged the royals to show they cared.

To the public, Diana was more than just a divorced royal; she was a princess and the mother of the future king. Many felt she had been mistreated by the royal family and Prince Charles. The subdued royal response to her death only exacerbated these feelings. Sensing the growing public resentment, politicians and courtiers urged the royals to take more visible action, leading to the planning of a ceremonial funeral.

On the eve of the funeral, the Queen delivered a televised address, a rare display of emotion for her. She spoke of the family's grief and their focus on supporting the princes. She praised Diana's unique qualities and addressed the nation as a "grandmother," a departure from her usual reserved demeanor.

However, the most resonant words of that period came not from the Queen, but from Earl Spencer during the funeral. He spoke of his family's sorrow and described Diana as a "symbol of selfless humanity" and a "standard bearer for the suffering." He poignantly highlighted the irony of a woman named after the Roman goddess of hunting becoming "the most hunted person of the modern age." He concluded by affirming that her "beauty, both internal and external, will never be extinguished in our minds."

The funeral arrangements borrowed from plans for the Queen Mother's eventual passing. Notably, Diana did not lie in state, a privilege typically reserved for monarchs and their consorts, and some prime ministers. While the Queen Mother had lain in state for three days in 2002, attracting over 200,000 visitors, Diana's public did not have this opportunity. Many believed Diana, as a future queen consort, deserved such an honor, and a lying in state, even for a day, might have helped to assuage public anger.

Furthermore, the princes were instructed by the palace to walk behind their mother's coffin. Earl Spencer objected, believing Diana would not have wanted this. He later revealed he was misled, told it was the princes' wish. Prince Harry has since echoed this sentiment, stating, "I don't think any child should be asked to do that, under any circumstances."

In the immediate aftermath of Diana's death, the royal family's initial reaction was a significant error. While the Queen's televised address and the funeral itself helped to slightly improve the royals' public standing, it took considerable time for the nation to fully forgive them.

Twenty years later, Diana's legacy continues through her sons. This year, a private ceremony at her family estate, Althorp, led by Earl Spencer and attended by the princes, commemorated her life. While the royal family has regained some popularity, it is the Queen, the Duke and Duchess of Cambridge, and Prince Harry who are widely admired. Prince Charles, in contrast, struggles to connect with the public, with significantly less media attention compared to William and Catherine. A recent poll indicated that only a third of Britons believe Prince Charles has benefited the monarchy.

Even other members of the royal family cannot take public affection for granted. Public opinion is fickle and can shift rapidly during future crises. Prince Charles and his inner circle likely hope that by the time he ascends the throne, the mistakes of the 1990s will be forgotten. However, Diana remains a powerful presence in the public imagination, and the royal family will likely continue to be viewed, and perhaps judged, through the lens of her life and death.

**Princess Diana: A Life of Royal Service and Global Impact**

Twenty years after her untimely death in a Paris car crash, the world still remembers Princess Diana, a figure whose life captivated millions and whose legacy continues to resonate. This timeline traces the pivotal moments of her journey, from her aristocratic roots to her profound influence on humanitarian causes and public perception of the monarchy.

Born Diana Frances Spencer on July 1, 1961, into a family with deep ties to the British aristocracy, her life took a dramatic turn when her engagement to the 32-year-old Prince Charles was announced on February 24, 1981. Their wedding on July 29, 1981, at London's St. Paul's Cathedral, was a global spectacle, watched by hundreds of millions and marking the beginning of her public royal life. Motherhood soon followed, with the birth of her first son, Prince William, on June 21, 1982, and her second son, Prince Harry, on September 15, 1984.

Diana's public life was not without its profound challenges. The publication of Andrew Morton's book in June 1992 brought to light her personal struggles, including battles with attempted suicide and an eating disorder. This was followed by the formal announcement of Charles and Diana's separation to Parliament by Prime Minister John Major on December 9, 1992. Her candid interview with the BBC on November 20, 1995, offered an unprecedented glimpse into her marital difficulties, personal affairs, and strained relationships within the royal family, leading to Buckingham Palace confirming the Queen's urging for a divorce on December 20, 1995. Their official divorce was finalized on August 28, 1996. Tragically, Princess Diana and her companion, Dodi Fayed, died in a high-speed car accident in Paris on August 31, 1997. Her funeral on September 6, 1997, saw thousands gather in London for a somber farewell to a beloved figure whose impact extended far beyond the confines of royalty.

The tragic events of August 31, 1997, in Paris's Place de l’Alma tunnel began with a high-speed Mercedes S280 crashing into the eastern entrance. The car, reportedly traveling over 70 mph, more than double the speed limit, became a stark symbol of the dangers of reckless driving and relentless paparazzi pursuit. Witness accounts described a cacophony of noise preceding the vehicle's entry into the 660-foot tunnel, as it was aggressively pursued by motorcycles and scooters.

The crash itself was devastating. Witnesses described hearing a violent explosion as the Mercedes collided with the tunnel's concrete divider, sending the car into a 360-degree spin. The vehicle's roof was crushed, and its airbags deployed. The chauffeur, Henri Paul, was killed instantly, his weight inadvertently activating the car's horn. A paparazzo immediately began documenting the scene, highlighting the intrusive nature of celebrity photography.

Parisian police arrived quickly, cordoning off the area and apprehending seven paparazzi implicated in the pursuit. A crowd that had gathered jeered as the photographers were led away, reflecting public anger over their perceived role in the tragedy. Inside the tunnel, the scene was grim. Officers confirmed the fatal injuries of Henri Paul and Dodi Fayed. Diana and her bodyguard, Trevor Rees-Jones, appeared to be clinging to life. Rescue teams worked to extricate them from the wreckage before they were transported to the hospital.

Despite extensive medical efforts, including a thoracotomy and cardiac massage, Diana succumbed to her injuries at 4 a.m. Paris time due to profound blood loss and systemic trauma. A thorough investigation by French authorities, led by examining magistrate Hervé Stephan, highlighted the perils of paparazzi pursuits, a practice that had long concerned Diana. The incident also underscored the critical pressures faced by emergency medical services in high-profile, chaotic situations.

The event sparked significant public debate on celebrity photography ethics and regulation, leading to legal ramifications and a re-evaluation of privacy laws. Experts in accident reconstruction and trauma medicine also reflected on the biomechanics of the crash and the challenges of emergency response, as documented in subsequent inquiries and reports.

He interrupted Miss Colombia, who waving to the crowd in her new crown and sash with flowers in hand. "OK folks, uh ..." Harvey began. "I have to apologize. The first runner-up is Colombia. Miss Universe 2015 is Philippines!" After a few awkward minutes, both Miss Colombia and Miss Philippines stood at the front of the stage. Another Miss Universe worker came out, took the crown off Miss Colombia's head, and placed it on the head of Miss Philippines. "Folks, let me just take control of this," Harvey then said. "This is exactly what's on the card," he said, holding it up for the cameras. "I will take responsibility for this. It was my mistake. It was on the card. Horrible mistake, but the right thing. I can show it to you right here," he said pointing. "The first runner-up is Colombia. Still a great night. Please don't hold this against the ladies. We feel so badly, but it's still a great night." It's truly brutal to watch. Here's a closer look at the ballot that caused Harvey to say the wrong name. You can see it says that the second runner-up is Miss USA, the first runner-up is Miss Colombia, and the winner is Miss Philippines, though the countries are all written in very small print. He quickly corrected himself to name the actual victor, Miss Philippines Pia Alonzo Wurtzbach. This live television gaffe became a global sensation, sparking widespread discussion and, predictably, numerous conspiracy theories.

Gutiérrez herself reflected on the event, stating, "Everything happens for a reason," and expressed deep gratitude to her supporters. Meanwhile, Steve Harvey issued an apology on Twitter, which was later deleted and notably contained spelling errors for "Colombia" and "Philippines."

Oh no! Steve Harvey crowns wrong woman Miss Universe CLOSE Talk about awkward. Miss Universe host Steve Harvey accidentally crowned the wrong winner during the Las Vegas pageant. VPC Just before the end of the year, Steve Harvey managed to have the worst television moment of 2015. As host of the Miss Universe pageant Sunday night in Las Vegas, Harvey announced Miss Colombia was the winner... but then he realized he had made a mistake, and she was actually the first runner-up. It was Miss Philippines who should have been crowned. Poor, Miss Colombia. She had her crown taken right off of her head. The de-crowning of Colombia (the actual runner up) and the crowning of Miss Philippines. #MissUniversepic.twitter.com/UM8SADSKk1 — Janet Mock (@janetmock) December 21, 2015 If that wasn't bad enough, a regretful Harvey went and angered the Internet even more by posting this tweet that has since been deleted (we screen-grabbed it): "I want to apologize emphatically to Miss Philippians and Miss Columbia. This was a terribly honest human mistake and I am so regretful." (Photo: Twitter/IAmSteveHarvey) You'll notice he misspelled both "Colombia" and "Philippines." Can we blame autocorrect? Let's blame autocorrect. Here's Harvey confirming his mistake: The correct winner, Miss Philippines, and first runner-up, Miss Colombia, are listed on his card. Maybe he confused "first runner-up" with "first"? (Photo: AP) However, Harvey says in the Miss Universe Snapchat that it was the teleprompter that misinformed him. On the Snapchat story, Steve Harvey says the Teleprompter said Miss Colombia won #MissUniverse2015pic.twitter.com/3dBclNY58I — Justin Taylor (@TheSmarmyBum) December 21, 2015 Watch the Miss Universe snap story... Harvey says "The TelePrompTer said Miss Universe- Colombia"... Someone in production is fired... — Miss Alabama (@MissAmericaAL) December 21, 2015 In other news, Harvey has rewritten his apology tweet, but didn't pass blame to the teleprompter: I'd like to apologize wholeheartedly to Miss Colombia & Miss Philippines for my huge mistake. I feel terrible. — Steve Harvey (@IAmSteveHarvey) December 21, 2015 CLOSE Miss Universe Host Steve Harvey explained that he made a mistake and didn't read his entire card when he announced the wrong pageant winner. He named Miss Colombia Miss Universe before realizing his error and announcing that Miss Philippines had won. AP Read or Share this story: http://usat.ly/1YuZkya

Donald Trump, the former owner of the Miss Universe pageant, has proposed a unique solution to the controversy surrounding the 2015 crowning: declare both contestants co-winners. Speaking with Matt Lauer on the "Today" show, Trump suggested, "What I would do is make them a co-winner." He elaborated that this approach would be a "very cool" way to resolve the situation stemming from host Steve Harvey's significant on-air error.

The pageant's host, Steve Harvey, mistakenly announced Miss Colombia Ariadna Gutierrez as the winner, only to later reveal that Miss Philippines Pia Alonzo Wurtzbach was the actual victor. This unprecedented gaffe led to Gutierrez being publicly stripped of her sash and tiara on live television. Miss Universe 2014, Paulina Vega of Colombia, had the difficult task of removing the crown from her countrywoman, a moment captured by photographers like John Locher of the Associated Press.

The situation was further complicated by Gutierrez's height, requiring her to bend in a form-fitting gown as Vega removed the crown. Trump described the incident as "devastating" for Gutierrez, noting the humiliation of being awarded the title for mere minutes before it was rescinded. He recommended a "beautiful ceremony" for the new owners, WME/IMG, to establish a co-winner, believing it would be beneficial for the pageant's brand.

Trump had sold the Miss Universe organization to WME/IMG in September, following NBCUniversal's decision to sever ties with him and withdraw from airing the Miss USA pageant after his controversial remarks about Mexican immigrants. After the broadcast, Trump took to social media, tweeting, "Very sad what happened last night at the Miss Universe Pageant. I sold it 6 months ago for a record price. This would never have happened!" This sentiment contrasted with an earlier tweet from the night of the pageant, where he expressed support for the final three contestants and the new ownership.

On the "Today" show, Trump adopted a more conciliatory tone, acknowledging that "things happen, it’s live television." He stated that the pageant coordinators had already scheduled a call with him to discuss the incident. Despite the host's error, Trump defended Steve Harvey, calling him a "terrific guy" and emphasizing that such mistakes can occur during live broadcasts.

query: What were the main reasons for Bill Simmons' apology regarding the article on Essay Anne Vanderbilt?

documents.text: Alyssa The 4 Most Important Points In Bill Simmons’ Apology For Publishing A Piece Outing A Trans Woman CREDIT: AP Images/Sang Tan Bill Simmons, the founder of Grantland, an ESPN-owned sports and entertainment site, issued a wide-ranging public apology on Monday for the site’s decision to publish a piece about the inventor of a golf putter who killed herself while the piece was being reported. Simmons’ piece answers many of the questions I and other critics have raised about the story, “Dr. V’s Magical Putter.” He acknowledged that the reporter, Caleb Hannan, should not have outed the subject of the piece, Essay Anne Vanderbilt, as transgender in a conversation with one of her investors. He admitted that Grantland had been careless in its use of gendered pronouns in referring to Vanderbilt, and in employing other language that implied that being transgender is strange, deceptive, or in keeping with fraud. And most strikingly and importantly, Simmons acknowledged that he and his staff had failed to supplement their own lack of understanding of transgender issues by bringing in outside editors, an omission that the site took a small step towards rectifying by publishing a thorough analysis of the piece by ESPN baseball reporter Christina Kahrl, who is herself transgender. For some of Grantland’s critics, Simmons’ focus on his own editorial processes, rather than on Vanderbilt’s death, is distasteful. And there are a number of issues he could have, and perhaps should have, touched on. I would have liked to see Simmons more directly address the separate issue of reporting on subjects who are mentally ill, or who have attempted suicide in the past, as Leonora LaPeter Anton did in this piece for the Tampa Bay Times. And I think questions remain about the choice of Vanderbilt, rather than a powerful CBS golf commentator who plugged her products, as the subject of the story, and about the extent to which the putter had actually gained a foothold in the competitive world of golf equipment. But Simmons’ self-accounting is a striking document that should raise the bar for editors who face similar criticism in the future. These are what seem to me as the most important points he makes in it: 1. Simmons recognizes that this was an organizational failure, and that responsibility doesn’t solely rely with Caleb Hannan: Simmons writes: Another reason we created Grantland: to find young writers we liked, bring them into the fold, make them better, maybe even see if we could become the place they remembered someday when someone asked them, “So what was your big break?” That matters to us. Just about every writer we have is under 40 years old. Many of them are under 30. I am our third-oldest writer, as crazy as that sounds. For us, 31-year-old Caleb Hannan had (and has) a chance to be one of those writers. That’s why it hurts so much that we failed him. I recognize that some readers have interpreted this section of the statement as prioritizing Hannan’s career over harm done to Vanderbilt. But in terms of organizational improvement, recognizing the role that editors play in preventing reporters from getting stories badly wrong is critically important. Ultimately, someone has to make the decision to run a story, and the way Simmons himself promoted the piece when it was published makes clear that he didn’t recognize the glaring issues with it as an examination of a transgender woman’s life, and as sports reporting. This doesn’t mean that Caleb Hannan is not to blame for the focus he chose, the way he reported out that interest, and the words he used to present the story. But one of the best reasons for large journalistic organizations to hire staff with a broad range of life experience and expertise, and to treat those perspectives as if they’re valuable and deserve deference, is so someone’s present to step in when a piece fails, to educate the writer in question, and to save subjects of pieces from journalistic malpractice, and publications from damaging themselves. I’ll get back to that in a moment. 2. It seems likely that the piece would not have run, absent Vanderbilt’s suicide: Simmons explains that: We first reached the “Is it worth it?” point with Caleb’s piece in September, after Caleb turned in a rollicking draft that included a number of twists and turns. The story had no ending because Dr. V wouldn’t talk to him anymore. We never seriously considered running his piece, at least in that version’s form. Our decision: Sorry, Caleb, you need to keep reporting this one. It’s not there. I would have really liked to see Simmons explain why a pitch that focused on a putter that doesn’t appear to have any real traction in professional golf was accepted in the first place. And I remain curious about why the piece focused on Vanderbilt, rather than on Gary McCord, the CBS commentator who is almost purely responsible for giving Vanderbilt’s putter public attention, and who confirmed many of her lies about her credentials and resume to Hannan. A more thorough interrogation of how the sports reporting in “Dr. V’s Magical Putter” went wrong would help clarify how the piece went off the rails in focusing on Vanderbilt’s gender identity. But at least Simmons acknowledges that the sports reporting wasn’t there. 3. Vanderbilt’s death is, essentially, the reason why piece ran. But it’s not clear that Grantland knew how to handle the sense that they had to publish: Simmons says: We had no plans to run the piece at that point, but we decided to wait a week or two before we officially decided what to do. When that period passed, Caleb decided to write another draft that incorporated everything that happened. A few more weeks passed, and after reading his latest draft after Thanksgiving, we seriously considered the possibility of running the piece. Here’s why we made that decision … For us, this had become a story about a writer falling into, for lack of a better phrase, a reporting abyss. The writer originally asked a simple question — So what’s up with this putter? — that evolved into something else entirely. His latest draft captured that journey as cleanly and crisply as possible. As editors, we read his final draft through the lens of everything we had already learned over those eight months, as well as a slew of additional information that ended up not making the final piece. When anyone criticizes the Dr. V feature for lacking empathy in the final few paragraphs, they’re right. Had we pushed Caleb to include a deeper perspective about his own feelings, and his own fears of culpability, that would have softened those criticisms. Then again, Caleb had spent the piece presenting himself as a curious reporter, nothing more. Had he shoehorned his own perspective/feelings/emotions into the ending, it could have been perceived as unnecessarily contrived. And that’s not a good outcome, either…we worried about NOT running the piece when Caleb’s reporting had become so intertwined with the last year of Dr. V’s life. Didn’t we have a responsibility to run it? I said to a number of people before Simmons issued his statement that I suspected the piece would have been scrapped entirely if Vanderbilt had not committed suicide. The sports and science reporting weren’t there. And I can see an argument for publishing a consideration of whether Hannan’s reporting led to Vanderbilt’s death. That, however, is not the piece that Grantland published. It seems Simmons recognizes that now. Though, even lack of familiarity with trans issues aside, I’m not sure how the Grantland editorial staff didn’t realize that the piece rang as tasteless and muddled. 4. No one on the Grantland staff is transgender, has experience with trans issues, or raised the possibility of running the story by anyone with any expertise or personal experience on the subject: This is maybe the most important thing Simmons acknowledges about himself and his team, that they failed in their knowledge and empathy, and in the drive to seek it: We read every incarnation of that piece through a certain lens — just like many readers did from Wednesday morning to Friday afternoon. Once a few people nudged us and said, Hey, read it this way instead, you transphobic dumbasses, that lens looked totally different. Suddenly, a line like “a chill ran down my spine” — which I had always interpreted as “Jesus, this story is getting stranger?” (Caleb’s intent, by the way) — now read like, “Ew, gross, she used to be a man?” Our lack of sophistication with transgender pronouns was so easily avoidable, it makes me want to punch through a wall. The lack of empathy in the last few paragraphs — our collective intent, and only because we believed that Caleb suddenly becoming introspective and emotional would have rung hollow — now made it appear as if we didn’t care about someone’s life. We made one massive mistake. I have thought about it for nearly three solid days, and I’ve run out of ways to kick myself about it. How did it never occur to any of us? How? How could we ALL blow it? That mistake: Someone familiar with the transgender community should have read Caleb’s final draft. This never occurred to us. Nobody ever brought it up. It’s hard to get starker than that. And it’s hard to consider better evidence of the value of having staff with a wide range of backgrounds, experiences, and perspectives–and of the willingness to go outside your own staff when they reach the limits of their wisdom. Doing this takes humility, and it takes curiosity, an acknowledgement that your own knowledge is not the sum of the world, and a voracious hunger to understand more of it. These are the basic qualities of good journalism. It’s remarkable that so many news organizations fail to apply them to considering the mix of their own staff and contributors. We can only hope that Grantland’s failures in that regard, and Simmons’ willingness to admit to them candidly and without reservation, serve as a warning to other organizations that might suffer from similar myopia.

## Acknowledging Our Errors: The Essay Anne Vanderbilt Feature

The question, "How could you guys run that?" began echoing through our Los Angeles office on Friday afternoon, just as the weekend was commencing. This persistent inquiry continued throughout Friday night, all day Saturday, and into Sunday, flooding our inboxes with dozens of outraged emails and dominating discussions on Twitter and Facebook. Critiques also proliferated across various blogs and message boards, an unrelenting onslaught. The precise moment of realization that we had profoundly erred remains elusive, but it undoubtedly occurred between Friday night and Saturday morning. By Sunday, ESPN issued an apology on our behalf, and I am extending that apology now. My deepest condolences go to Dr. V's friends and family for any pain our mistakes may have inflicted.

### The Genesis of the Feature

The complexity of our misstep lies in the details of the feature we published on Wednesday morning. This well-crafted piece, penned by Caleb Hannan, centered on an inventor named Essay Anne Vanderbilt, also known as "Dr. V." Caleb dedicated seven months to meticulously reporting this story. His initial fascination stemmed from an infomercial for a seemingly magical putter, which sparked his investigative curiosity and led to the pitch. The central questions were: could such a "magical" putter truly exist, and who was the enigmatic woman behind its creation? Caleb presented this idea to Rafe Bartholomew, our esteemed features editor and an original Grantlander. Rafe, in turn, reports to Dan Fierman, our editorial director, and myself, the editor-in-chief. Ultimately, the three of us collectively approved Caleb's feature. As a piece progresses towards publication, input is solicited from Sean Fennessey, our deputy editor, and Megan Creydt, our copy chief. This collaborative system ensures that everyone's perspective is considered. I delegate extensively, intervening only in critical decision-making processes. Disagreements regarding publication are exceptionally rare; there's usually an intuitive understanding of a piece's readiness. Key considerations include its overall effectiveness, quality, potential repercussions, the rigor of its reporting, its literary merit, and, most importantly, its inherent value.

### Our Editorial Philosophy and Growth

For 32 months, our editorial philosophy has deliberately eschewed the pursuit of page views or the sensationalization of individuals for mere traffic. Our aim is to distinguish ourselves through thoughtful and entertaining content, consistently surprising our audience and embracing calculated risks. This ethos was a foundational principle in the creation of Grantland. As the legendary John Wooden wisely stated, "If you're not making mistakes, you're not doing anything." Every error we've encountered has served as a valuable learning opportunity. Another core objective in establishing Grantland was to identify and nurture emerging writing talent, providing them with a platform to hone their skills and potentially achieve significant career milestones. Many of our writers are under 40, with a substantial number under 30. Even I, at 31, am considered one of our more senior writers. Caleb Hannan, at 31, possessed the potential to become one of those standout talents, which makes our failure to support him adequately all the more disheartening.

### Reporting and Ethical Considerations

I recall Rafe forwarding an early email exchange between Caleb and Dr. V, which may have been their very first correspondence. I was captivated by her distinctive and eccentric language, finding it unlike anything I had encountered before. She presented as an ideal subject for a unique feature exploring an unusual piece of sports equipment. The "Is it worth it?" juncture for Caleb's piece was first reached in September, following the submission of a dynamic draft filled with unexpected turns. The narrative lacked a definitive conclusion because Dr. V had ceased communication with Caleb. At that stage, we did not seriously contemplate publishing the piece in its existing form, instead instructing Caleb to continue his reporting. The subsequent development involved a final communication between Caleb and Dr. V in September, which included her threat and the "hate crime" accusation, both of which were subsequently included in the published article. It is crucial to clarify that Caleb had limited interactions with Dr. V, and at no point did he threaten to "out" her on Grantland. His sole objective was to report the story and verify discrepancies in her background. This is the fundamental role of a reporter: to gather facts and ask pertinent questions. Dr. V had been promoting a "magical" putter by fabricating credentials, and nearly every assertion she made to Caleb throughout his reporting process proved to be untrue. There was no harassment or badgering; the situation did not unfold in that manner.

Caleb's most significant error, in retrospect, was disclosing Dr. V's transgender status to one of her investors while she was still alive. I believe he did not fully grasp the ethical implications of this action, and frankly, neither did anyone at Grantland. This critical oversight only became apparent to me during a discussion with Christina Kahrl yesterday. This realization underscores our collective lack of understanding regarding the challenges faced by the transgender community, and more importantly, our failure to educate ourselves on these issues before seriously considering the publication of the piece. We learned of Dr. V's suicide in October, approximately four to five weeks after Caleb's final interaction with her. Caleb was understandably devastated. While we had no immediate plans to publish the story, we decided to postpone a final decision for a week or two. Following this period, Caleb revised the draft to incorporate all subsequent events. Several weeks later, after reviewing his latest draft post-Thanksgiving, we seriously contemplated its publication.

### The Decision to Publish

Our decision to proceed was influenced by several factors. For us, the narrative had evolved into a story about a writer navigating a reporting "abyss." The initial inquiry about the putter had transformed into something far more profound. Caleb's final draft effectively captured this journey with clarity and precision. As editors, we evaluated his submission through the lens of our extensive eight-month reporting process, alongside additional information that ultimately did not make the final cut. Critics who point to a perceived lack of empathy in the concluding paragraphs of the Dr. V feature are correct. Had we encouraged Caleb to incorporate a deeper reflection of his own feelings and his anxieties about culpability, it might have mitigated some of these criticisms. However, Caleb had consistently presented himself as a curious reporter, and injecting his personal emotions into the ending could have been perceived as contrived, which was also an undesirable outcome.

During our internal deliberations regarding publication, four primary concerns emerged. Firstly, we were uncertain about potential legal ramifications, prompting us to consult with multiple legal experts. Secondly, and most obviously, we were deeply concerned about publishing a story about an individual who had died during the reporting process. We questioned how the piece would be received externally, whether it would be perceived as too dark or exploitative, and if we would be held responsible for her death. Thirdly, we grappled with the ethical dilemma of *not* publishing the piece, given how intricately Caleb's reporting had become interwoven with the final year of Dr. V's life. Did we not have a responsibility to share this story? The fourth, and perhaps most self-evident, concern was our profound regret over Dr. V's fate and our inability to fully comprehend the reasons behind it. This lack of understanding might have been sufficient grounds to withdraw the piece entirely. In fact, we nearly did so on multiple occasions.

### Post-Publication Reckoning

Our lack of concern about posthumously "outing" Dr. V reflects our general ignorance on this subject. This is a point we should have addressed prior to publication. At the time, we operated under the assumption that one could not "out" a deceased individual, particularly if they were a public figure. Regardless of whether our stance was ultimately right or wrong, it is an indefensible mistake that we did not solicit input from any members of the transgender community. Even now, it remains difficult for me to accept that Dr. V's transgender identity was not a more central element of the story. Caleb was unable to uncover any information about her life prior to 2001 for a very specific reason. Had we omitted or obscured this reason, and it later emerged, the implications would have been significant. Before officially deciding to publish Caleb's piece, we subjected it to rigorous scrutiny, with approximately 13 to 15 individuals reviewing it. This included every senior editor except one, our two lead copy desk editors, our publisher, and even the editor-in-chief of ESPN.com. All of them were deeply impressed by the piece and unanimously recommended its publication. Ultimately, the final decision rested with me. Therefore, I urge anyone seeking to assign blame to direct their criticism towards me, rather than Caleb or any of my staff. This is my website, and any significant decision of this magnitude is my responsibility. My failure to ask the most crucial question before publication is solely my fault.

Upon its release on Wednesday morning, the piece was met with widespread acclaim. Readers were captivated, sharing and tweeting it extensively. A consistent stream of praise flowed in from respected writers and journalists. By Thursday, as positive feedback continued to pour in, we had already shifted our focus to other projects. The dramatic turn of events began on Friday afternoon, a full 56 hours after the article's publication. The narrative abruptly reversed, and by Friday night, prominent early supporters began retracting their initial commendations. Caleb started receiving death threats, and we faced intense backlash on social media. The subsequent events are now widely known. Like everyone involved, I spent the weekend oscillating between profound regret, self-recrimination, and contemplation of alternative actions. The answer lay within that 56-hour window between initial praise and subsequent condemnation.

We, like many readers from Wednesday to Friday, initially interpreted the piece through a specific lens. However, once individuals pointed out alternative interpretations, particularly regarding our insensitivity towards transgender issues,
