### Gaza Airstrike Claims Family Lives Amidst Escalating Conflict

An Israeli airstrike on a Gaza home on Sunday resulted in the deaths of at least 10 people, marking the deadliest day since Israel began its operation against Hamas militants the previous week. While Israel stated its target was a Hamas operative, at least nine members of a single family, including several children, perished in the strike. Heavy bombardment of the Gaza Strip continued into Monday morning, intensifying the ongoing conflict.

### Sunday's Fatalities and Operation Toll

**Deadliest Day:** On Sunday, at least 26 people were killed in Gaza by Israeli bombardments, according to reports. Gaza health officials confirmed that at least 14 of these casualties were women and children.

**Operation Pillar of Defence:** Since Israel launched Operation Pillar of Defence on Wednesday, the death toll in Gaza has reached 72, as reported by Gaza health officials.

**Israeli Casualties:** Three Israelis were killed on Thursday.

**Rocket Attacks:** Gaza militants continued to fire rockets into Israel, causing injuries in towns like Ashkelon and Ofakim. The Israel Defense Forces (IDF) reported that 76 missiles fired from Gaza hit Israel on Sunday, while an additional 38 were intercepted by the Iron Dome missile defense system.

### Devastation in Gaza City

BBC correspondent Paul Danahar described the scene north of Gaza City, where diggers worked to clear rubble from flattened buildings and rescuers frantically searched for survivors. Hamas identified the deceased as policeman Mohamed Dalou and eight members of his family, including children. At least one other individual also perished. Hamas's military wing vowed retribution for what it termed "the massacre of the Dalou family."

The casualties were transported to Shifa hospital, which shifted from organized calm to frantic chaos as medical staff treated injured children.

### Official Statements and Disputed Accounts

Israel's chief military spokesman, Yoav Mordechai, informed Israel's Channel 2 TV that the intended target was Yehiya Rabiah, the head of Hamas's rocket-launching unit. Mordechai acknowledged "civilian casualties." Israel's *Haaretz* newspaper suggested the strike may have inadvertently hit a neighboring house. According to BBC correspondents in Gaza, at least seven homes belonging to Hamas officials were targeted by Israeli strikes on Sunday.

Israeli government spokesman Mark Regev emphasized the military's efforts to conduct surgical strikes, contrasting them with what he described as "indiscriminate" militant rocket attacks.

### Life Under Threat of Rockets

Residents in Israeli towns are adjusting to the reality of being within rocket range. Israel's state radio reported a volley of 10 rockets targeting Ashdod, with three landing in a residential area and causing shock among seven individuals. A rocket from Gaza directly struck a residential building in Ashkelon, resulting in injuries and damage. Another rocket hit a car in Ofakim, causing injuries, according to the IDF.

Intensified Israeli attacks on Gaza resumed around 02:00 GMT. BBC correspondent Jon Donnison reported hearing numerous shells fired from Israeli warships. Two media buildings in Gaza City were struck, injuring eight Palestinian journalists, one of whom required a leg amputation. These buildings housed outlets including the Hamas television channel, al-Quds TV, as well as Sky News and ITN. The BBC had previously occupied offices in one of these structures.

### Humanitarian Crisis Deepens

The World Health Organization has reported that hospitals in Gaza are overwhelmed with casualties and facing shortages of medical supplies. Palestinian officials indicate that several individuals remain missing under rubble, bringing the total number of injured since Wednesday to 660. The Save the Children charity highlighted that families are running out of food and water, with many trapped in their homes and experiencing power outages of up to 18 hours daily.

### Ceasefire Efforts and International Reactions

Efforts to achieve a ceasefire are ongoing. Egyptian security officials reported that a senior Israeli official had arrived in Cairo for talks, though Israel has not commented on this.

US President Barack Obama stated on Sunday that Washington "fully support[s] Israel's right to defend itself." ## Dylan Lee's Plea for Peace Amidst Intense Family Conflict

The public fallout between Tommy Lee, his son Brandon, and ex-wife Pamela Anderson has cast a long shadow over their younger son, Dylan. The relentless media attention surrounding his family's deeply entrenched conflict has left Dylan heartbroken, prompting a heartfelt plea for an end to the public spectacle. He has unequivocally expressed his desire for all parties involved to "stand down" and cease the ongoing drama.

Dylan articulated his profound distress through a series of poignant Instagram stories. In one particularly emotional post, he shared a picture of himself listening to the LANY song "Walk Away." This song choice, accompanied by a stark broken heart emoji, served as a powerful visual representation of his emotional pain and his sincere yearning for the dispute to cease. The situation has evidently reached a critical juncture, leading him to believe that emotional distance and separation are the most viable paths toward healing and a return to normalcy.

### The Spark of the Public Dispute

The public airing of grievances commenced when Tommy Lee responded to an interview given by Pamela Anderson to Piers Morgan. During this interview, Anderson revisited the 1998 incident in which Tommy Lee assaulted her, an event that resulted in his arrest and a subsequent six-month jail sentence after he entered a no-contest plea. Tommy Lee's reaction to his ex-wife's comments appeared on Twitter on March 5th, igniting what many observers characterized as a series of "cheap shots." He posted, "Think she’d find something new to discuss instead of rehashing old s*** but I guess she has nothing else going on & needs attention. Signed, ‘The Abuser’ (who she texts everyday & asks for me back)." This public exchange of accusations seemingly prompted Brandon Lee to defend his mother, further escalating the family drama.

### Escalation and Divergent Narratives

The situation took a more intense turn on March 6th when Tommy Lee shared a photograph on Instagram depicting a "fat lip and bloody mouth," attributing the injuries to his son Brandon. In the accompanying caption, he conveyed his heartbreak, stating, "You can give your kids everything they could ever want in their entire lives and they can still turn against you. Good job Brandon! Great son." He later removed the image following considerable criticism from fans for publicizing a private family dispute. Despite retracting the photograph, Tommy Lee persisted in asserting on Twitter that his son had been the aggressor, thereby fueling the public narrative.

However, reports from TMZ presented a contrasting perspective, suggesting that Brandon acted in self-defense. According to their account, an allegedly intoxicated Tommy Lee lunged at his son, compelling Brandon to defend himself. This narrative directly contradicted Tommy Lee's own account of the events, which he shared on Twitter: "My fiancée and I were in bed when my son busted into the room and assaulted me. I asked him to leave the house and he knocked me unconscious. He ran away from the police. THAT’S the truth." The conflicting accounts from Tommy Lee and TMZ underscored the contentious nature of the incident and the inherent difficulty in establishing an objective truth.

### Law Enforcement's Involvement and Dylan's Anguish

The incident necessitated an intervention from law enforcement. Sheriff's deputies were dispatched to Tommy Lee's Calabasas residence on the evening of March 5th. The initial 911 call was reportedly placed by Tommy Lee's fiancée, Brittany Furlan. Deputies subsequently documented a report concerning a potential battery. The confluence of the dramatic 911 call and Tommy Lee's subsequent public accusations on social media undoubtedly contributed to Dylan's profound distress and his earnest plea for an cessation of the public conflict. The family's private struggles have regrettably transformed into a highly visible spectacle, leaving their younger son caught in the crossfire of the ensuing turmoil and emotional fallout.

### A Chronological Account of the Events

To provide clarity, here is a chronological overview of the reported events:

*   **March 5th:** Tommy Lee publicly addresses Pamela Anderson's interview on Twitter, leveling accusations and reigniting past controversies. This marked the initial public escalation of the family's discord.
*   **Later on March 5th:** Responding to a 911 call from his fiancée, Brittany Furlan, Sheriff's deputies visit Tommy Lee's Calabasas home. The call reported a possible battery, signaling a serious domestic disturbance within the household.
*   **March 6th:** Tommy Lee posts an image of his injuries on Instagram, accusing Brandon of assault. However, he later removes the post due to significant public backlash and criticism for airing private family matters.
*   **Following the incident:** Dylan expresses his deep sadness and desire for peace on Instagram. He utilizes a LANY song and a broken heart emoji to effectively communicate his feelings and his fervent wish for the conflict to end, highlighting the emotional toll on the younger generation.

**Brandon Lee's Intervention: A Father's Alcoholism and a Son's Defense**

The public narrative surrounding the physical altercation between Mötley Crüe drummer Tommy Lee and his son, Brandon Lee, has taken a significant turn. Brandon has chosen to address the incident, framing his actions as an intervention in his father's struggles with alcoholism. This account offers a starkly different perspective from Tommy Lee's initial claims that his son rendered him unconscious.

Tommy Lee, 55, alleged on Twitter that Brandon "busted into the room and assaulted me" while he and his fiancée, Brittany Furlan, were in bed. Lee further claimed that Brandon "knocked me unconscious" and then "ran away from the police." However, his ex-wife, Pamela Anderson, has vehemently challenged this version of events.

Anderson, who was married to Tommy Lee from 1995 to 1998 and shares sons Dylan (20) and Brandon with him, stated that Brandon's actions were a desperate attempt to intervene in his father's ongoing struggles with alcohol. She explained that Brandon acted in self-defense, punching his father after Tommy Lee allegedly behaved hurtfully towards "all of us who he has hurt." Anderson expressed profound concern for Tommy Lee, praying he "gets the help he needs" and describing his conduct as "desperate and humiliating." She noted that her sons had made "every attempt to help him in recent months," even living with their father in hopes of fostering positive change.

Brandon Lee corroborated his efforts to orchestrate an intervention in a statement to USA TODAY. He expressed a preference for his father's "hopeful sobriety and recovery to be a private family matter" but felt "forced to speak out" due to his father's public accusations. Brandon reiterated his affection for his father, stating, "I love my father and just want to see him sober, happy and healthy.”

Anderson also criticized Tommy Lee's fiancée, Brittany Furlan, suggesting she "keeps him drunk." She declared, "I will never talk to Tommy again before he is sober and in his right mind," adding, "It is impossible to reason with crazy." Despite the intense public scrutiny, Anderson clarified that Brandon does not wish for his father to face legal consequences, deeming any notion that Tommy Lee could blame his son or the family "delusional."

This complex situation highlights the profound challenges families face with addiction. Well-intentioned interventions can tragically devolve into public disputes. The divergent accounts underscore the intricate emotional landscape and the often desperate measures undertaken by family members striving to navigate a loved one's addiction, serving as a poignant reminder of alcoholism's devastating impact on familial relationships.

This is a critical moment, and I need to set the record straight. I have made no prior public statements to anyone about this situation, and I urge everyone to disregard any tabloid gossip. This is a matter of life and death, and despite the gravity, I feel a surreal sense of calm. I believe justice is at work, and I am not frantic or devastated as some reports suggest. Instead, I am hopeful.

This situation is not new to us. For the time being, I am staying in France. I have complete faith in Brandon and his dedicated team to navigate this unfortunate and deeply sad situation concerning his father. My sincere prayer is that Tommy receives the help he desperately needs. His recent actions are desperate, humiliating, and frankly, he is a disaster spinning out of control, behaving in a manner that is not fatherly. This pattern of behavior, however, is not new.

In recent months, my sons have made every conceivable effort to support him, even staying with him to ensure his well-being. Their presence was intended to convey love and hopefully encourage a change in his behavior. I had feared an escalation, but I also understood that this might be a necessary catalyst for healing old wounds. Any attempt by him to blame his son or us for his circumstances is delusional. We have all invested too much time and energy trying to protect and assist him, hoping he would eventually take better care of himself.

While I generally prefer not to comment on such matters, Tommy's recent "Trump-style" unthinking rampage across Twitter and Instagram has made it necessary. Ideally, this is a private matter between a father and son, perhaps leading to rehabilitation if the stars align. I will not engage with Tommy again until he is sober and of sound mind. Despite his attempts to contact me, I have blocked him. It is impossible to reason with someone in his current state. I am confident that the world can see the truth.

I stand firmly beside my son, who acted in self-defense and genuinely feared for his life. Few can comprehend the lifetime of disappointment this man has inflicted upon our family. He has consistently been the epicenter of sadness, drama, and confusion. From the moment his sons were born, he has harbored jealousy of their talent and beauty. He is unwell, embodying the very definition of a narcissist and sociopath. His fiancé actively enables his behavior by keeping him intoxicated, fulfilling his desire for someone to misbehave with. It is a terrible situation, and unfortunately, he has transformed a private matter into a very public spectacle. My intention has always been to set the record straight, to portray him not as a bad person, but as a human being who has made mistakes. He still struggles to confront the guilt of his abusive behavior and has never taken responsibility for his actions. My hope is that this experience will ultimately be his saving grace. I can only pray for him and, in turn, protect my sons, who fiercely protect me.

Brandon has specifically asked me to remain in France and not return home at this time. He is content knowing I am safe and loved where I am. He has invaluable support and is managing everything with remarkable composure. His heart is truly amazing. He still does not wish for his father to be incarcerated; his sole desire is for Tommy to achieve sobriety. Brandon was actively organizing an intervention, enlisting the help of friends and individuals Tommy respects and works with. The goal was to provide an opportunity for whatever remains of his life to be healthy and peaceful, and perhaps for him to become a positive influence in their lives one day, even in the lives of future grandchildren.

Brandon has risked everything to try and save his father. He is incredibly dedicated to his career, abstains from alcohol and drugs (fully aware of his genetic predispositions), and serves as an inspiration to many. He possesses tremendous talent that will undoubtedly captivate the world. However, he has put all of this on hold to address this family crisis. His family, his brother, and our entire family are his absolute priority. Believe me, he punched his father in the nose on behalf of all of us who have been hurt by his actions. Now, Tommy feels humiliated and is attempting to destroy his own son. This is the work of the devil, the devastating disease of alcoholism.

Tommy Lee's Son, Brandon, Says Dad Was Drunk, Punched Him in Self-Defense (UPDATE) Exclusive Details 4:35 PM PT -- The fight happened Monday night, and we've confirmed police responded to Tommy's Calabasas home around 10:45 PM. query: What reasons did Donald Trump provide for not participating in the Republican presidential debate on Fox News?

documents.text:
Donald Trump has announced he will not participate in the upcoming Republican presidential debate, citing unfair treatment by Fox News. His campaign manager, Corey Lewandowski, stated that Trump's campaign is instead planning a rally in Iowa for the Wounded Warriors organization during the debate's scheduled time.

Trump's decision stems from his ongoing criticism of Fox News host Megyn Kelly, whom he believes treated him unfairly during the first debate. He expressed to reporters that he "probably won't bother" with the upcoming debate. In response to Trump's complaints, Fox News issued a statement that Trump dismissed as poorly written. Megyn Kelly confirmed she would still co-moderate the debate, asserting that Trump does not control the media and that the debate would proceed with or without him. Fox News also stated that capitulating to a politician's demands regarding moderators violates journalistic standards and warned Lewandowski against further threats.

Instead of the debate, Trump plans to use the time to "raise some money for the Wounded Warriors" and "raise money for the vets." He explained that he was prepared to participate until he saw what he called "wise-guy press releases." His campaign further stated that Trump "knows a bad deal when he sees one," highlighting Fox News' financial success from debates while criticizing the country's leadership.

Other candidates reacted to Trump's withdrawal. Senator Ted Cruz suggested Trump was "afraid" to face questions and challenged him to a one-on-one debate. Ben Carson expressed skepticism, while Chris Christie noted Trump's absence would allow for more discussion time and questioned his willingness to engage when things aren't favorable. Jeb Bush shared a retweet suggesting Trump's inability to handle Kelly's questions would make him unable to handle Hillary Clinton.

Trump currently leads in Iowa polls ahead of the caucuses, and this debate is the final one before voting. He has previously considered skipping debates and has advocated for networks to donate debate advertising revenue to Wounded Warriors. Historically, top candidates have skipped debates, such as Ronald Reagan before the 1980 Iowa caucuses, who went on to win the presidency.

Donald Trump's decision to boycott the Fox News debate during the 2016 presidential campaign was a direct consequence of his ongoing dispute with co-moderator Megyn Kelly. This action was not spontaneous but rather the culmination of escalating tensions, particularly following their initial clash at a debate in August 2015.

Trump's campaign issued a statement explaining his absence from the scheduled Thursday debate. The statement framed the situation as an unacceptable arrangement, with Trump, drawing on his background as a businessman and author of "The Art of the Deal," emphasizing his ability to recognize and reject unfavorable terms. He noted that Fox News had achieved record viewership and significant profits from previous debates, a stark contrast to their typical audience numbers. The campaign asserted that Trump would not be manipulated by Roger Ailes and Fox News, whom he accused of deliberately provoking him.

The campaign further elaborated on Trump's perspective regarding his debate performance and overall strategy. Trump felt he had won all six prior debates, citing online polls from sources such as the Drudge Report, Slate, and Time Magazine as evidence. While he viewed himself more as a job creator than a traditional debater, Trump valued the debate format for its positive effect on his poll numbers and public image. As an alternative to the Fox News debate, Trump announced he would host a fundraising event in Iowa for Veterans and Wounded Warriors, positioning it as a response to the perceived inaction of current political leaders.

This strategic move, which captured considerable attention from political observers, was presented as a display of the "guts" and decisive leadership central to his "Make America Great Again" platform. Analysts from publications like The New York Times and The Washington Post discussed its potential effects on voter perception and the media environment. Experts, including political strategist David Axelrod, shared their analyses on CNN, examining the calculated nature of Trump's decision.

Donald Trump's decision to skip the final presidential debate before the Iowa caucuses, opting instead for a televised event challenging Fox News, has solidified his significant lead in the crucial leadoff state. This move exemplifies Trump's distinctive and often disruptive political style. His campaign framed it as a display of "guts" and the "kind of mentality our country needs in order to Make America Great Again." They also announced a separate event to benefit wounded veterans, aiming to connect with a key demographic.

The remaining Republican candidates faced a difficult choice after Trump's withdrawal. They could either confront the absent front-runner, risking amplifying his message, or ignore his absence. The latter option might leave voters questioning their engagement with Trump's bold action just days before the Iowa caucus. New Jersey Governor Chris Christie commented on Fox News, stating, "I think, you know, the media's been handled by him. And I think, you know, folks have allowed him to do things that no other candidate's ever been allowed to do in American presidential history." This reflected a growing frustration among rivals about Trump's perceived ability to control media attention.

Trump's campaign has been marked by attention-grabbing events, each designed to provoke a reaction and dominate headlines. Past controversies, like questioning John McCain's war hero status or proposing a ban on Muslims entering the U.S., were met with predictions of his political downfall. Despite these forecasts, Trump has maintained his commanding lead in polls. His enduring popularity stems from his ability to connect with Republican voters skeptical of the party establishment and mainstream news. Yvonne Galusha, a Trump supporter, explained at an Iowa rally, "I think in some ways, we've got to start pushing back. And I think he's just demonstrating that, pushing against the liberal media." Her words captured the sentiment that Trump represents defiance against perceived media bias.

The dispute with Fox News escalated when Trump demanded Megyn Kelly's removal as a debate moderator. The network's response, suggesting Iran and Russia would treat Trump unfairly if he became president, drew a defiant retort from Trump: "Let them have their debate and let's see how they do with the ratings." Rivals tried to portray his withdrawal as a sign of unsuitability for the presidency. Christie told Kelly, "If he doesn't want to be there, that's okay with me. But I'll tell you this: the American people should wonder if you're not willing to show up when everything isn't going your way and exactly the way you want to." However, this argument has not significantly impacted Trump's strong poll numbers.

Trump's supporters see his unconventional actions as proof of his core appeal: he operates by his own rules, even challenging party leadership and favored media. Trump himself stated, "With me, they're dealing with somebody that's a little bit different. They can't toy with me like they toy with everybody else." Republican officials, while outwardly concealing their apprehension about Trump's influence, privately acknowledged his right to withdraw. Steve Duprey, who oversees RNC debate initiatives, pragmatically noted, "Every candidate has the right to decide not to participate in a sanctioned debate. Time will tell whether this works to his benefit or hurts his campaign." Trump's campaign manager, Corey Lewandowski, confidently asserted on MSNBC, "Mr. Trump doesn't play games. ... He walks away from bad deals. At the end of the day, Mr. Trump is going to have the last laugh." This highlighted the campaign's belief in Trump's strategic success.

Fox News expressed uncertainty about how Iowan voters would react to Trump's withdrawal. "We're not sure how Iowans are going to feel about him walking away from them at the last minute," the network stated, acknowledging potential voter backlash. Marc McGee, a graphic artist, believed Trump erred, saying, "You should show up and go through the motions, you know. If I was mad at work, I don't just stay home, you know." However, McGee also called Trump's move "very ingenious," recognizing its calculated attempt to gain maximum attention. This dual perspective illustrates the complex reactions Trump's actions provoke.

*Associated Press writer Jill Colvin contributed to this report from Iowa City, Iowa.*
*Steve Peoples is a national political reporter for The Associated Press, covering the Republican candidates for president. Follow him on Twitter at: http://twitter.com/sppeoples*

Fox News has responded with a touch of humor to Donald Trump's recent social media activity, where he expressed reservations about participating in the upcoming GOP debate. Trump cited concerns about perceived unfairness from co-moderator Megyn Kelly, taking to Twitter and Instagram on Tuesday to poll his followers on his potential attendance.

### Fox News's Playful Response

In a lighthearted jab, Fox News humorously suggested to Mediaite that intelligence from a "clandestine source" indicated both Ayatollah Khamenei and Vladimir Putin intend to treat Donald Trump unfairly should he become president. This drew a parallel to Trump's apprehension regarding Megyn Kelly. Furthermore, a "shadowy informant" apparently revealed Trump's own secret strategy: to gauge his readiness for future events by substituting his Cabinet with his Twitter followers. This jest followed an earlier, more direct statement from a Fox News spokesperson on Monday. The spokesperson conveyed surprise at Trump's anxiety over Kelly's questions, emphasizing that as a presidential candidate, he would eventually need to accept that he cannot dictate which journalists cover him.

### Key Developments

*   **UPDATE — 4:57 p.m. ET:** In a direct reply to Mediaite's inquiry about potential shifts in the GOP debate roster, Fox News CEO Roger Ailes issued a definitive email statement. He firmly declared, "Megyn Kelly is an excellent journalist and the entire network stands behind her — she will absolutely be on the debate stage on Thursday night."
*   **UPDATE — 5:48 p.m. ET:** Donald Trump reacted to Fox News's statement via Twitter late Tuesday afternoon. He characterized it as a "pathetic attempt" to artificially inflate debate ratings, tweeting, "Pathetic attempt by @foxnews to try and build up ratings for the #GOPDebate. Without me they'd have no ratings!"

Donald Trump's decision to skip the final Fox News debate before the Iowa caucuses has ignited significant political debate. Trump cited his belief that moderator Megyn Kelly would be "really biased" and expressed distrust in Fox News's ability to host a fair event. Instead, he announced he would "host an event in Iowa to raise money for the Veterans and Wounded Warriors." This move prompts critical questions: Is this another calculated masterstroke from a candidate seemingly defying political gravity, an act of profound arrogance destined to be the undoing of his campaign, or a strategic negotiation tactic culminating in a last-minute agreement to debate? This analysis explores these three distinct possibilities, drawing on insights from political strategists and media analysts.

One possibility is that this is a calculated masterpiece. Donald Trump currently leads the Republican field, with his national and Iowa polling trends showing consistent upward momentum. From his perspective, the potential gains from participating in another debate appear minimal, while the risks are substantial. Trump likely views Megyn Kelly as having a personal vendetta, perhaps fueled by his own provocative behavior towards her. He fears she might engineer a moment designed to humiliate him and derail his campaign just days before the Iowa caucuses. By abstaining, Trump is poised to dominate media coverage surrounding the event, generating a significant media spectacle that overshadows his rivals in the crucial days leading up to Iowa. Furthermore, his alternative event, a fundraiser for wounded veterans, offers a positive public image. The actual debate, diminished by his absence, may appear lackluster and inconsequential. This decision also sends a clear message to future debate hosts: Trump is prepared to withdraw if his demands are not met. The most advantageous outcome for Trump would be Fox News capitulating. This could involve Megyn Kelly stepping aside, perhaps with another moderator taking her place for the network's benefit. In such a scenario, Trump would emerge as a brilliant negotiator, having compelled a powerful media institution to bend to his will.

Alternatively, this could be a monumental miscalculation. By conventional political standards, Trump's decision appears remarkably ill-advised. With the Iowa caucuses mere days away, many voters finalize their decisions late in the process. A significant portion of conservative voters rely on Fox News for their political information and would likely tune into a debate hosted by the network, a preeminent conservative media outlet. Trump is forfeiting a prime opportunity to sway these undecided voters, who could be influenced by a candidate who actively participates and performs well in the debate. Moreover, refusing to debate can be perceived negatively by voters. The established norm for major presidential candidates is to participate in debates, a practice even sitting presidents generally adhere to. This refusal could project an image of weakness and apprehension. While Trump can legitimately boast about his poll numbers, he has yet to secure any actual electoral victories. The debate itself, despite Trump's absence, is unlikely to suffer a drastic drop in viewership. Given its proximity to the Iowa caucuses and the unsettled nature of the Republican race, voters will still be keen to observe the proceedings. Crucially, by not being present, Trump grants his opponents a free platform to criticize him repeatedly without the possibility of immediate rebuttal. The question arises: why not simply attend and articulate his platform? He has navigated previous debates effectively.

A third possibility is that this is a strategic negotiation tactic. As articulated by former Obama administration official Dan Pfeiffer, a potential outcome involves Trump holding out until the eleventh hour, securing assurances of fair treatment from Fox News executives like Roger Ailes, and then making a dramatic entrance. During a rally in Clear Lake, Iowa, earlier this month, Trump critiqued the Obama administration's handling of the Iran nuclear deal negotiations, arguing that they failed to leverage the situation to secure the release of American prisoners held by Iran. He outlined his preferred approach: "You say, 'We want our prisoners back, gotta have 'em back before we start negotiations.' They’ll say no. And what we say is, 'Bye bye, we gotta go, enjoy yourself, we’re leaving, bye!' and wave goodbye, get up, leave." Trump suggested the administration should have walked away from the negotiation multiple times. He further proposed escalating sanctions, predicting that Iran would quickly agree to release the prisoners to resume negotiations. While the subsequent release of American prisoners days later casts some doubt on Trump's assertion of the Obama administration's incompetence, this anecdote highlights Trump's propensity for using walk-away tactics as a negotiation strategy. Is this the case here? According to Gabriel Sherman, a highly regarded reporter covering Fox News for *New York* magazine, Trump's team has indicated that negotiations with Fox News are ongoing, and that Trump is open to discussions with Rupert Murdoch. Sherman reported via Twitter: "Trump source says Trump isn't taking Ailes calls. Hannity just tried calling trump as an emissary. Trump only wants to deal with Murdoch now." "Per Trump source: Trump's position now is he is chairman of his company and he will only deal with Murdoch at a chairman-to-chairman level." The specifics of a potential resolution remain unclear. Trump's primary concern is his perception of Megyn Kelly's bias. However, it would represent a significant concession for Fox News to remove Kelly as moderator or restrict her questioning, potentially leading to embarrassment for the network. Nevertheless, the debate represents a substantial financial opportunity for Fox News, and the network would undoubtedly prefer Trump's participation. Conversely, the risk for Trump in skipping the final debate before Iowa is considerable. Therefore, an accommodation may well be reached. The situation warrants continued observation.

**VIDEO:** Understanding the Iowa Caucus Process

*Subscribe to Vox on YouTube for more insightful explainer videos.*

Trump’s campaign manager used slightly less absolute words on Wednesday about Mr. Trump’s plans to skip Thursday night’s Republican presidential debate, saying he didn’t “think” there was any way the candidate would change his mind about attending the event on Fox News. Corey Lewandowski, the campaign manager, spoke on MSNBC’s “Morning Joe” program. One of the hosts, Mika Brzezinski, pressed him on whether Mr. Trump might change his mind and join the debate in Iowa, the final one before the state’s caucuses on Monday. “Any chance that he will do this debate in any way, shape or form?” she asked. “Any way to turn this around?” “I just don’t think so,” Mr. Lewandowski said. “What you have with Mr. Trump, that you have a clear leader. A person who he says at best is not going to be toyed with. A person who understands when a bad deal is in front of him and is ready to walk away from a bad deal. Something that this country should be able to do.” He added, referring to the debate’s possible ratings: “And I just don’t see in any way where Fox is going to have a successful debate now, when you got the clear G.O.P. front-runner who has decided to walk away from this debate because he can’t be treated fairly at the debate stage. Maybe Fox was going to have a 20- or 25-million person debate. But without Mr. Trump’s participation, I think they may have a one- or two-million person debate.” A day earlier, Mr. Lewandowski said definitively that Mr. Trump would not be there. Mr. Trump and the network engaged in a high-stakes game of chicken, largely over a feud about Megyn Kelly moderating the debate. On Monday afternoon, the network released a statement mocking Mr. Trump in stark terms. That’s what the candidate said he was reacting to on Tuesday night.

FOX News is hosting a sanctioned debate in Des Moines, Iowa, on Thursday night. This event takes place just three days before the Iowa Caucus, where the first votes of the 2016 election will be cast.

Donald Trump has announced he will not participate in this debate with seven of his fellow presidential candidates. This decision is highly unusual. It remains to be seen how Iowans will react to his last-minute withdrawal.

However, it is evident that Trump's refusal stems from his ongoing conflict with Megyn Kelly. He has repeatedly attacked her since August and has demanded her removal from the debate stage for the past four days.

Allowing a politician to dictate debate moderators sets a dangerous precedent and violates journalistic integrity. This is further compounded by threats made against Megyn Kelly. Trump's campaign manager, Corey Lewandowski, contacted a FOX News executive on Saturday. He suggested Kelly had a difficult time after the previous debate and expressed concern she might experience similar distress again. Despite being warned against further threats, Lewandowski persisted.

FOX News will not yield to intimidation tactics against its employees. Trump remains invited to Thursday's debate and will be treated impartially, as he has been in his 132 previous appearances on FOX News and FOX Business. However, he cannot control who moderates the debate or the questions that will be asked.

query: What led to Liu Xiaobo's extended imprisonment and eventual diagnosis with liver cancer?

documents.text:
The passing of Liu Xiaobo, a Nobel Peace Prize laureate and a leading voice for democracy and human rights in China, has cast a shadow of global sorrow. Liu, who spent over a decade imprisoned for his activism, died at 61 from liver cancer.

Authorities in Shenyang confirmed his death, citing multiple organ failure. Medical staff reported his passing at 5:35 PM, with his wife, Liu Xia, and brothers present. His final words to his wife were, "Live on well." Doctors described his cancer as a rare, aggressive form that progressed rapidly.

News of Liu's death prompted widespread grief and tributes. The Norwegian Nobel Committee and governments in the United States, Germany, and France issued statements, urging Beijing to lift travel restrictions on his wife and family.

Shenyang authorities announced Liu's death shortly after 9 PM, with state news agency Xinhua reporting it later that evening. Xinhua noted the involvement of prominent Chinese doctors and international experts in his treatment. In Hong Kong, over 100 people gathered outside Beijing's liaison office, transforming a protest for Liu's release into a memorial vigil. Law Yuk-kai of the Hong Kong Human Rights Monitor observed that Liu's case demonstrated Beijing's firm grip despite international pressure, predicting a more challenging path to democracy for Hong Kong. Despite appeals from his family, friends, and the international community, Liu died before he could receive treatment in Germany with his wife. He is the second Nobel laureate to die in custody, following Carl von Ossietzky in 1938.

Liu was arrested in December 2008 for his role in drafting "Charter 08," a manifesto calling for political reform in China. He was sentenced to 11 years in prison in 2009 and awarded the Nobel Peace Prize the following year. The Shenyang Justice Bureau stated Liu was diagnosed with late-stage liver cancer during a routine check-up in late May, with news of his illness only becoming public at the end of June. Beijing maintained Liu received the best possible care, rejecting international calls for him to seek treatment abroad.

According to friends in contact with Liu's family, he fell into a coma early on Thursday but briefly regained consciousness before lapsing back into a coma. Exiled Tiananmen student leader Wang Dan described Liu as "a great man who left us, a light extinguished," calling his death "a naked political murder." Wang expressed hope that the world would "forever remember Xiaobo’s contribution to China’s progress" and pledged to continue the struggle for China's democratization.

A joint statement from ten of Liu's close friends hailed him as "one of the most important voices of the contemporary freedom and human rights movement in China" and a symbol of "the Chinese people’s relentless struggle for freedom." Bao Tong, a former aide to Zhao Ziyang, stated on Twitter that Liu defined patriotism, adding, "The state may have convicted him of subversion, but it must be pointed out that he was a great patriot."

Berit Reiss-Andersen, head of the Norwegian Nobel Committee, held the Chinese government "deeply responsible" for Liu's "premature death," expressing concern he was not transferred for adequate medical treatment. United Nations Secretary General Antonio Guterres conveyed his deep sadness. German Chancellor Angela Merkel mourned Liu as a "courageous fighter for human rights and freedom of expression." Berlin and Washington reiterated their calls for Chinese authorities to permit Liu's relatives to leave the country.

German Foreign Minister Sigmar Gabriel questioned whether China could "quickly, transparently and plausibly answer the question of whether [Liu Xiaobo’s] cancer could not have been identified much earlier." U.S. Secretary of State Rex Tillerson joined global mourners, urging the Chinese government to release Liu Xia from house arrest and allow her to depart China. Dissidents Wang Dan and Hu Jia echoed these calls, emphasizing the need to continue advocating for Liu Xia's freedom and the unfulfilled aspirations of "Charter 08" to "sustain the spirit of Liu Xiaobo."

Liu Xiaobo, China's most prominent political dissident, spent his final days battling liver cancer under intense surveillance. During this difficult period, he focused on what would become his last literary endeavor: a deeply personal tribute to his wife, Liu Xia. An artist and poet, Liu Xia endured lengthy house arrest throughout his 11-year imprisonment.

From his hospital bed in Shenyang, Liu Xiaobo penned handwritten fragments that offered a poignant glimpse into his profound affection for his wife. His words, such as "Love as intense as ice, love as remote as blackness," and his self-reflection, "My praise is perhaps an unforgivable poison," were intended for the preface of a collection of Liu Xia's photographs, tentatively titled "Accompanying Liu Xiaobo."

The existence of these intimate writings and the photo collection was revealed by a Chinese editor and close friend of the couple. This editor, who requested anonymity due to fear of reprisal, facilitated the sharing of this personal testament. Liu Xiaobo had reached out to the editor shortly before his passing, and subsequently, those close to him shared images of his hospital writings.

While this final tribute offers a tender look at Liu Xiaobo's personal life and his devotion to his wife, his enduring legacy is cemented by his unwavering commitment to dissent. Awarded the 2010 Nobel Peace Prize while imprisoned, his activism stemmed from his participation in a petition advocating for democratic reforms, which led to his arrest and sentencing in 2009.

China’s most famous dissident, Liu Xiaobo, has died in custody at age 61. The cause was liver cancer. His death, confirmed Thursday evening local time on July 13 by Chinese justice authorities (link in Chinese) in the northeastern city of Shenyang, comes more than two weeks after prison authorities announced he was being moved to a hospital for treatment, news that was followed by pleas from around the world to allow him to travel abroad for care. Thursday’s brief statement didn’t mention the Nobel Prize awarded to Liu for a life dedicated to the memory of the protesters killed in Beijing’s Tiananmen Square in 1989. In April 1989, Liu, a literary critic and comparative literature professor, was in New York City as a visiting scholar at Columbia University when he heard of the protests in Tiananmen Square. He hurried back to Beijing and spent weeks among the student protesters. Two days after the Communist Party’s deadly crackdown on June 4, Liu was detained and jailed. When he was freed in 1991, he resumed his public demands for greater freedoms in China over the next two decades. In 2008, along with other dissidents, he drafted Charter 08, a document that called on the Chinese state to fundamentally change its character and abandon one-party rule. Soon after he was tried for subversion. According to freedom-of-speech advocacy group PEN America, he was convicted in 2009 for writing seven sentences, a total of 224 Chinese characters. He was given an 11-year sentence. On June 26, 2017, after serving more than seven years of his sentence, the prison holding Liu announced that he was being transferred to a nearby hospital to be treated for late-stage liver cancer—the same disease that killed his father. Here is a timeline of the life of a man who has been deeply admired, who is being deeply mourned, and whose last days have become for many a symbol for many of the darkness that clouds the story of China’s economic rise. 1955 Liu Xiaobo is born on Dec. 28 in northeastern China’s Jilin province, the third of five brothers. 1966 The Cultural Revolution begins. Millions of urban youth head to the countryside to be “re-educated” by peasants, following leader Mao Zedong’s exhortations to “go up into the mountains and down to the countryside (上山下乡运动)” (paywall). Liu’s grandmother, whom he is very close to, is designated a “landlord” and sent away. Meanwhile, his father, a Communist Party member, is busy, and his older brothers go away to become Red Guards. Liu remembers this time of turmoil as one of unexpected freedom according to his biography, Steel Gate to Freedom: “My parents were off ‘revolutionizing,’ schools stopped, and I was for a time able to be rid of the constraints of an ‘education.'” 1977 Liu is admitted to Jilin University and studies Chinese literature. During his university life, he organizes The Innocent Hearts (赤子心), a poetry group with his friends. 1982 Liu starts a two-year master’s program in Chinese literature at Beijing Normal University. After receiving his master’s, Liu stays at Beijing Normal University to teach and marries Tao Li, with whom he has a son, Liu Tao. 1987 Liu begins his doctoral studies and publishes literary criticism in dozens of magazines. In 1987 he publishes his first book, Criticism of the Choice: Dialogues with Li Zehou, which critiques a prominent intellectual. It becomes a bestseller. 1988 Liu works abroad at universities in Oslo and Hawaii and at Columbia University as a visiting scholar. 1989 While Liu is still in America, Chinese students break into massive pro-democracy demonstrations in Tiananmen Square. He flies back to stand staunchly on the side of students. Liu, as well as others, including Zhou Duo and Hou Dejian (a Taiwan-born singer), play pivotal roles, including organizing a hunger strike. Thanks to Liu’s efforts to persuade students to leave the square, many of them escape death. However, because of his leadership role in the protest, Liu is detained, fired from his job as a teacher, and his books are banned. He later divorces his wife Tao in order to protect his family, according to the Guardian newspaper. His son, then about six years old, goes to live with his grandparents; Liu will see little of him for the rest of his life, according to his biography. 1992 Liu doesn’t stop writing. His book The Monologues of a Doomsday’s Survivor, about his memories and evaluation of the Tiananmen Square protest, is published in Taiwan. In the book, he points out what he sees as the misjudgments of the movement. He says that it delayed China’s democratization, and that students and intellectuals had become “conceited—just as if we had reverted to the time of the Cultural Revolution.” Will Burgess/Reuters Pro-democracy activist Liu Xiaobo makes a point during a conversation in March 1995. 1996 Liu is sentenced to three years behind bars for a letter criticizing party rule. While still in the labor camp, he marries his current wife, fellow poet Liu Xia. According to Ian Johnson, writing for the New York Review of Books: They had met in the 1980s but became close only after Tiananmen. In 1995, Liu Xiaobo was detained for writing an open letter calling for basic rights and then formally sentenced in 1996 to three years in prison. Liu Xia traveled regularly up to the labor camp and—in a phrase that is now legendary—told the guards that “I want to marry that enemy of the state!” Eventually she did, while he was still in prison; their wedding banquet was in the prison cafeteria. David Gray/Reuters Liu Xia, wife of Liu Xiaobo, was placed under house arrest in 2010. 1999 Liu Xiaobo is released in October. 2001 Liu Xiaobo helps establish the Independent Chinese PEN Center, a “non-political, non-profit organization of writers that fights for the protection of freedom of expression and publication,” and serves as its president from 2003 to 2007. During this time he writes the essay “The internet is God’s present to China,” about his belief that the internet is making China freer (republished later in the Times of London). 2008 Liu Xiaobo co-authors Charter 08 with other dissidents, a political manifesto. The document appeals for a fundamental political transformation including a new constitution, separation of powers, and guarantee of human rights. In December 2008, he is detained. 2009 He is put on trial. On Dec. 25, he is convicted of “inciting subversion of state power.” According to PEN America, his conviction is largely for writing seven sentences, including this one: “One-party monopolization of ruling privileges should be abolished.” 2010 In October, Liu wins the Nobel Peace Prize while in custody, “for his long and non-violent struggle for fundamental human rights in China.” His wife Liu Xia is placed under house arrest in Beijing the same day. At the December ceremony (video), the medal and diploma are placed on an empty chair since Liu is still in prison. Norwegian actress Liv Ullmann reads the statement Liu had written prior to his sentencing: “I have no enemies and no hatred.” Heiko Junge/Scanpix/Reuters The empty chair where 2010 Nobel Peace Prize winner, jailed Chinese dissident Liu Xiaobo, would have sat during the ceremony at Oslo City Hall on Dec. 10, 2010. 2011 Liu’s father dies of liver cancer; he is briefly paroled to attend the funeral, according to Steel Gate to Freedom. 2017 On June 26, the Liaoning Prison Administrative Bureau prison makes the announcement that Liu is being transferred from prison in Jinzhou to a nearby hospital in the final stages of liver cancer. His lawyer confirms the news, setting off an outpouring of grief and anger among people who know and respect him globally. As Liu’s condition deteriorates, concerns over his treatment grow. The couple seeks to go abroad for treatment, news reports say, but his doctors in China say he is too ill to travel. China invites German and US doctors to visit him. Liu Xia is with her husband in hospital in his final days.

### Commission to Document Crimes at Colonia Dignidad

Germany and Chile are establishing a joint commission to document the crimes committed at Colonia Dignidad, a secretive sect founded by a former Nazi in the Andes mountains. The Chilean foreign relations ministry announced the agreement, which was finalized on Wednesday.

#### A Center for Truth and Remembrance

This initiative will lead to the creation of a documentation center dedicated to the community's history. It will also establish a memorial to honor the victims who were tortured and killed within Colonia Dignidad's isolated compound.

#### The Legacy of Paul Schaefer

Colonia Dignidad was founded in 1961 by Paul Schaefer, a former medic in the German army during World War II. Under Schaefer's leadership, the community, which adhered to ultra-traditional values, operated as an isolated cult. Schaefer was responsible for the sexual abuse and torture of numerous young people.

#### A Site of Repression

During Chile's dictatorship from 1973 to 1990, Colonia Dignidad was also used as a site for detaining and torturing opponents of the regime.

#### Acknowledging Past Abuses

As Latin American nations have embraced democracy, truth commissions and public memorials documenting past human rights abuses have become increasingly common. In 2006, former members of Colonia Dignidad issued a public apology for 40 years of abuses, stating they had been brainwashed by Schaefer, whom many considered a divine figure.

#### Ongoing Controversy

Colonia Dignidad remains a source of controversy in Chile. The community has since been rebranded as a Bavarian-themed retreat for Santiago residents. However, victims argue this rebranding is disrespectful to those who suffered within its walls. Paul Schaefer died in 2010 while serving a 20-year sentence for sex abuse.

## China Defends Actions Following Democracy Advocate Liu Xiaobo's Death Amidst International Condemnation

Beijing has strongly rejected international criticism following the death of Nobel Peace Prize laureate and democracy advocate Liu Xiaobo, asserting the matter is an internal affair and labeling the 2010 Nobel award a "blasphemy." Liu, 61, died from multiple organ failure, becoming the first Nobel Peace Prize winner to pass away in custody since 1938.

China had previously denied international appeals to allow Liu to seek overseas medical treatment after his late-stage liver cancer diagnosis in May. Observers suggest this decision stemmed from fears Liu might use his final days to criticize the government's authoritarian rule. Geng Shuang, a spokesperson for China's Ministry of Foreign Affairs, dismissed international criticism as the work of "a few foreign officials" and stated China had lodged formal protests with "certain countries." He reiterated that "all-out efforts" were made to treat Liu with "humanity and in accordance with the law." However, human rights activists and friends of Liu dispute these claims, calling his death "ultimately a political murder."

Authorities reportedly pressured Liu's family for a swift cremation and a "simple farewell ceremony, under severe surveillance." Friends struggled to obtain funeral details, while state-run media reported the funeral would "respect the wishes of his family and local customs." Activist Ye Du was warned by security services against traveling due to a "national ban" on activists attending. The Norwegian Nobel Committee head, Berit Reiss-Andersen, was denied a visa to China.

Chinese internet censors actively removed content related to Liu, blocking search terms including his name and "I have no enemies." State media largely ignored his death, with one editorial portraying Liu as a pawn of Western forces rather than a victim of government policy.

Jerome Cohen, an expert in Chinese law, characterized Beijing's treatment of Liu and his wife, Liu Xia, as indicative of an "Orwellian China." Cohen predicted Beijing would suppress Liu's message of democratic change and expressed concern that Liu Xia would continue to be persecuted to prevent her from becoming a symbol of resistance. He stated, "One-party dictatorships can’t allow free speech. That is why they tried to keep her quiet." Cohen believes Beijing prevented Liu from traveling to avoid a public relations crisis, and friends urge Western governments to advocate for Liu Xia's freedom.

BEIJING — The death on Thursday of China’s most prominent political prisoner, Liu Xiaobo, set off a frenzied effort by government censors to block discussion of his legacy online. Candle emoticons and the phrase “R.I.P.” were banned on Weibo, a popular Chinese microblogging site. On many sites, searches of Mr. Liu’s name turned up zero results. Still, Mr. Liu’s admirers found creative ways around the controls, using code words, videos and photographs to show solidarity and to criticize the government’s treatment of China’s only Nobel Peace laureate. Here’s a look at the reaction: ‘Heaven Is Watching’

The Norwegian Nobel Committee has voiced profound concern over the situation of Liu Xia, the widow of the late Liu Xiaobo, China's most prominent dissident, who succumbed to liver cancer. The committee, in solidarity with the United Nations, has implored China to grant Liu Xia her freedom, as she has been held under house arrest without formal charges since 2010. Beijing has consistently rebuffed international condemnation regarding its refusal to permit Liu Xiaobo, who was serving an 11-year sentence for "subversion," to seek medical treatment abroad.

Berit Reiss-Andersen, the committee's leader, revealed that her visa application to attend a potential funeral for Liu Xiaobo was rejected by the Chinese consulate in Oslo, preventing her from offering condolences. She informed the BBC that while she had hoped to pay her respects to the dissident's family during this "tragic situation," Beijing had forfeited an opportunity for a "fantastic gesture" by denying her travel. The Nobel Committee, which bestowed the Peace Prize upon Liu Xiaobo in 2010, has stated that Beijing bears a "heavy responsibility" for his passing.

Amidst this unfolding controversy, the focus has increasingly turned to Liu Xia, with mounting anxieties surrounding her mental and emotional well-being. The Norwegian Nobel Committee issued a statement expressing its "deeply worried about Liu Xia's situation" and urged Chinese authorities to "lift all restrictions they have put upon her." The statement unequivocally declared, "If she wants to leave China, there is no justification for denying her the opportunity to do so." Key concerns and calls for action regarding Liu Xia include her reported suffering from depression due to years of house arrest and surveillance, a condition exacerbated by her isolation. Ms. Reiss-Andersen poignantly described Liu Xia as having been "contaminated by her husband's situation," highlighting her lack of political activism in contrast to her husband's lifelong struggle. International appeals for her freedom have been made by Germany, the UK, France, the United States, and Taiwan, all urging China to allow Liu Xia to depart the country if she so chooses. The UN High Commissioner for Human Rights, Zeid Ra'ad Al Hussein, has lent his endorsement to these calls, imploring China to "guarantee Liu Xia's freedom of movement." Furthermore, the human rights organization Amnesty International has initiated a petition for her release, asserting that "it's time the Chinese authorities stop cruelly punishing" the artist. The Chinese foreign ministry, however, maintains that Liu Xia will be treated in accordance with domestic law.

Liu Xiaobo, a distinguished university professor and unwavering human rights advocate, was officially designated a criminal by Chinese authorities and endured repeated imprisonments throughout his life. He is widely recognized for his courageous role in potentially saving lives during the 1989 Tiananmen Square student protests, where he and fellow activists played a crucial part in negotiating the safe departure of hundreds of demonstrators prior to the government's violent crackdown. His 11-year prison sentence, imposed in 2009, stemmed from his co-authorship of the Charter 08 manifesto, a document advocating for multi-party democracy. Liu Xiaobo was ultimately convicted of attempting to overthrow the state. The Chinese foreign ministry has defended its handling of Liu Xiaobo's case, characterizing it as an internal affair and criticizing any foreign interference. Despite being honored with the Nobel Peace Prize in 2010 for his "long and non-violent struggle for fundamental human rights in China," he was prevented from traveling to Norway to accept the award. In his final days, Liu Xiaobo was transferred from prison to a hospital in Shenyang due to liver cancer, under stringent security measures. Western nations repeatedly petitioned for permission for him to receive palliative care abroad, a request that Beijing consistently denied. While Chinese medical experts asserted he was too unwell to travel, Western physicians who examined him held a differing perspective. Liu Xiaobo passed away peacefully on Thursday afternoon, surrounded by his wife and relatives. His doctor, Teng Yue'e, reported his final words to his wife as: "Live on well." International reporting on Liu Xiaobo's death has been subject to extensive censorship within mainland China, with local media providing minimal coverage, primarily in English. The Communist Party's official newspaper, Global Times, described him in an English editorial as "a victim led astray" by the West. Online discussions in China concerning his demise have also faced censorship. Friends of Liu Xiaobo within China have reportedly been warned by authorities against organizing memorial events, with some activists, such as Germany-based Tienchi Martin-Liao, informing the BBC that "many have been detained already." In stark contrast, Chinese activists outside mainland China have openly mourned him, with hundreds participating in a vigil in Hong Kong.

The passing of Nobel Peace Prize laureate Liu Xiaobo has ignited a wave of global sorrow and strong condemnation, a stark contrast to the heavily censored and muted reaction within China. Liu, a distinguished writer and unwavering human rights advocate, died Thursday evening at the age of 61. He was in custody, having succumbed to liver cancer at a hospital in Shenyang. His funeral, held Saturday morning in the same city, was attended by "family members and close friends," according to a city government spokesperson. Liu's remains were cremated, and his ashes were scattered at sea in a private ceremony, reportedly to prevent his grave from becoming a focal point for dissent.

### Erasing a Symbol

"The authorities cannot afford to see Liu's grave become a landmark and symbol where people gather to pay tribute," explained Hu Jia, a prominent Chinese human rights activist. "They aim to erase all traces of him." Hu Jia added, "But three-quarters of the Earth's surface is covered by water. His grave is now everywhere."

During a government-organized press conference, Liu Xiaobo's eldest brother, who reportedly never supported his activism, conveyed the family's "profuse gratitude" to the state. He thanked the government for its "humanity" in providing medical treatment and arranging "perfect" funeral services. He departed the conference without taking questions. The Chinese government, which had long suppressed Liu's writings and even his name, continues to censor news of his death. Social media posts have been deleted, and online searches related to him are blocked. CNN's broadcasts in China are also blacked out when Liu's images or story are featured.

Domestic media, under the control of the Communist Party, largely ignored the news. The front page of the *People's Daily*, for instance, focused on President Xi Jinping's meeting with his Canadian counterpart. A few English-language outlets published brief reports, which emphasized Liu's "criminal" background and the efforts of Chinese doctors.

### Liu Xiaobo's Activism and Conviction

Liu Xiaobo was serving an 11-year prison sentence for "inciting subversion of state power." His conviction stemmed from co-authoring a document advocating for human rights and political reform in China. In 2010, while imprisoned, he was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize for his "long and non-violent struggle for fundamental human rights in China," a decision that angered Beijing.

The state-run *Global Times*, in a nationalistic editorial, characterized Liu as "a victim led astray by the West." It suggested his attempts to confront mainstream Chinese society, influenced by Western ideas, led to his "tragic life." The newspaper also posted a now-deleted message on Sina Weibo, mocking international reactions to his death.

### China's Response to International Condemnation

In response to global condolences and condemnations, the Chinese government issued a statement calling foreign officials' comments "improper." Foreign ministry spokesman Geng Shuang asserted that the handling of Liu's case is an internal affair and urged countries to respect China's judicial sovereignty. China conveyed its "strong dissatisfaction and firm opposition" to governments and international entities that commented on his death.

Despite China's warnings, even U.S. President Donald Trump expressed sorrow and referred to Liu as a "political prisoner." He also highlighted Liu Xia, Liu Xiaobo's wife, a poet and artist. Liu Xia has been under de facto house arrest since her husband's Nobel win, with her communication severely restricted. Her brother's imprisonment on what supporters claim are trumped-up charges has added to her distress.

### The Campaign for Liu Xia's Freedom

An international campaign is now focused on securing Liu Xia's freedom and her right to live abroad, with many believing these were her husband's dying wishes. "He loved her so much that he felt he owed it to her," said Liao Yiwu, a writer and friend of the Lius. "He didn't know about her condition and what had happened at home until not that long ago. That's why he agreed to go abroad with her."

Liu Xiaobo's doctors confirmed that Liu Xia was present at his deathbed. His last words urged her to "live a good life." Supporters fear the Chinese government's apprehension about Liu Xia becoming an international symbol for human rights. "Few knew Liu Xiaobo's thoughts in the last eight years, but she did, and they talked during his last days," Hu Jia noted. "I think the authorities are afraid of seeing even the simplest last words from him get out and spread."

Geng, the foreign ministry spokesman, repeatedly declined to comment on whether Liu Xia would be allowed to leave China, stating her case would be handled "in accordance with law." A Shenyang city government spokesman declared Liu Xia "free" but in need of privacy. Government photos and videos released after the funeral showed a grieving Liu Xia and purported evidence of her consent to the sea burial. Liu Xiaobo's eldest brother cited her "extremely weak condition" as the reason for her absence from the funeral press conference.

Amidst the censorship, some internet users have shared lines from Liu Xia's poems dedicated to her late husband. These include verses from "Wind": "Walls make you suffocate / you are the wind, and the wind / never tells me / when it comes and when it goes."

query: What modifications did Stephen Paddock make to his weapons to increase their lethality during the Las Vegas shooting?

documents.text: CLOSE Newly released body camera footage from the Las Vegas Metropolitan Police Department offers a firsthand glimpse of the massacre as it happened. USA TODAY Mourners pay tribute at a makeshift memorial on the Las Vegas Strip for the victims of a mass shooting in Las Vegas on Monday. (Photo: Eugene Garcia, EPA-EFE) LAS VEGAS — Investigators say that a retired accountant's shooting rampage was meticulously planned and included specially modified weapons and surveillance cameras aimed to spy on any police intruders who would try and stop his murderous deluge. At a late-night press conference Tuesday, authorities said Stephen Paddock made his attack even more deadly by adding more lethal components to his weapons. He had devices attached to 12 semiautomatic rifles that allowed them to mimic fully automatic gunfire. Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms Special Agent in Charge Jill Schneider said Paddock had nearly 50 guns — a combination of rifles, shotguns and pistols — in three locations. The gun attachment that mimics automatic gunfire is a little-known device called a “bump stock” that is not widely sold. The stocks have been around for less than a decade, and Schneider said officials determined they were legal. Paddock, who killed 58 people and eventually himself, also injured 527 others in what is the deadliest mass shooting in modern U.S. history. He also used surveillance cameras to monitor police approaches to his room at the Mandalay Bay high-rise — including a camera he positioned in the peephole of the door. “I anticipate he was looking for anybody coming to take him into custody,” Sheriff Joseph Lombardo said. During the Sunday night rampage, a hotel security guard who approached the room was shot through the door and wounded in the leg. “The fact that he had the type of weaponry and amount of weaponry in that room, it was preplanned extensively,” the sheriff said, “and I’m pretty sure he evaluated everything that he did and his actions, which is troublesome.” While Paddock's motive remains unclear, authorities were putting together a more complete picture of his work history and his past. The killer worked as a letter carrier for the U.S. Postal Service, an IRS agent and in an auditing department over a 10-year period. A spokeswoman for the Office of Personnel Management told The Associated Press on Tuesday that Stephen Paddock’s employment included about two years as a mail carrier from 1976 to 1978. After that, he worked as an agent for the Internal Revenue Service for six years until 1984. And then he worked a defense auditing job for about 18 months. He graduated from college in 1977 from Cal State Northridge and also worked for a defense contractor in the late 1980s. President Trump, as he left Washington for a trip to storm-ravaged Puerto Rico, briefly referenced Paddock, whose carnage Sunday night left 59 people dead and more than 500 injured. "He was a sick man, a demented man with a lot of problems, I guess, and we are looking into him very, very seriously," Trump said. "We are dealing with a very, very sick individual." The president also said the administration would be "talking about gun laws as time goes by," and he lauded the efforts of Las Vegas law enforcement. "How quickly the police department was able to get in (to Paddock's hotel room) was very much a miracle," he said. "They've done an amazing job." Read more: Why motive matters Gun dealer: 'My gut fell out' Country music star Jason Aldean was performing when Paddock opened fire from the window of his 32nd-floor hotel room overlooking the Route 91 Harvest music festival. Aldean, who was not injured, tweeted a statement Tuesday saying he has been overwhelmed with emotion since the attack. "Something has changed in this country and in this world lately that is scary to see," Aldean wrote. But he also added that it is "time to come together and stop the hate." Stories of heroism continued to emerge. One minute Marine veteran Taylor Winston was dancing the two-step at the concert, the next he was commandeering a pickup and shuttling the wounded to hospitals before ambulances arrived. Dawn-Marie Gray, a concertgoer who happened to have seven years experience as a paramedic, found herself providing CPR and tying tourniquets. When Nick Jones, 30, of Vancouver, Wash., heard about the shooting, he drove 20 hours to help. He offered to donate blood, but the need had been met. He offered to volunteer at hospitals, but they had plenty of help. Even the family assistance center was overwhelmed with volunteers. “I was worried that no one would do anything,” Jones said Tuesday. “I was happy to be proven wrong.” Assistant Sheriff Todd Fasulo pleaded for patience during the investigation, saying authorities were "hunting down" every clue to learn more about Paddock. Several minutes after the carnage began, officers who blasted into the room found Paddock's body. His brother Eric, who lives in Florida, says his multimillionaire brother was a big spender at casinos and often received free meals and rooms there. Eric Paddock also said he believed his brother owned a couple guns. Police found 23 guns, including semiautomatic rifles, in Paddock's room at the Mandalay Bay Resort and Casino. They found 19 more at his home in Mesquite, Nev., 80 miles northeast of here. And in yet another home owned by Paddock in Reno, authorities found five handguns, two shotguns, and a plethora of ammunition. CLOSE Las Vegas Metro Police have released footage from the body cameras worn by officers responding to the scene of the shooting Oct. 2, 2017. Las Vegas Metropolitan Police Department A federal law enforcement official, who was briefed on the matter but not authorized to comment publicly, said police also found two tripods positioned at the hotel windows in what appeared to be a fully equipped sniper’s nest. While the investigation intensified, authorities touted the city’s resilience in the aftermath of the tragedy. Fully stocked blood banks were soon turning away would-be donors. A GoFundMe page collected $3.3 million from almost 50,000 donors in little more than 24 hours. Overall, donations for the victims have exceeded $3.7 million, Commissioner Stephen F. Sisolak said Tuesday afternoon. Pieces of normalcy slowly returned to the iconic Strip. Club promoters hawked 2-for-1 deals, tourists took selfies and police cracked down would-be criminals. “Do you know how disrespectful it is to come out here and deal drugs the day after 50 people died one block away?’’ a bike cop snapped at a man in handcuffs Monday night. “Can’t you take one day off?’’ Derek Koerner, who identified himself as a licensed club promoter, said he was encouraged by the sizable crowds — and particularly grateful for the SUV full of men interested in his 2-for-1 deals on strip clubs. A $40 cover for a two-drink minimum gets the buyer into the second strip club for free. “There’s more people out here than I thought there’d be,’’ said Koerner, 48. “People seem to be upbeat and have a good energy. People seem to be moving on with their lives.’’ But less than a mile away, the mood was noticeably different. Dozens of people, some arriving with candles and flowers, returned to the scene of the horrific crime, marked by yellow police tape. Herman Gold and his ex-wife, Sara, stood near the concert grounds and explained how they escaped the hail of bullets and why they came back. “I just want closure,’’ Herman Gold said. “Why? What’s the reason that on that particular night, one individual would sacrifice everyone else for his insanity?’’ Joe Larsen, standing at the same corner and wearing a VIP wristband from the Aldean concert, expressed gratitude. Larsen, 49, who flew in from New Jersey for the concert, said that he watched the Twin Towers fall in 2001, lost his wife to cancer in 2015 and, on Sunday night, feared he was about to leave his son and daughter without a parent. He said he saw a young woman die from a bullet to the head before he called his father during the bedlam. “I told him, ‘I don’t know if going to make it. Tell my kids to live a good life’ ” Larsen recalled. As he and others shared their stories and wiped away tears, police officers closely monitored pedestrians, especially the wayward ones. “Sir, get out of the street,’’ one officer barked. “It’s the last time I’m telling you.’’ Read more: Shooter may have used trigger crank Machine guns have long been a draw in Las Vegas Bacon reported from McLean, Va. Contributing: Kevin Johnson and Ashley May, USA TODAY; Brett Kelman, The Desert Sun; Megan Cassidy, The Arizona Republic; Mike Benner, KGW-TV, Portland, Ore.
