**New Evidence Suggests Las Vegas Shooter May Have Targeted Life Is Beautiful Festival**

Investigators are exploring a new theory that Stephen Paddock, the perpetrator of the deadliest mass shooting in modern U.S. history, may have initially planned to attack the Life Is Beautiful Festival. This emerging idea, shared by a senior law enforcement official, suggests Paddock rented multiple condos with a clear view of the festival grounds. The popular music event, Life Is Beautiful, took place the weekend before the Route 91 Harvest Festival, where the tragic shooting ultimately occurred.

The investigation into this theory included examining The Ogden, a luxury high-rise known for its views of the Life Is Beautiful festival site. A representative for the property's owner stated they could not confirm or deny any information about Paddock's activities and directed inquiries to the police due to the ongoing nature of the investigation.

A law enforcement source indicated that Paddock's intentions might have shifted. This potential change in objective is believed to be why he checked into the Mandalay Bay Hotel on September 28, 2017. From a suite on an upper floor, Paddock opened fire on an estimated 20,000 attendees at the Route 91 Harvest Festival on October 1, 2017.

The subsequent investigation uncovered a large and carefully planned arsenal. Inside Paddock's Mandalay Bay suite, investigators found 23 firearms, including rifles modified to fire like automatic weapons. These weapons were brought into the hotel over time in 10 suitcases. Further searches at Paddock's home in Mesquite, Nevada, uncovered at least 19 more firearms, thousands of rounds of ammunition, and materials for making explosives, including ammonium nitrate. The detailed planning and the sheer quantity of weapons and materials suggest a premeditated act, with the Life Is Beautiful Festival now considered a possible earlier target.

Investigators trying to determine what sparked the deadliest mass shooting in modern U.S. history have found the massacre to be the work of a sophisticated planner with the means and desire to inflict unprecedented carnage. But on the second full day after Stephen Paddock smashed out the windows of a high-floor suite on the Las Vegas Strip and opened fire on a crowd of unsuspecting concertgoers, authorities still were trying to understand what drove him to such evil. Several new details emerged Tuesday about Paddock — a 64-year-old retired accountant — and how he worked methodically to thwart law enforcement as he killed scores of people and injured hundreds more. [ Two strangers bond over country music and beer. Then the gunshots started. ] As he fired round after round during an 11-minute stretch from a suite on the 32nd floor of the Mandalay Bay Resort and Casino, Paddock used video cameras to keep an eye out for police storming his hotel room, according to Clark County Sheriff Joe Lombardo. Paddock hid one camera in the peephole of his suite and two more in the hall, at least one of them disguised on a service cart, authorities said. At one point, he shot numerous rounds through the door, wounding a security guard. Paddock eventually put a gun in his own mouth and pulled the trigger as SWAT officers closed in. They found him with blood pooling behind his head and around the empty shell casings that littered the carpet, a handgun near his body. “It was preplanned, extensively, and I’m pretty sure that he evaluated everything that he did in his actions, which is troublesome,�? Lombardo said. The sheriff said investigators were “making progress�? on determining a motive, but complete answers remained elusive. There were precious few clues in Paddock’s background. An investigator works in the room at the Mandalay Bay Resort and Casino on Tuesday, in Las Vegas. A gunman opened fire from the room, killing at least 58 people and injuring hundreds. (AP Photo/Marcio Jose Sanchez) Neighbors in several states where Paddock owned homes in retirement communities describe him as surly, unfriendly and standoffish. Paddock was the son of a bank robber who was once on the FBI’s most-wanted list and who authorities described at the time as a “psychopath,�? but Paddock’s brother said their father was not involved in their lives when they were children. Relatives say the roots of Paddock’s loner lifestyle might have been planted on July 28, 1960. On that day, when Paddock was 7, a neighbor from across the street took him swimming. The neighbor told a local newspaper at the time that she knew authorities were coming for his father, and she wanted to spare the young boy from the trauma of seeing his father taken away. From that point on, Paddock’s family was never the same. Until carrying out the massacre Sunday night, Paddock had no criminal history himself. Despite repeated claims by the Islamic State to the contrary, he also had no ties to international terror groups, authorities said. He had done some government work during his career, as a letter carrier for the Postal Service, an agent for the Internal Revenue Service and an auditor for the federal government’s Defense Contract Audit Agency in the late 1970s and 1980s. He was divorced twice and recently had been dating a woman from the Philippines who has Australian citizenship. He was known to gamble routinely and extensively. Some public officials seemed to suggest Paddock’s mind was troubled, though there were no immediate indications that he had been diagnosed with a mental illness or was anything other than fully aware of what he was doing. “A normal person would not cause this type of harm to innocent people,�? said Rep. Ruben Kihuen (D-Nev.). “Clearly, there was something wrong with this man.�? People close to the investigation also said that in the weeks before the attack, Paddock transferred a large amount of money — close to $100,000 — to someone in the Philippines, possibly his girlfriend. The significance of that development was not immediately clear, though investigators said they were interested in probing Paddock’s finances and his avid interest in high-stakes gambling. The girlfriend, Marilou Danley, returned to the United States from the Philippines Tuesday night and was met at Los Angeles International Airport by FBI agents, according to wire reports. Investigators considered her a “person of interest,�? Lombardo said. Sen. Dean Heller (R-Nev.) said that police are eager to talk to her, as Paddock “doesn’t meet any profiles�? and the “best lead is through this girlfriend.�? “They don’t know a lot about who the girlfriend is and why she left the country a week prior to the shooting,�? said Heller, who has been briefed by authorities. “She is someone they need to have this discussion with to better understand the shooting and what his thought process was.�? Douglas and Sylvia Vick from Texas pray at a makeshift memorial on the Las Vegas Strip on Tuesday. (Mark Ralston/AFP/Getty Images) At his home in Orlando, Eric Paddock, Stephen Paddock’s brother, said he doubts Danley had any prior knowledge of the incident and speculated that Stephen might have been trying to quietly ensure her financial stability. Stephen Paddock loved and doted on his girlfriend, whom he had met when she was a hostess at a casino, Eric Paddock said. The couple often gambled side by side. “He manipulated her to be as far away from here and safe when he committed this,�? Eric Paddock said. “The people he loved he took care of, and as he was descending into hell he took care of her.�? The shooting — which left 59 people dead and injured more than 500 others — reignited the debate on Capitol Hill and across the country about whether lawmakers should impose new restrictions on firearms. Clark County Coroner John Fudenberg on Tuesday evening clarified that Paddock was among the 59 slain in the incident; previously, authorities had said he wasn’t. Undersheriff Kevin C. McMahill, speaking after Fudenberg, warned that the number of dead and injured could fluctuate as the investigation progresses. “The answer that the coroner provided you is the most recent, relevant number that we have,�? McMahill said. Caution tape lines the doors of the gunman’s suite on the 32nd floor of the Mandalay Bay hotel. (Bild Exclusive/Polaris.) Several Democrats used the massacre to push for new restrictions, and House Speaker Paul D. Ryan (R-Wis.) asserted Tuesday that Republican leaders have no plans to advance a bill, which passed a House committee last month, that would make it easier to buy gun silencers. Leaving the White House to visit hurricane-ravaged Puerto Rico on Tuesday morning, President Trump said that “we’ll be talking about gun laws as time goes by.�? Paddock brought 23 guns to his Mandalay Bay suite, which overlooked the concert grounds on the southern end of the Strip. Included in that cache was an AR-15-type rifle with a high-capacity magazine and 12 “bump stocks,�? a modification that would allow a gun to fire as if it were an automatic weapon. He also brought another AR-15-type rifle with a magnification scope commonly used for hunting and a bipod stand to help steady it, according to law enforcement officials and experts who reviewed images of the weapons posted online. “For this individual to take it upon himself to create this chaos and harm is unspeakable,�? Lombardo said. [ The lives lost in the Las Vegas massacre ] The guns appeared to be but a piece of the arsenal Paddock had acquired during a period of years, at least some of it legally obtained from major retailers such as Cabela’s, national retailer of guns and outdoor gear, and Guns & Guitars, a store in Mesquite, Nev., according to people familiar with the case. ATF Special Agent in Charge Jill A. Snyder said Tuesday Paddock had purchased shotguns, handguns and rifles in Nevada, Utah, California and Texas. At Paddock’s home in Mesquite, Nev., investigators recovered 19 more guns and an explosive, and in his car, they found ammonium nitrate, a chemical that can be used in bomb making. At another property in Reno, Nev., investigators recovered five more handguns, two shotguns and a “plethora�? of ammunition, Lombardo said Tuesday. Paddock probably secreted the weapons to his Mandalay Bay suite using more than 10 suitcases he brought with him. Lombardo said hotel housekeeping had been there since Paddock checked in on Sept. 28 and spotted nothing nefarious. Lombardo said investigators had scoured Paddock’s suite, his car and the homes in Nevada, and they were examining surveillance and body camera video evidence and other electronic items they had seized in their searches. Separately, Lombardo said the department had opened an investigation into the unauthorized release of images that show the crime scene. Another sheriff’s official confirmed the authenticity of those images. Police also continued documenting the crime scene and performing the grim task of identifying those slain, some of whom died blocks away from the concert as they tried to flee or seek help. As of late Tuesday afternoon, authorities had tentatively identified all of the victims. [ How the shooting unfolded ] Since the shooting, horrific stories have emerged of lives cut short, some taking bullets as they sought to protect their loved ones. Bobby and Jenny Parks, a married couple from California who had been high school sweethearts, were swaying to a love song when a bullet struck her. He held her as she died. After Cameron Robinson, a 27-year-old from Utah, was shot in the neck, his boyfriend, Robert Eardley, carried him to a vehicle. Robinson died before they made it to a hospital. Two people who met at the festival — Kody Robertson and Michelle Vo — were watching the final act together when a bullet struck her chest. Robertson threw his body over hers to protect her. Later, they were separated; he tracked her down to a hospital and was told late Monday morning that she was dead. Paddock’s weaponry would have allowed him to fire both rapidly and precisely at the crowd that was several hundred yards away and below him. The shots — fired from two windows of Mandalay Bay’s golden facade, which Paddock had smashed out — sent thousands of people scrambling for safety under Vegas’s neon glow. Police on Tuesday showed body camera footage of officers scrambling to determine where the shots came from as they yelled for people to leave the area. “They’re shooting right at us, guys,�? an officer can be heard saying. “Stay down! Stay down!�? Hospitals across the region continued to treat patients from the scene, many of them seriously injured. Sunrise Hospital and Medical Center says as of Tuesday night, 59 patients remain in the hospital and 31 of them are still in critical condition. University Medical Center said it had 64 patients from the attack, 12 of them critical. Roads inside the Mandalay Bay Resort are closed for investigation on Tuesday in Las Vegas. Davis, William Wan and Sari Horwitz in Washington contributed to this report.

## Diazepam Prescription Issued to Las Vegas Shooter Stephen Paddock Months Before Attack

A prescription for the anti-anxiety medication diazepam was issued to Stephen Paddock, the 64-year-old Mesquite resident who carried out the devastating Las Vegas shooting, by a doctor in Henderson. This prescription was filled on the same day in Reno, according to records obtained by the Review-Journal from the state's Prescription Monitoring Program. The anti-anxiety drug, known for its potential to induce aggressive behavior, was prescribed to Paddock in June, just months prior to the massacre where he killed at least 58 people and injured hundreds more with high-powered rifles.

### Details of the Diazepam Prescription

Records from the Nevada Prescription Monitoring Program, acquired on Tuesday, reveal that Paddock was prescribed 50 10-milligram tablets of diazepam by Henderson physician Dr. Steven Winkler on June 21. A representative from Dr. Winkler’s office declined to comment, neither confirming nor denying Paddock's status as a patient. Paddock purchased the medication, widely recognized by its brand name Valium, without insurance at a Walgreens in Reno on the very day it was prescribed. The prescribed dosage was one pill per day.

### Understanding Diazepam and Benzodiazepines

Diazepam belongs to a class of drugs known as benzodiazepines, which are classified as sedative-hypnotics. Scientific research indicates that these types of medications can, in some instances, trigger aggressive behavior. Furthermore, according to information from drugabuse.com, chronic use or misuse of sedatives like diazepam can also lead to the development of psychotic experiences.

### Expert Insights on Potential Behavioral Effects

Dr. Mel Pohl, chief medical officer at the Las Vegas Recovery Center, provided insight into the potential effects of such medications. "If somebody has an underlying aggression problem and you sedate them with that drug, they can become aggressive," Dr. Pohl explained. "It can disinhibit an underlying emotional state. It is much like what happens when you give alcohol to some people; they become aggressive instead of going to sleep." Speaking from the Netherlands, Dr. Pohl also noted that alcohol can significantly amplify the effects of diazepam.

### Research Linking Benzodiazepines to Aggression

A significant 2015 study published in the journal *World Psychiatry* examined 960 Finnish adults and teenagers who had been convicted of homicide. This research found that their likelihood of committing a killing was 45 percent higher during periods when they were taking benzodiazepines. Earlier, the *Australian and New Zealand Journal of Psychiatry* published a study titled, “Benzodiazepine Use and Aggressive Behavior.” The authors of this study concluded, “It appears that benzodiazepine use is moderately associated with subsequent aggressive behavior.”

### Expert Opinion on Premeditation vs. Impulsivity

Dr. Michael First, a clinical psychiatry professor at Columbia University and a recognized expert on benzodiazepines, commented on the Finnish study, stating that its findings were compelling. However, he also shared with the Review-Journal on Tuesday his belief that these drugs are more likely to fuel impulsive aggression rather than premeditated actions. Dr. First pointed to the Las Vegas attack as an example that appeared to be meticulously planned, referencing reports of Paddock bringing an extensive arsenal of weapons into the Mandalay Bay hotel and setting up surveillance cameras in and around his room prior to the shooting.

### The Significance of Prescription History

Dr. First emphasized the importance of understanding the underlying reasons behind Paddock's prescription. "That may have more to do with why he did what he did," he suggested. The Nevada state monitoring report also indicated that Dr. Winkler had prescribed 50 10-milligram tablets of diazepam to Paddock in 2016. That prescription was also filled on the same day it was written, this time at Evergreen Drugs in Henderson, with a prescribed dosage of two tablets per day.

### Broader Discussion on Psychiatric Medications and Violence

The potential link between psychiatric medications and mass shootings in the United States has been a subject of ongoing discussion within scientific and public spheres. However, researchers have yet to establish a definitive causal connection despite numerous studies. Critics of such discussions argue that unsubstantiated claims circulating in the media and on social media, which link psychiatric drugs to violence, can unfairly stigmatize individuals who benefit from these medications. This stigma, they contend, could potentially lead them to discontinue life-saving treatments. Actress Kirstie Alley recently sparked controversy with a tweet suggesting that guns and psychiatric drugs are common factors in recent U.S. mass shootings, although she provided no specific evidence regarding Paddock's drug use.

**Off-Duty Firefighters Emerge as Critical First Responders During Las Vegas Shooting**

During the horrific Las Vegas country music festival shooting, off-duty firefighters displayed remarkable bravery and decisive action. Witnessing the mass panic, Benjamin Kole immediately directed, "Not one (vehicle) leaves without a patient, a victim," emphasizing the urgent need for medical aid amidst the chaos. Jesse Gomez, despite his wife's pleas to evacuate the dangerous scene, prioritized the injured. He instructed his wife to take a wounded concertgoer to the hospital while he stayed to assist others. Anthony Robone, after ensuring his injured brother was safely evacuated, bravely returned to the massacre site.

These three men, who were attending the festival as civilians, were thrust into a critical role. Lacking their standard equipment, such as radios, they relied on their extensive training and inherent desire to help. As gunfire erupted for eleven harrowing minutes, these off-duty first responders, alongside untrained civilians, sprang into action. They provided immediate medical care, triaged the wounded, established makeshift triage areas, and coordinated the transport of numerous injured individuals to nearby medical facilities.

Kole, Gomez, and Robone were among dozens of off-duty firefighters present at the concert. Tragically, twelve other firefighters sustained gunshot wounds, with two requiring CPR at the time of their injury, according to the International Association of Fire Fighters union. Speaking at the Las Vegas Fire Fighters Union Hall less than 48 hours after the devastating event, these firefighters shared the profound emotional toll and difficult decisions they faced. Kole recounted the agonizing necessity of apologizing to critically wounded individuals, stating, "there was nothing we could do" and they had to move on to assist others.

Initially, many mistook the first sounds for celebratory fireworks during Jason Aldean's performance. However, Kole, accompanied by a friend who is a corrections officer, recognized the distinct pattern of gunfire during a second volley. His friend's quick action in knocking him to the ground saved him from immediate harm. Kole then realized his daughter, Rachel Kole, who worked for an ambulance company, was also present at the concert. Despite the ongoing gunfire, he ran to her location. Upon reuniting, he declared, "it was time to get to work," and together they began tending to the injured. Kole also noted the remarkable composure and assistance provided by regular citizens who remained calm and offered help.

Jesse Gomez attended the concert with his family. When the shooting began, he realized the sounds were not fireworks as the performers fled the stage. Amidst the unfolding panic, he saw a woman bleeding profusely from her head. He directed his family to safety while he and others aided the injured woman. He then contacted his wife, informing her of his decision to stay and help. When they met in the parking lot, his wife implored him not to return, but he felt compelled to do so. He described the terrifying sensation of the bullet impacts seemingly drawing nearer, as if a gun battle was unfolding mere feet away. He and another man then "went back to work," a testament to their dedication. Gomez expressed his astonishment at the number of people who chose to stay and offer assistance. He observed civilians utilizing tarps, trash cans, and fences to transport the wounded. He also described the heartbreaking task of informing grieving individuals that they had to move their deceased loved ones, as they were still in the line of fire.

Anthony Robone initially reassured his girlfriend that everything was fine. However, upon realizing the sounds were likely gunshots, he turned to discover his brother had been shot and was spitting blood. While a friend escorted his girlfriend to safety, Robone and another companion carried his brother towards what appeared to be medical tents. He described the relentless nature of the gunfire, stating, "The rounds seemed like they’d never stop." After applying makeshift bandages to his brother's wounds, Robone's professional training took over. He witnessed numerous fatalities, lamenting, "Unfortunately we couldn’t help at that time." Yet, he also observed an inspiring display of community resilience, with civilians applying pressure to wounds and fashioning tourniquets from their own clothing. He highlighted the profound selflessness of those with survivable injuries, recounting how some, despite being shot themselves, urged responders to prioritize those with more severe wounds, such as head or neck injuries.

The deadliest mass shooting in modern U.S. history unfolded in Las Vegas on Sunday, October 1, 2017, when a gunman opened fire on an outdoor music concert. The attack, which claimed the lives of 59 people and injured over 500, has prompted a shift in the investigation towards the gunman's girlfriend, Marilou Danley. Danley, who had been abroad for weeks, returned to the United States from the Philippines on Tuesday night, arriving in Los Angeles where FBI agents were present. Authorities, including Sheriff Joseph Lombardo, are hopeful that Danley can provide crucial insights into the motives behind Stephen Paddock's actions.

Meanwhile, interviews with Danley's family in Australia have shed some light on her potential involvement, or lack thereof. Her sisters, speaking anonymously to Australia's Channel 7 TV network, expressed their belief that Paddock may have sent Danley away to prevent her from interfering with his planned rampage. One sister tearfully stated that Danley, whom she described as a "good person," would have intervened had she been present. They also conveyed surprise at Danley's departure to the Philippines two weeks prior to the shooting.

Further adding to the shock and disbelief surrounding the event, a nephew of Stephen Paddock's girlfriend, Jordan Knights, revealed his astonishment at the gunman's actions. Knights, who spent time with Paddock and Danley in Las Vegas just months before the shooting, told Australia's Channel 9 that Paddock "didn't seem like he was the type of guy to do that." He described Paddock as someone who "just looked after my aunty and that was it," and admitted he had no prior knowledge of Paddock's interest in firearms. The 23-year-old nephew only gave Paddock another thought after he was identified as the perpetrator of the horrific attack.

As the investigation continues, President Donald Trump is scheduled to visit Las Vegas on Wednesday to meet with public officials, first responders, and some of the individuals injured in the attack. The return of Marilou Danley to the United States marks a significant development, with law enforcement officials, speaking on condition of anonymity to the Associated Press, emphasizing the importance of her testimony in understanding the circumstances leading up to this tragic event.

A federal law enforcement official earlier said two rifles were outfitted with scopes and set up on tripods in front of two big windows. Another official said that among the weapons were AR-15-style assault rifles. Both officials spoke on the condition of anonymity because they were not authorized to divulge details of the investigation. Sheriff Lombardo said that Mr. Paddock brought at least 10 suitcases into his hotel room over a period of time. The sheriff said that Mr. Paddock fired through his hotel room door at security guards, striking one in the leg. The guard is still alive, he said. SWAT officers went in after the guard was shot. In addition to the weapons at the hotel, the sheriff said the police retrieved 19 firearms, as well as explosives, several thousand rounds of ammunition and “electronic devices” from Mr. Paddock’s home in Mesquite, Nev. Paddock raised no suspicions at a store where he bought firearms. The investigation into Mr. Paddock’s gun arsenal led investigators to New Frontier Armory, a large North Las Vegas dealer, distributor and manufacturer of firearms. David M. Famiglietti, the company’s president, issued a statement on Tuesday confirming that Mr. Paddock purchased “several” rifles and shotguns from the business last spring — all at the same time. It’s not uncommon for customers to do this to save money on background check fees, Mr. Famiglietti said. “The firearms he purchased did not leave our store capable of what we’ve seen and heard in the video without modification,” he said. “They were not fully automatic firearms, nor were they modified in any way — legally or illegally — when they were purchased from us.” Mr. Famiglietti said that nothing about Mr. Paddock raised red flags with the store’s sales clerk. On the contrary, according to Mr. Famiglietti, Mr. Paddock seemed to be interested in competitive shooting, asking the clerk a lot of questions about three-gun shooting matches — an increasingly popular sport in which players use a rifle, a shotgun and a pistol. “He just seemed like a normal guy,” said Mr. Famiglietti, whose store has been the target of hate mail, threatening phone calls and fake reviews since the shooting. “We obviously did not sell him these firearms with the intent that he would use them to hurt anyone in any way.” On Monday, a Mesquite, Nev., gun dealer, Guns & Guitars, acknowledged having sold two rifles and a handgun to Mr. Paddock. Like the purchases at Frontier, those were cleared by state and federal background checks. The gunman was ‘not an avid gun guy at all,’ said one of his brothers. Mr. Paddock, 64, was described as a high-flying gambler who lived in a quiet retirement community and played golf. Officials said he had no significant criminal history and drew little attention to himself. Investigators are trying to piece together his financial history to search for clues that could help determine what set him off. Image Stephen Paddock in a photo provided by his brother Eric Paddock. Credit via Associated Press Mr. Paddock often bet heavily at the major casino hotels, and his girlfriend, Ms. Danley, 62, who was out of the country at the time of the shooting, had worked for some of those hotels. They lived in Mesquite, Nev., 80 miles northeast of Las Vegas, and he also owned a home in Reno. Ms. Danley boarded a flight from Manila to Los Angeles on Tuesday, according to Antonette Mangrobang, a spokeswoman for the Philippine Immigration Bureau. The authorities, who met Ms. Danley at the Los Angeles airport, have called her a “person of interest” in the shooting investigation, which does not necessarily mean that she is suspected of committing a crime. ‘I’ve never been that scared in my life,’ said one witness. The police estimated that when the shooting began, there were 22,000 people at the Harvest Festival, listening to Jason Aldean, the final act of the three-day event. Video of the shooting captured nine seconds of continuous, rapid fire, followed by 37 seconds of silence from the weapon and panicked screaming from the crowd. Gunfire then erupted again and again in extended bursts. Some concertgoers thought the noise was fireworks, but as it became clear what was happening, people fled, many of them unsure where the shots were coming from, or where they should go. “Everyone was running, you could see people getting shot,” said Gail Davis, one of the witnesses. “I’ve never been that scared in my life,” she added. “To have this happen, I can’t wrap my mind around it.” Video from the shooting showed Mr. Aldean running off the stage as the gunfire erupted.

Following the deadliest mass shooting in U.S. history, gun control advocates are intensifying calls to ban "bump stocks." These largely unregulated novelty devices may have been used by Las Vegas shooter Stephen Paddock to convert semi-automatic rifles into weapons mimicking fully automatic fire. The Associated Press, citing law enforcement sources, reported that at least two bump stocks were recovered from Paddock's hotel room, though their use in the attack remains unconfirmed.

Bump stocks attach to semi-automatic firearms and leverage the weapon's recoil to enable a shooter to fire rounds at a significantly accelerated rate compared to manually pulling the trigger. As Bump Fire Systems advertised on its website, users could achieve "simulated full-auto firing" legally for $99.99. However, gun experts like those cited by the National Shooting Sports Foundation have characterized bump stocks as "toys" or items for "gun geeks," rather than serious shooting tools where accuracy is paramount. Prior to the Las Vegas incident, these devices were prominently featured in online videos by gun enthusiasts, including those shared by channels like Full30.

Key figures in the gun control movement are urging immediate action:

*   **Senator Dianne Feinstein**, who previously authored the 1994 assault weapons ban and has sought its reinstatement, stated that she has long advocated for prohibiting bump stocks. "This is the least we should do in the wake of the deadliest mass shooting in US history. It should be our highest priority," Feinstein declared.
*   **Shannon Watts**, founder of the gun control organization Moms Demand Action, echoed this sentiment on Twitter, asserting that "Hardware that turns legal guns into automatic weapons should be prohibited," as it circumvents federal regulations on automatic weapons.
*   **David Chipman**, a former ATF agent and policy advisor for Americans for Responsible Solutions, a gun control advocacy group, noted that the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives (ATF) has consistently determined that bump stocks and similar devices are not strictly regulated under the National Firearms Act or the Gun Control Act. Chipman explained that bump stocks facilitate rapid fire by "throwing the trigger against your finger as opposed to your finger pulling on the trigger," describing the device as a "masterful creation of a technical workaround creating a loophole to circumvent the intention of the law."

One individual, a refinery operator from Wilmington, Delaware, who requested anonymity, shared his experience. He purchased a Bump Fire Systems device approximately two years prior for around $100. He sought it as the "closest thing that I could have that would simulate a machine gun," particularly in Delaware, where machine gun ownership is prohibited. He noted that purchasing the bump stock did not require a background check, stating, "It’s just like ammo: you order them online, they ship them to your house." While he found the device enjoyable as a "range toy," he acknowledged its difficulty to master and its inaccuracy, emphasizing that "This thing requires practice." He also suggested that many gun stores refrain from selling bump stocks, potentially to avoid attracting "irresponsible gun owners" who might misuse them after viewing online videos. Similarly, many shooting ranges, such as those affiliated with the National Shooting Sports Foundation, prohibit their use.

Another individual, who identified himself as Rich, found the novelty of the bump stock eventually wore off. He concluded it was "definitely not reliable as a self-defense method or anything else." He anticipated that the devices would face intense scrutiny and calls for bans, which he personally finds regrettable. "I don’t want to see anything banned because of the actions of one person," he stated, adding, "That just doesn’t jive with my principles of freedom."

query: What is the primary goal of the Internet Archive's initiative to crawl and archive web pages?

documents.text: These crawls are part of an effort to archive pages as they are created and archive the pages that they refer to. That way, as the pages that are referenced are changed or taken from the web, a link to the version that was live when the page was written will be preserved.Then the Internet Archive hopes that references to these archived pages will be put in place of a link that would be otherwise be broken, or a companion link to allow people to see what was originally intended by a page's authors.The goal is to fix all broken links on the web . Crawls of supported "No More 404" sites.

HBO's *Game of Thrones* season seven premiere, episode one, delivered significant plot developments, including Daenerys Targaryen's strategic arrival in Westeros and the escalating threat of the White Walkers. However, the episode's narrative momentum was largely overshadowed by a pre-announced celebrity cameo. The most widely discussed moment was not the intricate political maneuvering or the impending war, but the appearance of pop superstar Ed Sheeran. This focus on a musician, rather than the show's complex storylines, highlights a recurring pattern within *Game of Thrones*: a perceived tendency to feature artists whose appeal stems more from mass-market popularity than dramatic depth.

While the episode offered compelling moments, such as Arya Stark's infiltration of the Riverlands, these were largely predictable outcomes of established plotlines. The true talking point, known since March, was Sheeran's brief role. His scene, where he encountered Arya while she was alone, served as a brief respite from the episode's darker themes. He shared wine and spoke of the futility of war and the importance of kindness, offering Arya a moment of reflection after her brutal experiences.

Sheeran's cameo, while not particularly memorable in itself, reinforces a trend observed throughout *Game of Thrones*' run. The series has a history of enlisting musicians whose appeal lies more in their mainstream popularity than their dramatic talent. This pattern suggests a deliberate choice by the show's creators, including executive producers David Benioff and D.B. Weiss, to integrate these artists into the Westerosi landscape.

Key musical cameos in *Game of Thrones* include:

*   **Season Three:** Will Champion of Coldplay.
*   **Season Four:** Gary Lightbody of Snow Patrol and the band Sigur Rós.
*   **Later Seasons:** Robson and Jerome were notably cast in the role of Bronn.

Sheeran's appearance, while potentially a "dud" in terms of narrative impact, undeniably placed him within this established lineage of musical cameos. Critics, such as The Ringer's Joanna Robinson, have noted this trend, underscoring the ongoing debate about the show's casting choices and their effect on the immersive quality of the narrative. The author concludes on a somewhat positive note, suggesting that at least the cameo was not as potentially jarring as an appearance by Justin Bieber might have been. This perspective highlights the delicate balance the show attempts to strike between its epic fantasy world and the inclusion of contemporary cultural figures.

Season 7, Episode 1, ‘Dragonstone’ If you have designs on a dynasty, the first thing you have to do is get yourself a sweet map. That was one lesson from the Season 7 premiere of “Game of Thrones,” as the story’s two most powerful queens plotted on opposite sides of Blackwater Bay. But there were others: Shared leadership is best shared out of public view. Be decent to people, lest you later find their skeletons in your closet. The pursuit of knowledge can be a filthy business. And if your cantankerous uncle unexpectedly invites you to a festive party, with lots of toasting and wine, maybe let him take the first sip. [Want exclusive “Game of Thrones” explainers as well as the internet’s best articles on this week’s episode? Sign up for our Season 7 newsletter here.] Sunday’s episode, largely picking up where the Season 6 finale left off, sketched out the contours of the clashes to come, suggesting some possible paths of convergence and identifying free agents who will fill in the gaps.

## "Dragonstone": A Surprisingly Humorous Start to Game of Thrones Season 7

While *Game of Thrones* is renowned for its often grim and intensely dramatic narratives, the Season 7 premiere, titled "Dragonstone," delivered an unexpected and welcome dose of humor. This lighter tone, a noticeable departure from the show's customary bleakness, was significantly shaped by the writing of David Benioff and Dan Weiss. These seasoned showrunners, also credited with penning the witty "Kissed By Fire" episode in Season 3, appear to have strategically employed humor in this installment. The absence of immediate, devastating character deaths or extreme violence in "Dragonstone" may have provided the creative team with the space to inject moments of levity before the inevitable descent into the season's overarching conflicts.

### Dark Humor and the Echoes of Vengeance

The episode immediately set a darkly comedic tone with a particularly memorable scene. Arya Stark, masterfully disguised as the deceased Walder Frey, orchestrated the poisoning of his remaining loyal followers. This calculated act of revenge was a direct and chilling response to their complicity in the Red Wedding, a brutal massacre that claimed the lives of Arya's mother, brother, and numerous other allies. While many viewers and critics discussed the cameo appearance of musician Ed Sheeran, the humor derived from that particular moment was largely novelty-based. The episode's more substantial comedic strength, however, stemmed from the sharp and often cutting interactions between established characters like Cersei Lannister and Jaime Lannister, and the introduction of the flamboyant new character, Euron Greyjoy.

### Euron Greyjoy's Grand and Boastful Entrance

Euron Greyjoy, the self-proclaimed "Crow's Eye," made a truly memorable entrance, his flamboyant style and audacious demeanor immediately drawing comparisons to theatrical villains like Captain Hook. His primary objective in this initial appearance seemed to be impressing the formidable Cersei Lannister. He attempted to achieve this through a series of taunts directed at Jaime. "Ever since I was a little boy, I wanted to grow up and marry the most beautiful woman in the world, so here I am with a thousand ships and two good hands," Euron boasted, ostentatiously displaying his intact fingers to a clearly annoyed Jaime. When Cersei, ever the pragmatist, questioned Euron's trustworthiness, pointedly mentioning his fratricide, his disturbingly cheerful reply, "You should try it, it feels wonderful," elicited a priceless, almost imperious reaction from Jaime, highlighting the complex dynamics at play.

### Unpleasant Duties and Silent Witticisms

Beyond the sharp, often venomous dialogue, the episode found unexpected humor in an extended montage dedicated to Samwell Tarly's arduous and decidedly unpleasant chores at the Citadel. The focus on Sam meticulously shoveling excrement, a task both visually and olfactorily revolting, was rendered surprisingly effective through the escalating rhythm of the cuts and the almost musical soundscape of the liquids. This gross-out humor, while certainly not for everyone, demonstrated the show's willingness to push boundaries even in its comedic moments. Furthermore, the show's renowned skill in character development was evident in subtle moments that elicited laughter without a single spoken word. The ongoing, unspoken dynamic between Tormund Giantsbane and Brienne of Tarth, where Tormund's persistent, albeit endearing, advances were met with Brienne's weary, stoic resignation, provided a subtle yet highly effective comedic beat that resonated with long-time fans.

### Sansa Stark's Assertive Wit and Growing Command

However, arguably the standout comedic performance of "Dragonstone" belonged to Sansa Stark. Her sharp, dismissive, and increasingly assertive responses to Petyr "Littlefinger" Baelish showcased a significant evolution in her character. When Littlefinger, ever the manipulator, inquired, "What do you want that you do not have?" Sansa coolly and decisively replied, "At the moment, peace and quiet." Her ability to effortlessly shut down his attempts to re-engage her in his machinations, by stating, "No need to seize the last word, Lord Baelish, I'll assume it was something clever," demonstrated a remarkable command of wit and a newfound confidence. This display of sass and self-possession was a welcome addition to the episode, suggesting that *Game of Thrones* could indeed benefit from more such moments of sharp, character-driven humor. The positive reception and extensive fan discussions following the episode's premiere certainly supported this notion, with many praising Sansa's burgeoning strength and wit as a highlight. The episode, as analyzed by critics from publications like *The Hollywood Reporter* and *Vulture*, effectively balanced its darker themes with these lighter, character-focused moments, setting a promising tone for the season ahead.

## Game of Thrones Season 7 Premiere Hit by Widespread HBO Streaming Issues

The highly anticipated East Coast premiere of *Game of Thrones* season seven was marred by significant technical difficulties that disrupted HBO's digital platforms. Many viewers eager to watch the unfolding drama in Westeros faced substantial problems accessing the premiere episode, with widespread reports of outages on HBO's main digital services.

### HBO.com Blackout

The most significant issue affected HBO.com, the network's primary website, which experienced a complete blackout. This prevented a large portion of the audience from streaming the premiere as the threat of the White Walkers loomed. Users trying to access HBO.com encountered error messages, indicating the site was down. While the website was eventually restored, this happened only in the latter half of the episode's broadcast, leaving many fans unable to watch for a crucial period.

### HBO Now Struggles

The technical problems extended to HBO's streaming service, HBO Now. Numerous viewers reported that HBO Now was unavailable during the initial and most critical part of the premiere. This meant that for a considerable number of global viewers, the new episode, released simultaneously across time zones, was frustratingly inaccessible. User reports detailed constant loading screens and slow buffering, making it difficult to enjoy the premiere.

### Social Media Reaction and Regional Differences

Understandably, frustrated fans took to social media, especially Twitter, to express their disappointment and share their experiences. Many posted screenshots of their screens, lamenting their inability to stream the episode. However, some users on Twitter indicated they were successfully watching via HBO Now, suggesting that while the problem was widespread for many, it wasn't a universal outage.

### HBO's Official Statement

In response to the widespread reports, an HBO spokesperson provided a statement to *The Hollywood Reporter*. She explained that HBO Go was experiencing outages specifically in Latin America on Sunday evening and that the technical team in that region was working to resolve the issues. Crucially, she added that HBO had not observed any widespread outages affecting either HBO Go or HBO Now within the United States.

### Context: Heightened Anticipation and Streaming Services

The premiere of *Game of Thrones* season seven had been met with particularly high anticipation, partly due to a delayed production schedule that pushed back the show's return by several months. HBO Now, launched in April 2015, offers a standalone streaming option for viewers without traditional cable subscriptions, costing $15 per month. HBO Go, on the other hand, is a complimentary benefit for existing cable subscribers who authenticate through their service providers.

### A Recurring Pattern of Technical Issues

This incident is not the first time HBO's streaming services have faltered during a major event, highlighting a recurring pattern of challenges with their digital infrastructure. Past incidents include:

*   **2016:** HBO Now experienced significant outages during the broadcast of the pivotal "Battle of the Bastards" episode from *Game of Thrones*' sixth season.
*   **2014:** HBO Go crashed during the finale of the first season of *True Detective*.
*   **2014:** HBO Go also faced widespread problems during the premiere of *Game of Thrones* season four.

Notably, HBO Go did not appear to suffer the same widespread outages as HBO Now on Sunday evening, suggesting potential differences in the technical vulnerabilities affecting each service during this particular event.

### Season Seven Overview

The seventh season of *Game of Thrones* features the return of beloved characters like Jon Snow (Kit Harington) and Daenerys Targaryen (Emilia Clarke). This season consists of a condensed seven episodes, a departure from previous seasons, and will lead into the series' eighth and final season.

*Update: July 16, 6:54 p.m. This report has been updated to reflect that HBO.com resumed service and to include an official response from HBO regarding the technical difficulties experienced during the Game of Thrones season seven premiere.*

The season seven premiere of HBO's *Game of Thrones*, titled 'Dragonstone,' immediately ignited a fervent discussion among fans due to a scene that drew striking parallels to the beloved *Harry Potter* series. The pivotal moment occurred when Samwell Tarly, masterfully portrayed by John Bradley, sought permission to access the "restricted area of the library" at the Citadel, aiming to advance his scholarly pursuits. However, his request was met with a firm denial from Archmaester Ebrose, a character making his *Game of Thrones* debut and brought to life by the esteemed actor Jim Broadbent.

This specific narrative beat resonated deeply with *Harry Potter* aficionados, who swiftly recognized the clear echo of Hogwarts' own "Restricted Section." Within the *Harry Potter* canon, this hallowed and often forbidden area housed arcane texts detailing dark magic. It was famously within these shelves that Harry Potter delved into the complex research surrounding Horcruxes, the potent artifacts Lord Voldemort employed to fragment and conceal pieces of his very soul.

Further cementing this perceived connection, fans recalled a remarkably similar scenario from *Harry Potter and the Half-Blood Prince*. In that installment, a young Tom Riddle, who would later become the formidable Lord Voldemort, also attempted to gain entry to "the Restricted Section of the library," this time petitioning Professor Slughorn. The uncanny link is amplified by the fact that both Archmaester Ebrose and Professor Slughorn are portrayed by the same versatile and celebrated actor, Jim Broadbent, creating a delightful meta-narrative for viewers.

This perceived deliberate homage to the *Harry Potter* universe sparked a significant online fervor, with social media platforms becoming a vibrant hub for fan reactions and theories. The delight was palpable, as evidenced by a widely shared tweet that captured the sentiment: "Sam literally just became Harry Potter... talking to Slughorn and trying to get into the restricted section of the library. #gameofthrones." Another user offered a humorous observation on Broadbent's recurring role, noting, "Poor Jim Broadbent, everywhere he is, people are asking him about the restricted section in the library #GameOfThrones meets #HarryPotter." The visual resonance was also a prominent talking point, with one fan exclaiming, "Sam going into the restricted area at the Citadel is straight out of Harry Potter! #GameOfThrones."

Beyond this notable *Harry Potter* parallel, the *Game of Thrones* season seven premiere also featured a highly anticipated cameo by musician Ed Sheeran, a detail that particularly captured the attention of followers of George R.R. Martin's expansive original book series, *A Song of Ice and Fire*. In other premiere-related developments, dedicated fans engaged in detailed analyses of character screen time, seeking to definitively identify the show's most pivotal character. For those who missed the initial broadcast, the episode 'Dragonstone' was scheduled for a repeat airing on Sky Atlantic and NOW TV on July 17th at 9 pm, offering another opportunity to witness these memorable moments. The convergence of these two iconic fantasy worlds, through narrative parallels and shared talent, provided a rich tapestry of discussion for the show's dedicated fanbase.

It's been a long year-and-three-weeks since fans of HBO's mega-hit Game of Thrones said our temporary and permanent goodbyes to our friends and frenemies in King's Landing, the North, Dorne, and sundry other spots in George R.R. Martin's fictional continents of Westeros and Essos. By the end of Season Six, the show had completely outpaced all of Martin's written material; the existing sample chapters from the sixth A Song of Ice and Fire book either take place well before the opening of Season 7, or involve characters who were cut from the show (Arianne Martell, we never knew ye.) Voyager has passed the termination shock. With that, all elements of Season Seven, Episode 1, “Dragonstone” are a surprise, even for book readers. Here are eight favorites: “Tell them the North remembers. Tell them winter came for House Frey.” There’s no suspense on the demise of House Frey. Arya poisons all the men at once in the cold open, using a dose of the strangler and “the finest Arbor gold-proper wine for proper heroes.” The Frey children should have been suspicious when the irascible Lord Walder praised them. Welcome to the Map, Oldtown For the first time, Oldtown appears on the map, and it’s adorable, with its flaming lighthouse tower. It’s always a fun moment when they add one more thing to the map. Also, the camera fails to cross the narrow sea. It’s a good sign that we are done with Essos. “I don’t need your permission to defend the North.” That’s what fierce little lady Lady Lyanna Mormont sticks it to the lords once again when they object to Jon’s orders that the Northern women ages 10 to 60 be trained to fight the Wight Army alongside the men. She’s formidable, and a delight. Euron’s Black Ships Euron built that armada pretty darn quick, but it’s stunning, all black and silver. Euron, in a jab at Jaime, offers Cersei “a thousand ships, and two good hands.” His boats are incredible to look at, although it’s curious that he could have built a thousand majestic ships in the time elapsed since Yara and Theon stole his last fleet towards the end of Season 6. More ‘Shipping With Brienne and Tormund Tormund and Brienne have rarely interacted. It's cool that he’s into her martial prowess. Mostly, Tormund gazes lasciviously at her, while she rolls her eyes skeptically in return. Tormund directly signaled his attraction last season by gnawing lustily on what looked like the roasted rib bone of some large, dead animal. And a thousand memes were borne. It was satisfying to have more. Ed Sheeran’s Cameo Ed Sheeran shows up as a singing soldier in the Lannister army, and he and his friends share a rabbit and some blackberry wine with Arya. Thank the gods that she says “I’m going to kill the Queen” and spares them. Arya has become such a little sociopath that we might have worried for a minute. Dragonstone is a Mountain of Dragonglass Sam gets his moment of revelation after suffering through the chamberpot montage, which is strikingly shot but too gross to qualify as a cool moment on its own, as is Jorah’s scaly arm. Poor Jorah. It doesn’t look like greyscale victims have library privilege at Oldtown. He must be bored waiting for the Dragon Queen. Which leads us to… Daenarys’s Landing: “Shall We Begin?” There are many gorgeous shots in this sequence, wordless up to the last moment. It seems odd that Stannis didn’t leave any guard on Dragonstone, but that’s not worth quibbling about. We see dragons fly around Dragonstone, and Daenerys leaves a handprint in the sand on the island she was born on. She’s never been home before. Every moment feels full of connection, for her, to the family she never knew, aside from abusive Viserys. Another plus: the ever-annoying Sand Snakes make zero appearances in this episode. Their absence is welcome. Here’s something we haven’t seen yet -- another Stark sibling reunion. Remember, the only one we’ve seen so far was between Jon and Sansa in Season 6 -- and they barely exchanged a line of dialogue back in Season 1. GoT wouldn’t give us a Bran/Jon reunion so soon. That would be too much for one season premiere.

**Brienne and Tormund's "Ship" Sails On: A Look at Their Season 7 Premiere Reunion**

Fans of HBO's "Game of Thrones" got a moment they'd been eagerly anticipating in the season seven premiere: the reunion of Brienne of Tarth and Tormund Giantsbane. While Brienne, portrayed by Gwendoline Christie, may not share the same enthusiasm, the audience's desire to see these two characters together is palpable.

The episode finds Brienne at Winterfell, diligently training her squire, Podrick Payne, in swordsmanship. Despite her consistent dominance in their sparring sessions, Brienne finds herself momentarily distracted by Tormund's persistent gaze. This lapse allows Podrick to land a blow, snapping Brienne back to attention. Tormund, observing the exchange, offers a comment laced with admiration, "You're a lucky man," directed at Podrick, while giving Brienne a look that speaks volumes. Brienne, however, appears visibly annoyed and disgusted, quickly concluding the training.

The "non-couple" status of Brienne and Tormund has captivated viewers since their initial meeting in season six, episode four. Tormund's lingering glances, first at Castle Black and later during a shared meal, sparked what fans affectionately term "shipping" – the hope for a romantic relationship to develop. This latest interaction has reignited that fervent fan sentiment. Despite Brienne's apparent lack of romantic interest in Tormund, the audience continues to root for their eventual union.

query: What was the cost of the cladding installation at Grenfell Tower, and which company carried out the work?

documents.text: The company paid £3million to install the cladding, Harley Curtain Wall Ltd, has since gone bust after one of their customers refused to pay them almost £500,000 for disputed work. The director of the company, Ray Bailey, who has since started a new firm Harley Facades Limited, said that they had used a common Aluminium Composite Material (ACM) panels which are a commonly used product in the refurbishment industry. The London Fire Brigade warned ministers that building regulations were not taking into account the vertical fire issues that cladding could cause in 1999, warning that because of cladding regulations were "inadequate". Glyn Evans from the Fire Brigades Union told a Select Committee on Environment, Transport and Regional Affairs that after the Great Fire of London only horizontal fires were considered and "we do not really recognise the problem of vertical envelopment. If you get multistorey buildings you will get fire spread up the outside if the cladding will permit it." The Health and Safety Executive, the police and the fire service are now expected to launch a large-scale investigation into the safety of the building and whether regulations were complied with.

**Grenfell Tower Fire: A Nation's Grief and the Intensifying Search for Answers**

The Grenfell Tower fire, a catastrophic inferno that consumed a west London residential block, has left the nation in profound mourning. As the devastating scale of the tragedy unfolds, the death toll has tragically climbed, with authorities issuing stark warnings that this figure is likely to rise further. The sheer ferocity of the blaze has left rescue teams with little hope of finding additional survivors within the smoldering remains of the 24-storey building in North Kensington. Specialist search dogs are now meticulously working through the debris, a painstaking effort to uncover evidence and aid in the identification of those still missing. Her Majesty The Queen has expressed her deep sorrow, stating her "thoughts and prayers" are with all those affected by this terrible event. Currently, over 30 individuals remain hospitalized, with 17 in critical condition, a grim testament to the severity of injuries sustained. Prime Minister Theresa May has unequivocally pledged a thorough and comprehensive investigation into the circumstances surrounding the fire, while Labour Leader Jeremy Corbyn has stressed the paramount importance of "the truth to come out."

The fire ignited in the early hours of Wednesday morning, trapping hundreds of residents, many of whom were asleep. Awakened by neighbours or urgent shouts, a significant number managed to escape the rapidly escalating danger. Firefighters displayed extraordinary bravery, rescuing 65 individuals, though tragically, some were trapped by the swift and relentless advance of smoke and flames. London Fire Commissioner Dany Cotton confirmed that while some individuals have been identified, the full casualty count is still being painstakingly determined. She cautioned that a complete search operation could take several weeks due to the immense size and complexity of the building. Met Police Commander Stuart Cundy noted an extraordinary detail: one individual had been reported to the casualty bureau an unprecedented 46 times, underscoring the widespread concern and the potential for overlooked warnings. Commander Cotton described the ongoing search as "difficult and painstaking," a sentiment echoed by all involved.

Urgent questions are being raised about the fire's rapid and devastating spread. Reports from BBC Newsnight's Chris Cook have suggested that the cladding installed during a 2015 refurbishment, which featured a polyethylene core, may have significantly contributed to the inferno's ferocity. This type of cladding has been implicated in previous fires internationally, raising serious concerns about its use. In response to these alarming findings, the government has announced comprehensive checks on tower blocks with comparable upgrades across the country. Rydon, the construction firm responsible for the refurbishment, initially stated that the work met "all fire regulations." However, Sian Berry, housing committee chairwoman of the London Assembly, has suggested that fire risk assessments in tower blocks have become "less rigorous" over time. The absence of functioning fire alarms and sprinklers in the building has also drawn intense scrutiny. Russ Timpson of the Tall Buildings Fire Safety Network noted that while tall buildings can be constructed without sprinklers, regulations may need to be updated, and he expressed that overseas colleagues are "staggered" by the UK's practice of building tall residential structures with only a single staircase. Roy Wilsher, chair of the National Fire Chiefs Council, posited that sprinklers might not have been effective if the fire spread externally, emphasizing that current building regulations are primarily designed to contain fires within individual flats. "Clearly something's gone wrong in this case," he stated, a sentiment that resonates with the public's growing unease.

Dozens of displaced residents found immediate refuge in makeshift shelters, and an overwhelming outpouring of support saw community centres inundated with donations of essential items. Notable figures, including singer Adele and Rita Ora, visited the scene to offer comfort and practical assistance to those affected. Messages of love, hope, and palpable anger have been inscribed on a wall of condolence, with some expressing that their prior safety concerns had been tragically disregarded. Kensington and Chelsea council has placed 44 households into emergency accommodation, a crucial step in providing immediate support. Appeals for information on social media continue to circulate, seeking details on those still unaccounted for. Among the missing are 12-year-old Jessica Urbano Ramirez and 66-year-old Tony Disson. Labour MP David Lammy is actively seeking information about his friend, Khadija Saye, and her mother, Mary Mendy, highlighting the personal impact of this tragedy. An emergency hotline, 0800 0961 233, has been established to assist those seeking information or to report missing persons.

Heart-wrenching accounts from survivors are emerging, painting a vivid and terrifying picture of the night's events. One local resident described witnessing desperate screams of children, noting a family with five young children under the age of 10 is still missing. Michael Paramasivan, who resided on the seventh floor, defied official advice to remain in his flat, instead fleeing with his family through smoke-filled corridors. He recounted, "If we had stayed in that flat, we would've perished." Witnesses have reported seeing a baby thrown from a window in a desperate bid for survival and individuals attempting to descend the building using makeshift ropes. Jody Martin recounted his pleas for evacuation being met with the devastating response, "We can't leave our apartments, the smoke is too bad on the corridors." These personal testimonies underscore the sheer terror and the desperate choices faced by residents during the Grenfell Tower fire.

Here's a revised version of the document, focusing on improving query similarity, semantic uniqueness, and conciseness, while preserving all factual information:

**Grenfell Tower Fire: Individuals Still Missing**

Following the devastating fire at Grenfell Tower in west London, numerous individuals remain unaccounted for. This report details those still missing, based on appeals from friends, family, and social media.

**Photographer Khadija Saye and her Mother Mary Mendy**

Khadija Saye, a 24-year-old photographer whose work was recently exhibited at the Venice Biennale, is among those missing. She is believed to have been in her flat with her mother, Mary Mendy, 53, who had resided in the tower for at least two decades and lived on the 17th floor. David Lammy, MP for Tottenham and a friend of Saye's, appealed for information, describing her as a "dear friend, a beautiful soul and emerging artist." Saye's last known communication was a Facebook message at 3 am, stating she was trapped by thick smoke.

**Jessica Urbano, Separated from Family**

Twelve-year-old Jessica Urbano, a resident of the 20th floor, became separated from her family. Her aunt, Ana Ospina, a makeup artist, reported searching multiple hospitals for her niece, who was also believed to have been taken to a hospital by ambulance. Another aunt, Sandra, told the Mirror that Jessica had spoken to her mother at 1:29 am and 1:39 am, reportedly with a group on the fire stairs.

**The Belkadi Family: Farah, Omar, and Baby Leena**

Farah Hamdan, her husband Omar Belkadi, and their six-month-old daughter Leena Belkadi were on the 20th floor. Two other children from the family have since been found and are in hospital; one is in a coma, while the other is traumatized but otherwise stable. Farah called her sister around 1 am and was advised to leave, but had been instructed to "stay put."

**Mariem Elgwahry and Family**

Friends have appealed for information regarding Mariem Elgwahry, 27, who is believed to have lived on the 19th floor. She was last heard from at 2:30 am, speaking with her mother. Ahmed Elgwahry is also listed as a resident of the property.

**The Wahabi Family: Abdulaziz, Faouzia, and Children**

Abdulaziz Wahabi, his wife Faouzia, and their children Nurhouda, Yassin, and Medhi lived on the 21st floor. Abdulaziz's sister, Hana, spoke to him on the night of the fire. He reported being told to stay inside and seal their room with towels, but the smoke was too intense. Hana last saw them waving from their window, and Abdulaziz was on the phone with the fire brigade when she last spoke to his wife.

**Ali Yawar Jafari and Family**

Ali Yawar Jafari, 82, a resident of the 10th floor, is missing. His son, Hamid Ali Jafari, appealed for information on Twitter. He stated his father had lost contact with his mother and sister, who lived with him. Hamid recounted that his father was in the lift with his mother and sister when it stopped on the 10th floor. His father, unable to breathe due to the smoke, exited the lift, and it continued to the ground floor without them.

**Mohamednur Tucca and Family**

Mohamednur Tucca, his wife Amalahmedin, and their three-year-old daughter are missing after visiting relatives. Mike Morgan, CEO of the Red Consultancy, described Tucca as a valued member of their team for nearly a decade.

**Italian Couple Gloria Trevisan and Marco Gottardi**

Young Italian couple Gloria Trevisan, 26, and Marco Gottardi recently moved into the 23rd floor. Gottardi's cousin posted on Facebook that they had not been heard from since the previous night and their phones were off. Trevisan called her mother in Italy around 3:30 am to report the building was ablaze.

**Raymond "Moses" Bernard and Karen Bernard**

Raymond Bernard, 65, known as Moses, is missing. He was registered at flat 201 along with Karen Bernard, 53. They had resided there since at least 2003.

**Rania Ibrahim and her Children**

Rania Ibrahim, 30, believed to be the mother of two young children, aged three and five, uploaded a Facebook Live video appearing to be trapped at the top of the tower. She has not been heard from since. The video showed her seeking help in a smoke-filled corridor before returning to her flat and looking out from her balcony. Her husband was reportedly on holiday.

**Hesham Rahman**

Noha Baghdady shared a picture of her brother, Hesham Rahman, 57, who lived on the 20th floor. His nephews were searching hospitals. Rahman's last contact with family was around 3 am, when he reported smelling smoke. He reportedly has diabetes, which makes descending stairs difficult.

**Dennis Murphy**

Stevan Racz shared a post seeking information about his uncle, Dennis Murphy, who had called while trapped on the 14th floor. No further contact has been made.

**Tony Disson**

Tony Disson, 66, is missing. His son Lee Disson posted on Facebook seeking information. He is believed to have lived in flat 194. Another relative tweeted that Tony Disson lived on the 10th floor and that neighbors were sharing terrible news. He lived with his wife, Cordelia.

**Mohamed "Saber" Neda**

A friend, Ariana Neumann Rodger, posted that Mohamed "Saber" Neda's family is severely injured and in hospital, desperate to find him. Zia Popal described Neda as his uncle and stated he was on the top floor trying to help people before going missing. Hospitals had no record of him.

**Zainab Dean and Son Jeremiah**

Francis Dean, 47, reported that his sister Zainab had called him in the early hours of Wednesday, stating firefighters had instructed her to remain in her 14th-floor flat with her two-year-old son, Jeremiah. Dean fears the worst.

**Sheila Smith**

Adam Smith appealed for information about his 84-year-old mother, Sheila, who is believed to have lived in flat 132.

**Nadia Choucair and Family**

Council records show Nadia Choucair, 33, and Malak Choucair lived in flat 193. Nadia worked as a nursery officer and is understood to have lived on one of the upper floors with her husband, three daughters, and mother-in-law. A neighbor told the Telegraph that Nadia's daughter had called a friend saying, "I don’t think I’m going to make it."

**Nura Jamel**

Friends reported that Nura Jamel had contacted them from the building as the fire took hold. She was living at flat 192. A friend, Hibo Yussuf, 35, told the Telegraph that Nura's daughter is in hospital and has been unable to reach her mother. Nura had reportedly called a friend saying, "Forgive me, the fire is here, I’m dying."

In the wake of the tragic Grenfell Tower fire, Londoners displayed extraordinary resilience and community spirit. A spontaneous human chain formed, effectively managing the immense influx of donations. This powerful display of generosity and solidarity, captured by media like the BBC and LBC, highlighted the community's response to tragedy.

Volunteers in the human chain efficiently sorted and distributed vital supplies. Donations arrived from all over, including personal care items, clothing, and non-perishable food. The overwhelming public support, as reported by the BBC, initially strained collection points, underscoring the need for organized distribution, which the human chain successfully provided.

This community effort was marked by spontaneous organization, efficient logistics, and profound generosity. LBC presenter Matthew Stadlen, who joined the chain, called it "London at its very best in truly tragic circumstances." He noted the diverse participation and "an enormous outpouring of human kindness." The Grenfell Tower, a 24-story building housing around 400 residents, became the setting for this potent symbol of strengthened community bonds, as observed by witnesses and documented by organizations like the Red Cross.

## Grenfell Tower Fire: Questions Mount Over Safety and Speed of Blaze

A devastating fire ripped through Grenfell Tower in west London on Wednesday, June 14, 2017, claiming at least 12 lives and leaving residents and community leaders demanding answers. The ferocity and speed with which the flames engulfed the 24-story public housing complex have raised urgent questions about fire safety protocols and the materials used in recent renovations.

### The Unprecedented Speed of the Fire

Eyewitness accounts and extensive video footage depict flames climbing the exterior of Grenfell Tower with alarming rapidity. "I've never seen a fire like that in my life," stated Joe Ruane, former deputy chief fire officer for U.S. Air Force bases in Britain, emphasizing the unusual nature of the blaze in a residential building. Fire and police officials have yet to pinpoint the exact cause, but investigators are scrutinizing the materials used in the building's recent £10 million ($12.8 million) refurbishment, which included the installation of insulated exterior cladding and double-glazed windows. Ruane suggested that the rapid spread indicates multiple fire protection safeguards failed, rather than a single issue.

### The "Stay Put" Policy Under Scrutiny

A key point of contention is the building's fire safety policy, which, according to a 2014 newsletter from the Kensington and Chelsea Tenant Management Organization (KCTMO), advised residents to "stay put" in their apartments during a fire unless the blaze was in their immediate vicinity or they were instructed to evacuate. This policy was reportedly based on the building's design adhering to "rigorous fire-safety standards," including new apartment doors designed to withstand fire for 30 minutes. While effective in smaller incidents, this strategy proved tragically inadequate as the Grenfell Tower fire rapidly breached apartment doors and filled escape routes with smoke and flames. The London Fire Brigade, arriving within six minutes of the initial reports, found themselves unable to reach residents on higher floors.

### A History of Ignored Warnings

Survivors and community groups have pointed to a pattern of ignored warnings regarding fire safety at Grenfell Tower. The Grenfell Action Group, a local community organization, had been raising concerns about fire hazards since 2013 through blog posts detailing issues with fire-fighting equipment maintenance and blocked emergency access. "All our warnings fell on deaf ears, and we predicted that a catastrophe like this was inevitable and just a matter of time," the group stated on Wednesday.

Furthermore, the KCTMO, which manages Grenfell Tower and other properties for the Kensington and Chelsea Council, has a documented history of fire safety citations. An October 2015 arson fire at Adair Tower, another KCTMO-managed building, resulted in the London Fire Brigade issuing an enforcement notice for improvements to fire safety in staircases and the installation of self-closing devices on apartment doors. Similar notices were issued for Hazelwood Tower.

### Parallels to Past Tragedies

The Grenfell Tower disaster bears a disturbing resemblance to the 2009 Lakanal House fire, where a fast-moving blaze in a 14-story public housing project killed six people. A coroner's inquest into the Lakanal House fire identified a series of failures and made recommendations to prevent future incidents. Jim Fitzpatrick, a former firefighter and Member of Parliament, noted that investigators will need to examine whether these recommendations were implemented at Grenfell Tower. "These will be matters for the scientists and the engineers... to find out exactly how the fire started, why it spread so quickly and what could have been done to prevent it," Fitzpatrick told Sky News.

In response to the tragedy, the Kensington and Chelsea Council has pledged a full investigation and public accounting. The UK government has also initiated checks on tower blocks undergoing similar renovations to those at Grenfell Tower. The KCTMO stated it is cooperating with investigators and taking all tenant concerns seriously, which will be part of the forthcoming investigations.
