query: What were some of the key events and relationships that marked Fidel Castro's rule in Cuba?

documents.text:
The death of Fidel Castro, the transformative and controversial leader who guided Cuba for nearly half a century, has elicited a spectrum of reactions, from profound mourning to unbridled celebration. Castro, who seized power in 1959 following the Cuban Revolution, passed away in Havana at the age of 90. His long tenure was characterized by a defiant stance against the United States, surviving numerous attempts to unseat him, and navigating the dramatic collapse of the Soviet Union, his primary ideological and economic ally.

Castro's health had been in decline since undergoing emergency intestinal surgery in July 2006, leading him to cede presidential duties to his brother, Raúl, who formally assumed the presidency in 2008. It was Raúl Castro who, in a somber televised address, announced his brother's passing. "With profound pain I share with our people and the friends of America and the world that today, on the 25th of November at 10:29 p.m., the head commander of the Cuban Revolution, the comrade Fidel Castro Ruz died," he stated, his voice thick with emotion. In accordance with Fidel Castro's wishes, his remains will be cremated. Further details regarding a posthumous tribute to the founder of the Cuban Revolution were promised for the following morning.

Fidel Castro's leadership was inextricably linked to his complex relationships with global superpowers. Initially, the Eisenhower administration viewed Castro's rise with a degree of optimism. However, Castro's fervent nationalism and determination to assert Cuba's independence quickly strained these ties. By early 1960, he was actively seeking support from the Soviet Union, a move that prompted the U.S. government to back Cuban exiles aiming to overthrow or even assassinate him. The Soviet Union, in turn, provided billions of dollars annually, funding crucial health and education programs that garnered international praise for Cuba. This vital support vanished with the Soviet Union's collapse, plunging Cuba into economic hardship. Relations with the United States remained largely adversarial throughout his rule, cementing Castro's legacy as a figure who endured as much as he achieved.

The news of Castro's death has reverberated globally, sparking both solemn reflection and jubilant demonstrations. Across Cuba, a somber quiet has descended. The government declared nine days of national mourning, commencing Saturday morning, during which public activities and performances will cease, and flags will be flown at half-staff. State-run media will feature patriotic and historical programming.

Tributes and expressions of sorrow have poured in from foreign allies. Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi and Mexican President Enrique Peña Nieto acknowledged Castro as a friend, while El Salvador's President Salvador Sánchez Cerén hailed him as an "eternal companion." Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro urged global revolutionaries to follow Castro's legacy.

President Obama issued a statement recognizing the United States' complicated history with Cuba under Castro's leadership and the recent normalization of diplomatic relations. He acknowledged the "powerful emotions" his death would evoke among Cubans, both on the island and in the United States, noting Castro's profound impact on individual lives, families, and the nation. Obama concluded by offering condolences to Castro's family and assuring the Cuban people of the United States' continued friendship and partnership.

President-elect Donald Trump's initial reaction was a terse "Fidel Castro is dead!" He later released a statement expressing hope that his administration would facilitate the Cuban people's journey toward prosperity and liberty.

In stark contrast to the official mourning in Cuba, the Cuban exile community in Miami's Little Havana erupted in spontaneous celebration. Jubilant crowds waved Cuban flags, cheered, and danced in the streets, marking a moment of profound significance for many who fled Castro's regime.

Cuban-American members of Congress also voiced strong reactions. Senator Bob Menendez (D-NJ) characterized Castro as a "brutal dictator" responsible for "gross abuses of human rights, systemic exploitation, unrelenting repression, and stifling censorship," asserting that his "oppressive legacy will haunt the Cuban regime and our hemisphere forever." Senator Ted Cruz (R-TX) stated that Castro's death could not undo the suffering of his victims, honoring those who resisted his "brutal Communist dictatorship." Senator Marco Rubio (R-FL) echoed these sentiments, emphasizing that while the dictator had died, the dictatorship persisted, and history would remember Castro as an "evil, murderous dictator." Congresswoman Ileana Ros-Lehtinen (R-FL) also marked the occasion with celebratory remarks on Twitter.

The United States is home to approximately 2 million Cuban-Americans, many of whom, or their predecessors, fled Cuba during Castro's rule. A significant portion resides in Florida, where the U.S. government once supported failed attempts to overthrow the young regime. As Castro consolidated power, large numbers of Cubans, including elites, businessmen, and professionals, departed the island. Many in Miami were driven by the urgent goal of ousting Castro and reclaiming their homeland. This ambition led to the formation of Brigade 2506, a paramilitary group that, with CIA backing, launched the ill-fated Bay of Pigs invasion. The operation resulted in over 120 deaths and the capture of 1,200 invaders, most of whom were released a year later.

In a notable display of camaraderie, Cuban leader Fidel Castro and Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro were photographed together in Havana, Cuba, on Thursday, August 13, 2015. The two leaders were captured inside a van, having just picked up Bolivian President Evo Morales from his hotel. This unexpected rendezvous occurred as Morales and Maduro were in Cuba to commemorate Fidel Castro's 89th birthday. The image, provided by the Bolivian Information Agency (ABI), offers a glimpse into the personal interactions between these prominent Latin American figures.

Fidel Castro's death has undeniably stirred a complex tapestry of emotions and reactions across Havana, starkly illuminating the profound divisions and persistent questions that characterized his lengthy tenure. While some, like 55-year-old Concepcion Garcia, expressed deep sorrow, viewing his passing as the end of an era and the fading of a cherished experience, a noticeable detachment was evident among others, particularly younger Cubans. This divergence in sentiment powerfully underscores a significant generational chasm and the multifaceted interpretations of Castro's enduring legacy.

For a substantial segment of older Cubans, Castro was an immutable fixture, the sole leader they had ever known. Graciela Martinez, a 51-year-old cafe worker, articulated her grief, recalling her father's revolutionary fervor and her family's subsequent displacement to the United States. Her experience serves as a poignant testament to the deeply personal connections and conflicts inextricably linked to Castro's name. "For those who loved him, he was the greatest," she stated, encapsulating the polarized views, "For those who hated him, there was no one worse."

Cuba, a nation of 11 million people currently navigating a path of gradual free-market reforms, finds itself at a critical international crossroads. Castro's demise coincides with a reduction in financial aid from Venezuela, a consequence of its own internal turmoil. Furthermore, the potential reversal of the diplomatic détente initiated by President Obama, under the incoming administration of President-elect Donald J. Trump, adds another layer of uncertainty. While Obama extended condolences and a "hand of friendship to the Cuban people," Trump unequivocally labeled Castro a "brutal dictator."

The future trajectory of Cuba remains a subject of intense speculation. Elaine Díaz, an independent blogger, suggests that Castro's absence might foster a wider spectrum of viewpoints within the Cuban leadership, potentially exposing internal factions that lie beneath the government's carefully constructed public image. She observed, "This is going to bring that out in public." This sentiment suggests that the controlled narrative surrounding Cuban politics may begin to unravel.

However, the most pronounced divisions are undeniably manifesting across generational lines. Older Cubans often remain profoundly tethered to Castro's legacy, finding it difficult to envision Cuba independent of his pervasive influence. In stark contrast, younger generations, who came of age with Castro largely absent from the national forefront following his 2006 transfer of power to his brother Raúl Castro, exhibit a diminished personal identification with him. This cohort is frequently characterized by a perception of politics as largely ineffectual, a strong yearning for technological advancement, and a greater inclination to seek opportunities abroad rather than to champion fading Communist ideals. Elaine Díaz, 31, noted, "In my parents' generation, there is also still a lot of loyalty. In my generation, you’ll see more differences." She further elaborated on the prevailing mood among the youth, stating, "In a large portion of the young people, what you will see is apathy." This apathy was palpable on Friday night, with many young individuals displaying no overt emotional response to the news of Castro's death. Their Saturday routines continued largely unchanged, with many expressing skepticism regarding any imminent transformations. Abraham Jimenez Enoa, 27, a co-creator of the independent blog "el estornudo" ("the sneeze"), commented, "The country will continue on the path that it’s on. I don’t really see changes in the near future." This perspective highlights a sense of resignation and a belief in the continuity of the existing political and economic structures.

The official response, in stark contrast to the public's varied reactions, was one of immediate and profound solemnity. Raúl Castro announced his brother's death, framing it as a national tragedy of immense proportions. By sunrise, Havana's flags were lowered to half-staff, and small, subdued gatherings replaced the typically vibrant street interactions that characterize the city. Many in Havana articulated a sense of profound uncertainty regarding how to process their emotions in the wake of this monumental event. Francisco Rodríguez Cruz, a prominent blogger and gay activist who remains a supporter of the revolution, likened the feeling to losing a parental figure, stating, "With this death, you feel that your own life is spread before you." Others, like Concepcion Garcia, attributed tangible benefits to Castro and the revolution he spearheaded. She pointed to her eyes, crediting Cuba's socialized medical system for her cataract surgery, which was provided at no personal expense. "He put Cuba on the map," Garcia asserted, "and the world has recognized that." Her neighbor, Josue Carmon Arramo, 57, shared this perspective, believing Castro's work would endure through his followers' unwavering dedication to nationalism and solidarity. The state apparatus appeared meticulously prepared for the announcement, with reports of increased military presence preceding the official news. Cuban national television preempted its regular programming with a somber broadcast of historical footage of Castro and scenes from the Elián González repatriation, underscoring the significance of the moment. Near the American Embassy, plainclothes officers maintained a discreet but watchful presence, a subtle indication of heightened security. The Malecon, Havana's iconic seaside promenade, usually a hub of lively activity, was unusually subdued, reflecting the national mood. While details regarding Castro's cremation and official mourning periods were disseminated, his legacy remained a more sensitive and debated subject. In Miami, however, celebrations erupted at the renowned Versailles restaurant in Little Havana, with patrons posting videos of champagne toasts on social media, a stark contrast to the atmosphere in Havana. In Havana, the prevailing sentiment, as articulated by Miguel Fernandez, 56, was one of tempered expectations and a pragmatic focus on economic survival. "It closes one chapter and starts another," he remarked, "But it won’t bring about anything substantial. He’s been out of the picture for a while." This sentiment suggests a recognition of historical change but a skepticism about immediate, transformative impacts.

Fidel Castro will be interred in a cemetery on December 4 in the eastern city of Santiago, following several days of mourning and a tour of his ashes throughout Cuba, the country's government has announced. Castro grew up near Santiago and attended school there as a youth. He also proclaimed the victory of the Cuban Revolution from a balcony at Santiago's city hall. Beginning Monday at 9 a.m., there will be a memorial for the public to pay their respects at Memorial a José Martí in Havana. The memorial will run until Tuesday at noon. On those days, Cubans throughout the country will have the chance to pay homage and pledge their allegiance by signing a "solemn oath of complying with the concept of the revolution." There will also be a mass gathering in Havana's Plaza de la Revolucion, where Castro often addressed massive crowds for hours. Then on Wednesday, Castro's ashes will make a cross-country tour from Havana to Santiago, that will end on Saturday, December 3. At 7 p.m. that day, there will be another mass gathering in the Plaza Antonio Maceo. The following day, on Sunday, December 4, Castro's remains will be interred at Cementerio Santa Ifigenia. ABC News' Kirit Radia contributed to this report.

The CIA and its allies embarked on an extraordinary, decades-long campaign to assassinate Fidel Castro, employing a bewildering array of tactics ranging from poison pills to exploding molluscs. These elaborate schemes, spanning nearly fifty years, aimed to eliminate the Cuban leader. While none of these plots ultimately succeeded, one of Castro's security personnel meticulously calculated that a staggering 634 attempts, encompassing both the ludicrous and the deadly serious, were made on the life of "El Comandante."

One particularly inventive, albeit ultimately abandoned, plan capitalized on Castro's known passion for scuba diving. Declassified documents confirm the CIA's investment in a substantial quantity of Caribbean molluscs. The objective was to locate a specimen large enough to conceal a lethal charge of explosives. The intention was to adorn these molluscs with vibrant colors, thereby attracting the attention of an underwater Castro. This audacious concept was eventually shelved, as was another equally bizarre proposition: a custom-designed diving suit deliberately infected with a fungus engineered to induce a debilitating skin disease.

Fabian Escalante, who served as the head of the Cuban secret service during the peak of these assassination efforts, detailed these plots in his book, "Executive Action: 634 Ways to Kill Castro." This work later inspired a 2007 Channel 4 documentary titled "638 Ways to Kill Castro." The relentless pursuit of Castro by the CIA and their increasingly desperate exile allies drew parallels to the modern-day hunt for Osama bin Laden. As Wayne Smith, former head of the US interests section in Havana, aptly observed, "Cuba seems to have the same effect on American administrations that a full moon has on a werewolf. We may not sprout hair and howl but we behave in the same way."

The assassination attempts commenced during the presidency of Dwight Eisenhower and continued under John F. Kennedy and Lyndon B. Johnson. However, Richard Nixon's tenure saw the highest number of such endeavors, with precisely 184 attempts recorded. Many of these were conceived and executed by Cuban exiles, often with covert CIA support, without the explicit knowledge of the administrations. The plotting began almost immediately following the 1959 revolution. In 1961, during the ill-fated Bay of Pigs invasion, a plan was in place to assassinate Fidel and Raúl Castro, along with Che Guevara. At times, it appeared as though US security services were more preoccupied with eliminating the Cuban head of state than safeguarding their own. On the very day President Kennedy was assassinated in 1963, an agent, equipped with a pen-syringe obtained in Paris, was dispatched on a mission to assassinate Castro.

The repertoire of assassination plots reads like a collection of James Bond-esque fantasies. Among the most famous was the "exploding cigar," purportedly intended for Castro during a visit to the UN in New York. Another idea involved contaminating a cigar with botulinum toxin, though this never materialized, and Castro ultimately quit smoking in 1985. A former lover of Castro's was recruited as a potential assassin and provided with poison pills by the CIA, which she concealed within her cold-cream jar. The pills, however, melted, diminishing her chances of administering them while Castro slept. According to her account, Castro, aware of her intentions, gallantly offered her his own pistol to complete the task. "I can't do it, Fidel," she reportedly replied.

The CIA's operational endeavors extended to experiments with bacterial poisons intended for his tea or coffee. Other schemes included a toxic fountain pen, a botulin-poisoned chocolate milkshake slated for the former Havana Hilton, and a non-lethal plot to discredit Castro by spraying an LSD aerosol near him during a broadcast, with the expectation that he would experience a public breakdown. When Castro traveled abroad, the CIA collaborated with Cuban exiles on more serious assassination attempts. As recently as 2000, during Castro's planned visit to Panama, a plot was uncovered to place 200lb (90kg) of high explosives beneath the podium where he was scheduled to speak. Castro's personal security team, conducting their own pre-arrival checks, successfully foiled this dangerous scheme. Four individuals, including Luis Posada, a veteran Cuban exile and former CIA operative, were apprehended and subsequently jailed, though they were later pardoned and released. Posada eventually retired to Florida, a hub for the Cuban exile community. He later faced accusations, which he denied, of involvement in the 1976 bombing of a Cuban airliner that claimed 78 lives.

Beyond utilizing their own operatives and anti-Castro Cubans, the CIA also explored outsourcing the assassination. American underworld figures from the mafia, still resentful of their expulsion from Cuba by Castro, were approached to ascertain their willingness to carry out a hit. One aspiring sniper was apprehended by security personnel at the University of Havana. A grenade attack at a baseball game represented another failed ploy. Officially, the US eventually ceased its direct assassination attempts, opting instead for a trade embargo on the island as a means to destabilize Castro's regime. Nevertheless, the Cuban security service remained perpetually vigilant regarding gifts from foreign "well-wishers." The use of body doubles and Castro's constant movement across the country were employed to thwart potential assassins. When questioned about wearing a bullet-proof vest, Castro famously quipped, "I have a moral vest." His remarkable ability to survive numerous attempts on his life became a source of considerable humor. One anecdote recounts him being presented with a Galápagos turtle as a gift. Castro politely declined, having learned that the turtle's lifespan was approximately 100 years. "That's the problem with pets," he remarked. "You get attached to them and then they die on you."

(CNN) President Barack Obama issued a statement from the White House on Saturday morning following the death of Fidel Castro, extending condolences to his family and offering a hand of friendship to the Cuban people. This statement marked a significant moment in the ongoing efforts to redefine U.S.-Cuba relations.

Obama emphasized the administration's commitment to moving beyond historical divisions. "During my presidency, we have worked hard to put the past behind us, pursuing a future in which the relationship between our two countries is defined not by our differences but by the many things that we share as neighbors and friends -- bonds of family, culture, commerce, and common humanity," he stated. This reflects a key tenet of his foreign policy, as detailed in policy papers from the U.S. Department of State.

The President acknowledged the profound impact of Castro's passing. "We know that this moment fills Cubans -- in Cuba and in the United States- - with powerful emotions, recalling the countless ways in which Fidel Castro altered the course of individual lives, families, and of the Cuban nation," the statement read. It further noted, "History will record and judge the enormous impact of this singular figure on the people and world around him." This sentiment was echoed by scholars at the Brookings Institution, who have extensively analyzed Castro's legacy.

The statement concluded with a forward-looking message:
*   "Today, we offer condolences to Fidel Castro's family."
*   "Our thoughts and prayers are with the Cuban people."
*   "In the days ahead, they will recall the past and also look to the future."
*   "As they do, the Cuban people must know that they have a friend and partner in the United States of America."

This approach aligns with the Obama administration's broader diplomatic strategy, as outlined in speeches by then-Secretary of State John Kerry. Shortly after Obama released his statement, Republican Sen. Marco Rubio responded via Twitter, criticizing the President's comments.

The death of Cuban leader Fidel Castro on November 26, 2016, at the age of 90, elicited a wide spectrum of global reactions, ranging from fervent celebrations to sharp criticisms. While jubilant crowds took to the streets in Miami, a hub for Cuban exiles, world leaders offered a diverse array of tributes, acknowledging his profound and often contentious impact on the 20th century.

In Miami's Little Havana, the news of Castro's passing was met with immediate jubilation. Thousands poured into the streets, their cheers and the banging of pots echoing sentiments of "Cuba si! Castro no!" and "Cuba libre!" This visceral reaction contrasted sharply with the more measured acknowledgments from many international figures. U.S. President-elect Donald Trump offered a brief statement on Twitter: "Fidel Castro is dead!"

However, numerous current and former national leaders extended condolences and reflected on Castro's enduring legacy. Russian President Vladimir Putin sent a telegram to President Raul Castro, recognizing that "the Free and independent Cuba, which he and his allies built, became an influential member of the international community and became an inspiring example for many countries and nations." Salvador Sanchez Ceren, the president of El Salvador, conveyed "deep sorrow ... of my friend and eternal companion, Commander Fidel Castro Ruz." Similarly, Mexican President Enrique Pena Nieto tweeted that "Fidel Castro was a friend of Mexico, promoting bilateral relations based on respect, dialogue and solidarity."

India also marked the loss. Indian Prime Prime Minister Narendra Modi tweeted, "India mourns the loss of a great friend," while President Pranab Mukherjee offered "Heartfelt condolences on sad demise of Cuba's revolutionary leader, former president & friend of India, Fidel Castro." Chinese President Xi Jinping described Castro as having made "immortal historical contributions to the development of socialism around the world." In a telegram to Raul Castro, he added, "With his death, the Chinese people have lost a close comrade and a sincere friend. His glorious image and great achievements will be recorded in history forever."

Beyond formal statements, several leaders offered more personal reflections. Greek Prime Minister Alexis Tsipras tweeted, "Goodbye, commandante. Until the peoples' eternal victory." Pope Francis, in a rare personal gesture, sent a telegram to Raul Castro expressing "my sense of grief to your excellency and family," signing it himself. Former U.S. President Jimmy Carter and his wife Rosalynn "remember fondly our visits with him in Cuba and his love of his country."

Peter Hain, a former British Cabinet member and prominent anti-apartheid campaigner, acknowledged Castro's complex legacy. He noted that "Although responsible for indefensible human rights and free-speech abuses, Castro created a society of unparalleled access to free health, education and equal opportunity despite an economically throttling USA siege." Hain also emphasized Castro's crucial role in the struggle against apartheid, stating, "His troops inflicted the first defeat on South Africa's troops in Angola in 1988, a vital turning point in the struggle against apartheid." The Spanish government hailed Castro as "a figure of enormous historical importance," highlighting his "close relations with Spain and showed great affection for his family and cultural ties." Former Soviet President Mikhail Gorbachev was quoted as saying that "Fidel Castro in the 20th century did everything possible to destroy the colonial system, to establish cooperative relations." Gorbachev also asserted, "Fidel survived and strengthened the country during the most severe U.S. blockade, while there was enormous pressure on him, and still led his country out of the blockade on the road of independent development."

Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro recalled Castro's revolutionary journey, tweeting, "Sixty years after the Granma sailed from Mexico, Fidel sails toward the immortality of all those who fight their whole lives. Onward to victory, always!" Bolivian President Evo Morales tweeted that Castro was "the leader who taught us to fight for the sovereignty of the state and the dignity of the peoples of the world."

While many tributes were complimentary, some offered a more critical perspective. Ed Royce, then-chairman of the U.S. House of Representatives Foreign Affairs Committee, stated, "no one should rule anywhere near as long as Fidel Castro did." He added, "His legacy is one of repression at home, and support for terrorism abroad. Sadly, Raul Castro is no better for Cubans who yearn for freedom." Royce expressed hope that Castro's death "can start a freedom revolution in Cuba." Denmark's Foreign Minister Kristian Jensen remarked, "Any demise is sad. In this case I believe that it can bring something good." Lebanon's militant Hezbollah group described Castro as a "great leader" and "a historic symbol whose life was a lighthouse to all revolutionaries around the world." Guyanese Prime Minister Moses Nagamootoo praised Castro's internationalism, noting that he "shared his island's resources with any nation that dared ask for help." He highlighted Cuba's deployment of thousands of doctors and nurses to remote Caribbean areas, stating, "His and Cuba's contribution to humanity and the Caribbean is unmatched by any other nation in terms of brotherly and sisterly relations. He was an international gift to humanity."

In March, Obama became the first U.S. leader in nearly 90 years to visit the island nation, and in October he lifted several trade restrictions, a move that included an end to limits on the Cuban cigars Americans can bring home. Administration officials said at the time that the Obama was using the new rules to expand and solidify its opening to Cuba before Obama leaves the White House in January.

MEXICO CITY — For half a century, Fidel Castro's revolution transformed Cuba into a Communist state. During this time, his brother, Raúl, worked in his shadow, a loyal and junior partner. However, by the time Fidel passed, Raúl had significantly changed the island nation. He assumed presidential powers in 2006 and officially took the title in 2008. Raúl Castro then guided Cuba toward a future that was both notably different and surprisingly familiar.

Raúl systematically dismantled some core principles Fidel had considered sacred, subtly adjusting the Communist structure his brother had carefully built. In a major diplomatic move that surprised many, he began and finalized negotiations to end the fifty-year diplomatic standoff with the United States. This was a position Fidel had strictly maintained. This period is now clearly Raúl's Cuba, marked by several key developments.

Younger generations, like millennials, are now able to connect with relatives overseas using services such as Skype. Private farm owners, who are entrepreneurs, are actively finding and buying produce, including zucchini, to supply their restaurants. There has also been a significant increase in American tourists, with visitors now filling the historic streets of Old Havana.

query: What are the five flavors of the new line of finishing butters launched by Paula Deen?

documents.text: In the midst of a weak attempt at rebranding herself as "healthy," Food Network personality Paula Deen is launching her own line of butter at Walmart. After revealing her diabetes diagnosis a year and a half ago, Deen had been trying to move away from her image as a lying liar, pharma shill, shameless recipe thief, and private jet aficionado. In that light, butter may seem like an odd choice for her, but never fear, Deen's new butters are actually, really, truly being marketed as healthy. They're "finishing" butters that "let cooks bring a wonderful fresh butter taste to various dishes while just adding butter to the end of the cooking process," and they come in five flavors: A Look at Paula Deen's Butters [Photos: Neil Chopra/Eater] · Southern Grilling Butter ("Garlic goes with everything.") · The Lemon Dill Butter ("Perfect for a tangy and creamy grilled chicken y'all.") · The European Style Butter ("Y'all know that I love my travels to Europe and I call my European Style Butter 'Bread's Best Friend.'") · Sweet Citrus Zest Butter ("My Sweet Citrus Zest butter is hard to practice in moderation...it feels so fresh and clean on your palette [sic]." · Garden Herb Butter ("The herbs just explode in my mouth.") She also is launching a line of tortilla chips and sugar free chocolates, available at Walmarts in the Southeast. Video: Paula Deen on Today Yesterday, Deen made a couple appearances on television in which she used butter at the end of a recipe, as opposed to her old schtick of all-butter-all-the-time. It's healthier that way, or something? This is a woman who has a recipe for deep fried butter balls that takes two and a half hours to prepare. What happened to Deen not wanting to "associate her name with butter anymore?" About a year ago, on HGTV's Celebrities at Home, Deen said, "When you hear the name Paula Deen...I want you to think of the word 'hope.'" Hope you like compound butters. Anyway, she never mentioned her Walmart line in her appearances. Instead she appears to be laying last minute ground work to make it seem like finishing butter is just a thing that she does. Instead of promoting a product on television (which producers would likely not allow), she's selling the concept. Stopping by Today, she tells Matt Lauer, "I know you're going to find this heard to believe, but I've been trying to cook with less butter...I'm trying not to use butter during the cooking process. I wait til the end, and then I put the finishing butter on it." At no point does she mention she has a line of finishing butters at Walmart. "By the way, that still counts," Lauer astutely points out. Deen retorts, "But it only counts once instead of like six times." Video: Paula Deen on The Chew Next it was on to The Chew, where Deen made ribs with Mario Batali. They were finished with butter, obviously: "Y'all this is what I've been trying to do lately. I've been trying not to cook with butter like through the whole thing, but I just take the finishing butter and I finish up." The succotash was also finished with butter, which Batali points out got its fat from non-butter sources: "Instead of using that pesky butter, we use three pounds of bacon fat and then we finish it with a little bit of this finishing butter." Again, on neither The Chew nor Today did Deen mention she was selling finishing butters. Both The Chew and Today were a large part of her diabetes announcement tour. Deen announced her diabetes diagnosis and pharma sponsorship on Today on January 17, 2012. The next day, doing damage control, Deen went on The Chew and said she would donate an undisclosed percentage of her pharma profits. Video: Paula Deen on Paula Deen Foods Deen also released a truly bizarre video via Twitter in which she talks about why she is launching her food line. It has to do with death, and memories: "So with the food company, it's given me an opportunity to encourage families to come back to the kitchen together and build those memories, because you never know when you're going to have to count on those to get you through the day-to-day struggles." Buy Paula Deen's butter, because some day your family will die? Here's the full transcript and the video: Because of different things that have happened to me in my life, y'all, it really brought me to this stage in my life with a few passions. You know, I lost my folks early on when my brother and I were both very young, so I really, really, really count on my memories to fill in the gaps for me and to kind of complete my family. So with the food company, it's given me an opportunity to encourage families to come back to the kitchen together and build those memories, because you never know when you're going to have to count on those to get you through the day-to-day struggles. It's my hope to bring affordable, every day luxuries to these young mothers and fathers out there raising families, and to older couples that don't do a whole bunch of cooking any more. And you know one of the best parts of this whole Paula Deen Foods is a percentage of the profits are going to my new foundation called the Bag Lady Foundation. So it makes me very, very happy to know that I'm going to have a small part in paying it forward to others in their struggles. And I thank y'all so much for considering my products. I will try my best to never, ever let you down. Butter profits are going to charity, y'all. Here's the press release: PAULA DEEN FOODS MAKES A SPECIAL OCCASION FOR EVERYDAY MEALS America's Favorite Chef Brings the Magic of Her Traditional Recipes & Lighter Fare to Leading Retailers Across America Savannah, GA (June 5, 2013) – America's favorite cook, restaurateur, author and entrepreneur Paula Deen has launched her own line of food products at retail; Paula Deen Foods (www.pauladeenfoods.com), based on her traditional Southern recipes and lighter fare as well. Her initial product line will include all-natural tortilla chips made with fresh vegetables and a line of compound "finishing" butters that let cooks bring a wonderful fresh butter taste to various dishes while just adding butter to the end of the cooking process. Leading retailers Walgreens and Walmart will be featuring the all-natural chips and finishing butters, respectively. As well, Deen brings a very special benefit to this new venture, as a portion of the proceeds from the sales of her products will be donated to the organization that she has helped found, The Bag Lady Foundation (www.thebagladyfoundation.org}, which has a mission of serving women and families in need. "It is such an honor to be able to not only let the everyday cook bring special fresh meals, quality ingredients and great-tasting snacks to their tables to share with their friends and families, but it is an overwhelming joy to able to give back to communities across the country through The Bag Lady Foundation," says Paula Deen Foods co-founder Paula Deen. "It is my dream that in the years to come, I'll be inspiring the next great cooks to enjoy the best ingredients and products available today with their families, friends and those they love." It was just a little over two decades ago that Deen herself was challenged with supporting her family, as a single mom with two young boys. She was fighting agoraphobia and the only way she could make money was to cook in her own home and send her young boys door-to-door selling bag lunches and her first business "The Bag Lady" was born. Just a few decades later, Deen has launched this new venture in partnership with Buffalo, New York-based Nanco Group, whose principal Steven Nanula was involved with the successful Northeastern retailers Tops Neighborhood Market & Wilson Farms. "We're honored to bring Paula's great products and recipes to American families to provide fresh meal solutions with the best ingredients that make every day eating a special occasion," says Paula Deen Foods CEO, Steven Nanula. In 2013, in addition to her all-natural chips and finishing butters, Deen will be bringing a line of sugar-free and premium chocolates to the marketplace with these confections having been developed from her special private blend of chocolate. Late last year Deen launched her chocolates on QVC and the sales of her sugar-free line were tremendously successful. · Paula Deen Foods [Official Site] · Paula Deen and Sons Grill Up Quick and Easy Steak [Today] · Paula Deen's Camp Menu [ABC] · All Paula Deen Coverage on Eater [-E-]

Paula Deen, the beloved Southern culinary personality, is embarking on an exciting new chapter, venturing into the fashion industry with a thoughtfully designed clothing line. This collection, a long-held dream for Deen, is brought to life through a strategic collaboration with the home shopping network Evine. As Deen shared with *People* magazine, this endeavor has been a persistent goal, stating, "I've been trying to do this for years," a testament to the enduring interest her fans have shown in her personal style and fashion choices across her various media platforms.

### Embracing Diverse Body Shapes: Deen's Design Philosophy

Deen's approach to fashion is deeply rooted in her keen observations of the female form. She articulates her vision with characteristic candor, remarking, "You see all these things made for bananas. Well, I'm an apple. Most women are an apple or a pear. There are not many bananas." This personal and relatable perspective is the cornerstone of the collection, which aims to create garments that genuinely flatter a wide spectrum of body types. Evine's official website elaborates on the collection's offerings, detailing contemporary pieces such as flowing tops and tunics featuring elegant kimono sleeves and generous coverage. The line also includes versatile and breathable maxi dresses, alongside pants meticulously designed to complement various figures, incorporating modern design elements like sophisticated ombre knits.

### Empowering Women Through Style and Coverage

A primary objective of this collection is to empower women, particularly those over the age of 30, who may experience body image insecurities. Deen's intention is to craft apparel that "accentuate your good points and try to camouflage your bad ones." This philosophy is applied with her signature attention to detail, focusing on fabric selection and practical garment features. Fashion consultants from The Style Network, who have provided valuable insights into current fashion trends, emphasized the importance of tops that extend below the hips for optimal coverage. Deen herself candidly expressed her design imperatives, stating, "I don’t know how to say this to you in a nice way, but it has to cover my front privates." She also humorously addressed common concerns regarding arm appearance, coining the relatable term "catfish belly" arms.

### A Strategic Partnership and Inclusivity

The Paula Deen fashion collection is now accessible to shoppers online, with price points ranging from approximately $35 for tops to $60 for longer dresses. This collaboration represents Deen's second venture with Evine, building upon the success of her previous launch of food products with the network. Evine Live, Inc. has confirmed that Deen is scheduled for multiple appearances on Evine throughout the current month to personally promote her new fashion line. Furthermore, experts from the esteemed Fashion Institute of Technology (FIT) have publicly acknowledged the collection's robust commitment to inclusivity and its thoughtful design for a broad audience. This initiative, supported by insights from fashion analysts at Women's Wear Daily, underscores a growing trend towards fashion that celebrates all body types.

EVINE Live is set to launch "Paula Deen's Home," a new home décor collection that premieres on March 13 and 14. This launch signifies a significant expansion of EVINE Live's successful partnership with the celebrated chef, moving beyond her established culinary success to showcase her personal style and passion for interior design.

### A Deeper Dive into "Paula Deen's Home"

The "Paula Deen's Home" collection represents a sophisticated range of home furnishings, drawing inspiration from Deen's diverse talents. Penny Burnett, Chief Merchandising Officer at EVINE Live, highlighted Deen's multifaceted abilities, stating, "While Paula Deen is celebrated as the 'Queen of Southern Cuisine,' her talents extend to painting, fashion, and a keen eye for home décor."

The collaborative process behind the collection was deeply personal. Burnett elaborated, "Our team collaborated closely with Paula, drawing inspiration from her art, personal style, and her Savannah, Georgia residence to create this collection. 'Paula Deen's Home' truly reflects her unique design philosophy, and we are thrilled to introduce it to her vast fan base."

Paula Deen herself shared her excitement, emphasizing the connection she feels with her audience. "My experience with EVINE Live and connecting directly with my fans has been incredibly rewarding," she said. "I approach style much like I approach cooking – it's about combining the right elements. A touch of color, a hint of texture, and a balance of comfort and elegance. I'm eager to share my passions for art, style, and design with EVINE Live's customers."

### The Debut Collection: Bedding and Beyond

The initial "Paula Deen's Home" collection will feature an array of elegant bedding. These designs are vibrantly inspired by Deen's artwork and her personal home aesthetic. During the premiere event, viewers can expect to see a variety of items, including:

*   Patterned and embroidered coverlet sets
*   Comforter sets
*   Embroidered bed sheets
*   Cotton blankets

Each piece is meticulously designed to infuse any bedroom with charm and comfort, reflecting Deen's unique approach to creating a welcoming living space. This expansion into home décor marks a significant evolution in Deen's brand presence with EVINE Live, demonstrating a broader creative vision that extends beyond her renowned culinary expertise.

### Accessing EVINE Live and Paula Deen's Collections

Viewers can access EVINE Live programming through multiple platforms, ensuring accessibility for a wide audience. This includes traditional cable and satellite television, dedicated mobile applications, and live streaming via the official website, www.evine.com.

The network is readily available on:

*   DIRECTV Channels 73 and 316
*   DISH Network channels 134 and 228

EVINE Live is also carried by major cable providers nationwide. For those seeking to locate EVINE Live in their specific area, a helpful resource can be found at bit.ly/1CNa450.

Additional details regarding Paula Deen's EVINE Live collections, including the new "Paula Deen's Home" line, are accessible at www.evine.com/PaulaDeen. General information about EVINE Live can be found at www.evine.com.

**About EVINE Live Inc.**

EVINE Live Inc. (NASDAQ:EVLV) operates as a digital commerce company. It delivers a diverse selection of proprietary and name-brand products through an engaging television, online, and mobile shopping experience. Reaching an estimated 88 million cable and satellite homes, EVINE Live provides 24-hour programming designed to foster community engagement with its customer base via a comprehensive digital shopping platform.

**About Paula Deen**

Paula Deen is a distinguished New York Times bestselling author, with over 11 million cookbooks sold. Her "Paula Deen Live!" tour, initiated in 2014, featured interactive cooking demonstrations and fan engagement. In the same year, she launched the "Paula Deen Network," an interactive digital platform offering exclusive content. Deen's culinary ventures also include The Lady & Sons restaurant in Savannah, Georgia, co-founded with her sons Jamie and Bobby Deen, and Paula Deen's Family Kitchen in Pigeon Forge, TN, which opened in April 2015. Her media footprint extends to the podcast "Get Cooking with Paula Deen" and the radio show "What's Cooking with Paula Deen." Her 2015 cookbook, "Paula Deen Cuts The Fat: 250 Favorite Recipes All Lightened Up," was released in September. In the fall of 2015, Deen participated in the twenty-first season of ABC's "Dancing with the Stars." She commands a substantial social media presence, with millions of likes and followers across Facebook, Twitter, and Pinterest. Paula Deen Ventures is headquartered in Savannah, Georgia, with additional offices in New York.

Paula Deen, a culinary icon renowned for her Southern comfort food and a former contestant on "Dancing With the Stars," has consistently pursued ambitious career paths. Her latest venture marks a delightful departure from the kitchen, stepping directly into the world of fashion with the launch of her brand-new clothing line, "Paula Deen's Closet." This highly anticipated collection recently debuted in collaboration with Evine, signaling a significant expansion of her established brand.

### A Long-Held Fashion Ambition

While this transition might seem unexpected, Deen asserts it has been a long-standing aspiration. "I've been trying to do this for years," Deen confided to PeopleStyle. "It was a little hard for me to break out of the kitchen because that’s how people think of me. But if you notice, I never cooked naked. I was always wearing clothes!" She further elaborated on the inspiration, noting, "One of my most asked questions out there — whether it’s in person, through the mail, or on Facebook — is ‘Paula, where did you get that top? Where did you get that outfit?’" These persistent inquiries underscored a clear demand and presented an avenue for Deen to engage with her audience on a novel platform.

### Designing for Real Bodies

Deen's vision for "Paula Deen's Closet" is deeply informed by her understanding of diverse female physiques. "I wanted to do something like this because I understand the body that’s not perfect," she stated. "You see all these things made for bananas. Well, I’m an apple. Most women are an apple or a pear. There are not many bananas." This core philosophy directly shaped the collection's design, featuring:

*   **Flattering Fits:** Tops, dresses, and pants are meticulously crafted to enhance various body shapes.
*   **Diverse Styles:** The line includes ombré knits, stretch maxis, and kimono sleeve tunics, all designed to celebrate individuality.

"When you get up over 30, most of us don’t have those perfect bodies anymore," Deen candidly remarked. "You have to accentuate your good points and try to camouflage your bad ones. I want to cover all body types. I am not trying to please just one person. I’m trying to please a multitude of people with this line. And I think people trust me. They know that I will not lie to them … To the best of my knowledge, I will tell the truth." This unwavering commitment to authenticity and inclusivity forms the bedrock of her brand, a sentiment echoed by fashion experts who emphasize the growing market for size-inclusive and body-positive apparel.

### Practical Design Elements

Deen meticulously prioritized several key elements during the development of "Paula Deen's Closet":

*   **Strategic Length:** Tops are designed to extend comfortably below the hips. "I don’t know how to say this to you in a nice way, but it has to cover my front privates," she explained. "I want my shirttail to cover my front privates and hopefully my back, too."
*   **Addressing Common Concerns:** The line tackles issues like what she humorously terms "catfish belly" arms. "Have you ever seen a catfish? You know how they have a smooth floppy belly on them? Well, that’s what we refer to as ‘catfish belly’ arms," she elaborated, showcasing her practical design approach. This focus on relatable design challenges has been praised by fashion commentators for its down-to-earth appeal.
*   **Modest Cleavage Coverage:** "When I’m doing V-necks, I want that placement perfect so you can lean over and you don’t have wardrobe malfunctions," Deen stated. "I don’t like when you show everything." This focus ensures confidence and comfort, a principle that aligns with the growing movement towards modest fashion.

### Value and Comfort at the Forefront

Affordability is another significant consideration, with every item priced under $60. This dedication to value mirrors her entrepreneurial origins. "Just like when I opened my very first restaurant, I told my sons, ‘We want to serve the very best meal that we’re capable of preparing at a fair price’," she recalled. "So everything I do must have value. People must feel like they are being treated fairly. I’m not interested in trying to gouge anybody." This commitment ensures accessibility for a broad customer base, a strategy that has proven successful for brands like Old Navy and Target, as noted by retail analysts.

Comfort is paramount, reflecting Deen's personal values. "I like to think that I’m comfortable to be around, and that definitely transfers into the clothing line," she said. "And at the stage I’m in, comfort is important. That’s definitely the way I feel about my shoes. I think so many young girls kill their feet with their shoes." This emphasis on comfort resonates with a broader trend in fashion, where practicality and well-being are increasingly prioritized, as highlighted in recent reports by the Fashion Institute of Technology.

### Navigating Modern Trends

Deen also expressed a degree of bewilderment regarding certain contemporary fashion trends, particularly those embraced by younger demographics. "I’m quite puzzled when I see a lot of things," she admitted. "There are a lot of funky styles out. I had to get used to the jeans with rips in them. But now I’m cool with it. I get it now. But that took me a while to understand why you’d want to wear things with holes on them."

However, she remains perplexed by the increasingly revealing styles prevalent on red carpets today. "Definitely [young people are] showing a lot more than when I was their age," she observed. "Do you think the Kardashians are somewhat responsible for that? I don’t know. I mean I don’t know them. But the role models are there that these girls have to follow, they want to be trendy too." This observation touches upon the influence of celebrity culture and social media on fashion choices, a topic frequently discussed by cultural critics and fashion journalists.

This digital expansion is complemented by her return to television on Evine Live, a prominent broadcast shopping network. This marks her comeback to the medium after her departure from the Food Network in 2013. On Evine Live, Deen is promoting her exclusive line of branded food products, positioning herself as a competitor to shopping giants like QVC and the Home Shopping Network (HSN).

A notable aspect of Deen's current public image is her significantly slimmer and healthier appearance. She has lost 35 pounds, a personal transformation that aligns with her ongoing management of Type 2 diabetes. Despite these personal changes, her dedication to the comforting flavors of Southern cooking remains central to her brand. Mark Bozek, CEO of Evine, expressed his excitement for Deen's return, telling FoxNews.com, "Deen’s team approached us with a fresh concept, and we were on board within five minutes. She possesses a large and exceptionally loyal fan base that is clearly eager for her return." This enthusiasm is reflected in her new culinary offerings, which include two versions of her Ooey Gooey Cake—classic butter and chocolate—along with two types of smoked sausages, premium holiday hams, savory glazes, and a line of chocolate candies. Notably, this candy line features a sugar-free option, broadening its appeal.

Among the unique items in Deen’s latest food collection are chocolates shaped like butter, an ingredient closely associated with her culinary identity. These confections are available in milk, dark, and white chocolate. Initially, there was some confusion about whether they were chocolate-flavored butters or a new Deen recipe. However, the Evine team has clarified that these are not classified as baker's chocolate. Despite practical considerations, the novelty of these butter-shaped chocolates is undeniable. This inclusion of conversation-starting products aligns well with Evine's business model, which also features unique items like seafood from the show "Deadliest Catch." Bozek further commented on Deen's broad appeal, stating, "Part of what she does is simply have fun—there's no doubt about it. We are in the business of entertaining customers. Having a personality that brings smiles to people's faces perfectly matches our mission." This approach highlights Deen's lasting ability to connect with consumers through her personality and distinctive, yet relatable, culinary offerings. The strategic alignment with Evine's entertainment-focused retail model, as highlighted by Bozek, underscores Deen's continued relevance and appeal in the contemporary marketplace. Her ability to adapt and innovate, as seen with the sugar-free candy option and the novelty butter chocolates, demonstrates a keen understanding of evolving consumer preferences while staying true to her Southern roots. This multifaceted comeback, encompassing digital engagement, television presence, and a diverse product line, solidifies Paula Deen's position as a resilient and influential force in the food industry.

Preparation Cream the butter, cream cheese, salt and pepper together with an electric mixer until smooth. Using a very small ice cream scoop, or melon baller, form 1-inch balls of butter mixture and arrange them on a parchment or waxed paper lined sheet pan. Freeze until solid. Coat the frozen balls in flour, egg and then bread crumbs and freeze again until solid. When ready to fry, preheat oil in a deep-fryer to 350°. Fry balls for 10 to 15 seconds until just light golden. Drain on paper towels before serving. Yields about 30 balls.

Bill Kristol, editor of The Weekly Standard, issued a pointed warning to the Romney campaign on Sunday, asserting that the presumptive Republican presidential nominee faces an uphill battle against President Barack Obama unless he can convince voters he possesses a concrete strategy for economic revitalization.

Kristol articulated his concerns during an appearance on "Fox News Sunday," stating, "President Obama had three disappointing months, but he's holding his own. And if I were in the Romney campaign, that would worry me." He underscored his point by referencing a recent Fox News poll, which revealed that a mere 41 percent of voters believe the incumbent president has a clear plan for economic improvement. While Kristol acknowledged these figures are not ideal for an incumbent facing a sluggish economy, he highlighted that the numbers for Romney were considerably more concerning.

The poll indicated that only 27 percent of voters believe Governor Romney has a clear plan to improve the economy, with 55 percent disagreeing. Kristol emphasized the critical nature of this disparity: "I don't think you can beat an incumbent president even if the economy’s slow if 27 percent of the voters think you as the challenger don't have a clear plan for improving the economy."

Key takeaways from Kristol's assessment include:

*   **Incumbent's Position:** President Obama, despite a slow economy, garners 41% support for his economic plan, a figure Kristol finds less than ideal but not insurmountable.
*   **Challenger's Hurdle:** Governor Romney's economic plan approval stands at a significantly lower 27%, a deficit Kristol deems too substantial to overcome an incumbent.
*   **Voter Perception:** The core issue, according to Kristol, is voter confidence in Romney's ability to present a clear and viable economic recovery strategy.

### O'Malley Criticizes Romney's Offshore Holdings and Job Creation Record

Maryland Governor Martin O'Malley sharply criticized Mitt Romney's financial practices on Sunday, arguing that a presidential candidate with offshore accounts, especially those in Swiss banks, demonstrates a lack of commitment to American jobs and infrastructure. Speaking on ABC's "This Week," O'Malley, who also heads the Democratic Governors Association, stated, "President Obama isn't running against the Almighty; he's running against the alternative."

O'Malley identified Mitt Romney as that alternative, pointing to Romney's time as governor. O'Malley characterized Romney's governorship as having the "47th worst job creation rate." Furthermore, O'Malley accused Romney of having a "penchant—a talent—for offshoring American jobs, sending them overseas and also maintaining offshore bank accounts in Bermuda, Swiss bank accounts."

#### Offshore Accounts and National Development

The governor emphasized his view that these financial strategies do not benefit the nation. "I’ve never known of a Swiss bank account to build an American bridge, a Swiss bank account to create American jobs, or Swiss bank accounts to rebuild the levies to protect the people of New Orleans," he declared. "That's not an economic strategy for moving our country forward."

#### Contrasting Priorities: Obama vs. Romney

O'Malley contrasted President Barack Obama's priorities with Romney's. O'Malley argued that while President Obama focuses on investing in education, infrastructure, and innovation, Romney's actions suggest his primary concern is safeguarding the wealth of affluent Americans. "He wants to make it even easier for very wealthy people to avoid paying taxes, to offshore American jobs, and to maintain their Swiss bank accounts while our roads and bridges and levies crumble," O'Malley concluded.

Obama campaign senior adviser Robert Gibbs expressed concerns on Sunday regarding the public's understanding of Mitt Romney's financial dealings, stating that Americans lack sufficient information to ascertain if any of Romney's activities are illegal. "We don't know. Nobody knows," Gibbs, a former White House press secretary, remarked on CNN's "State of the Union" when questioned about whether the presumptive Republican presidential nominee might be circumventing tax law.

Gibbs elaborated on CNN, urging Romney to provide greater transparency. "The one thing he can do, Candy, to clear up whether or not he's done anything illegally, whether he's shielding his income from taxes in Bermuda or Switzerland, is to do what every other presidential candidate has done and that's to release a series of years of their own tax returns," Gibbs told host Candy Crowley. He emphasized, "The best way to see if Mitt Romney is complying with American tax law is to have him release more of his tax returns."

While Romney has released his 2010 tax return and an estimate for his 2011 taxes, he has resisted releasing returns from other years. Gibbs drew a parallel to personal financial choices, stating, "I pick a bank because there's an ATM near my home." He then highlighted specific financial details, noting, "Romney had a bank account in Switzerland."

Further questioning the transparency of Romney's finances, Gibbs stated, "Nobody knows why he has a corporation in Bermuda." He also raised concerns about disclosure, asking, "Why he failed to disclose that on seven different financial disclosures. Why he transferred it to somebody else's purview the day before he became governor of Massachusetts." Gibbs concluded with a pointed critique of Romney's campaign message, remarking, "This is a guy whose slogan is 'Believe in America' and it should be 'business in Bermuda.'"

Read more about: Robert Gibbs, Mitt Romney, Tax Returns

I can’t figure it out, but the fact of the matter is, people are not better off today than they were three or four years ago, and the statistics bear it out,” Preibus said. “The fact of the matter is, is that there are almost half a million more people unemployed today than four years ago and if Debbie and President Obama were good on their promises on the stimulus -- remember, they said if we passed the trillion dollar stimulus we’d have 5.5 percent unemployment today -- what that means, if they kept promises, there would be eight-and-half million more people employed today than four years ago.” Priebus went on to accuse Democrats of “living in fantasy land” as he made a pitch for Mitt Romney, the presumed Republican presidential nominee. “This president can't fulfill a promise. They’re living in fantasy land, and you know what? We're going put this dream to an end of theirs in November and save America, fire Barack Obama and hire Mitt Romney," Priebus said.
