query: What happened to Vera Smith's body after Hurricane Katrina, and who took action to ensure she was buried?

documents.text: For five days after Hurricane Katrina struck, the body of Vera Smith lay where it fell, alone on the roadside, partly covered by a tarpaulin. Eventually – with the authorities apparently having neither the resources or inclination to remove the corpse – a local man buried her under a makeshift grave. He said prayers, fashioned a cross and placed a plastic sheet over the soil on to which someone painted the words: “Here Lies Vera. God Help Us”. Ten years after the storm that killed 1,833 people and whose destruction topped $100bn, the death of 66-year Vera Smith and the utter break-down of authority symbolised by her abandonment, continue to haunt the city. There are some in New Orleans – many of whose residents have an intense relationship with the dead – who literally believe they have felt her spirit. Vera Smith, in an undated photograph provided by her family Visiting the junction of Magazine Street and Jackson Avenue in the Garden District today, it is hard to imagine the scenes that played out ten years ago in the chaotic aftermath of the storm, when Ms Smith had gone to a local shop to try and find supplies for her and her partner, Max Keene. Across the US and around the world, people watched stunned and outraged as the media carried images of the seemingly countless corpses left stranded by the storm. They lay rotting on the roadside, floating in floodwater, hanging from wires. The US government appeared both hapless and heartless, failing to come to the aid of some of its most most vulnerable people, a large percentage of whom were black. Television viewers could not believe that what they were watching was happening in their country, rather than somewhere in the distant developing world. President Barack Obama, who will this week visit the city to mark the anniversary, said there had been a “shameful breakdown in government.” More than 1,800 were killed by Katrina and the flooding it triggered Ms Smith did not die in the actual storm but was killed in the lawless aftermath, apparently the victim of a hit and run accident, the perpetrator of which was never found. Her frail partner – who passed away the following year - did the little he could to provide some dignity to her body. After five days of intense heat, someone decided more serious was required. That person was John Lee, an engineer and businessman who has a home in the Garden District. Over a bottle of root beer in Stein’s Deli, located on Magazine Street, Mr Lee said he had gone to the local police station and asked them to remove her body. Mr Lee said they not only declined to do so, but also refused him permission to move it. Eventually, they said he could bury it but could not move it. As a result, he dug the grave on the actual pavement. Mr Lee has written a book of his experiences, Our Sleepless Nights: Surviving Katrina and Burying Miss Vera. In it he details the break-down of authority and the looming fear that permeated – some of it exacerbated by inaccurate reports about looting – in the ten days after Katrina. But his book is also testimony to the acts of quiet heroism that took place, the coming together of people, often strangers, to try and help one another. John Lee led efforts to build the makeshift grave for Vera Smith “I think of her often,” Mr Lee said of Ms Smith, whom he used to see around the neighbourhood. “I do have faith. I do wish she is at rest.” He said he remembered the prayers that he and two black women – people he had met that day – spoke as they buried her. “We were praying for her soul.” Ms Smith, whose full name was Elvira Briones Smith, was born in Mexico and grew in Texas before she moved to New Orleans. She was married four time has two adult daughters. For 20 years or more she lived with CN Keene, better known as Max. She liked clothes and shoes, shopping and drinking, and was a regular at the local Catholic church, St Mary’s Assumption. They had met when she was working as a waitress in a bar and he was working off-shore for an oil company. “She was married, her old man left her. I had a different girlfriend then, she left me. It was the right time. We just got together. Every now and then it happens that way,” Mr Keene told The Independent ten years ago. “We used to lie in bed. I’d drink bourbon, she’d read books.” Large parts of New Orleans were inundated by flooding Ms Smith’s family learned of her death from the front page of a Texas newspaper. Three months after Katrina, Ms Smith’s body was eventually collected, taken to a mortuary and her ashes returned to her family in Santa Rosa. In November 2005, they were interred in a family plot. Ms Smith’s daughter, Cindy Briones, said her mother had returned to visit the family every year for Thanksgiving Day. She said she was also delighted to be among her relatives and spend weeks in advance of her visit buying special gifts for each of them. Speaking from Santa Rosa, she said she was grateful to Mr Lee and the others who had built the makeshift grave, but said it had been important for the family to have her returned to Texas. She said she and her sister remained traumatised by what happened to their mother and the their emotions had been stretched every year on the anniversary of her death. “I still have flash-backs. I have seen her body on the internet, pictures of before it was buried,” she said. This image - believed to be of Vera Smith - was left at the grave In some limited respects, Ms Smith’s family was fortunate in that they were able to locate and recover her body. Even today, some of those killed by the storm remain unidentified and unclaimed. Asked what officials had used to identify the remains, the office of Jeffrey Rouse, the coroner for New Orleans, said their work made use of dental records, DNA testing, plane manifests, unique scars and tattoos, follow-ups on missing persons cases, jewellery, unusual hair, finger-prints, medical implants and government identification. In the City Park neighbourhood, a memorial to Katrina, designed in the shape that the Category Five storm assumed as it made landfall, was erected in the memory of those who died. Even today, the remains of 30 people who died during Katrina have not been identified Despite the effort of the coroner’s office, the remains of 30 people were not identified while a total of 43 were never claimed. “The memorial received the remains of the unidentified or unclaimed,” read the words on a black granite stone. Jayson Orlando, who works at the nearby Sacred Grinds coffee shop, said New Orleans was less care-free since Katrina hit. “Even cities feel trauma,” he said. “It’s not just people.” Since 2005, several memorials have also been erected in the memory of Ms Smith. The one that currently stands attached to a wall was built by an artist, Simon Hardeveld, who is originally French, and metal worker Scott Evert. Mr Hardeveld, whose memorial features a working fountain and bright colours, said he had been asked to make it two years ago by the owner of an adjoining burger restaurant, built on the site where Ms Smith’s grave had stood. He had contacted him amid reports that the restaurant was haunted. The reports were taken so seriously that a local television channel dispatched a film crew. “This is New Orleans. People believe these things,” he said with a shrug. Blaine Presenbach built a restaurant on the plot where the makeshift grave had stood Blaine Presenbach, 36, the owner of the Charcoal restaurant, said some of his workers had joked after the “ghost of Vera” when a piece of equipment broke down. But he said nobody had really believed it. Rather, he had commissioned the memorial out of respect for Ms Smith and what had befallen her at that location. “We don’t want to forget it,” he said. Mr Evert, the man who did the metal work for the memorial, heard the stories too and was unsure what to think. And then he set about the task of shaping the memorial. “It was weird. I don’t really believe this sort of thing,” he said. “But the job became really tedious and difficult.” He said that each point in the job, something went wrong. Each star – something that would normally attach simply – fell off. “It seemed to me she did not like what I was doing,” he said. Simon Hardeveld and Scott Evert at the memorial they built Mr Evert said he too felt the trauma of having lived through the storm, having used a boat to help rescue stranded people and remembering the terrible scenes. Even now it upset him to recall what happened to his city, to people such as Ms Smith. He likened the memorial built by himself to small shrines he had seen when he visited Thailand, a place where a spirit might sit. “The point we wanted to make was that we wanted to remember Vera,” he added. “It was just to say ‘this was someone who lived here and something terrible happened to her and people think she was worth remembering’.”

Hurricane Katrina, a devastating Category 5 storm, is often remembered as a moment of national unity. However, a deeper look reveals a more complex story. The catastrophe exposed significant governmental failures and deep-seated inequalities, making it a symbol of dysfunction rather than just a natural disaster. The storm, which killed over 1,800 people and displaced hundreds of thousands, did not affect everyone the same way. Instead, it highlighted how race, economic status, and location shaped people's experiences. The disaster continues to be remembered differently by Black and White Americans, a difference rooted in their distinct experiences during and after the storm.

The way people perceived Katrina was heavily influenced by race. Many White Americans saw it as bad luck and government incompetence. Black Americans, however, viewed it as a clear sign of indifference to Black lives. This sentiment was famously captured by Kanye West's statement, "George Bush doesn't care about black people." Sociologists noted that Katrina became a social disaster, revealing the nation at its worst, especially for Black Americans who saw harrowing images of suffering. This experience led to a profound shift towards racial pessimism in Black America, a feeling that continues to fuel movements like "Black Lives Matter."

Several factors widened this perceptual divide. Media coverage played a significant role. Studies showed that Black survivors were often labeled "refugees," and exaggerated reports of violence and crime were common. For example, a Black man carrying soda was labeled "looting," while a White couple doing the same was described as "finding bread and soda." Conservative commentators often amplified these differences. Research also indicated that White respondents were more likely to blame residents and the mayor for being trapped, while Black respondents saw those taking supplies as desperate survivors. Right-wing media outlets frequently expressed disdain for those left behind. Polls revealed that Black Americans strongly believed the response would have been faster and better if the victims had been wealthier and White. Then-Mayor Ray Nagin echoed this, stating a similar event in a wealthier, Whiter area would have received a different governmental response. Academic research confirms this heightened, racialized response, finding that Black Americans across the country identified with the primarily African American victims and experienced deep anger and depression.

This difference in perception might have been different if more White Americans had recognized the racial bias. However, many dismissed claims of bias, believing the government response would have been the same regardless of race. In contrast, a large majority of Black Americans believed the response highlighted racial inequality. This disparity led to a significant decline in Black racial optimism. Data showed a marked drop in Black Americans' perception of race relations in the years following Katrina. Surveys indicated that the period after Katrina was a turning point, with more Black Americans reporting their lives had worsened. Black optimism continued to decline for three years, and overall satisfaction with society dipped significantly. While the election of Barack Obama temporarily boosted Black optimism, this proved to be short-lived. Recent events have led to a renewed sense of pessimism among Black Americans regarding their place in American life, with views on race relations returning to pre-Obama, post-Katrina levels of pessimism.

## The "Katrina Effect": How a Catastrophe Reshaped New Orleans Education

Hurricane Katrina's landfall in 2005 devastated New Orleans. Beyond the physical destruction, the storm triggered a massive transformation in public education, known as the "Katrina effect." This change arose because the storm destroyed the city's schools, forcing closures and displacing families.

Even before Katrina, New Orleans schools were underperforming. In 2003, the Louisiana Legislature created the Recovery School District (RSD) to improve failing schools. By the time Katrina hit, only five New Orleans schools were charter schools under the RSD.

### Reimagining Education After the Storm

Instead of rebuilding the old system, leaders decided to create a new public school system. Over 100 failing schools were transferred to the RSD. Traditional schools closed, and their staff were let go. A large network of new charter schools replaced them, creating a "portfolio" model. Today, 92 percent of New Orleans students attend charter schools. This reform is one of the most significant in American history, documented by the Louisiana Department of Education, Tulane University's Cowen Institute, and the Education Research Alliance for New Orleans.

### Improved Academic Outcomes

New Orleans public school students have shown significant academic improvement since Katrina. Before the storm, in 2005, New Orleans was the second-lowest-performing district in Louisiana. Now, students are closing the achievement gap, with higher graduation rates and better test scores. Research from the New Orleans Education Equity Project and the RAND Corporation highlights these gains.

In 2008, only 30 percent of RSD schools were not failing. By 2014, over 80 percent were performing above failing status. This shows what systemic change can achieve. The storm led to a model for transforming failing schools, something previous efforts couldn't do.

Education policy expert Dr. Paul Hill notes that New Orleans offers lessons for other cities. The key takeaway is that school transformation shouldn't require a disaster. Instead, it needs a proactive commitment to educational excellence, as promoted by organizations like the National Association of Charter School Authorizers.

A decade after Hurricane Katrina's devastation, a significant portion of the estimated 250,000 evacuees who found refuge in Houston have established permanent homes in the Texas city. Up to 100,000 individuals likely remain, marking what some have described as the largest climate-driven migration in the United States since the Dust Bowl of the 1930s. This enduring presence highlights the profound impact of the storm and the subsequent resettlement patterns.

Hurricane Katrina displaced over a million people, with many never returning to New Orleans. While evacuees scattered to cities like Baton Rouge, Birmingham, San Antonio, Dallas, and Atlanta, Houston, a major Gulf Coast city familiar with hurricanes, became the primary destination for the largest number of those fleeing the storm's epicenter, located approximately 350 miles away.

As New Orleans marked the 10th anniversary of Hurricane Katrina, a substantial number of its former residents were absent. Tens of thousands traded the unique cultural landscape and historic charm of New Orleans for the sprawling, car-dependent urban environment of Houston, a metropolitan area that has grown considerably since the storm.

Estimates suggest that of the quarter-million evacuees who arrived in Houston, as many as 100,000 have established permanent residency. Mtangulizi Sanyika, an academic who relocated to Houston after his New Orleans home flooded and whose wife secured employment at a local hospital, aptly described Houston as "New Orleans West." Sanyika now chairs the New Orleans Association of Houston, an organization planning commemorative events.

New Orleans Mayor Mitch Landrieu has actively engaged with displaced residents in cities like Atlanta and Houston. He has expressed gratitude for the host cities' hospitality and highlighted recovery efforts in his hometown. As noted by NPR, Mayor Landrieu reportedly told former residents during a church service, "Y'all can come home whenever y'all want." This invitation comes as New Orleans itself experienced a significant population decline. Between 2000 and 2006, the city's population decreased by more than half. Its current population stands at approximately 384,000, representing 79% of its pre-Katrina numbers. Notably, the African American community saw a decline of about 30%, or roughly 97,000 individuals.

While New Orleans has demonstrated impressive resilience in certain aspects, economic factors present a significant barrier to return for many former residents. According to the Texas Organizing Project, a community advocacy group, the unemployment rate for Black Texans was 9.5%. A 2011 study by Loyola University revealed that 52% of working-age Black men in New Orleans were unemployed. Furthermore, escalating housing costs have rendered downtown living unaffordable for many low-income individuals.

Professor Carl Lindahl of the University of Houston identified two primary demographic groups among the displaced who were less likely to return:

*   **Parents of young children:** They perceived Houston as offering greater safety and superior educational opportunities.
*   **The elderly:** They felt New Orleans lacked adequate social services.

In the immediate aftermath of the storm, the Astrodome, Houston's renowned indoor sports arena, served as a critical shelter, receiving evacuees transported from the Superdome. This period saw an outpouring of community support, with many Houstonians donating time, money, and goods. "The initial arrival brought out the best in the city," Lindahl observed, noting that this spirit of generosity extended for approximately two months. However, within a year, a narrative emerged linking New Orleanians to crime and a purported surge in murders. Lindahl also recounted instances where individuals faced job discrimination due to their New Orleans 504 cellphone area code.

*Texas Monthly* reported that twelve months post-disaster, at a Houston church gathering, hundreds of white residents demanded the mayor expel "Katrina illegal immigrants" back to Louisiana. Fueled by initial police reports and accounts of looting and violence in the chaotic post-hurricane environment, it took years for this fear-mongering to be dispelled by factual data.

In 2005, Lindahl co-founded a project where survivors shared their personal narratives. When asked about their motivations for participating, interviewees cited therapy as a benefit, while some received payment. However, Lindahl noted, "the most prevalent answer was: ‘We want people to know who we are’."

Despite being dispersed across Houston's vast landscape, the displaced New Orleanians maintained connections through their shared culture, as described by evacuee and photographer Dallas McNamara. "Things like music allowed people to get together," she explained, noting the formation of bands, the establishment of New Orleans-based church branches, and the opening of restaurants. McNamara added, "I think people are kind of surprised by how much they like Houston. They have a nicer home, they like the schools. They’re blown away by the amount of driving that they do but they tend to become pleasantly surprised." Nevertheless, she expressed missing the ingrained politeness of New Orleans and observed that Houston has "more rules."

For Sanyika, the primary drawbacks of Houston for many New Orleanians are transportation and the distinct culinary scene. "The other is the food. It’s a very different kind of taste," he remarked, humorously noting that "A Texas gumbo doesn’t taste quite the same." He misses the organic, "bottom-up" cultural expression characteristic of New Orleans, particularly from its streets, neighborhoods, and working-class residents. Despite these sentiments, he expressed contentment in Houston, stating, "You never lose your cultural heritage and roots, you simply learn to integrate them in whatever environment you find yourself in." The 73-year-old continues to visit New Orleans regularly, acknowledging a persistent sadness upon departure. "New Orleans is in your soul, your heart, your roots, it anchors who you are and you take it with you wherever you go," he concluded.

The narrative of educational reform in New Orleans, particularly following Hurricane Katrina, often glosses over persistent challenges. A significant issue highlighted by the Education Research Alliance is the prevalent practice of "creaming," where school principals strategically select or encourage students with higher expected standardized test scores to remain, while those with lower scores are often counseled out. The alliance's forthcoming research is expected to further demonstrate that students with the lowest scores are less likely to transition to higher-performing schools.

This disconnect between reform rhetoric and reality is stark. For instance, Paul G. Vallas, who led the Recovery School District from 2007 to 2011, recently claimed that only 7 percent of the city's students attend failing schools, a dramatic decrease from 62 percent pre-Katrina, asserting this was achieved "with no displacement of children." However, this assertion is demonstrably false.

Consider Joseph S. Clark Preparatory High School, one of the last traditional public schools to be converted. When it reopened under new management in the fall of 2011, a substantial majority of its 366 students did not re-enroll. During its inaugural year under the charter management organization FirstLine, Clark retained only 117 students, according to a study by Stanford University's Center for Research on Education Outcomes (Credo). FirstLine could not provide details on the whereabouts of the students who departed. Jay Altman, FirstLine's chief executive, stated via email that prior to FirstLine's takeover, a similarly low proportion of students, around 35 percent, were returning. The school district did not respond to inquiries regarding Clark.

A contributing factor to this lack of accountability is the decentralized nature of the charter school system, where no single agency is tasked with tracking all students. Two years prior, the Recovery School District, expressing concern over high school attrition, began assigning counselors to assist in relocating students from schools slated for closure.

Louisiana's official dropout rates are considered unreliable. However, a recent report by Measure of America, a project of the Social Science Research Council, utilizing 2013 Census Bureau survey data, found that over 26,000 individuals in the metropolitan area, aged 16 to 24, are classified as "disconnected" – neither employed nor enrolled in school.

Ironically, schools like Clark can inadvertently bolster the New Orleans success narrative. When underperforming schools are taken over, their "F" grades are automatically converted to a "T" for "turnaround." Consequently, in the 2013-14 school year, schools with "T" grades, along with the 16 schools receiving a "D," were not counted as "failing." Approximately 40 percent of Recovery School District schools received a "D," "T," or "F" grade that year.

Further complicating the assessment of the New Orleans educational experiment is the selective dissemination of Louisiana's education data, often favoring pro-charter researchers, and the prevalence of flawed research. Last fall, the Cowen Institute for Public Education Initiatives was compelled to retract a study that claimed most New Orleans schools were achieving higher-than-expected graduation rates and test scores. Similarly, a study released last spring by Credo, examining 41 urban charter districts including New Orleans, purported to show charter school outperformance compared to urban public schools on standardized tests. However, this study was also deeply flawed. Its methodology relied on comparing each charter student to a virtual "twin," a composite of up to seven public school students with matching demographics and test scores from "feeder" schools. The critical issue in New Orleans is the near absence of local feeder schools from which to draw these comparisons.

The persistent tendrils of cat's claw vines cascade from the rooftop of a derelict dwelling, aggressively reclaiming what remains of a once-proud Victorian cottage in the Tremé neighborhood. This skeletal ruin stands as a stark testament to Hurricane Katrina's fury, yet the oppressive humidity and the ominous rumble of an approaching thunderstorm on this particular day signify not a few months or even years post-storm, but a full decade. In July, I accompanied David Spielman, a seasoned freelance photographer in his mid-sixties with piercing blue eyes, through neighborhoods still bearing the brutal scars of the hurricane. Our vehicle navigated roads so fractured and potholed they seemed poised to shatter axles and wreak havoc on suspension systems. Spielman, who has meticulously documented the post-Katrina landscape since the storm's landfall, has amassed over 10,000 images. "This is a struggle that many, many areas of the city will continue to endure for a very long time," he observed. His photographic chronicle powerfully illustrates the devastation wrought by Katrina and the city's struggle to rebuild, despite billions in aid, substantial insurance payouts, numerous success narratives, and a pervasive spirit of goodwill.

On either side of these ravaged thoroughfares lie vast expanses of untamed vegetation, punctuated only by the fractured concrete driveways or slabs that hint at the homes that once graced these sites. In the Press Park area, the skeletal remains of brick and concrete townhomes, originally constructed by the city, still stand. Their rendering uninhabitable by Katrina represented a devastating second blow to residents, as these homes were unfortunately situated atop a toxic landfill. To date, they have received no restitution from municipal authorities. Chain-link fences now cordon off the remnants, graffiti mars every available surface, and stray dogs seek refuge from the sun. An elementary school stands as a silent, empty monument. Yet, interspersed among this desolation, one occasionally encounters clusters of meticulously maintained, one-story homes on Press Park's periphery. These residences, huddled together with manicured lawns and predominantly late-model vehicles, are not elevated, leaving them perpetually vulnerable to future inundation. This is not a neighborhood in the throes of recovery; it is a community staggering under the weight of its enduring casualty status. Spielman has dedicated himself to ensuring this facet of the hurricane's legacy remains unforgotten, even as the city actively promotes its recovery narrative around the 10th anniversary.

Spielman's photographic oeuvre, spanning from 2005 to 2015, captures scenes of widespread destruction across virtually every district. Image after image reveals empty, decaying structures: residences, educational institutions, commercial establishments, places of worship, and healthcare facilities. This collection, a profound visual testament to the storm's aftermath, is now compiled in Spielman's recent publication, *The Katrina Decade: Story of an Altered City*. "One of the critical things I wanted to show is that it happened everywhere," Spielman stated. "I have just a few pictures of the Lower 9th Ward because so many people became convinced that that was the only place that the damage was located. The enormity is something that everyone is overwhelmed with once they get out of the French Quarter. This was equal-opportunity flooding."

### The Eerie Silence

In late August 2005, Hurricane Katrina, a colossal and foreboding force, bore down on the Gulf Coast. Shortly after 10 a.m. on the 28th, the National Weather Service issued a dire warning: most of the affected area would be uninhabitable, even deadly, for weeks. The majority of New Orleans residents who possessed the means to evacuate did so. Spielman, however, chose to remain. Having weathered numerous hurricanes during his three decades in the city, the Tulsa, Oklahoma native had been drawn to New Orleans' vibrant culture in the early 1970s, a period of significant urban expansion following years of stagnation. The city was experiencing a renaissance of its unique heritage in music, cuisine, rituals, art, and architecture. While oil wealth initially fueled this boom, a sharp decline in fuel prices in the mid-1980s led to an economic downturn that persisted for approximately twenty years until Katrina's arrival.

On that fateful August day, Spielman assisted a small community of nuns, the Poor Clare Sisters, in their storm preparations. They were long-time friends and neighbors in New Orleans' Uptown district, an area typically spared the worst of hurricane flooding due to its elevation of two feet above sea level. He secured the third-story windows of their century-old monastery, a robust brick edifice with eighteen-inch-thick walls and heavy cypress shutters. When the eight nuns invited him to shelter with them, he accepted, believing the structure offered safety. Late that night, amidst the hurricane's peak intensity and a complete power outage, the building shuddered, and the shutters rattled in the profound darkness. At one point, the occupants heard a deafening crack as a sixty-foot pine tree snapped like a twig. The following morning, upon opening the convent doors, they were met with a scene of widespread debris: shingles, shattered glass, fallen tree limbs, and a tangled mass of power lines resembling Silly String. Spielman's initial thought was, "Oh boy, this is going to be 10 days of hell, no power, intense heat, and a lot of physical labor helping people clean up."

He ventured out on foot, noting an unnerving silence, devoid of even the sounds of birds or insects. "I was out running checking on some friends’ houses,” he recalled, “trying to get a lay of the land.” A small group of individuals approached him, seeking guidance on departing the city. They reported witnessing looting, and when a photographer present attempted to document the scene, gunfire erupted. This was his first indication of the escalating chaos. Communications, including cell service, were non-operational, leaving residents unaware that a critical levee along the Industrial Canal, connecting the Mississippi River to Lake Pontchartrain, had been breached. Tragically, at least fifty more levees were on the verge of failure.

### Whisked Away to the Morgue

Later that day, Spielman observed police officers in patrol cars passively witnessing the ransacking of stores, and in some instances, participating in the pilfering of goods. It appeared to him that law enforcement had effectively surrendered. "It dawned on me that this was going to be a devastatingly difficult period, so I started thinking about how I could get the nuns out," he explained. They agreed to relocate to a convent in Texas. "One of the last things I told them was please do not stop and help anyone. They said they would be very careful, and drove away, not knowing if they would ever see the place again." Spielman remained alone and disoriented within the deserted monastery, contemplating how to document the unfolding tragedy. "And that’s when I started putting together my plan—to approach it in the way the WPA photographers of the Dust Bowl and the Depression went about their business." He resolved to adopt a long-term perspective, remaining with the story long after the national media's attention waned. "It was very important for me to find the images that would be really telling and different," he stated. His objective was to maintain neutrality, "looking only for the most powerful and complete images that didn’t need a caption or explanation."

Spielman endured forty days without electricity amidst sweltering heat. Gasoline became a scarce commodity, making those who possessed it targets of potential attack. The widespread wreckage transformed streets into a labyrinth, necessitating constant detours and backtracking for any vehicular movement. Armed with his Leica camera, he traversed the diverse neighborhoods that constitute New Orleans' residential fabric: Central City, Tremé, Press Park, Bywater, and the Seventh Ward. These areas were largely deserted, their inhabitants having been evacuated to cities like Houston or Atlanta, or, in nearly 700 tragic instances, to the morgue. "Right after the storm, I felt so inadequate as a photographer," Spielman admitted. "Standing in any of the locations there were images right in front of me, more images to the left and right of me and dozens of images behind me. What warranted being shot? How could one photographer handle this once-in-a-lifetime event?"

It was only when Spielman joined a rescue helicopter mission that the true, staggering scale of the disaster became apparent. "It’s something that even I still have trouble grasping. We were flying over parts of the city that I had been to hundreds and hundreds of times and I couldn’t recognize them. Stores, landmarks, neighborhoods were gone—underwater." Entire sections of the city had been obliterated. Within a mere few days, New Orleans' population plummeted from over 485,000 to approximately 230,000, as victims caught in the destruction evacuated. The older, more affluent, and historically renowned districts, including the French Quarter and the Garden District, were largely spared, as were certain elevated areas along the Mississippi River.

Today, visitors to New Orleans encounter the same vibrant architecture and verdant gardens for which the city is celebrated. However, Jack Davis, a journalist and preservationist, describes the French Quarter as a mere "set." He elaborates, "There is the New Orleans that has been rebuilt and proudly displayed for the world, and those parts look better than they did before the storm, but that’s probably less than half of the city’s territory."

### Forever Diminished

As the hurricane's 10th anniversary approached, New Orleans embarked on an aggressive marketing campaign to highlight its recovery successes. New industries, such as technology firms and feature film production, are contributing significant tax revenue. Tourism is robust, with more restaurants operating now than prior to Katrina. The billions of dollars in

## New Orleans: A Decade After Katrina, Progress and Persistent Inequality

Ten years after Hurricane Katrina's devastating floodwaters receded, James Gray returned to the shell of his New Orleans home. The few salvaged belongings he could fit into a handbag were a stark reminder of the loss. Yet, he and his wife have since meticulously restored their New Orleans East residence, a home they've cherished for over two decades. Many of their neighbors have also returned, contributing to a palpable sense of rebirth in a district historically home to a significant portion of the city's black middle class.

Gray, now a City Council representative for the neighborhood, points to tangible signs of progress: a new, state-of-the-art hospital opened last year, schools are either newly constructed or undergoing renovation, and national retailers like Wal-Mart and CVS have re-established their presence after years of absence. "All of those things are bigger, prettier, shinier than what we had before the flood," Gray remarked. "I think we suffered a lot, [but] I think we have recovered far better than any of the experts thought we would recover."

### The Complex Economic Landscape

However, the improvements in Gray's neighborhood do not encapsulate the complete narrative of New Orleans in the decade since its levees failed. The economic landscape presents a more complex picture, particularly concerning the black middle class, a cornerstone of the city's cultural identity. While New Orleans's economy is arguably stronger today than before the storm, its remarkable recovery has, to a concerning degree, bypassed many African-Americans, who still constitute the majority of the population.

Data reveals a troubling trend: Black New Orleanians are less likely to be employed than before the 2005 storm and are more prone to living in poverty. Black household incomes, when adjusted for inflation, have declined, and the income disparity between black and white residents has widened. Erika McConduit-Diggs, president of the Urban League of Greater New Orleans, noted, "A lot of people see a lot of shiny new buildings, new streets, renovated airports, new hospitals and things like that, and that’s tremendous progress. But when you start peeling back that onion and getting to the heart of that challenge … you see that we have not done that well."

New Orleans has transformed over the past decade, emerging from destruction and rebirth as a smaller, wealthier, and more diverse city, yet also one marked by increased inequality. While many residents, both black and white, express pride in the revitalized tourism sector, the ambitious school system overhaul, and the influx of educated, socially conscious young professionals driving entrepreneurship, concerns linger about rising rents, gentrification, and the erosion of the city's unique culture.

### Demographic Shifts and the Black Middle Class

These shifts are inextricably linked to race. In the year following Katrina, over 175,000 black residents departed New Orleans, with more than 75,000 choosing not to return. Concurrently, the non-Hispanic white population has nearly rebounded to its pre-storm numbers, and the Hispanic population has grown by over 30 percent. These demographic changes have reduced the African-American share of the population to 59 percent in 2013, down from 66 percent in 2005.

Beyond sheer numbers, the economic standing of African-Americans within the city has fundamentally altered. While black residents have historically comprised the majority of the city's poor, they also previously formed a majority of the middle class and were well-represented in professional fields. Post-Katrina, this pattern has reversed: the poor remain overwhelmingly black, but the affluent and middle classes are increasingly white. Furthermore, the existing black middle class is aging. Many middle-class African-Americans who returned were retired or nearing retirement, while younger black professionals sought better opportunities elsewhere.

This demographic shift in the black middle class contrasts sharply with the influx of young, educated, and predominantly white professionals who have reshaped the city. Between 2011 and 2013 alone, New Orleans welcomed nearly 10,000 college graduates under the age of 40. Many were drawn by a burgeoning startup scene that emerged in the wake of the disaster, positioning New Orleans as a national hub for entrepreneurship. The city has become a particular draw for "social entrepreneurs" applying tech-savvy, business-oriented solutions to issues ranging from failing schools to food deserts.

Propeller, a business accelerator focused on socially oriented startups, originated from spontaneous neighborhood volunteer efforts post-Katrina. Andrea Chen, Propeller's co-founder and executive director, explained their focus on projects that address community needs, including those of the black community. "We were inspired by a lot of the grass-roots activity that happened right after the storm," Chen stated. "We saw people who were regular people … who couldn’t turn their backs on what was happening in their own neighborhood."

### Gentrification and Housing Affordability

However, the arrival of these newcomers has contributed to gentrification, intensifying pressure on the already strained black middle class. Housing costs in some New Orleans areas now rival those in expensive coastal cities like Boston and New York, despite significantly lower average incomes. According to The Data Center, a New Orleans research group, the city ranks among the worst for housing affordability, with 37 percent of renters dedicating over half their pre-tax income to rent and utilities.

"The squeeze is on in a big way," commented LaToya Cantrell, a City Council member representing areas heavily impacted by gentrification. She expressed concern that rising rents could displace artists and musicians vital to New Orleans's culture and tourism, while also eroding the tradition of multi-generational black families living in close proximity. "I do worry about people who live here and are from here and have been here for generations but don’t feel like they can live in their neighborhoods any longer," she said. "It’s not just about the lower-income, it’s across the board."

### Local Initiatives and Persistent Challenges

Not all young entrepreneurs are external gentrifiers. Larry Irvin, CEO of the education startup Brothers Empowered to Teach, is a New Orleans native. His family, displaced by the storm, found refuge in his small apartment, an experience that instilled resilience and ultimately inspired his return to help rebuild New Orleans. "I think subconsciously following graduation, I had a conversation with myself," Irvin shared. "If none of the natives are hanging around, what good is that going to do?"

Brothers Empowered to Teach, a three-year-old nonprofit, seeks to recruit black men into teaching, aiming to provide positive role models for black boys. Currently, only 3 percent of New Orleans teachers are black men, according to Irvin. He and co-founder Kristyna Jones emphasize the critical need for black men to help dismantle the "school-to-prison pipeline" that disproportionately affects young African-Americans. "We can show children that there are people who look like them who are in positions of power who want to help them," Jones stated. "We need students to see more people who look like them telling them that they can succeed."

Irvin and Jones view their organization as a step toward revitalizing the city's black middle class. Despite his sense of purpose, Irvin observes little change since the storm. The murder rate remains alarmingly high, and opportunities for well-paying jobs remain scarce. A staggering 35 percent of black men under 25 in New Orleans are neither employed nor in school, compared to 5 percent of their white counterparts. For many, Irvin noted, achieving a better life still necessitates leaving town. "With regards to the aspirations and ambitions and forecasts for the young coming up, is it much different now than it was pre-Katrina? No," he concluded. "I think they’re still trying to get to Atlanta and New York."

New Orleans leaders consistently emphasize that Katrina exposed pre-existing problems rather than creating them. High crime rates, a dysfunctional school system, and a lack of viable job opportunities had been driving middle-class residents away long before the storm. City Council member Gray acknowledged, "New Orleans East and all of New Orleans was in decline when looked at by several measures before the flood."

### Widening Economic Disparities

While the post-Katrina resurgence has benefited the white middle class, the erosion of the black middle class has persisted or even accelerated. The median black household earned just over $25,000 in 2013, a $5,000 decrease from 2000 after inflation adjustment. In contrast, the median white household earned over $60,000, nearly two and a half times as much. These diverging economic paths are partly attributable to decades of inequality and segregation. Traditionally white neighborhoods, situated on higher ground, fared better during the storm. Black neighborhoods, even those considered affluent, were often located in low-lying areas that sustained more severe damage. Furthermore, as is common across the U.S., even affluent African-Americans possessed less accumulated wealth to facilitate a rapid recovery compared to their white counterparts. The 2008-2009 recession further exacerbated these disparities, disproportionately impacting African-American families nationwide.

Experts also highlight policy decisions that hindered the black middle class's recovery. As author Gary Rivlin notes,

The devastating events of 2005, when levees failed and floodwaters engulfed New Orleans, leaving residents stranded on rooftops, in the Superdome, the Convention Center, and on the interstate, serve as a stark reminder of the critical need for preparedness. To prevent such a tragedy from recurring, a comprehensive plan is now in place, uniting the deep compassion of everyday New Orleans residents with robust government planning. This initiative, spearheaded by Evacuates.org, aims to ensure that every individual has a safe and timely means of evacuation.

Experts estimate that as many as 40,000 people may require assistance during the next major hurricane. In response, over 500 New Orleanians have pledged their commitment to this vital cause, working diligently to guarantee that no one is left behind again. This collective effort, drawing on the spirit of community and the lessons learned from past failures, is actively seeking volunteers ready to contribute to this crucial mission.

query: What specific concerns do the Standing Rock Sioux Tribe have regarding the potential impact of the Dakota Access Pipeline on their water supply?

documents.text:
The Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL) has ignited a national conversation, extending far beyond its immediate geographical impact. While not directly related to border construction, this massive infrastructure project, designed to transport oil across four Midwestern states, has become a focal point for environmental justice concerns, disproportionately affecting minority communities.

The DAPL's proposed route begins in North Dakota and traverses South Dakota and Iowa before reaching its terminus in Illinois. The core of the controversy lies in the pipeline's potential to contaminate water resources, a concern that has fueled months of sustained protests by the Standing Rock Sioux Tribe and their allies. This situation is not unprecedented; the public outcry and eventual cancellation of the Keystone XL Pipeline, which also faced significant opposition from Native American communities, should have served as a clear warning.

Recent weeks have seen an escalation of these protests, with supporters converging from across the nation. The situation reached a critical point when site security guards reportedly used pepper spray and unleashed dogs on a crowd of protesters, including children. In response, a federal judge issued a temporary injunction halting construction, pending a final ruling on the case by September 9th. At its heart, the legal challenge against the nearly $4 billion pipeline centers on the fundamental issue of water.

For the Standing Rock Sioux and many other Native American tribes, water is a sacred resource. The DAPL's planned path includes placing the pipeline directly beneath the Missouri River, a mere mile from the Standing Rock Sioux Reservation's water intake. This proximity raises serious concerns about potential contamination, which could devastate the reservation's water supply. North Dakota, already grappling with water contamination issues stemming from fracking and the region's oil boom, faces a heightened risk of environmental devastation.

While North Dakota has a relatively small Latino population, ranking 49th nationally, and neighboring South Dakota fares only slightly better, the Latino community's close attention to the DAPL issue is warranted. Safe water access is a critical concern that disproportionately impacts minority communities nationwide. The Flint, Michigan water crisis, which severely affected a predominantly African-American population, serves as a stark recent example.

Furthermore, a 2011 study published in the American Journal of Public Health highlighted ongoing water safety issues in California's Central Valley, where Latinos constitute 40% of the population. This research indicated that water quality violations were most prevalent in tribal communities, areas along the U.S.-Mexico border, and other predominantly Latino communities. The study found that counties with the highest number of drinking water violations had a significantly higher proportion of residents of Latino ethnicity compared to counties with the fewest violations. It is unacceptable that in a nation like ours, tens of thousands of individuals in Latino and tribal communities lack access to safe drinking water. Projects that further endanger this access should be unthinkable.

The situation at Standing Rock is further compounded by a history of systemic abuse and marginalization. Over 150 tribes have formally expressed their support for the Standing Rock Sioux, recognizing a shared history and potential future threatened by such projects. Communities of color consistently bear the brunt of negative impacts from these types of developments. Notably, construction plans for the DAPL were revised to move the site closer to Standing Rock. These projects often enrich a select few multi-millionaires and billionaires at the expense of affected communities, whether through luxury property development and urban gentrification or the actions of large oil corporations like those behind the DAPL.

The Standing Rock community is still dealing with the repercussions of a dam project initiated by the Army Corps of Engineers over a generation ago. This project, which utilized eminent domain to annex land, resulted in the flooding of over 200,000 acres of reservation farmland. With the DAPL posing a direct threat to the community's water supply, the sentiment of "enough is enough" has taken hold. The tribe also asserts that the pipeline's construction would lead to the destruction of sacred and cultural sites.

What began as a modest gathering of protesters has now evolved into an international story, capturing global attention and inspiring a powerful movement. Dr. Erich Longie, a member of the Spirit Lake Dakota and a tribal historic preservation officer, emphasizes the strength of collective action. He has repeatedly traveled to Standing Rock to participate in the protests, believing that the unified efforts of the Standing Rock Sioux and their diverse allies can ultimately determine the pipeline's fate.

Dr. Longie observes a broader shift, stating, "On a larger scale, minorities are tired of old white men running everything. I think we’re seeing a seismic shift around what this protest could mean for a lot of communities." The Latino community stands firmly among them.

*Maria Burns Ortiz is a journalist and entrepreneur, a regular contributor to Fox News Latino, and co-author of the New York Times best seller "My Fight/Your Fight" with Ronda Rousey. She is also the co-founder of 7 Generation Games. Follow her on Twitter: @BurnsOrtiz*

The Internet Archive's "No More 404" initiative undertakes extensive web crawls with a primary objective: to meticulously archive web pages at the moment of their creation, alongside the content they reference. This proactive approach ensures that even as linked pages are altered or removed from the live web, a persistent link to the version that existed when the original page was published remains accessible.

This preservation strategy, as detailed by the Internet Archive, aims to address the pervasive issue of broken hyperlinks. By maintaining archived versions of referenced pages, the organization hopes to facilitate the replacement of defunct links with functional pointers to the archived content. Furthermore, these archived links can serve as companion resources, enabling users to view the original intent of a page's authors, thereby mitigating the impact of content evolution or deletion. The overarching ambition of this endeavor is to systematically repair broken links across the internet, ensuring a more stable and reliable web experience for all users.

The Sacred Stones Overflow Camp, a burgeoning center of activity situated along North Dakota Highway 1806 and across the Cannonball River from the Standing Rock Sioux Indian Reservation, has become a focal point for escalating tensions surrounding the Dakota Access pipeline. This expansion of the camp reflects the increasing number of individuals arriving in solidarity with the Standing Rock Sioux tribe's determined opposition to the controversial project.

A federal judge is expected to issue a pivotal ruling by Friday on the Standing Rock Sioux tribe's lawsuit. This legal challenge directly contests the federal permits granted for the four-state, $3.8 billion Dakota Access pipeline. At the heart of the tribe's argument lies the pipeline's proposed route, which places it less than a mile upstream of the reservation. They contend this proximity poses a significant and unacceptable threat to the drinking water supply for over 8,000 tribal members and millions more downstream. The volatile situation has also been punctuated by concerning confrontations between protesters and private security personnel on private land, where the tribe asserts that construction activities have disturbed ancient sacred sites of profound cultural significance.

Here's a detailed examination of the Dakota Access pipeline and the ongoing tribal protest:

*   **The Dakota Access Pipeline Project:**
    This extensive $3.8 billion undertaking, owned by Dallas-based Energy Transfer Partners, spans an impressive 1,172 miles. Its primary function is to transport approximately half a million barrels of crude oil daily from North Dakota's prolific oil fields. The pipeline's meticulously planned route traverses South Dakota and Iowa before connecting to an existing pipeline in Patoka, Illinois, thereby facilitating crucial access to Midwest and Gulf Coast markets.

*   **The Tribe's Legal Challenge:**
    The Standing Rock Sioux tribe, with dedicated legal assistance from the esteemed environmental organization Earthjustice, has filed a comprehensive lawsuit against federal regulators. The suit specifically challenges the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers' decision to grant permits for over 200 water crossings. The tribe argues forcefully that the project violates several critical federal laws, including the National Historic Preservation Act, and carries the inherent risk of disturbing sacred sites located beyond the vast 2.3-million-acre reservation boundaries.

*   **Pipeline Safety Measures:**
    Energy Transfer Partners asserts that the Dakota Access pipeline is outfitted with state-of-the-art safety features. These include sophisticated leak detection equipment and advanced remote monitoring capabilities, strategically located in Texas, which they claim can activate block valves within a mere three minutes of detecting a breach.

*   **The Standing Rock Protest:**
    Since April, a nonviolent protest, predominantly led by dedicated members of the Standing Rock Sioux tribe, has been established at a "spirit camp." This camp is strategically located at the confluence of the Cannonball and Missouri rivers, directly in the pipeline's intended path. The protest has witnessed significant growth with the inspiring arrival of Indigenous peoples and non-Native allies from across the United States, including notable figures like actress Shailene Woodley. However, the protest has also experienced heightened tensions, leading to nearly 40 arrests. Among those arrested was Standing Rock Sioux Tribal Chairman David Archambault II. It is important to note that none of these arrests were directly linked to a confrontation between protesters and construction workers that occurred on a recent Saturday. Green Party presidential candidate Jill Stein and her running mate Ajamu Baraka have also participated in the protest. Stein was subsequently charged with misdemeanor counts of criminal trespass and criminal mischief for allegedly spray-painting construction equipment. In anticipation of the federal ruling, North Dakota authorities are mobilizing law enforcement officers statewide to ensure security at the protest site.

*   **Concerns Regarding Cultural Sites:**
    Tribal officials have reported that construction crews bulldozed several sites of "significant cultural and historic value" on private land the preceding weekend. Energy Transfer Partners has vehemently denied this claim. This incident reportedly precipitated a clash between protesters and private security guards employed by the pipeline company. Law enforcement reported that four security guards and two guard dogs required medical attention. Conversely, a tribal spokesman stated that six individuals were bitten by guard dogs and at least 30 were pepper-sprayed. U.S. District Judge James Boasberg has partially granted the tribe's request for a temporary halt to work near Lake Oahe to protect sacred sites, though this injunction did not extend to the private land where the initial protest erupted. North Dakota's State Archaeologist, Paul Picha, is scheduled to inspect the area in the upcoming week.

*   **Broader Protest Activity:**
    While the most prominent demonstrations are occurring in North Dakota, protests have also taken place in other locations. In August, construction equipment at several sites in Iowa was targeted by arson, resulting in over $1 million in damages.

*   **Rationale for Pipeline Construction:**
    The Dakota Access pipeline, first announced in 2014, is promoted with the argument that it will open new markets for crude oil and reduce the reliance on more hazardous transportation methods, such as trucks and oil trains. The latter has become a significant concern following a series of fiery train derailments involving North Dakota crude oil.

Thousands of Native American activists, representing at least 200 tribes, have converged in North Dakota to protest the construction of the Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL). This unprecedented inter-tribal gathering, described by one elder as a show of unity not seen since the Battle of Big Horn, has drawn significant attention, with live streams of protesters engaging with bulldozers circulating on social media. A large encampment, known as Sacred Stone Camp, has been established, complete with traditional sweat lodges and educational facilities. The sheer scale of the protest has overwhelmed local law enforcement, prompting requests for assistance from neighboring counties.

At its core, the DAPL protest presents a complex interplay of motivations, often in conflict. On the surface, it appears to be a direct confrontation between the oil industry and Native American tribes. Energy Transfer Products (ETP), the company behind the $3.8 billion DAPL project, asserts that the pipeline, designed to transport 570,000 barrels of crude oil daily from the Dakotas to Illinois for East Coast distribution, is crucial for national energy independence. ETP and its supporters, including various agencies and courts, maintain that the pipeline poses minimal environmental risks.

However, opponents of DAPL strongly disagree. They highlight that the pipeline's route crosses the Missouri River just half a mile upstream from the Standing Rock Sioux Tribe's reservation, raising concerns about potential contamination of their water supply in the event of a leak. Furthermore, activists point out that while the pipeline's primary route may skirt the reservation itself, it traverses tribal lands containing historical and cultural artifacts that have already been unearthed during construction. In response, on July 27, the Standing Rock Sioux Tribe, represented by the environmental law firm Earthjustice, filed a lawsuit against the Army Corps of Engineers. The Army Corps is responsible for federal lands the pipeline crosses and granted ETP the necessary permits. The lawsuit alleges violations of the Clean Water Act, the Rivers and Harbors Act, and the National Historic Preservation Act, arguing that the Corps failed to adequately consult with the tribe regarding historical impacts and should have conducted a full environmental impact statement instead of issuing a Finding Of No Significant Impact (FONSI).

While the narrative might seem straightforward—Big Oil versus Native Americans, energy versus the environment—the reality is more nuanced. As actor Leonardo DiCaprio tweeted in support of the Great Sioux Nation, the fight extends beyond immediate water and land concerns. The DAPL is part of a larger $4.8 billion energy initiative, involving 26 banks and nine partners, as detailed by researchers at Food & Water Watch.

On the environmental front, the objections are multifaceted. While safety and pollution are primary concerns, a significant driving force behind the campaign is the broader fight against climate change. Organizations like #keepitintheground, with prominent climate activist Bill McKibben as a leading voice, advocate for keeping fossil fuels in the ground. The ultimate goal of many anti-DAPL activists is not just to prevent this specific pipeline but to halt all fossil fuel development, accelerate the transition to renewable energy sources, and mitigate global climate change. Currently, oil, gas, and coal constitute 81 percent of U.S. energy consumption, with renewables making up only 5 percent. This broader agenda explains the strong opposition to pipelines in general, including the highly publicized Keystone XL pipeline and others like the Spectra Algonquin AIM pipeline.

The Dakota Access Pipeline, spanning 1,134 miles and sometimes referred to as the "New Keystone," faces scrutiny due to the potential for devastating spills. A 2010 rupture of an Enbridge Energy pipeline in Michigan released over a million gallons of crude oil into the Kalamazoo River, necessitating a four-year, $765 million cleanup and rendering 35 miles of the river unusable. In 2014 alone, 119 pipeline accidents were documented. The prospect of such an event occurring near Standing Rock is a grave concern.

Beyond immediate environmental and tribal concerns, these protests and legal challenges aim to increase the financial burden of fossil fuel development, which remains heavily subsidized by the government. By factoring in the costs of lawsuits and law enforcement, environmentalists seek to make fossil fuel projects less economically attractive, thereby shifting the market towards renewables.

However, this strategy faces significant challenges. One concern is the potential for "false consciousness" and exploitation, questioning whether environmental groups are genuinely allied with Native Americans or using their cause for their own objectives. If the immediate alternative to pipelines like DAPL is increased oil transport via trains and trucks—which, while potentially better for Native American lands, is worse for the climate—would environmentalists support it? This raises questions about the authenticity of their solidarity with the Sioux.

Furthermore, protests can become volatile, with tensions escalating and the focus shifting from the underlying issues to the conflict itself. This has already occurred at Standing Rock, with more attention paid to issues like police actions and states of emergency than to the protection of Native American relics and land rights. The tactics employed at Standing Rock have drawn comparisons to other protest movements, but the stakes are considerably higher for Native Americans who have endured centuries of oppression, with some individuals being arrested and potentially caught in the crossfire of a larger conflict.

Law enforcement faces the difficult task of maintaining order and upholding court orders, such as ensuring access to construction sites and keeping roads open. The costs associated with these efforts are borne by local entities like the Morton County Sheriff’s Department, rather than the energy companies.

The slogan "Shut It Down," while emotionally resonant, represents a short-term approach that yields temporary victories. The ultimate goal, according to some critics, should be to dismantle the systemic reliance on fossil fuels and address the root causes of environmental degradation, rather than focusing solely on halting individual construction projects.

As of September 9, the D.C. Circuit Court of Appeals is expected to issue an initial ruling on the tribe's lawsuit, which is ongoing. The tribe's legal team, reviewing the extensive complaint, believes they have a strong case, particularly given the unique impacts of DAPL on Native American lands and artifacts. If the Army Corps of Engineers is mandated to conduct more thorough reviews and issue additional permits, the process could be significantly delayed, potentially allowing environmentalists to replicate the political pressure that ultimately stalled the Keystone XL pipeline. Such a delay would serve to protect Native American lands and incrementally increase the costs associated with climate-damaging fossil fuels, depending on which of these outcomes is prioritized.

Continental Resources, a major oil producer, highlighted its strategies for capital spending and profit margins in a September 2016 investor presentation. This detailed report included specific information on the Bakken Shale, emphasizing how increased pipeline capacity would improve Continental's oil transport. A key focus was the Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL) and the ETCOP pipeline, which Continental identified as crucial for its marketing plans.

The presentation showed how the anticipated completion of DAPL would significantly boost the volume of oil Continental could move from the Bakken region via pipelines. This shift in strategy is notable given the past advocacy of Harold Hamm, founder of the Domestic Energy Producers Alliance. Hamm had previously been a strong supporter of TransCanada's Keystone XL pipeline and had actively lobbied for its construction, including a connection to the Bakken.

However, facing political hurdles with Keystone XL's northern segment, Hamm changed his approach. In late 2014, Hamm, who had also advised Mitt Romney's presidential campaign, told Politico that his focus had shifted to other pipeline projects, stating, "We’re supporting other pipelines out there, we’re not waiting on Keystone. Nobody is." He expressed that the time for Keystone XL had passed.

The Dakota Access Pipeline appears to be one of the alternative projects that has gained Hamm's attention. While neither Hamm nor President Trump have publicly commented on DAPL, Continental Resources declined to provide details on their pipeline contracts when approached by DeSmog.

Questions have been raised about where the oil transported by DAPL will ultimately go. Energy Transfer, the company behind DAPL, has stated the pipeline will carry "100% Domestic produced crude" for "100% domestic consumption." However, an analysis by Lee Fang for The Intercept suggested that some oil from DAPL might be exported.

Hamm's Domestic Energy Producers Alliance was instrumental in the successful lobbying efforts to end the U.S. crude oil export ban, as detailed in a DeSmog investigation from December 2015.

The Dakota Access Pipeline controversy has created a divide among various groups, including Native American tribes, environmental advocates, private property rights supporters, Energy Transfer Partners, and government agencies involved in permitting the project. The Standing Rock Sioux Tribe is currently awaiting a court ruling on its lawsuit against the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers.

Angie Carter of the Women, Food and Agriculture Network, part of the Bakken Pipeline Resistance Coalition, criticized Hamm, calling him an "oil profiteer exploiting the health of the water, farmland, and communities." She also noted that in Iowa, calls were made to both presidential candidates to oppose the pipeline.

Thousands of people have joined the Standing Rock Sioux Nation's fight against construction of a contentious oil pipeline, a showdown Indigenous leaders in North Dakota warn won't end anytime soon. They're opposed to the Dakota Access Pipeline, a multimillion-dollar project that's supposed to transport light sweet crude oil from the Bakken oilfield near the Canadian border to Illinois. An area cleared for the Dakota Access Pipeline can be seen from the side of Highway 6, south of Bismarck, N.D. (Trevor Brine/CBC) Tribal leaders and their supporters fear a potential leak in that pipeline would poison the Missouri River, which borders the entire western edge of the reservation. For weeks, people from across North America have been gathering at camps that have sprung up in and around Cannon Ball, N.D., a town within the Standing Rock Reservation, just south of Bismarck, N.D. "It's overwhelming," Standing Rock Sioux Tribal Council chair David Archambault said. "I never expected it to be this big, but I'm thankful and proud to be Native American, because I know that with unity, there's a lot of things we can overcome." 'Good hosts' The largest camp, called the Red Warrior Camp, has swelled even more since a recent confrontation involving private security guards armed with dogs and pepper spray, sparked after construction crews allegedly bulldozed an area believed to be a tribal burial ground. The camp was already populated by Indigenous people from across the U.S. and Canada, but even more have arrived since video and photos of that incident were shared widely on social media. Thousands of people are now living in what has become a small town of teepees, lodges, tents and RVs, where people on horseback are a common sight — and where even more people seem to arrive by the hour. Those entering the massive camp are greeted by a road lined with flags from dozens of Indigenous nations that have offered support to Standing Rock. Flags from dozens of Indigenous nations that support Standing Rock's fight against the pipeline greet people arriving at the camp. (Tim Fontaine/CBC) Those who stay in the camp are fed from a huge kitchen that seems to operate round the clock, offering hot meals to an army of people. Clothing, camping supplies and toiletries are also distributed from a tent to anyone who needs them. Most of what's offered is donated; the rest is provided by the Standing Rock Sioux Tribe. "We want to be good hosts," said Archambault . 'Uplifting' experience for youth Layha Spoonhunter, 26, is from the Wind River Indian Reservation in Wyoming. Already a youth advocate in his own community, Spoonhunter said living in the camp for over two weeks now has given him something of a spiritual reawakening. "I'm not looking at a television each day. I'm getting to hear from elders, I'm getting to hear our stories, and hearing the different songs from all the different tribes that have come here, it's really uplifting." Spoonhunter is among a group that has organized a two-day youth gathering at the Red Warrior Camp that kicks off on Thursday. While most of the people who have arrived to support Standing Rock are Indigenous people from the United States, many people living north of the border are travelling here. Kevin Hart, a regional chief for the Assembly of First Nations in Manitoba, recently spent a few days in the area. More from Manitoba and other parts of Canada are expected to arrive in the coming days. Emergency declaration But depending on where people are travelling from, the trip to this area can take a little longer than normal. The quickest way to get to Standing Rock from Bismarck is usually south on Highway 1806, which winds along the western bank of the Missouri River. The trip normally takes under an hour. But for weeks, North Dakota Highway Patrol have been redirecting many people onto a detour that can double the normal travel time, depending on traffic. As we leave Standing Rock earlier, a police checkpoint south of Bismarck pic.twitter.com/invPuXnltw — @anishinaboy It's the result of an emergency declaration signed by North Dakota Gov. Jack Dalrymple on Aug. 19, in response to the growing protest. The governor's office has said there have been complaints about the increase in traffic on that route since the protests began, and the detour is for safety reasons. "The governor's executive order does not include activation of the North Dakota National Guard, but makes available other state resources for the purpose of protecting the health, safety and well-being of the general public and those involved in the protest," reads a statement from the governor's office. 'Not going to end anytime soon' On Tuesday, a federal judge ordered work must stop on portions of the Dakota Access Pipeline, but many here are waiting for a separate ruling expected this Friday — an injunction that could potentially halt all work in and around the reservation. Regardless of the outcome on Friday, Archambault believes the losing side will file appeals. "This didn't just begin two weeks ago, and it's not going to end any time soon," he said.

The Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL) is a $3.7 billion project designed to transport crude oil across four states, igniting significant debate. While proponents champion its economic advantages and contribution to U.S. energy self-sufficiency, opponents voice grave concerns regarding environmental degradation and the infringement upon sacred Native American ancestral lands.

The DAPL, a 1,172-mile pipeline, is engineered to convey crude oil originating from the Bakken Formation, a substantial oil reserve situated in Montana and North Dakota near the Canadian border. Its route traverses southeast through South Dakota and Iowa, culminating in Illinois. The U.S. Geological Survey estimates the Bakken Formation holds approximately 7.4 billion barrels of undiscovered oil within the United States. Upon becoming operational, the pipeline is projected to transport an estimated 470,000 barrels of crude oil daily, a volume equivalent to producing roughly 374.3 million gallons of gasoline each day. From its terminal in Illinois, the oil can be distributed to refineries and markets across the Midwest, East Coast, and Gulf Coast.

The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers issued the final permits for the Dakota Access Pipeline in July, a decision met with fervent opposition, particularly from the Standing Rock Sioux Tribe. The tribe has initiated legal proceedings, asserting that the pipeline jeopardizes their environment and economy. Furthermore, they contend that the pipeline's construction and operation pose a risk of damaging or obliterating sites of historical, religious, and cultural significance. Although the Army Corps of Engineers has refrained from commenting on the ongoing legal disputes, an advocacy group has contested the tribe's assertions, maintaining that the pipeline's path does not traverse the Standing Rock Sioux Tribe's reservation.

**Key Project Specifications:**

*   **Total Length:** 1,172 miles
*   **Pipeline Diameter:** 30 inches
*   **Projected Daily Crude Oil Transport:** 470,000 barrels
*   **Estimated Daily Gasoline Production Equivalent:** 374.3 million gallons

**Arguments in Favor of the Pipeline:**

Supporters of the Dakota Access Pipeline underscore its potential for economic growth, projecting job creation and substantial tax revenue for local and state governments. Energy Transfer Partners, the project's developer, forecasts an annual contribution of approximately $156 million in state and local sales and income taxes, alongside the creation of 8,000 to 12,000 construction jobs. A central argument for the pipeline is its role in bolstering U.S. energy independence by diminishing reliance on foreign oil imports. Proponents also believe the pipeline will alleviate pressure on the rail network, which currently handles a significant volume of crude oil transport, thereby freeing up rail capacity for other essential commodities like agricultural products.

**Concerns Raised by Opponents:**

Opponents of the Dakota Access Pipeline articulate serious apprehensions regarding its cultural, spiritual, and environmental implications. The Standing Rock Sioux Tribe has declared that construction threatens to "destroy our burial sites, prayer sites and culturally significant artifacts." Tribal leaders, including Chairman David Archambault II, have highlighted the exploitation of indigenous lands and rights by federal authorities and corporations, even amidst the nation's pursuit of energy independence and economic prosperity. A primary concern for opponents is the potential for severe environmental damage, including the risk of contaminating critical water sources like the Missouri River in the event of a leak. Fears are also mounting over future greenhouse gas emissions stemming from increased fossil fuel transport. A petition on CredoAction.com encapsulated these anxieties, stating the pipeline would "fuel climate change, cause untold damage to the environment, and significantly disturb sacred lands and the way of life for Native Americans."

The environmental impact of the Dakota Access Pipeline remains a focal point of contention. Developers, such as Energy Transfer Partners, assert that pipelines offer a safer and more environmentally responsible method for transporting crude oil compared to alternatives like rail or trucking. They frequently cite incidents, such as the 2013 Lac-Mégantic rail disaster in Quebec, to illustrate the inherent dangers of transporting oil by rail. Conversely, Standing Rock Sioux Chairman David Archambault II advocates for a transition to renewable energy sources and opposes the expansion of fossil fuel infrastructure. However, the Midwest Alliance for Infrastructure Now aligns with the developer's perspective, noting the safe passage of many existing pipelines across the Missouri River, suggesting the DAPL can operate without significant risk.

Energy Transfer Partners anticipates considerable economic benefits, estimating the pipeline will generate approximately $156 million in sales and income taxes for state and local governments and create between 8,000 and 12,000 construction jobs. Nevertheless, Chairman Archambault emphasizes the tribe's paramount objective: to halt construction. He offers a nuanced viewpoint, stating, "We're not opposed to energy independence. We're not opposed to economic development. The problem we have... is where the federal government or corporations take advantage of indigenous lands and indigenous rights."

The Dakota Access Pipeline has been the scene of prolonged protests in North Dakota, frequently leading to confrontations between demonstrators and law enforcement. Accounts from these events detail numerous arrests, the deployment of crowd control measures, and instances of vandalism. Energy Transfer Partners has affirmed its commitment to avoiding residential areas and to securing voluntary land acquisition agreements with landowners at fair market prices. Despite these assurances, tribal leaders maintain that Native Americans disproportionately bear the environmental and cultural burdens associated with national objectives like energy independence and economic development, calling for an end to the exploitation of indigenous lands and rights.
