query: What are the main features of the iPhone X that were highlighted during the Apple event?

documents.text:
## Apple Event Unveils Revolutionary iPhone X and Enhanced Device Ecosystem

Apple's recent product launch event, held at the newly inaugurated Steve Jobs Theater, signaled a significant shift in the company's device strategy, prominently featuring the groundbreaking iPhone X alongside updated iterations of the Apple Watch and Apple TV. This highly anticipated gathering, commencing at 1 p.m. PT, underscored advancements in design, technological innovation, and user interaction, with a distinct emphasis on the transformative capabilities of the iPhone X.

The undisputed highlight of the event was the iPhone X, a device that Apple CEO Tim Cook heralded as "a new generation of the iPhone and a huge step forward," boldly declaring it "the biggest leap forward since the original iPhone." Introduced a decade after the debut of the first iPhone, the iPhone X boasts an immersive, edge-to-edge Super Retina display utilizing OLED technology, achieving the highest pixel density ever seen in an iPhone, as detailed by Phil Schiller, Apple's senior vice president of worldwide marketing. Its construction features a robust stainless steel frame seamlessly integrated with durable glass, engineered for superior water and dust resistance.

Central to the iPhone X's innovative user experience is **Face ID**, a sophisticated facial recognition system that replaces the traditional home button. Schiller elaborated that the probability of an unauthorized individual unlocking an iPhone X using Face ID is an astonishing 1 in 1 million, a remarkable improvement over Touch ID's 1 in 50,000. This advanced system intelligently adapts to the user's facial features over time and seamlessly integrates with Apple Pay and a wide array of third-party applications. For added security and to address potential "evil twin" scenarios, an optional passcode remains available. The absence of a physical home button is compensated by a simple tap on the screen to wake the device, and a swipe up from the bottom intuitively returns users to the home screen, a gesture Schiller described as both "simple" and "intuitive."

The **TrueDepth Camera System** integrated into the iPhone X empowers users to capture striking selfies with Portrait Mode, further enhanced by the innovative Portrait Lighting feature. In a demonstration of its advanced capabilities, Apple showcased collaborations with Snapchat, enabling the development of sophisticated face filters that accurately track and respond to user expressions in real-time. A novel addition to the iPhone X is **Animoji**, a feature that translates the user's facial expressions into animated emojis, bringing a new dimension to digital communication. While sharing camera advancements with the iPhone 8 models, the iPhone X distinguishes itself with dual optical image stabilization for enhanced photo and video quality. The iPhone X is slated for release with preorders commencing October 27 and shipping beginning November 3, priced at $999.

Complementing the iPhone X, Apple also unveiled the **iPhone 8 and iPhone 8 Plus**. Tim Cook characterized these models as representing a "huge step forward" for the iPhone line, featuring a completely redesigned aesthetic with glass construction on both the front and back, available in elegant silver, space gray, and a new gold finish. Phil Schiller emphasized the phone's glass as the "most durable ever" incorporated into a smartphone. Powering both the iPhone 8 and 8 Plus is the new **A11 Bionic chip**, which Schiller lauded as "the most powerful and smartest chip ever in a smartphone." A significant convenience introduced is **wireless charging**, a feature Schiller noted as fulfilling Apple's long-held vision of a truly wireless future. Camera upgrades include a 12-megapixel sensor designed to capture over 80 percent more light, significantly improving low-light performance. These models also offer Portrait Lighting and deliver the highest quality video capture capabilities in a smartphone. Furthermore, the iPhone 8 was highlighted as the first smartphone specifically engineered for augmented reality experiences. The iPhone 8 starts at $699, and the iPhone 8 Plus at $799, both offered with 64GB or 256GB of storage. Preorders began September 18, with availability commencing September 22.

Jeff Williams, Apple's Chief Operating Officer, introduced the latest iteration of the **Apple Watch**, a product he proudly stated is the world's leading watch brand. The **Apple Watch Series 3** introduces integrated cellular connectivity, granting users unprecedented freedom and independence from their iPhones. This **cellular connectivity** allows users to make calls, send text messages, and stream music directly from their wrist, a capability vividly demonstrated by Williams during a live call while paddleboarding. Apple Watch users now have access to stream an extensive library of 40 million songs. A new **sport loop band** has also been introduced, and the watch now offers connectivity with exercise machines. The **Watch OS 4 updates** bring enhancements to workout features, including intelligent activity coaching and high-intensity interval training programs. The heart rate sensor, recognized as the most utilized in the world, has also seen notable improvements. The Apple Watch Series 3 with cellular capabilities is priced at $399, while the model without cellular is available for $299. Preorders commenced September 15, with availability starting September 22 across nine countries, supported by 14 carriers.

Eddy Cue, Apple's senior vice president of internet software and services, presented the new **Apple TV 4K**, a device boasting significantly enhanced performance over its predecessor. It features CPU performance more than twice as fast and graphics capabilities four times as fast. A key highlight is its **4K HDR support**, which Tim Cook emphasized would deliver "the most stunning visuals ever," bringing true cinematic quality to home viewing experiences. Notably, the pricing for 4K movies in the iTunes Store will be standardized to match the cost of HD movies. The Apple TV 4K is priced at $179, with preorders opening September 15 and shipping commencing a week later.

Angela Ahrendts, Apple's senior vice president of retail, provided details on **"Today at Apple,"** a new initiative offering free in-store learning sessions covering a diverse range of topics, including coding, photography, and illustration. Updates to Apple's flagship retail stores in Paris, Milan, and the iconic 5th Avenue store in New York, which will see the return of its renowned glass cube, were also announced. The event itself began with a moving tribute to Steve Jobs, featuring a recording of his voice, before Tim Cook took the stage to officially inaugurate the Steve Jobs Theater.

Phil Schiller, Apple's senior vice president of worldwide marketing, demonstrated the iPhone X on Tuesday, touting what CEO Tim Cook called "the biggest leap forward since the original iPhone." It will be $999 and will ship on Nov. 3. As anticipated, the phone features a souped-up display and design and prominently features facial recognition technology. It has what Schiller called a "super retina" display with 2.7 million pixels, the highest resolution in pixel density ever in an iPhone. It has a 1 million-to-one contrast ratio on its 5.8-inch OLED display. The handset, pronounced "iPhone ten," has a glass back with pearly finish and comes in space gray and silver. It has 2 hours more battery life and supports wireless charging. With no home button, the phone wakes up with a tap, and Siri can be activated with a larger side button. To unlock the phone, users can activate Face ID, which identifies users with a one-in-a-million chance of mistakes. (But it's not immune to glitches, it seems). Face ID doesn't work if you're looking away, but does work with new hairstyles and facial changes, Apple said. The feature works with Apple Pay, and the sensors can animate emojis. The phone also has 3-D touch. The full coverage of the iPhone X is here.

Apple is embracing a wireless future, finally enabling users to charge their iPhones without a cable. The newly unveiled iPhone 8, iPhone 8 Plus, and iPhone X all feature support for Qi wireless charging, aligning with the standard used by Samsung devices. This move towards interoperability is a significant step for the company.

While Apple highlighted this advancement at its recent event, it did not release any dedicated wireless charging hardware for these new iPhones this year. Instead, a preview was offered of their upcoming charging solution:

*   **AirPower:** This innovative charging mat is designed to power multiple Apple devices simultaneously, including iPhones, the Apple Watch, and AirPods.
*   **Universal Compatibility:** Apple is collaborating with the Qi standard to integrate this functionality, ensuring broader compatibility.
*   **Fast Charging:** The AirPower mat will also incorporate fast charging capabilities.

The AirPower charger is slated for release next year. For the interim, Apple confirms that all Qi-certified products are compatible with the new iPhones, including those manufactured by third-party brands like Belkin and Mophie. A comprehensive list of compliant devices can be found on the Qi website.

The iPhone X marked a significant turning point for Apple, ushering in a new era of mobile technology with its most substantial redesign since the original iPhone. This device offered a compelling glimpse into the future of smartphones, though its premium price and initial limited availability presented challenges for some consumers. Unveiled at Apple's new Cupertino campus, the iPhone X stood out as the most revolutionary of the three new iPhone models introduced.

The iPhone X dramatically redefined the traditional iPhone silhouette. Its front is dominated by a nearly edge-to-edge display, featuring a distinctive notch at the top that houses the advanced front-facing camera and sensor array. The iconic physical home button was eliminated, paving the way for a fully immersive, all-screen experience. Constructed from glass and stainless steel, its build quality evoked the premium feel of the iPhone 4. This notched display, while a bold design choice, contributed significantly to the phone's unique and captivating visual identity. The iPhone X was available for pre-order starting October 27th, priced at $999, with shipments commencing November 3rd.

Central to the iPhone X experience was its 5.8-inch Super Retina Display. This OLED panel, reportedly sourced from Samsung, signified Apple's first adoption of OLED technology for an iPhone. It delivered exceptional contrast ratios and true blacks by illuminating each pixel individually, resulting in richer colors and remarkably crisp text. Apple's TrueTone technology further enhanced the visual experience by dynamically adjusting the display's white balance for natural color reproduction under any ambient lighting conditions.

With the removal of the home button, core device functions were managed through intuitive gestures and the new "side button." A long press of the side button activated Siri, while a double-click initiated Apple Pay. Despite its sleek, glass-forward exterior, the iPhone X provided satisfying tactile feedback. The elimination of the home button and the reduction of bezels allowed for a larger screen within a physical footprint comparable to the iPhone 7, a testament to the design vision of Jony Ive's team.

The iPhone X was powered by Apple's A11 Bionic chip, coupled with 3GB of RAM, providing the processing power for its advanced features, including its immersive display and Apple's growing emphasis on artificial intelligence and augmented reality. Apple stated that the iPhone X offered up to two hours of additional battery life compared to the iPhone 7. The device also embraced wireless charging, supporting the Qi standard and compatible with existing Qi charging pads from manufacturers like Mophie and Belkin. Apple also introduced its AirPower charging mat, designed to simultaneously charge an iPhone and an Apple Watch.

The camera system on the iPhone X saw significant enhancements. Users could capture slow-motion video in higher definition and record 4K footage. The new Portrait Lighting mode provided creative control over lighting effects, simulating studio-quality illumination. The rear camera system featured dual 12-megapixel sensors with f/1.8 and f/2.4 apertures, both equipped with dual optical image stabilization. This configuration was similar to that found in the iPhone 8 Plus and devices like the Samsung Galaxy Note 8. A quad-LED True Tone Flash further improved low-light photography.

A groundbreaking new feature was Face ID, a sophisticated facial recognition system that replaced the fingerprint sensor. During setup, users scanned their face using the TrueDepth front-facing camera. Face ID was utilized for unlocking the device, authorizing payments, and logging into applications. Apple emphasized its robust security, engineered to prevent spoofing by masks or photographs. The system also incorporated "Attention Aware" features, preventing the screen from dimming or alerts from sounding if the user was actively looking at the device. These capabilities were powered by the TrueDepth camera system, which mapped the user's face using infrared dots and a dedicated processor for analysis, ensuring accurate recognition even with changes like wearing glasses or growing facial hair. This same technology enabled Animoji, animated emoji that mirrored the user's facial expressions. Apple positioned Face ID as both more secure and more convenient than Touch ID, with onstage demonstrations highlighting its speed and reliability.

For consumers seeking alternatives, Apple also introduced the iPhone 8 and 8 Plus. These models retained the familiar design with a home button but incorporated significant internal upgrades. Both models supported wireless charging, featured the A11 Bionic processor, and came with 2GB of RAM. Their glass backs offered a refreshed aesthetic. While not equipped with OLED displays, they benefited from TrueTone technology and were capable of recording 4K video. Priced at $699 for the iPhone 8 and $799 for the iPhone 8 Plus, these devices presented a compelling option. Pre-orders for the iPhone 8 and 8 Plus began on September 15th, with availability on September 22nd.

Many of the iPhone X's innovations were anticipated due to numerous leaks, including reports from sources like Bloomberg's Mark Gurman and developer Steve Troughton Smith, as well as a significant leak of the iOS 11 firmware. Despite the lack of complete surprise, the iPhone X represented a substantial leap forward, pushing the boundaries of smartphone design and functionality. It moved closer to the concept of a device that is "just a screen in your hand," a vision long explored in science fiction. Apple's influence was evident in its ability to popularize technologies such as wireless charging. Most importantly, the iPhone X signaled a strong commitment to augmented reality, suggesting its integration into our daily lives was becoming increasingly imminent.

Ten years after its initial launch, the iPhone had evolved from a simple phone, internet communicator, and iPod into a multifaceted device. The iPhone X, with its dynamic display, vertically aligned cameras, minimal bezels, and powerful processor, was designed to serve as a portal to experiences beyond the device itself, encouraging users to look *through* it rather than solely *at* it.

## iPhone X: Ushering in a New Era of Apple's Flagship Smartphone

Apple has officially unveiled the highly anticipated iPhone X, a premium device poised to redefine the smartphone experience. Pronounced "10," this groundbreaking release marks a significant evolution for Apple's iconic smartphone line, celebrating the 10th anniversary of the original iPhone. This pivotal moment in Apple's history introduces a device that not only commemorates a decade of innovation but also sets a bold new direction for the future of mobile technology. Alongside the iPhone X, Apple also introduced the iPhone 8 and iPhone 8 Plus, which represent more iterative updates to the existing lineup, offering refined performance and features for a broader range of users.

The iPhone X boldly embraces an all-screen future with its striking new design, characterized by a nearly borderless, 5.8-inch "Super Retina" OLED display. This marks a significant departure for iPhones, though OLED technology has been a staple in Samsung devices for years, with models like the Galaxy S8 and Note 8 already showcasing similar edge-to-edge aesthetics. The elimination of the traditional home button was a crucial design choice, enabling Apple to maximize the screen real estate and deliver a truly immersive visual experience. This seamless integration of hardware and software creates a device that feels entirely new, from the moment you pick it up.

A pivotal innovation within the iPhone X is Face ID, a sophisticated facial recognition system that replaces the familiar Touch ID fingerprint sensor. This advanced technology utilizes a TrueDepth camera system to project over 30,000 invisible infrared dots, creating a detailed depth map of the user's face. This enables secure unlocking of the device and seamless authentication for Apple Pay, offering a more intuitive and secure user interaction. The integration of Face ID underscores Apple's commitment to pushing the boundaries of biometric security and user convenience.

The iPhone X is packed with cutting-edge features designed to enhance user experience and performance. A glass back design facilitates convenient wireless charging capabilities, a feature that is becoming increasingly standard in premium smartphones. At its core lies the powerful A11 Bionic chip, delivering unprecedented speed and efficiency for demanding tasks and gaming, ensuring that the device can handle even the most intensive applications with ease. The sophisticated 12-megapixel dual-camera system boasts improved sensors and advanced computational photography capabilities, promising stunning photos and videos that rival professional equipment. Furthermore, leveraging the TrueDepth camera, Animoji allows users to create personalized animated emojis that mirror their own facial expressions, adding a new and engaging dimension to digital communication.

The iPhone X is positioned as a premium device, with pricing starting at $999 for the 64GB model. A 256GB version is available for $1,149. This represents a substantial price increase compared to the iPhone 7's initial launch price of $549, reflecting the advanced technology and premium materials incorporated into this flagship device. Preorders for the iPhone X commenced on October 27, with devices beginning to ship on November 3, marking a highly anticipated release for consumers worldwide.

The launch event itself was held at the state-of-the-art Steve Jobs Theater on Apple's Cupertino campus, a fitting tribute to the company's visionary co-founder and a symbol of its ongoing commitment to innovation. The iPhone X's release also coincides with the 10th anniversary of the original iPhone, a device that Steve Jobs famously described in 2007 as "a revolutionary and magical product that is literally five years ahead of any other mobile phone." This historical context imbues the iPhone X with a sense of legacy and forward-thinking ambition.

The iPhone has been a cornerstone of Apple's remarkable success, historically accounting for over two-thirds of its revenue. However, the company has navigated challenges, including a broader slowdown in the global smartphone market. According to IDC, the industry experienced its slowest growth rate in recent years, with total shipments rising only 2.5 percent to 1.47 billion units. Projections for the current year indicated a modest 1.7 percent increase, with continued but less explosive growth anticipated through 2021. Despite these market dynamics, Apple is tasked with delivering innovative and compelling devices that capture consumer attention and drive sales. The iPhone remains its most critical product, not only driving direct sales but also fueling the ecosystem of other Apple products, including Macs, Apple Watches, and the upcoming HomePod speakers. Apple CEO Tim Cook emphasized the iPhone's profound global impact, stating, "No other device in our lifetimes has had the impact on the world that the iPhone has." He positioned the iPhone X as a device that will "set the path for technology for the next decade," highlighting its potential to influence the direction of mobile innovation for years to come.

While the iPhone X represents a significant leap forward, the iPhone 8 and iPhone 8 Plus offer refined performance and features for a broader audience. These models, while visually similar to their predecessors, incorporate several significant enhancements. Both models feature an upgraded 12-megapixel camera with improved sensors and enhanced video recording capabilities, ensuring high-quality photography and videography. They share the same powerful A11 Bionic chip found in the iPhone X, ensuring robust performance for a wide range of applications. New AR features are integrated, opening up new possibilities for interactive experiences and gaming. A new glass back design enables wireless charging for both devices, adding a layer of convenience. The displays remain 4.7-inch for the iPhone 8 and 5.5-inch for the iPhone 8 Plus, catering to different user preferences.

The less dramatic upgrades on the iPhone 8 and 8 Plus, compared to the revolutionary iPhone X, could present a strategic challenge for Apple. Analyst Jan Dawson of Jackdaw Research commented, "What Apple doesn't want is for people to want the best but not be able to afford it, and therefore hold onto their existing phones rather than buying what they consider second best." This highlights the delicate balance Apple must strike between offering groundbreaking innovation in its premium model and ensuring its more accessible devices remain compelling enough to drive upgrades.

The iPhone 8 will be available in 64GB and 256GB configurations, starting at $699. The iPhone 8 Plus will offer the same storage options, beginning at $799. Preorders for both models commenced on Friday, with general availability on September 22, providing consumers with clear options and pricing for Apple's latest smartphone offerings.

Apple welcomed the press into its brand-new Steve Jobs Theater today to show off its latest slate of products . Many waited with bated breath for the new gizmos the company would announce. All morning, before the keynote began, the company’s stock remained pretty stagnant. Then, once it started, the price began to rise. As Tim Cook announced a new Apple Watch and Apple Store design, it rose steadily. Then, at around 1:55 p.m. EST, Apple stock peaked at $163.54. Coincidentally, this is also right when Apple announced its much anticipated new iPhone products: iPhone 8 and iPhone X. Since then, the price has fallen, eventually falling below its opening price of $161.50. The price is very likely to bounce back, of course, and shares are still up from last week when they bottomed out at $159.63. But if we take this immediate Wall Street reaction as any indication, not everyone is excited for the new iPhone models.

## iOS 11: Key Features and Enhancements for iPhone and iPad

Apple's iOS 11, unveiled at WWDC and released to the public on September 19, introduces significant upgrades for iPhones and iPads. While the visual design remains familiar, this update focuses on enhancing user experience and productivity through several key features.

A major highlight is the **Files app**, which centralizes document management by consolidating files from your device and cloud services like iCloud and Dropbox into a single, accessible location. This functionality mirrors macOS's Finder, offering a more streamlined approach to organizing digital content. Developers can integrate their apps into the Files app, allowing their documents to appear as distinct folders, simplifying cross-app file transfers. The **Control Center** has also been redesigned with customizable, bubble-like icons, enabling users to select which tools appear. This larger display area accommodates more functions, many of which are accessible via 3D Touch.

iOS 11 also brings notable improvements across the board, including **Apple Pay in iMessage** for peer-to-peer payments, an **updated Siri** with a new voice and typed request capabilities, and a **revamped App Store** with separate sections for Games and Apps. A new safety feature, **Do Not Disturb While Driving**, automatically silences notifications when the device detects you are in a moving vehicle.

For iPads, iOS 11 introduces substantial enhancements. The **persistent Dock** is now always visible, allowing easy app switching and access to **Split View** for multitasking. The updated **App Switcher** remembers preferred Split View app combinations, streamlining multitasking sessions. The practical **Drag and Drop** feature allows users to move content between apps, similar to copy-pasting, and can even be used by dragging content to the Home screen. **Apple Pencil** users benefit from **Instant Markup** for screenshot annotation, a "Mark Through PDF" option to annotate any screen as a PDF, and **inline drawing** that seamlessly integrates handwritten notes into text fields. These advancements position the iPad as a powerful alternative to laptops.

query: What did Rep. Paul Ryan describe the IRS targeting of conservative groups as, and what did he vow regarding the investigation?

documents.text: Rep. Paul Ryan said Sunday the IRS targeting of conservative groups “is arrogance of power, abuse of power, to the nth degree” and vowed the investigation into the misconduct will continue. “Look, people have no trust that their government is being impartial,” the Wisconsin Republican said on “Fox News Sunday.” “This is arrogance of power, abuse of power, to the nth degree, and we're going to get to the bottom of this.” Ryan said there is “credible evidence that donors were targeted, that the IRS leaked private information to the public, which served political purposes.” “So there's so much more that we have just uncovered that we do not know the root causes of. And so to suggest that this is some bureaucratic snafu, that's been disproven, Chris,” Ryan told Fox host Chris Wallace. “The other point I'd say, as bad as this is, the person in charge of this bureaucratic snafu has now been put in charge of Obamacare.” Putting Sarah Hall Ingram, who formerly oversaw the tax exempt division before heading to her current post helping the agency implement Obamacare, in charge of implementing the health care law is “rotten to the core,” Ryan said. “This is arrogance,” he said. “This is big government cronyism. And this is not what hard-working taxpayers deserve. People deserve a government they can trust, that's honest, that's impartial, equality before the law, and that is not what we're getting here. And so to try to suggest that this is just bureaucratic snafus, we already know that's not true.” Wallace also asked Ryan about how he views the Benghazi talking points in light of the fact during that time he was Mitt Romney’s running mate and whether he thought the White House purposely misled the American people in order to defeat the GOP ticket. “I don’t know the answer to that question, so I’m not even going to speculate, but that’s what good oversight does,” Ryan said, noting that he won’t make a conclusion before the investigation is completed. What the American people do know right now, he added, is that the White House was “misleading.” Ryan also blasted Obama’s second term, saying “now we're seeing big government in practice. Now we're seeing the arrogance, the cronyism in practice in this second term. And that is even uglier than big government in theory.” Read more about: Paul Ryan, IRS, Obamacare

The Wayback Machine's vast archive of web pages served as the starting point for our recent crawl. This comprehensive data collection effort focused on Level 1 URLs, meaning we captured not only the primary web pages but also their embedded content and all linked outbound URLs, along with their respective embeds. Unfortunately, the resulting WARC files, which contain the raw data from this crawl, are not yet accessible to the public.

Senate Minority Leader Mitch McConnell has voiced concerns about a prevailing "culture of intimidation" within the current administration, pointing to the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) as a prime example. During an appearance on NBC's "Meet the Press," the Kentucky Republican argued that the IRS's actions against conservative groups reflect a governmental attitude of "the government knows best." He explained, "The nanny state is here to tell us all what to do. And if we start criticizing, you get targeted."

McConnell suggested this atmosphere sends a clear message to IRS employees. "It's no wonder that the agents in the IRS sorta get the message," he commented, adding that "The president demonizes his opponents." However, McConnell stressed the need for more information, noting that the investigation into the matter "has just begun."

### Initial Findings and Concerns

"I don't think we know what the facts are," McConnell stated. "The investigation has just begun, so I'm not going to reach a conclusion about what we may find. But what we do know happened is they were targeting tea party groups. We know that." This perspective aligns with concerns raised by various conservative organizations, such as the Tea Party Patriots, who have expressed worries about potential overreach by federal agencies.

### Expert Commentary

Experts from the Heritage Foundation have also commented on the situation, emphasizing the critical role of transparency and accountability in government operations.

### Woodward: Modern Scandals Echo Benghazi's Shifting Narratives

Veteran journalist Bob Woodward recently drew parallels between current political scandals and the aftermath of the Benghazi attack. Speaking on NBC's "Meet the Press," Woodward distinguished today's controversies from Watergate but identified concerning "Nixonian" tendencies within the current administration.

Woodward elaborated on his concerns, stating, "This is not Watergate, but there are some people in the administration who have acted as if they want to be Nixonian. And that’s a very big problem." He pointed to the evolving account of the Benghazi incident, specifically referencing the CIA's initial talking points. These early drafts, Woodward noted, "very explicitly said we know that activists who have ties to al Qaeda were involved in the attack."

He expressed alarm at the subsequent revisions to these points. "And then you see what comes out a couple of days later and there is no reference to this," Woodward observed. He stressed the fundamental importance of honesty in government, concluding, "This is a business where you have to tell the truth and that did not happen here."

Woodward's remarks, informed by his Watergate reporting, highlight critical questions about information management following the Benghazi attacks. His perspective resonates with previous concerns raised by figures like former CIA Director Michael Hayden, who has also emphasized the necessity of accurate intelligence dissemination. The National Archives and Records Administration's guidelines on transparency further underscore the gravity of these issues.

**Issa: Iraq's ISIL Response Lacks Urgency, Kurds Ready to Fight with U.S. Support**

Following a recent visit to Iraq, Chairman of the House Oversight and Government Reform Committee, Darrell Issa, expressed concerns on Sunday that the Iraqi government remains "quite delusional" regarding the immediate threat posed by the Islamic State of Iraq and the Levant (ISIL). The California Republican stated on ABC's "This Week" that Baghdad's focus on "long-term training before they're ready to fight" is misplaced.

Issa indicated his readiness to authorize the U.S. military campaign against ISIL, a move President Barack Obama has requested. "The fact is we're already there," Issa asserted, emphasizing the necessity of U.S. involvement. He also stressed that ground combat against ISIL should be led by Iraqis, not Americans.

Highlighting the capabilities of the Kurdish forces, Issa stated, "I have no doubt whatsoever that the Kurds will fight." He believes their success hinges on U.S. air support and technical expertise. "All they need is our air support and our technical know-how, and they will do it," he concluded.

Senate Minority Leader Mitch McConnell charged on Sunday the Obama administration “made up a tale” about the attacks on the U.S. consulate in Benghazi, Libya, in fall 2012 because it “was inconvenient within six weeks of the election for the administration to, in effect, announce that it was a terrorist attack.” NBC “Meet the Press” host David Gregory also asked McConnell about the talk of impeachment from some Republicans over Benghazi and their accusation it was a “massive cover-up.” “I don't think I've said any of those things,” the Republican senator from Kentucky said. “I think you're talking about others may have said various things about this. Let me tell you what i think about it. It's clear that there was inadequate security there, and it's very clear that it was inconvenient within six weeks of the election for the administration to, in effect, announce that it was a terrorist attack. I think that's worth examining. It is going to be examined. And it's important, you know, this is the first time we've had an ambassador killed in the line of duty since the late 70s.” “You are the one of the leading Republicans in America,” Gregory replied. “Would you call on Republicans who talk about impeaching the president or who talk about this as an Nixonian style cover-up with regard to Benghazi, would you like them to stop it?” “What I think we ought to do is complete the investigation and find out what exactly happened,” McConnell said. “And I think we have a sense of what happened. We know there was inadequate security, we know an American ambassador and three other brave Americans got killed, and we know the administration kind of made up a tale here in order to make it seem like it wasn't a terrorist attack. I think that's worthy of investigation and the investigations ought to go forward.” Clearly, McConnell said, the talking points were “not accurate.” “Getting to the bottom of that is an important investigation,” he said. Read more about: Mitch McConnell, Libya, Benghazi

**AP CEO Denounces DOJ's "Unconstitutional" Seizure of Phone Records**

Associated Press President and CEO Gary Pruitt has strongly condemned the Department of Justice's (DOJ) seizure of AP's phone records, calling it an "unconstitutional act" and a move that chills free speech. Speaking on CBS's "Face the Nation," Pruitt acknowledged the DOJ's investigative authority but argued that their methods in this instance were deeply flawed and overreaching.

Pruitt characterized the DOJ's actions as broad, hidden, intimidating, and excessive. The department obtained records for "thousands" of AP calls as part of an inquiry into a leak concerning a 2012 terrorist plot that the CIA had successfully thwarted. The AP had reported on this foiled attack, citing administration officials, and Pruitt emphasized that the Obama administration was aware of the AP's accurate reporting. He recalled that the White House had even requested the AP delay publication for five days due to national security concerns, a request the AP honored.

"I really don’t know what their motive is," Pruitt stated. "I know what the message being sent is — it’s that, ‘If you talk to the press, we’re going to go after you.’" He further highlighted that the DOJ failed to inform the AP of its investigation and, in his view, violated its own internal rules by not making its records request as specific as possible. "Under their own rules, they are required to narrow this request as narrowly as possible so as to not tread upon the First Amendment," Pruitt explained. "And yet they had a broad, sweeping collection. And they did it secretly."

The AP CEO expressed significant concern that this action has already made sources hesitant to speak with journalists, potentially harming the free press's ability to gather information. This incident has ignited serious debate about balancing national security investigations with the First Amendment rights of the press, a concern echoed by organizations such as the Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press, the American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU), and the Committee to Protect Journalists.

Former Defense Secretary Donald Rumsfeld has advocated for a "zero tolerance" approach to military sexual assault cases, suggesting that current procedures may require a thorough review. During an appearance on NBC's "Meet the Press," Rumsfeld discussed potential changes to how these crimes are reported and the possibility of appointing an external special prosecutor.

Rumsfeld acknowledged that while he wasn't certain a special prosecutor would be the ultimate answer, he recognized the strong case for a new strategy in handling these grave offenses. He speculated that a considerable number of sexual assaults within the military might go unreported. "It's a terrible thing, there has to be zero tolerance," Rumsfeld emphasized, highlighting the seriousness of the issue. He added, "And it appears that something different's going to have to be done, and I wish I knew what the answer was, I don't." Rumsfeld concluded by underscoring the necessity of accountability. He stated that leaders "better really land all over people that are engaged in any kind of abuse of that nature."

query: What controversial comparisons were made about Stephen Bannon in a French cable news report?

documents.text: PARIS—Never mind that the French cable television report on Stephen Bannon made him look like one of the winos living on grates in Paris, or that it compared Bannon, Donald Trump’s grizzled éminence grise and newly-named White House chief strategist, to Adolf Hitler’s propaganda chief Josef Goebbels. And never mind the program’s citations of rampant sexism on Bannon’s pseudo-news service, Breitbart. All he had to do in that clip was say he’d like to expand Breitbart’s operations to France and allude to Marion Maréchal-Le Pen as “the new rising star” on the French version of the alt-right and she started gushing on Twitter in English: Interestingly, the citations from Bannon on that broadcast by the mainstream LCI network did not mention Marine Le Pen, the 48-year-old woman who has turned her father’s fringe right-wing party, the National Front, into the most dynamic and aggressive political force in the country. The reference cited was to “the Le Pen women” generally, as if there were so many, and then to the comely Marion, a member of the French parliament who is only 26 years old and has the xanthachroidal allure of a younger Ann Coulter or Laura Ingraham. Must be something in the air, l’esprit du temps, as they say. Let’s take a breath and look at what’s really going on here. Bannon’s support for European far-right parties runs far deeper than his interest in Marion Maréchal-Le Pen or the National Front. He brags about his international Breitbart operation as “the platform” for the American alt-right, and has for years been thinking globally, with an affinity for the United Kingdom Independence Party (UKIP), Alternative for Germany (AfD), and the Party for Freedom Party (PVV) in the Netherlands, all of which have earned glowing coverage on the pages of Breitbart. But the election of Bannon’s man Donald Trump as president of the United States has made the globalization of Breitbart and its message infinitely more plausible than it ever was before, and politicians once considered Europe’s deplorables are now rushing to bask in the gilded glow of Trump and Bannon. On Saturday, Britain’s Nigel Farage, whose blatant and acknowledged lies helped convince his countrymen to opt out of the European Union in the Brexit vote, visited the president-elect in his eponymous Fifth Avenue tower. Farage emerged from the meeting looking like he’d just won the jackpot at one of the pre-bankruptcy Trump casinos, suggesting that the new president’s “inner team” was not too happy with Tory Prime Minister Theresa May, since she’d been skeptical of Brexit before the vote. Would that “inner team” be Bannon? In our post-factual world, maybe we can say, “People say…” Breitbart, which currently has operations in London and Jerusalem, certainly has plans to expand in France and Germany with new bureaus to cultivate and promote the populist-nationalist lines there. “He has long wanted to work with all of those parties, but that was only in promoting them with Breitbart,” a source close to Bannon told The Daily Beast. “Now he has the power of the White House to do it.” Bannon, elevated Sunday night from the head of Trump’s favorite public-relations outfit masquerading as news outlet to a White House senior counselor, is right now the direct line between the European far-right and Donald J. Trump, leader of the free world. “If Stephen gets the offer to be Chief of Staff and says yes, then he runs the show,” a Trump aide told The Daily Beast before Republican National Committee Chairman Reince Priebus was picked for that job, and Bannon received his very, very senior position. That’s more than a little bit disturbing, considering that Bannon was openly influenced by Nazi propagandist Leni Riefenstahl, who rose to prominence as a blonde bombshell before producing such masterpieces of propaganda as “Triumph of the Will.” But again, the Trumpian zeitgeist makes us digress. So strong are the winds of right-wing change at the moment—even if it’s not clear whether they’re just gusts or sustained—that when the European Union’s foreign policy chief, Federica Mogherini, convened EU foreign ministers for an urgent dinner Sunday night to discuss the impact of the Trump victory, two quickly declined: Britain’s once and future Brexiteer, Boris Johnson, which was not much of a surprise; and France’s Jean-Marc Ayrault, who demurred as if he had something better to do. In fact, his Socialist government is scrambling to find right-wing credibility before elections next spring: a Sisyphean task given polls showing President François Hollande now has an approval rating of 4 percent (you read that right: 4 percent). From the Netherlands, The Daily Beast’s Nadette De Visser reports that Geert Wilders (famous for his dyed blonde hair) is tapping into Trump's one-liners: “We will make the Netherlands great again,” he tweets, adding: “I will give the Netherlands back to the Dutch because the Netherlands is our country.” “Everywhere democratic revolutions are underway. They will drive the elites from power,” says Wilders, whose Twitter feed seems to mirror Trump’s—or is it Bannon's?—at every turn. Get The Beast In Your Inbox! Daily Digest Start and finish your day with the top stories from The Daily Beast. Cheat Sheet A speedy, smart summary of all the news you need to know (and nothing you don't). By clicking “Subscribe,” you agree to have read the Terms of Use and Privacy Policy Subscribe Thank You! You are now subscribed to the Daily Digest and Cheat Sheet. We will not share your email with anyone for any reason. Similartly, Wilders has no qualms about using the Kremlin’s RT television network to broadcast his message. In an interview with RT, Wilders said, “Politics will never be the same and what I call the ‘patriotic spring’ is an enormous incentive. What I say to the Europeans is, ‘Look at America, what America can do, we can do as well.’” And up to a point the strategy appears to be working. Geert Wilders’s PVV is on the rise in some of the polls, which suggest it will be the biggest political party in the Netherlands. After Trump’s victory, the PVV went from 27 to 29 potential seats in the Dutch parliament, which would make it the single biggest bloc. But Wilders's record shows he’d find it almost impossible to pull together coalition partners in the 150-seat assembly. So where he’ll continue to build his notoriety will be on the international stage—a mission Breitbart is likely to make very possible. Barbie Latza Nadeau reports from Rome that Matteo Salvini of the Northern League, who may be the most blatantly racist and xenophobic of all the West European wingnuts, says he’s offered to help Trump make inroads in European politics. “If Brexit teaches us something, if the election of Donald Trump teaches us something,” Salvini tweeted, “it is that today we get going to take over the power.” But not everybody is as flattered by Trump/Bannon’s attentions as Marion Maréchal-Le Pen, Nigel Farage, Geert Wilders, and Matteo Salvini have shown themselves to be. And the least flattered, most offended of all may be the European leader most often compared to Donald Trump in terms of background and style. Former Italian Prime Minister Silvio Berlusconi is patently offended by the analogy. “Of course there are some similarities in that he is an entrepreneur who decided to use his expertise to help his country,” Berlusconi said of Trump to Corriere della Sera. “But I have never opted for protectionist or isolationist policies that would hurt the country,” and “politics has taught me that people are not judged by programs, but by their behavior. Let’s see him at work.” Good idea. And once the Trump tsunami has passed, let’s see what is left of Europe. Reported by Christopher Dickey in Paris, Asawin Subsaeng in Washington, Barbie Latza Nadeau in Rome, and Nadette De Visser in Amsterdam.

Donald Trump's White House transition team has made pivotal appointments, naming Reince Priebus as White House Chief of Staff and Steve Bannon as Chief Strategist and Senior Counselor. This dual appointment signaled a strategic approach to leadership, with both Priebus and Bannon intended to operate as "equal partners" in shaping the administration's agenda. Trump expressed profound confidence in their collaborative capabilities, attributing their successful partnership during the campaign to a "historic victory." He underscored their critical roles in actualizing his overarching vision to "make America great again." Bannon echoed this sentiment, emphasizing his "very successful partnership" with Priebus. In his own statement, Priebus articulated the administration's foundational priorities, which encompass fostering an inclusive economy, fortifying national borders, initiating the repeal and replacement of the Affordable Care Act (ACA), and actively combating radical Islamic terrorism.

Reince Priebus, a seasoned political operative deeply entrenched in Washington and with strong ties to Wisconsin, played an instrumental role in galvanizing Republican leadership behind Donald Trump's candidacy. He adeptly navigated the often contentious rhetoric that defined the campaign. Following the Republican party's 2012 electoral setback, Priebus spearheaded an "autopsy report." This comprehensive analysis recommended strategic pathways for enhanced engagement with Hispanic voters and women, aiming to broaden the party's appeal. Throughout Trump's presidential bid, Priebus served as a crucial liaison, connecting with senior Republicans and major donors, and effectively managing party resources. The White House Chief of Staff position is one of immense influence, tasked with providing comprehensive assistance to the President across nearly all executive duties and overseeing the vast operational machinery of government. This role demands a deep understanding of policy, personnel, and political strategy, ensuring the smooth functioning of the executive branch.

Steve Bannon, representing the more populist and nationalist faction within the Republican party, offers a distinct ideological counterpoint to Priebus's more traditional fiscal conservatism. A former Goldman Sachs executive, Bannon led Breitbart News, a platform that has frequently faced accusations of antisemitism. The website regularly publishes content that includes racist and sexist stereotypes, alongside various conspiracy theories. Jonathan Greenblatt, CEO of the Anti-Defamation League (ADL), welcomed Priebus's appointment but expressed significant apprehension regarding Bannon's role. Greenblatt described it as a "sad day" for the nation, given Bannon's leadership of the "alt-right" website and his attainment of a senior position within the White House. Republican strategist John Weaver echoed these concerns, tweeting that the "racist, fascist extreme right is represented footsteps from the Oval Office." This sentiment highlights the deep divisions and concerns surrounding Bannon's ideological stance and its potential impact on the administration's policies and public perception.

Priebus, however, staunchly defended Bannon, asserting the undeniable effectiveness of their partnership in guiding Trump to the presidency. He attributed any controversial headlines published on Breitbart to other writers, stating, "Together, we’ve been able to manage a lot of the decision making in regard to the campaign. It’s worked very, very well." This defense underscores Priebus's belief in their complementary strengths and their shared commitment to achieving Trump's objectives. The dynamic between Priebus and Bannon, representing different wings of the Republican party, was seen as a key element in their campaign success. Their ability to bridge ideological divides and present a united front was crucial in mobilizing diverse segments of the electorate.

Bannon's past has been a subject of considerable scrutiny and public debate. His ex-wife alleged in 2007 that he made antisemitic remarks, claims that he has consistently and vehemently denied. He was also charged with domestic violence in 1996, a case that was subsequently dismissed. In August of the preceding year, reports emerged indicating that Bannon was registered to vote at an apparently vacant house in Florida. This situation raised questions about potential violations of election laws, although he later amended his voter registration to address the discrepancy. Bannon has also been a vocal critic of establishment Republicans, including House Speaker Paul Ryan, for what he perceived as insufficient adherence to conservative principles. Notably, neither Priebus nor Bannon had held elected office prior to these appointments. However, Priebus possesses extensive government experience dating back to 2004 and has chaired the Republican National Committee (RNC) since 2011, providing him with a deep understanding of party operations and political strategy.

Earlier in the summer, Bannon had replaced former lobbyist Paul Manafort as campaign manager, a move that signaled a shift in campaign strategy and leadership. Priebus's unwavering support for Trump throughout the campaign's numerous controversies earned him considerable praise from the president-elect. Trump described Priebus as an "unbelievable star" with whom he "never had a bad second," highlighting the strong personal and professional bond they had developed. This endorsement underscored Priebus's loyalty and his effectiveness as a key figure in the campaign's success.

In his first broadcast interview as president-elect, Trump outlined ambitious plans for immigration enforcement. He stated his intention to deport up to 3 million individuals with criminal records, gang affiliations, or drug offenses, declaring, "we’re getting them out of our country or we’re going to incarcerate." This policy proposal aligns directly with his prominent campaign promise to deport all undocumented migrants, a group estimated to number approximately 11 million. Regarding the Supreme Court, Trump indicated his intention to appoint justices who would advocate for returning abortion rights "back to the states." However, he also clarified that he would not seek to overturn marriage equality, which he considers a "already settled" issue. The Supreme Court had been operating with eight members since the death of Justice Antonin Scalia, with Senate Republicans having previously blocked President Obama's nominee, Merrick Garland. Furthermore, Trump suggested that certain aspects of Barack Obama's signature healthcare reforms, the Affordable Care Act, might be retained, indicating a potential for bipartisan compromise on healthcare policy.

Stephen Bannon's transition from Breitbart News chairman to Donald Trump's campaign CEO, and subsequently to chief White House strategist and senior counselor, has ignited significant controversy. Political strategists and observers have sharply criticized the appointment, citing Bannon's close ties to the "alt-right" movement, a group embraced by white nationalists.

Before joining the Trump campaign in August, Bannon served as the executive chairman of Breitbart News. In his White House role, he will hold significant authority over the administration's strategic direction, mirroring positions held by figures like Karl Rove under George W. Bush and John Podesta under President Obama. Bannon, alongside Republican National Committee Chairman Reince Priebus (who will be White House chief of staff), is set to be among Trump's most influential advisors.

The announcement has prompted widespread concern and condemnation, particularly from those critical of Breitbart News's association with the alt-right. This fringe conservative movement is characterized by racially insensitive rhetoric and elements of white nationalism. The Southern Poverty Law Center, a hate-watch organization, has explicitly accused Breitbart of embracing ethno-nationalism. Following Bannon's appointment, the center highlighted controversial Breitbart articles published under his leadership, including one that defended the Confederate flag shortly after a mass shooting at a black church in Charleston, South Carolina. The Southern Poverty Law Center stated via Twitter that Bannon was the "main driver behind Breitbart becoming a white ethno-nationalist propaganda mill" and urged Trump to reconsider the hire, emphasizing Trump's stated intention to be president for "all Americans."

Critics from across the political spectrum voiced their strong disapproval on social media. John Weaver, a political strategist, warned of the far-right's proximity to the Oval Office, while Jon Lovett, a former presidential speechwriter, expressed concern that Trump was actively terrifying the country with such an appointment. Dan Pfeiffer, another former Obama aide, sarcastically noted the nation's relief that a white nationalist advisor was only in the "second most influential job."

The Anti-Defamation League (ADL) also expressed its strong disapproval, with CEO Jonathan Greenblatt calling Bannon's appointment "a sad day" and urging Trump to nominate individuals committed to the well-being of all Americans. Similarly, the Council on American-Islamic Relations (CAIR) denounced the appointment, criticizing Breitbart for publishing misogynistic and racist content targeting various groups. Nihad Awad, CAIR's executive director, stated that Bannon's position sends a "disturbing message that anti-Muslim conspiracy theories and White nationalist ideology will be welcome in the White House" and urged Trump to reconsider if he sought to unite the country.

Bannon's personal history has also been a source of controversy. Court documents revealed that his ex-wife, Mary Louise Piccard, accused him of domestic violence and using anti-Semitic language in 2007. According to statements made to the court, Piccard alleged that Bannon expressed concerns about the number of Jewish students at their daughters' school, stating he disliked "the way they raise their kids to be 'whiny brats'" and did not want his daughters attending school with Jewish children. Bannon has denied these accusations.

Ben Shapiro, a former Breitbart editor who worked with Bannon, described him as a "legitimately sinister figure" in an article for The Daily Wire. Shapiro wrote that many former Breitbart employees feared Bannon, characterizing him as "vindictive, nasty... infamous for verbally abusing supposed friends and threatening enemies."

Some of the Trump campaign's most contentious actions in its final months were attributed to Bannon, known for his aggressive and unfiltered communication style. For instance, Bannon was seen smiling broadly at the back of the room when Trump invited women who had accused Bill Clinton of sexual misconduct to a pre-debate news conference.

In a statement released by the campaign, Bannon expressed his gratitude to President-elect Trump for the opportunity to work with Reince Priebus in advancing the administration's agenda. He highlighted their successful partnership during the campaign, which led to victory, and anticipated a similar collaboration in achieving Trump's goals.

Bannon's appointment brings into sharp focus the racial tensions that characterized Trump's campaign, particularly his strong anti-immigrant rhetoric. Throughout the election, Trump faced accusations of employing such language and exploiting racial grievances to mobilize supporters, charges he consistently denied. In a "60 Minutes" interview aired on Sunday, Trump expressed surprise when questioned about racial slurs directed at African Americans and other minority groups following his election, stating, "I am very surprised to hear that. I hate to hear that... I would say don't do it, that's terrible, because I'm going to bring this country together." He reiterated his sadness and urged those engaging in such behavior to stop, directly addressing the cameras to say, "Stop it."

Steve Bannon, a figure once labeled "the most dangerous political operative in America," has ascended to a position of significant influence as Donald Trump's newly appointed chief strategist. This strategic move by the Trump campaign was confirmed on Sunday night, alongside the announcement of Reince Priebus as chief of staff. Notably, Bannon was listed first, with his role described as Priebus's "equal," a designation that has reportedly sparked concern within Washington D.C. This dual appointment signals a consolidation of power, with Bannon, 62, poised to play a pivotal role in shaping the administration's agenda.

Off Message 5 takeaways from Trump's first 5 days The president-elect is learning what he's up against — and so is the country. This is what winging it looks like, America. Donald Trump is compulsively improvisational and ran the most successful back-of-the-napkin operation in American political history, but the challenge confronting him is, by his own admission, nothing like anything anybody has ever faced. Like practically everybody else in the country, Trump (despite his statements to the contrary) really didn’t think he’d be spending this weekend trying to staff the upper management of the world’s sole remaining superpower. Story Continued Below His transition process was practically nonexistent — and was thrown into chaos by the ouster of New Jersey Gov. Chris Christie by allies of Vice President-elect Mike Pence and Trump Tower staffers, including Steve Bannon, who feared that Christie's Bridgegate scandal would overshadow their efforts. It’s been five days since the reality TV star became the reality president and judging from his public pronouncements and a slightly dizzy “60 Minutes” appearance, he still seems to be grappling with the vast implications of his stunning and unexpected victory. But in the past few days — amid protests in several major cities and a massive case of the national frights about his fitness to govern — Trump has made a handful of moves that offer the first hints of what kind of president he will be. So what do we know? He’s basically the same brash invader who sacked the establishment citadel on Election Day — but seems a lot more flexible than the sloganeering populist who vowed, in an oath of iron and blood, to build that wall, trash Obamacare and overcome the “rigged system.” In the space of the past 48 hours, a mellower, more presidential Trump seems a bit more comfortable with the system he will soon lord over: His selection of Republican National Committee Chairman Reince Priebus has already sparked a minirebellion among some supporters who were hoping Trump would, for real, detonate the establishment. “Priebus would not have been my choice,” longtime Trump adviser Roger Stone told me. “It's going to upset people ... but one appointment does not an administration make.” The presidency is, simply put, the most complex job on the planet — and has bedeviled men with far more preparation and experience. Will he imperiously control his underlings — as he did on "The Apprentice?" Or will his disinterest in the mechanics of governing render a politician whose appeal is rooted in strength a staff-controlled weakling? Nobody knows — especially Trump. Here are five takeaways from Trump’s five days as improbable president-elect. He’s going to build a big, beautiful … what? Since his election (and a 90-minute sober sit-down with a suddenly admirable President Barack Obama) he’s softened nearly every one of the big, bold policy promises he made — to wild approval — at his rallies. He's always bought himself wiggle room by emphasizing his flexibility as a deal-maker, but back-pedaling has been the prevailing feature of his brief pre-presidency. Trump says he won the election “easily,” but he’s well aware that’s not exactly the case, people in his orbit freely admit. Clinton is likely to prevail by 1 or 2 percentage points in the popular vote nationally, when California completes its long, slow count this week — and that erodes his argument for having a clear mandate, Democrats say. Moreover, he’s well aware of the frayed nerves, domestic and foreign, in the wake of his win. That explains why his personal volume knob has gone, in the space of less than a week, from a Spinal Tap 11 to an I Like Ike 4. Repeal and replace Obamacare? Sure, but only if some version of the prohibition against discrimination of patients with pre-existing conditions is preserved. Lock her up? Not a big priority. Let’s talk infrastructure, he says. Drain the swamp of “lobbyists and special interests”? Eventually, but not right away. “[E]verybody that works for government, they then leave government and they become a lobbyist, essentially. I mean, the whole place is one big lobbyist,” he told CBS. “I'm saying that they know the system right now, but we're going to phase that out.” The threat to re-evaluate security partnerships with allies like South Korea? One call to Seoul cleared that up on the eve of his victory — he reiterated in standard presidentese his commitment to maintain the status quo. That deportation force that will forcefully eject millions of illegal south-of-the-border types? That’s been modified, in the course of several interviews, to a more moderate plan to more aggressively catch and punish illegals who commit crimes here. How about the 800,000 college students who might deported if Trump repeals Obama’s 2012 executive order? Priebus, Trump’s new right hand, told MSNBC the day after the election: “He’s not calling for mass deportation”; not a mass rollback of Obama’s Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals program. But the biggest nonshocker came on Sunday night when “60 Minutes" host Lesley Stahl asked Trump whether he still planned to build his big beautiful wall with Mexico — or whether less drastic measures will do. "[In] certain areas, a wall is more appropriate,” he said “I'm very good at this, it's called construction, [but] there could be some fencing.” Reince reigns. Cue the theme song for the “Odd Couple” — and an updated version of the show's iconic opening question: Can two men who want to dictate the direction of the Republican Party share a West Wing without driving each other (or Trump) crazy? On Sunday, the campaign announced the not-unexpected news that Republican National Committee Chairman Priebus — one of the few mainstream GOP leaders to remain (mostly) loyal through Trump's darkest days — would be awarded the most powerful job next to the presidency, chief of staff. Bannon, the enigmatic Trump Whisperer who runs Breitbart, an alt-right news/propaganda emporium that has been accused of anti-Semitism, racism and sexism, was given the important but subsidiary position of chief strategist and counselor. In a statement, Trump described the two men (the dapper, disciplined and deeply establishment Priebus — Felix — and the rumpled, rich, literary, laid-back and zealous Bannon — Oscar) “equal partners to transform the federal government.” They aren’t. Trump, who is not exactly a student of presidential history, may not know it yet, but he will eventually: Chiefs of staff, by definition, run things — controlling the day-to-day flow of information that reaches the president, critical under-the-radar appointments (like deputy Cabinet secretaries) and, most importantly, the tenor and direction of the communications, political and legislative teams that determine the direction of an administration. Reince Priebus exits the stage after speaking at the Republican National Convention in Cleveland in July. | AP Photo One senior Trump aide likened Priebus to one of Trump’s property managers. “Trump's attitude is just get the job done, and do anything you need to do.” Bannon's role of counselor (last occupied in the Obama White House by John Podesta) can be a powerful position, but it is essentially advisory, and fits Bannon’s chosen (and paradoxical) role during the campaign. He was a goad, pushing Trump to take on a crooked system. But he was also an insecure candidate's Linus blanket, calming Trump down and urging him to resist the urge to haphazardly tweet or defend himself to distraction. “Bannon doesn’t give a s—t about the day-to-day stuff and didn’t want to get into that kind of minutiae during the campaign, so he’ll be cool with this,” said a Trump ally close to both men. “People get Bannon all wrong. ... They think Trump loves him for the ideology. Wrong. He’s businessman. He loves him because he helped him win. That’s why he’s in the White House, period.” Two West Wings? No two presidential advisers have ever represented such radically different visions of the conservative movement as Priebus and Bannon. Trump aides say everybody gets along pretty well. But suppressed rivalries have a way of popping up under the pressure of running the country. Even before Trump takes office, the Priebus-Bannon appointments set up a natural, if not irreconcilable, factional division in the West Wing. The 44-year-old chief-to-be has never held a government job, but he’s a seasoned and temperate political operative who rose from running the Wisconsin GOP to the big national job by dint of his flexibility and commitment to enlarging the party’s appeal. It was Priebus who presided over the famous-infamous 2012 RNC post-mortem of Mitt Romney’s losing campaign, which pinned the blame on archaic ground and data operations and policies that alienated blacks, Hispanics and women. “Our message was weak; our ground game was insufficient; we weren't inclusive; we were behind in both data and digital; our primary and debate process needed improvement," Priebus said when the document — which was shredded, burned and mocked by Trump’s team in 2016 — was released four years ago. "There's no one solution. There's a long list of them." But Priebus is a pragmatist, and the RNC’s commitment to providing Trump with the rudiments of a battleground state ground game (coupled with Trump’s innate appeal to white working-class voters) helped deliver his candidate’s big win in the upper Midwest — including a razor-thin victory in basic-Blue Wisconsin. Bannon couldn’t be any more different. His conception of the GOP establishment is as a diseased host organism ripe to be taken over by outsider candidates who tap the coiled rage of the forgotten men and women of the lapsing white electoral majority. Under Bannon, Breitbart has become a traffic-driving, baldly pro-Trump site promoting a populist, anti-globalist agenda that targets establishment Republican stances on trade, world affairs and the inclusiveness championed by Priebus and the party’s macerated mainstream. He's also been accused of pushing racist, xenophobic and anti-Semitic rhetoric — and reportedly told his ex-wife he didn't want his kids hanging around Jews. View Trump 'saddened' by reports his supporters are harassing minorities During his first sit-down interview after being elected president, Donald Trump told CBS’s Leslie Stahl on '60 Minutes' that he wants his supporters to 'stop it.' Most importantly, the 62-year-old former naval officer and Goldman Sachs investment banker has encouraged a war’s worth of Trump frontal assaults — and was one of the principal authors of the president-elect’s midnight-in-America closing argument that targeted bankers, media elites, and the Democratic coalition of minorities and coastal elites as villains who were keeping America from being great again. “Trump likes to have his subordinates battle it out — but this is something else,” said one senior Republican Party official friendly with Priebus. “This is either going to be ‘Team of Rivals’ or ‘Hunger Games’ — or maybe both.” Paul Ryan, winning. Priebus is close to the squeaky clean, policy-obsessed House speaker — a widow’s peaked anti-Trump who delayed his endorsement, then flayed the GOP nominee for the “Access Hollywood” tape. Trump returned the favor by calling the speaker “weak and ineffective,” while tweeting (a month before Election Day): “Despite winning the second debate in a landslide (every poll), it is hard to do well when Paul Ryan and others give zero support!” But Trump’s pique with Ryan was nothing compared with Bannon’s animus, which predated the candidate’s tussle with the former House Ways and Means Committee chairman. Bannon railed against Ryan as “the enemy” and claimed his policies were aimed at creating “a one world government,” according to Breitbart insiders interviewed by The Hill’s Jonathan Swan. When one staffer suggested building a bridge to Ryan in a December 2015 email leaked to Swan, Bannon replied that his goal was to see Ryan “gone by spring.” Ryan and Trump have, for the moment, patched things up: The speaker lavishly praised Trump’s win and even gave him props for minimizing GOP congressional losses, a move one Trump associate called “paying his tribute.” And Ryan is likely to stay out of the firing line as long as Priebus remains in the president-elect’s good graces. “I think it could be a problem,” said one top Trump adviser who has talked with him several times since Election Day.“ Reince is Paul’s guy, not Trump’s.” Trump is nervous — and he should be. White House officials present for Trump’s visit to the Oval Office last week said the man who ridiculed Obama and questioned the legitimacy of his birth certificate seemed genuinely humbled by the encounter and grateful for assistance. “It’s enormous. I’ve done a lot of big things, I’ve never done anything like this,” he told Stahl of his reaction to being elected. “It is — it is so big, it is so — it’s so enormous, it’s so amazing. ... I realized that this is a whole different life for me now.” The country feels the same way.

The appointments of Steve Bannon and Reince Priebus to pivotal roles in the incoming administration have ignited significant debate, particularly surrounding Bannon's contentious past. As the former executive chairman of Breitbart News, Bannon has been accused of disseminating divisive rhetoric, including remarks about "importing more hating Muslims," drawing parallels between Planned Parenthood and the Holocaust, and labeling conservative commentator Bill Kristol a "renegade Jew." He also advised women experiencing online harassment to "just log off," suggesting they were "screwing up the internet for men."

The selection of Priebus as chief of staff may concern some grassroots activists who view him as a dealmaker overly inclined to compromise on critical issues such as taxes, immigration, trade, health care, and environmental policy. However, the transition team has emphasized that Priebus and Bannon will operate as "equal partners" in their endeavor to reshape the federal government, with numerous decisions requiring their joint approval. Bannon was assured he would report directly to President-elect Trump, bypassing Priebus. This dual authority structure mirrors Trump's established business and campaign management style, which often involved creating competing power centers. The influence of Trump's children, particularly Ivanka Trump and her husband Jared Kushner, is also substantial. Both reportedly advocated for a chief of staff who would not be overly controversial. Kushner, despite his lack of political experience, is anticipated to wield considerable influence. While Trump appears comfortable with Priebus, Bannon is perceived as the advisor more capable of direct and forceful communication with the president-elect during challenging periods.

These appointments seem designed to reassure Trump's devoted supporters, who feared Priebus's selection might signal a capitulation to the Washington establishment. The official statement's emphasis on Bannon's mention was intended to appease him. Bannon stated, "We had a very successful partnership on the campaign, one that led to victory. We will have that same partnership in working to help President-elect Trump achieve his agenda." Priebus expressed his eagerness to collaborate with Bannon and Trump to "create an economy that works for everyone, secure our borders, repeal and replace Obamacare and destroy radical Islamic terrorism."

Key figures and their roles in the new administration include:

*   **Reince Priebus (Chief of Staff):** Expected to manage hundreds of White House staff members and serve as the primary gatekeeper for President Trump, guiding his agenda through Congress. His role is vital for Trump, who lacks prior government experience and established political connections. Priebus maintained neutrality during the primaries and supported Trump after he secured the nomination. As RNC chairman, he focused on modernizing the Republican Party and its finances, demonstrating loyalty by bolstering campaign resources and encouraging Republican support.
*   **Steve Bannon (Senior Advisor):** Formerly the executive chairman of Breitbart News, Bannon has been accused of promoting divisive rhetoric. A former Goldman Sachs banker, he cultivated a media persona favoring a confrontational political style and deep skepticism of government and corporate elites. His influence was instrumental in pushing Trump toward a populist stance. Court records indicate past misdemeanor charges of domestic violence and allegations of threatening his wife two decades ago, though the case was dropped when his then-wife did not appear in court. Despite his public image, Bannon is also recognized for his deal-making acumen.
*   **Jared Kushner (Advisor):** Anticipated to wield considerable influence despite lacking political experience, Kushner reportedly advocated for a less controversial chief of staff.

Priebus's selection concludes a tumultuous year for the Republican Party, during which Trump challenged its established norms and leaders. Priebus, a lawyer by training, ascended through the Wisconsin Republican Party to become chairman of the Republican National Committee in 2011. Despite occasional difficulties defending Trump's actions, Priebus demonstrated loyalty. Trump publicly praised Priebus after his victory speech, dismissing rumors of tension and calling him "an unbelievable star." Bannon's appointment generated strong reactions, with the Anti-Defamation League calling him "hostile to core American values" and a spokesman for Senator Harry Reid commenting on the appointment of "one of the foremost peddlers of white supremacist themes and rhetoric."

Donald Trump's White House is taking shape with key staff appointments. Reince Priebus will serve as Chief of Staff, and Steve Bannon has been named Chief Strategist and Senior Counselor. This duo was instrumental in Trump's presidential campaign victory.

Priebus, the current chairman of the Republican National Committee, offers deep experience in party leadership. Bannon, who led Trump's campaign as CEO, previously headed Breitbart News, a media outlet that strongly backed Trump.

The President-elect expressed his trust in their abilities, stating, "Steve and Reince are highly qualified leaders who worked well together on our campaign and led us to a historic victory. Now I will have them both with me in the White House as we work to make America great again."

Both Priebus and Bannon were anticipated choices for White House roles. Bannon highlighted their successful campaign partnership, noting, "We had a very successful partnership on the campaign, one that led to victory. We will have that same partnership in working to help President-elect Trump achieve his agenda."

Priebus called his appointment "truly an honor" and outlined priorities such as fostering an inclusive economy, securing borders, repealing and replacing Obamacare, and combating radical Islamic terrorism. He also expressed confidence that Trump "will be a great President for all Americans." Breitbart News confirmed the appointments on Sunday.

query: What is the main purpose of the Internet Archive's crawling efforts?

documents.text: The Internet Archive undertakes extensive web crawling initiatives, meticulously archiving not only the pages as they are initially published but also the content to which those pages refer. This deliberate process ensures that even as referenced web pages undergo modifications or are entirely removed from the internet, a persistent link to the specific version that was active at the time of the original page's creation remains accessible. Consequently, the Internet Archive endeavors to facilitate the replacement of broken links with these preserved archived versions, thereby restoring functionality and intent.
