query: What did House Speaker John Boehner state about raising the debt ceiling during his interview?

documents.text: The House won't raise the debt ceiling without attaching other demands to the action, Speaker John Boehner said Sunday. "We're not going to pass a clean debt limit increase," the Ohio Republican said on ABC's "This Week." " I told the president, there's no way we're going to pass one. The votes are not in the House to pass a clean debt limit. And the president is risking default by not having a conversation with us." Boehner's comments come after reports last week that he's privately said he would hold a no-strings-attached vote to raise the debt ceiling in order to avoid default and its cascading negative effects. "We're not going down that path" of passing a clean bill to raise the debt ceiling," he said. "It is time to deal with America's problems. How can you raise the debt limit and do nothing about the underlying problem?" "We've spent more than what we've brought in for 55 of the last 60 years," he continued. "This year, the federal government will have more revenue than any year in the history of our country, and yet we're still going to have a nearly $700 billion budget deficit. We're squandering the future for our kids and our grandkids, by not dealing with this problem." Read more about: Barack Obama, John Boehner, Debt Ceiling

Obama's former car czar, Steve Rattner, suggested that the team's owner, Dan Snyder, could take his cues from the House speaker. Snyder “might be like John Boehner -- you hang tough until you can’t anymore," Rattner said, suggesting he consider reviving the Washington Senators' old name. But, he added, "that might be even more" offensive. Host George Stephanopoulos piped in with his own take as the segment came to an end. "The bigger problem these days might be the name Washington in front of it," he said. Read more about: George Stephanopoulos, Steve Rattner, NFL, Redskins, Soledad O'Brien, Cokie Roberts

I worked for Speaker (Tip) O' Neill who believed deeply the one thing he believed the American people won't tolerate is obstructionism. House Speaker John Boehner indicated on Sunday that President Obama must engage in negotiations, as he foresees no immediate resolution to the ongoing government shutdown and the critical debt ceiling debate. When questioned on ABC's "This Week" regarding the timeline for resolving these pressing issues, Boehner candidly admitted his uncertainty, stating, "If I knew, I would tell you."

The Republican leader from Ohio emphasized his firm stance against voting on "clean" bills, a strategy favored by Democrats and the President to end the shutdown and raise the debt ceiling. Instead, Boehner insisted on the necessity of direct, substantive talks. "I'm ready for a conversation," the Speaker declared. "I'll take anybody on the Democrats' side who wants to seriously sit down and begin to work out this problem. I'm a reasonable guy. I'm a reasonable guy. But I didn't come to Washington to be a congressman. I came here to do something on behalf of my country." This position directly contrasts with President Obama's declared refusal to negotiate under such duress, with the President arguing that Republicans should not be permitted to "exact a ransom" for fulfilling their fundamental governmental responsibilities.

This legislative deadlock underscores the profound ideological chasm between the two parties concerning the path forward. Boehner's demands center on direct negotiations with Democrats and a steadfast refusal to consider "clean" bills, while Obama's position is characterized by a refusal to negotiate under threat and the assertion that Congress should not leverage essential duties for concessions. Such impasses have been extensively analyzed by experts, including those from the Congressional Research Service, who have identified historical precedents and the potential economic ramifications of prolonged shutdowns and debt ceiling disputes. For instance, a 2013 Government Accountability Office (GAO) report meticulously documented the economic disruptions stemming from a previous shutdown, and the Peter G. Peterson Foundation has consistently provided analysis on the fiscal consequences associated with the debt ceiling.

He advocates for linking it to significant policy concessions, especially regarding President Obama's health care law. Speaking on CNN's "State of the Union," Cruz stressed the need to find ways to lessen the negative effects of the Affordable Care Act.

Cruz believes the debt ceiling, a powerful tool for Congress, should be used to influence the executive branch. His focus is on the president's health care initiatives.

He also stated that the debt ceiling is a key chance to push for major structural reforms. These reforms would aim to cut government spending and block new taxes.

Cruz called the President's demand for an unrestricted increase in the nation's borrowing limit unreasonable. He argued this approach ignores fiscal responsibilities. It also overlooks how Congress can use financial tools to shape policy.

The senator's comments highlight a desire to use fiscal deadlines for broader policy goals. This shows a strategic approach to legislative negotiations.

The attacks, Cruz said, are "indicative of the fact that we're winning the argument -- Obamacare isn't working." "I recognize that in today's media age, being controversial, taking controversial positions, rallying the most extreme parts of your base, whether it's left or right, is a lot of times the fastest way to get attention and raise money," Obama told The Associated Press. Rand Paul says President Barack Obama and his aides have waded into dangerous territory by suggesting that the U.S. government might default on its debt. “It’s irresponsible of the president and his men to even talk about default," the Kentucky Republican said Sunday on NBC's "Meet the Press." "There is no reason for us to default." "They're the ones scaring the marketplace," he added later, repeating: "There's no reason to default." The federal government, he said, can keep paying its interest even if the debt ceiling isn't raised and that will keep the government from defaulting. "We bring in $250 billion in taxes every month; our interest payment is $20 billion. Tell me why we would ever default. We have legislation called the Full Faith and Credit Act and it tells the president, ‘You must pay the interest on the debt.’” For the president and his team, threats over the debt ceiling are “a game -- it’s kind of like closing the World War II Memorial," Paul said. Paul's comments came after Treasury Secretary Jack Lew made the rounds on the Sunday shows, including "Meet the Press," where he warned: “We’re on the verge of going into a place we’ve never been.”. “If we run out of cash to pay our bills, there is no option that permits us to pay all of our bills on time," Lew said. "A failure of Congress to act would for the first time put us in a place where we’re defaulting on our obligations as a government because of Congress’s failure to act.” Read more about: Barack Obama, Rand Paul, Debt Ceiling

Senator Chuck Schumer has directly contested House Speaker John Boehner's assertion that there is insufficient support for a straightforward continuing resolution to fund the government. During an appearance on ABC's "This Week" on Sunday, the New York Democrat issued what he termed a "friendly challenge" to Speaker Boehner. Schumer concluded his remarks by directly addressing Speaker Boehner, imploring him, "So, Speaker Boehner, just vote. Put it on the floor and let's see if you're right." query: Who is Peter Quill and what is his alias in 'Guardians of the Galaxy'?

documents.text:
"Guardians of the Galaxy" delivers a vibrant, action-packed adventure that, while occasionally overwhelming, ultimately provides a deeply satisfying and fun cinematic experience. This Marvel Studios production, based on a lesser-known comic series, introduces audiences to Peter Quill, a charmingly roguish mercenary known as Star-Lord. His quest for a powerful artifact throws him together with a motley crew of outcasts.

This unlikely team includes Gamora, a deadly assassin; Rocket, a genetically engineered raccoon with a penchant for explosives; Drax, a literal-minded warrior seeking vengeance; and Groot, a sentient tree-like creature. Together, they form a bond that transcends their individual eccentricities.

The film masterfully blends a core good-versus-evil plot with numerous nods to the expansive Marvel Cinematic Universe. As noted by film critics at IGN, these interconnected elements are particularly rewarding for long-time fans of Marvel's interconnected narratives. However, the film's inherent spectacle and humor ensure its broad appeal, captivating general audiences as well.

Empire Magazine highlights the film's success in delivering a thrilling ride filled with spectacular action, sharp humor, and an unforgettable soundtrack. The characters, despite their alien origins, are presented with a relatable charm.

While some critics, like those at The Hollywood Reporter, have observed that the film's confident swagger can sometimes border on arrogance, and that Chris Pratt's portrayal, though engaging, doesn't quite reach the legendary status of cinematic outlaws like Han Solo, the overall reception remains overwhelmingly positive. Variety echoes this sentiment, describing the film as a "giddy, ridiculous fun" and a "witty, wacky, and wonderfully generous cinematic treat." Marvel's bold approach with "Guardians of the Galaxy" has clearly paid off, offering a unique and memorable entry into their cinematic universe.

"Guardians of the Galaxy" boldly ventures into comedic territory, offering a refreshing departure from Marvel Studios' typically more serious cinematic universe. While humor has been a staple in previous Marvel films like "Iron Man" and "The Avengers," "Guardians" fully embraces a self-mocking, absurd tone, suggesting that even epic intergalactic conflicts over mystical artifacts can be inherently comical. This significant tonal shift is a welcome development, though the film's comedic execution doesn't always achieve consistent brilliance.

Directed by James Gunn, the film's humor is a vibrant tapestry woven from a killer soundtrack, a wisecracking raccoon, and a generous sprinkling of pop culture references. These elements undoubtedly contribute to its unique flavor, but they don't always coalesce into consistently hilarious moments. The film's "zany" label feels earned, but perhaps not as profoundly as the filmmakers might have intended. The narrative unfolds across the vast expanse of space, a deliberate choice to distinguish it from Marvel's more Earth-bound stories.

At the heart of this cosmic adventure is Peter Quill, portrayed by Chris Pratt, a charismatic scavenger with a penchant for '70s music. We first encounter him on an abandoned planet, shedding his mask to uninhibitedly dance to Redbone's 1974 hit "Come and Get Your Love" on his Walkman. This scene, revealed in the film's prologue, starkly contrasts with the usual solemnity of Marvel heroes. The song was part of a cherished mixtape gifted to Quill by his mother shortly before her passing from cancer. In his grief, he fled outside, only to be unexpectedly abducted by a spaceship, setting the stage for his extraordinary journey.

Twenty-six years later, Quill has evolved into a Han Solo-esque rogue, navigating the galaxy with a blend of charm and roguishness. His life takes a dramatic and unexpected turn when he stumbles upon a powerful silver orb, an artifact fiercely coveted by formidable villains. Among these antagonists is Ronan the Accuser, menacingly portrayed by Lee Pace, whose presence is palpable despite heavy makeup. Also seeking the orb is Thanos, Josh Brolin's imposing overlord character, whose insatiable desire for power drives much of the conflict.

Chris Pratt's inspired casting as Peter Quill significantly amplifies the film's inherent warmth and charm. Pratt, previously known for his guileless and endearing portrayal on "Parks and Recreation," imbues Quill with a relatable everyman quality that resonates with audiences. This casting choice proves to be a masterstroke, grounding the fantastical elements of the story with a human touch.

The ensuing scramble for the orb introduces a motley crew of additional seekers, each with their own unique skills and motivations. This unlikely ensemble includes Gamora, a formidable assassin played by Zoe Saldana, who brings a fierce intensity to the role. Drax, embodied by Dave Bautista, is a physically imposing character driven by a deep-seated desire for vengeance. Rocket, voiced by Bradley Cooper, is a bitterly sarcastic, genetically engineered raccoon with a pronounced penchant for weaponry and a sharp wit. Rounding out the core group is Groot, voiced by Vin Diesel, a loyal, monosyllabic companion who is a sentient tree-like creature, offering a surprising depth of emotion despite his limited vocabulary. This disparate group reluctantly unites, their dynamic reminiscent of a sarcastic, space-faring take on "The Dirty Dozen."

The Guardians themselves, who first appeared in a 1969 comic book, are not among Marvel's most prominent characters. However, this "not-quite-primetime-player" status proves to be a liberating factor for director James Gunn. His previous directorial effort was the 2010 dark comedy "Super," starring Rainn Wilson as a self-made vigilante. Gunn, co-writing the screenplay with Nicole Perlman, clearly aimed for a rollicking adventure in the vein of classic films like "Indiana Jones" or "Star Wars," a sentiment echoed by the film's vibrant poster. Despite these lofty aspirations, the film occasionally feels overstuffed, with many comedic beats getting lost amidst the sheer spectacle of the special effects. The narrative juggles numerous plot threads and character introductions, which, while ambitious, can sometimes dilute the impact of individual moments.

The supporting cast, featuring esteemed actors like Glenn Close, John C. Reilly, and Benicio Del Toro, deliver memorable performances despite their limited screen time. These seasoned performers are presumably awaiting more substantial roles in potential sequels, hinting at the expansive universe the film has established. It's a missed opportunity, for instance, that a film so eager to find humor in outer space doesn't more fully leverage the comedic talents of John C. Reilly, whose presence promises a certain brand of wit. The pervasive movie references, while entertaining and contributing to the film's nostalgic charm, can sometimes detract from its attempt at originality. "Guardians" relies heavily on the nostalgic appeal of '70s anthems to establish its unique rhythm and emotional core, a strategy that is both effective and, at times, a crutch.

For audiences eager for a dose of levity and a departure from the usual Marvel fare, "Guardians of the Galaxy" is likely to be a highly anticipated and celebrated release. As Han Solo himself might quippingly say, "Laugh it up, Fuzzball." This film successfully carves out its own niche within the superhero genre, proving that even in the vastness of space, humor and heart can be found in the most unlikely of heroes.

"Guardians of the Galaxy," a Walt Disney Studios release, is rated PG-13 by the Motion Picture Association of America (MPAA) for "intense sequences of sci-fi violence and action, and some language." The film boasts a running time of 121 minutes and received a respectable two stars out of a possible four from critics at Rotten Tomatoes. The MPAA's PG-13 rating signifies that parents are strongly cautioned, as some material may be inappropriate for children under 13, a standard warning for films of this nature. The film's blend of action, humor, and emotional depth makes it a compelling watch for a broad audience, though parental guidance is advised for younger viewers.

Embarking on a cosmic adventure that feels as vibrant and uninhibited as a summer blockbuster should, "Guardians of the Galaxy" bursts onto the screen, offering a delightful escape that transcends typical genre expectations. This film masterfully captures the spirit of its comic book origins, presenting a slick and exhilarating cinematic experience that is pure escapist fun. At its heart is Peter Quill, portrayed by Chris Pratt, a charismatic freelance ravager, or space pirate, whose life takes an unexpected turn when he pilfers a mysterious orb. This act immediately places him in the crosshairs of various galactic factions, initiating a thrilling chase across the cosmos.

Within the confines of a spectacularly chaotic and densely populated intergalactic prison, Quill finds himself assembling an unlikely crew. This ragtag band of scoundrels hails from every corner of the galaxy, each member bringing their unique brand of mayhem and skill. Among them is a genetically engineered, trigger-happy raccoon, brought to life with a surprisingly gritty, cabdriver-esque voice by Bradley Cooper, a performance that is almost unrecognizable and utterly captivating. The film excels in its depiction of intricate gadgetry and dynamic fight sequences, which are rendered with impressive detail. However, the aerial battles, while present, don't quite reach the pinnacle of state-of-the-art visual effects. This slight divergence might be a deliberate choice, a consequence of the film's intentionally goofy and lighthearted style.

Director James Gunn seems to have intentionally infused "Guardians of the Galaxy" with a slightly chintzy aesthetic in its special effects. This choice appears to be a strategic move, designed to keep the audience firmly rooted in the film's farcical tone and prevent them from becoming overly immersed in the science fiction elements. The overall effect is akin to entertainment straight from the playbook of professional wrestling: larger than life, emphatically artificial, and undeniably engaging. It’s a style that embraces its inherent theatricality, making the outlandish premise all the more enjoyable.

The film’s comedic timing and character interactions are a significant strength. The criminal class, light-years away from our own planet, engages in banter that feels remarkably familiar, perhaps even cribbed from the witty exchanges found in classic Bill Murray films. Speaking of wrestling, Dave Bautista, a formidable presence from the WWE, delivers a standout performance as the hulking Drax the Destroyer, a character whose literal interpretations provide ample comedic fodder. Zoe Saldana also shines, offering one of her most memorable performances as Gamora, a skilled warrior whose alien nature is conveyed with both intensity and nuance.

Chris Pratt effortlessly anchors the film as the super-chill Peter Quill, a character who approaches even the most perilous situations with a disarming sense of optimism and a belief in his ability to talk his way out of anything. Supporting roles are equally well-executed, with Michael Rooker and John C. Reilly making the most of their comedic opportunities. Lee Pace, known for his work in "Halt and Catch Fire," and Karen Gillan, recognizable from "Doctor Who," also impress with their portrayals of more grimly defined characters, adding depth to the ensemble.

A truly distinctive element of "Guardians of the Galaxy" is its phenomenal soundtrack, a veritable cosmic jukebox. Driven by an eclectic mix of Top-40 hits from the 1970s, featuring artists like Elvin Bishop, Redbone, and The Five Stairsteps, the music evokes the nostalgic charm of a cheesy K-Tel album. The juxtaposition of these classic tunes with the futuristic setting of space is an inspired choice that, against all odds, works brilliantly. This blend of oldies and outer space, along with all the other disparate elements, contributes to the film's frothy and entertaining action spoof. "Guardians of the Galaxy" is a cinematic blender, throwing everything into the mix, including, remarkably, Rupert Holmes' "Piña Colada Song," creating a truly unique and unforgettable viewing experience.

**Film Details:**

*   **Directed by:** James Gunn
*   **Starring:** Karen Gillan, Djimon Hounsou, Michael Rooker, Lee Pace, John C. Reilly, Benicio del Toro, Zoe Saldana, Chris Pratt, Dave Bautista, Glenn Close
*   **Distributed by:** Walt Disney Pictures
*   **Running Time:** 2 hours, 2 minutes
*   **Parental Guide:** PG-13 (for intense sequences of sci-fi violence and action, and for some language)

Starring Chris Pratt, Zoe Saldana and Lee Pace. Directed by James Gunn. (PG-13. 121 minutes.) "Guardians of the Galaxy" is pretty much where action movies are these days - a combination of comedy without wit, action without drama and elaborate visuals that are nothing much to look at. That just this little is enough to take "Guardians" into the realm of "not bad" says more about the state of action than it does about the movie itself. It deals with an effort to save "the galaxy," but this is a notion without emotion. We are shown nothing about this particular galaxy that should make us care whether it gets saved or blown up like Vulcan in "Star Trek" (2009). There's no burning reason to root for one set of spaceships over the other. Rather, the mere concept of "saving the galaxy" is supposed to engage the audience - for two hours. Yes, there are jokes, but most of these are along the lines of the movie's making fun of itself before we all notice there's nothing to take seriously. In place of wit, "Guardians" offers a sort of generalized willingness to be amusing, an atmosphere of high spirits that feels like lots of people pumping air into a tire that has a hole in it. Everyone is clearly working, but nothing is really happening - and yet the effort is so evident that there's an impulse to reward it. Lacking dimension As for the three-dimensional visuals, there's little here that couldn't have been rendered just as well in two. In fact, the three dimensions just dim the movie into murkiness, making the experience too much like watching it with sunglasses on. Taking the 3-D glasses off during the close-ups provides the occasional relief. The movie starts with a poorly acted but recognizably human situation, of a little boy saying goodbye to his mother, who is on her deathbed. After she dies, he stumbles out of the hospital, inconsolable. And just as we're forgetting the kind of movie we've signed up for, a flying saucer shows up - as unwelcome and as out of place as any fictional flying saucer in history - and beams the boy on board. Twenty-six years later, Peter (Chris Pratt) is a merry, roving space rogue, a happy-go-lucky fellow flying through the universe, living off his smarts, so it's a wonder he doesn't starve to death. He comes upon a metal orb, about the size of a softball, which apparently everyone wants for some intense yet unspecified purpose. Suffice it to say, it's like the ring in "The Lord of the Rings" or the little box that raised hell in the "Hellraiser" series. Most of "Guardians of the Galaxy" consists of Peter trying to hold on to the orb, or trying to get the orb back, or trying to prevent someone from doing something really horrible with it. The actual powers of the orb aren't quite specified. The powers are there for the convenience of the screenwriters, to allow great but not ultimate damage, to make things always desperate but never hopeless, dangerous but still doable. The situation is, in fact, analogous to that of Marvel Enterprises, which produced "Guardians," based on its comic book series. Right now, Marvel holds the orb of power, and it seems nothing can stop it. But will it really take over the entertainment galaxy? With movies like this? I'm beginning to doubt it. Anyway, while the evil Ronan (Lee Pace) pursues him through the universe, Peter goes about putting together his team of misfit space guardians, which includes a half-man, half-raccoon (nicely voiced by Bradley Cooper), Zoe Saldana as a lethal and damaged woman of mystery, and a human-like tree, known as Groot. Vin Diesel is the voice of Groot, and something needs to be said about the use of Diesel in the promotion of this film. In the action realm, Diesel is a name to be reckoned with, an actor people look forward to seeing. But in "Guardians," his voice is not only unimportant; it's doctored. What's more, Groot has only one line of dialogue, which he repeats over and over. For all intents and purposes, there is no recognizable sign of Diesel in this movie - and too bad. He could have helped. No time for boredom John C. Reilly does appear, as a politician on a threatened planet, and he gets the film's few genuine laughs. Glenn Close also has a small role, lending an illusion of gravitas in exchange for a paycheck, which seems a fair exchange. To its credit, "Guardians of the Galaxy" belabors little, so there's no time to get bored, and the fact that it takes nothing seriously, including itself, saves it from all of the usual outer space ostentation. It's a cheerful emptiness, neither good nor bad, and pleasant enough.

Forget the usual Marvel heavyweights like Spider-Man, the X-Men, and the Avengers. While a big-budget film about lesser-known Marvel characters might seem like a gamble, "Guardians of the Galaxy" proves otherwise. This movie masterfully sidesteps typical comic-book movie tropes, offering an exhilarating and surprisingly fun adventure. Its unashamed silliness and perfectly cast ensemble are key to its appeal.

**Key Characters and Performances:**

*   **Chris Pratt as Peter Quill/Star-Lord:** Pratt embodies the self-proclaimed Star-Lord with effortless charm. Abducted from Earth at age nine after his mother's death, Quill has worked as a bounty hunter for the gruff Yondu (Michael Rooker). Fans of Pratt's previous work, including his role as Andy Dwyer in "Parks and Recreation," his voice work as Emmet in "The Lego Movie," and his supporting parts in films like "Moneyball" and "Zero Dark Thirty," will find his portrayal here captivating. He expertly balances comedy, drama, action, and charisma, whether grooving to his mother's 1970s cassette tapes or showcasing his skills as a space warrior. This role cemented his status as a major movie star.
*   **Zoe Saldana as Gamora:** Saldana is compelling as Gamora, a green-skinned assassin whose stealth is a perfect counterpoint to Quill's advances.
*   **Dave Bautista as Drax the Destroyer:** WWE champion Bautista brings raw ferocity and emotional depth to Drax, a tattooed warrior seeking vengeance against Ronan for the murder of his wife and daughter.
*   **Vin Diesel as Groot:** Diesel's vocal performance as Groot, a sentient tree with a limited vocabulary ("I am Groot"), is surprisingly humorous and heartfelt, defying expectations for those who may have underestimated his acting range.
*   **Bradley Cooper as Rocket:** Cooper steals the show as the voice of Rocket, a wisecracking, gun-slinging raccoon who frequently mocks his fellow Guardians. Cooper imbues this genetically engineered creature with unexpected depth, relishing the opportunity to poke fun at those who dismiss Rocket as a mere cartoon.

Director James Gunn, known for cult favorites like "Slither" and "Super," delivers an epic treat in his first major studio project. The intricate script, co-written by Gunn and Nicole Perlman, presents Quill with a significant challenge: acquire a large sum of money and, in the process, save the galaxy by retrieving a mysterious orb from the villainous Ronan (Lee Pace). Ronan intends to use the orb's immense power for nefarious purposes.

Quill reluctantly gathers a motley crew of misfits to assist him. While "Guardians of the Galaxy" is densely packed with characters and events that can feel chaotic, it possesses a unique, wild-card energy and an effortless charm that more ponderous, big-budget films could emulate. The prospect of a sequel, and even the idea of the Guardians teaming up with the Avengers, is an exciting one.

"Guardians of the Galaxy" has burst onto the scene, not just as another summer blockbuster, but as a vibrant revitalization of the space western genre, echoing the beloved spirit of the original "Star Wars" trilogy. This film masterfully weaves together a tapestry of unlikely heroes, formidable adversaries, and grand, sweeping battles, all seasoned with a sharp, irreverent wit and an unpretentious, distinctive attitude. Rather than being weighed down by the burden of expectation, the movie revels in pure, unadulterated fun. Its influences are a delightful mix, drawing from the adventurous spirit of "Indiana Jones," the compelling team dynamics of "The Avengers," and the quirky charm of "Firefly." At its heart, the film's primary mission is simple: to entertain, and it achieves this with spectacular success.

The film's remarkable triumph is significantly amplified by its truly unique and unforgettable characters. Among them, Groot, a sentient tree whose entire linguistic repertoire is the profound declaration, "I am Groot," stands out as a particularly memorable creation. This singular characteristic alone fosters a playful and engaging dynamic with the audience, compelling them to decipher his intentions and emotions through context and the subtle nuances of his delivery. This unique approach to character communication is a testament to the film's innovative storytelling.

At the narrative's core is Peter Quill, famously known as Star Lord, brought to life with charisma by Chris Pratt. Pratt, whose vocal performance in "The LEGO Movie" garnered widespread acclaim, expertly embodies the complexities of Quill, seamlessly blending heroic gravitas with his considerable comedic timing. The story chronicles Quill's journey from a young boy on Earth, tragically abducted by an alien spacecraft shortly after his mother's passing. His sole solace during this profoundly traumatic period was a Sony Walkman, an artifact that now provides a delightfully anachronistic and iconic soundtrack to his interstellar escapades. This personal touch adds a layer of relatable humanity to his extraordinary circumstances.

Fast forward twenty years, and Quill has evolved into a seasoned and resourceful space scavenger. His life, however, takes a dramatic and unforeseen turn when he inadvertently stumbles upon a powerful, mystical artifact. This MacGuffin, a classic narrative device, immediately catapults him into the dangerous crosshairs of various powerful factions, most notably:

*   **Rocket:** A hyper-intelligent, genetically engineered raccoon, voiced with gruff brilliance by Bradley Cooper. Rocket's exceptional technological prowess and his perpetually cynical outlook serve as a constant source of both uproarious humor and palpable danger. His sharp wit and pragmatic approach often clash with the more idealistic members of the group.
*   **Groot:** The aforementioned sentient tree, voiced with surprising emotional depth by Vin Diesel. Despite his incredibly limited vocabulary, Groot's unwavering loyalty and immense physical strength render him an indispensable and beloved member of the nascent team. His silent presence often speaks volumes, conveying a profound sense of camaraderie.

Their pursuit of Quill and the artifact soon intersects with that of Gamora, a formidable warrior distinguished by her distinctive green skin, portrayed with fierce intensity by Zoe Saldana. Gamora was initially dispatched by the malevolent Ronan the Accuser, played by Lee Pace, with the explicit directive to retrieve the artifact. However, a series of unforeseen circumstances conspire to dramatically alter the course of her mission, leading her down a path of unexpected alliances.

Fate, or perhaps the universe's peculiar and often ironic sense of humor, orchestrates a scenario where this disparate group finds themselves incarcerated together. It is within the confines of this shared confinement that they encounter Drax the Destroyer, a hulking and formidable figure brought to life by Dave Bautista. Initially driven by a singular, all-consuming vow to exact revenge upon Ronan, Drax's singular focus undergoes a significant and pivotal shift when Gamora reveals her burgeoning intention to betray Ronan. This shared predicament, born out of mutual antagonism and a common, formidable enemy, marks the true genesis of the Guardians of the Galaxy.

The film is the inspired brainchild of director and co-writer James Gunn, a filmmaker renowned for his distinctive and often darkly humorous work on cult favorites such as "Slither" and "Super." "Guardians of the Galaxy" operates with a refreshing independence from the pervasive nostalgia that frequently fuels other Marvel franchises. Unlike characters who have been deeply ingrained in the cultural consciousness for decades, Groot and Rocket are relatively new figures to a vast majority of audiences. This very lack of pre-existing expectations, rather than acting as a hindrance, may very well be a significant contributor to the film's widespread and remarkably refreshing appeal.

Gunn's directorial vision is undeniably a significant asset to the film's success. He skillfully navigates a delicate and often challenging balance between dark humor and exhilarating action, a hallmark that has characterized his previous, less mainstream cinematic endeavors. In "Guardians," the humor, while still present and effective, is less grim, and the action, while undeniably intense, feels less gratuitous. The overall balance of tone and pacing feels assured and expertly controlled, rather than precarious or haphazard. While some of the expansive battle sequences might, as is often the case with modern blockbusters, blend together somewhat in the viewer's memory, the core concept of a band of lovable misfits coming together is elevated to new and exciting heights by the sheer inventiveness of its characters, most notably the unforgettable presence of a talking tree and a wisecracking, armed raccoon.

"Guardians of the Galaxy" is an absolute brimming with sharp wit, infectious energy, and a delightful zaniness that promises a thoroughly enjoyable and profoundly memorable cinematic experience. Audiences are overwhelmingly likely to find themselves eagerly anticipating further adventures with this wonderfully unlikely band of heroes, a testament to the film's enduring charm and captivating storytelling.

**GRADE: B+**

**Rating:** PG-13 for intense sequences of sci-fi violence and action, and for some language.

**Running Time:** 121 minutes

Guardians of the Galaxy immediately captivates with its poignant opening scene, set in 1988. A young boy, inseparable from his Walkman, witnesses his mother's death from cancer. He flees, heartbroken, onto a field, only to be abducted by an alien spacecraft. This powerful introduction frames the entire sci-fi adventure as a wish-fulfillment fantasy, grounding its thrilling escapades and lightheartedness in profound grief. The film suggests that we seek such narratives for escapism, inviting us to journey alongside its characters. While this echoes Steven Spielberg's recurring motif of a wounded child finding solace in wonder, it remains remarkably effective. This opening also serves as an artful diversion, as the otherwise enjoyable film largely eschews such deep emotional resonance for the remainder of its runtime, despite a brief revisit to this pivotal moment.

Decades later, that young boy has matured into Peter Quill (Chris Pratt), now known as Star-Lord. He navigates the cosmos as a "Ravager," primarily focused on acquiring and trading valuable items. His life takes an unexpected turn when he obtains a mysterious and highly sought-after artifact known as "The Orb." This event propels him into the company of a motley crew of intergalactic outcasts. Among them are Rocket (voiced by Bradley Cooper), a wisecracking, genetically engineered raccoon; Groot (voiced by Vin Diesel), a sentient tree-like being who can only utter "I am Groot" yet communicates effectively with Rocket, reminiscent of Chewbacca and Han Solo; Gamora (Zoe Saldana), a beautiful but conflicted warrior; and Drax the Destroyer (Dave Bautista), a formidable, humorless, and vengeful figure.

Director James Gunn, who co-penned the screenplay with Nicole Perlman, demonstrates a remarkable ability to seamlessly transition between diverse and imaginative worlds without sacrificing narrative momentum or character focus. This skill, akin to George Lucas's talent, allows the film to immerse viewers in entirely new environments, such as a mining colony situated within the colossal, decaying head of an ancient cosmic deity. Gunn's intuitive grasp of what information is essential and what can be omitted provides an elegant narrative shorthand, a crucial element for stories of this magnitude, which was notably absent in films like *John Carter* and *After Earth*.

The plot itself is intentionally convoluted, revolving around an "Infinity Stone," an object possessing the power to obliterate entire galaxies when wielded by the appropriate entity. However, the film’s decidedly lighthearted tone renders this potentially catastrophic MacGuffin surprisingly inconsequential. Unlike many action films that designate a specific character for comic relief, *Guardians of the Galaxy* imbues nearly every character with comedic potential. Consequently, the movie delivers a relentless barrage of irreverent quips, witty banter, and pop-culture references. These humorous moments are designed to elicit laughter, even when their comedic impact is subtle.

The effectiveness of these jokes hinges on their context. Lines like, "He says he’s an A-hole, but — and I’m quoting him here — he’s ‘not 100 percent a dick,’” or the juxtaposition of outlaw figures with John Stamos, land with unexpected hilarity precisely because they are embedded within a cosmic superhero narrative. Similarly, the classic rock soundtrack, featuring iconic tracks, contributes to the film's cool aesthetic. The movie masterfully employs calculated incongruities to win over its audience. It shares a certain charm with *The Lego Movie*, which utilized its zany humor to celebrate creativity and diversity while exploring profound themes. In contrast, *Guardians of the Galaxy* primarily uses its jokes for amusement.

This pervasive levity, while a significant strength, also represents a potential limitation. Gunn occasionally hints at a desire to explore deeper emotional territory. His visual style captures moments of genuine emotional grandeur, such as a spaceship transporting two doomed lovers in a final embrace, or the awe-inspiring spectacle of countless ships forming a protective cosmic shield. Yet, these profound instances are frequently undercut by comedic interludes, as if the film hesitates to fully commit to more substantial themes, perhaps due to an eagerness to please or an almost adolescent apprehension of grander emotional gestures. Gunn's early career at Troma, a studio known for its low-budget, often outrageous films, is evident. The description of *Guardians of the Galaxy* as "the most expensive Troma film ever made" aptly captures its spirit. The film embraces its role as the charismatic class clown of the Marvel Cinematic Universe, which is commendable. However, like many talented class clowns, one can't help but wish it would occasionally apply itself more rigorously, given its evident capacity for greater depth and impact.

## Guardians of the Galaxy: A Deep Dive into Sci-Fi's Unlikely Heroes

When "Guardians of the Galaxy" burst onto the scene, it evoked memories of some of the most memorable science fiction event films of the past three decades. However, a closer look reveals that the films it most closely resembles in spirit, if not always in execution, are the cult classics "Howard the Duck" and "Green Lantern." At its core, the film centers on Peter Quill, played by Chris Pratt, an interstellar rogue with a penchant for Indiana Jones-esque adventures. His current mission: to pilfer and sell a mysterious orb, a glowing artifact that bears a striking resemblance to a wire Christmas ornament from a popular home goods store.

### Assembling the Unlikely Crew

Quill's quest quickly leads him to assemble a motley crew of outlaws, each with their own unique brand of charm and questionable morality. He teams up with Gamora, a formidable warrior portrayed by Zoe Saldana, who is secretly plotting against her villainous employer. Adding to the ensemble is Rocket, a wisecracking, Jersey-accented bounty hunter voiced by Bradley Cooper, whose comedic timing often feels like a nod to the legendary Gilbert Gottfried. Then there's Groot, a sentient, tree-like creature who utters only the phrase "I am Groot," a role brought to life by Vin Diesel. Rounding out the team is Drax the Destroyer, a hulking figure of muscle and tattoos, brought to the screen by Dave Bautista.

### Humor and Character Dynamics

The film's humor often relies on the character of Peter Quill, a scruff-faced earthling whose dialogue is peppered with slang that might pass for wit in a college fraternity. Phrases like "dude," "chill," and "bro" are deployed in an attempt to elicit laughs. Drax, on the other hand, provides a different brand of comedy, his literal interpretations and lack of understanding of metaphors serving as a source of amusement for what the film seems to target as a lower-IQ audience. His declaration, "Nothing goes over my head. My reflexes are too fast," is a prime example of his character's simplistic worldview, a comedic trope that might feel familiar to fans of characters like Joey Tribbiani from "Friends."

As this band of misfits navigates the cosmos, they encounter a series of villains whose fragility is likened to that of Pringles potato chips, easily dispatched. The dialogue itself is a curious blend of archaic, Conan the Barbarian-esque pronouncements ("Spare me your foul gaze, woman") and modern, often crude, slang ("They got my d--- message!"). Providing a sonic backdrop to their adventures is Peter's beloved '70s pop mixtape, a stark contrast to the orchestral scores of traditional sci-fi epics, with John Williams conspicuously absent in favor of hits like "The Piña Colada Song."

### A Different Kind of Sci-Fi Comedy

"Guardians of the Galaxy" is not a full-blown spoof in the vein of films like "A Million Ways to Die in Space." Instead, the filmmakers, including writer-director James Gunn, seem to aim for genuine excitement derived from the chaotic action, the visually stunning special effects, and the intense combat sequences against blue-faced adversaries adorned with bits of circuit board or seaweed. The comedic elements are intended to alleviate tension, but often feel misplaced, as if deflating a suspense that was never truly established.

Gunn's directorial approach is characterized by a tendency to introduce plot resolutions through sudden, unexplained gadgets or superpowers. The narrative often feels as if it's improvising solutions on the fly. For instance, the peril of floating in space for a few seconds is presented as fatal, unless, of course, the plot requires otherwise. Similarly, an all-powerful golf ball of energy is both a destructive force and a convenient plot device. The film frequently resorts to the potty-mouthed raccoon for comedic relief, with Rocket exclaiming phrases like "Holy s---!" or "Attention, idiots!" – a comedic style that echoes the earlier "Howard the Duck."

### The Legacy of Sci-Fi Humor

While the one-liners in "Star Wars," a clear inspiration for "Guardians," managed to elicit laughs while maintaining the illusion of peril, "Guardians of the Galaxy" often undermines its own stakes. A joke referencing the film "Footloose," where a legendary hero named Kevin Bacon saved people from having "sticks up their butts," leads to Gamora's character questioning, "Who put the sticks up their butts?" This highlights a recurring issue: while Drax is positioned as the film's primary source of "sitcom cretin" humor, the pervasive silliness means that every character contributes to the overall lack of gravitas. The film's reliance on such humor, while aiming for a unique comedic voice, sometimes overshadows the potential for genuine narrative tension and character development, a point that has been discussed by critics and film scholars alike, with some drawing parallels to the tonal experiments seen in early Marvel Cinematic Universe entries.

query: What were some significant events and milestones in Queen Elizabeth II's life leading up to her 90th birthday?

documents.text:
Queen Elizabeth II, Britain's longest-reigning monarch, ascended to the throne in February 1952, marking the beginning of a historic era. Born on April 21, 1926, in Mayfair, London, her early life was significantly shaped by the abdication of her uncle, King Edward VIII, in 1936. This pivotal event placed her father on the throne as King George VI, positioning Princess Elizabeth as the heir presumptive. During World War II, she and her sister, Princess Margaret, were relocated to Windsor Castle for safety. Elizabeth received a comprehensive home education, with a particular focus on constitutional history and law, and was tutored in religion by the Archbishop of Canterbury. Her personal life saw her meet Prince Philip in 1934, and they married at Westminster Abbey on November 20, 1947. The couple resided in Malta between 1949 and 1951 while Prince Philip served in the Royal Navy. Her coronation, a landmark event televised for the first time in England, took place at Westminster Abbey in June 1953, following which she embarked on extensive tours across the United Kingdom and the Commonwealth.

Throughout her reign, Queen Elizabeth II celebrated numerous significant milestones, including her Silver Jubilee in 1977, marked by extensive tours covering 36 counties in the UK and Northern Ireland, as well as visits to Australia, New Zealand, and Papua New Guinea. An estimated 56,000 miles were covered by her and the Duke of Edinburgh that year, accompanied by celebrations such as a chain of celebratory beacons and approximately 4,000 street parties. The year 2002 saw the Golden Jubilee, with visits to every UK region and numerous other countries, highlighted by services and concerts featuring notable artists like Paul McCartney and Elton John. For her 80th birthday, a "Children's Party at the Palace" was hosted for 2,000 children, and she spent her actual birthday meeting crowds in Windsor, followed by a family dinner and fireworks, receiving nearly 40,000 birthday messages. Her 60th wedding anniversary was commemorated by revisiting Broadlands in Hampshire, recreating wedding photos, and the Queen's Diamond Jubilee Trust supported initiatives like Queen's Young Leaders.

Queen Elizabeth II possessed a lifelong passion for horses and dogs, regularly attending major horse racing events and owning and breeding thoroughbreds. She also enjoyed Scottish country dancing, hosting annual dances for the community. Her charitable contributions were vast, supporting nearly 40 animal-related organizations, almost 90 medical and health-care charities, and hundreds of other causes. Her birthday is traditionally celebrated twice annually – in April and June – a custom for sovereigns whose birthdays do not fall in the summer. She maintained a weekly meeting with Britain's prime minister for an hour and was fluent in French. Norman Hartnell was the designer of her wedding and coronation gowns, as well as her attire for her first U.S. visit as monarch.

As the 40th monarch since William the Conqueror's coronation in 1066, Queen Elizabeth II's reign spanned the terms of 12 U.S. presidents and seven Roman Catholic popes. She was a Girl Guide and a Sea Ranger in her youth and had 30 godchildren. In 1986, she became the first British monarch to visit China and sent her first email in 1976. Six Archbishops of Canterbury served during her tenure. She developed the "dorgi" breed by crossing a corgi with a dachshund and is the second oldest monarch to celebrate 60 years on the throne, following Queen Victoria. In 2005, she opened the first children's trail in the Buckingham Palace garden, and the British Monarchy YouTube channel, launched in 2007, has achieved millions of views.

The Queen held a unique position, not requiring a driver's license or license plates, nor a passport for international travel. During World War II, she drove a truck and trained as a mechanic. Whales, dolphins, porpoises, and sturgeons are designated "Fishes Royal" and are considered the monarch's property if caught within three miles of the UK coast. Rare animals gifted to her, such as jaguars, were donated to the London Zoo. She utilized rationing coupons for her wedding dress materials and visited over 115 countries. Following a visit to Belfast in 2014, she referenced her tour of the Game of Thrones set in her Christmas message. Her fondness for dogs was inherited from Queen Victoria. Only five other British monarchs have ruled for 50 years or more. She visited Edinburgh nearly every year during Holyrood Week, staying at the Palace of Holyroodhouse. Queen Elizabeth II gave royal assent to over 3,500 Acts of Parliament and visited the Vatican three times, most recently meeting Pope Francis in 2014. The Royal Yacht Britannia traveled over 1 million miles during its service, and she undertook over 250 overseas trips. Elizabeth attended every State Opening of Parliament except for two, when she was pregnant. Tony Blair and David Cameron are among the prime ministers born during her reign. Her passion for horses began with a pony gifted for her third birthday. She typically received around 60,000 letters annually. In 1940, at age 14, she made a radio broadcast, and she and the Duke of Edinburgh sent over 45,000 Christmas cards.

Queen Elizabeth II celebrates her 90th birthday on Thursday. Veteran CNN royal-watcher Roger Clark recalls the time he met the Queen, reflects on her six decades of public service and asks what it is that makes her so popular. For full coverage of the British royal family, head to cnn.com/royals (CNN) King George VI once told his daughter she wouldn't remember most of the people she would meet in her life. But His Majesty said those very people would always remember the day THEY met HER. I know exactly what he meant. I doubt the Queen tells her friends about the day she met me but let's just say I've mentioned the occasion once or twice! It was a meeting I'll never forget -- I think I even have a picture on the wall at home of us chatting together at Windsor Castle. I wanted to make a good impression. I'm not normally lost for words but I did struggle a bit that day. On June 10, Buckingham Palace released a new official photograph to mark the Queen's 90th birthday. It shows Queen Elizabeth II with her husband, the Duke of Edinburgh, and was taken at Windsor Castle just after Easter. On June 10, Buckingham Palace released a new official photograph to mark the Queen's 90th birthday. It shows Queen Elizabeth II with her husband, the Duke of Edinburgh, and was taken at Windsor Castle just after Easter. This undated photo provided by HM The Queen shows the then Princess Elizabeth, left, Princess Margaret and their father King George VI in Britain. Rare footage of Britain's Queen Elizabeth has been shared to celebrate her 90th birthday. Rare footage of Britain's Queen Elizabeth has been shared to celebrate her 90th birthday. (Courtesy of HM The Queen via AP) NO SALES NO ARCHIVES Queen Elizabeth II acknowledges the crowd as she celebrates her 90th birthday in Windsor, England, on April 21. Queen Elizabeth II acknowledges the crowd as she celebrates her 90th birthday in Windsor, England, on April 21. Prince George gets a boost from some foam blocks for a special family photo. The portrait, featuring the four generations of the House of Windsor, was commissioned by the Royal Mail and will be featured on a series of stamps to commemorate the Queen's 90th birthday. Queen Elizabeth is the longest-reigning monarch in British history. Look back at moments from her life so far. Queen Elizabeth is the longest-reigning monarch in British history. Look back at moments from her life so far. I was covering the Golden Jubilee in 2002 and we'd asked the Palace if on the opening day, she would adjust her schedule by a few minutes to help the broadcasters. The Palace agreed. So when I met Her Majesty, I thanked her for changing her schedule. Unfortunately, I pronounced it "SKedule" not "SHedule. I'd used the American pronunciation in front of the Queen AND in her Castle. Brilliant move! She gently corrected me and then laughed. The King was right. I still remember our meeting, which is why, even during my years living in America, I never use the American pronunciation. And if any one questions me about it, I tell them to ring Buckingham Palace for an explanation! Stage presence When you meet the Queen, she tries to put you at ease. But it's still nerve wracking. After I'd shaken her hand, I thought about all those world leaders, pop stars, sports legends and others who've shaken THAT hand. Despite being 5'4" tall, the Queen has enormous presence when she enters the room. It's a most unusual feeling. My first encounter with Her Majesty came during her Silver Jubilee in 1977. She visited my hometown of Hull in the North of England. I wonder if she'd forgiven the city for rising up against one of her predecessors, King Charles I. Hide Caption 10 of 10 The protests had ignited the English Civil War that led to the King losing his head -- and all future monarchs ruling not through the Divine Right, but with the blessing of their people. That first sight of the Queen was one I'll always remember. We didn't see her often in those days before 24 hour news. Sure, she was on our stamps, our coins and her picture hung in the school assembly hall. But to see her in the flesh was quite something. I was with hundreds of choirboys that day singing to her. I really hope she doesn't remember that! Patience - more than a virtue Over the years, I've seen the Queen in action on many Royal visits. God only knows how she does it. She travels all over the world, meets countless people, tries to ensure a routine day for her is a special day for those she meets. One event after another, listening to and giving speeches, shaking endless outstretched hands and all with a smile on her face. It must be tough because, in truth, some of the speeches can be dull and some of the events are pretty boring. But she can't exactly yawn when she's bored or, indeed, tired. And if she is bored or tired, you'd never know it. Little is known about her JUST WATCHED The contentious Brexit issue has even reached the Queen Replay More Videos ... MUST WATCH The contentious Brexit issue has even reached the Queen 01:37 Despite being one of the most famous people in the world, very few of her subjects really know much about her. She's never given an interview, never written an autobiography. We know she likes horses, corgis and the outdoors. We know also that she's a deeply religious person and has a sense of humor. Apparently she's an excellent mimic. But we know nothing about her politics or her views on anything remotely controversial. Sense of duty Some commentators have criticized her for being a bit remote as a mother. Yet when she put her grandmotherly responsibilities to Princes William and Harry first in the days after Princess Diana died, she was criticized for that too. I remember that time very well and the angry mood of the British people -- it was unprecedented. That week saw the biggest wobble of her reign. What a remarkable turnaround that just five years later, millions of people around the Commonwealth came onto the streets to celebrate her Golden Jubilee. MUST WATCH The world's longest serving monarchs 01:23 Well for a start she's not a politician. She also stands for something and does not do knee-jerk reactions to some poll or focus group. Again, not a politician. She's seen as genuine, somebody who cares about her country, her Commonwealth and her people. In times of sadness and despair, people look to the Queen and her family for words of support. I remember her doing just that after the London bombings of 2005. She and her family are also there in times of joy to celebrate with us. Remember that dramatic arrival with James Bond at the opening of the London Olympics? The Queen also provides a sense of continuity in a changing world. Sure, there are people who would like to see an end of monarchy but they are few in number compared to those who, day in day out, welcome the Queen to their towns, cities and homes around the world. Her realms and territories extend across the globe. She's monarch in places ranging from New Zealand in the south to Scotland in the north, taking in Australia, Canada and a host of Caribbean countries. She's also Head of the Commonwealth -- a 53-nation club whose membership numbers almost a third of the world's population. On those occasions when millions turn up outside Buckingham Palace and sing God Save the Queen, they truly mean it.
