People along the way in every decade have to push the envelope a little bit, and he was one of the guys that did that. Diff’rent Strokes (1978) and The Facts Of Life (1979)—Theme Songwriter AVC: In the late ’70s, you wrote two theme songs that will ricochet through pop-culture forever. How does it work when you’re writing a theme song? Do you get the script and write it from there? Do you see a pilot? What’s the process like? AT: It’s all of that. The challenge was, you have 24 seconds to do something catchy and memorable and sum up the entire premise of the show in case somebody had never seen it before. You had to do it with an internal rhyme scheme and a perky little ditty. So it was an interesting challenge that way. It varied. I did over 40 themes altogether. Those happen to be two of the most memorable ones, and long-lasting. It would vary. Sometimes it would be brought in at the very last minute, when a show was already completely shot and the pilot had been edited, and they’d say “Here, add your 24 seconds.” There were other instances, and Diff’rent Strokes was one of them, where you got the information, you were included, from day one and page one. From the notion “Well, we’re developing this idea, and we kind of have an idea that it’s a couple of young black guys with an older white guy.” And then you would get a copy of the script a month later and the revision a month after that, and then you’re invited to the taping, and then you do your own editing right through their editing, so that hopefully you all come together at the end when it’s time to deliver. I’ve experienced it both ways. Nowadays, I get paid by technology that didn’t even exist then. I get ringtone royalties now. Apparently what happens is, you get college kids after a couple of rounds of Beer Pong, and they start to bet each other who can remember the most lyrics to an iconic sitcom, and they order it up on ringtones, and I get 11 cents. It doesn’t keep up with the current state of the global economy, but it’s always a pleasant little surprise in the mail. AVC: When you were writing those songs, did you get the sense that you’d nailed it, that the themes just worked? AT: Back then, theme songs were more important. They were a part of every show. Nowadays, they don’t aspire to have memorable themes for every show, because the networks are so competitive that as soon as you hit 8 o’clock, or 8:30, or 9 o’clock, that show needs to be in progress already. They don’t want to waste the time with 25 or 30 seconds of a ditty. They get right to the action, and that’s the case in both comedy and drama now. And certainly theme songs with a lyric, they have no time for that. So I think that’s almost a lost art. Back then, we took it a little more seriously, and you knew that your song would be heard every episode. So yeah, you spent some time at it, and took a little pride in it, and worked hard. Like, the Facts Of Life internal rhyme scheme was intricate and one that I remember finishing and saying “Yeah, that’s pretty good. That all rhymes. I got a lot of rhyming words in 24 seconds.” Thicke Of The Night (1983-1984)—Host AT: I remember that when I saw the first episode on the air, I literally fainted. I was in a writers’ meeting at my house preparing the next day’s material, and I got lightheaded and walked up to my kitchen and fell. The writers heard the clinking and clamoring, the disturbance, and came and rescued me, but that’s how disturbed I was at myself. The show was lousy, and I was super-lousy in it. To this day, I can’t even really watch it. It was a dark period in my life. I’m happy to say that it did, nevertheless, lead me to my opportunity on Growing Pains. But Thicke Of The Night was… I made the big mistake of listening to, paying attention to, some of my press clippings and thinking I was all that, and I really wasn’t. And the irony is that in today’s marketplace, those ratings would be considered a huge hit. Back then, it was just me and Johnny Carson, and I was getting clobbered, but I was getting clobbered and putting up numbers that today would be competitive in late-night with anybody, including Dave [Letterman] and Conan [O’Brien]. Not that we deserved it. It’s not false modesty when I say I was pretty bad at it. I was quite terrific as an afternoon host, as a schmoozer kind of guy, more in the Mike Douglas, Merv Griffin mode, back when those shows were popular. I was good at that. I had a very successful show in Canada doing that. But in late-night, you can’t take yourself too seriously. You can’t think you’re all that or too sexy. It’s an environment in which you have to go for the jugular. You have to be prepared to be mean and nasty and get your laughs. And I say that with great admiration for the guys who do it. It really takes a stand-up mentality, where you can go for the killer joke at anybody’s expense at any time, in your monologue and beyond. That just wasn’t my background. AVC: You lacked the killer instinct? AT: I did. I don’t know if it’s just an instinct performance-wise, or even as a writer. Although as a writer for guys like Pryor, and other people I really loved comedically, like Sam Kinison, my feeling always was “I can write naughty things for other people to say.” I never had the balls to say them myself. AVC: Johnny Carson was such an icon at the time that people were almost offended that anyone would dare compete with him. AT: Oh, absolutely. And we made the terrible mistake—and I was compliant and just went along with it—but we made the terrible mistake of having an entire promotional campaign that said, “We’re going to take on Johnny and beat him, and nobody’s ever been able to do it.” That was an outrageous thing for an unknown, semi-talented, no-name Canadian, a terrible position for him to take against an American broadcasting icon. That wasn’t my idea, but I let it go, I let them do it, because they said “This is how we’re going to get attention. This is how we’re going to get eyeballs on the show.” They were half-right, but the show didn’t deserve to stick anyway. It didn’t probably deserve to die the horrible death and be the scourge of the land that it became. But we certainly invited that kind of reaction by this horrible promotional campaign. AVC: The stakes were very, very high. AT: Yeah, and I became the go-to guy as the guru of late-night failure after that. Literally, a lot of guys who followed would call me and take me to dinner to find out how I blew it so badly. Pat Sajak, Dennis Miller, Rick Dees, Arsenio Hall, they all called and asked, “Alan how did you fuck this up so bad? Tell us what mistakes not to make.” I did have a couple of important tips for the guys who followed, but it wasn’t until Chevy Chase and Magic Johnson came along that the stench of Thicke Of The Night began to dissipate. AVC: They usurped you as the people behind the ultimate failed talk show? AT: I had to be preempted by guys who raised the bar or lowered it, I guess. Growing Pains (1985-1992)—“Dr. Jason Seaver” AT: Loved it. Proud of it. Proud of what it stood for. I share the corny family values espoused on that show. Happy for the role, both, as I said, what it stood for and what it did for me and my life and my family and my career. So if that’s what goes on my tombstone, I’m perfectly comfortable with it. It was a great opportunity that made my life good and something that I can show to my 12-year-old now in reruns. Corny and dated as it is, it’s still relatable, understandable, and he can look at it and say “Yeah, I get it. Now I see what you did before I was born.”

Two days before her husband Alan Thicke passed away at the age of 69, Tanya Thicke shared a sweet photo of the couple on Instagram. “I’ve got the cutest designated driver EVER!!!” Tanya captioned a photo of herself looking adoringly at her husband while sitting in a car. “JP & @eloisedejoria sure know how to throw a party! Thx you for having us. #friendsforever #patron #family,” Tanya continued. Days earlier, Tanya. who married Alan on May 7, 2005 in Cabo San Lucas, shared a throwback photo of herself with her husband and stepson Carter Thicke. “When you’re just trying to get that perfect Xmas card pic! #2010 #tbt,” she captioned the Christmas-themed photo from 2010. The couple wed just one month after they announced their engagement in April 2005. “I’ll be on my third honeymoon, so I’m more of an authority than I care to be,” Thicke quipped in a statement about his engagement to his longtime model girlfriend. The Growing Pains actor gushed about his wife in an April 2014 interview with PEOPLE: “My wife who is a wonderful yin and yang personality – hot and cold – I call her the sexy, loveable tornado is completely fascination and out of control and very sexy.” On Tuesday, Dec. 13, Alan passed away at the age of 69, a publicist for his son Robin Thicke confirmed to PEOPLE. The veteran actor is survived by his wife, Tanya, and his three sons: Robin, Carter and Brennan.

## Remembering Alan Thicke: A Beloved Actor and Father

The entertainment world and fans alike are mourning the sudden loss of Alan Thicke, the cherished actor best known for his iconic role as TV's favorite dad. Thicke passed away unexpectedly on Tuesday after suffering a heart attack while playing hockey with his son, Carter Thicke. He leaves behind his wife, Tanya, and his three sons: Robin, Brennan, and Carter.

### A Flood of Tributes

News of Alan Thicke's passing quickly spread, sparking an immense wave of heartfelt tributes from fans, friends, and colleagues across social media. His son, Carter, shared his profound grief, posting on Twitter, "Today I lost my best friend and my idol, and the world lost one of its finest. You are a legend and I love you Pops. Until next time." This touching message was shared alongside a touching photograph of Alan and Carter.

Singer Robin Thicke also paid a moving tribute to his father in an interview with the *L.A. Times*. He described his father as "the greatest man I ever met" and reflected on his passing, noting, "the good thing was that he was beloved and he had closure."

### A Life Lived Actively

Thicke's sudden death comes as a profound shock, particularly given his consistently active lifestyle. Just eighteen months before his passing, the avid hockey player had shared a photo of himself enjoying the sport on the ice with his son, Carter, highlighting their shared passion.

The sudden and tragic passing of beloved actor Alan Thicke on December 13th, 2016, sent profound shockwaves through Hollywood and resonated deeply with fans worldwide. For his son, Carter Thicke, the loss was particularly devastating, as he poignantly described his father not only as his "best friend" but also as his "idol." The heartbreaking event occurred on a Tuesday evening while Alan, a celebrated figure renowned for his iconic role as Dr. Jason Seaver in the hit sitcom "Growing Pains," was engaged in a cherished pastime – playing ice hockey with Carter. The 69-year-old actor suffered a massive heart attack, which tragically led to his death shortly thereafter, as confirmed by reports from the Los Angeles Times.

The news of the Golden Globe-nominated actor's untimely demise broke with astonishing speed. Within hours, Carter, who was 19 years old at the time, took to social media to share an incredibly heartfelt tribute. His poignant tweet, accompanied by a touching photograph of him and his father, captured the essence of their profound bond: "Today I lost my best friend and my idol, and the world lost one of it’s finest. You are a legend and I love you Pops. Until next time." This raw and honest expression of love and admiration immediately resonated with many, highlighting the deep connection between father and son.

Alan Thicke, a proud Canadian, shared a deep and abiding passion for ice hockey, the nation's most prevalent winter sport, with his son Carter. This shared love for the game was beautifully illustrated in a sweet photograph Alan posted in July 2015. The image showcased the father-son duo on the ice rink, both clad in hockey uniforms. Alan captioned the heartwarming picture with the affectionate words: "Like Father, like son. The Thicke apple doesn’t fall far from the tree. #CanadianAndProud #Hockey #TBT." This intimate glimpse into their shared activities underscored the strong and joyful connection they cultivated through their mutual enthusiasm for the sport.

Following his father's unexpected death, Robin Thicke, Alan's 39-year-old son and fellow musician, also spoke to the Los Angeles Times, sharing his deep respect and immense love for his father. Robin described Alan as "the greatest man I ever met" and found a measure of solace in the knowledge that his father "was beloved and he had closure." Despite the abrupt nature of his passing, Robin revealed that he had recently spent significant quality time with his father, during which he had openly expressed his profound love and admiration. "I saw him a few days ago and told him how much I loved and respected him," Robin shared, emphasizing the profound importance of those final, heartfelt moments. This sentiment was echoed by other close family members and friends who noted Alan's consistent warmth and affection.

Alan Thicke's enduring legacy extends far beyond his extensive acting career and his contributions to television. He was a devoted family man, a role he cherished deeply, and is survived by his wife, Tanya, and his three sons: Robin, Carter, and Brennan. His work on shows like "Growing Pains" and his engaging, often humorous, on-screen persona left an indelible mark on popular culture. Furthermore, his behind-the-scenes work as a songwriter and producer showcased his multifaceted talent. The overwhelming outpouring of tributes from friends, colleagues, and fans alike serves as a powerful testament to the positive and lasting impact he had on the lives of those around him. The world remembers Alan Thicke not merely as a talented actor, but as a loving father, a devoted husband, a supportive friend, and a true gentleman whose wit and warmth will be deeply missed. His influence can still be felt in the entertainment industry and in the hearts of those who knew him.

Alan Thicke is widely remembered for his iconic role as the warm and wise father, Jason Seaver, on the beloved sitcom "Growing Pains." This defining part, which he played from 1985, saw him portraying a work-from-home psychiatrist navigating family life with his wife, played by Joanna Kerns. Thicke's genuine understanding of psychology, stemming from his own background, lent an authentic and relatable quality to his portrayal of the caring patriarch, solidifying his image as a quintessential television dad.

Following the show's conclusion, Thicke's personal life saw a significant shift. He divorced his wife of 14 years, Gloria Loring, and successfully gained custody of their two sons, Brennan and Robin, who were then 11 and 9 years old respectively. This period marked a new chapter for Thicke, balancing his professional legacy with his role as a devoted father.

The entertainment world is in mourning following the unexpected death of Alan Thicke, the beloved actor best known for his iconic role as Jason Seaver on the 1980s sitcom "Growing Pains." Thicke, who was 69 years old, passed away on Tuesday night after suffering a heart attack while playing hockey with his 19-year-old son, Carter. His sudden departure has sent ripples of grief through Hollywood, with numerous friends and fellow celebrities sharing their heartfelt tributes.

The outpouring of sadness underscores Thicke's significant impact on the industry and the personal connections he forged. Celebrities like Ellen DeGeneres expressed profound sorrow, tweeting, "America loved Alan Thicke. I'm so sad he's gone. Sending so much love to his family." Larry King echoed these sentiments, remembering Thicke as "A wonderful talent, a wonderful man." Dave Coulier shared a personal anecdote, posting, "RIP Alan Thicke. We skated side by side for years. Say hello to Gordie and The Rocket for me."

Further tributes highlighted Thicke's warmth and kindness. Bob Saget lamented, "So sad is the passing of Alan Thicke. Such a good husband, father, brother, and friend. He will be deeply missed." Candace Cameron Bure, who recently worked with Thicke on "Fuller House," shared, "I'm sad beyond words that Alan Thicke has passed away. I've known you since I was 8 years old…" Ryan Seacrest noted Thicke was "a great friend over the years," while Gilbert Gottfried fondly recalled him as a "Wife Swap partner." Russell Simmons declared, "The world has lost an icon." Alyssa Milano described Thicke as "always the nicest and happiest person in the room," and Olivia Munn remembered him as "Such a nice guy." Demi Lovato recalled meeting Thicke and his wife Tanya, sending prayers to his family. Montel Williams questioned the year's toll, stating, "Now Alan Thicke… Can we get a do-over on 2016? Too many good people lost." John Legend, who knew Thicke through his son Robin, remembered him as "always so kind to me." Thicke's recent work included a role in the 2016 sequel "Fuller House," where he reunited with former co-stars Candace Cameron Bure, Bob Saget, and Dave Coulier.

query: What changes were made to the Mega Millions game structure on October 28, 2017?

documents.text: MEGA MILLIONS FAQs Can I Buy Mega Millions Tickets in Illinois? Yes! Mega Millions is available for purchase at all Illinois Lottery retailers, online and through the mobile app. Why is Mega Millions changing? Mega Millions is changing to have to faster growing jackpots. When did Mega Millions change? The changes to Mega Millions occured on Saturday, October 28, 2017. The first drawing impacted will be the Tuesday, October 31, 2017 drawing. The last drawing under the current game structure will be Friday, October 27, 2017. How much does it cost to play Mega Millions? The price to play Mega Millions increased on 10/28/2017 to $2 (from $1). The Megaplier is an additional $1 to the cost of your ticket. What exactly has changed with Mega Millions? Beginning October 28, 2017, the starting jackpot increased to $40 million (previously $15 million). Players will now select 5 numbers from 1 to 70 (previously 75) and choose 1 Mega Ball number from 1-25 (previously 15). The smallest prize is now $2 (previously $1). The new prize structure should produce faster growing jackpots, and will have better odds of winning $1 million at 2nd prize level! How did the October 28, 2017 Mega Millions changes impact multi-draw? Beginning August 4th, 2017 the amount of advanced draws available for purchase began to decrease, preventing players from purchasing draws beyond October 27th, 2017. Multi-draw and advance play options will resume to 25 draws on October 28th, 2017. How did the October 28, 2017 Mega Millions changes impact subscriptions? Beginning August 4th, 2017 the length of subscriptions available for purchase began to decrease, preventing players from purchasing subscriptions with draws beyond October 27th, 2017. If you held a Mega Millions subscription, a new subscription will need to be purchased at illinoislottery.com on or after October 28th, 2017. Where can I find the winning numbers? Winning numbers can be found on the Illinois Lottery website: www.illinoislottery.com or the Illinois Lottery Mobile App. Where can I find information on how to play and win Mega Millions, as well as how to play my numbers in multiple drawings? You can find information on how to play Mega Millions at retail or online, how to win, and information on our popular Multidraw option all on the Mega Millions page of the Illinois Lottery website. Simply select the appropriate tab: “How to Play at Retail,” “How to Play Online,” or “How to Win.” Where and when do the drawings take place? Mega Millions drawings are conducted Tuesday and Friday evenings at 11:00 p.m. EST by the Georgia Lottery. Where can I find Mega Millions prize information? The prize payout information for Mega Millions jackpots will be updated on www.illinoislottery.com and the Illinois Lottery Mobile App by Wednesday and Saturday, following scheduled drawings. The payout information must be verified by all participating states before being released. Note: Illinois Lottery only reports the payout information for Illinois winners. For payout information of all Mega Millions participating states please visit: www.megamillions.com Can I Purchase Mega Millions On The Internet? Yes, you can purchase Mega Millions online. Just, visit illinoislottery.com. Can I purchase Megaplier on the internet? Yes, Megaplier is available for purchase on the internet at the time you make a Mega Millions purchase, for an additional cost. Is Mega Millions available for purchase on the Illinois Lottery Mobile App? Yes, you can purchase Mega Millions on the Illinois Lottery mobile app, as well as check winning numbers and find Lottery retailers. If you have an Apple iPhone, the app can be downloaded from the iTunes store. If you have an Android device, visit www.illinoislottery.com from your mobile phone to download the app. Did I win? To see if you’ve won, enter your numbers into the Winning Number History Search or scan the barcode on your ticket at your favorite Illinois Lottery retailer. You can cash your prize up to $600 at most lottery retailers. Find a prize claim center near you, to cash prizes over $600. Mega Millions Subscriptions FAQs What length of subscriptions are available for Mega Millions? You can purchase customized subscriptions for 1 to 25 consecutive drawings or purchase one of the preset subscription lengths of up to 26 weeks. How Can I Choose The Number Of Drawings I’d Like To Purchase For Mega Millions Online? Purchasing Mega Millions is easy. Start by visiting illinoislottery.com and selecting the "Buy Now" button below the Mega Millions jackpot. Then, select the number of tickets you want to purchase, pick your numbers, and select the number of drawings you would like to purchase from the drop down. After you've made all of your desired selections, select "Add to Cart." Do I Have The Same Options For My Online Mega Millions Purchases That I Have In The Retail Stores? Yes. You have the same options online that you have in retail stores. Can I extend my play and subscribe on the Mobile App? Yes, you can extend your play and subscribe to Mega Millions via the Illinois Lottery Mobile App or website. Learn more about the Illinois Lottery Mobile App. How do I buy more than one set of numbers? After you have created your ticket and added one set of numbers to your shopping cart, an "Add Another Game" section will appear under your ticket details. Just select the "Buy Now" button under the Mega Millions logo to add more tickets. Learn more about the online shopping cart and other online play features. I purchased a Mega Millions subscription. When does my subscription start? Your subscription will start with the next available drawing. What happens when my subscription ends? When the end of your subscription is approaching, you will receive a notification email of the approaching end date. After your subscription ends, you can log in to your online account and repurchase your subscription. My subscription is over and I want to replay my numbers. How can I do that? If your subscription has already ended, you can easily replay your original numbers with the same options by copying your subscription. Just log in to your Illinois Lottery online account and go to My Account>>My Subscriptions and identify the subscription you wish to replay under the "Expired Subscriptions" tab. Click the "Copy" (stacked squares) icon and the options for that subscription will be visible. You can then add those numbers to your cart as is, or make edits to your subscription details. Your new subscription will start with the next available draw as long as you have completed your purchase prior to 9:15 p.m. on Monday, Thursday or Saturday evening. On-Line Game Rules

**Illinois Lottery Halts Powerball and Mega Millions Sales Amidst Budget Crisis**

Illinois players hoping to try their luck with Powerball and Mega Millions will be disappointed, as the Illinois Lottery has temporarily stopped selling tickets for these major games. This unexpected move, announced on Wednesday, stems directly from the Illinois General Assembly's failure to pass a budget that allocates necessary funds for the lottery in the upcoming 2018 fiscal year.

Greg Smith, the acting director of the Illinois Lottery, highlighted the critical need for public trust. He stated, "Players should be confident knowing the Illinois Lottery has the money to pay these winning claims. That means the General Assembly needs to approve a truly balanced budget that includes lottery funding in order to ensure all prize payments will occur."

This unusual halt could lead to significant delays for individuals winning prizes exceeding $25,000 if a budget resolution isn't reached by July 1. This scenario echoes a similar budget impasse in 2015, which resulted in delayed payouts for approximately 3,900 lottery winners, amounting to over $112 million. During that prior period, the lottery projected a revenue shortfall of up to $71.5 million due to these payment disruptions.

Lottery administrators had previously cautioned about this potential suspension, pointing to the state's ongoing three-year budget deadlock and its impact on agreements with the Multi-State Lottery Association.

The specific timing for the sales suspension is as follows:
*   **Powerball:** Ticket sales will end at 9 p.m. CT on June 28.
*   **Mega Millions:** Ticket sales will conclude on Friday, June 30, at 9:45 p.m. CT.

Importantly, any Powerball and Mega Millions tickets purchased before these suspension deadlines will still be honored.

The current legislative session highlights a significant disconnect between public promises of compromise and the reality of political action. Governor Bruce Rauner has promoted a "Capitol Compromise" plan, developed by Republicans without Democratic input, across his social media. Conversely, Democratic House Speaker Michael Madigan has brought several of Rauner's key legislative priorities to a vote, framing them as a "substantial compromise." Republicans, however, view these moves as insincere. This stark contrast reveals how "compromise" has become a rhetorical tool, used by both parties to assign blame for the impending budget deadline.

Despite the serious consequences of a budget failure, a resolution seems unlikely before the Friday deadline. Governor Rauner has warned, "If lawmakers fail to send a balanced budget package to my desk by Friday, we will have no choice but to keep them in session until they get the job done." The core of the two-year legislative deadlock remains: both sides agree a tax increase is necessary, but Rauner insists it be paired with regulatory reforms to boost the economy. Speaker Madigan opposes these reforms, arguing they would primarily benefit the wealthy.

Several key disagreements and recent events illustrate the complexity of this impasse. Governor Rauner has long pushed for a property tax freeze. Democrats proposed their own freeze, but Republicans criticized it for including numerous exemptions, claiming it wouldn't provide real taxpayer relief. While this specific measure failed, Democrats who voted for it may have gained political protection. Rauner's demand is a four-year statewide property tax freeze in exchange for a four-year income tax increase. The Democratic counter-proposal would freeze property taxes for four years but exempt Chicago, its public schools, and struggling districts. Crucially, it would also exclude taxes for debt or pension payments, a major sticking point for Republicans. Adding to the financial strain, a federal judge, Joan Lefkow, has indicated she might order the state to increase Medicaid payments. Comptroller Susana Mendoza has warned that delayed payments could impact other state spending. Judge Lefkow has sided with Medicaid patient advocates, suggesting the state might be violating federal court orders by not ensuring adequate care access for low-income individuals. Lawyers for Medicaid patients are expected to present evidence of harm from outstanding bills.

Behind the scenes, legislative leaders, including House Minority Leader Jim Durkin, have been in closed-door negotiations, with Governor Rauner reportedly working through Durkin. Publicly, however, each side continues to blame the other for the budget stalemate. On Wednesday, Speaker Madigan's office arranged votes on bills addressing Rauner's priorities in areas like workers' compensation, property tax relief, government consolidation, and pensions. House Minority Leader Durkin dismissed these votes as "political theater" for "gotcha votes," arguing they were premature given ongoing discussions. Republicans contended that Democrats were voting on incomplete measures for political gain.

This strategy of calling votes on contentious issues allows Speaker Madigan to achieve multiple goals. First, it offers political cover for Democratic lawmakers, especially those facing re-election, by letting them vote on popular measures. Second, it enables Madigan to use Republican votes against these measures as ammunition for political attacks. Third, it pressures GOP members, particularly Downstate Republicans affected by the budget impasse, potentially encouraging them to diverge from Governor Rauner. Representative David Harris (R-Arlington Heights) was the sole Republican to support the Democratic property tax freeze, stating, "If this is what it takes to help get us to a budget, then I think we really seriously need to be looking to vote 'yes' on the bill."

The consequences of failing to pass a budget by month's end are severe. Illinois risks its credit rating falling below investment grade, exclusion from multi-state lotteries like Powerball and Mega Millions, and potential loss of university accreditation. These outcomes underscore the urgent need for a bipartisan solution to the ongoing fiscal crisis.

Illinois's fiscal cliff raises a critical question: what happens when a state cannot pay its debts? Investors holding Illinois debt are understandably concerned due to the state's prolonged budget impasse. This anxiety is reflected in declining bond prices and recent downgrades by rating agencies, pushing Illinois's debt to just one notch above "junk" status. Moody's Investors Service and Standard & Poor's have issued dire warnings, signaling a significant threat to the state's fiscal health.

The market's reaction to Illinois's budget deadlock is amplified by the unfolding crisis in Puerto Rico. The U.S. territory, grappling with its own severe debt crisis, has secured federal assistance. However, its recently adopted budget, endorsed by a congressionally appointed Fiscal Oversight Board, prioritizes other expenditures and pensioners while effectively "stiffing" its bondholders. Pensioners are largely protected, and other government expenses are slated for increases. This approach has sent ripples of concern through financial markets, as noted by analysts at Fitch Ratings.

This Puerto Rican precedent carries significant implications for Illinois. Bondholders fear a similar outcome should Illinois face a fiscal collapse. While Illinois pensioners might initially welcome such a prospect, the more profound lesson from Puerto Rico, as articulated by experts like Ike Brannon, a visiting fellow at the Cato Institute, is the potential erosion of constitutional protections when the federal government intervenes. Puerto Rico's constitution explicitly mandates that general-obligation bondholders be paid before all other obligations. This promise was a cornerstone of its ability to borrow at favorable rates, yet the current plan involves foregoing more than $2 billion in annual interest payments for at least the next five years, effectively disregarding this mandate under the oversight of the Fiscal Oversight Board.

Illinois's constitution, while not containing such an explicit promise to bondholders, does contain provisions that could be vulnerable in a federal intervention scenario. Crucially, it prohibits the state from impairing promised benefits to pensioners. If the federal government were to step in to resolve an Illinois budget crisis, bondholders would likely bear a significant burden, according to analyses by the Brookings Institution. Furthermore, the state's rigid pension protections and its prohibition on progressive taxation would likely be placed on the negotiating table. This scenario, while perhaps not immediate, could be closer than many anticipate.

The inevitable downward spiral for Illinois could be triggered by the next economic downturn, coupled with a stock market correction. This combination is poised to cause a precipitous decline in Illinois's tax revenues and a plummet in the value of its pension funds. This decline could reach a point where no achievable rate of return on remaining pension assets could ensure solvency, as projected by the American Enterprise Institute. Such a development could render the state unable to borrow money, inevitably forcing federal intervention.

Should this federal intervention occur, it is a safe assumption that the burden of adjustment would be shared broadly, with taxpayers, bondholders, and public pensioners alike facing difficult compromises. In the most optimistic scenario for state workers, the outcome might resemble the pension reform bill that was invalidated by the Illinois State Supreme Court in 2015, which sought to end automatic cost-of-living increases and raise the retirement age. However, pension adjustments could extend much further, potentially involving reductions in benefits for those who artificially inflated their final salaries or for those receiving multiple state pensions. Moreover, once federal authorities permit the abandonment of the prohibition on progressive tax rates, Illinois could resemble New Jersey, with top marginal income tax rates approaching 10 percent, as observed by the Tax Foundation.

Investors are justified in their wariness of holding Illinois bonds. However, the most critical lesson from Puerto Rico's fiscal crisis is that constitutional promises can become remarkably fragile once the federal government becomes involved. Consequently, Illinois's pensioners and taxpayers have as much reason to be concerned as its bondholders. The potential for federal intervention introduces a complex web of considerations, impacting not only the state's financial obligations but also the fundamental rights and protections of its citizens. The precedent set by Puerto Rico suggests that even constitutionally enshrined promises may be subject to renegotiation under duress, a prospect that should concern all stakeholders in Illinois's fiscal future.

Illinois stands on the precipice of a historic credit downgrade, a situation that could mark it as the first state since 1970 to lose its investment-grade status. This critical juncture stems directly from a prolonged budget impasse, with state leaders poised to miss a crucial deadline. Consequently, Illinois will enter its third consecutive year without a comprehensive spending plan. The Illinois House's failure to vote on a budget by Wednesday ensures the state government, already mired in gridlock, will not pass a budget by midnight. This delay significantly complicates an already difficult task. Approving a budget, a process that has eluded Illinois for 700 days, will now require a supermajority of three-fifths.

The core of this fiscal crisis lies in the persistent disagreement between Democratic lawmakers, who control both legislative chambers, and Republican Governor Bruce Rauner. Their repeated inability to reconcile their approaches to addressing chronic budget deficits has led to this precarious state. This problem was exacerbated by the expiration of tax hikes in January 2015. John Humphrey, head of credit research for Gurtin Municipal Bond Management, which manages approximately $10.1 billion in state and local debt and has deliberately avoided Illinois bonds, expressed grave concern. "We are probably approaching that point of impaired ability to function at basic level," Humphrey stated, adding, "We’ve already probably passed that point. We haven’t seen this in a modern state before." This self-inflicted fiscal crisis has left Illinois, the fifth most populous state, with a staggering $14.5 billion in unpaid bills. Essential entities such as universities and social service providers, heavily reliant on state funding, have been severely impacted. Furthermore, Illinois's standing in the bond market has been significantly undermined. If no last-minute deal emerges before midnight, it is increasingly probable that Illinois will commence its third fiscal year on July 1 without a budget.

The legislative stalemate is a primary driver of the crisis. Democratic House Speaker Michael Madigan has accused Governor Rauner of "holding the budget hostage" to his agenda. Madigan announced in an emailed statement on Wednesday that Democrats would continue working on a budget and planned to hold public hearings, beginning in Chicago on June 8. Governor Rauner, in turn, criticized the Democrats, labeling the failure a "complete dereliction of duty by the majority in the General Assembly." He insists that any tax increases must be contingent upon a property tax freeze and local control over those levies. Governor Rauner, the first Republican to lead Illinois since 2003, is adamant that any spending plan must align with his core priorities, including a property tax freeze. While the Senate approved measures on Tuesday to lock in most local real estate levies for two years, Republicans argued these provisions did not go far enough.

Bond-rating agencies have issued stark warnings of further downgrades, underscoring the potential for Illinois to lose its investment-grade status. Investors have long penalized the state for its financial instability, and this penalty has intensified amid the ongoing stalemate. Illinois's 10-year bonds currently yield 4.4 percent, a premium of 2.5 percentage points over top-rated debt. This yield gap, a key indicator of perceived risk, is the widest it has been since at least January 2013 and surpasses that of any other of the 19 states tracked by Bloomberg. Moody's Investors Service reported in a March report that the current-year operating deficit stands at approximately $6 billion. Both Moody's and S&P Global Ratings have cautioned that Illinois could face further downgrades if a budget is not enacted. The agencies currently rate the state at Baa2 and BBB, respectively, which are two notches above junk status.

The mounting backlog of unpaid bills further exacerbates the fiscal strain. The state comptroller estimates that Illinois will incur at least $800 million in interest and fees on overdue bills by the end of June. Ted Hampton, Moody's lead analyst on Illinois, described this backlog as "headed in the direction of being a factor that just by itself really threatens the sort of financial foundations of the state," citing ongoing litigation from creditors. He added, "There is kind of an uncertain but very real legal and political limit to the state’s ability to keep deferring payments."

A delegation of school superintendents from across the state held a press conference at the Illinois State Capitol on Wednesday, advocating for a full-year budget rather than a temporary stopgap measure and calling for reforms to the school funding formula. Illinois currently owes school districts $1.1 billion, a figure described as "unconscionable" by Tony Sanders, CEO of the Elgin-based school system, the state's second-largest. The ongoing impasse is also severely impacting social service agencies that support vulnerable populations, including children, the elderly, and individuals with mental illness. Laurence Msall, president of the Civic Federation, a group that monitors the state's finances, stated at a press conference last week, "Forty-nine other states would never try this experiment." He continued, "Only Illinois, which has seen its credit rating crumble, which has seen its social service infrastructure weaken, which has seen its higher education punched into the stomach in terms of what its future looks like, would try this experiment. It’s not working. We have to have a state budget to move forward."

Despite the legislative gridlock, Illinois has not defaulted on any bond payments, and state law mandates continued monthly deposits into its debt-service funds. However, the protracted conflict has hindered any progress in addressing the state's retirement system, which carries over $129 billion in unfunded liabilities—a significant stressor that has contributed to its declining credit rating.

State leaders have until the end of June to reach an agreement before the start of fiscal year 2018 on July 1. Last year, lawmakers passed a six-month stopgap budget to ensure schools remained open and state operations continued. However, achieving consensus has proven elusive. On May 23, the Illinois Senate, with only Democratic votes, approved a spending plan that included income tax increases and an expanded sales tax. As of Tuesday afternoon, this plan still required House approval. Governor Rauner has maintained his demand for a property tax freeze before considering any income tax hikes, and he rejected the two-year property tax freeze passed by the Senate, with spokeswoman Eleni Demertzis calling it "a phony two-year freeze riddled with holes" due to its exemption of Chicago and Rauner's preference for a longer duration.

As Illinois lawmakers grapple with an impending budget deadline, Governor Bruce Rauner and House Speaker Michael Madigan are pursuing divergent paths, highlighting the deep divisions in Springfield. With the fiscal year ending Friday, the state faces significant repercussions, including potential shutdowns of transportation projects and a downgrade of its bond status to "junk" if a balanced budget isn't enacted.

Governor Rauner has declared his intention to keep legislators in session until a budget is passed, stating, "If the legislature fails to send a balanced budget package to my desk by Friday, we will have no choice but to keep them in session until they get the job done." This ultimatum comes as legislators have been in a special session since June 21, initially called by Rauner for ten days to foster agreement.

Despite the ongoing negotiations, disagreements persist regarding several of Governor Rauner's key reform proposals, which he deems essential for his signature on a budget. Nevertheless, House Democrats have advanced four reform measures, with only a four-year property tax freeze failing to pass. This particular measure, which exempted home rule municipalities and was set to take immediate effect, required 71 votes for approval.

The other reform initiatives, which garnered passage with a lower threshold of 60 votes, included:

*   Workers' compensation reform
*   Pension reform
*   Local government consolidation

The passage of some of these reforms allows Speaker Madigan to assert that he has addressed Governor Rauner's budgetary demands as part of the spending plan. Democrats urged Republicans to support these measures, even if not fully aligned with their positions. State Representative Will Guzzardi (D-Chicago) implored, "Please, take a win. We are going to put bills on the board that are not 100 percent of what you want here but they’re going to be awfully, darn close." He added, "At some point in the next 72 hours, vote on bills that you can go home and say we got reform. Take a win, please. The state needs you guys to rise to the occasion right now."

Republicans, however, rejected this sentiment, arguing that negotiations are still active and that voting on these reforms prematurely disrupts the process. They contend that these reforms are not merely the governor's preferences but are crucial for restoring the state's financial health. State Representative Steve Andersson (R-Geneva) stated, "We can’t do this. We can’t vote in good conscience on this. This isn’t about not taking the win. It’s about finishing the work and then taking the win together."

Concerns were raised by Senate Democrats regarding the four-year property tax freeze, particularly its potential impact on struggling school districts. The House Democratic proposal specifically excluded Chicago Public Schools and 18 other financially challenged districts, while also omitting pension debt. House Majority Leader Barbara Flynn-Currie (D-Chicago) highlighted the extent of their concessions, noting that the property tax bill demonstrated "how far out of the way we have gone." She explained, "This is our effort to say to the governor, although we don’t think we need non-budgetary items to govern budgetary decisions, we understand that it is very important to the person who currently occupies the second floor. So we are prepared to go a good, long way toward what it is that Governor Rauner has requested in order to see to it that we can have a budget."

While the workers' compensation bill did pass, the Illinois Manufacturers’ Association expressed opposition, citing that despite some cost-cutting changes, it increased employer costs and failed to adequately address high medical treatment expenses by not altering the medical fee schedule. Legislative leaders were scheduled to reconvene on Thursday morning. Madigan's spokesman, Steve Brown, characterized the extended special session as unsurprising, asserting, "All he [Rauner] needs to do is understand that the Legislature has gone way past middle ground to compromise. We’ve been working on his reforms."

Illinois is on the verge of a significant financial event: becoming the first U.S. state to receive a "junk" credit rating. This warning comes from S&P Global Ratings, which states that this downgrade could happen if lawmakers don't agree on a budget before the fiscal year begins on July 1. This is the third year in a row Illinois has faced the possibility of starting the year without an approved budget.

### The State's Deepening Fiscal Crisis

Illinois is already dealing with serious financial problems. These include:

*   **A large amount of unpaid bills:** The state owes approximately $15 billion, which is about 40% of its operating budget. This is much higher than in previous years.
*   **Significant pension debt:** Moody's estimates that the state has $251 billion in unfunded pension liabilities. This money is owed to retired teachers and other state employees.
*   **Extremely low cash reserves:** The state's cash is so low that it might soon be unable to pay lottery winners.

### Consequences of a "Junk" Rating

A "junk" credit rating, also known as below investment grade, signals a higher risk of default. Historically, this has preceded bankruptcy for cities like Stockton, California, and Detroit, as well as for Puerto Rico. While Moody's points out that only a small fraction of local governments have such ratings, the impact on a state would be much more severe.

S&P Global Ratings has warned that "Illinois is now at risk of entering a negative credit spiral." This warning was issued after the legislature adjourned without a budget deal. Both S&P and Moody's have already lowered Illinois's credit rating to just one step above junk status.

Governor Bruce Rauner has emphasized the urgency, tweeting, "Time is running out." He has urged lawmakers to pass a compromise plan to "bring real changes to our broken system."

### Political Stalemate and Its Impact

The ongoing political disagreement has not only led to the backlog of unpaid bills but also threatens essential state services. For example, sales of Powerball and Mega Millions tickets in Illinois could be halted if a budget is not approved by Wednesday. S&P's main concern is that "The unrelenting political brinksmanship now poses a threat to the timely payment of the state's core priority payments." This continued financial instability could further damage investor confidence and make it more expensive for the state to borrow money.

Illinois is on the precipice of a significant financial milestone, poised to become the first U.S. state to have its credit rating downgraded to "junk" status. This impending downgrade, warned by S&P Global Ratings, threatens to exacerbate the state's already substantial multibillion-dollar financial deficit and will likely result in increased borrowing costs for taxpayers for years to come.

The warning from S&P comes with a stark condition: the agency will likely lower Illinois' creditworthiness below investment grade if feuding lawmakers fail to agree on a state budget for a third consecutive year. This failure to reach a budget agreement directly impacts the state's ability to borrow money for essential infrastructure projects, such as building roads, and for refinancing existing debt. As the July 1 start of the new fiscal year loomed, lawmakers meeting in Springfield for a special legislative session remained deadlocked, casting a grim outlook on the possibility of a timely resolution.

Brian Battle, director at Performance Trust Capital Partners, a Chicago-based investment firm, emphasized the widespread implications of this fiscal impasse. He stated, "It isn't a political show. Everyone in Illinois has a stake in what's happening here. One day everybody will wake up and say 'What happened? Why are my taxes going up so much?'"

### Understanding the Current Situation and Potential Consequences

**Why is this happening now?**
Ratings agencies have been progressively downgrading Illinois' credit rating for several years. However, the pace of these downgrades has accelerated due to the prolonged stalemate between Republican Governor Bruce Rauner and the Democratic-controlled General Assembly. Key concerns fueling these downgrades include:

*   **Massive Pension Debt:** The state's unfunded pension obligations represent a significant financial burden.
*   **Unpaid Bills Backlog:** A substantial $15 billion backlog of unpaid bills highlights immediate fiscal pressures.
*   **Revenue Decline:** The expiration of a temporary income tax increase in 2015 led to a notable drop in state revenue.

S&P explicitly stated its concern when it dropped Illinois' rating to one level above junk status, noting, "In our view, the unrelenting political brinkmanship now poses a threat to the timely payment of the state's core priority payments." Moody's echoed this sentiment, observing, "As the regular legislative session elapsed, political barriers to progress appeared to harden, indicating both the severity of the state's challenges and the political difficulty of advocating their solutions."

**What does a 'junk' rating signify?**
A "junk" rating is analogous to a credit score, but applied to a governmental entity rather than an individual. When a state, city, or county needs to borrow money, it issues bonds. Investors assess the risk associated with purchasing these bonds based on ratings provided by agencies like Moody's and S&P. A junk rating indicates a heightened risk that the state may default on its debt obligations. Consequently, many mutual funds and individual investors, who collectively represent over half of the bond market, will refrain from purchasing these bonds. Investors willing to assume the higher risk, such as distressed debt investors, will only do so if compensated with a significantly higher interest rate. While no other U.S. state has reached this status, municipalities like Chicago, Atlantic City, and Detroit have experienced junk ratings. Notably, Detroit saw its rating restored to investment grade in 2015 after emerging from bankruptcy, a recourse unavailable to states by law.

**What are the projected costs?**
Battle anticipates that the increased interest payments Illinois will incur the next time it needs to sell bonds could amount to "tens of millions" of dollars annually, or even more. This elevated cost of borrowing directly reduces the funds available for essential public services, including education, state parks, social services, and infrastructure maintenance. "For the taxpayer, it will cost more to get a lower level of service," Battle explained. Comptroller Susana Mendoza, responsible for managing the state's finances, concurred, stating, "It's going to cost people more every day. Our reputation really can't get much worse, but our state finances can."

**Are there other impacts?**
The state's deteriorating creditworthiness is already affecting other entities that have historically relied on state funding, such as local governments and universities. S&P has already downgraded the bonds held by:

*   The Metropolitan Pier & Exposition Authority
*   The Illinois Sports Facilities Authority

These entities are responsible for managing key Chicago landmarks and venues, including Navy Pier, McCormick Place, and Guaranteed Rate Field. Furthermore, five Illinois universities have also received a junk rating:

*   Eastern Illinois University
*   Governors State University
*   Northeastern Illinois University
*   Northern Illinois University
*   Southern Illinois University

This story has been corrected to reflect that the venue where the White Sox play is now called Guaranteed Rate Field, not U.S. Cellular Field.

query: What was the decision made by the Orlando Sentinel's editors regarding the front page after the nightclub shooting?

documents.text: Click to email this to a friend (Opens in new window) Click to share on Google+ (Opens in new window) Click to share on LinkedIn (Opens in new window) Click to share on Facebook (Opens in new window) Click to share on Twitter (Opens in new window) Editors at the Orlando Sentinel had a couple of options for Monday's front page: Offer a traditional treatment with news of the death of 50 in a nightclub shooting early Sunday morning or go with full-page editorial? When Todd Stewart, senior editor for multimedia and visuals, left a late afternoon meeting, he thought the answer was a compromise that included the news and some of the editorial. But, he thought, the compromise felt a little soft. The front page some 30 hours after the nation's worst mass shooting should make a statement. "I actually never worked up the compromise." All day, people in Orlando and around the world saw images of the crime scene. "It's time for the newspaper to put a voice to what we're all feeling," said Stewart, who started working early this morning before coming into the newsroom at 7:30 a.m. For the first 10 hours of his day, no one talked about the print newspaper, he said. "That's a change, even in the last couple of years," he said. "I'm proud of that." Unlike the profusion of cable news pundits and politicians who used the shooting as a jumping-off point to make a political point or further their own agendas, the Sentinel opted for a message of unity in its editorial: Beyond offering our abundant prayers and sympathy, we must ensure that those who survive — who will forever carry the scars from the trauma — know that they are not alone today, tomorrow or in the months and years to come. Let our community define itself by our unequivocal response: United. Included in the Sentinel's ongoing work on Sunday — 30 videos and 40 stories. Avido Khahaifa, the Sentinel's editor and publisher, isn't sure there's a right or wrong decision for Monday's front page. He understands the impulse to take a traditional approach. "I just think this called for something different from us," he said. "What we're doing kind of captures what the day has felt like."

CLOSE Scores of people were killed and many more were wounded in the shooting at a gay nightclub in Orlando, Florida. Orlando police officers seen outside of Pulse nightclub after a fatal shooting and hostage situation on June 12, 2016 in Orlando, Florida. The suspect was shot and killed by police after 20 people died and 42 were injured. (Photo by Gerardo Mora/Getty Images) (Photo: Gerardo Mora, Getty Images) A night of partying and dancing at Pulse, a gay nightclub in Orlando, turned into a killing ground and three hours of terror for early-morning revelers when a gunman opened fire around 2 a.m. ET inside the club, killing 50 people and wounding at least 53 in what is being called the worst mass shooting in U.S. history. Here’a a rough timeline of the three hours from the time the gunman gained entrance to the club, Pulse Orlando, around 2 a.m. ET to the time authorities killed the assailant around 5 a.m. ET. (All times are Eastern standard time). SIEGE BEGINS: 2:02 a.m. Shots ring out as a police officer working as a security guard engages in gun battle with assailant, Omar Mateen, 29, of Port St. Lucie, Fla., outside the Pulse Orlando nightclub around closing time, according to Orlando police chief John Mina. Mateen was armed with an AR-15-style assault rifle, a handgun and an unidentified device. The gunman makes way into club, where more than 300 people were enjoying Latino night, and opens fire, causing chaos as patrons realize what is unfolding. Mateen fired an "unknown number of rounds," the police chief said. "There were numerous shots." Eyewitnesses said people on the dance floor and around the bar area dived to the floor, with some lucky enough to flee. Jackie Smith, who was inside the club and says two friends were shot, told the Associated Press after release from Orlando Regional Medical Center: "Some guy walked in and started shooting everybody. He had an automatic rifle, so nobody stood a chance. I just tried to get out of there." 2:06 a.m. Mina Justice was sound asleep when, she told the Associated Press, she got a text from her son, Eddie, from inside the club. “Mommy I love you,” the message said. “In club they shooting.” CLOSE Mina Justice's 30-year-old son Eddie texted her from the Pulse nightclub when the shooting occurred. He said he ran into a bathroom with other patrons to hide and told her to call police. He then texted her: "He's coming, I'm gonna die." AP 2:09 a.m. About seven minutes after the first shot, the Pulse Orlando nightclub's Facebook page zapped out a chilling message: "Everyone get out of pulse and keep running." 2:17 a.m. @bjoewolf (Brandon Wolf), tweets: “Omg. Shooting at pulse. We hid in the bathroom. And we can’t find our friends." Club goer Christopher Hansen who escaped the club, later told CNN, “It’s just shocking, I just saw bodies going down.” Hansen estimates that 20 or 30 or even more shots were fired. Speaking to reporters after the assault, Florida Rep. Alan Grayson, a Democrat, said: "All the killing that was done was with an assault rifle, a single weapon...it was done very quickly also." Investigators from the office of the medical examiner investigate on the west side of Pulse nightclub. (Photo: Doug Clifford, AP) MULTI-HOUR HOSTAGE SITUATION ENSUES: 2 a.m. ET to 5 a.m. ET. Law enforcement and the shooter engage in three-hour standoff, with the assailant holding hostages. Updates and pleas for help from frightened patrons, many hiding in a restroom, trapped in the club were zapped out to police, friends and the world via text messages and social media, such as Twitter. The gunman, according to the FBI, made a call to 911 during the attack and pledged his support to the Islamic State, or ISIS. Multiple emergency vehicles and law enforcement teams respond to the scene of the shooting. Bomb units were sent to the scene, as were hazardous material teams. Around 3 a.m. Orlando Fire Department calls in the bomb squad. SWAT teams were also called in. 3:58 a.m. Orlando police, via Twitter, warn public to stay away from the nightclub area. Shooting at Pulse Nightclub on S Orange. Multiple injuries. Stay away from area. pic.twitter.com/5Di2mc6XUY — Orlando Police (@OrlandoPolice) June 12, 2016 Law enforcement officials say there was "some communication" with assailant, but would not provide details. "Once the initial shots stopped we were dealing with a hostage situation," Mina said in a press conference. Orlando Mayor Buddy Dyer says authorities learned through text messages from hostages in the club that between five and eight hostages were with the shooter in a room, while 15 to 25 others were in another room elsewhere in the nightclub. SIEGE ENDS AS POLICE STORM CLUB, KILL ASSAILANT 5 a.m. About three hours after the standoff began, Orlando police chief John Mina says the decision was made to storm the club and "rescue patrons (hiding) in a bathroom" and others still trapped inside the club in an effort to minimize loss of life. The police, Mina said, used a BearCat armored rescue vehicle to "get through a club wall." Cops also set off a "controlled detonation" around this time to distract the assailant. After the BearCat rammed the club, the shooter opened the club's door, according to Mayor Dyer, and that's when cops took aim at the shooter. Gunfire was "exchanged between the SWAT team and the suspect at 0500 hours," or 5 a.m., officials said later during a press conference. Police said the gunman was killed in the firefight. “We exchanged gunfire with the suspect, and he was dead at the scene," Mina said, adding that the SWAT team's decision to go in saved the lives of "at least 30 possible victims." 5:53 a.m. About an hour later the Orlando police department confirm via Twitter that the assailant, Mateen, had been killed: “Pulse Shooting: The shooter inside the club is dead.” Pulse Shooting: The shooter inside the club is dead. — Orlando Police (@OrlandoPolice) June 12, 2016 Skip in Skip x Embed x Share CLOSE DEADLY MASS SHOOTING AT ORLANDO NIGHTCLUB Melbourne vigil for Orlando victims | 1:43 Several hundred gather in Melbourne, Florida to honor the victims of the terror attack in Orlando. Video by Tim Shortt. Posted 6/17/16. 1 of 39 Skip in Skip x Embed x Share CLOSE DEADLY MASS SHOOTING AT ORLANDO NIGHTCLUB Human Rights Campaign headquarters stages seven-story Orlando memorial | 0:53 The Human Rights Campaign transformed its Washington, D.C. Headquarters on Friday, June 17, with large-scale images of the 49 victims of the June 12 shooting at Pulse, a gay nightclub in Orlando, Florida. 2 of 39 Skip in Skip x Embed x Share CLOSE DEADLY MASS SHOOTING AT ORLANDO NIGHTCLUB "We're here to hold your heart." Counseling in Orlando crisis. | 1:57 Counselors helping those affected by the mass shooting in Orlando share insight into recognizing need, solutions and why they help. The First Unitarian Church of Orlando is among those hosting counseling. For more information call 407-822-5036. 3 of 39 Skip in Skip x Embed x Share CLOSE DEADLY MASS SHOOTING AT ORLANDO NIGHTCLUB Remembering the 49 | 2:51 The names and faces of all 49 victims in the Orlando shooting. USA TODAY 4 of 39 Skip in Skip x Embed x Share CLOSE DEADLY MASS SHOOTING AT ORLANDO NIGHTCLUB Obama offers condolences to Orlando victims | 1:09 President Barack Obama offered his condolences to those impacted by the deadly nightclub shooting in Orlando, Florida. Obama said that today the country is reminded of what is good and that there is compassion, empathy and decency. (June 16) AP 5 of 39 Skip in Skip x Embed x Share CLOSE DEADLY MASS SHOOTING AT ORLANDO NIGHTCLUB Survivor: 'I was begging God please take me' | 1:51 A shooting victim describes how she made peace with dying as she saw bodies around her on the bathroom floor in the Orlando nightclub. (June 14) AP 6 of 39 Skip in Skip x Embed x Share CLOSE DEADLY MASS SHOOTING AT ORLANDO NIGHTCLUB FBI investigators interview Orlando gunman's wife | 0:38 The wife of the Orlando nightclub gunman is being interviewed by police and is cooperating with authorities. Noor Salman told police that her husband became radicalized in the last year. Glitz Ultra Lounge in Orlando, Florida, after the dust cleared from the shooting. All photos by Sofia Valiente VICE is tracking mass shootings in America in 2016, and comparing the numbers with their European counterparts. Read our rationale for the project and the metrics we're using here. As part of this project, every so often VICE will take a deeper look at one event that, while representative of a common type of mass shooting, didn't receive widespread coverage. This month, we look back on a February 2016 mass shooting at a nightclub in Orlando, Florida, in which two people were killed and nine more wounded. The first shots barely registered with Brandon*. From where he stood by the sinks in the men's room at the Glitz Ultra Lounge in Orlando, the noise was just a low pop-pop-pop swallowed by the din of around 300 people partying to blaring reggaeton. Outside the bathroom door, witnesses heard screams and saw people rushing from deeper within the crowded dance floor to the exits. But beyond that fulcrum of action, the sounds were so soft and brief that many in the club initially brushed them off. "The DJ said, 'Oh, it's just fireworks,'" Brandon recalls. "'Keep partying!'" Situated on the corner of Universal and Carrier, near the heart of Orlando's tourist district—a mile south of Universal Studios and down the block from the kiddy-theatrical Pirate's Dinner Adventure restaurant—Glitz seemed like it was in a safe part of town. But just before 1 AM on February 7, a mass shooting touched off in the outwardly family-friendly area. After those first few shots, a second round of gunfire unleashed by at least one additional shooter slammed into the wall separating the club from the bathroom with a loud bam-bam-bam. One bullet pierced the wall, striking a young man in the lower back as Brandon instinctively dove to the ground. Outside, the bullets ripped into the throng clustered at the edge of the floor by the men's room. Maria*, a young mother with another child on the way who was waiting for her fiancé to finish up in the bathroom, suddenly felt a bullet rip through her wrist. Then she saw a man with a gun rush past. Maria was one of at least three people shot by the bathroom door in that second burst of gunfire. By this point, the crowd near the bathrooms had devolved into a scrum rushing for the exits. But it took at least a couple more minutes before the terror penetrated the rest of the venue, still dominated as it was by throbbing neon and bass. A cellphone video shot in the bathroom shows the man who was struck in the back laying on the ground, at the end of a trickle trail of blood. Someone in the background yells, "He's shot, bro!" and a security guard pushing his way in screams, "Where?" over and over, while music continues to blast away. At 12:59 AM, a security guard referred to in a police account only as "Lehman" ran to Jerome Kenon, Landon Thomas, and Manuel Genao—all off-duty-but-uniformed Orlando Police Department officers hired to sit outside and project a sense of security. Within a minute of that trio's call to police dispatch, victims started streaming out in droves. Another video from inside the club around the same time shows the lights cut and the last attendees filing out, more confused than hectic, as security personnel scream, "Everybody out!" and a man in a long coat appears to perform CPR on a body splayed near the exit by the men's room. The rear entrance to the venue As they waited for on-duty cops to arrive, Officers Genao and Thomas followed Lehman around the exterior of the club to the exit by the bathroom. Witnesses there told them that a man with a gun had just run out of the building through that door and turned the corner toward the club's rear exit. When they went around back, the group found 23-year-old Jose Jaime Brull-Lopez holding a Glock with an extended magazine, his shirt covered in blood. He stood over the body of 22-year-old Joseph Lugo Villalobos. Once he spotted the off-duty officers and Lehman, Brull-Lopez tossed the gun onto the roof of a storage shed attached to the rear of the club. The officers then ordered him to the ground at gunpoint. Soon after, on-duty Orlando cops arrived and found another loaded magazine in the suspect's pocket. They eventually booked Brull-Lopez into custody on charges of carrying a concealed firearm and tampering with physical evidence, but initially declined to charge him for the shooting itself. Brull-Lopez, for his part, at first tried to deny that he'd been inside the club or had a gun. But he eventually admitted to tossing the Glock in a police statement. He maintained he'd never fired the gun, which he said he brought into the club for protection and pulled near the men's room because he was aware of a "beef" between people inside. He went on to claim Villalobos was his friend (although he could not think of his surname without police prompting), that the wounded man had run to him for help, and that his shirt had been bloodied while helping Villalobos to the ground. Brull-Lopez threw his Glock away, he said, because he'd acquired it illegally. On February 18, he was arrested a second time—after bailing out on his initial charges—on charges of shooting at, within, or into a building and using a firearm or weapon in the commitment of a felony, suggesting that the police now believe he was one of the shooters. In the end, the tragedy left nine people with bullet wounds and Villalobos, along with 33-year-old Jonathan Avila Rojas, dead. Local police were unsure how many shooters took part in the incident, but as of the following Monday, they suspected as many as three gunmen were involved. Stacked chairs and costume party detritus left at Glitz Four months later, the Orlando Police investigation into the Glitz shooting is still open. As such, cops have been reluctant to share names of victims and have declined to comment on the case beyond a few scant official statements. Some survivors are choosing to remain anonymous for reasons of privacy, others for fear of the shooters, one or two of whom may remain at large. Hopes that security or management from Glitz might be able to clear up what happened have also been dashed, as the club was shuttered immediately after the incident and management and official employees have been unreachable ever since. "If I asked you to search me, you probably wouldn't reach around my junk."—Robert C. Smith, nightclub security expert Despite those roadblocks, conversations with victims, witnesses, people who worked in the club, and security experts offer insight into how a massive shooting like this could happen at a venue with an off-duty police detail and a significant number of guards. Which in turn sheds light on the shocking commonality of mass shootings in clubs and other nightspots across America. Within minutes of the off-duty cops' call to dispatch, a number of police arrived and rapidly secured the chaotic scene. By 1:25 AM, they started releasing vehicles, and less than 20 minutes later, Universal Boulevard had been reopened to traffic. By about 2 AM, most of the wounded had either been found by first-responders or made their own way to local hospitals. A grand total of 113 police units stayed on site or in the hospital with victims, trying to figure out what had happened, on and off throughout the following day. Within 24 hours, a preliminary investigation suggested to police that the violence had been the result of a conflict between two groups of Hispanic men. By Friday, February 12, Orlando cops were saying Rojas and Villalobos had been members of the two conflicting sides, along with three of the wounded. Social media reactions on the night of the shooting and interviews with employees at businesses near the club suggest the incident may have been part of a drug-trafficking conflict between Puerto Rican gangs. Local police have not endorsed that theory, and only obliquely reference gang reactions to the attack in Brull-Lopez's arrest report. But there's a fair amount of evidence supporting the gang hypothesis. For starters, Rojas and Villalobos, as well as Brull Lopez and 21-year-old Luis Gadiel Cruz-Nazario—who was also arrested in connection with the shooting on February 18—were originally from Puerto Rico, and although they hadn't been arrested for serious crimes in the United States, Rojas and Villalobos were under investigation by Puerto Rican authorities for drug trafficking. Rojas, also known as "Pescue" and described by some in Orlando as a veritable kingpin, had also been pegged by Puerto Rican cops as a person of interest in at least four murders, and he was suspected of violating local gun laws on the island. Reports in Puerto Rican news outlets also linked him to the island's Alkaedas drug ring. For his part, Brull-Lopez doesn't appear to have clear gang affiliations, but his statement to the police on the night of the shooting does suggest he was aware of the "beef" between the alleged gang elements in the club. These details might shed a bit of light on the nature of the conflict inside Glitz that night, but they don't explain how the suspects were able to get the guns they used inside the club. According to a police spokeswoman addressing the press on the day of the shooting, the club wasn't just in an apparently safe touristy part of town—it had an "extensive security system." Indeed, people who worked in the venue say at least a dozen guards were on duty any given night, a couple of them tasked with thoroughly patting down everyone coming in for weapons. The police spokeswoman insisted at the time that "the club really did everything that it's supposed to do," and cops later stressed to local reporters that this was an isolated event. The parking lot at the Glitz Ultra Lounge Some experts I spoke to think the local police simply overstated the quality of Glitz's security protocols, either accidentally, or in an effort to assuage fears that might harm Orlando's vital tourism sector. "Small mistakes do happen," offers Robert C. Smith, an ex-cop and head of San Diego's Nightclub Security Consultants, the only American firm exclusively focused on bouncer training at the national level. Smith was chairperson of the committee that wrote California's relevant security curriculum; he also flew out to Florida in February to touch base with local clients and contacts, and boned up on the Glitz shooting before leaving town. "Allowing an underage person to slip by you happens," he tells me. "Serving someone excessively in a crowd of thousands of people every night—that happens. But allowing not [a] gun, but guns to come in, allowing people to die, that's not just a fluke of nature." When I visit in late March, it's easy to suspect Glitz was never up to snuff. A squat white building with blacked-out windows and a tattered red carpet under its black awning, perched on the edge of a strip-mall parking lot, the place doesn't exude a feeling of safety. It looks like what it is: a failed eatery—the club used to be the Volcano Bar & Grill Mexican Restaurant—sitting on a busy intersection forgotten by through traffic, reborn with an extra dose of neon. Employees in the decrepit strip mall regarded Glitz, which used to open long after they closed for the day, with mild suspicion. The receptionist at the Palm Construction School, the closest business to the club, says she assumed it was abandoned until the morning she found police cars outside. Three employees of the nearby Pirate's Dinner Adventure, along with a barber from the strip mall, cite rumors of crime and intrigue, but admit they never visited the club themselves. Rather than attracting locals, sources in the local club world say Glitz drew different crowds almost every night from all over greater Orlando—people who followed DJs or performers, rather than venue loyalists. Lawyers hired by Maria and two others wounded in the attacks—they're filing two separate civil lawsuits against the club for negligence—suspect its operators were irresponsible and failed to institute or enforce security measures proportionate to the threat level. Opened in 2013 by Setay McKnight of Apopka, a suburb north of Orlando, the club was run by DiMaggio's Ultra Lounge, an LLC registered to McKnight's home address in 2011. Whereas many of the businesses in the expensive tourist district were owned by hard-hitters in the Orlando real estate world, McKnight doesn't appear to own any other business ventures. Judging by a résumé posted to the job site Indeed, almost from the start, he left the club under the management of Arvind Kalipersaud—a 26-year-old man who'd previously worked retail and appears, according to voter registration records, to have lived at McKnight's home. There is no record of code violations or serious complaints for Glitz. But Thomas "T. C." Roberts, one of the lawyers representing Maria, says he found an eviction notice on the venue's door soon after the shooting indicating the owners owed $82,663 in unpaid rent. John Phillips, Maria's other lawyer, suspects the club was operating without insurance, citing the disappearance of McKnight and co. The attorney hopes to flush them out as the case grinds on, but based on past experience, Smith imagines some sort of settlement could be reached even if the operators don't make it to court in the flesh. In the year prior to the attack, police responded to 54 calls at Glitz for everything from fights to car burglaries. Two of these calls were for shootings in the club's parking lot: In June 2015 , a shooting injured one there; that October , another shooting injured two more. According to Smith, who cites a photo one of his trainees showed him, at least one bouncer took to wearing a Kevlar vest at the venue. And a person who worked in the club during events on and off for over two years tells me that for a while, at least, Glitz was full of people sporting gang colors and flashing signs. The club apparently cracked down on gang symbols after complaints and upped the number of security guards and cameras in the space following a large scuffle on the floor. But the event worker says many of the same gang-affiliated patrons kept coming back. Other regulars allege that, even if the venue had a large number of bouncers, their screenings were inconsistent at best. Maria says she and her fiancé were both patted down on the night of the shooting, but adds that it was a loose and quick affair that would have been easy enough to sneak something through. One witness told local reporters that while Glitz security was performing pat-downs, they never checked anyone's ankles. Another patron told Phillips that not everyone underwent even such a mediocre search. And Brull-Lopez said in his statement to police that he was able to get his handgun and spare clip into the club because he was neither patted down nor checked with a metal detector wand, as other patrons were. Brandon, who recalls that a few weeks before the shooting he saw someone packing a gun during a fight at Glitz, thinks the bouncers were allowing guns to make it in—which Smith says isn't entirely uncommon for nightlife security in general thanks to the natural temptations of bribery and friendship. Despite Brandon's suspicions, corruption isn't the only way to explain guns making it inside—the possibility of poor training and execution looms. No one involved with the club could connect me to a bouncer there, and none of the lawyers pursuing suits against Glitz are clear on whether the club employed a security firm or retained its own security in-house. But it's legal in Florida to just post on Facebook looking for people to post up at a venue door for, say, ten bucks an hour, $15 if they have a security certificate.
