And even those trained by dedicated firms might miss things from time to time. "If I asked you to search me, you probably wouldn't reach around my junk," Smith says. He's done this exercise with his students before—and managed to sneak cutlery past them because they don't want to get up in his crotch with their hands. (This is where Brull-Lopez told police he hid the extended clip later found in his pocket.) Check out our documentary about gun culture in the Sunshine State. &amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;lt;span id="selection-marker-1" class="redactor-selection-marker"&amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;gt;&amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;lt;/span&amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;amp;gt; There's no real metric for measuring violence in American nightclubs, but the Glitz attack was just one of three mass shootings at US night spots that week alone. That doesn't mean club mass shootings are about to become an everyday occurrence. Nightclub violence writ large is still relatively uncommon, and when it does occur, it's usually an overzealous bouncer roughing someone up, a shooting in the parking lot beyond security's reach, or a fight without lethal weapons. And these tragedies tend to cluster on weekends. But the fact that America can witness three such incidents within a week is alarming enough in its own right. Smith doesn't buy into the fatalistic notion that this type of violence is inevitable, though. He believes we can reverse this trend—without undertaking Sisyphean gun control efforts. Citing the example of a club in a rough part of Washington, DC, that operated for eight years with nary a stabbing or shooting inside, he claims that with the right level of professionalization, training, and oversight, security guards can keep every weapon out of a dedicated venue. He notes that three (soon-to-be-four) states now require that bouncers be licensed, some with club-context-specific training—a regimen that owners and insurers are starting to favor out of concern for the effect of violence on their bottom lines. If enough states force clubs to hire sufficiently professionalized guards—people whose jobs depend on their diligence—then venues with metal detectors and comprehensive pat-downs (the protocols Orlando Police say they would have recommended if Glitz reopened) might be able to stop everyone from random hotheads to organized gangs from wreaking havoc. Of course, such measures could incur expenses that force bars operating on narrow margins to close up shop. They might also simply serve to push violence between gangs to other venues. But given the sheer number of mass shootings American clubs suffer each year, and the role lax security plays in at least some of these attacks, a new wave of regulation is long overdue. "If you know that's your crowd, I don't give a fuck if it's Crips and Bloods, Hell's Angels, or Mongols, doesn't matter to me," Smith says. "If you handle your door, no guns are coming in." * Citing safety concerns or a desire for privacy, several individuals in this story have been granted pseudonyms. Follow Mark Hay on Twitter.

On Saturday night, Brandon Wolf found himself at the Pulse Orlando nightclub, a decision he initially hesitated about but was persuaded to make by a friend. The club, buzzing with energy on Latin Night, was filled with people dancing and socializing. As closing time approached, Wolf and a friend were in the bathroom when the first gunshots rang out – perhaps 20 in total. The sounds grew closer, forcing them to flee.

At 2:02 a.m., an off-duty Orlando police officer working security at the club heard the initial gunshots. Omar Mateen, armed with an AR-15, a handgun, and an explosive device, was outside. The officer engaged Mateen, and two more officers soon joined the confrontation, opening fire. Mateen retaliated, then retreated into the Pulse nightclub, trapping dozens inside. By 2:09 a.m., a message appeared on Pulse Orlando's Facebook page: "Everyone get out of pulse and keep running." Inside, Wolf and his friend remained hidden in the bathroom, the relentless gunfire a terrifying soundtrack. "All I heard was gunfire after gunfire," Wolf recounted. "Eventually, I thought you were supposed to run out of ammunition. But it just kept going and going." Ivory Mcneal, in the club's patio area, described the scene of panic: "People were running everywhere. Gunshots were going off. People were ducking." For the next three hours, over 100 law enforcement officers converged on the scene, assessing the unfolding tragedy while people inside made desperate 911 calls from hiding.

At 5 a.m., police initiated their entry strategy. An armored vehicle breached a wall, creating an opening. Two Distraction Devices were deployed, their loud bangs and bright lights intended to disorient. Mateen fired at the approaching officers; one bullet struck an officer's Kevlar helmet, a detail that likely saved his life. The officers returned fire, and Mateen was killed. Thirty individuals were rescued alive from the club. Tragically, 39 other people were found dead inside, with an additional two fatalities occurring outside the venue. Shortly after 5 a.m., Brandon Wolf found himself in a 7-11 parking lot, having escaped with the friend he was with in the bathroom. A third friend was seen being taken away on a gurney, injured. However, their fourth companion, Drew Leinonen, remained missing. Wolf contacted Drew's mother, Christine Leinonen, who immediately drove from Lakeland. Upon arriving at the parking lot, she and Wolf shared a tearful embrace.

By 7 a.m., at a press conference, Orlando Police Chief John Mina confirmed that at least 20 people had been killed and 42 hospitalized. Florida Department of Law Enforcement Special Agent in Charge Danny Banks characterized the shooting as an act of terrorism, suggesting potential motivation from Islamic extremism. Orlando Mayor Buddy Dyer described the event as a "horrific crime" that would leave a lasting impact on the community. By 10:30 a.m., officials confirmed the grim toll: 50 people dead and 53 injured. Mayor Dyer stated the immediate focus was on victim identification and family notification. Imam Tariq Rasheed of the Islamic Center of Orlando condemned the attack, emphasizing that no religious tradition could justify such violence. At 1:45 p.m., President Barack Obama addressed the nation, labeling the attack as an act of terror and hate, and acknowledging the particular heartbreak for the LGBTQ+ community targeted at a place of gathering. U.S. Senator Bill Nelson later reported that the Islamic State had claimed responsibility.

Throughout the day, the City of Orlando began releasing the names of the victims. By 3:39 p.m., the first four were identified: Edward Sotomayor Jr., 34; Stanley Almodovar III, 23; Luis Omar Ocasio-Capo, 20; and Juan Ramon Guerrero, 22. By 5:20 p.m., two more were identified: Eric Ivan Ortiz-Rivera, 36, and Peter O. Gonzalez-Cruz, 22. The following day, by 6:20 p.m., Luis S. Vielma, 22, was identified as the seventh victim, and vigils began across Central Florida. Kimberly Morris, 37, was identified as the eighth victim by 9:36 p.m. In the early hours of Monday morning, two more victims were identified: Eddie Jamoldroy Justice, 30, and Darryl Roman Burt II, 29. By 7:30 a.m., nine additional victims were identified: Amanda Alvear, 25; Martin Benitez Torres, 33; Luis Daniel Wilson-Leon, 37; Mercedez Marisol Flores, 26; Xavier Emmanuel Serrano Rosado, 35; Gilberto Ramon Silva Menendez, 25; Simon Adrian Carrillo Fernandez, 31; Oscar A Aracena-Montero, 26; and Enrique L. Rios, Jr., 25. During a press conference at 7:30 a.m. on Monday, officials clarified that all victims had been removed from the scene by approximately 11 p.m. the previous night, with the extensive crime scene posing challenges. They confirmed a total of 49 victims, plus the shooter, resulting in 50 fatalities. By 8 a.m., Javier Jorge-Reyes, 40, and Miguel Angel Honorato, 30, were identified as two more victims.

The Orlando Sentinel's front page for Monday's edition was carefully designed to reflect the widespread feelings shared on Sunday after the tragic shooting at the Pulse nightclub. A deep sadness was evident in conversations, recognizing Orlando's sad place among devastating mass shootings in the United States. Yet, a different message grew stronger as the day went on: a firm belief that this terrible event would not shape the community's lasting memory.

This feeling was strongly captured in the front page message: "Let our community define itself by our unequivocal response: United." This statement, along with a touching picture of two people holding each other sadly, led eight more pages of detailed reporting in the newspaper. This thorough coverage included information about the victims whose stories were known before the print deadline.

Changing the usual front page style during a tragedy is not new, and sadly, the Orlando Sentinel expects it might not be the last newspaper to do this. The goal is that this editorial choice clearly shows the deep feelings and shared spirit felt by the community and the newspaper's staff during this hard time. John Cutter, the managing editor of the Orlando Sentinel, guided this powerful presentation.

The Pulse Orlando nightclub shooting, a horrific act of domestic terrorism that unfolded on June 12, 2016, stands as the deadliest mass shooting in modern U.S. history. This tragedy struck Pulse, a vibrant sanctuary for the LGBTQ+ community, resulting in the loss of 49 innocent lives and leaving many more critically wounded. The perpetrator, Omar Mateen, a 29-year-old security guard, carried out this devastating assault.

Mateen, an American citizen residing in Fort Pierce, Florida, and employed by G4S, reportedly contacted 911 during the attack, pledging allegiance to the Islamic State. While the extremist group's radio outlet, Al-Bayan Radio, broadcasted a statement attributing the assault to "soldiers of the caliphate in America," the Islamic State did not officially claim responsibility. Experts believe this broadcast was an opportunistic statement rather than a pre-planned endorsement.

The attack commenced in the early hours of a Sunday morning, shattering an evening of dancing and revelry at Pulse, a venue celebrated for its welcoming and inclusive atmosphere. Armed with an AR-15 rifle, Mateen unleashed a torrent of gunfire. A subsequent exchange of gunfire with SWAT officers ultimately resulted in Mateen's death, bringing the total fatalities, including the attacker, to 50.

In the immediate aftermath, a somber procession began as medical personnel, including those from the Orlando Medical Examiner's Office, worked diligently to identify the victims. By late Sunday, all 49 victims had been identified. Orlando Mayor Buddy Dyer confirmed that 39 individuals died at the club, while 11 succumbed to their injuries at local hospitals. By Monday morning, families of 24 victims had been notified.

Witness accounts provide a harrowing glimpse into the terror experienced that night. Jon Alamo, a Pulse patron, described the gunman's actions as akin to a Marine firing indiscriminately, expressing his profound fear of dying. Eddie Justice, another patron, sent a series of heartbreaking text messages to his mother, Mina, including: "Mommy I love you. In club they shooting," followed by, "He's coming. I'm gonna die."

The investigation into Mateen's background uncovered several concerning details. He had been interviewed twice by the FBI in 2013 due to inflammatory comments made to co-workers, though these interviews were deemed inconclusive. In 2014, officials noted minimal contact with an American suicide bomber, which at the time did not constitute a threat. FBI agent Ronald Hopper indicated "suggestions that individual has leanings towards" radical Islamic terrorism. Trevor Velinor of the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, and Firearms confirmed that Mateen legally purchased at least two firearms in the week preceding the attack.

Mateen's father, Mir Seddique, speaking to NBC News, recalled his son becoming angry after witnessing two men kissing in Miami, suggesting this might have been a contributing factor to the assault. He expressed shock and offered apologies for the incident.

The incident prompted a strong response from President Barack Obama, who characterized the shooting as an "act of terror" and an "act of hate" targeting a place of "solidarity and empowerment." He urged Americans to reflect on the kind of country they wished to be.

In a separate, concerning development, an Indiana man was arrested in Southern California on the same Sunday, armed with assault rifles and bomb-making chemicals, reportedly en route to a West Hollywood gay pride parade. This arrest, made by the Los Angeles Police Department, highlighted broader concerns about potential threats to LGBTQ+ events.

The investigation, led by entities including the Florida Department of Law Enforcement, with agent Danny Banks involved, aimed to determine if Mateen acted alone. Orange County Sheriff Jerry Demings classified the event as a "domestic terror incident." The Department of Homeland Security also played a role in the subsequent analysis of the attack's motivations and potential wider implications.

Pulse nightclub itself posted updates on its Facebook page during the ordeal, urging patrons to evacuate and later requesting prayers and support. The club's final update on Monday morning expressed gratitude for the outpouring of thoughts and love as they navigated the tragic event. The club's owner, Barbara Poma, later established the onePulse Foundation to honor the victims and provide support to the community.

The previous deadliest mass shooting in the U.S. occurred in 2007 at Virginia Tech, where student Seung-Hui Cho killed 32 people before taking his own life. Mateen's family originated from Afghanistan, and he was born in New York before the family relocated to Florida.

Associated Press writers Eric Tucker, Terrance Harris, Mike Schneider, and Tamara Lush, along with photographer Alan Diaz, contributed to this report, providing comprehensive coverage of the event and its aftermath. The FBI's Orlando Field Office also released statements detailing their involvement in the investigation.

Orlando Police Chief John Mina revealed new details on Monday regarding the gunman's demeanor during a critical phone call with negotiators. The individual responsible for the tragic attack at Orlando's Pulse nightclub, which resulted in the deaths of fifty people, including himself, and left over fifty others wounded, reportedly remained "cool and calm" throughout the exchange. This revelation comes as authorities continue to investigate potential motives behind the devastating incident.

During an early-morning press conference, Chief Mina elaborated on the events leading up to the SWAT team's intervention. He explained that Omar Mateen, the gunman, had barricaded himself in a bathroom with hostages following an initial exchange of gunfire with responding officers. Mina described Mateen's communication with negotiators as surprisingly composed, stating, "He really wasn't asking for a whole lot. We were doing most of the asking." This account, shared by the Orlando Police Department, offers a glimpse into the psychological state of the perpetrator during the tense standoff.

Facebook's Safety Check feature was activated in the United States for the first time following the tragic mass shooting at the Pulse nightclub in Orlando, Florida. This devastating event, which President Obama characterized as an "act of terrorism," led to at least 50 fatalities.

Introduced in 2014, Facebook's Safety Check tool is designed to enable individuals in crisis zones to quickly inform their friends and family of their well-being. The system automatically identifies users located near an emergency and prompts them to confirm their safety with a single click, allowing them to broadcast their status to their social network. Facebook has since enhanced this feature for more rapid deployment during emergencies, with an automated process now enabling activation by teams across different time zones at any hour. The company is also investigating methods for users to report local crises, fostering a community-driven approach to disaster response. Following the community-initiated activation in Orlando, Facebook officially launched its own Safety Check for the event, stating, "We hope the people in the area find the tool a helpful way to let their friends and family know they are okay." This expansion reflects Facebook's growing commitment to leveraging its platform for assistance during critical incidents. The tool's scope has broadened significantly; after the Paris attacks in November of the previous year, Facebook began including terror attacks alongside natural disasters, recognizing how users relied on the platform to share information and check on loved ones. This evolution from solely natural disaster coverage to a wider range of emergencies underscores Facebook's adaptive strategy for user safety. The frequency of Safety Check activations has also increased, with 17 uses between January and May of the current year, compared to 11 over the preceding two years, encompassing events such as earthquakes in Ecuador, wildfires in Alberta, Canada, cyclones in Bangladesh, and flooding in Sri Lanka.

query: What has contributed to the shift in attitudes towards pit bulls and breed-specific legislation over the past few decades?

documents.text: KANSAS CITY, Mo. (AP) — For much of the past three decades, pit bulls have been widely regarded as America's most dangerous dog — the favorite breed of thugs, drug dealers and dog-fighting rings, with a fearsome reputation for unprovoked, sometimes deadly attacks. Hostility toward "pits" grew so intense that some cities began treating them as the canine equivalent of assault rifles and prohibited residents from owning them. But attitudes have softened considerably since then as animal activists and even television shows cast the dogs in a more positive light. The image makeover has prompted many states to pass new laws that forbid communities from banning specific breeds. And it illustrates the power and persistence of dog-advocacy groups that have worked to fend off pit bull restrictions with much the same zeal as gun-rights groups have defeated gun-control measures. "Lawmakers are realizing that targeting dogs based on their breed or what they look like is not a solution to dealing with dangerous dogs," said Lisa Peterson, a spokeswoman for the American Kennel Club. Seventeen states now have laws that prohibit communities from adopting breed-specific bans. Lawmakers in six more states are considering similar measures, and some cities are reviewing local policies that classify pit bulls as dangerous animals. Pit bull advocates hail the changes as recognition that breed-specific laws discriminate against dogs that are not inherently aggressive or dangerous unless they are made to be that way by irresponsible owners. The dogs' foes complain that their message is being drowned out by a well-funded, well-organized lobbying effort in state capitols. The debate puts millions of pit bull owners up against a relatively small number of people who have been victimized by the dogs. Ron Hicks, who sponsored a bill in the Missouri House to forbid breed-specific legislation, said he was surprised when nobody spoke against his proposal last month at a committee hearing. "I figured a few parents would be there who would bring tears to my eyes," the Republican said. "Would it have changed my opinion or what I believe in? No." A version of Hicks' legislation was endorsed by a House committee last month and needs to clear another committee before a full House vote. The state Senate is considering a comparable bill, as are lawmakers in Utah, South Dakota, Washington, Vermont and Maryland. In Kansas, the communities of Bonner Springs and Garden City repealed their pit-bull bans earlier this year. Summer Freeman did not know there was a ban when she moved to Bonner Springs last year after a divorce. She panicked when an animal-control officer discovered her pet and told her she had 15 days to get rid of the dog named Titan or move out of town. "I think of him like my son," she said. "He's my dog-son, I guess you could say. He's at my hip all the time. He's just a big baby that wouldn't hurt a fly." Freeman was forced to leave Titan at a shelter in Lawrence for nine months until she successfully fought to overturn the law in January. For dog owners and pit bull opponents alike, the battle is as deeply personal as any gun-control or religious issue. Each side accuses the other of lying, exploiting emotions and using bullying tactics. Pit bull owners insist their dogs are harmless, loving family members that shouldn't be blamed for something they didn't do. To opponents, they are a volatile breed whose genetics drive them to kill more than two dozen people in the U.S. each year, many of them young children. Popular television shows such as "Pit Boss" and "Pit Bulls and Parolees" on Animal Planet glorify the animals and minimize the tragedies that occur when pit bulls turn on humans, pit bull opponents say. "Everything is telling us these animals are safe if you raise them right," said Jeff Borchardt, an East Troy, Wis., man whose 14-month-old son was mauled to death a year ago by two pit bulls that tore the child from the arms of their owner, who was baby-sitting. "My son's dead because of a lie, because of a myth. My life will never be the same." The two dogs that killed Borchardt's son had lived with their owner since soon after they were born, were well-cared for and had no history of aggressive behavior, he said. Both had been spayed or neutered. That contradicts the contention that only mistreated, neglected or abused pit bulls attack people. Colleen Lynn, founder of DogsBite.org, pointed to a friend-of-the-court brief her organization submitted in a 2012 case in which the Maryland Court of Appeals declared pit bulls "inherently dangerous." "Appellate courts agree with us. Doctors and surgeons agree with us. That is credibility right there," Lynn said. "We also have the support of three divisions of the U.S. military, huge, massive bodies in the U.S. government." The Marines, Army and Air Force all have banned dangerous dogs — including pit bulls and rottweilers — from their bases because of the "unreasonable risk" they pose to safety, Lynn said. On the other side stand the American Bar Association and National Animal Control Association, which oppose breed-specific laws because they are discriminatory against a type of dog that isn't really a single breed. Three main breeds — Staffordshire bull terrier, American pit bull terrier and American Staffordshire terrier — along with mixes of those breeds are generally considered pit bulls. But many muscular, square-jawed, boxer-type dogs often are misidentified as pit bulls, making breed-specific bans hard to enforce. And because fatal pit bull attacks are a rarity compared with other causes of death such as auto accidents, dog advocates argue that breed-specific bans amount to legislative overkill. "All communities deserve comprehensive dog laws that demand responsible dog ownership and that hold reckless owners accountable when their poor decisions wind up getting other dogs or other people hurt," said Ledy Vankavage, a top lobbyist for the Best Friends Animal Society. Don Bauermeister, assistant city attorney for Council Bluffs, Iowa, led the effort to pass a local pit bull ban that took effect in 2005. He recalled first reading about the issue in the July 27, 1987, issue of Sports Illustrated, which carried a full-cover shot of an angry pit bull baring its teeth. Across the top, it said "BEWARE OF THIS DOG." After the Council Bluffs ban went into place, the number of pit bull attacks that resulted in hospitalization plummeted from 29 in 2004 to zero the past few years — proof, Bauermeister said, that breed-specific bans work. The opposition to pit bull bans, he added, is a sign that many American pet owners have lost touch with reality. "Fifty years ago, you could take a sick animal behind a barn and put it out of its misery," he said. "That's just the way it was done. Now they would investigate you for doing that. The emotional irrationality of Americans and their dogs has never been worse than it is today."

Breed-specific legislation (BSL), a policy targeting certain dog breeds for ownership restrictions or bans, remains a contentious issue in public safety and animal welfare. These laws, which can include outright bans, mandatory spay-neuter requirements, specialized licensing, increased insurance costs, or specific confinement and leash rules, often lead to the euthanasia of many pets.

Leading animal welfare and veterinary organizations, such as the American Veterinary Medical Association (AVMA), the ASPCA, the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), and the Humane Society of the United States, strongly oppose BSL. This position is widely supported by numerous state and local veterinary and humane groups. Scientific evidence does not substantiate the claim that specific breeds are inherently more dangerous. Extensive studies on dog bites, including a comprehensive AVMA survey, have failed to identify any breed as being disproportionately prone to biting humans. Research into fatal dog bites has consistently pointed to multiple contributing factors, none of which are exclusively breed-specific.

Moreover, BSL has not demonstrated effectiveness in reducing dog bites. Analyses indicate that even if high-risk breeds could be accurately identified, the sheer number of dogs that would need to be removed to prevent a single serious injury would be prohibitively large. Real-world data corroborates this. For instance, Denver, Colorado, has reported an increase in dog bite hospitalizations following the implementation of a breed ban. Similarly, the Netherlands repealed its breed ban after a study concluded it was ineffective, and Ontario, Canada's breed ban failed to significantly decrease dog bites. Experts emphasize that the most effective approach to reducing dog bites involves promoting responsible pet ownership and implementing breed-neutral safety measures. These include comprehensive education on dog behavior, consistent training and socialization, and strict enforcement of leash laws, as advocated by organizations like the American Kennel Club (AKC).

**Why Breed-Specific Legislation Fails to Prevent Dog Bites: A Focus on Responsible Ownership and True Risk Factors**

Breed-specific legislation (BSL), which targets dog ownership based solely on breed, is an ineffective and misguided approach to public safety, according to leading animal welfare organizations. The Humane Society of the United States (HSUS) and the American Veterinary Medical Association (AVMA) both oppose BSL, asserting that it does not enhance safety, reduce dog bite incidents, or protect communities. Instead, these laws are costly to enforce, harm families and their canine companions, and distract from more effective strategies for humane dog management. Research from organizations like the National Canine Research Council (NCRC) further supports the ineffectiveness of BSL, highlighting that no scientific data supports the claim that any particular breed is inherently more prone to biting.

The true risk factors for dog bites are complex and multifactorial, extending far beyond breed. These include hormonal influences, with unneutered dogs statistically more likely to exhibit aggressive behaviors. A dog's quality of life, including isolation or confinement, can lead to frustration or fear-based aggression, as noted by the ASPCA. Furthermore, a dog's upbringing, socialization from a young age, and consistent training are crucial. Individual personality plays a significant role, as each dog possesses unique temperaments. Crucially, human recognition of warning signs, such as subtle body language or overt growls, is vital in preventing bites. When these warnings are ignored, a dog may feel compelled to bite.

Effective dog bite prevention strategies should focus on understanding and minimizing these real risk factors, rather than on breed bans. Approximately 80 million dogs reside in American households, and proactive solutions are imperative. These strategies, supported by stakeholders and lawmakers, should include ensuring all dog owners have access to essential resources such as affordable veterinary care, spaying and neutering services, professional training, and comprehensive information on socialization and health. The AVMA emphasizes that focusing on responsible ownership and addressing individual dog behavior, rather than breed, is the most effective path to reducing bite incidents and promoting public safety.

The White House officially opposes Breed-Specific Legislation (BSL) at the federal level in the United States, recognizing it as an ineffective and resource-draining policy. This stance aligns with extensive research, including a comprehensive review by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC).

In 2000, the CDC analyzed two decades of data on dog bites and human fatalities across the U.S. Their findings indicated that:

*   Fatal attacks constitute a minuscule fraction of overall dog bite incidents.
*   Accurately calculating bite rates for specific breeds is practically impossible.
*   Individuals intent on exploiting dogs are not deterred by breed regulations; they simply shift to unregulated breeds when bans are enacted.
*   Any dog, regardless of breed, can exhibit dangerous behavior if intentionally or unintentionally trained for aggression.

Based on this evidence, the CDC officially recommends against breed-specific legislation, deeming it an "inappropriate" approach. Further details can be found in their published reports.

Instead of breed-specific policies, the CDC advocates for a community-centered strategy to prevent dog bites. This approach is considered a more effective pathway to fostering safer environments for both pets and their owners. The White House agrees that such community-based initiatives offer a more promising future for building stronger relationships between pets and the people who care for them. We encourage your continued feedback on this response and the We the People platform.

## Shifting Tides: Communities Reconsider Pit Bull Bans

For decades, many American cities have enforced breed-specific legislation (BSL), particularly targeting dogs commonly referred to as "pit bulls." However, a significant shift is underway, with an increasing number of municipalities reconsidering and overturning these long-standing bans. This trend reflects a growing debate about the effectiveness and fairness of breed-specific laws, prompting a closer examination of their impact on public safety and responsible pet ownership.

### The Momentum to Repeal and Reevaluate

The movement to repeal breed-specific legislation is gaining considerable momentum. Since June alone, at least nine communities in the Midwest have reversed their pit bull bans. Hallsville, Missouri, recently joined this growing trend after a family successfully appealed to the City Council, highlighting that their beloved family dog was a pit bull mix. Over the past two years, more than 100 municipalities nationwide have lifted restrictions on dogs within the pit bull family. This broad term encompasses breeds such as the American pit bull terrier, Staffordshire bull terrier, and various mixed breeds characterized by their distinctive head shapes and muscular builds.

This reevaluation of BSL extends to larger jurisdictions as well. The unified government of Wyandotte County and Kansas City, Kansas, is currently considering repealing a nearly 25-year-old ban as part of a broader overhaul of its animal control policies. Nearby, Roeland Park, Kansas, has also initiated a review of its ban, and Bonner Springs, Kansas, has already lifted its restrictions this year, signaling a clear shift in policy.

### Arguments Against Breed-Specific Legislation

Advocates for repealing these bans present compelling arguments that they have proven ineffective in reducing dog bites and have unfortunately led to the unnecessary euthanasia of millions of dogs. They contend that animal control officials, law enforcement, and the media often misidentify dogs as pit bulls, unfairly targeting responsible owners and their well-behaved pets. This misidentification can have devastating consequences for families and their canine companions.

Janelle Holland, a pit bull owner who was compelled to leave Roeland Park over a decade ago due to the ban, stated, "The only ones that are being affected by these bans are responsible dog owners." Her experience underscores the personal impact of BSL on individuals who are committed to responsible pet ownership.

Statewide action is also gaining traction in the fight against BSL. South Dakota and Utah have joined 17 other states in enacting laws that prevent local governments from implementing breed-specific legislation. These laws effectively prohibit cities from enacting bans that target specific breeds like pit bulls, promoting a more uniform approach to animal control across states.

### Historical Context and Persistent Opposition

The rise of breed-specific legislation in the mid-1980s was largely a response to a surge in fatal dog bites, with pit bull-type dogs disproportionately implicated in these incidents. The breed's unfortunate association with illegal dogfighting and its perceived use by individuals involved in criminal activity contributed significantly to its negative reputation and the subsequent implementation of restrictive laws.

However, some communities remain steadfast in their commitment to maintaining their bans. In Aurora, Colorado, residents voted by a substantial 2-to-1 margin to uphold their pit bull ban, demonstrating a strong public sentiment in favor of continued restrictions. Similarly, Miami-Dade County voters opted to keep their ban in place in 2012, reflecting a similar public stance. Jeff Borchardt, whose son was fatally mauled by two pit bulls, urges leaders to consider these tragic examples before repealing bans. He stated, "There's this pro-pit-bull movement that tries to paint these dogs as nanny dogs and sweet, lovely and kind. It's disgusting, it's dangerous, and it's irresponsible." His plea highlights the deeply emotional and personal nature of the debate for some.

### Expert Opinions and Data Discrepancies Cloud the Debate

Several prominent organizations, including the American Veterinary Medical Association, the Humane Society, and the American Bar Association, have suggested that focusing on individual "problem animals" rather than entire breeds would be a more effective approach to ensuring public safety. This perspective emphasizes the importance of evaluating a dog's behavior and history rather than its breed.

The Obama administration also voiced opposition to breed-specific legislation in response to public petitions, further indicating a growing consensus against BSL among national organizations. However, the debate is often clouded by conflicting data and differing interpretations of available research, making it challenging to reach a definitive conclusion.

The National Canine Research Council, which actively opposes BSL, points to a 2013 study it partially funded. Published in the *Journal of the American Veterinary Medical Association*, this study analyzed 256 dog bite-related fatalities between 2000 and 2009. It found that environmental factors, such as the absence of an able-bodied person to intervene, a history of abuse or neglect, and failure to neuter dogs, were present in over 80% of fatal incidents. This suggests that a dog's environment and upbringing play a more significant role in bite incidents than its breed.

"It's becoming more and more obvious that breed-specific legislation doesn't improve public safety," said Janis Bradley, director of communication for the NCRC. "Its purpose is to reduce injuries from dog bites, but there is no municipality or state where it's enacted where they've been able to show that it's accomplished this." Her statement directly challenges the efficacy of BSL in achieving its stated goals.

The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), which also opposes BSL, notes that fatal attacks represent a small fraction of the approximately 4.7 million dog bites Americans experience annually. Furthermore, the CDC highlights that accurately calculating bite rates for specific breeds is inherently challenging due to various factors, including breed misidentification and reporting inconsistencies.

Conversely, DogsBite.org, a group advocating for BSL, cites its own research based on news reports of dog-bite fatalities. This research indicates that pit bulls or Rottweilers were involved in 74% of incidents between 2005 and 2013. Colleen Lynn, founder of DogsBite.org, dismisses the CDC's stance, asserting that BSL is "not designed to reduce all dog bites" but rather "to reduce pit bull maulings and fatalities," as she considers pit bulls inherently aggressive. This highlights a fundamental disagreement on the scope and purpose of BSL.

### Ongoing Deliberations and Future Directions

Despite the growing number of communities repealing bans, some continue to consider their implementation. Riverside, Alabama, recently debated enacting a pit bull ban following a fatal mauling incident. While the city ultimately opted against it, Mayor Rusty Jessup expressed a preference for no pit bulls in his community, citing concerns about enforcement challenges and the difficulty of accurately identifying breeds. This sentiment reflects the ongoing public safety concerns that fuel the debate.

The evolving landscape of breed-specific legislation demonstrates a clear trend towards reconsidering outdated and potentially ineffective laws. As more communities examine the evidence and consider the impact on responsible owners, the movement to repeal BSL is likely to continue, paving the way for more nuanced and behavior-focused approaches to animal control and public safety. The conversation is far from over, and the future of BSL will likely be shaped by ongoing research, community dialogue, and a commitment to evidence-based policy.

Aurora voters have overwhelmingly chosen to uphold the city's ban on pit bulls, rejecting a ballot measure that sought to repeal this breed-specific legislation. The decisive outcome of ballot question 2D, with more than 70,000 votes cast across Adams and Arapahoe counties, signals a clear public desire to maintain existing restrictions on these breeds. This decision reinforces a policy that has been a focal point of debate for years, with a significant majority of residents indicating their preference to exclude pit bulls from the city. The vote has substantial implications for ongoing discussions about public safety and animal welfare, reflecting the community's approach to managing breeds perceived as posing a higher risk.

The vote highlighted a clear division in public opinion, with a majority in both counties supporting the continuation of the ban. In Arapahoe County, 68 percent of voters opposed lifting the restrictions, while 63 percent in Adams County expressed the same sentiment. Colleen Lynn, founder of dogsbite.org, a vocal supporter of breed bans, expressed satisfaction that the margin of victory for maintaining the ban exceeded her expectations. She suggested that while pit bull advocates are often vocal, a "silent majority" harbors reservations about sharing their communities with these dogs but prefers not to voice these concerns publicly. Lynn believes that "the majority of Americans do not want to live next door to a pit bull period. And that is usually a very silent majority." She views ballot measures like Aurora's as crucial avenues for this segment of the population to privately express their concerns, suggesting that public sentiment, even when not overtly expressed, significantly shapes policy.

Aurora's current ordinance, initially enacted in 2005, prohibited 10 breeds commonly identified as pit bulls. The city council revised this ban in 2011, narrowing its focus to three specific breeds: American pit bull terriers, American Staffordshire terriers, and Staffordshire bull terriers. The updated ban allowed existing pit bulls within city limits to remain if owners registered them and adhered to specific regulations. Owners also had the option of using DNA testing to confirm if their dogs fell under the prohibited categories. City data shows approximately 90 registered pit bulls currently reside in Aurora under these regulations, a notable decrease from the roughly 500 registered when the ban was first implemented. Tragically, since the ban's inception, the city has euthanized over 1,100 dogs of restricted breeds, a statistic frequently highlighted by animal welfare organizations like the ASPCA.

Opponents of breed-specific legislation, such as the Animal Legal Defense Fund, argue that the focus should be on the behavior of individual dog owners rather than penalizing entire breeds, emphasizing responsible ownership and effective training. Conversely, proponents, including Aurora's Animal Care Division and the National Canine Research Council, contend that pit bulls possess inherently more dangerous traits, justifying their exclusion. Concerns have also been raised that Aurora could become a "dumping ground" for pit bulls if neighboring cities maintain similar bans, a scenario discussed by the Humane Society of the United States. The continued enforcement of Aurora's ban, reaffirmed by the recent vote, underscores the city's commitment to its established public safety policies concerning these breeds and will likely influence similar discussions in other municipalities.

Breed-specific legislation (BSL) targets certain dog breeds, most commonly "pit bull types," based on the unsupported idea that these dogs are inherently more dangerous. These laws often lead to strict rules or outright bans on owning specific breeds. While enacted in many U.S. and Canadian communities to reduce dog bites, BSL is widely seen as an ineffective and unreliable way to improve public safety. The American Veterinary Medical Association (AVMA) officially opposes breed-specific legislation.

Dog bites are a significant public health issue. The CDC reports over 4.5 million people are bitten by dogs each year, with more than 800,000 needing medical attention, and about half of those incidents involving children. However, a dog's breed is just one of many factors influencing the likelihood and severity of a bite. Crucial elements include the dog's individual temperament and history, the owner's responsibility and management practices (like supervision and socialization), the specific circumstances of the encounter, and the dog's physical characteristics like size and strength. BSL offers a simplistic solution to a complex problem, diverting resources from more effective, behavior-focused strategies.

BSL is problematic for several key reasons. Firstly, accurately identifying a dog's breed, especially in mixed breeds, is extremely difficult and often relies on subjective visual assessments rather than genetic evidence. This leads to widespread misidentification, as demonstrated by studies showing even experienced professionals struggle. Consequently, many dogs are wrongly labeled, creating enforcement issues. Secondly, these laws unfairly penalize responsible owners and their well-behaved dogs, who may face euthanasia or relocation based on perceived breed, regardless of their individual behavior. This discrimination affects dogs that serve vital roles as service animals, in law enforcement, or in the military. Thirdly, BSL fails to address the root causes of aggression, such as irresponsible ownership, inadequate socialization, and lack of spaying/neutering. It can create a false sense of security, leading owners of other breeds to neglect essential care. Finally, enforcing breed bans is an inefficient use of community resources that could be better spent on proven public safety initiatives like licensing programs, leash laws, and interventions for dogs exhibiting problematic behaviors.

Quantifying breed-specific risk is also challenging due to unreliable data. Bite reports often lack accurate breed information, and less severe bites go unreported, skewing statistics. Incomplete licensing records and inconsistent tracking of repeat offenses further complicate analysis. Studies attempting to link specific breeds to bite risk have generally found weak or no connection. Instead, factors like thorough socialization, neutering, and secure containment are consistently identified as significant risk-reduction factors for preventing dog bites.

An enhanced strategy for dog bite prevention should focus on responsible pet ownership and identifying individual dogs that pose a genuine risk, rather than targeting entire breeds. The AVMA advocates for a comprehensive approach that includes enforcing general dangerous dog laws applicable to any dog exhibiting dangerous behavior, rigorously enforcing animal control ordinances like leash laws and proper containment, prohibiting and prosecuting dog fighting, promoting spaying and neutering to mitigate behavioral issues, and implementing comprehensive public education initiatives on responsible pet ownership and bite prevention.

query: How are the daily lives of South Koreans affected by the increasing tensions between the U.S. and North Korea?

documents.text: SEOUL—The possibility of a military confrontation between the U.S. and North Korea is dominating headlines across South Korea, but in the streets of Seoul, concerns about a North Korean missile or nuclear test—or even a pre-emptive U.S. military strike—are drowned out by the rhythms of daily life. “North Korea makes threats, but they will not do it. No way,” said Yu Jeom-soon, a 59-year-old shop worker in downtown Seoul, who sat on a...

PYONGYANG/SEOUL (Reuters) - North Korea displayed what appeared to be new long-range and submarine-based missiles on the 105th birth anniversary of its founding father, Kim Il Sung, on Saturday, as a nuclear-powered U.S. aircraft carrier group steamed towards the region. Missiles appeared to be the main theme of a giant military parade, with Kim’s grandson, leader Kim Jong Un, taking time to greet the commander of the Strategic Forces, the branch that oversees the missile arsenal. A U.S. Navy attack on a Syrian airfield this month with Tomahawk missiles raised questions about U.S. President Donald Trump’s plans for reclusive North Korea, which has conducted several missile and nuclear tests in defiance of U.N. sanctions, regularly threatening to destroy the United States. Kim Jong Un, looking relaxed in a dark suit and laughing with aides, oversaw the festivities on the “Day of the Sun” at Pyongyang’s main Kim Il Sung Square. Goose-stepping soldiers and marching bands filled the square, next to the Taedonggang River that flows through Pyongyang, in the hazy spring sunshine, followed by tanks, multiple-launch rocket systems and other weapons. Single-engine propeller-powered planes flew in a 105 formation overhead. Unlike at some previous parades attended by Kim, there did not appear to be a senior Chinese official in attendance. China is North Korea’s lone major ally but has spoken out against its missile and nuclear tests and has supported U.N. sanctions. China on Friday again called for talks to defuse the crisis. Weapons analysts said they believed some of the missiles on display were new types of intercontinental ballistic missiles (ICBM). The North has said it has developed and would launch a missile that can strike the mainland United States but officials and experts believe it is some time away from mastering all the necessary technology. Related Coverage A picture and its story: North Korea on parade “EARLY DAYS” North Korea showed two new kinds of ICBM enclosed in canister launchers mounted on the back of trucks, suggesting Pyongyang was working towards a “new concept” of ICBM, said Melissa Hanham, a senior research associate at the U.S.-based Middlebury Institute of International Studies at Monterey, California. “However, North Korea has a habit of showing off new concepts in parades before they ever test or launch them,” Hanham said. “It is still early days for these missile designs.” The Pukkuksong submarine-launched ballistic missiles (SLBM) were also on parade. It was the first time North Korea had shown the missiles, which have a range of more than 1,000 km (600 miles), at a military parade. Displaying more than one of the missiles indicates North Korea is progressing with its plan to base a missile on a submarine, which are hard to detect, said Joshua Pollack, editor of the Washington-based Nonproliferation Review. “It suggests a commitment to this program,” said Pollack. “Multiple SLBMs seems like a declaration of intent to advance the program.” North Korea, still technically at war with the South after their 1950-53 conflict ended in a truce but not a treaty, has on occasion conducted missile or nuclear tests to coincide with big political events and often threatens the United States, South Korea and Japan. Choe Ryong Hae, a close aide to Kim Jong Un, addressed the packed square with a characteristically bellicose warning to the United States. Military vehicles carry missiles with characters reading 'Pukkuksong' during a military parade marking the 105th birth anniversary of country's founding father, Kim Il Sung in Pyongyang, April 15, 2017. REUTERS/Damir Sagolj “If the United States wages reckless provocation against us, our revolutionary power will instantly counter with annihilating strike, and we will respond to full-out war with full-out war and to nuclear war with our style of nuclear strike warfare,” he said. PENCE TO VISIT SOUTH State news agency KCNA said the Trump administration’s “serious military hysteria” had reached a “dangerous phase which can no longer be overlooked”. The United States has warned that a policy of “strategic patience” with North Korea is over. U.S. Vice President Mike Pence travels to South Korea on Sunday on a long-planned 10-day trip to Asia. China has also stepped up economic pressure on North Korea. It banned all imports of North Korean coal on Feb. 26 under U.N. sanctions, cutting off the North’s most important export product. China’s national airline, Air China, weeks ago canceled some flights to Pyongyang due to poor demand but it has not suspended all flights there, it said on Friday, denying a report by Chinese state broadcaster CCTV that all flights run by the airline between the two cities were to be suspended. China’s Global Times newspaper, which is published by the People’s Daily, the Communist Party’s official paper, said North Korea must have felt the shockwave from the 11-ton “mother of all bombs” dropped by U.S. forces on Islamic State-linked fighters in Afghanistan on Thursday. “It would be nice if the bomb could frighten Pyongyang, but its actual impact may just be the opposite,” it said in an editorial. North Korea on Friday denounced the United States for bringing “huge nuclear strategic assets” to the region as the USS Carl Vinson strike group with a flag-ship nuclear-powered aircraft carrier steamed closer. In Dandong, China’s main border post with North Korea, hundreds of North Koreans gathered at a cultural center carrying floral displays. Slideshow (24 Images) With the men wearing pins adorned with photos of Kim Il Sung, and the women in brightly colored traditional dress, crowds lined up to bow to portraits of their state founder before touring an exhibition of photos and North Korean paintings. KCNA was gushing in its praise of Kim Il Sung, recalling the time he met former U.S. president Jimmy Carter in 1994. “Former U.S. President Jimmy Carter was so fascinated by his personality as to say that Kim Il Sung is greater than that George Washington, Thomas Jefferson and Abraham Lincoln put together, eulogizing him as the great sun god of human destiny.” it said.

The arrival of the U.S. Air Force's WC-135 Constant Phoenix, nicknamed the "nuke-sniffer," at Kadena Air Base in Okinawa on April 7, 2017, underscores heightened U.S. readiness amid escalating tensions on the Korean peninsula. This specialized aircraft is designed to detect and collect radioactive debris from nuclear detonations, making its deployment a significant indicator of U.S. monitoring capabilities.

Here's a breakdown of key details surrounding this event:

*   **Confirmation of Arrival:** Satoru Kuba, an Okinawan who tracks military aircraft at Kadena, confirmed the WC-135's presence. Photographic evidence shared by *Stars and Stripes* further validated the report.
*   **Official Verification:** While the U.S. Air Force's 18th Wing at Kadena initially offered no comment, and Japan's Ministry of Defense withheld immediate verification, a senior official within Japan's Self-Defense Forces later confirmed the aircraft's presence, as reported by Japan's *Nikkei* newspaper.

The strategic positioning of the WC-135 Constant Phoenix in the Asia-Pacific is a recurring measure. The Pentagon has historically deployed one of its two WC-135 aircraft to the region, particularly following North Korea's 2006 underground nuclear test. The aircraft also made a notable appearance in Japan in 2011 after the Fukushima Dai-ichi nuclear power plant disaster, as documented by *The Washington Post*. This modified C-135B variant is equipped with advanced external flow-through devices for collecting air samples and particulate matter. These samples are then sent to specialized labs for analysis, providing critical intelligence on potential nuclear activities.

The Constant Phoenix's arrival in Okinawa coincides with a period of significant geopolitical instability. North Korea has recently conducted two nuclear tests and approximately 30 missile tests since the previous year. Acting South Korean President Hwang Kyo-ahn has warned of potential further "provocations" by Pyongyang, particularly around key dates:

*   The 105th birthday of Kim Il Sung, North Korea's founder, on Saturday.
*   The 85th anniversary of the Korean People's Army on April 25th.
*   The upcoming presidential elections in South Korea in May.

In response to U.S. missile strikes in Syria, North Korea has issued a warning of "catastrophic consequences" if the U.S. pursues a pre-emptive strike. This volatile environment has led U.S. Pacific Command to reroute the USS Carl Vinson strike group toward the Korean peninsula, a move prioritizing regional security. The Trump administration has signaled a firm stance against Pyongyang's nuclear program and is seeking increased cooperation from China. Chinese President Xi Jinping has expressed a willingness to work with the U.S. on denuclearizing the peninsula.

The specific WC-135 Constant Phoenix that landed in Okinawa had previously been stationed at RAF Mildenhall in England in March, according to photographic evidence of its tail numbers. At that time, Air Force officials told *Stars and Stripes* that the aircraft was on a routine mission, refuting speculation about its involvement in investigating a Russian nuclear test.

Prior to its arrival in Okinawa, the Constant Phoenix experienced an unscheduled diversion. While en route to Kadena, the aircraft encountered engine trouble, forcing an emergency landing in Banda Aceh, Indonesia. A spokesperson for the Indonesian air force confirmed to *The Associated Press* that the plane requested permission to land due to a single engine failure. The aircraft was carrying 20 American military personnel who were traveling from Diego Garcia. The cause of the engine failure is under investigation, and no injuries were reported.

## Navigating the North Korean Conundrum: A Strategic Approach

The Trump administration faces a familiar challenge: how to manage North Korea's escalating provocations, including the potential for a sixth nuclear test, and how to leverage China's influence to curb Pyongyang's nuclear ambitions. In a clear signal of intent, the Pentagon deployed a strike group to the western Pacific, a move National Security Advisor HR McMaster deemed "prudent" given the proximity to North Korea. This deployment was met with a defiant response from North Korea's state media, which threatened a "pre-emptive strike" against the US.

President Trump's decision to launch an airstrike in Syria during his summit with Chinese President Xi Jinping may have been a calculated message, suggesting a willingness to act unilaterally against North Korea, mirroring his actions in Syria. A subsequent tweet from Trump underscored this stance: "if China decides to help, that would be great. If not, we will solve the problem without them!"

### The Intricacies of the North Korean Challenge

Addressing the North Korean issue is as complex as brokering an Israeli-Palestinian peace deal. It involves not only mitigating regional fears of a North Korean attack but also understanding and potentially alleviating Pyongyang's deep-seated security concerns. Current US policy reportedly emphasizes increased pressure on Beijing to rein in North Korea, alongside further sanctions.

Two crucial factors warrant consideration: the strategic acumen of North Korean leader Kim Jong-un, and the pivotal role of South Korea. While a prevalent view in the US attributes North Korea's intransigence to Kim's alleged irrationality – a sentiment echoed by figures like Senator John McCain, who referred to him as "this crazy fat kid" – a more compelling explanation lies in the strategic and economic logic underpinning Pyongyang's actions.

### Deterrence and Domestic Stability: Kim Jong-un's Motivations

Kim Jong-un's pursuit of nuclear deterrence, aimed at preventing a fate similar to that of Saddam Hussein or Muammar Gaddafi, provides a clear rationale for North Korea's weapons program. As one participant in Track II dialogues with North Korea recounted, North Koreans themselves posed the question: "Would the Americans have gone in and done what they did to Gaddafi, and to Syria, if they had what we have?"

Beyond deterrence, Kim's actions likely serve to project an image of strength domestically, crucial for maintaining his grip on North Korea's kleptocratic system. The more dangerous North Korea appears, the greater its leverage to extract concessions from countries like China and, particularly, South Korea.

### South Korea's Stakes and Past Precedents

China, fearing a North Korean collapse and the ensuing refugee crisis, has a vested interest in regional stability. South Korea, however, faces a more immediate and existential threat. For decades, Pyongyang has menaced Seoul with the promise of turning the South Korean capital into a "sea of fire," a threat made credible by the proximity of its weaponry to the border.

Kim Jong-un may anticipate further financial gains from Seoul, especially with the likelihood of a new South Korean president favoring engagement with Pyongyang. The 2015 memoir of former South Korean President Lee Myung-bak offers a glimpse into past negotiations. In 2009, during discussions about a potential summit, North Korea reportedly demanded substantial aid, including 100,000 tons of corn, 400,000 tons of rice, 300,000 tons of chemical fertilizers, and $100 million for road construction. Additionally, they sought $10 billion, ostensibly for an economic development bank, but likely intended to solidify Kim's domestic power base.

When Lee rejected these demands, Pyongyang shifted tactics. In March 2010, the sinking of the South Korean naval vessel Cheonan, which resulted in the deaths of 46 sailors, was attributed to North Korea.

South Korea also channeled funds to North Korea through the Kaesong Industrial Zone, a joint venture near the border. Following Seoul's closure of the zone in February 2016, South Korea's Unification Ministry stated that approximately 70% of intended wages and fees had been diverted to Pyongyang's weapons programs and luxury goods for Kim. Since its inception in the early 2000s, Seoul and South Korean companies reportedly invested over $820 million in Kaesong.

While the extent of Chinese aid to North Korea remains undisclosed, China is undeniably Pyongyang's most significant trading partner. Since 1995, the US provided over $1.2 billion in foreign assistance, a program largely halted by the Obama administration in 2009.

### The Financial Leverage and Diplomatic Imperative

China offers North Korea crucial market access and integration into the global financial system, while South Korea provides direct financial support. If Seoul were to again provide hundreds of millions of dollars to the North Korean elite, it would significantly undermine the impact of international sanctions.

South Korea stands to gain the most from eventual unification but also faces the greatest risk of a devastating conflict. Therefore, it is imperative that President Trump and Secretary of State Rex Tillerson maintain close communication and coordination with South Korea, recognizing that $450 million in cash can have a profound impact, particularly within the North Korean economy.

(Adds U.S. official plays down report in 11th paragraph, no comment from Pentagon) * South Korea expects to be told of any pre-emptive U.S. strike * China urges North Korea to halt nuclear programme * Tension rises as U.S. carrier group steams towards Korean peninsula * Trump strategy focusing on economic sanctions - U.S. officials By Michael Martina and Sue-Lin Wong BEIJING/PYONGYANG, April 13 (Reuters) - Military force cannot resolve tension over North Korea, China said on Thursday, while an influential Chinese newspaper urged the North to halt its nuclear programme in exchange for Chinese protection. Concerns have been growing that North Korea could soon conduct a sixth nuclear test or more missile launches in defiance of U.N. sanctions and stark warnings from the United States that a policy of patience was over. With a U.S. aircraft carrier group steaming to the area in a show of force and tensions rising, fears of a confrontation have been rising. China, North Korea’s sole major ally and neighbour, which nevertheless opposes its weapons programme, has called for talks leading to a peaceful resolution and the denuclearisation of the peninsula. “Military force cannot resolve the issue,” Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi told reporters in Beijing. “Amid tensions we will also find a kind of opportunity to return to talks.” While U.S. President Donald Trump has put North Korea on notice that he would not tolerate any provocation, U.S. officials have said his administration was focusing its strategy on tougher economic sanctions. Trump said on Thursday Pyongyang was a problem that “will be taken care of” and that he believed Chinese President Xi Jinping would “work very hard” to help resolve the challenge. Trump has also said the United States is prepared to tackle the crisis without China, if necessary. Trump diverted the USS Carl Vinson aircraft carrier group towards the Korean peninsula last weekend in a show of force to try to deter North Korea from conducting another nuclear test or launching more missiles to coincide with important events and anniversaries. But a senior Trump administration official described as “flat wrong” an NBC News report citing senior U.S. intelligence officials as saying the United States is prepared to launch a pre-emptive conventional weapons strike should officials be convinced North Korea was about to follow through with a nuclear weapons test. Another U.S. official also dismissed the report, calling it “speculative at best.” The Pentagon declined comment, saying, as a policy, it does not discuss future operations “nor publicly speculate on possible scenarios.” Scores of foreign journalists are gathered in Pyongyang for North Korea’s biggest national day, the “Day of the Sun”, marking he 105th anniversary of the birth of state founder Kim Il Sung on Saturday. They were taken to what officials billed as a “big and important event” early on Thursday which turned out to be the opening of a new street in the centre of the capital, attended by current leader Kim Jong Un. In 2012, two days before the centenary of Kim Il Sung’s death, it tried but failed to launch a long-range rocket carrying a satellite. It tested a newly developed intermediate-range missile on the anniversary last year, a launch that also failed. A Washington-based think tank that monitors North Korea, 38 North, said satellite images on Wednesday showed activity around the North’s Punggye-ri nuclear test site on the east coast that indicated it was ready for a new test. South Korean and U.S. officials and the think tank have been saying for weeks that North Korea could test a sixth bomb at any time. U.S. HOPES FOR CHINA INFLUENCE CIA director Mike Pompeo said North Korea was closer now than it had ever been to being able to threaten the United States with a nuclear-tipped intercontinental missile and increased its technical know-how with each new test. This in turn reduced U.S. options and “makes it more likely that you get a bad decision, a tough day for the leader of North Korea,” he told Washington’s Center for Strategic and International studies. Asked if there was hope that China would do more to slow or suspend its nuclear programme, he replied: “I’m counting on it.” Speculation about U.S. military action grew after the U.S. Navy fired 59 Tomahawk missiles at a Syrian airfield last week in response to a deadly gas attack. Washington said North Korea should see the strikes as a sign of U.S. resolve, but U.S. officials have played down the prospect of any military strike against North Korea, which would likely provoke massive North Korean retaliation and huge casualties in Japan and South Korea and among U.S. forces in both countries. The United States has remained technically at war with North Korea since the 1950-53 Korean conflict ended in an armistice rather than a peace treaty and the past six decades have been punctuated by periodic rises in tension and rhetoric that has always stopped short of a resumption of active hostilities. Analysts have interpreted China’s warnings as an effort to persuade North Korea to shelve plans for more tests. Wang warned that history would hold any instigator to account. “Whoever provokes the situation, whoever continues to make trouble in this place, they will have to assume historical responsibility,” Wang said. South Korean Foreign Minister Yun Byung-se told parliament in Seoul he believed Washington would consult Seoul if it was considering a pre-emptive strike. An influential state-backed Chinese newspaper, the Global Times, said North Korea’s best option was to give up its nuclear programme, and added that China would protect it if it did. “As soon as North Korea complies with China’s declared advice and suspends nuclear activities ... China will actively work to protect the security of a denuclearised North Korean nation and regime,” it said in an editorial. Japanese Prime Minister Shinzo Abe underscored fears about North Korea, saying it could have the capacity to deliver missiles equipped with sarin nerve gas. But a senior Japanese diplomat said Japan also did not see a high risk of military action. On Tuesday, North Korea warned of a nuclear attack on the United States at any sign of American aggression. The White House dismissed the threat, saying there was no evidence North Korea could carry it out. North Korea regularly threatens to destroy the United States and South Korea. U.S. officials said Trump was considering sanctions that could include an oil embargo, banning North Korea’s airline, intercepting cargo ships, and punishing Chinese banks doing business with it. Customs data in Beijing on Thursday showed China’s coal imports from North Korea had plunged 51.6 percent in the first three months in 2017 from a year earlier. China suspended permit issuance for North Korean coal imports on Feb. 18 as part of its effort to implement U.N. sanctions.

Ex-acting CIA chief: Trump is making North Korea situation worse President Donald Trump would be best served to simply ignore the provocations of North Korean dictator Kim Jong Un, a former acting director of the CIA said Friday, and is “making it worse” by replying with a show of force. Kim has moved his repressive, communist state to the top of the president’s international priority list in recent weeks, first with a missile test and now with preparations consistent with the test of a nuclear weapon. North Korea was a main point of discussion during the president’s meeting with Chinese President Xi Jinping last week, and last weekend Trump ordered the aircraft carrier USS Carl Vinson and its accompanying battle group into the waters off the Korean peninsula. Story Continued Below “We have a new president and Kim Jong Un is trying to challenge him, is trying to get him back to the negotiating table,” former CIA acting Director Mike Morell said Friday on “CBS This Morning,” praising former President Barack Obama for largely ignoring the North Korean regime’s efforts at saber rattling. “Kim Jong Un wants to get back to a situation where we give them gifts when they do something bad. And then we are also making it worse, right? With our bluster and by sending aircraft carriers in there, we’re raising the crisis.” "It's best to just ignore this guy and to deter him from ever using these weapons or selling them and to build our defenses," said Morell, who also served as deputy CIA director, continued. Morell was a vocal opponent of Trump's during the 2016 presidential election and publicly supported Hillary Clinton. Beyond deploying an aircraft carrier to the region, Trump has turned up the heat on his rhetoric toward North Korea as well. Both in his meetings with Xi and in public, the president has sought to pressure China, North Korea’s principle international benefactor, into corralling the Kim regime. If China will not, Trump has said, “we will solve the problem without them.” And on Thursday, after news broke that the U.S. had dropped one of its largest non-nuclear weapons on an Islamic State target in Afghanistan, Trump told reporters that “I don't know if this sends a message” to North Korea, adding that “it doesn't make any difference if it does or not.” “North Korea is a problem,” he continued. “The problem will be taken care of.” North Korea, for its part, has responded with its typical bellicose rhetoric, warning in a statement to CNN that “we will make the U.S. fully accountable for the catastrophic consequences that may be brought about by its high-handed and outrageous acts.” In an interview with The Associated Press, North Korea’s vice foreign minister said his nation is prepared for war with the U.S. should it come to that. "We've got a powerful nuclear deterrent already in our hands, and we certainly will not keep our arms crossed in the face of a U.S. preemptive strike," he said. "Whatever comes from the U.S., we will cope with it. We are fully prepared to handle it."

The Trump administration has adopted a decidedly more assertive stance toward North Korea, signaling a departure from previous policies. Key pronouncements from President Donald Trump and his foreign policy advisors since last November have emphasized this shift, with phrases like "The policy of strategic patience has ended" and "All options are on the table" becoming prominent. This rhetoric suggests that a preemptive strike against North Korea's nuclear and missile facilities could be a primary consideration for Washington.

These strong words were underscored by tangible actions, such as the deployment of advanced strategic bombers and an aircraft carrier fleet to the Korean Peninsula for joint military exercises. This heightened military posture has fueled growing international concern about a deteriorating security situation, with some observers believing the peninsula may be on the brink of conflict.

Against this backdrop of heightened tension, President Trump met with Chinese President Xi Jinping. Trump repeatedly voiced his dissatisfaction with China's efforts to curb North Korea's activities, stating that the U.S. would act independently if China failed to exert sufficient control. Consequently, the summit garnered significant international attention, with high expectations for an agreement on North Korea.

However, the summit concluded without a joint press conference or a joint communiqué, leaving the specifics of their discussions on North Korea largely unknown, beyond a shared acknowledgment of their opposition to nuclear weapons and support for strong sanctions.

The United States and China share a clear objective regarding North Korea's weapons of mass destruction. The U.S. will not tolerate the development of an intercontinental ballistic missile (ICBM) capable of reaching the U.S. mainland. From Washington's perspective, Pyongyang would cross a critical threshold with a successful atmospheric reentry test for such a missile, a final step in acquiring ICBM technology. In such a scenario, the Trump administration would seriously contemplate a preemptive strike.

Such a preemptive strike carries the grave risk of reigniting the Korean War. A future South Korean administration would face the difficult choice of either preventing a U.S. preemptive strike or joining the U.S. if North Korea demonstrates clear provocation. It is imperative that South Korea does not permit the U.S. to unilaterally decide to initiate hostilities on the peninsula.

President Trump's approach, characterized by its directness and urgency, pressured Xi to control North Korea through economic leverage. However, President Xi, a seasoned diplomat, maintained China's established principles and refrained from making firm commitments regarding North Korea. This impasse presents a significant challenge. Even with the deployment of U.S. strategic assets, Kim Jong-un may not alter his behavior. If North Korea successfully launches a mid-range ballistic missile, advancing its ICBM capabilities, or conducts a sixth nuclear test, President Trump will be compelled to act to maintain credibility in America's extended deterrence. South Korea must proactively articulate its position on a U.S.-led preemptive strike.

A speculative report suggesting an agreement between Trump and Xi to facilitate Kim Jong-un's exile abroad appears to stem from a misunderstanding of Kim's current confidence and China's strategic interests. For China, which is engaged in geopolitical competition with the U.S. in the western Pacific, including the South China Sea, North Korea serves as a crucial strategic buffer. This explains why China has not severed its oil supply to North Korea, despite international pressure.

A proposal gaining traction in the U.S. suggests a "cap" approach to resolve the nuclear crisis, representing a compromise between a nuclear freeze and complete, verifiable, and irreversible dismantlement. A nuclear freeze, however, carries the inherent risk of future resumption, making it an undesirable option. Conversely, complete dismantlement is currently unrealistic, given North Korea's apparent unwillingness to abandon its nuclear program. A cap on North Korea's nuclear capabilities would require a guarantee that the cap would not be lifted. However, the absence of such a guarantee means this approach shares the same inherent weakness as a freeze. Proponents of the nuclear cap plan advocate for parallel efforts to establish diplomatic ties between North Korea and the U.S., alongside peace negotiations.

The U.S. deployment of strategic assets to the peninsula could also be interpreted as an initial step toward initiating dialogue with North Korea. Fortunately, opposition to a preemptive strike exists within the U.S., with those opposed recognizing its potential to trigger war. The primary obstacle remains North Korea's insistence on direct talks with the U.S. and its apparent distrust of China.

It is crucial that South Korea does not find itself bearing the consequences of bilateral U.S.-North Korea agreements, as it did with the 1994 Geneva Agreed Framework. The priority for South Korea's next administration must be to establish a clear roadmap for inter-Korean talks, peace, and unification, and to ensure that major powers incorporate this roadmap into their North Korea policies.
