April Fools' Day is the only "fake news" day that's almost welcome. Almost. And one of the longstanding masters of the tech prank, Google, is at it again with a fresh batch of trickery. This year, the jokes are more obviously fake this time around in what appears to be an effort to avoid anyone actually thinking these products actually exist. Nevertheless, some of these products would be kind of cool, despite how wildly impractical they are. Here's the running list of what Google's "rolled out" today so far: Google Gnome Although the name rhymes with the very real Google Home, this imagined Google digital assistant is instead designed to sit outdoors and help you out with all matters related to weekend backyard lounging and the occasional bit of garden work. Delivering information bits about weather, edible plant life and a surprisingly dark array of opinions, Google Gnome is what Google Home would be like if it showed up in an episode of Black Mirror — helpful, but just a tad too ominous with its robot apocalypse leanings. Haptic Helpers The quickest way to get a tech-centric laugh nowadays is to show some idiot fumbling around while blinded by yet another new VR headset. But Google smartly goes in another direction and actually addresses an issue that has yet to be solved in virtual reality: haptics. Haptic Helpers actually brings human Google workers into your home to assist you with your VR experience by providing you with all of the other sensory input that you can't otherwise get on current VR Systems. From smell, to taste, and even touch, Haptic Helpers show us the next best solution for full VR immersion until we actually have holodeck-level sensory input. Google Wind Stepping up the April Fools' Day game to a new level, Google's team in the Netherlands came up with an imaginary system called Google Wind that links the country's 1,170 windmills together using "machine learning [that] enables all the mills to collaborate." Once those windmills are linked, they are able to literally blow clouds out of the sky to allow the country to have more sun-filled days. Somehow, as ridiculous as this one looks, it's reminiscent of the very real weather modification initiatives that have been documented in years past. Nevertheless, the special effects showing people being blown away by the power of the windmill mechanisms adds just the right amount of whimsy to let you know that Google is in fact just playing around with yet another wild "what if" idea. Google Puchi Puchi Keyboard Also getting in on the international prank fun is Google Japan, whose team came up with perhaps the most ingenious fake gadget in a keyboard made of bubble wrap. The Puchi Puchi keyboard (in Japanese, "puchi puchi" translates as bubble wrap), allows the user to simultaneously enjoy the feeling of popping those tiny plastic bubbles included in packing materials while typing out a message. Once the message has been popped out, a Puchi Puchi reader rolls over the popped material to decipher the message. Yes, this is stupid. But for some reason, I actually want this. They even added a special version of the Puchi Puchi Keyboard that releases pleasant fragrances when you pop the bubbles, including air from places like Hawaii. Someone please actually make this. Google Cloud on Mars Definitely one of the biggest pranks on the list is Google's claim that it is launching a data center on Mars in 2018. The data center, named "Ziggy Stardust," is designed as an off-world backup in case Earth experiences some sort of catastrophe. Image: google "By opening a dedicated extraterrestrial cloud region, we're bringing the power of Google’s compute, network, and storage to the rest of the solar system, unlocking a plethora of possibilities for astronomy research, exploration of Martian natural resources and interplanetary life sciences," reads the very long and detailed blog post accompanying the April Fools' gag. They even went as far as creating a data center location on Google Maps that you can explore. And while this may be a joke now, it's not unrealistic to imagine a very real Google data center on Mars in the decades ahead as companies like SpaceX work to colonize the red planet. Google Maps Ms. Pac-Man Finally, Google updated an old Google maps twist from 2015 by re-introducing its Pac-Man Google Maps game. In the current updated version on Android and iOS, you play Ms. Pac-Man using a map from anywhere in the world. Image: GOOGLE It's obvious why Google decided to rehash this one, it's incredibly addictive and deserves another look. Find your own neighborhood and try playing "just one" game and putting it down. So far, this looks like all the April Fools' Day weirdness Google has up its collective sleeve, but we’ll keep our eyes open out for any new ones and add them as we find them.

This imaginative concept, as detailed by Amazon's press release, aimed to bridge the communication gap between humans and their animal companions through voice commands.

**George Takei's April Fools' Congress Bid: A Prank That Resonated with Political Discontent**

In a move that initially ignited widespread excitement and speculation, beloved "Star Trek" actor and activist George Takei announced on Twitter his intention to run for Congress in California in 2018, directly challenging Republican incumbent Devin Nunes. The announcement, made on a Saturday morning, was accompanied by the hashtag #Takei2018 and a link to an article, leading a significant portion of his followers to believe the actor was genuinely embarking on a political career. However, this elaborate and attention-grabbing announcement was soon revealed to be an April Fools' Day jest, a fact Takei himself confirmed several hours later.

### Initial Enthusiasm for Takei's Candidacy

The news of Takei's supposed congressional bid rapidly captured national attention. Many expressed genuine enthusiasm for the prospect of the outspoken critic of President Trump entering the political arena. California environmental attorney Mike Levin was among those who voiced their hope, tweeting, "I was hoping @GeorgeTakei running against Devin Nunes wasn't an April Fools' joke. It would have been amazing! #Takei2018." Levin's sentiment was echoed by countless others who saw Takei as a potential champion for progressive causes and a much-needed voice in Washington. The actor, renowned for his sharp wit and consistent advocacy for civil rights and social justice, had clearly resonated with a segment of the electorate feeling disillusioned by the prevailing political climate.

### A Commentary on the Contemporary Political Landscape

Beyond the initial excitement, Takei's prank also served as a clever commentary on the current state of American politics. He later clarified his intentions, stating, "No, I'm not running for Congress. Happy 4/1! But Jon Ossoff is on 4/18. Help him win ... Let's flip Congress in '18." This strategic pivot highlighted Takei's genuine commitment to fostering political change, specifically by endorsing Jon Ossoff's campaign for a crucial special election in Georgia. Takei's adeptness at both entertaining his audience and subtly mobilizing them for a cause underscored his unique influence as a public figure.

### Varied Reactions to the Playful Deception

The public's reaction to the prank was predictably varied. Some expressed a sense of disappointment at being fooled, while others appreciated the humor and the underlying political message Takei intended to convey. TV producer Joe Henderson shared his experience, writing, "I went to bed realizing I'd been April fooled by George Takei. Because I really wanted him to run for congress. We live in strange times." Actress Deanna Russo humorously likened the joke to a parental deception, tweeting, "George Takei April-fooling us that he's running 4 congress is like ur parents saying ur going to DisneyWorld but then take u 2 the dentist." These diverse reactions illustrated the emotional investment many had in the idea of Takei entering politics, seeing him as a potential disruptor of the status quo.

### Devin Nunes: The Target of the Prank

Devin Nunes, the Republican congressman representing California's 22nd congressional district, has been a prominent and often controversial figure in recent political discourse. His district is situated in the heart of the San Joaquin Valley, encompassing significant portions of Tulare and Fresno counties. As the former chairman of the House Intelligence Committee, Nunes was at the epicenter of investigations into Russia's alleged involvement in the 2016 presidential election, a role that garnered him both staunch supporters and vocal critics. The congressman had recently faced significant public backlash, notably being met by nearly 300 angry protesters upon his return to California from Washington, D.C., a testament to the strong feelings his actions and political stances evoked among his constituents and the broader public.

### The Prank's Underlying Message and Impact

George Takei's April Fools' prank, while ultimately a lighthearted jest, tapped into a palpable desire for political change and a yearning for figures who could effectively challenge the established order. His closing remark, "We're living in a world where every piece of news about the White House could have qualified as an April Fool's headline in years past," resonated deeply with many, capturing the often surreal and unbelievable nature of current events. The incident served as a potent reminder of the significant power of celebrity endorsements and the substantial impact public figures can wield on political discourse, even through playful deception. It highlighted how a well-timed joke could amplify a message and underscore a collective sentiment of political fatigue and a desire for new leadership.

Sinbad celebrated April Fools' Day by perpetuating one of the internet's greatest myths: The existence of his genie film Shazaam. On Saturday. College Humor shared "lost footage" of the comedian starring in the (fake) film on Saturday. Related Watch 'Pokemon Go' Creator Explain Origins of the Game Thanks to years of crowd-sourcing street data via its first game, Niantic's John Hanke was ready when the Pokemon Company came calling Based on countless testimonies found on internet message boards, a genie film titled Shazaam that starred Sinbad has been falsely implanted into the memories of thousands of Nineties kids. This alternate universe theory, or the Mandela Effect as the phenomena is called, is similar to the Berenstein Bears/Berenstain Bears debate that similarly divided the internet; the common theory is that people are confusing the nonexistent Shazaam with the real film Kazaam, starring Shaquille O'Neal as a genie. Sinbad himself has long denied that he was ever cast as a genie – "Have you noticed no one my age has seen this so called Sinbad Genie movie, only you people who were kids in the 90's. The young mind," he tweeted in September – but that didn't stop the comedian from reviving the Shazaam myth for April Fools' Day. "It was my first time directing and the studio wasn’t happy with the project," Sinbad said in a statement after College Humor unearthed an alleged Shazaam VHS from a shuttered Blockbuster. "It was pulled from theatrical release. I would prefer if we all just pretended this never happened." College Humor said in a statement, "Like most Americans, we had weird memories of this movie existing, but just thought we were confused. Then, one of our producers found this VHS in a box behind a shut-down Blockbuster. It’s quite damaged, and most of it has been recorded over, but what’s there is classic cinema and movie magic. We're excited to share it with audiences at large."

query: What are the main events taking place in various towns along the path of totality for the upcoming total solar eclipse?

documents.text: Next Monday, sky watchers from the beaches of Oregon through the midsection of America and down the South Carolina coastline, will be trying to catch a glimpse of the first total solar eclipse across the U.S. in 99 years. But in the so-called path of totality—a 70-or-so-mile-wide band in which parts of the country will be in complete daytime darkness for two-plus minutes—bartenders, winemakers, and distillers are hoping for another cosmic call: “Another round, please!” Here’s how five distillers, winemakers, and brewers are prepping for when the sun suddenly goes dark. MADRAS, OREGON—Totality Strikes: 10:19 a.m. PDT When it comes to claims to fame, tiny Madras, Oregon (pop: roughly 6,700), usually boasts about being America’s carrot seed capital. But on eclipse day, Madras becomes a prime viewing spot in the state where the eclipse first strikes U.S. soil. More than 100,000 people are expected to cram into the central Oregon high-desert town for the event, attracted by wide-open vistas, predictably clear summer skies, and 2 minutes, 2 seconds of total darkness, one of the longest spans in the Northwest. Rick Molitor has something else waiting for them: American Strong’er, a 97-proof whiskey he has distilled specially for eclipse day. Two years ago, Molitor, along with four drinking buddies, opened New Basin and came out with American Strong Light, a whiskey named after Jedediah Strong Smith, an early Western settler. For the Strong’er version, he tweaked the final product, upping the proof from 80 to 97. Why 97? It’s a nod to Highway 97, which bisects Madras and will lead many an eclipse watcher into town for the moon show—and ideally to his distillery. In the days leading up to the eclipse Madras will stage Solarfest, a county fair-like celebration featuring 20 bands, balloon rides, and NASA scientists holding court for the thousands of campers slated to hit town. Molitor is also hosting a VIP breakfast that day for about 50 guests. They’ll first get to see his 300-gallon still, and then sit back and view the eclipse without having to compete with the throngs for a spot. He’s also thinking of using the three hours it takes for the moon to pass by the sun to keep his still running. “If we do a small batch and get it just dialed,” he says, “I’ll be able to claim that it was made during the eclipse.” CASPER, WYOMING—Totality Strikes: 11:42 a.m. MDT Amber Pollock says her hometown of Casper, Wyoming, has long been viewed as “a beer-drinking town.” Come eclipse day, she’s seeking to change that perception—if not forever then at least for the time it takes for midday darkness to come and go. She’s hosting an all-day watch party at her family-owned Backwards Distilling Company, and during two-hour blocks, Pollock will serve cocktails named after the evolving sky. For instance, starting at 9 a.m., there’s the Eclipse Chaser—cold-brewed coffee and fresh-squeezed orange juice, with muddled blueberries, tiki bitters, and an optional shot of Backwards’ own Sword Swallower Rum. Then, around 11 a.m., shortly after the moon first starts shading the sun, Pollock will offer up the First Contact: house-made pineapple lemonade, house-made grenadine, and 307 Eclipse Edition Vodka, a special version of the spirit that helped launch her family’s distillery two-and-a-half years ago. Named after Wyoming’s sole area code, the 307 Vodka was developed by Pollock’s younger brother, Chad. “There were a lot of places serving Jack & Cokes [in Casper], but not many where you were going to find fresh squeezed juice,” says Pollock, a former elementary school teacher who launched Backwards with her brother and parents in 2014. “We had to open a facility to showcase our products in a way we wanted them to be showcased.” Backwards, Casper’s only spirits distillery, has a tasting area that seats 52. Pollock hopes to double that number on eclipse day, drawing from the 35,000 visitors the city is expecting. (Total eclipse time in Casper: 2 minute, 26 seconds.) Besides offering unobstructed views from the distillery’s site, located on an elevated space away from downtown, Pollock has booked Inda Eaton, a Long Island-based singer-songwriter who grew up in Casper, to perform. Eaton goes on once the sun returns, and once Pollock’s last eclipse-day concoction—the Corona, a mix of milk can cinnamon moonshine, pineapple juice, lemon juice, vanilla, and bitters—is making the rounds. MAKANDA, ILLINOIS—Totality Strikes: 1:20 p.m. CDT Get The Beast In Your Inbox! Daily Digest Start and finish your day with the top stories from The Daily Beast. Cheat Sheet A speedy, smart summary of all the news you need to know (and nothing you don't). By clicking “Subscribe,” you agree to have read the Terms of Use and Privacy Policy Subscribe Thank You! You are now subscribed to the Daily Digest and Cheat Sheet. We will not share your email with anyone for any reason. Back in 2014, Jim Ewers, who owns and operates Blue Sky Vineyard in Makanda, got a call from a stargazing junkie in Germany. The caller wanted to know if she could book a room at Ewers’ winery—for August 2017. Before that reservation request, Ewers had no clue about the coming eclipse, and that his vineyard was prime real estate for eclipse chasers. “I basically added 50 percent to the price I was charging at the time,” recalls Ewers, whose winery includes a few guest rooms. “She never took it, which worked out well because I’ve since gotten more [for the room].” If the moon and sun line up according to star experts’ predictions, then Makanda is considered Ground Zero; total darkness in the southern Illinois town (pop: 544) is slated to last 2 minutes, 40.2 seconds, the longest duration of darkness in the country. Ewers plans to take full advantage of the designation. He’s expecting 3,000 to 5,000 people to make use of his 600-acre vineyard in the Shawnee Hills region of southern Illinois. (Ewers is charging $100 a night for RV campers.) He also bottled 100 cases of Solar Eclipse Wine, a specially made dry and, befitting the day, dark red wine he hopes to sell out by the time the crowds leave. The wine is a blend of two grapes grown in Ewers’ vineyard: Champbourcin, a French-American hybrid that Ewers says is low in tannin and fairly fruity, and Norton, a dark grape. “You can crush it and you get a high percentage of skin to juice,” Ewers explains. “You can extract more color out of the skin so you get a nice rich color out of it.” Along with pushing the wine, Ewers has used the final days before the eclipse to map out where the rush of RVs will park, where to put a group of stargazing enthusiasts from Spain so they have a bit of seclusion and where to mount a specially commissioned piece of abstract artwork— aptly titled “He Flew Too Close to the Sun”—near the winery. The pace has left Ewers to admit, “I can’t wait until the 22nd. I want it all behind me.” Perhaps, but he also knows what lies ahead. Come April 8, 2024, when the next total solar eclipse is slated for the U.S., his vineyard once again will be in the path of totality. HOPKINSVILLE, KENTUCKY—Totality Strikes: 1:24 p.m. CDT Peg Hays has been aware of the approaching eclipse for 10 years, even before she and her husband, AJ Jones, bought land in Hopkinsville and decided to open the Casey Jones Distillery on it. She’s watched dozens of clips of past eclipses on YouTube. She jokingly calls herself the “Empress of the Eclipse.” So, when darkness strikes on Aug. 21, she’ll be ready. Totality, she says, is “at 1:24 pm Central Daylight Time, for 2:40 seconds. At 1:20 I’m going to go to the bathroom; I have that on my calendar already. Then I’m going to get outside and be with the crowd and watch it, let everything else go to hell, and watch this eclipse.” A couple of weeks before the big day, Hays isn’t sure if the crowd camping out on her property will be “500 or 5,000.” Doesn’t matter. She’s hosting a three-day party featuring blue-grass bands, food trucks, and a barber giving E-clips. And, no doubt, she’ll be pouring her distillery’s Total Eclipse Moonshine, made from yellow dent corn that’s locally grown. “We are Kentucky proud,” she boasts, then jokes: “You can have a total or partial eclipse right now by opening up a bottle.” The trademarked 100-proof spirit comes from a still that’s a duplicate of the last one built by Casey Jones, a Prohibition-era still maker and AJ’s grandfather. “This region [western Kentucky] was known during Prohibition as a great place to get great moonshine product,” says Hays, who began distilling on her property with AJ two years ago. Hays has already sold more than 5,000 bottles of the special moonshine. During eclipse weekend, she expects to sell a few more. That’s because Hopkinsville, with 32,000 residents, is gearing up for 200,000 sightseers, scientists, and, well, party-chasers to come for eclipse weekend. Hopkinsville even rebranded itself “Eclipseville” thanks to researchers determining it’s where the Point of Greatest Eclipse will occur—or “where the width of the moon’s shadow is widest,” according to Solar.com. (Darkness in Hopkinsville will be just .1 second shorter than in Makanda.) But come totality time don’t expect Hays to be behind the counter. “In fact,” she says, “when we hand out parking passes, we’re going to tell people that at 1 p.m. we’re locking up the distillery and letting everyone experience the eclipse, including our staff.” She pauses, then, with a laugh, says, “Last call.” COLUMBIA, SOUTH CAROLINA—Totality Strikes: 2:41 p.m. EDT Speaking of last call… Two years ago, when Bryan O’Neal heard that his home state, South Carolina, would be the end of the line for Eclipse 2017, it got him thinking. And then, when he heard about the estimated number of out-of-state visitors the eclipse would draw to the state capital, Columbia, located 50 miles south of his brewery, it really got him thinking. He told himself, “If there’s going to be a million people, we’re going to brew a beer for this. This is a once-in-a hundred-year opportunity.” O’Neal, who built swimming pools for 22 years before opening Benford Brewing on his cattle farm in Lancaster four years ago, quickly got to work. Wanting an “extremely dark” IPA for the day, he tweaked one of his recipes by using midnight wheat. “So it has a hint of porter at the beginning,” says O’Neal, “and then the hops explode with Mosaic, El Dorado, and Citra.” He called it Carolina Blackout and had a specially designed label prepared for it, using a variation of the state symbol—a Palmetto tree with a crescent moon—by switching in an eclipse moon. He included the date, Aug. 21, 2017. And he trademarked Carolina Blackout. By starting early, he says, “we would beat all the other brewers to the punch and own the eclipse. And this is what pretty much happened.” Fortunately for O’Neal, the path of totality in South Carolina includes Columbia, Clemson, and Charleston, locations of many of his retailers. “It’s going to be like St. Patrick’s Day,” says O’Neal. “In the week of the eclipse they are going to want [Carolina Blackout] like they want green beer.” On eclipse day, O’Neal will head to Columbia to watch the moon at work from a friend’s rooftop terrace, have a Blackout (or two), and revel in a once-in-a-lifetime sight. “South Carolina is home, and I am proud of my state,” says O’Neal. “The fact that we are getting this attention nationally is a good opportunity for the brewery and the state. It’s going to be a pretty special day.”

## Weiser, Idaho: Navigating the Unprecedented Influx for the Great American Eclipse

Weiser, Idaho, a community typically home to just 5,507 residents, is preparing for an extraordinary surge in population, with projections estimating up to 70,000 visitors for the upcoming total solar eclipse. This rare astronomical spectacle, the first coast-to-coast total solar eclipse in the U.S. since 1918, presents significant logistical and infrastructural challenges for this rural Idaho town. The Idaho Department of Commerce-Tourism Development highlights the immense rarity of such an event, noting that another eclipse won't be visible in Idaho for 152 years, and actively encourages travelers to "make plans to be part of this amazing experience."

### Economic Opportunity and Underlying Concerns

For many in Weiser, the eclipse represents a substantial economic opportunity. Patrick Nauman, chairman of Eclipse Fest 2017 and co-owner of Weiser Classic Candy, optimistically described the event as "Mother Nature’s apology for the hard winter" and "a big economic shot in the arm." However, this optimism is tempered by anxieties, particularly from Mayor Diana Thomas. She acknowledges residents' concerns, especially after a harsh winter that brought record cold and snowfall, leading to the collapse of Weiser's only grocery store roof and damage to the local bowling alley. The impending eclipse, therefore, casts a shadow of uncertainty over this rural Idaho community, raising questions about its capacity to handle such an unprecedented surge in population.

### Travel Advisories and Safety Precautions

The Idaho Transportation Department has issued stark warnings to the anticipated hundreds of thousands of visitors flocking to the state. To ensure a smoother experience and mitigate potential issues, travelers are strongly advised by the department to carry paper maps, bring ample water, ensure vehicles are fully fueled, and exercise extreme caution to prevent wildfires due to dry conditions.

### Local Preparations and Resource Management

Preparations are actively underway within Weiser to manage the influx. Approximately 70 portable toilets have been procured to supplement existing facilities. Concerns were palpable at Weiser's final pre-eclipse city council meeting, where Pastor Kevin Bettinger offered an invocation for protection and guidance. Police Lieutenant Troy Krahn detailed security measures, including borrowing two officers from Emmett to bolster the 12-person department, with extended shifts planned. The school district is organizing shuttle buses from designated parking areas to Memorial Park, and fire trucks will be strategically positioned to navigate potential traffic gridlock.

### Traffic Congestion: The Primary Challenge

Traffic management has been identified as the primary challenge. Weiser's remote location, accessible by only three two-lane highways, means over half of the expected eclipse viewers are anticipated to arrive on the day of the event. Nauman estimated driving times from Boise could stretch from 90 minutes to five to eight hours, a figure Mayor Thomas later revised upwards to 10 to 10.5 hours, underscoring the potential for significant delays.

### Accommodation Solutions and Student Involvement

Accommodation presents another significant hurdle. Camping on the grounds of Weiser High School or in private backyards, for a fee, will be the most viable option for many visitors. Weiser High has already rented out over 170 of its 300 available campsites and 200 parking spots. Student groups are actively participating by operating food and information booths, and selling essential items, contributing to the town's preparedness efforts. Principal Dave Davies has voiced concerns about the feasibility of 60,000 people arriving simultaneously, expressing sadness at the prospect of visitors missing the eclipse due to traffic congestion on U.S. Route 95.

### Aviation Demand and Limited Lodging

The town's airport is also experiencing unprecedented demand. Airport manager Jim Metzger has had to turn away numerous pilots, capping aircraft at 118, with many expected to camp near their planes. Weiser's three motels, offering a combined total of 40 to 50 rooms, have been fully booked for at least a year, highlighting the extreme scarcity of traditional lodging.

### Scientific Observation and Departure Concerns

Weiser has also become a focal point for scientific observation. Astronomer Matt Penn, affiliated with the National Solar Observatory, has chosen Weiser as his base for the CitizenCATE experiment, which involves telescopes at 68 sites across the path of totality. Despite his advance booking efforts five months prior, Penn, like many others, will be staying with a local superintendent. Following the eclipse, Penn anticipates joining the exodus of approximately 70,000 astronomy enthusiasts departing Weiser via the same limited two-lane highways. This mass departure is Mayor Thomas's most pressing concern: "How," she asks, "do we get people out of here when it’s over?" The town is working with state agencies, including the Idaho Office of Emergency Management, to develop strategies for managing the egress.

Prepare for a unique celestial event as Bonnie Tyler, the iconic voice behind "Total Eclipse of the Heart," headlines Royal Caribbean's Total Eclipse Cruise. This special voyage offers guests a prime spot to witness the upcoming solar eclipse from within the path of totality. Tyler's performance is timed to coincide with the moon's passage across the sun, promising an unforgettable experience.

Royal Caribbean International President and CEO Michael Bayley called Tyler a "natural choice" for this momentous occasion, noting her perfect alignment with the event. Her 1983 power ballad, a global hit and karaoke staple, will be performed during a significant astronomical event for the first time. "It’s going to be so exciting," Tyler told TIME, emphasizing the rarity of such an occurrence. She will be a featured guest on the Oasis of the Seas for part of the week-long cruise, which departs from Orlando, Florida, on August 20th, heading to the Caribbean. The pop-rock band DNCE, fronted by Joe Jonas, will also perform. Tyler, whose career spans decades with extensive global tours, expressed confidence about the voyage, telling TIME, "I have a ship of my own. I go with the flow, darling. I’m not worried about things like that." To ensure a smooth performance, Tyler and DNCE will rehearse the day before, and Tyler is diligently preparing vocally. She humorously cautioned, "So if anybody hears strange noises coming from my cabin while aboard ship, they’ll know what it is." Tyler also noted the eclipse's duration of "2 minutes and 40 seconds," contrasting it with her hit song, which "had to be chopped about, because it was so long."

The creation of "Total Eclipse of the Heart" highlights Tyler's persistence. After her 1978 U.S. success with "It’s a Heartache," Tyler sought to collaborate with Jim Steinman, the songwriter behind Meat Loaf's hits. Despite initial doubts from an A&R representative, Tyler's determination led to a meeting with Steinman, who completed the song in three weeks and entrusted it to her. Tyler was deeply moved upon hearing it, stating, "It made me cry, because I loved it so much." The original recording was a massive commercial success, selling six million copies and becoming one of the decade's best-selling records. Although it received Grammy nominations in 1984, awards went to Michael Jackson and Irene Cara. Nevertheless, "Total Eclipse of the Heart" remains a beloved karaoke favorite and a radio staple, frequently featured on talent shows like *X Factor* and *Britain's Got Talent*. Tyler has noticed a surge in the song's popularity around eclipse events, with Spotify reporting a 75 percent increase in streams after the March total solar eclipse. Anticipation for the upcoming August event has already boosted YouTube views of the music video, filmed at the Holloway Sanatorium near London under challenging conditions where "it was snowing on the ground, and I had to run barefoot," to over 300 million. While the Total Eclipse Cruise offers a unique viewing experience, Tyler will eventually depart to continue her touring schedule, including her most recent album, *Rocks and Honey*.

**Oregon Man's 1962 Retinal Burn Serves as a Stark Warning for the Upcoming Solar Eclipse**

As a significant solar eclipse approaches, Lou Tomososki, an Oregon man, is sharing his deeply personal and painful experience from 1962. At just 19 years old, Tomososki and a friend made a critical mistake: they looked directly at a partial solar eclipse without any protective eyewear. This decision, born from a misguided notion that prolonged staring would lessen the sun's intensity, led to permanent vision damage for both of them.

The incident unfolded on a bright afternoon near Marshall High School. Tomososki remembers the sun being positioned high in the sky. He impulsively turned to his friend, Roger, and suggested they gaze at the sun. "I said to Roger, ‘If you stare at it long enough the brightness goes away,’” Tomososki recalled. This moment of youthful curiosity quickly transformed into a lifelong regret.

By the time evening arrived, both Tomososki and his friend began noticing serious vision problems. The damage, unfortunately, proved to be irreversible. "It doesn’t get any worse and it doesn’t get any better," Tomososki explained, describing the ongoing visual impairment. Now both in their 70s, they continue to live with the lasting effects. Tomososki vividly illustrates his condition: "You know how the news people blur a license plate out. That’s what I have on the right eye, about the size of a pea, I can’t see around that."

A later medical evaluation confirmed that Tomososki had sustained a retinal burn during the 1962 eclipse. Dr. Brandon Lujan from the Casey Eye Institute reinforces the inherent danger, stressing that looking at the sun without proper protection at any point during an eclipse can cause harm. "I think anytime is too much," Lujan told KGW-TV. "Anytime looking can do damage."

While Tomososki understands the excitement building for the upcoming August 21st eclipse, he plans to experience it with a different approach. He intends to be outside and observe the remarkable phenomenon of the sky darkening. However, he will not be looking directly at the sun. His story serves as a powerful reminder of the absolute necessity of protecting one's vision during such extraordinary celestial events.

For safe eclipse viewing, remember these crucial points:

*   **Never look directly at the sun during an eclipse without certified eye protection.** This is the most important rule.
*   **Even very brief glances can cause permanent retinal damage.** The sun's rays are incredibly powerful.
*   **Standard sunglasses are absolutely NOT sufficient for viewing solar eclipses.** They do not offer adequate protection.
*   **Always use only ISO-certified solar eclipse glasses or viewers.** Ensure they meet the safety standards.

Solar eclipses have captivated and terrified humanity for millennia, driving both scientific progress and ancient superstitions. The journey from ancient fears to precise predictions has been a long one, marked by significant milestones and the contributions of numerous scholars and organizations.

Here's a look at the evolution of eclipse understanding:

*   **Ancient Records and Fears:**
    *   The oldest intact record of a solar eclipse comes from a Babylonian clay tablet, dating to March 5, 1223 BCE, which chillingly noted, "The sun went down, its gatekeeper was Mars."
    *   On October 22, 2134 BCE, a total solar eclipse darkened the skies over China. Emperor Chung K’ang’s records state, "the Sun and Moon did not meet harmoniously." His court astronomers, Hsi and Ho, were beheaded for failing to predict this event.
    *   Ancient civilizations attributed eclipses to supernatural forces. Chinese astronomers feared a celestial dragon was devouring the sun, while Babylonians believed the moon god was besieged by seven demons.
    *   The Babylonian tablet from Ugarit, detailing the March 5, 1223 BCE eclipse, also included the observation, "Two livers were examined: danger," signaling impending misfortune for the king.

*   **Early Predictions and Cycles:**
    *   Classical scholar John Steele of Brown University noted that the earliest Babylonian records of lunar eclipse predictions date to 745 BCE.
    *   By 600 BCE, lunar eclipse prediction had become routine, with astronomers sending warning letters to Assyrian and Babylonian rulers, as Steele confirmed.
    *   A survey of 61 solar eclipse predictions from 357 BCE to 6 CE showed remarkable accuracy in predicting the time of an eclipse within an eight-hour window, though only 28 were visible from Babylon.
    *   The key to this success was the meticulous recording of past eclipses, leading to the formalization of the **Saros cycle** (18 years, 11 days, 8 hours), which enabled the repetition of eclipses.

*   **Medieval and Renaissance Advancements:**
    *   By the Middle Ages, astronomers compiled "ephemerides" (tables of celestial movements) based on the work of Greek astronomer Ptolemy (around 150 CE).
    *   However, a subtle factor—the gradual lengthening of Earth's day by about 40 seconds per century due to lunar gravitational pull—began to undermine these predictions. The precise Earth-moon distance wasn't accurately measured until Apollo astronauts placed mirror reflectors on the lunar surface.

*   **The Dawn of Modern Prediction:**
    *   Isaac Newton's 1687 theory of gravity revolutionized understanding.
    *   Edmond Halley used Newton's principles to calculate eclipses, publishing the first public "broadsheet" illustrating an eclipse's path across England in 1715. While off by about 20 miles, historian Alice Waters of the University of Massachusetts, Lowell, notes Halley's work alleviated public and royal fears.

*   **Testing Einstein and the Digital Age:**
    *   In 1919, English astronomer Arthur Eddington tested Albert Einstein's theory of gravity during a solar eclipse on Príncipe, measuring the bending of starlight near the sun. Eddington's observations confirmed Einstein's predictions, catapulting him to fame.
    *   Fred Espenak, former NASA astronomer and manager of NASA's eclipse website, explained to BuzzFeed News, "It has been centuries of gradual improvement that just piled up over time." He added, "The floodgates really opened when digital computers came online" in the 1970s, significantly streamlining complex calculations.

*   **Ongoing Challenges and Future Outlook:**
    *   Espenak cautions that long-term predictions remain challenging due to the moon's gradual recession from Earth and its complex orbit influenced by Earth, the sun, and Jupiter. "The moon is devilishly difficult to predict," he stated.
    *   Climate change, with its effects on sea levels and tidal patterns, could also influence future lunar eclipse predictions, though Espenak concluded, "But we are certainly a lot better at predicting than they were all those centuries ago."

For more on eclipses, explore BuzzFeed's eclipse stories. Dan Vergano, a science reporter for BuzzFeed News, contributed to this report.

So we asked eclipse fanatics to tell us what makes them so damn special. As you might've heard, the total solar eclipse that will occur on Aug. 21 is kind of a big deal. For the first time in 99 years, the moon's shadow will make its way across the entire United States from the west coast to the east. This means that for the first time in a few generations, anyone in the contiguous 48 states will be within a day's drive of the path of totality, when the moon will completely block the sun and day will briefly turn to night. Experiencing an eclipse from within the path of totality is considered so singular and marvelous that amateur eclipse chasers and professional astronomers alike started booking travel, scheduling transportation, and reserving lodging years in advance. BuzzFeed News talked to eclipse chasers and challenged them to describe as best they could the life-changing magic of witnessing a total solar eclipse. Here's what they shared. “I feel as if I am in the presence of a deity, and I understand that in this vast universe, I am nothing.” "Experiencing a total solar eclipse is the closest most of us will ever get to space travel. During totality, the earth's blue sky draws back like a receding curtain, revealing what's above our heads but unseen at any other time: the inner reaches of our solar system. You see a most un-sunlike sun — a frilly, shimmering halo — flanked by planets and bright stars. Advertisement It's as if you are standing on some distant world, looking back at creation. I should note that in my normal life I am a staid, logical, scientifically minded person. A total solar eclipse taps a whole other part of my being. I feel as if I am in the presence of a deity, and I understand that in this vast universe, I am nothing. It's a terrifying and humbling realization, and yet —paradoxically — it brings me great peace." —David Baron author of American Eclipse: A Nation's Epic Race to Catch the Shadow of the Moon and Win the Glory of the World Boulder, Colorado “The sky is a lovely shade of twilight blue, and a handful of bright stars and planets will shine forth, and all around the horizon you’ll see pastel sunset colors …” "Inside the path of totality, you will see the most spectacularly beautiful sight you have ever seen in nature: the everyday sun totally blocked by the moon, revealing the sun’s outer atmosphere, or corona. The silhouette of the moon appears blacker than any black you’ve ever seen. The solar corona is a diaphanous glow all around the sun, pearly white, with loops and streamers extending several solar diameters in various directions, tracing the sun’s otherwise invisible magnetic field. At the very beginning/end of totality, you’ll see brilliant red arcs of the sun’s chromosphere, or lower atmosphere, just beyond the moon’s advancing/retreating limb, or edge, and you may see some prominences — jets, arcs, or loops of red chromospheric gas — jutting off the edge of the sun. The sky is a lovely shade of twilight blue, and a handful of bright stars and planets will shine forth, and all around the horizon you’ll see pastel sunset colors coming from beyond the moon’s shadow in all directions, where the sun is still shining. You’ll feel the temperature drop noticeably, and you may feel changes in the wind — both its speed and its direction. All of this lasts just a couple minutes, though it feels as if it’s over within seconds." —Rick Fienberg press officer, American Astronomical Society Washington, DC Advertisement “The only thing I can compare that to is if you’re snorkeling ... things underwater look all different colors than they do up on land.” “When you experience a total eclipse, it kind of looks like the end of the world. It really does. If it’s a clear day, the sun is high in the sky. It’s supposed to be there — there’s not supposed to be a black hole in the sky right where the sun should be. And that black hole has pink flames and silver streamers around it. Then it gets dark — it’s doesn’t get totally dark — it’s more like a deep dusk. And because the light that’s provided is coming from the sun’s silvery corona, what you see all has a different color than normal. Advertisement The only thing I can compare that to is if you’re snorkeling ... things underwater look all different colors than they do up on land. But you know you’re not snorkeling — you know you’re not under the water. And so it’s the only time in my life where all the colors that I’m used to living with every day start to go weird. Your brain goes, Whoa, what’s going on? I’m not on a drug. I’m not in a movie. Yet everything is changing. And that happens just before the total eclipse, maybe a couple minutes before. And whenever that happens, the hair on the back of my neck starts to stand up, because it’s just so weird ... and I’ve now chased about 10 or 12 of them since 1970.” —Doug Duncan astronomer, Department of Astrophysical & Planetary Sciences of the University of Colorado director of the Fiske Planetarium Boulder, Colorado "It became eerily dark on the hills and over the bay, where intermittent camera flashes signaled the presence of unseen boats." “I went to southern England for the total solar eclipse in 1999. But of course, England isn't known for clear skies, and my choice to watch from there came with some degree of risk. When the day came, I gathered with a small group of locals on a hill overlooking the ocean, and we stood under a clouded sky to wait. As the eclipse progressed, once in a while, the crescent sun peeked through a break in the clouds, and we watched the strange narrowing shape with our eclipse glasses. By the time totality arrived, the cloud cover was complete, but the occasion was deeply surreal and affecting nonetheless. It became eerily dark on the hills and over the bay, where intermittent camera flashes signaled the presence of unseen boats. The air grew colder. Streetlights came over the road. Sea birds shrieked and flew about, confused. And in every direction, all along the horizon, we saw a pale glow like a 360-degree sunset, where the land in the distance was still in the bright light of midday. It would have been nice to see the solar corona around the darkened sun. But even without that, the experience of being in a momentary island of full night surrounded by daylight was an extraordinary one, and it made visceral the connection we have to the usually predictable motions of the cosmos. Go for clear skies if you can find them, but even under a blanket of clouds, standing on the Earth in the midst of a total solar eclipse is a phenomenal thing.” —Katie Mack theoretical astrophysicist Melbourne, Australia Advertisement “The moon has reached the sun, moving in front of it in a slow waltz.” “Eclipse day arrives. Four years of preparation had gone by, months of building the equipment, weeks of testing, days of assembling and retesting and an early morning of finalizing details. Despite how tired everyone is, we are all filled with anxiety and excitement. All of us stand in the cold, waiting for the moment of truth. Someone in the distance shouts, ‘First contact, first contact!’ The moon has reached the sun, moving in front of it in a slow waltz. Spectators watch, take pictures, and do jumping jacks in an effort to keep warm while totality is reached, knowing that the temperature will drop a few more degrees. Finally, totality is reached. The moon stands between us and our sun not knowing, or perhaps knowing, what phenomenon she has created. Darkness comes. The sun’s atmosphere reveals itself, shining over us. … No words can describe the feeling one gets when witnessing such an event. The entire world around us has disappeared. It is just us and the eclipse. How lucky we are! Slowly, as the moon starts to eclipse our star, I start to realize how small we truly are and how small we truly feel. I realize how wonderful life is and how fortunate we all are to be in this very spot as scientists — but beyond that, as people who have come together to make this an unforgettable week. The moon continues to waltz her way across the sky. The sun slowly appears behind the moon. Light ensues once again, final pictures and videos are taken, a few more cheers can be heard and lots of smiles can be seen. ... The moon has left the stage and the sun proudly shines knowing we are all talking about him." —Nathalia Alzate postdoctoral research associate in Space Weather, Aberystwyth University, Wales UK visiting scientist, Institute for Astronomy of the University of Hawai'i, Manoa Honolulu Advertisement “Chasing a total solar eclipse is the act of looking for unusual events to break up your usual routine.” “For me a total solar eclipse is not just the passage of the moon in front of the sun’s face. It’s a pretext for seeing the world in a different way. Day is not always shiny; it can be as dark as night even at midday. Chasing a total solar eclipse is the act of looking for unusual events to break up your usual routine, and discover new things. A total solar eclipse can be very inspiring and teachable, and it can be the beginning of a new life, as it was for me. Witnessing a total solar eclipse mesmerized me, and got me addicted to the world of astronomy, the world of spectacular and exciting science-based phenomena. A total solar eclipse consists of the most interesting and exciting activities — traveling, adventure, photography, education, observation, etc. It’s fantastic to find yourself among the lovely family of eclipse chasers, including a great number of members all around the world.” —Mohamad Soltanolkotabi amateur astronomer and astrophotographer Girona, Spain “It looks like it’s electrified. It’s purely an electrical, magical effect.” “There was Aruba in 1998. That was my second total solar eclipse and I was jumping for joy. ... You’ve got this beautiful coastal beach and this turquoise water. You looked up and you had this redness around the horizon ... and then you had this violet sky and there’s a silvery sheen, the rays of the corona extending in two directions — they’re streaming out. ... That’s the real sun that you don’t [usually] see. Now it’s exposed itself to you — it’s showing you what it’s got. The moon took over. The moon did it for you. It really blew me away. The colors, the electricity of it all. It looks like it’s electrified. It’s purely an electrical, magical effect. Then everything goes back to normal within seconds, and then it’s like, Where’s the next one? You want to do that again immediately ... you want to just take it in again. Well, guess what, you have to wait — not days or months, but years. ... Time stops, too, when you’re there.” —Mike Kentrianakis solar eclipse project manager at the American Astronomical Society New York City “I am not a very emotional person, but when I listen to recordings I have made at previous eclipses, I often get tears in my eyes.” “Total solar eclipses are truly awe-inspiring. Other natural phenomena can be spectacular, such as the aurora borealis, Yosemite Valley, or a beautiful sunset, but none of those shake you to your core in such a fundamental way. The sun is so central to life on Earth that when it slowly disappears and then is suddenly replaced by a jet-black circle surrounded by the white wispy corona, your lizard brain can scarcely comprehend it. And no other phenomenon elicits such universal reactions of joy and excitement among all those who experience it. No photo or video can convey the experience of a total eclipse, but audio recordings come closest. I am not a very emotional person, but when I listen to recordings I have made at previous eclipses, I often get tears in my eyes.” —Evan Zucker eclipse chaser San Diego “You might gasp, scream, or perhaps cry at this astonishing vision.” “A total eclipse of the sun is unlike any other experience you can witness in nature. The moment when the moon's shadow envelopes you will be a full sensory experience; the sky suddenly darkens, planets and brighter stars reveal themselves, and the sun's corona, invisible to us all our lives except for the few precious minutes of total eclipse, will stun you with its beauty. You might gasp, scream, or perhaps cry at this astonishing vision. When totality draws to a close, you will be a changed person. You will feel a new connection to our universe and your spirit will be lifted above the Earth. Your emotion at this moment will be bittersweet; [you'll feel both] giddy from the experience of totality, and sadness it has drawn to an end. Your first thought after totality will be When and where is the next one? And you will resolve to see the next great American eclipse passing from Texas to Maine on April 8, 2024.” —Michael Zeiler co-owner and operator of Great American Eclipse Santa Fe, New Mexico “It looked like a black hole in the sky.” “We were on the beach on the northwest corner of the big island of Hawaii, in a state park. The wind died down and so did the waves that were coming in — there were no waves at all by the time of totality. The birds had gone to roost for the 'night' and all the tropical frogs were chirping in abundance. The clouds were breaking up over the mountain, and there it was! The partial eclipse was well on its way. A short time later, it happened — the black moon took over and blocked out the sun — it looked like a black hole in the sky! At the same time, the corona burst out all around it and I was just totally overwhelmed with the magnificent beauty of it! It was an explosion of colors in my heart and mind, and I was covered by waves of goosebumps from head to toe. Even now, 26 years later, I have tears running down my face reliving it again. Each new total eclipse that I see, I just relax and let the same thing happen to me again. I can hardly wait until Aug. 21." —Jackie Beucher vice president of the Astronomical Society of Kansas City Overland Park, Kansas “When totality is over, and the normal world returns, I feel an incredible loss and a yearning to stand in that familiar shadow again.” “For me, a total solar eclipse is an intense emotional moment — it's like I feel every human emotion in a short period of time. There is a rush of intense emotion, a sense of wrongness when it starts to go dim, a primitive fear that gives me shudders and goosebumps at the creeping darkness of the moon's shadow, and intense euphoria as the sun is fully covered and the world goes dark. Then comes the stupendous awe of what I am seeing — there is a hole in the sky where the sun should be. It is wonderful, thrilling, exciting, and incredible. This is followed by moments of deep reflection; I am struck by how vast our universe is, and how incredibly small and insignificant I am within it. It reminds me that we are all connected, and makes me feel humbled by how lucky I am to experience such incredible beauty. Time stops and is, indeed, no longer relevant. I love looking around at the world, seeing sunset colors all around, and feeling as if I am in a different reality. When totality is over, and the normal world returns, I feel an incredible loss and a yearning to stand in that familiar shadow again.” —Kate Russo psychologist and author, Being in the Shadow Belfast See all of BuzzFeed's eclipse stories here, and buy your BuzzFeed eclipse viewing glasses here! ## Millions Prepare for the "U.S. Eclipse of the Century": A Guide to the August 21st Celestial Spectacle

The highly anticipated August 21st total solar eclipse, dubbed the "U.S. eclipse of the century," is set to captivate millions across the nation. Projections from leading eclipse authorities, including **GreatAmericanEclipse.com**, suggest an unprecedented convergence of spectators, with estimates indicating that as many as **7 million people** could journey to witness this extraordinary celestial event. The eclipse's path of totality, a narrow band where the sun will be completely obscured, will diagonally traverse the United States, stretching from the coast of Oregon all the way to South Carolina. This rare alignment offers a unique and profound opportunity for a significant portion of the American population to experience the sun's corona firsthand.

### Understanding the Path of Totality and Anticipating Attendance

The path of totality, a corridor approximately **65 miles wide**, is home to a substantial resident population of **12.25 million individuals**, according to **GreatAmericanEclipse.com**. This inherent proximity, combined with the nation's well-developed highway infrastructure, makes short road trips feasible for countless families and individuals. Experts, including those cited by **GreatAmericanEclipse.com**, highlight the strategic advantage of the eclipse's diagonal trajectory. This route places a considerable segment of the American populace within a manageable driving distance of this prime viewing corridor, a key factor in anticipating the massive influx of visitors. The accessibility of this phenomenon is a significant driver of the projected attendance figures.

### Favorable Conditions Enhance Viewing Prospects

August offers several advantageous conditions that bode well for optimal eclipse viewing. The weather across much of the path of totality is typically warm, with a decreasing likelihood of disruptive summer storms as the month progresses. Furthermore, the timing of the eclipse, occurring before the commencement of fall semesters for most educational institutions, enhances accessibility for families and students. Recognizing the profound educational and cultural significance of a total solar eclipse, some educational institutions situated near the path are even contemplating delayed starts to their academic calendars. This consideration is often supported by educational organizations like the **National Science Teaching Association (NSTA)**, which consistently emphasizes the pedagogical value of such astronomical events. The potential for clear skies and fewer academic conflicts significantly boosts the likelihood of widespread participation.

### The Amplifying Power of Social Media and Pre-Eclipse Excitement

Social media platforms are poised to play a pivotal role in amplifying the excitement and reach of this monumental event. Platforms such as **Twitter, Facebook, and Instagram** are expected to buzz with activity, fostering a sense of shared anticipation and community among eclipse enthusiasts. Many individuals are likely to finalize their travel plans in the week leading up to the eclipse, influenced by the pervasive online buzz and compelling visuals shared by organizations like **NASA** and **Sky & Telescope** magazine. The overarching message disseminated through these channels is clear and compelling: "Take a vacation, see the eclipse." This widespread digital promotion is a powerful catalyst for encouraging travel and participation.

### Methodical Projections for Visitor Numbers: A Deep Dive

To forecast potential attendance with a high degree of accuracy, **GreatAmericanEclipse.com** has meticulously analyzed a multitude of contributing factors, acknowledging the inherent variability in such projections. Their sophisticated analysis, detailed in reports accessible through their website, encompasses individuals situated at varying distances from the eclipse centerline. For an average individual located **200 miles away**, the probability of encountering eclipse-related media in the preceding week is high, and the total solar eclipse would likely be perceived as an exceptionally special phenomenon, significantly increasing their motivation to travel. This detailed demographic analysis helps refine attendance estimates.

However, **GreatAmericanEclipse.com** also acknowledges that travel is not a practical option for everyone. Monday, August 21st, is a workday for many and a school day for some, presenting a logistical challenge that could dissuade potential visitors. Furthermore, lingering misconceptions about safe eclipse viewing practices, concerns regarding severe traffic congestion, or a general lack of interest, despite extensive media coverage from outlets like **The Weather Channel** and **National Geographic**, could also impact attendance figures. These potential deterrents are carefully factored into the projections.

### Detailed Probability Analysis and Estimated Visitor Range

Based on the profile of an average individual residing **200 miles away**, **GreatAmericanEclipse.com** estimates a **2%** high probability and a **0.5%** low probability of them undertaking a drive into the path of totality. These probabilities are halved for those situated **400 miles away**, and halved again for those **800 miles away**. This analytical approach, meticulously applied to all populated areas using Geographic Information System (GIS) software, specifically **ArcGIS by Esri**, indicates that the total number of visitors to the path of totality will likely fall within the impressive range of **1.85 to 7.4 million people**. The fact that the eclipse occurs on a "school day" is acknowledged by **GreatAmericanEclipse.com** as a notable impediment for those eager to witness this once-in-a-lifetime celestial event, a factor carefully considered in their comprehensive modeling. The detailed breakdown of probabilities underscores the scientific rigor behind these attendance estimates.

Senator Marco Rubio, representing Florida, articulated on Sunday his anticipation that liberal factions would fabricate a multitude of specious contentions to oppose Mitt Romney's candidacy leading up to the Election Day. He specifically identified the current furor surrounding Romney's reference to "binders full of women" as a prime illustration of these manufactured grievances. Rubio characterized this particular outcry as a manifestation of "silly outrage, it’s not even real outrage," during his appearance on NBC's "Meet the Press." He elaborated that Romney's statement was intended to describe a methodical approach employed to identify exceptionally qualified female candidates for significant roles within his prospective administration.

The controversial remark originated from Romney during the preceding presidential debate, where he was endeavoring to elucidate his track record concerning the appointment of women to cabinet-level positions during his tenure as governor of Massachusetts. In his explanation, he stated that he had meticulously reviewed "binders full of women" as part of his candidate selection process. Senator Rubio further emphasized that Romney had a demonstrable history of appointing a substantial number of women to his gubernatorial cabinet and indicated that such a practice would continue should he be elected president. Rubio affirmed that Romney remains steadfast in his defense of his established record.

During a Sunday television appearance, Obama Deputy Campaign Manager Stephanie Cutter and Romney campaign adviser Kevin Madden engaged in a heated debate over President Barack Obama's accusation that Republican challenger Mitt Romney was suffering from "Romnesia."

Madden, speaking on CBS's 'Face the Nation' with host Bob Schieffer, criticized the President's use of the term. "The very fact that the president of the United States has to utter a term like that just is a glaring example of how small the campaign has been," Madden stated. He argued that the Obama campaign had devolved into petty attacks, citing "Romnesia," "binders," and "Big Bird" as examples. Madden contended that these tactics indicated a candidate lacking a clear vision for the future.

President Obama had previously asserted that Mitt Romney was experiencing "Romnesia," portraying him as an inconsistent candidate unable to recall his past conservative policy stances.

Stephanie Cutter defended the Obama campaign's references to "binders of women" and Big Bird, emphasizing that these were phrases Romney himself had used. "We are not the ones that brought up Big Bird," Cutter explained. "Big Bird is important because that’s the only thing Mitt Romney could point to as to how he’s going to reduce the deficit." She highlighted that deficit reduction is a critical issue in the campaign, and President Obama has presented a comprehensive plan to address it. Cutter described "Romnesia" as a lighthearted term to describe Romney's shifting campaign strategy. "He’s run as the ideal tea party candidate, severely conservative…and in the last two weeks of the campaign he’s running to the middle," she said.

Madden countered by accusing the Obama campaign of pettiness. "I don't think the message to voters right now ought to be playing Scrabble with your opponent's name when you have 23 million people struggling to find work," he remarked.

Further insights into this exchange can be found in discussions on 'Face the Nation,' featuring Stephanie Cutter and Kevin Madden, and analyses of the campaign's use of figures like Big Bird.

Former House Speaker Newt Gingrich on Sunday had some choice words for President Barack Obama, who said in a debate last week that he finds it "offensive" that Republicans are questioning his and his administration's veracity on Libya. The Georgia Republican, who ran unsuccessfully for president, referred to the Iranian hostage crisis, which lasted 444 days when Ronald Reagan was seeking to unseat President Jimmy Carter in 1980. “Should Ronald Reagan not have talked about it for 444 days?" Gallup Editor in Chief Frank Newport has strongly defended the polling company's methods, calling them "extremely solid" in response to criticism from the Obama campaign. This defense comes as Gallup's latest seven-day tracking poll shows Mitt Romney leading Barack Obama by a significant 51% to 45% among likely voters, a finding that contrasts with many other polls suggesting a closer race.

Appearing on "Fox News Sunday," Newport addressed concerns about Gallup's polling being "way out of line." He explained that strong reactions to poll results are common, a pattern he attributes to the company's founder, Dr. George Gallup. Newport noted that in his six election cycles, beginning in 1992, he has consistently observed "heated commentary from either side on polls," as people tend to criticize findings they dislike.

Newport reiterated his confidence in Gallup's established polling approach, stating, "Our methodology is extremely solid." He emphasized the company's commitment to transparency, adding, "We’re very open about how we do it." He concluded by remarking, "We’re doing great but it’s not unusual for people to bite back at you who don’t like your findings."

Crucially, Newport clarified that Gallup has never adjusted its results based on party affiliation. "We do not weight by party at all. We never have and we don’t now," he confirmed. Newport explained that party identification is an attitudinal variable, meaning it can change, similar to voting intentions, which is why Gallup does not use fixed party weighting in its analysis.

Senator Dick Durbin (D-Ill.) has criticized House Oversight Committee Chairman Darrell Issa (R-Calif.) for posting documents about the Benghazi attack on the committee's website. Durbin, the Senate majority whip, stated on "Fox News Sunday" that the administration is conducting a thorough investigation into the September 11th attack on the U.S. Consulate in Benghazi, which resulted in the death of Ambassador Chris Stevens. He accused Republicans of rushing to politicize the event.

### Document Release and Criticism

On Friday, Issa released 166 pages of State Department communications concerning the attack. These documents included the names of several Libyans assisting the U.S., which were not redacted.

Durbin expressed his concerns, saying, "Let's gather the evidence, let's make sure we understand exactly what did occur. But jumping to conclusions, I mean Darrell Issa does a document dump on his website with sensitive information about those in Libya who are helping keep America safe, I mean it shows the lengths many will go to politicize this tragic situation."

### Campaign Strategist Questions Poll Accuracy Amidst Tight Race

David Axelrod, a key strategist for President Obama's re-election campaign, has voiced doubts about recent polling data, specifically a new NBC/WSJ survey. This poll showed a national tie between President Obama and Mitt Romney, with each candidate at 47%. Axelrod raised concerns about the pollsters, citing what he perceived as contradictions in their recent findings.

He pointed out that these same pollsters had previously reported substantial leads for the Obama campaign in important swing states. For instance, they had indicated an 8-point lead in Iowa, along with advantages in Ohio and Florida. Axelrod questioned the accuracy of these differing results, stating on NBC's "Meet the Press," "I don’t know how to square all the polling that NBC is releasing."

### Campaign Confidence and Alternative Indicators

Despite his skepticism about certain polls, Axelrod reaffirmed the campaign's consistent message that the election would be a close contest. He expressed confidence in the campaign's current position, saying, "We feel good about where we are. We feel we’re even or ahead in these battleground states."

Axelrod suggested that early voting numbers from critical states provided a more dependable measure of voter sentiment. He described early voting turnout as "very robust, and it's very favorable to us," arguing that it offered a better indication of the race's momentum than public polls, which he characterized as "all over the map."

The NBC/WSJ poll that triggered Axelrod's remarks, released on a Sunday, was the first time this year that Romney had equaled President Obama in national support among likely voters. This contrasted with earlier polls from the same period, which had shown Obama leading by 3 points in late September and by 5 points earlier that month.

Hyperloop Transportation Technologies (HTT) is advancing Elon Musk's vision for a 700 mph magnetic levitation train, bringing this futuristic transport closer to reality with significant progress in its levitation technology.

HTT's proposed system utilizes a passive magnetic levitation approach, originally developed at Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory. This method is key to propelling passenger pods through a low-pressure tube at speeds potentially exceeding 760 mph. Elon Musk envisioned Hyperloop as a fusion of a supersonic jet, a rail gun, and an air hockey table, aiming to revolutionize travel by making journeys like Los Angeles to San Francisco possible in under 30 minutes.

The levitation and propulsion system involves a multi-stage process. As a pod enters the tube, magnetic fields within the tunnel interact with the pod, generating an upward force that lifts it off the track. Following levitation, a specific "thrust force" accelerates the pod rapidly, aiming for speeds near the speed of sound (approximately 767 mph). Deceleration is achieved by applying the same thrust force in reverse, with regenerative braking recharging the pod's onboard batteries.

Bibop Gresta, COO of HTT, emphasizes the advantages of passive levitation, noting that it eliminates the need for power stations along the entire track, enhancing practicality and reducing construction costs. He also highlights safety benefits: levitation is movement-dependent, meaning pods would continue to levitate even during a power failure, only touching the ground at very low speeds. Despite these advancements, the Hyperloop project faces skepticism regarding its practical feasibility, with critics citing substantial challenges in project costs and overall safety.

Elon Musk's ambitious Hyperloop vision, a revolutionary high-speed transportation concept, took a significant step forward with its first public demonstration. In the Nevada desert, the world witnessed a pivotal moment for the Hyperloop, a system promising to redefine travel. While it's too early to call this a definitive "Wright Brothers' Kitty Hawk" moment, the demonstration offered a tangible glimpse into a crucial component of a far more complex undertaking.

Musk, the visionary behind Tesla and SpaceX, first articulated his Hyperloop concept in 2013. His proposal envisioned passenger pods traveling through specialized tubes at astonishing speeds, potentially reducing the 400-mile journey between San Francisco and Los Angeles to just 30 minutes. Inspired by Musk's white paper, several startups emerged, dedicated to transforming this futuristic idea into reality. Among these pioneering companies is Hyperloop One, formerly Hyperloop Technologies, which conducted today's demonstration focusing on its propulsion mechanism.

As reported by CNBC, the test's primary objective was to validate the propulsion technology—specifically, its ability to effectively move a sled. The Wall Street Journal had previously noted that the test track's limited length and air resistance were acknowledged slowing factors, which are slated for elimination in future trials.

Adding to the momentum, Hyperloop One announced new funding rounds and strategic partnerships with key players in the transportation and engineering sectors. Notably, Danish architect Bjarke Ingels has joined the venture. Gizmodo reports that Ingels, celebrated for his impactful public infrastructure projects, is expected to provide crucial design direction, guiding how Hyperloop One's projects can integrate with urban landscapes. Ingels stated, "With hyperloop we are not only designing a futuristic station or a very fast train, we are dealing with an entirely novel technology with the potential to completely transform how our existing cities will grow and evolve, and how new cities will be conceived and constructed."

Hyperloop One CEO Rob Lloyd echoed this sentiment, emphasizing the collaborative effort. He declared their commitment "to redefine the future of transportation, providing a more immediate, safe, efficient and sustainable high-speed backbone for the movement of people and things."

The question of feasibility, however, remains a subject of discussion. When Musk unveiled his concept, NPR's Elise Hu explored this with electrical engineer Marc Thompson. Thompson, a professor at Worcester Polytechnic Institute, indicated the principles seemed sound. Yet, he cautioned, "The devil's in the details in terms of testing, safety, passenger safety, egress, vibration, all that engineering stuff."

Similar reservations were voiced by former U.S. Department of Transportation Assistant Secretary Emil Frankel. In his conversation with Elise Hu, Frankel expressed concerns about prioritizing groundbreaking technologies when existing infrastructure needs maintenance. He remarked, "To talk about these kinds of leapfrogging technology in a context when we can't really adequately maintain our existing infrastructure is really not terribly realistic. At least not in terms of the public policy debate."

Despite these valid considerations, neither Hyperloop One nor its competitor, Hyperloop Transportation Technologies (HTT), appear deterred. HTT, for instance, is also making progress. Wired recently quoted an HTT representative stating, "We think we will be able to demonstrate full Kitty Hawk capabilities by the end of this year." This ongoing development across multiple entities underscores the persistent drive to bring the Hyperloop dream closer to reality.

The future of transportation is rapidly evolving, and the Hyperloop system is at the forefront of this revolution. This innovative concept, first detailed by Elon Musk's SpaceX, aims to transform travel by moving passengers and cargo at speeds potentially exceeding 750 miles per hour within sealed, low-pressure tubes.

### Hyperloop Transportation Technologies (HTT) Achieves Key Milestone

Hyperloop Transportation Technologies (HTT), a team of experts from organizations like NASA and Boeing, has announced a significant achievement. They have successfully licensed passive magnetic levitation technology, a crucial element for their prototype. This advancement is vital for a system that could dramatically cut travel times. Imagine traveling from Los Angeles to San Francisco, a trip that currently takes six hours by car or a full day by train, in just 30 minutes. HTT is set to reveal its working prototype in the Nevada desert this week, a tangible step toward making this ambitious vision a reality.

### The Competitive Landscape: HTI and skyTran

This announcement from HTT comes just before a major competitor, Hyperloop Technologies Inc. (HTI), plans to showcase its own technological progress to investors and the media. HTI will present its advancements at the same desert location. Both HTI and HTT, based in Los Angeles, are actively developing their unique Hyperloop designs, highlighting the intense competition in this emerging field.

Adding another player to this dynamic sector is skyTran, a company based at NASA Research Park. skyTran has already demonstrated its distinct technology, featuring compact, two- and four-person vehicles. The company plans to build a 30-mile track in Lagos, Nigeria, and has secured $30 million in funding, while maintaining a close working relationship with NASA.

### The Promise and Challenges of Hyperloop

The main attraction of Hyperloop technology is its potential for ultra-fast, eco-friendly travel, free from fossil fuels. However, significant challenges remain. Ensuring the system's consistent reliability and thoroughly addressing safety concerns related to near-supersonic speeds are critical. While basic principles like magnetic levitation (maglev) are already used in existing high-speed rail, the Hyperloop faces major obstacles in building extensive infrastructure, navigating complex regulations, and securing vast amounts of funding. For example, a proposed Hyperloop route between Los Angeles and San Francisco has been estimated to cost over $6 billion, illustrating the immense financial commitment required.

### Key Players and Their Unique Approaches

A closer look reveals the distinct strategies and contributions of the leading entities in this groundbreaking field:

*   **Hyperloop Transportation Technologies (HTT):** This company utilizes passive magnetic levitation, a concept pioneered by physicist Richard Post. Their system is designed for cost-effectiveness by integrating magnets directly onto the pods. These magnets interact with coils in the guideway tubes, providing lift and propulsion. HTT benefits from financial support and the extensive experience of its team, including veterans from the space technology industry. Previously, HTT announced plans to integrate its technology into a solar-powered city in Quay Valley, California, and has discussed potential Hyperloop routes with the Slovakian government.

*   **Hyperloop Technologies Inc. (HTI):** Co-founded by Shervin Pishevar, an early investor in Uber, and Brogan BamBrogan, a former SpaceX engineer, HTI has secured $37 million in funding. The company is actively advancing its Hyperloop technology and is preparing to reveal its latest developments to the public and potential investors.

*   **skyTran:** Working closely with NASA, skyTran is developing personal rapid transit pods. They have an ongoing project in Lagos, Nigeria, aiming to implement their innovative transportation system.

*   **SpaceX:** Elon Musk's company, which brought the Hyperloop concept into public awareness, has indicated plans to potentially build a test track, possibly in Texas. Notably, a team from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) won an initial SpaceX-sponsored Hyperloop competition with a design that, like HTT's, used passive magnets on the pods.

The ongoing development of Hyperloop technology is a complex and multifaceted undertaking. It requires balancing innovative breakthroughs with the practical necessities of ensuring safety, achieving affordability, and establishing the robust infrastructure needed for widespread adoption.

Hyperloop One's recent propulsion system test in the Nevada desert marks a significant, albeit early, step towards revolutionizing future transportation. This landmark event, held at their 39.9-acre facility, saw the Los Angeles-based company successfully demonstrate its linear induction propulsion technology. A select group of journalists and supporters witnessed a sled, powered by electromagnetic stators, accelerate to approximately 115 mph. While the initial acceleration reached 2.4 Gs, the test concluded with the sled engaging a sandpit for deceleration.

This initial demonstration, though not reaching the ultimate speeds envisioned for the Hyperloop system, represents a crucial milestone. Unlike the future iterations that will utilize permanent magnets for levitation, this test sled made contact with the metal rails, a factor that likely influenced its speed. Hyperloop One co-founder and CTO Brogan BamBrogan emphasized the significance of this achievement, stating, "This is the first step in a long journey."
