Bernie Sanders' push for a single-payer healthcare system, Medicare for All, has gained considerable traction among many liberal senators, including those eyeing a 2020 presidential run. However, the broader Democratic Party remains divided on this pivotal issue. As Sanders prepares to reintroduce his legislation, numerous congressional leaders and vulnerable Senate incumbents are strategically maintaining distance.

While the progressive wing celebrates Sanders' success in shifting the Democratic agenda leftward following his 2016 primary challenge, this influence appears to have its boundaries. Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer indicated he would review all Democratic proposals for expanding healthcare access but stopped short of endorsing Sanders' plan. House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi prioritized defending the Affordable Care Act (ACA) from potential Republican repeal efforts.

Connecticut Sen. Chris Murphy, a Democrat facing 2020 speculation who has not yet co-sponsored the bill, expressed concern about the party diverting attention to long-term healthcare policy while the ACA's future remains uncertain. He warned, "I think the risk is that we get distracted. September’s not done. They can still ram through a repeal bill."

Wisconsin Sen. Tammy Baldwin became the first Senate Democrat up for reelection next year in a state carried by Trump to support the single-payer bill. However, other politically vulnerable incumbent Democrats have declined to back Sanders.

*   **Sen. Claire McCaskill** stated that more work is needed to control healthcare costs before expanding Medicare to everyone. She believes a national single-payer system would present "a lot of problems," though she supports allowing individuals aged 55 and older to buy into Medicare.
*   This Medicare-at-55 proposal is also supported by **Sens. Sherrod Brown** (Ohio) and **Debbie Stabenow** (Michigan), both red-state Democrats facing reelection. Stabenow, a member of Democratic leadership, emphasized her focus on her Medicare-at-55 plan due to potential bipartisan interest. She argued the party's immediate priority should be strengthening the ACA and stabilizing markets, followed by efforts to lower prescription drug costs and increase healthcare options.

Improving the ACA is a central focus of a bipartisan effort in the Senate health committee. Sen. Patty Murray, the committee's ranking member and a Democratic leader, also did not endorse Sanders' bill, stating she needs more time to review the various Democratic proposals.

Republicans have quickly capitalized on the projected costs of a single-payer system, which Sanders proposes to fund through new taxes on employers and wealthy individuals. The National Republican Senatorial Committee criticized Baldwin's support for "the left’s radical plans for government-run health care." Sen. John Barrasso (R-Wyo.) reminded reporters that Vermont, Sanders' home state, abandoned its own single-payer initiative due to its economic burden.

Supporters of Sanders' bill acknowledge the significant political challenge but view single-payer as a crucial benchmark for the Democratic Party's future. As the party defines itself beyond opposition to the Trump administration, proponents of single-payer see an opportunity to set ambitious long-term goals while simultaneously defending the ACA.

Sen. Richard Blumenthal, who co-sponsored the bill, stated, "There’s nothing about the politics of the moment or the Affordable Care Act that in any way precludes supporting Medicare-for-all as the ultimate goal, and there’s a clear path to it." Sen. Al Franken, also considered a potential 2020 candidate, is expected to sign on, viewing the bill as an aspirational long-term goal while emphasizing the immediate need for bipartisan policies to improve the current healthcare system.

However, for some Democrats, the timing for single-payer may not be right. Sen. Ben Cardin supports universal coverage but has "certain concerns" about a single-payer approach, citing both political feasibility and ensuring adequate healthcare resources. Sen. Joe Manchin, a key target for Republicans in 2018, expressed skepticism about single-payer but advocated for careful consideration of all options through regular order to understand their impact on people and the economy. Sen. Dianne Feinstein, facing pressure from liberals in California where a state-level single-payer effort failed, stated she needs to see the cost projections before taking a position, noting her support for a public option.

Sens. Chris Murphy and Brian Schatz have proposed alternative approaches to advance the party's healthcare debate without adopting Sanders' full plan. Murphy is developing legislation for a Medicare buy-in for all individuals and businesses, while Schatz plans to release a Medicaid buy-in proposal. Murphy urged the party to "take some time and look at all the options available to us before we decide on one unitary route."

Despite potential primary challenges from some Sanders-aligned activists against those who don't support single-payer, other liberals are celebrating Sanders' ability to rally significant caucus support. Adam Green, co-founder of the Progressive Change Campaign Committee, believes this strategy mirrors how other major policy ideas have entered the mainstream, with a clear long-term vision and incremental progress building party consensus.

Senator Bernie Sanders is introducing legislation for a government-provided health insurance plan for all Americans. This proposal, often called "Medicare for All," aims to create a single-payer system. This comes as Republican senators are also preparing their own plan, a final effort to repeal and replace the Affordable Care Act (ACA).

These two legislative packages are very different and both face significant challenges. This renewed focus on healthcare reform follows the Republican Party's failed attempt in July to dismantle the ACA.

The Republican plan, led by Senators Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.) and Bill Cassidy (R-La.), proposes:

*   Changing and reducing funding for Medicaid.
*   Giving ACA funds directly to states.
*   Removing penalties for people without health insurance.

However, this bill has not gained much support. Senate GOP leadership admits it will be hard to pass. No Democrats are expected to back it, meaning it would need almost all Republican senators' votes. Senate Majority Leader Mitch McConnell (R-Ky.) has expressed doubts about holding another vote that could lead to an embarrassing loss for his party. Some conservatives also feel the bill doesn't go far enough in ending the ACA's coverage requirements.

Meanwhile, Senator Sanders' plan seeks to extend Medicare to cover everyone. This idea is supported by the progressive wing of the Democratic Party. Supporters believe it would make healthcare simpler and lower costs for individuals and businesses. The plan is designed to cover the millions of Americans still without insurance under the ACA. Under Sanders' proposal, people would get coverage by showing a card, with no deductibles or out-of-pocket costs. Instead, premiums would be based on income. Those earning the least would pay nothing, while higher earners and corporations would pay more in taxes.

This initiative creates a political dilemma for Democrats. Opposing Sanders' plan could upset liberal voters, while supporting it might lead Republicans to accuse them of pushing for higher taxes and a government-controlled healthcare system that could end employer-provided insurance. Senator Sanders, however, argues this is a distraction and points to public support for a Medicare-for-all system.

Several prominent Senate Democrats have co-sponsored Sanders' bill, including potential presidential candidates Kamala Harris, Elizabeth Warren, Kirsten Gillibrand, and Cory Booker. Some analysts see Sanders' plan as a strategic move for his presidential ambitions, while others question if it could win in a general election.

To address this political situation, some Democrats are proposing alternative bills that expand coverage without fully adopting Sanders' plan. Senators up for re-election in Republican-leaning states are focusing on strengthening the current ACA.

Republicans have stated they are ready for these Democratic proposals. Separately, bipartisan efforts to stabilize individual insurance markets are underway, led by Senators Lamar Alexander (R-Tenn.) and Patty Murray (D-Wash.). Their discussions involve continuing federal payments to insurers, which President Trump has threatened to stop. However, disagreements remain. Republicans want more state control over insurance rules, while Democrats worry about potential increases in patient costs.

The Internet Archive is undertaking a significant effort to preserve the web's history by archiving pages as they are created, along with any pages they reference. This proactive approach ensures that even if referenced pages are later altered or removed from the live web, a stable link to the version that existed at the time of the original page's creation will remain accessible.

The Internet Archive's vision is that these preserved links will serve as a vital resource, replacing broken links and offering a companion to current content. This allows users to see the original intent of a page's authors, effectively aiming to "fix all broken links on the web." This initiative is supported by crawls of sites participating in the "No More 404" program.

## The Promise and Peril of Single-Payer Healthcare in the United States

The concept of single-payer healthcare, a system where a single public entity finances healthcare for all citizens, is a topic of significant debate in the United States. Nations such as Canada, Australia, and many European countries have successfully implemented such models, consistently demonstrating superior performance. These systems frequently yield both reduced expenditures and enhanced life expectancies when contrasted with the United States' current multi-payer, largely private insurance-based model. This compelling evidence forms a cornerstone argument for the adoption of a single-payer system in America. As articulated by Senator Bernie Sanders on "The Late Show with Stephen Colbert," the aspiration is clear: "In America, we should join every industrialized country and guarantee health care to all Americans as a right."

### The American Healthcare Paradox: Innovation Amidst Inefficiency

While the American healthcare landscape is undeniably marked by exorbitant costs and operational inefficiencies, it simultaneously stands as a global vanguard of medical innovation. The United States consistently produces revolutionary pharmaceuticals, sophisticated medical devices, and pioneering therapeutic interventions. Furthermore, the United States is home to many of the world's preeminent medical institutions and esteemed researchers, drawing affluent individuals from across the globe seeking unparalleled specialized care. Institutions like the Mayo Clinic, Cleveland Clinic, and Johns Hopkins Hospital are renowned for their cutting-edge treatments and attract patients from around the world. The National Institutes of Health (NIH) also plays a pivotal role in driving groundbreaking medical research. A thoughtfully implemented single-payer framework could potentially retain these critical strengths while systematically addressing and mitigating existing waste. However, a more granular explanation from proponents detailing precisely how this delicate balance would be achieved remains a crucial desideratum, a point often raised by healthcare policy analysts.

### Navigating the Political Minefield

The path to implementing a single-payer system within the United States is fraught with formidable political obstacles. Even acknowledging the potential for significant cost reductions and demonstrable quality improvements, the sheer magnitude of the transition would inevitably prove disruptive. A substantial portion of the population would be compelled to alter their existing insurance arrangements, a prospect that vividly recalls the considerable political turbulence encountered during the rollout of the Affordable Care Act (ACA). Moreover, the fundamental shift from a predominantly private healthcare expenditure model to one reliant on public funding would necessitate a substantial increase in taxation.

While Senator Sanders deserves commendation for his unwavering dedication and meticulously detailed proposals, his 2016 plan, as rigorously analyzed by the Urban Institute, notably underestimated the scale of the required tax increases. The unsuccessful attempt to establish a single-payer system in Vermont, an endeavor that significantly impacted the governor's political trajectory, serves as a stark illustration of the intense political opposition such a transformative system invariably encounters. Experts at the American Enterprise Institute have also voiced concerns regarding the potential economic impacts and the feasibility of such a large-scale government undertaking.

### Seeking a Pragmatic Middle Ground

The discourse surrounding single-payer healthcare is far from a simplistic binary choice; indeed, a pragmatic middle ground exists, offering a more nuanced approach. Nations with robust government involvement in healthcare, including those in Europe, Canada, and Australia, consistently report lower overall healthcare expenditures, a finding supported by extensive data compiled by organizations like the World Health Organization (WHO). Concurrently, citizens in these countries frequently enjoy longer life expectancies than their American counterparts, according to analyses from entities such as the Commonwealth Fund.

Conversely, the United States, despite its systemic inefficiencies and high costs, excels in medical innovation, with institutions like the National Institutes of Health (NIH) spearheading groundbreaking research and leading hospitals, including the Mayo Clinic and Cleveland Clinic, attracting international patients for advanced treatments. The challenge lies in preserving these American strengths while transitioning to a more equitable and cost-effective system. Analyses from the Congressional Budget Office (CBO) underscore the complexities of the financial implications, particularly the necessity of increased taxation, and past political experiences, such as the difficulties encountered in Vermont, highlight the significant legislative hurdles. Therefore, the broader discussion on healthcare reform presents an invaluable opportunity to explore a spectrum of approaches that extend beyond a rigid adherence to or rejection of single-payer, potentially incorporating elements of public option plans or enhanced regulatory oversight as suggested by think tanks like the Brookings Institution.

He wanted to send a message to everyone watching that nobody was tougher, both mentally and physically, than this California kid." In a legendary playoff game, the Snow Bowl in 2002 (the Tuck Rule Game), when the Patriots played the Raiders in the snow, Brady once recalled in an NFL Films interview that "he took the field for warm-ups wearing a sleeveless T-shirt in the thick snow. He was 24 years old, at the beginning of a career only he saw coming. He wanted to send a message to everyone watching that nobody was tougher, both mentally and physically, than this California kid." Worthy of your time.

Senator Bernie Sanders (I-VT) is proposing a significant overhaul of the U.S. healthcare system with his "Medicare for All Act." This single-payer plan aims to provide government-funded health insurance to every American, fundamentally changing how healthcare is accessed and paid for. The proposal seeks to eliminate out-of-pocket costs for essential medical services, offering a benefit package that is more comprehensive than many existing single-payer systems worldwide.

### A Broad Spectrum of Covered Services

The Sanders plan outlines a remarkably extensive list of benefits intended to cover all residents. This includes comprehensive coverage for hospital stays, primary care, medical equipment, lab tests, maternity care, and prescription drugs. A key feature that sets this plan apart is the inclusion of vision and dental benefits, services often not covered or only partially covered in other countries' single-payer models. For example, while Canada's universal healthcare system covers necessary medical services, it typically does not include routine vision or dental care, requiring many Canadians to purchase private insurance for these needs. Similarly, countries like the Netherlands and Australia have limits on the extent of dental and vision coverage within their public healthcare systems.

A central element of the Sanders proposal is the elimination of most out-of-pocket expenses for patients, covering everything from routine doctor visits to emergency care. This contrasts sharply with international systems like Taiwan's, which charges for certain services (though with exceptions for low-income individuals), and Australia's, where patients pay a portion of the cost for specialist appointments. Even within the current U.S. system, Medicare beneficiaries aged 65 and older often pay 20 percent of doctor visit costs after meeting deductibles, and employer-sponsored plans commonly have deductibles exceeding $1,000. The Sanders plan's broad benefits, combined with the absence of patient cost-sharing, represent a substantial departure from these established models.

### Potential for Cost Savings and Increased Access

A core argument for a single-payer system, as championed by Senator Sanders, is its potential to control rising U.S. healthcare costs. Supporters believe a unified national system could significantly reduce administrative waste. A 2003 article in *The New England Journal of Medicine* estimated that the U.S. spends twice as much on administrative costs as Canada. Further research, including a 2011 study in *Health Affairs*, found that American doctors spend considerably more time dealing with insurance companies than their Canadian counterparts.

Under a single-payer system, a standardized pricing structure for medical services could address the wide variations in costs for different procedures. The Sanders plan suggests using current Medicare rates as a benchmark, which are generally lower than those negotiated by private insurers. This standardization could lead to overall cost reductions.

However, the plan also includes provisions that could increase healthcare use and, consequently, costs. The extensive benefits and the elimination of cost-sharing are likely to drive higher demand for medical services. Research, such as the RAND Health Insurance Experiment, has shown a link between lower patient cost-sharing and increased use of medical treatment. Additionally, the plan proposes increasing reimbursement rates for providers serving Medicaid beneficiaries, as current Medicaid payments are typically lower than those from Medicare and private insurance. Integrating the approximately 50 million individuals currently covered by Medicaid into a universal Medicare plan would likely result in higher per-visit payments for physicians. The Congressional Budget Office has not yet released an official analysis of the Sanders plan, making it difficult to definitively predict the net economic impact of these competing cost factors.

### The Unresolved Issue: Funding

The most significant challenge facing the Sanders proposal is its financing. While the "Medicare for All Act" details a comprehensive benefit package, it currently lacks a clear plan for how such a system would be funded. A spokesperson for Senator Sanders has stated that detailed financing options will be provided, and Sanders himself has acknowledged the complexity, indicating that a range of revenue-raising proposals will be presented.

The financial undertaking is undeniably substantial. Harvard health economist William Hsiao has noted that about half of countries that establish single-payer systems face considerable difficulties, often during the financing stage. A cautionary example is Vermont's 2014 attempt at a single-payer plan. Although less ambitious than Sanders's current proposal and still requiring some patient cost-sharing, it ultimately failed due to the projected tax increases needed for its funding. Vermont's plan would have required an 11.5 percent increase in payroll taxes and a 9 percent increase in income tax.

While increased taxes do not automatically mean higher overall health spending and could potentially be offset by eliminating private insurance premiums, the fair distribution of this tax burden is a critical concern. Single-payer systems typically aim for a more progressive tax structure, placing a greater financial responsibility on higher earners. The proposed tax increases in Vermont, for instance, were projected to disproportionately affect individuals with higher incomes. Until the Sanders bill clarifies who will bear the increased costs and by how much, the overall feasibility and public acceptance of the plan remain subjects of intense debate.

Senator Bernie Sanders has reintroduced his "Medicare for All" health care bill, a significant initiative building on his 2013 proposal. This time, the bill boasts backing from a substantial number of Senate Democrats, including Senators Kirsten Gillibrand and Richard Blumenthal, who joined Sanders in criticizing the current healthcare system, labeling its costs as "insane and unaffordable." Sanders projected financial relief for average families, stating, "you will no longer be writing checks to private insurance companies." He addressed concerns about potential tax increases, assuring that "that expense will be more than offset by the money are you are saving with the elimination of private insurance costs."

Sanders outlined plans for a nationwide rollout, including public engagement through workshops. He also directly challenged Republicans, asserting their lack of "credibility on the issue of health care." He urged, "To my Republican colleagues, please don't lecture us on health care," referencing their recent attempts to repeal the Affordable Care Act.

Senator Elizabeth Warren framed the single-payer proposal as a continuation of a long-standing liberal commitment to expanding healthcare access. She traced this lineage from Franklin Roosevelt's Social Security to Medicare and Medicaid under Lyndon Johnson, and most recently, Barack Obama's landmark legislation. "But we are here today to take another step," she declared. "We will not back down in our protection of the Affordable Care Act, we will defend it at every turn, but we will go further -- we will go further and we will say that in this country, everyone gets the right to basic health care."

Before the senators' statements, personal accounts highlighted the struggles faced by healthcare providers and patients. An oncology nurse shared a distressing experience with a cancer patient who could not afford continuous fluid drainage from her lungs. Insurance only partially covered the cost of drainage bags, forcing the patient to ration their use. This situation underscored the real-world impact of healthcare affordability issues.

### A New Vision for Healthcare

The legislation, introduced just over six weeks after the successful effort to preserve the Affordable Care Act, proposes a fundamental transformation of the American healthcare system. It aims to establish a government-administered, single-payer program. Key components of the bill include:

#### Universal Coverage and Services

*   **Universal Medicare Card:** This card would grant access to comprehensive health services. This includes hospital stays, doctor visits, substance abuse treatment, dental care, vision care, and reproductive care.

#### Managing Costs

*   **Prescription Drug Costs:** Consumers might face out-of-pocket expenses up to $250 for prescription drugs. The bill includes incentives favoring generic medications. The government would also negotiate drug prices.
*   **Elimination of Private Insurance Costs:** Premiums, deductibles, and co-pays associated with private health insurance would be eliminated. This aims to simplify healthcare finances for individuals.

#### Funding the System

*   **Funding Mechanism:** While not yet officially scored by the Congressional Budget Office, the proposed plan involves a new 2.2% income tax on all Americans. It also includes a 6.2% levy on employers. Additional tax increases would target the wealthy.

Long-term care, which was part of Sanders' presidential campaign proposal, will be addressed in separate legislation.

### Transitioning to Single-Payer

Senator Kamala Harris emphasized the business advantages of a single-payer system. She highlighted improved taxpayer returns and universal access from birth. While the immediate discussion focused broadly on the concept, the bill outlines a four-year transition period. This period is designed to phase out the current employer-sponsored private insurance system. This transition includes specific steps:

*   **Year 1:** Expanded Medicare benefits would be implemented. This includes dental, vision, and hearing aid coverage. The eligibility age would be reduced to 55. Children up to age 18 would gain immediate access.
*   **Years 2 & 3:** The Medicare eligibility age would be further reduced. It would drop to 45 in Year 2 and then to 35 in Year 3.
*   **Year 4:** Universal eligibility for all Americans would be achieved.

### Growing Support and Momentum

Public support for a national health care plan is growing. A June Kaiser Family Foundation poll indicated that 53% of Americans favor such a system. This represents an increase from previous surveys. This rising momentum is also evident among prominent Democratic officeholders. Many of these individuals are considered potential presidential candidates for 2020. Senators Harris and Warren were early cosponsors of the bill. They were joined by Cory Booker, Kirsten Gillibrand, and Jeanne Shaheen. Notable absences from the list of cosponsors included Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer, Sherrod Brown, Dianne Feinstein, and Tim Kaine.

Activists from the progressive grassroots movement that supported Sanders' 2016 campaign expressed considerable satisfaction. RoseAnn DeMoro, Executive Director of National Nurses United, stated that the bill's introduction was a "direct product of how Sen. Sanders made Medicare for all, and healthcare as a human right, such a signature issue of his campaign." This sentiment reflects the deep engagement of grassroots movements in advocating for healthcare reform.

query: What marked the significance of Alibaba's IPO on September 19, 2014, in relation to previous IPOs like Facebook's and General Motors'?

documents.text: By Rana Foroohar When the opening bell of the New York Stock Exchange rang out on Sept. 19, it marked the biggest IPO the world had ever seen — bigger than Facebook’s or General Motors’. This new Wall Street juggernaut, based 7,000 miles away in Hangzhou, China, is Alibaba. Its founder and chairman, 50-year-old Jack Ma, is now challenging some of the most powerful companies on the Internet, including Amazon, eBay and PayPal. And he is doing so by melding Western entrepreneurship with a canny—and sometimes controversial—sense of how to profit in the world’s most populous nation. As with so many Chinese enterprises, comprehending the enormity of Ma’s creation requires thinking on a different scale. Alibaba is used by more than a third of China—some 500 million people—making it easily one of the largest e-commerce companies in the world. Those customers come to Alibaba to shop in the electronic malls it operates, where some 8.5 million merchants, large and small, ply their goods. Increasingly, Chinese pay for those goods—and conduct all kinds of other transactions—using Alipay, a PayPal-like service affiliated with Alibaba that is now one of China’s most important financial entities. In fiscal year 2014, Alibaba generated $8.5 billion in revenue, nearly half of it profit. And Alibaba’s $25 billion IPO made Ma one of the world’s wealthiest individuals. (Read More: Behind the Person of the Year Images) But even those figures obscure the significance of Alibaba’s rise and the extent to which it has been propelled by Ma’s deft understanding of how to do business in his homeland. Since it began in 1999 by linking Chinese manufacturers with foreign and domestic buyers, Alibaba has played a crucial role in China’s digital opening to the West. Having previously worked in the Ministry of Trade, Ma was able to ensure support from the Communist Party government and take on bigger competitors in China’s private sector. His innovations were not just technical but also diplomatic and strategic. Like many Chinese, Ma comes from almost nothing: he is the son of traditional storytelling performers who taught himself English by tagging alongside tourists at a local hotel. Later he became a teacher and traveled to the U.S. Searching the World Wide Web for information on Chinese beer and coming up dry, he vowed to bring China online, at a time when only 1% of the population was on the web. A few years later, Ma started Alibaba in his apartment with 17 friends. His first employee was his wife, who now looks after the couple’s two children. Zhang Yin has claimed that she fell for Ma despite his unexceptional looks because he had the capacity to do things other men couldn’t. Friends have also been drawn to that ambition. “Jack had the biggest dreams of anyone I’ve ever known,�? says Porter Erisman, an American adman who left his job at Ogilvy & Mather in Beijing to work for Ma early on. “You’d come up with a goal, and he’d immediately ask you to triple it.�? A consummate spinner of corporate narrative, Ma is full of Shaolin-monk-style sound bites. “eBay is a shark in the ocean. We are a crocodile in the Yangtze,�? he famously proclaimed. “If we fight in the ocean, we will lose. But if we fight in the river, we will win.�? Ma claims to base his management technique on Chinese martial-arts teachings, encouraging employees to adopt nicknames from kung fu novels. (His own, Feng Qingyang, is a nod to an aggressive master swordsman.) Like the best martial artists, he is capable of turning weakness into strength. In the years before Alibaba went public, for instance, Ma leveraged his freedom from Wall Street pressure on quarterly earnings to keep his site free for a long time, building market share. Obliged to show shareholders progress on profit margins, eBay watched as Alibaba scooped up business.

By Nancy Gibbs Not the glittering weapon fights the fight, says the proverb, but rather the hero’s heart. Maybe this is true in any battle; it is surely true of a war that is waged with bleach and a prayer. For decades, Ebola haunted rural African villages like some mythic monster that every few years rose to demand a human sacrifice and then returned to its cave. It reached the West only in nightmare form, a Hollywood horror that makes eyes bleed and organs dissolve and doctors despair because they have no cure. But 2014 is the year an outbreak turned into an epidemic, powered by the very progress that has paved roads and raised cities and lifted millions out of poverty. This time it reached crowded slums in Liberia, Guinea and Sierra Leone; it traveled to Nigeria and Mali, to Spain, Germany and the U.S. It struck doctors and nurses in unprecedented numbers, wiping out a public-health infrastructure that was weak in the first place. One August day in Liberia, six pregnant women lost their babies when hospitals couldn’t admit them for complications. Anyone willing to treat Ebola victims ran the risk of becoming one. Which brings us to the hero’s heart. There was little to stop the disease from spreading further. Governments weren’t equipped to respond; the World Health Organization was in denial and snarled in red tape. First responders were accused of crying wolf, even as the danger grew. But the people in the field, the special forces of Doctors Without Borders/Médecins Sans Frontières (MSF), the Christian medical-relief workers of Samaritan’s Purse and many others from all over the world fought side by side with local doctors and nurses, ambulance drivers and burial teams. Ask what drove them and some talk about God; some about country; some about the instinct to run into the fire, not away. “If someone from America comes to help my people, and someone from Uganda,” says Iris Martor, a Liberian nurse, “then why can’t I?” Foday Gallah, an ambulance driver who survived infection, calls his immunity a holy gift. “I want to give my blood so a lot of people can be saved,” he says. “I am going to fight Ebola with all of my might.” MSF nurse’s assistant Salome Karwah stayed at the bedsides of patients, bathing and feeding them, even after losing both her parents—who ran a medical clinic—in a single week and surviving Ebola herself. “It looked like God gave me a second chance to help others,” she says. Tiny children watched their families die, and no one could so much as hug them, because hugs could kill. “You see people facing death without their loved ones, only with people in space suits,” says MSF president Dr. Joanne Liu. “You should not die alone with space-suit men.” Those who contracted the disease encountered pain like they had never known. “It hurts like they are busting your head with an ax,” Karwah says. One doctor overheard his funeral being planned. Asked if surviving Ebola changed him, Dr. Kent Brantly turns the question around. “I still have the same flaws that I did before,” he says. “But whenever we go through a devastating experience like what I’ve been through, it is an incredible opportunity for redemption of something. We can say, How can I be better now because of what I’ve been through? To not do that is kind of a shame.” So that is the next challenge: What will we do with what we’ve learned? This was a test of the world’s ability to respond to potential pandemics, and it did not go well. It exposed corruption in African governments along with complacency in Western capitals and jealousy among competing bureaucrats. It triggered mistrust from Monrovia to Manhattan. Each week brought new puzzles. How do you secure a country, beyond taking passengers’ temperatures at the airport? Who has the power to order citizens to stay home, to post a guard outside their door? What will it take to develop treatments for diseases largely confined to poor nations, even as this Ebola outbreak had taken far more lives by mid-October than all the earlier ones combined? The death in Dallas of Thomas Eric Duncan, the first Ebola patient diagnosed on U.S. soil, and the infection of two nurses who treated him, shook our faith in the ability of U.S. hospitals to handle this kind of disease. From there the road to full freak-out was a short one. An Ohio middle school closed because an employee had flown on the same plane as one of Duncan’s nurses. Not the same flight, just the same plane. A Texas college rejected applicants from Nigeria, since that country had some “confirmed Ebola cases.” A Maine schoolteacher had to take a three-week leave because she went to a teachers’ conference in Dallas. Fear, too, was global. When a nurse in Spain contracted Ebola from a priest, Spanish authorities killed her dog as a precaution, while #VamosAMorirTodos (We’re all going to die) trended on Twitter. Guests at a hotel in Macedonia were trapped in their rooms for days after a British guest got sick and died. Turned out to have nothing to do with Ebola. The problem with irrational responses is that they can cloud the need for rational ones. Just when the world needed more medical volunteers, the price of serving soared. When nurse Kaci Hickox, returning from a stint with MSF in Sierra Leone with no symptoms and a negative blood test, was quarantined in a tent in Newark, N.J., by a combustible governor, it forced a reckoning. “It is crazy we are spending so much time having this debate about how to safely monitor people coming back from Ebola-endemic countries,” says Hickox, “when the one thing we can do to protect the population is to stop the outbreak in West Africa.” Ebola is a war, and a warning. The global health system is nowhere close to strong enough to keep us safe from infectious disease, and “us” means everyone, not just those in faraway places where this is one threat among many that claim lives every day. The rest of the world can sleep at night because a group of men and women are willing to stand and fight. For tireless acts of courage and mercy, for buying the world time to boost its defenses, for risking, for persisting, for sacrificing and saving, the Ebola fighters are TIME’s 2014 Person of the Year. Read more: TIME’s 2014 Person of the Year: The Ebola Fighters Runner-Up: Ferguson Protesters, The Activists Runner-Up: Vladimir Putin, The Imperialist Runner-Up: Massoud Barzani, The Opportunist Runner-Up: Jack Ma, The Capitalist

**Healthcare Heroes on the Ebola Frontlines: Courage and Resilience in the Face of Crisis**

The Ebola epidemic in West Africa presented unprecedented challenges, not only to the affected communities but also to the dedicated healthcare professionals who risked their lives on the front lines. This document shares the profound experiences of three nurses—Kaci Hickox, Iris Martor, and Nina Pham—offering a firsthand perspective on the realities of the outbreak, the complexities of patient care, and the personal toll of confronting such a devastating disease. Their narratives highlight the critical need for informed responses, compassionate care, and global solidarity in the face of public health emergencies.

Kaci Hickox, a nurse with Doctors Without Borders (MSF), found herself at the epicenter of the Ebola crisis in Sierra Leone, serving as the Medical Team Lead at an Ebola Case Management Center in Bo. Her account underscores the arduous nature of working in Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) under extreme heat. The full-body suits, while essential for protection, were cumbersome and non-breathable, hindering deep personal care and communication. Hickox emphasized that Ebola treatment in West Africa primarily involved maintaining patient hygiene, encouraging hydration, and ensuring adequate nutrition. She recounted heartbreaking moments, like informing a friend of his positive diagnosis, but also celebrated patient recoveries. Upon returning to the U.S., Hickox was subjected to monitoring and quarantine despite lacking symptoms. She expressed surprise at the officials' fear and disorganization, and after a suspected false positive temperature reading, she was transferred to a hospital tent. After testing negative, she was released to home quarantine, a decision later overturned by a judge. Hickox hoped compassion and knowledge would triumph over fear, allowing for Ebola's eradication, and desired to return to West Africa when circumstances permit.

Iris Martor, a nurse at the More Than Me Academy in Monrovia, Liberia, shared her experiences, illuminating the initial skepticism surrounding Ebola in Liberia and the subsequent shift in public perception. Initial reports were often met with disbelief, but the outbreak at Redemption Hospital, where healthcare workers succumbed to the virus, prompted widespread fear and facility closures. The rainy season, typically marked by an increase in malaria and diarrhea cases, compounded the crisis. With most hospitals closed, Ebola Treatment Units (ETUs) became overwhelmed. Recognizing that people were dying from treatable illnesses due to lack of accessible healthcare, Martor and her team initiated home-based care, assessing patients and coordinating transfers to ETUs if Ebola symptoms were suspected. The inherent danger stemmed from the unknown environments within homes. As a school nurse, Martor extended her care to her students and their families in the West Point slum, providing education and resources. She acknowledged her initial fear but drew strength from her professional oath and a sense of responsibility. Martor cautioned against premature celebration, emphasizing that Ebola had not been eradicated and that complacency could lead to resurgence, stressing the ongoing risk posed by the virus's presence in neighboring Guinea and Sierra Leone. Her strong recommendation for policymakers was to establish robust contingency plans, drawing lessons from the Ebola crisis to prepare for future health emergencies.

Nina Pham, a nurse at Texas Health Presbyterian Hospital, recounted her experience detailing her direct involvement with the first Ebola patient diagnosed in the United States, Thomas Eric Duncan. Pham learned about Ebola through media coverage and, despite her fear, accepted the assignment to care for Mr. Duncan, viewing nursing as a calling. She identified the initial days of Mr. Duncan's care as the period of highest risk, meticulously following all recommended protocols regarding PPE. Following her care for Mr. Duncan, Pham was interviewed by the CDC, who initially deemed her to be at no risk. Therefore, the arrival of her Chief Nursing Officer and a CDC officer, both in full protective gear, was a profound shock. Pham admitted to initial denial and overwhelming flashbacks of Mr. Duncan's deteriorating condition. She expressed a hope that Americans would recognize the ongoing severity of the Ebola crisis in West Africa and understand the importance of global cooperation, underscoring that the solution lies not in isolationism but in mutual support and assistance.

The experiences of Kaci Hickox, Iris Martor, and Nina Pham offer invaluable insights into the human dimension of the Ebola epidemic. Their courage, dedication, and resilience in the face of immense adversity serve as a powerful reminder of the critical role healthcare professionals play and the enduring need for global preparedness and compassion.

“To have an African doctor, who grew up in a shantytown in probably one of the most disadvantaged countries in the world, on the cover of TIME magazine’s Person of the Year is the right thing to do,” says Jackie Nickerson, the fine-art photographer who shot the cover of TIME’s Person of the Year. This year, TIME chose to highlight the incredible work Ebola fighters are doing to bring to a stop an epidemic that has killed more than 6,000 people. TIME commissioned Nickerson and U.S. photographer Bryan Schutmaat to shoot more than 20 portraits in 12 locations around the world—from London to Geneva, Boston to Dallas, and all the way to Monrovia, Liberia. “I was working on a job in Paris when Kira Pollack and Paul Moakley [the director and deputy director of photography at TIME] called me,” says Nickerson. “They told me they had this assignment for me and asked if I could go to Monrovia. I immediately knew this was an once-in-a-lifetime opportunity to bear witness to this moment in history. There was no doubt in my mind that it was something I wanted to do.” Nickerson was surprised. “I had actually never worked for TIME before,” she says. “I had no idea what the commission process was, so this call came out of the blue.” Meanwhile, Schutmaat was in Amsterdam attending World Press Photo’s Joop Swart Masterclass when he received his phone call. “My schedule was very busy, and I thought [being in Europe wouldn’t work], but photo editor Natalie Matutschovsky, who’s been championing my work at TIME, just said: ‘That’s good, because two of the subjects we need you to shoot are in Europe already.’” The following week, he was on a flight to Geneva. Both photographers were selected for their strong sense of aesthetics, which come from their fine-art backgrounds. “They are celebrated artists,” says Pollack. “There is a heartbeat to Nickerson's portraits that lent itself to just the right mood for this project. She’s spent a considerable amount of time working throughout Africa. She is agile and informed on how to photograph in the harsh African light, and her portraits are honest and beautiful. Schutmaat's studied portraits have an almost painterly quality. There is something very telling about capturing a body posture or a simple gesture.” For weeks, TIME had been preparing for Nickerson’s assignment. “We talked to NGO workers, journalists and photographers who had been in the field before we decided to go ahead do this,” says Moakley. “We talked with the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, and we packed everything we needed—including Personal Protective Equipment suits for all of us.” TIME’s Africa Bureau Chief Aryn Baker, who spent the summer covering the Ebola epidemic, met Nickerson and Moakley in Monrovia. “Aryn did a lot of the work ahead of time as well,” says Nickerson. “With Paul, she had selected the sitters, so we had a very good idea of who we were going to shoot, so that made things incredibly easy for me.” Few people would know Dr. Jerry Brown’s name. The Liberian doctor, who is featured on the first of five covers shot for TIME’s Person of the Year issue, opened his country’s first Ebola treatment center in early 2014, at a time when many of his colleagues failed to react to the growing epidemic. “There was a kind of gravity to the way Dr. Brown and his staff were working,” says Nickerson. “When we met [him], we had the idea to do something very simple against a plain color, something of a more formal portrait. And then, he invited us to go into the reception area where he gets dressed. It was a very simple, bare room. It had a single light bulb, and I just thought it captured the atmosphere and gravity of what they were doing.” The photograph is not a staged shot: It’s a portrait that was caught in the middle of Brown’s regular dressing procedure. Over four days, Nickerson, Moakley and Baker witnessed the commitment of dozens of health workers and body-retrieval teams. “Sometimes, we would be waiting to get access to someone and we’d be chatting to other people with incredible stories,” says the photographer. “It just never stopped. Their stories really touch you—the self-sacrifices that people are making. They are doing such a brilliant job.” In the U.S., meanwhile, Schutmaat was meeting with Dr. Kent Brantly. The 33-year-old physician with Samaritan’s Purse was the first American citizen to be diagnosed with Ebola while working in Monrovia. “Kent was doing a lot of hard, selfless work to help people out,” says Schutmaat.“I met him at his church in Fort Worth, Texas. TIME’s photo editors and I felt that since he was a man of faith and since he was guided by that faith, it would be good to photograph him in there.” Schutmaat had no idea then that it would be featured as part of the magazine’s Person of the Year franchise. “I just thought I was doing a big story on Ebola that would end up somewhere inside the magazine,” he says. “I didn’t think it would be such a huge deal.” Nickerson was similarly in the dark: “Kira Pollack had said it could be an important story, and I knew that Person of the Year happened around this time of the year, but I didn’t dare to hope because I think there had always been people of status on the cover, and I couldn’t believe it was going to be a non-famous, African doctor on the cover.” It was only when TIME’s photo editors ordered the final, high-resolution images that both photographers found out the real purpose of their work. “It’s probably the biggest privilege of my professional career,” says Nickerson. “There’s no question about it. Doing this whole story was a privilege.” “I’m honored,” adds Schutmaat. “And to know that the editors at TIME would think that my photos would stand up next to Nickerson’s is pretty awesome.” A sentiment Nickerson reciprocates. “Bryan’s a great photographer. I love his work. I’m really happy to be sharing this story with him.” Jackie Nickerson is a fine-art photographer based in Dublin, Ireland. She is represented by Jack Shainman Gallery . TIME last featured her work, Terrain , on LightBox . Bryan Schutmaat is a Austin-based photographer. He is represented by Sasha Wolf Gallery . Olivier Laurent is the editor of TIME LightBox. Follow him on Twitter and Instagram @olivierclaurent. Paul Moakley , TIME's deputy director of photography, and Natalie Matutschovsky , a senior photo editor at TIME, edited this photo essay.

Massoud Barzani, a figure synonymous with Kurdish resilience, embodies the enduring struggle of the world's largest stateless ethnic group. His life mirrors the complex journey of the Kurds, a people whose quest for self-determination has spanned centuries, marked by both profound aspiration and persistent challenge. Barzani's distinctive traditional attire, often a Kurdish jumpsuit cinched below the chest and topped with a turban, became a recognizable symbol, even during his 2005 White House visit. This sartorial connection extends to his father, Mustafa Barzani, a revolutionary leader and the most prominent Kurd since the 12th-century general Saladin. Mustafa dedicated his life to establishing a Kurdish state, achieving a brief but significant independence in 1946, the year Massoud was born, only for this nascent nation to be reabsorbed by Iran, reigniting the ongoing fight for Kurdish self-determination.

Despite the historical absence of a unified Kurdish state, a de facto nation has emerged in northern Iraq. The Kurdistan Regional Government (KRG) presides over a territory transitioning from desert to stony foothills and mountains, boasting its own army, legislature, border control, and foreign policy, all under the leadership of President Massoud Barzani. His tenure has coincided with a pivotal moment for the Kurds, suggesting their long-awaited era of autonomy may finally be dawning. The concept of "Kurdistan" exists vividly in the hearts and minds of its people, represented by maps and the symbolic yellow, red, and green flags. It is a poignant observation that the most dependable ally in the global campaign against ISIS is, in essence, an imaginary country.

As ISIS advanced in June 2014, threatening Kurdish territories, the approximately 22 million Kurds across the region faced a critical juncture. Iraqi Kurdistan, in particular, had achieved unprecedented strength, boasting a functional military and a burgeoning economy. Though predominantly Sunni Muslims, the Kurds adhere to a tolerant tradition that stands in stark contrast to the fundamentalist ideology of ISIS. Initially, the Kurdish peshmerga forces fought valiantly against the well-equipped extremists. It was not until ISIS artillery neared the KRG capital of Erbil that Barzani issued a resolute command to "fight the terrorists to the last breath." By this time, U.S. air support was imminent, commencing the following day and preceding President Obama's formal strategy announcement to defeat ISIS.

The stakes for Barzani, the leader of a stateless people, were immense. He found himself in a position to influence the future of Iraq and, potentially, the broader Middle East. His life has been a continuous navigation of regional fault lines. He left formal education at 16 to join the fight, yet possesses fluency in Arabic and Farsi alongside Kurdish, and understands English. While his traditional attire reflects a deep connection to Kurdish heritage, some reformers view it as indicative of a more feudal approach to leadership, especially given the KRG's internal structure with a nephew as Prime Minister and his son as intelligence chief. Ayub Nuri, editor of the Kurdish news site Rudaw, notes that while Barzani faces criticism for his tribal affiliations, this very characteristic is crucial for maintaining unity, explaining, "The tribal man knows how to keep things together."

The Kurds in Iraq have enjoyed a significant degree of autonomy since 1991, protected by a U.S.-imposed no-fly zone. The post-2003 invasion saw the KRG grow in strength as Baghdad weakened. The 2014 crisis marked a new threshold for Iraqi Kurds. When Iraqi federal forces retreated from ISIS, Barzani directed the peshmerga to secure Kirkuk, a strategically vital, oil-rich city long claimed by Kurds but also by Sunni Arabs. This move not only protected Kirkuk from ISIS but also presented Baghdad with the challenge of reclaiming it. Under the new Iraqi Prime Minister Haider al-Abadi, and with U.S. air support, Baghdad has begun to rally against ISIS. However, the Kurds remain at the forefront of the fight against the jihadists, a role that aligns with their distinct aspirations. While ISIS leader Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi seeks to establish a caliphate that transcends national borders, Barzani and his people ardently desire their own nation-state.

Two days after al-Baghdadi declared himself Caliph Ibrahim, Barzani announced that Iraqi Kurds would hold a referendum on independence within months, stating, "From now on, we won’t hide that that’s our goal." The potential emergence of a new Kurdish state, however, carries the risk of destabilizing the Middle East. For the immediate future, the Kurds appear poised to leverage their current position to enhance their standing within a unified Iraq, deferring the ultimate push for full independence. A significant development occurred on December 2, when Barzani approved a pact with the new Iraqi government, strengthening ties with Baghdad and resolving a dispute over oil exports. This agreement, strongly supported by the U.S., highlights both the constraints on the KRG and Barzani's adeptness at navigating complex political landscapes.

The Kurdish leadership's history is as challenging as their geography, with their population dispersed across four nations. Despite past betrayals by the international community, they now count the U.S., Israel, and Iran among their allies. Barzani has even cultivated a relationship with Turkey, home to a substantial Kurdish population, a country that has historically viewed Kurdish aspirations as a threat. However, Turkey has increasingly recognized Iraqi Kurdistan as a valuable market, with annual trade reaching approximately $8 billion. Barzani solidified this alliance in September 2012 by addressing Turkey's ruling party convention, notably in a suit and tie. Two years later, he extended support to Syrian Kurds defending Kobani, a border town that became a focal point of the U.S.-led anti-ISIS campaign. Nuri reflects on the deep respect for Barzani, stating, "When I was a kid, it was forbidden in our house to say anything critical of his father. It’s not a blind love. We need a leader." Massoud Barzani inherited his leadership role by birthright, and through his strategic partnership with the U.S., he may well fulfill a pivotal role extending beyond his own people.

The death of Michael Brown in Ferguson, Missouri, on August 9th, 2014, ignited a national movement for racial justice, transforming isolated incidents of police brutality into a powerful, widespread demand for accountability. Johnetta Elzie, a prominent voice who joined the protests to honor Brown, witnessed firsthand how the event escalated from a somber tribute to widespread demonstrations met with a forceful police response, sparking a crucial national conversation about systemic racism and the pursuit of justice. As Elzie stated, "We made the world pay attention," a significant achievement in itself, bringing to light other incidents that might have otherwise gone unnoticed, such as the fatal shooting of 12-year-old Tamir Rice in Cleveland and the grand jury's decision not to indict the officer who choked Eric Garner to death in New York City. Erica Garner, Eric Garner's daughter, articulated the broader impact, declaring, "This is not a black-and-white issue. This is a national crisis." The chants and hashtags originating in Ferguson wove these disparate incidents into an undeniable narrative of injustice, demonstrating protest as a powerful tool for illumination and change.

In this era of protest, thousands found their voice and their role, disrupting daily life and demanding attention through various means, from clogging freeways and carrying symbolic coffins to staging "die-ins" on university campuses and raising fists in solidarity, a gesture echoed by athletes and politicians alike. Even President Barack Obama was compelled to engage, meeting with protest leaders and announcing initiatives for police accountability, while Republican Senator Rand Paul criticized disproportionate incarceration rates and advocated for criminal justice reform. New York City Mayor Bill de Blasio emphasized, "People need to know that black lives and brown lives matter as much as white lives." This outcry, more focused and impactful than previous movements, felt profoundly authentic, with expressions of powerlessness fueling the hopes of those in power. Attorney General Eric Holder told TIME, "What happened in Ferguson could be one of those seminal moments that transform the nation." Ferguson's underlying tensions, marked by racial division and a history of racial profiling, were exacerbated by the police response, which St. Louis alderman Antonio French noted, "Every opportunity in those first 48 hours to be compassionate, they escalated the situation instead." While some, like Dhoruba Shakur, viewed the looting as a justified response to government failure, the ambiguities surrounding Michael Brown's death did little to quell the sense of injustice, as the fact that an unarmed Black man was killed by a white officer remained undeniable. Despite the challenging and dangerous profession of police officers, the demonstrations polarized the nation, sparking debates about blame. Once the immediate chaos subsided, activists like Johnetta Elzie, along with leaders such as DeRay Mckesson, sustained the movement through crowdfunding, community meetings, and social media, with Mckesson stating, "We will not be silent anymore in the face of oppression. Silence can so easily look like comfort."

Dr. Kent Brantly, 33 Physician with Samaritan’s Purse Bryan Schutmaat for TIME I’ve thought a lot about the moment when I was infected with Ebola. I will never know for certain when it happened, but I do remember one overnight shift, about nine days before I got sick, a woman came into the ER with her daughter. When I went to check on her, she was very sick. At one point, the daughter took her mother into the bathroom because she had diarrhea. We needed to get her out of the ER and into our Ebola treatment unit, but her daughter was incredibly distrustful of the situation and of us. I had to counsel her extensively to reassure her that we were trying to do what was best for her mother. To have that conversation, I took off my mask, gloves and apron. I probably held her hands or put my arm around her shoulder, as I often do. I don’t think I was infected by her mother, but since the daughter had taken her to the bathroom, there’s a chance she didn’t wash her hands after helping her mom. The mother died by the morning, and a postmortem test showed she did indeed have Ebola. A little more than a week later, I woke up feeling a little warm. My temperature was 100.0—higher than normal but not too concerning. I took a rapid malaria test; it was negative—not a good sign. I called our team leader, who sent physician colleagues to my home in full protective gear. After two more negative malaria tests, I knew I would be in isolation for at least three more days. In the meantime, I grew sicker. My fever hit 104.9. We all hoped it could be dengue fever. On the fourth day the team leader came to my bedroom window with news. “Kent, buddy, we have your results. It’s positive for Ebola.” I didn’t know what to think. I just asked, “So what’s our plan?” This was July, and I’d been in Monrovia since October 2013. The first time I heard about the Ebola outbreak was at the end of March 2014, at a picnic for expatriates living in the area. Someone asked if I had heard about the outbreak in Guinea. I had not, but within a couple of months I was one of only two doctors in Monrovia treating Ebola patients—and at that point we had only one survivor. My wife Amber and I were both at the disadvantage of knowing how this illness can end. But even with the bad news, I felt strangely at peace. God blessed me with a peace that surpasses understanding. At some point, I was told about an experimental drug called ZMapp. It had worked on monkeys but had never been tested in humans. I agreed to receive it but decided that Nancy Writebol, a medical aide I worked with, should get it first, since she was sicker. I wasn’t trying to be a hero; I was making a rational decision as a doctor. Over the next couple of days, though, my condition worsened. The doctor decided to give me the drug too, and within an hour my body stabilized a bit. It was enough improvement for me to be safely evacuated to Emory University Hospital in Atlanta. Shortly after I arrived at Emory, Amber called me from a phone outside my room. I don’t really remember that conversation, I was so delirious, but she said to me, “We watched you walk off that ambulance.” I said, “You were watching me?” and she said, “Oh, Kent. The whole world was watching you.” I’ve had time to reflect on what happened to me. Am I the same person I was before Ebola? In a lot of ways, yes. I don’t live every moment with a conscious awareness of what I’ve been through. I still have the same flaws I did before. But I think whenever we go through a devastating experience, it’s not about there being some inherent redemptive narrative, but it is an incredible opportunity for the redemption of something. We can say, “How can I be better now because of what I’ve been through?” To not do that is kind of a shame. When I thank God for saving my life, I am not unique in that. If you watch videos of survivors in Liberia, so many of them thank God for saving their lives. I chose a career in medicine because I wanted a tangible skill with which to serve people. And so my role as a physician is my attempt to do that. I’ll probably get tired of talking about my experience some day, but I went to Liberia because I long felt it was my vocation to spend my career as medical missionary. Deep in the core of my heart, I still think that’s my calling. I don’t want to go on with life and forget this.—as told to Alexandra Sifferlin Dr. Jerry Brown, 46 Medical director and general surgeon at the Eternal Love Winning Africa [ELWA] Hospital in Monrovia and director of the ELWA 2 Ebola treatment center Jackie Nickerson for TIME I first heard about Ebola in March, when I was listening to the radio. That was late March. We had patients with Ebola presenting in Lofa County. In the counties that border with Guinea. So it was then that we really started panicking and thinking, Now that one of our counties is involved, what next? Sooner or later it might reach us, we thought. So from then on we started thinking about Ebola. The very first challenge I had as medical director at that time was that if we started having Ebola patients show up in our emergency room, well, “Where do we keep them, doctor?” The only place that came through my mind was our chapel, because we didn’t have the financial capability to construct something. Our intent was not to create a treatment unit but instead to transform the chapel into a holding unit such that whenever we had a patient in the emergency room and we had the suspicion of the person being a probable or suspected case, the person could be kept in the holding unit until the patient would be transported or transferred to the treatment unit. So that was our goal for having the chapel used initially. It was June 12, in the late evening after surgery. I had just left the OR when I saw a series of missed calls on the phone [from the Deputy Minister of Health] and decided to call her back. She said, “There are two patients that we have, they are Sierra Leoneans, and they visited one of the hospitals already, Redemption Hospital. A couple of their staff have already encountered them. But their presentation, their signs and symptoms, and from where they have traveled, tell me that they are probable cases and they most likely have Ebola. And the only place I thought about that we could keep them until we can have an investigation done is at your center.” The very first thing I felt was, this is going to be challenging to have those patients brought here. We had not attended to such patients before, so in the back of my mind I kept thinking, What would it be like, to have those patients here? From that day onward, I always had the fear of myself or one of my staff getting infected, and what would become of me thereafter. The first two patients were brought in an ambulance, with two of their relatives, in the back of the ambulance, along with the patients. The front of the ambulance had the driver and two health workers; one nurse and one physician assistant. What was shocking was that the nurse and the physician assistant never had any personal protective equipment. The only thing they had was gloves, and dressed in their ordinary scrubs. That was frightening. The two patients in the back of the ambulance, one had died on the way. And the other was lying there. And then a boy about the age of 13 and his brother, maybe around his late 20s, were both in the back of the ambulance. We could tell that they were Sierra Leoneans by their accents and the way they spoke. We were shocked. First question was why the health workers allowed the patients’ caregiver to ride in the ambulance with them. It all boils down to people not knowing what a disease Ebola actually was. So no one knew what was happening. And I tell you, if we had not done some training initially, and spoke about Ebola repeatedly during our regular devotions, it would have been a greater disaster in the ELWA hospital. Through the training a few persons had some knowledge of what Ebola was all about: the mode of transportation, the means of prevention and the signs and symptoms related to the disease. So the bulk of the workers at this institution knew a lot about it. So it was a pity to see those workers in the ambulance dressed as they were. One of them is said to have died of the disease later, the nurse. So initially, that is the situation we had. Dr. Kent Brantly and Dr. Debbie Eisenhut were the first two who were willing to dress in full PPE and took the patient down to the unit. It was very challenging to get the nurses on board the first day, though they had been trained. The patient died a few days later. Before the end of June, the chapel, where we only had six beds, could no longer take additional patients because it was full to capacity. The initial intent of the unit in the chapel soon changed from a holding unit to a treatment unit. The chapel then became the first treatment unit in Monrovia. So all the patients that were presenting with the symptoms [of Ebola] were all rushed here, to the chapel. [They] were dying every three, four, five days. Because of the rapid rate at which people were dying, you could easily find places to put someone else. By then the government in the middle of July created another ETU at JFK Hospital that became the second unit in Monserrado County, in Monrovia. When the chapel became full and we could no longer take additional patients, and more people were getting ill and dying, the ELWA management decided to turn over our newly built kitchen and laundry for the new hospital under construction to be transformed into a treatment unit. Thus ELWA 2. Within a week it was filled. We had 20 beds. Within one week we had more than 20 patients. Patients were now in the corridors and under the eaves of the building, and still patients kept pouring in daily. The outpatient department of the hospital was then turned over to be used as an extension of the unit, shutting down outpatient services to the public. When it all started, and we had a unit set up, my wife did warn me not to enter the unit. So the first time I did enter I didn’t tell her I had entered. But she knew I had been trained. I went home and said nothing to her. And the very first time she had a suspicion of me going to the unit was when Dr. Abraham Borbor, who was one of my mentors, got ill. And I had to go in and attend to him. I had been in the unit for two weeks without her knowing. And I went home one evening and started discussing a few things about Dr. Borbor. Borbor was my role model, especially when I was in medical school. How he was now ill, and how I was feeling so sorry for him, and how his condition was not improving despite what we were doing. And she paused, and asked, “You are not working in the unit. How do you know these things?” and I caught myself. And tried to change the topic. It went to something else, and she never noticed. But then she started having suspicions. I remember her saying, “I hope you are not being stubborn. I told you not to go to the ETU.” So I said, “Well, I will do my best.” I formed some excuse and we just abandoned the topic. A few days later she noticed changes in the color of my boxers from the bleach or chlorine solution used for disinfection when leaving the unit. And so she said, “Ah, what is this?” and so I had no option but to finally confess. It did not go down well with her. I apologized, and we kind of talked about it for some time, and then she accepted. What she said was, “I can’t stop you. I realize this is something you like, so I am not going to stop you. But just be careful. So when you are back home, change all your clothes in the garage before you enter the house. I do not want my children and I to get infected.” But before she said this, I never took any of my outfits back home. I had special scrubs that I kept at the hospital. Once I got to the hospital I changed completely into my scrubs. At the end of each work day, I changed back to my ordinary wear and went back home. It was challenging to have her to accept this. She was afraid of me getting infected. She later realized that I was safe, and we kept trusting God to remain safe prayerfully. —as told to Aryn Baker Dr. Mosoka Fallah, 44 An American-educated Liberian infectious-disease expert who returned to his country last year to help establish a school of public health and now leads the effort to find, monitor and isolate the contacts of Ebola victims Jackie Nickerson for TIME As an infectious-disease student [at Harvard] I studied emerging and re-emerging infectious disease, and Ebola was always what we were talking about. At the time I had come back to Liberia to work on a USAID project. It was March. Ebola was the only thing on my mind. It was already in Guinea. I understood the interaction between the people on the border, between Guinea and Liberia. And I knew that there was definitely going to be Ebola coming to Liberia. It was one thing reading about a disease and then being in a disease. Maybe I could have raised the alarm, but I didn’t. I regret that now. I regret that we didn’t stop it in Guinea. Then the cases came to Liberia from Guinea. It was March, the 24th or 25th. I called [Assistant Health Minister] Mr. Tolbert Nyenswah: “I’m hearing about this Ebola, how can I help?” Everyone was coming in and thinking about how to mount an effective response. It was a task force. It became much more personal for me, because one of the relatives of the [first person to be killed in the outbreak] in Lofa County had taken a taxi to Monrovia. And she slept in the Chicken Soup Factory [neighborhood]. I grew up there. My mom is there, all of my relatives are there. I said, “We’ve got to get our boots on the ground. We’ve got to go to Chicken Soup Factory.” And they said to me, “This is the task force. We construct policies and strategy.” And I said, “O.K., I’m going to go down to the county [level] where we can discuss boots on the ground.” I led a team to Chicken Soup Factory. The index case had already left. But there were contacts like the taxi driver. I talked to him. He was frightened. He said, “I tried to help this old lady. If I do have the disease, I am going to spread it.” We told the townspeople to keep an eye on him. [We said], “Don’t stigmatize him. Don’t let him on the street.” Our contacts officer would go and take his temperature every day. And after 21 days, he did not come down with the disease. That case had me running around recruiting and training people. And passing awareness. Then [in] April we saw the situation slow down, the number of cases drop. By the end of April, we weren’t getting any more cases. But I was a little bit worried. Given the fluid nature of our borders and the nature of Ebola, I was worried. I remember sending an email to a friend. “Very soon it will be in Liberia again,” I said, thinking of the increasing cases in Sierra Leone. Because when it hit Sierra Leone, it hit with so much force and ferocity. The cases mounted at an alarming rate. I didn’t know that was prophetic when I wrote that line to my friend. On June 27 we had a new case. I got a call from the Ministry of Health. And it was in New Kru Town [one of Monrovia’s most crowded slums]. The population density and the denial allowed the disease to escalate. A landlady we approached [to do contact tracing] said, “If anyone says they have Ebola in this house, I will give you a slap.” I just walked out. There was so much resistance, so much denial. As we tracked case by case, we got to know that the first index patient who died had gone to Redemption Hospital. He had come into contact with all the nurses. So we had to consider every nurse and doctor at Redemption a possible contact. Actually, I followed about 45 people that were listed as contacts. This nurse, Esther, had touched him, and she became infected. And Dr. Sam [Samuel Muhumuza Mutooro] from Uganda—he treated him, and he became infected. And then the man who took the index patient in the back of his car died, and the sister died. What we knew was only the tip of the iceberg. Even before the ambulance had gone [to take the first case for treatment], six people had died. There is a communal bathroom that all the houses use. It just blew our minds. All of a sudden we have this situation in New Kru Town, of all places. We were worried that very soon it would spread to West Point [another congested slum]. I was working to mobilize tracers, to train tracers. And there were contacts that we could not find. Contacts were becoming symptomatic and moving and exposing other contacts. Twenty-six people got infected by one person. And then the cases hit us so hard. We had contacts everywhere becoming symptomatic and generating more contacts. That’s when the tension was on. Seven days a week, 10 hours a day. And we were getting from 50 to 200 phone calls. The phone calls would come until midnight and start at 5 a.m. I couldn’t afford to turn the phone off. All through Ebola, I think I turned it off once or twice, and only when a friend said to me, “Go and rest. You look like a dead body right now. We don’t want you around reminding us of death.” And I slept for two days. Very soon I realized that the response is not a single piece. It has to be holistic. There has to be enough ambulances. There has to be enough case investigation teams. There has to be enough burial teams. The sick will be waiting for someone to die [in the Ebola treatment centers] so we can have space. Everything we did on Ebola was dependent on the ETUs [Ebola treatment units]. And we didn’t have enough. August and July were quite tough for us. People would die, and we were helpless. We just couldn’t do anything. I would have a contact tracer follow a family. The mother died. The sister died. The maid died. The wife died. The father died. And she [the contact tracer] would go there every day to do contact tracing on the symptomatic people. She would encourage them, but every time she came back, there would be one more body. We were left alone. I have to say that. I always say that in July and August we lost a good window of opportunity. If they had come in with the ambulances and the tents, we could have averted a lot of death. If everyone had rushed in and built another [treatment unit]. They took such a long time to be built. Meanwhile, people were dying. The bodies would accumulate in the street. The burial teams would go someplace for burial and they would be chased out. I was always afraid that Ebola would be in West Point. The unsanitary conditions. The population density. The fluid nature of the population. It was Aug. 12 that I got a call. [A volunteer] called me and said, “Mosoka, you better come to West Point.” He said, “There is one dead in the house with the door locked. And the other is sick and vomiting and toileting in the street.” I left everything and I ran to West Point. Nobody wanted to talk, nobody wanted to explain what was happening. I called a couple of leaders [from West Point], and they began to reveal to us that for the past two weeks there had been massive secret burial going on. They would take [the bodies] to an island across the bay and they would bury them. There was no investigation or contact tracing. That night I called the WHO Country Representative. I said, “Our worst nightmare has happened. West Point has Ebola.” The decision to open a [Ebola] holding center in West Point was a desperate decision. West Point is congested. There is no way you can walk in West Point without touching. You have the sick walking around, vomiting and toileting. I said, “The best we can do for West Point is to get the sick from the population.” The community didn’t understand. And then it was Aug. 19, the worst day of my life. It was a rainy day. That day alone we took six dead bodies from West Point. I think there was a lot of misinformation. There was still a lot of denial. And then [rioters] broke into the West Point [Ebola] center. They took mattresses. Where were those contacts? Where did they take the mattresses? There was a very big panic. The government had to deal with people running around with infected mattresses. They also knew that they had the secret burials. So the government quarantined West Point. Later, the government lifted the quarantine. The lesson we have to learn is that the government, before taking action, should consult with the community. I’m cautious about the declining number of cases because with Ebola, a single case is an epidemic. Before we were fighting a big war. Now it’s a guerrilla war and we’re fighting little pockets. If it comes back, it’s going to be even more intense. It only takes one case, and then all our achievements are reversed. On Tuesday, Nov. 11, we got a call that a prayer leader had died from Ebola. Thursday morning, they brought a casket to carry the body. There were people there rubbing oil on her and praying for her. Forty people. Children as young as 3, 4. All of them under a tent. So as I speak to you, three of the people that were in that room are in the [treatment unit] today. This is in New Kru Town, where it all started. That’s what I am trying to say. It came back. —as told to Aryn Baker Dr. Philip Ireland, 44 Liberian doctor at John F. Kennedy Medical Center in Monrovia, Liberia’s largest hospital Jackie Nickerson for TIME We had several cases of clinicians at the John F. Kennedy hospital who have come down with Ebola. Dr. [Samuel] Brisbane [and] physician’s assistant Stephen Vincent. And then I came down next. It was in a meeting [on July 24] when I had this splitting headache. I saw flashes of lightning.
