I have never had a headache like this in my entire life. And I knew something was very, very wrong. I did my temperature; it was 38.1 Centigrade [100.58 Fahrenheit]. And I said this sounds like, smells like Ebola. This can’t be malaria because I’ve had malaria a thousand times. And I know how it is. This is completely different. I called the chief medical officer, told him I wasn’t feeling so well. I told him, “Doc, I think I have been exposed to Ebola, and I want to be tested.” And he was instrumental on the next day to have me tested. The guy came in the house dressed in the entire space suit, into my room. They did the test. The next day we found out that I was Ebola positive. By day seven I could not feel my radial pulse. I was in early shock. I was really sick. Very, very ill. My colleagues said that if we don’t get him out of here [his home] today, he’s going to pass. And I felt that way for the first time on day seven. I felt I was in shock. If I didn’t get IV lines going I was probably going to die that day. So my fellow doctors, a pediatric resident at the hospital, and an ob-gyn resident, and also my wife and her brother, all of them were calling to see if they could get an ambulance. But in Monrovia there were no ambulances [available] at the time. So they found this ambulance that was having some parts replaced at a garage. They were repairing the breaks. And [one of the doctors] literally sat there and waited until this ambulance was repaired and brought to the house. Then we got to the hospital, to the Ebola treatment unit. I collapsed on the bed and passed out. I woke up and found out that they have given me three liters of IV fluids, saline. And I felt a little better. That night was the worst night at ETU. I had 46 episodes of diarrhea and 26 episodes of vomiting. I was in a sea of mess. The next day there was this physician’s assistant—I will never forget him. His act of love towards me, to wash me, was so much so that I will never forget it in my entire life. He cleaned me totally. He dressed me, put me in a clean bed. And I felt that was so, so, so nice. I really appreciated that. I felt so relieved. The very first night I woke up to some loud music. They had turned the SUV around and opened the doors and turned the music up very loud. Gospel music. And all the artists were singing, “You’re going to make it, you’re not going to die.” At that time I already had headaches; I felt like a nightmare. Like you had the speakers right [next] to my windows and boom, boom, boom. And then they had a mixture of different kind of people, like some people were in there with fever or malaria. We weren’t all Ebola patients. Some people had strength, and those who had the strength will have prayer services of what we call vigils. And there was singing and praise God the entire night. They were singing and praying the entire night. And that was tiring. They would sing and pray the entire night. After one night of that, [two infected doctors] said, “No, no more praying to God. If you want to talk to God, do it silently in your heart.” So they stopped the vigils. By day three, the morning of day three, I started to do some terrible, terrible hiccups [a symptom of advanced Ebola]. That was when the clinicians taking care of me thought I was going to die. And they communicated that with one another. In fact, at one time I was hiccupping with every breath. So they thought I wasn’t going make it. They were even discussing whether I would be cremated or buried. And the news circulated that selfsame day that I had passed. I was in bad shape at that time. But after that day, day four in the ETU, I started to get better. When they came in to check on me the next morning they met me standing. And they were very surprised. I stayed there getting better, getting better. I was actually in the Ebola Treatment Unit for 14 days. And by day 10 in that place, which was like 17 days of illness, I was feeling much better. There was no more diarrhea, I didn’t have fever. Now I did have complications. I had pneumonia, I had hallucinations of all kinds of different things. Besides the acute renal failure. So by day 17 I was much better. So the guy did another test and the test results came back negative. And then came day 14 [in the ETU]. I was told 20 minutes before I left the Ebola Center that I would leave the Ebola Center. They said, “Dr. Ireland, you have to take a chlorine bath.” And I said, “Is the water warm or hot?” And they said, “No, it’s cold.” And then I said, “I’m staying. I’m not going anywhere.” Where I was, you have mosquitoes flying all over the place and cockroaches. So I said, man, let me get out of here. So in the evening I mustered up enough courage to do the cold chlorine water bath. They had me strip in front of all the nurses and the physician’s assistants. And the male physician’s assistant had to ask them to leave to give me some privacy. I had two buckets of the chlorine water. Very cold, icy water. And then I put on this T-shirt, sweatpants. And I had to go to the spray where I got sprayed thoroughly. In my ears, in my face, with my clothes on. When I left I was so wet. I was drenched with chlorine water. And I came outside. Now you have to bear in mind I am very, very fragile and weak. I’m still sick. I’m just glad I made it, that’s all. And I walk out and there are people singing all around me. The nurses were singing with their beautiful—they were singing and so happy. I was being released at the same time as a nurse. Nurse Barbara. And we had people from JFK there, my family, my elder brother was there, my wife was there. We had a lot of other doctors that were there. We had members of the press. A lot of people had turned out. And I felt like Nelson Mandela. I always use that description. It felt like the Long Walk to Freedom. And even though it was a short walk, for me, because the energy I had to expend because I was so weak, it was like a walk to freedom. And I walk up and I raised my hands to heaven, thanking God for saving my life. And then I noticed something also. There were a lot of crying people, people happy to see me. And when I got close to anybody, they actually backed away. —as told to Aryn Baker

Vladimir Putin's 2014 annexation of Crimea was a strategic move driven by Russia's historical ambitions and a deep sense of national grievance after the Soviet Union's collapse. This action reasserted Russian power, echoing centuries of imperial expansion. For Putin and many Russians, the Soviet Union's dissolution in 1991 was a national tragedy, transforming Russia from a superpower into a "corrupt petrostate," as Putin himself described it. This feeling of diminished status was shared by figures like Google co-founder Sergey Brin, who called Russia "Nigeria with snow." Even Mikhail Gorbachev lamented that Russia was "pushed aside" and marginalized in global politics, with "America calling the shots!" This pervasive sense of lost greatness fueled Putin's assertive foreign policy.

The annexation of Crimea offered Putin a chance to restore Russia's prestige and national pride. This resonated with the Russian public, boosting Putin's approval ratings to 88% in October 2014. This surge surpassed the popularity gained from economic prosperity or the 2014 Sochi Olympics. Despite projected development costs exceeding $18 billion, the acquisition was met with public enthusiasm, including the purchase of Putin-themed merchandise, reflecting a desire for restored national strength.

Sociologist Lev Gudkov noted that the annexation helped Russians feel their "great-power status was restored," dissipating the "frustration and humiliation" felt since the Soviet collapse. For Putin, a former KGB agent, the fall of the Berlin Wall symbolized Russian decline, strengthening his resolve to re-establish Russia's influence. This view highlights a divergence in historical perception, with Putin seeing the post-Soviet era as a period of weakness to be countered.

However, Putin's actions in Ukraine had significant consequences. The downing of Malaysia Airlines Flight 17 in July 2014, attributed to Russian-backed militias, intensified international condemnation. Russia, already excluded from the G-8, faced further isolation at the G-20 summit. Canadian Prime Minister Stephen Harper confronted Putin directly, and German Chancellor Angela Merkel warned that Russia's violation of international law "calls the entire European peaceful order into question." This growing consensus viewed Russia's actions as a threat to regional stability.

This diplomatic isolation coincided with a sharp decline in oil prices, Russia's main export, worsening economic difficulties. Inflation surged, the ruble depreciated, and a recession ensued. The value of Russian government debt fell, forcing Putin to cancel the South Stream pipeline project due to EU regulatory pressures. Putin stated, "We will steer the flow of our energy resources to other regions of the world." The core question remains whether Putin's pursuit of imperial grandeur justified the economic sacrifices. For those with deep historical grievances, the answer appears affirmative. Former Kremlin chief of staff Alexander Voloshin argued that after the Soviet collapse, "America became a monopoly," dictating global affairs. He suggested Putin's actions were a response to this perceived imbalance and an assertion of Russia's right to self-determination and a significant global role. This perspective frames Putin's actions as a necessary correction to a perceived imbalance of global power.

query: What specific services and functions within the federal government are required to continue operating during a government shutdown?

documents.text:
A looming government shutdown this week is poised to disrupt federal services and jeopardize the paychecks of more than 800,000 federal employees. While not all government operations will halt, certain essential agencies are legally mandated to continue functioning, even with unpaid staff. These critical services encompass national security, public safety, and the administration of vital benefits such as Social Security. The U.S. Postal Service will also maintain its operations without interruption.

To navigate this situation, agencies have developed detailed contingency plans, many of which have been updated this year. These plans outline how essential functions will be preserved during a funding lapse. For example, the Department of Homeland Security, as detailed in its 2013 contingency plan, has established specific procedures to ensure the continuity of its core operations.

Similarly, the Department of Health and Human Services has also released its operational plans, highlighting its commitment to maintaining critical health and safety programs. The Office of Management and Budget (OMB) has issued guidance to agencies regarding shutdown procedures, as documented in their official circulars. Furthermore, research from entities like the Congressional Research Service offers valuable insights into the historical effects of government shutdowns on federal operations and employee morale.

The U.S. government plunged into its first shutdown in 17 years on October 1, 2013, as a deeply divided Congress failed to agree on funding for federal agencies, a crisis exacerbated by disputes over President Obama’s signature health-care initiative, the Affordable Care Act (ACA). This impasse propelled the nation toward a critical deadline for raising the federal debt limit, threatening broader economic instability.

House Republicans, spearheaded by Speaker John A. Boehner (R-Ohio), advanced a short-term spending bill designed to undermine the ACA by delaying its "individual mandate," a provision requiring all Americans to secure health insurance, and by stripping lawmakers and their aides of long-standing government health benefits. The Democratic-led Senate, however, swiftly rejected this proposal on a party-line vote, with Senate Majority Leader Harry M. Reid (D-Nev.) urging Boehner to abandon ACA-focused demands and pass a straightforward bill to maintain government operations, warning that "the responsibility for this Republican government shutdown will rest squarely on his shoulders." Boehner’s refusal to yield led to the approval of a plan to establish a special House-Senate committee to reconcile the parties' differences, leaving the livelihoods of millions of federal workers and the services they provide in uncertainty. The White House Office of Management and Budget subsequently issued a directive for agencies to "execute plans for an orderly shutdown due to the absence of appropriations."

The shutdown resulted in the furlough of approximately 800,000 federal workers, leading to the closure of national parks, monuments, museums, and most federal offices. Essential personnel, including air-traffic controllers, prison guards, and Border Patrol agents, were required to work without guaranteed immediate pay. Numerous congressional hearings, including one concerning the Washington Navy Yard shootings, were postponed. In a last-minute development, Congress did approve and send to the White House an agreement to continue issuing paychecks to active-duty troops. President Obama decried the gridlock, stating, "Keeping the people’s government open is not a concession to me. Keeping vital services running and hundreds of thousands of Americans on the job is not something you ‘give’ to the other side. It’s our basic responsibility." Privately, senior Republicans anticipated the closure would persist for at least a week, while some younger House Republicans, lacking experience from the 1995-1996 government shutdown, reportedly questioned the political wisdom of such actions.

Democrats predicted that an extended shutdown into the weekend could merge the funding dispute with the more critical battle over the federal debt limit, as the Treasury Department was projected to exhaust its cash reserves by October 17 unless Congress authorized additional borrowing. Democratic aides suggested that negotiations to reopen the government might be combined with debt-limit talks. In a call with Boehner, President Obama reiterated his opposition to negotiating on the debt limit, insisting that Congress must honor its existing financial obligations. Boehner, speaking on the House floor, quoted Obama and argued that the Republican stance was rooted in "fairness" for working people, citing delays in ACA mandates for employers and unions while constituents faced a disliked and unaffordable bill.

Despite a show of unity, Republicans remained divided on their ACA strategy. Moderate Republicans, including Rep. Shelley Moore Capito (W.Va.), expressed frustration with yielding to opposition forces, led by figures like Sen. Ted Cruz (R-Tex.) and groups such as Heritage Action for America, with Capito stating, "I don’t want to shut down the government" and indicating support for a "clean" funding bill. Senate Republicans also voiced discontent, arguing that House leaders' tactics defied public opinion, which polls indicated overwhelmingly disapproved of using a shutdown threat to defund the ACA. Sen. John McCain (Ariz.) remarked, "By wanting to repeal Obamacare using this method, it defies what the popular will is."

Adding to the tension, Boehner’s proposal to strip lawmakers, staff, and White House aides of employer subsidies for health insurance, valued at approximately $5,000 annually for individuals and $10,000 for families, drew criticism. Conservative groups had labeled these subsidies a "special exemption." House leaders hoped this provision would garner conservative support and pressure Senate Democrats. Rep. Peter Roskam (R-Ill.) framed it as a choice for senators: "On what flooding peninsula can you stand when it’s a question of delaying the individual mandate, ending member subsidies and funding the government?" He argued that if Reid blocked it, "the senators he’s going to protect become the subject of incredible scrutiny." Some Republicans expressed unease about their aides facing significant pay cuts, a sentiment echoed by Sen. Richard J. Durbin (D-Ill.), who called it an "outrage" and noted that Boehner had reportedly worked with Reid and the Obama administration to ensure these subsidies would remain for congressional employees joining the exchanges.

During a closed-door meeting, House Republicans debated the plan for over an hour and a half, with a notable number of public dissenters, including Rep. Peter T. King (N.Y.), who ultimately voted against the proposal, stating, "I don’t want to be the facilitator of a disastrous process and plan." Nevertheless, most Republicans endorsed the deal, albeit reluctantly, with Rep. Tom Cole (R-Okla.) describing it as "a principled call by leadership and it has the support of the con­ference." Democrats remained united against Republican concessions, particularly with the looming debt limit fight. Sen. Charles E. Schumer (D-N.Y.) warned, "The bottom line is very simple. You negotiate on this, they will up the ante for the debt limit."

*This report was contributed to by Ed O’Keefe, Rosalind S. Helderman, Jackie Kucinich, and Jeff Simon.*

**Government Shutdown Threatens NASA's 55th Anniversary and Critical Operations**

As NASA approaches its 55th anniversary on October 1st, a looming government shutdown poses a significant threat to its vital operations. A faction of congressional Republicans is blocking a clean continuing resolution, which could force NASA to halt most of its activities. President Obama has warned that the agency would "shut down almost entirely" if this funding impasse persists.

This shutdown would have a severe impact, with an estimated 17,701 of NASA's 18,250 employees facing furlough. Only a skeleton crew of 549 individuals would remain to maintain essential functions. According to a memo from NASA's Chief Financial Officer, Elizabeth Robinson, only two categories of operations would continue:

*   **International Space Station (ISS) Operations:** NASA would continue supporting the six astronauts and cosmonauts aboard the ISS to ensure their safety and the station's integrity. This includes monitoring the situation for any necessary crew transportation or cargo resupply missions to prevent imminent threats.
*   **Satellite Mission Operations:** For satellites already in orbit, NASA would maintain activities crucial for their safety and continuous data reception. However, work on any satellite missions not yet launched would generally cease.

The 549 employees who will remain on duty will bear the immense responsibility of keeping our satellites operational and our astronauts safe. Their dedication to these complex operations, often in solitary conditions, is deeply appreciated.

The potential disruption to ongoing research, development, and critical space missions underscores the need for stable government funding. The continuation of programs like the ISS, a symbol of international cooperation, is directly jeopardized by these political stalemates. Even temporary losses of scientific data from operational satellites can have long-term consequences for research and future mission planning.

The Senate has rejected House-passed healthcare provisions attached to a stopgap spending bill, sending it back to the House without policy demands. This action followed a swift Senate session where a weekend budget proposal from House Republicans was also quickly dismissed. Senate Majority Leader Harry Reid characterized the House's repeated attempts as a sign of irrationality, stating, "They've lost their minds."

With the fiscal year ending at midnight, the federal government faced a shutdown. However, a measure was signed late Monday to ensure military personnel continued to receive pay, a move supported by President Obama, who emphasized the principle of not holding essential government functions hostage. "You don’t get to extract a ransom for doing your job," President Obama declared. A brief phone call between President Obama and House Speaker John Boehner yielded no progress, with the President reportedly stating, "I’m not going to negotiate."

Within the House, some conservative members appeared resigned to the shutdown, viewing it as an inevitable consequence of their efforts to dismantle the healthcare law. This strategy, however, was creating internal party divisions. Representative Devin Nunes voiced frustration with the rigid stance, noting, "You have this group that keeps saying somehow if you’re not with them, you’re for Obamacare." He further criticized the shutdown as "moronic." The long-term unity of Republicans on demanding healthcare concessions during a shutdown remained uncertain, with Representative Phil Gingrey admitting, "I don’t know" when asked about party cohesion.

Earlier, the Senate had voted along party lines to eliminate the previous House plan. Senators then proposed a bill to fund the government through November 15, devoid of policy riders. House leadership, however, insisted on significant changes to the healthcare law as a condition for reopening the government.

Senator Reid placed the blame for the shutdown squarely on Speaker Boehner, declaring, "Our negotiation is over with." He likened the situation to dealing with a bully, stating, "You know with a bully you cannot let them slap you around, because they slap you around today, they slap you five or six times tomorrow. We are not going to be bullied." Reid also criticized the "banana Republican mind-set" of the House and urged Boehner to allow a vote on the Senate's bill, which was expected to pass with Democratic and moderate Republican support.

House Republicans had attempted to attach specific language to the funding bill, aiming to delay the individual mandate for health insurance and require members of Congress, their staff, and White House personnel to purchase health insurance through the new exchanges without government subsidies. Conservative activists framed this as holding Congress and the White House to the same standards as ordinary Americans. However, this provision would disproportionately affect lower-paid junior staff. While most individuals purchasing insurance on the exchanges would receive subsidies, and most Americans would retain employer-sponsored insurance with tax deductions, the proposed language would force lawmakers and their staff, political appointees, and the President and Vice President to bear the full cost of their health insurance out-of-pocket. Representative Peter T. King described this as junior staff being "used as a sacrifice" in a political maneuver driven by hard-line Republicans, suggesting it was a "dead end that Ted Cruz created."

This budget confrontation, which threatened to halt federal operations and furlough workers, originated from a Republican effort to repeal a law that had been in effect for three years and largely upheld by the Supreme Court. A key component of the law, the opening of insurance exchanges, was scheduled to commence the following day. Republicans contended that the administration had already delayed aspects of the law and argued for a year-long postponement. Democrats countered that Republicans were using the budget process to achieve healthcare concessions unattainable through traditional legislation, driven by extreme elements within their party. Representative Chris Van Hollen expressed concern about the lack of a clear resolution, stating, "The scary thing about the period we’re in right now is there is no clear end."

A list of key questions and answers from Washington Post coverage of the likely effects on federal government employees. Live updates: The shutdown showdown When will I know if I'll be furloughed due to the shutdown? Federal workers will get an e-mail or phone call from their supervisors by Monday telling them to report for work or remain at home. Those who would not be working would get a few hours Tuesday morning to come into the office to secure their files, send e-mails and put things in order before signing off. It would be illegal for them to conduct any work until they were called back to their jobs. - Read more from Lisa Rein Who is "exempt" and who's not? That's up to individual agencies. A recent OMB memo told them to review plans they made in 2011. The federal government is required by law to maintain functions that: - provide for the national security, including the conduct of foreign relations; - provide for benefit payments and the performance of contract obligations under no-year or multi-year contracts; and - protect life and property. - Read more from Eric Yoder Who decides? Federal managers must review which of their employees would be excepted or exempted and required to come to work, and which would be non-excepted or non-exempted and sent home during a shutdown. - Read more from Lisa Rein and Eric Yoder How many workers will be affected? A government shutdown this week would jeopardize the paychecks of more than 800,000 federal workers who could be told to stay home. The federal government has more than 2 million employees. Those workers who remain on the job as well as active military would be entitled to their salaries, but might not be paid on time. - Read more from Lisa Rein Will I be paid? A recent Office of Management and Budget memo says much the same: "Without further specific direction or enactment by Congress, all excepted employees are entitled to receive payment for obligations incurred by their agencies for their performance of excepted work during the period of the appropriations lapse," the memo says. "After appropriations are enacted, payroll centers will pay all excepted employees for time worked." The OMB memo did not directly address pay for "non-exempt" employees. The OPM guidance says that "Congress will determine whether furloughed employees receive pay for the furlough period." - Read more from Eric Yoder If I am paid, will my paycheck be on time? Gregory Junemann, president of the International Federation of Professional & Technical Engineers, said the administration officials indicated that employees who are required to work would be paid "eventually." "They can't guarantee [the workers] will be paid on time," Junemann said. "They are pretty comfortable with the statement that everybody who works will be paid eventually." Those furloughed might be paid, but Republicans could move to block that. - Read more from Joe Davidson If I'm furloughed, why can't I take annual leave or other paid time off instead? Because that would create a debt obligation to the government not payable under the Antideficiency Act if no appropriation has been made. This includes a requirement to cancel any paid leave that already had been scheduled. - Read more from Eric Yoder The OPM publication states that employees cannot substitute paid leave for furlough time and that even previously scheduled paid time off must be canceled "because the requirement to furlough supersedes leave and other paid time off rights." - Read more from Eric Yoder How would a new plan offered by House Republicans affect me? The new plan, like those Republicans have pushed before, would increase employee contributions to their retirement benefits. The plan now under discussion apparently is like a bill that Republicans used their House majority to approve in December. Had it passed the Senate, the measure would have required federal workers to pay an additional 5 percent of salary over five years toward their retirement contributions, saving the government about $80 billion. - Read more from Joe Davidson How is this possible shutdown different from previous ones? Compared with the shutdowns of the 1990s, many more federal workers are in danger of being furloughed this year, because Congress has not passed a single funding bill. In the past, Congress had passed appropriations bills that funded various large agencies, including the Defense Department, meaning they could continue to operate even if other parts of the government could not. - Read more from Lisa Rein What if I'm a government contractor? Contractors said they assumed they would get little notice about whether their employees would go to work or not. And then, they would have to decide -- on a contract by contract basis -- what to do with those workers. - Read more from Marjorie Censer Will I be reimbursed for time missed? In past shutdowns, federal employees have been reimbursed for time missed, said Alan Chvotkin, counsel at the Professional Services Council, an industry group. But contractors have not fared as well. "Contractors have never been reimbursed," Chvotkin said. A shutdown has been "just lost revenue, lost salary to those affected." - Read more from Marjorie Censer While there is no law requiring that nonessential employees be compensated if they are ordered off the job, Congress has in the past voted to reimburse their losses once shutdowns ended. But this go-round could be different. The bitterly divided Congress includes many lawmakers who are unsympathetic to the plight of federal workers and could be loath to help them recoup their money. - Read more from Lisa Rein Will overseas military operations be affected? Defense Department spokesman George Little said overseas operations, including those in Afghanistan, would not be directly affected. - Read more from Lisa Rein What's the status of employee benefits? Coverage under the federal employee health insurance program will continue, with the employees' share accumulating until they return to paid status. Coverage under the life insurance program also continues, without cost to the employee. For the long-term care and vision/dental insurance programs, enrollees must continue to pay the premiums; those paying through payroll deduction will be billed directly if the unpaid period lasts a number of weeks. - Read more from Eric Yoder What if I'm deemed "essential," but get sick? You might want to take steps to assure they are feeling well in addition to feeling good, because if you become too sick to work during a shutdown they would face the same uncertainty over their pay for the time off work as those who are furloughed. ... For example, the Navy has issued new guidance to its civilian employees warning that "Excepted employees who are not able to work due to sick leave, jury duty, etc., must be placed in a furlough status (i.e., non-duty, non-pay) until they are able to return to duty." - Read more from Eric Yoder If I'm a retired federal worker, will I still get my check? For millions of federal retirees who may be concerned about their annuity checks, which arrive on the first day of each month, the Office of Personnel Management said they would be paid on Oct. 1 as normal. - Read more from Lisa Rein Federal retirement payments, like payments such as Social Security benefits, fall under the "mandatory" budget category not funded through annual appropriations and thus not affected by these kinds of funding deadlocks. According to the OPM document, federal retirees "will still receive their scheduled annuity payments on the first business day of the month." - Read more from Eric Yoder What about other services to retirees, such as issues with missed payments or questions? OPM's Retirement Services office employees are excluded from the shutdown because that office gets its operating money from the federal retirement trust fund. The Retirement Information Office at 1-888-767-6738 is open. - Read more from Eric Yoder How does a shutdown end? It's up to Congress and the White House. No doubt there would be plenty of pressure from the public and workforce. There is no law setting a time limit. - Read more from Lisa Rein and Eric Yoder Published Oct. 1, 2013.

A looming government shutdown threatens to disrupt a wide array of essential services and significantly impact countless individuals across the nation. As a critical midnight deadline passed without a resolution between House Republicans and Senate Democrats, federal agencies are bracing for the cessation of most operations. The sole exception is a minor bill ensuring that military personnel will continue to receive their pay, a crucial measure amidst the broader disruption. This shutdown will profoundly affect daily life and the fundamental functioning of government nationwide, with far-reaching consequences for public health, safety, economic stability, and individual well-being.

### Public Health and Safety Under Threat

The ramifications for public health and safety are particularly concerning. The **Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)** will suspend its vital annual flu vaccination program, leaving a gap in preventative healthcare during a critical season. The **U.S. Forest Service** faces furloughs precisely during its peak fire season, potentially compromising wildfire response efforts. The **Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA)** will halt its regular factory inspections, potentially increasing workplace hazards.

The **Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)** will largely shut down, impacting crucial areas such as pesticide regulation and air quality monitoring. The CDC's ability to track infectious disease outbreaks at the local level will be severely hampered. The **Grain Inspection, Packers and Stockyards Administration** will be unable to investigate alleged violations, and a critical aspect of food imports will go uninspected. Furthermore, the **Department of Health and Human Services**, including the **Food and Drug Administration (FDA)**, will furlough staff, directly affecting food and drug safety research and oversight.

### Impact on Military Personnel and Veterans

The shutdown will also cast a shadow over military personnel and veterans. While military personnel will continue to receive their pay, approximately 400,000 civilian defense employees will be placed on unpaid leave, creating significant financial hardship. Families of fallen soldiers may experience delays in receiving crucial death benefits. The **Department of Veterans Affairs (VA)** may exhaust its funding for veteran pension checks within weeks, though essential medical care services are expected to continue. Disability payments for veterans could also face disruptions, adding further stress to those who have served.

### Travel and Immigration Services Face Delays

Individuals seeking to travel or immigrate to the United States will encounter substantial disruptions. Visa applicants will experience holds on tens of thousands of applications, creating uncertainty and potential hardship. Passport application processing will be significantly delayed, impacting those with upcoming travel plans. The **Coast Guard** will reduce its routine patrols and navigation assistance services, potentially affecting maritime safety.

### National Parks and Recreational Areas Close

The shutdown will lead to the closure of all 401 **National Park Service** sites, a move that will significantly impact tourism-dependent businesses and the economies of surrounding communities. National Park Service golf courses will also cease operations. Individuals residing within national parks and forests will be given a stark 48-hour notice to relocate, causing considerable disruption. Even beloved attractions like the **National Zoo's** Panda-cam will be unavailable to the public, a small but symbolic loss.

### Economic and Financial Services Setbacks

The economic fallout will be widespread. Gun owners may encounter delays in processing their permit applications. The **Small Business Administration (SBA)** will furlough a substantial portion of its workforce, directly affecting those applying for crucial small business loans. The **Department of Homeland Security's** e-Verify program, essential for employment verification, will be offline.

The **Federal Housing Administration** and the **U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA)** will stop guaranteeing new mortgage loans, potentially impacting the housing market. The **Bureau of Land Management** will halt permit processing for oil and gas drilling and suspend its wild horse and burro adoption programs. Funding for chemical site facility security regulations will end, raising concerns about industrial safety.

The **National Institutes of Health** will stop admitting new patients, impacting critical medical research and treatment. The **Bureau of Ocean Energy Management** will cease new offshore renewable-energy projects, hindering progress in sustainable energy development. The **Office of Surface Mining Reclamation and Enforcement** will suspend its oversight of coal mines.

The USDA's **Women, Infants, and Children (WIC)** program, a vital support for low-income families, will stop making payments on October 1. The **Department of Transportation** will not review routine defects or recall information, potentially delaying safety improvements. The **Department of Energy’s ARPA-E** program, focused on advanced energy research, will shut down. The **Nuclear Regulatory Commission** could furlough most of its employees, impacting oversight of nuclear facilities. The **Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program** could be affected, leaving vulnerable populations at risk.

The **Commodity Futures Trading Commission** will furlough most of its employees, potentially impacting financial market regulation. The **Internal Revenue Service (IRS)** will close its tax hotline and halt the processing of tax payments, creating significant inconvenience for taxpayers. Cutbacks at the **Department of Education** could slow Pell grant and student-loan payments, affecting students' ability to finance their education. The **Bureau of Economic Analysis** will reduce its data collection efforts, impacting the accuracy of economic indicators. Funding for **Temporary Assistance for Needy Families** (welfare) runs out on October 1, and the **Head Start** child development program will stop issuing new grants on the same date, leaving many families without essential support.

### Government Operations Grind to a Halt

Beyond specific services, the shutdown will bring many core government operations to a standstill. The **Social Security Administration** will not respond to Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) requests, hindering transparency and public access to information. All **Smithsonian Institution** museums in Washington, D.C., will close their doors, a significant cultural loss. **NASA** anticipates furloughing most of its staff, leading to the shutdown of its website and live-stream broadcasts, impacting public engagement with space exploration.

A government shutdown presents a stark contrast to the drawn-out consequences of sequestration. This shutdown means indefinite furloughs for hundreds of thousands of federal employees, the closure of beloved national parks like Yosemite and Acadia, and a potential halt to veterans' benefit payments if the funding impasse extends into late October. Unlike the across-the-board spending cuts of sequestration, which often fell short of expectations, the public will soon feel the direct impact of disrupted or canceled government services.

As former Clinton administration official, central to planning the 1995-1996 shutdown, noted, "It’s a different kettle of fish when you say people aren’t going to show up at all." This sentiment highlights the tangible and immediate effects of a full government closure.

President Barack Obama, speaking just hours before the deadline, assured the nation that essential services would continue. Air traffic controllers and federal prison guards would remain on duty, and Social Security and Medicare benefits would be unaffected. However, he cautioned that the situation would still be difficult, particularly for federal workers sent home without pay until a congressional agreement is reached. "A shutdown will have a very real economic impact on real people right away," Obama stated.

The official directive to initiate shutdown procedures came not from a countdown clock, but from a guidance memo issued by White House Office of Management and Budget Director Sylvia Mathews Burwell. This memo instructed department heads to implement their specific contingency plans.

Here's how the government effectively closes its doors:

*   **Tuesday Morning:** All federal employees are expected to report to work. Managers will then distribute official notices identifying essential personnel.
*   **Defining Essential Personnel:** This designation, fraught with legal and psychological complexities, primarily falls to Cabinet secretaries. It generally includes roles critical for public safety and government property protection, such as military troops, meat inspectors, and border patrol agents.
*   **Furloughed Employees' Tasks:** Those furloughed will have until approximately lunchtime Tuesday to conclude immediate tasks. This includes:
    *   Securing files.
    *   Canceling meetings, conferences, and trips.
    *   Setting up "out-of-office" email replies.
    *   Updating voicemail greetings to indicate their unavailability until a budget agreement is reached.
*   **Staying Informed:** Federal employees are advised to monitor news outlets for updates on their return-to-work status.
*   **Guidance on Rules:** For questions regarding furlough regulations, such as prohibitions on voluntary work or moonlighting, employees can consult a comprehensive Office of Personnel Management guidance document.

The Pentagon anticipates some of the largest furloughs, affecting approximately 400,000 civilian workers. Defense Secretary Chuck Hagel expressed concern, noting that these employees had already endured significant impacts from sequestration, including unpaid furlough days during the summer. He emphasized that decisions to furlough staff are "dictated solely by the law" for protecting people and property. In a memo to Defense Department employees, Hagel stated, "The furloughs are in no way a reflection of the importance of your work, the hard effort you put forth every day, or your dedicated service to our department and our nation." Uniformed troops are also required to report to work, but will receive their pay due to a bipartisan legislative agreement.

The outlook for veterans is less optimistic. Benefit checks from the Department of Veterans Affairs, including disability and pension payments, are projected to continue for only two to three weeks into the shutdown. Support for veteran-run businesses will cease, and rehabilitation and education counseling services are likely to be curtailed.

Some government operations will continue uninterrupted. These typically rely on mandatory spending or funding streams independent of annual congressional appropriations. This is why the Obamacare insurance exchanges, launching on Tuesday, will proceed as planned, despite being a focal point of the budget dispute. Similarly, the U.S. Postal Service, Amtrak, and certain transportation agencies like the Federal Highway Administration, Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration, and parts of the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration will maintain operations, as they are funded by the federal fuel tax. However, many other government functions will face significant disruptions.

The U.S. government shutdown, triggered by a bitter budget fight, brought federal operations to a standstill for the first time in nearly two decades. This closure impacted iconic landmarks like New York's Liberty Island and Alaska's Denali National Park, idling hundreds of thousands of federal workers and halting all but the most critical government services.

The shutdown, which began Tuesday, September 30, 2013, stemmed from Congressional bickering over President Barack Obama's health care reform law. This impasse cast doubt on Americans' access to essential government services, from federally-backed home loans to supplemental food assistance for children and pregnant women.

For many federal employees, the shutdown meant an immediate loss of paychecks as they were placed on unpaid furloughs. Those still working faced delays in receiving their salaries. Park Ranger Darquez Smith, a father-to-be, expressed his concern, stating, "I've got a lot on my plate right now _ tuition, my daughter, bills. I'm just confused and waiting just like everyone else." Smith, 23, works at Dayton Aviation Heritage National Historical Park in Ohio.

The shutdown's impact varied:

*   **Immediate and Far-Reaching:**
    *   Nearly 3,000 Federal Aviation Administration safety inspectors were furloughed.
    *   Most employees of the National Transportation Safety Board (NTSB), including accident investigators, were furloughed.
    *   Almost all of NASA ceased operations, with the exception of Mission Control in Houston.
    *   National parks, the Smithsonian museums, and the National Zoo in Washington D.C. were closed. Even the zoo's popular panda cam went dark.
*   **Unaffected Services:**
    *   Emergency funds for flood recovery in Colorado continued to flow, though federal worker furloughs were expected to cause delays.
    *   National Guard soldiers rebuilding roads and active-duty military personnel were slated to be paid on time, thanks to a bill passed before the shutdown.
    *   Existing Social Security and Medicare benefits, veterans' services, and mail delivery remained operational.

Visitors expressed frustration with elected officials. Chris Fahl, visiting the Abraham Lincoln Birthplace National Historic Park in Kentucky, remarked, "There is no good thing going to come out of it. Taxpayers are just going to be more overburdened." Emily Enfinger, touring the Statue of Liberty, urged politicians to compromise, stating, "They should be willing to compromise, both sides, and it discourages me that they don't seem to be able to do that. They're not doing their job as far as I'm concerned."

Marlena Knight, an Australian visiting Independence National Historical Park in Philadelphia, found the shutdown's focus on healthcare particularly baffling. "We can't imagine not having a national health system," she said. "I just can't believe that this country can shut down over something like a national health system. Totally bizarre, as an Australian, but fascinating."

The shutdown's full effect was not instantaneous. Many federal workers were permitted to enter their offices on Tuesday, September 30, 2013, to update voicemails or complete time cards. However, they were then under strict orders to cease all work, including checking emails.

The duration of the budget standoff posed further risks. Barbara Haxton, executive director of the Ohio Head Start Association, warned that its preschool programs could be jeopardized if the shutdown extended beyond two weeks. She highlighted that previous automatic budget cuts in March had already led to nearly 3,000 children losing access to services, and further delays could have dire consequences for "the poorest of the poor children." Haxton also pointed out the disparity, noting, "And our Congressman still gets his paycheck. His pay doesn't stop and his health insurance doesn't stop."

Associated Press reporters Kathy Matheson in Philadelphia, Joan Lowy in Washington, D.C., Dylan Lovan in Louisville, Kentucky, Terence Chea in San Francisco, and Amanda Lee Myers in Cincinnati contributed to this report.

query: What date is officially recognized as the start of the current bull market, and how much has the S&P 500 Index increased since then?

documents.text: No one can predict when the current bull market will end. But we can all agree on when began, right? You might think so, but no. By the most commonly accepted definition, the bull market in stocks that followed the 'Great Recession' and global financial crisis turns eight years old Thursday. On Monday, March 9, 2009, the Standard & Poor's 500 Index closed at 676, having touched 666 the prior Friday, and then reversed higher. By the standard custom for classifying bull and bear markets, this rebound became a new bull market once it gained 20 percent – just two weeks later. And those unwritten rules say that this bull market remains in force unless and until the S&P declines at least 20 percent from a high. That has not happened since March 2009, which is why this 250-percent advance is still generally considered one, long bull market – the second-longest ever, in fact. Yet plenty of professional investors and longtime students of the market argue that dating the bull market to the initial snap-back rally from a 12-year low overlooks some of the setbacks since then and perhaps overstates the bull's age. Here are some alternative start dates commonly cited by those who think the bull market truly got rolling well after the tumultuous swings around the financial-crisis climax: Source: CNBC September 2011: In a sort of financial-crisis aftershock, stress in European debt markets, surging oil prices and a U.S. debt-ceiling standoff struck a still-fragile economic recovery and dragged the S&P 500 down 19.4 percent from May to September that year. The decline exceeded 20 percent on an intraday basis and most global indexes suffered declines of at least that much. The record keepers file this under the heading of "severe market correction," but to many it was close enough to consider the recovery from the September 2011 akin to a new, or renewed, bull market. March 2013: In some respects, this equity advance didn't begin to behave as a real bull market until late 2012. Treasury yields had bottomed in mid-2012, and riskier assets benefited from the unclenching of tensions that this represented. Risk spreads on speculative high-yield debt began collapsing, and for the first time in this economic cycle, the price-earnings multiple on stocks began expanding. By early 2013, with Washington gridlocked, the Federal Reserve looking to end quantitative easing and equities appearing cheap relative to bonds, the S&P gained upward momentum. By late March 2013, the index finally eclipsed its former all-time high from October 2007 of 1565. Some strategists say this was the end of the "secular bear market" that had begun in the year 2000 and the start of a new "secular bull market." The term "secular" refers to a long-term structural move in asset prices. An earlier secular bear lasted from 1966 to 1982, when the Dow Jones Industrial Average was contained below the 1000 market, followed by the stupendous secular bull of 1982 to 2000. Obviously within these periods are significant cyclical swings, often of more than 20 percent against the long-term trend. There's no real science to defining or playing such periods. And, frankly, there have been so few that it's hard to generalize about their behavior with too much confidence. But the idea that a push to a new all-time high touches off a decade-plus of further upward movement has some evidence behind it, and the likes of Bank of America Merrill Lynch have been using this framework since the 2013 breakout. February 2016: The S&P 500 fell "only" about 15 percent from the summer of 2015 to the early-2016 low as oil prices collapsed and global recession and deflation fears flared. Yet that understates the carnage beneath the surface. The small-cap Russell 2000 sank 26 percent over eight months. And Strategas Research technical strategist Chris Verrone notes that 63 percent of the stocks in the S&P 500 index fell at least 20 percent from their 52-week high. So, bear market or not, it was a pretty good washout - one that many now argue cleansed the market of excesses, rebuilt value and reinvigorated the bull. In some ways this is akin to the Asian financial crisis of 1998, when the S&P 500 never fell by more than 20 percent but 70 percent of all stocks did. Once it bottomed, that bull market put in a ferociously lucrative final push over the next 18 months. So how much does this matter for an investor trying to set expectations for future equity performance? Not terribly much. Even if we say the bull is eight years old, and therefore a "mature" one, bull markets don't die of old age; they are put down by recession, financial panic or the Fed. On the other hand, even if we decree that the bull market truly started five-and-a-half years, or four years ago, or 13 months ago, it doesn't change the fact that stock valuations are close to the top of their historical range and the eight-year returns delivered by the market are among the best ever. Ryan Detrick of LPL Financial looked back at all eight-year periods since 1936. The past eight years' gain was only exceeded 35 times, placing the current run in the top four percent of all past periods. Most prior instances occurred in clusters in the mid-1950s, and then the late-'90s and early 2000. The one-, five- and ten-year average and median returns following the other such periods aren't great – small losses one year out and subpar appreciation for longer periods. Granted, those averages are heavily undercut by the Tech Bubble readings. But taken together, they suggest that no matter when you say the bull market began, it's certainly not over but it's probably not early.

**Wall Street's Enduring Bull Market: Drivers and Potential Pitfalls**

Wall Street's remarkable bull market, now in its eighth year, continues to defy expectations of an imminent end. Experts from prominent firms like UBS Securities and Kingsview Asset Management largely anticipate sustained gains, attributing this optimism to robust corporate profits, anticipated tax cuts, and a business-friendly governmental approach, as reported by Reuters. However, these same analysts caution that elevated stock prices could precipitate a market correction, potentially as significant as 10 percent.

The primary driver behind the stock market's persistent ascent is the diminished likelihood of a recession in the near future. As Paul Nolte, a portfolio manager at Kingsview Asset Management, aptly states, "Just because the (bull) market has hit a birthday, or is among the longest ever, doesn’t mean that it will ‘die’ of old age. Markets don’t really pay attention to the calendar or the candles on the cake." This sentiment is echoed by Ryan Detrick, senior market strategist at LPL Financial, who believes that with continued earnings growth projected, the bull market could extend into its ninth year, even if the journey encounters some turbulence.

Key market indicators underscore the bull market's strength. Since its low point on March 9, 2009, the S&P 500 index has surged by an impressive 250 percent, marking it as the second-longest bull market on record without a 20 percent decline, the typical harbinger of a bear market. The market has demonstrated remarkable resilience, maintaining its upward trajectory even during periods of declining corporate earnings, such as in 2016. Historically low interest rates have further bolstered its appeal, making stocks a more attractive investment compared to U.S. Treasury bonds. As of Wednesday, the index had climbed nearly 6 percent in 2017, closing at 2,362.98. This performance aligns with a December Reuters poll that forecast the S&P 500 to finish 2017 at 2,350, a projection bolstered by expectations surrounding President Donald Trump's economic agenda, including tax reform and deregulation.

Despite the potential for market corrections, strategists generally maintain a positive outlook on the bull market's longevity. Julian Emanuel, executive director of U.S. equity and derivatives strategy at UBS Securities, anticipates a healthy 5 to 10 percent dip, viewing it as a mechanism to "refresh" the market and present opportune buying moments rather than a signal of distress. Nevertheless, Emanuel highlights a significant concern: "valuations have outrun earnings potential and the state of fundamentals generally." The S&P 500's forward price-to-earnings ratio, trading around 18, exceeds its historical average of 15, indicating that sustained and robust corporate profit growth is essential for valuations to normalize.

Earnings growth projections and positive economic indicators continue to underpin market strength. Thomson Reuters I/B/E/S data forecasts earnings to expand by a solid 10.7 percent this year, a figure that, while slightly revised downward, still signifies substantial growth. Furthermore, encouraging U.S. economic data, such as a strong February jobs report, suggests the economy is not on the verge of a downturn, providing additional support for stock prices. Kingsview's Nolte, who anticipates a potential dip of up to 5 percent, shares this optimistic perspective, stating, "We do not yet see any of the warning signs that point to a larger decline. A recession does not seem to be in the cards just yet." Complementing these factors, research from firms like Goldman Sachs points to robust consumer spending and business investment as crucial elements of economic stability. The Federal Reserve's measured approach to interest rate hikes, as indicated in their recent policy statements, also contributes to a favorable investment climate for equities.

Here's a revised version of the document, aiming to meet your quality targets:

**Eight Years On: The Peculiarities of the Longest-Running Bull Market**

Eight years ago today, the stock market hit rock bottom. The Dow Jones Industrial Average plunged to 6,547, a stark symbol of widespread job losses and a global financial system on the brink of collapse. Fast forward to today, and this bull market has achieved a rare feat, surviving to celebrate its eighth birthday – a milestone matched by only one other bull market in history. The Dow has since surged over 14,000 points to 20,855, and the S&P 500 has delivered an impressive annual return of 20% for investors who weathered the storm. However, this ascent has been far from smooth. This post-World War II bull market has been characterized by its unpredictability, puzzling nature, and unique traits.

**An Unloved and Persistent Rally**

Perhaps the most striking aspect of this bull market is how "unloved" it has been. Despite more than tripling stock values over eight years, household equity ownership has actually declined, reaching its lowest point in 19 years. This sentiment is echoed by market strategists who describe it as the "least loved, most questioned and most doubted bull" they can recall.

This persistence is remarkable considering the headwinds it has faced. Major industries like banking and auto manufacturing teetered on collapse. The U.S. government experienced a 16-day shutdown, and political brinkmanship brought the nation close to defaulting on its debt. Globally, European nations grappled with debt crises, terrorist attacks spread, and China's economy experienced a significant slump. Yet, the stock market continued its upward trajectory.

**Near Misses and Surprising Calm**

The market's resilience was tested significantly just last year. While President Trump's election ushered in a period of economic optimism, the preceding months were fraught with uncertainty. Concerns over China's growth, weak oil demand, the UK's Brexit vote, and Trump's unexpected victory all contributed to a volatile period. Despite these challenges, stocks rebounded from a dismal winter in 2016 to post a 22% gain over the following 12 months, marking the third-largest year-over-year increase since March 2009.

Remarkably, this period of volatility did not translate into widespread market swings. Compared to the bull market of 1990-2000, the past year has seen significantly fewer days with gains or losses exceeding 1%. This suggests a surprising level of calm amidst the underlying economic and political shifts.

**Valuation Concerns and Broad Gains**

As this bull market matures, its valuation has become a key talking point. Both stocks and bonds are now considerably more expensive than they were eight years ago. The S&P 500's current price-to-earnings ratio, based on projected profits, is about 12% above its 25-year average. This elevated valuation may be fueled by expectations of tax cuts and increased government spending. Another P/E calculation, based on a decade of averaged corporate profits, places the market's P/E at 29.8, a level only previously seen during the dot-com bubble and the Great Depression.

Despite these valuation concerns, the bull market has proven surprisingly egalitarian in its long-term returns. While individual sectors have experienced distinct ups and downs – with financial stocks soaring and then falling, and utilities outperforming energy in some years – the overall trend has been one of broad-based gains. From 2009 to 2017, the difference in annual returns between the top and second-best performing sectors was a mere 1 percentage point. Five sectors delivered annual returns between 22% and 26%, with even the weakest performer, energy, achieving a respectable 9.4%.

**The Fed's Role and the Road Ahead**

A significant factor contributing to this bull market's longevity has been the Federal Reserve's accommodative monetary policy. Typically, bull markets see the Fed begin to raise interest rates as the economy strengthens, eventually contributing to their end. However, throughout this eight-year rally, interest rates have remained at historically low levels, with only two modest quarter-percentage-point hikes. This has allowed the market to continue its ascent without the usual braking mechanism.

Looking forward, the market is approaching its ninth birthday, coinciding with one of the longest business cycles in American history. While concerns about a potential recession and market downturn are natural, experts suggest that bull markets don't typically die of old age. Instead, they succumb to "fright" – a sudden shock that causes investors to sell en masse. While economic indicators currently suggest continued strength, with robust demand for automobiles and homes, and the Fed signaling confidence in the economy's ability to withstand higher rates, investors should temper expectations. Future returns may not mirror the exceptional gains of the past, as earnings growth slows, dividend yields and bond coupons remain low, and the potential for valuation multiples to revert to their historical averages exists.

Eight years after the significant market downturn of 2008-2009, the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA) has demonstrated remarkable resilience and growth, significantly outperforming alternative investments and many global equity markets. For investors who maintained positions in this key index, the subsequent years have likely resulted in substantial portfolio appreciation. The DJIA, a benchmark comprising 30 leading U.S. corporations, serves as a vital indicator of the broader market's health and is widely recognized as a primary proxy for U.S. stock market performance.

Following the turbulent economic climate of March 2009, when the DJIA hovered around the 6,500-point mark, the index has since experienced an impressive ascent, achieving an increase exceeding 200%. This significant surge is largely attributable to the accommodative monetary policies enacted by central banks, including the Federal Reserve, in response to the 2008-2009 financial crisis, measures designed to stabilize and stimulate economic activity. In contrast, during the same period, alternative investments such as gold saw a more modest increase of 30.8%, and crude oil gained 17.5%, underscoring the distinct outperformance of equities. When compared to the performance of international equity markets, the Dow's trajectory appears particularly robust; for instance, the Stoxx Europe 600 index advanced by 138%, the United Kingdom's FTSE 100 rose by 108%, China's Shanghai Composite added 50%, and Japan's Nikkei saw a gain of 175%, highlighting the resilience and recovery of the U.S. corporate sector.

Among the Dow components that have remained in the index since 2009, Home Depot Inc. has emerged as a standout performer, with its stock price appreciating by an astounding 608%, while American Express Co. and Walt Disney Co. have also delivered robust returns, with both companies' stock prices surging by over 500%. Several companies added to the Dow's roster since the commencement of this significant bull market have also contributed to the index's overall upward trajectory; notably, UnitedHealth Group Inc. has experienced a remarkable 752% increase in its stock price, and Visa Inc. has seen its stock price skyrocket by an impressive 524%. Apple Inc., a more recent addition, has surged nearly 1,000% since 2009, though its performance since becoming a Dow component two years ago has been a more modest 9.3%, reflecting the dynamic nature of stock market performance over different time horizons. However, it is crucial to acknowledge that not all Dow stocks have achieved such exceptional growth; major oil company Exxon Mobil Corp. has seen a relatively modest increase of 23% since March 2009, and other established companies, including International Business Machines Corp., Procter & Gamble Co., Verizon Communications Inc., and Walmart Inc., have also posted comparatively subdued returns, illustrating the diverse performance within even the most prominent index. Since 2009, a limited number of companies have been removed from the Dow Jones Industrial Average, including AT&T Inc., Bank of America Corp., Alcoa Corp., Citigroup Inc., General Motors Co., Kraft Heinz Co., and HP Inc., with some former components like Bank of America and Citigroup outperforming current Dow constituents in percentage gains since March 2009.

## Bull Market: Riding the Highs, Watching for the Dips

The stock market has seen significant growth since its low point on March 9, 2009. Back then, the Dow Jones Industrial Average closed at 6,547.05. Since that trough, the Dow has more than tripled. This means a $20,000 investment made at that time would now be worth around $64,000.

While most of these gains happened during Barack Obama's presidency, the Dow has still climbed over 2,500 points, or about 12%, since Donald Trump was elected. It recently passed 21,000 for the first time. During this period, social media company Snap went public. Even though it consistently lost money, it was valued at $34 billion on its first day of trading. President Trump noted these positive trends, tweeting that since Election Day, the stock market had gained $3.2 trillion, consumer confidence was at a 15-year high, and jobs were plentiful.

### Investor Enthusiasm and Emerging Concerns

These figures have encouraged many people to invest. According to the Investment Company Institute, about $75 billion has flowed into U.S. equity and exchange-traded funds since October. This suggests investors expect the bull market to continue under President Trump's administration.

However, some caution remains. This skepticism isn't due to political reasons but rather nonpolitical factors. These include current market valuations, the Federal Reserve's monetary policy, and the tendency for investors to follow the crowd, known as herding behavior.

Snap's high valuation, though its stock has since fallen, serves as a warning. It's not as extreme as the speculative IPOs of 1999 or the risky mortgage loans of 2004-2007. Still, the S&P 500 index's price-to-earnings ratio is currently over twenty-six. This is higher than in any bull market since World War II, except for the late 1990s. Other valuation measures, like Robert Shiller's cyclically adjusted P/E ratio and James Tobin's "Q ratio," also point to potential risks.

Investment analyst Andrew Smithers notes that while the current stock market overvaluation isn't at the extreme peaks of 1929 and 1999, it's approaching other historical highs seen in 1905, 1936, and 1968.

### Factors Fueling the Market and Potential Headwinds

Market optimists often point to historically low interest rates, which make stocks more appealing than bonds. They also cite market momentum, suggesting that even overvalued markets can keep rising. The Federal Reserve's policy of keeping borrowing costs low has certainly helped fuel the bull market.

However, the Fed is now increasing interest rates. Projections indicate at least two more rate hikes by the end of the year. While rising rates don't always cause stock market declines, historical data shows a connection in periods like the mid-2000s, late 1990s, late 1970s, and late 1960s. The early 1990s were an exception, thanks to a surge in productivity and GDP growth.

President Trump's promises of economic growth could potentially lead to a similar outcome. However, the current low unemployment rate (4.8%) and weak productivity figures make sustained growth unlikely, according to many economists.

The "Trump factor" is undoubtedly contributing to the market's rise. Many hedge fund managers welcome his proposals for tax cuts and deregulation. David Tepper, head of Appaloosa Management, suggested such policies could "release animal spirits" and that in the current environment, investors "can't be short."

### The Risk of Herding Behavior

This fear of missing out seems to be a major driver. Professional investors are buying stocks because others are, risking losses if they don't participate. George Soros, for example, reportedly lost a billion dollars last year after betting against the market.

John Maynard Keynes warned that herding behavior can push markets far from rational valuations. He famously stated, "The market can stay irrational for longer than you can stay solvent." This makes predicting short-term market movements very difficult.

With many investors remaining optimistic, stock prices could continue to climb for a while. However, overvalued markets eventually correct themselves and return to levels supported by economic fundamentals. These corrections are rarely smooth, and there's little reason to believe this cycle will be different.

Wall Street's current bull market, characterized by a sustained rise in stock prices, is nearing a significant historical marker. As it approaches its eighth anniversary on March 9th, its remarkable performance has begun to quiet those who doubted its endurance. This extended period of growth is not only proving resilient but is also statistically noteworthy, securing its place in market annals.

In terms of performance, this bull market ranks as the fourth-longest on record, underscoring its substantial gains. While the epic bull run of the 1990s, which saw a 417% surge, remains the top performer, the current market exhibits considerable staying power. Research from organizations like the **National Bureau of Economic Research (NBER)**, which officially dates U.S. business cycles, would likely place this bull market among the longest in modern history. This sustained growth has fueled significant wealth creation, with the stock market's value increasing by over $21 trillion, according to data from **S&P Dow Jones Indices**.

Several key factors have underpinned this market's success, as highlighted by analyses from financial institutions such as **Goldman Sachs** and **J.P. Morgan**:

*   **Top Performing Stock:** Incyte (INCY), a biotechnology company, has been a standout performer. Its stock has experienced substantial appreciation, largely propelled by the success of its bone marrow treatment, Jakafi, and a robust pipeline of potential future drug therapies, as noted in reports by **FierceBiotech**.
*   **Biggest Underperformer:** Southwestern Energy (SWN) has been the bull market's most significant laggard, heavily affected by a sharp downturn in oil prices, a trend extensively covered by **Bloomberg** and **Reuters**.
*   **Leading Sector:** The consumer discretionary sector has led the market over the past eight years. This outperformance is largely attributed to the resilience of U.S. consumers, a phenomenon frequently discussed by economists at institutions like the **Federal Reserve**. Companies within this sector, including Home Depot, Starbucks, and Nike, have benefited from an improving economic climate and consumers' willingness to spend on non-essential items, a trend detailed in market research from **Nielsen** and **Morning Consult**.
*   **Lagging Sector:** Conversely, the energy sector has lagged considerably. This underperformance is understandable given the severe earnings contractions experienced by companies in this industry, a situation exacerbated by declining energy prices, as documented by the **U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA)**.

## Navigating the Unprecedented Calm: Is This Bull Market a Sign of Strength or Complacency?

The current bull market, now in its eighth year, continues its remarkable ascent from the Great Recession's depths without faltering. This rally, the second longest on record, has surprisingly avoided the typical existential crises that often plague extended market periods. While valuations appear stretched and the market's longevity places it in rare territory, volatility, a key indicator of investor anxiety, remains notably absent. This unusual market tranquility has ignited a significant debate: is this calm a sign of a healthy market, or are investors simply ignoring potential dangers in the current political climate? Regardless of the underlying reasons, the outcome has been overwhelmingly positive for those invested. Since November, equity values have surged by an impressive $3 trillion. Sam Stovall, chief investment strategist at CFRA, advises investors to be wary of a "fear-of-missing-out mindset." He cautions that this could signal overconfidence and mark the market's final lap. Stovall further notes that "volatility will remain a potential challenge to the intestinal fortitude of many investors and cause their emotions to become their portfolio’s worst enemy."

### Historical Context and Uncharacteristic Stability

Historical data compiled by CFRA, dating back to World War II, reveals that the S&P 500 Index's 250 percent advance since 2009 surpasses any bull market at the eight-year mark, and indeed, all those that concluded earlier. Typically, stocks tend to become more volatile as rallies mature, but this trend is conspicuously absent today. Over the past twelve months, the S&P 500 has experienced only 23 days with 1 percent gains or losses. This stands in stark contrast to the 85 such days observed during the eighth year of the 1990-2000 bull run. Fueled by accommodative monetary policy from central banks and a doubling of corporate profits, this enduring bull market shows few signs of waning as it enters its ninth year. Following President Trump's election in November, the S&P 500 has climbed 11 percent. Notably, it has gone 55 days without an intraday swing of 1 percent, the longest such period recorded since at least 1982.

### The Complacency Conundrum: Metrics and Concerns

The question of whether surging markets without volatility are inherently good or bad is a frequent topic of discussion on trading desks across Wall Street. Recently, charts emerged suggesting that "complacency" has reached record highs. This is calculated by dividing the S&P 500's price-earnings ratio of approximately 22 by the CBOE Volatility Index (VIX), which is hovering near 11. This quotient has now surpassed levels seen during the dot-com bubble. Even the Federal Reserve has acknowledged the unusual lack of market turbulence. In the minutes from the central bank's most recent meeting, some officials "expressed concern that the low level of implied volatility in equity markets appeared inconsistent with the considerable uncertainty attending the outlook" for President Trump's pro-growth agenda. However, Michael Shaoul, chief executive officer of Marketfield Asset Management, argues that neither the P/E ratio nor the VIX, individually or in combination, are reliable indicators of a market top. He stated in an interview, "This idea can be summed up by the old term ‘two wrongs don’t make a right.’ We can complain that valuations are no longer appealing and that the steady staircase-like move is unnerving, but we cannot say that these factors in themselves mean the end is nigh."

### Investor Behavior and Shifting Dynamics

The consistent gains have successfully lured investors back to equities. Data compiled by the Investment Company Institute and Bloomberg reveals that American equity ETFs and mutual funds have seen inflows of $60 billion over the last four months. This amount has only been surpassed twice in similar periods during this bull market. In contrast, from 2009 to 2016, withdrawals exceeded $200 billion. Investors' past reluctance to embrace equities may be understandable. While market disruptions following Britain's vote to leave the European Union and President Trump's election proved temporary, they represented two of only five instances in just two years of what Deutsche Bank AG categorizes as an extreme shift in equity volatility. Prior to this period, it took two decades to record five such events. The current market tranquility indeed appears somewhat out of sync with historical patterns. In an average bull market, the number of days with a 1 percent move typically more than doubles to 65 by year seven, after declining in the initial three years. During the Internet boom, the only post-war bull market that outlasted the current one, year nine saw such days surge to 95.

### Potential Catalysts for Future Volatility

John Stoltzfus, chief market strategist at Oppenheimer & Co., suggests that while President Trump's proposed tax cuts and increased public spending could accelerate corporate profit growth, anticipating the continuation of this peaceful advance might be a miscalculation. He outlined numerous potential catalysts for volatility outbreaks in a March 6 note, including Federal Reserve tightening, disappointment with Trump's policies, and risks associated with the French presidential election and China's currency policy. Stoltzfus commented, "The ever so unloved and disdained bull market might have more upside than even its earliest adapters and supporters project." However, he added, "With the transition taking place in Washington, changes in monetary policy stateside as well as upcoming elections abroad with issues around populism and nationalism, we expect the market will not show much tolerance should it detect investor complacency."

query: What were the key targets of the ISIS attacks in Iran, and what was the impact on casualties?

documents.text: Gunmen and bombers stunned Iran on Wednesday when they stormed parliament and the mausoleum of Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini, leaving 12 people dead in what appear to be the first ISIS attacks in the country. After a string of ISIS-claimed attacks targeting Western countries, many are left wondering — why would the group suddenly target Iran? The World reached out to William McCants, author of "The ISIS Apocalypse" and director of the Project on US Relations with the Islamic World at the Brookings Institution, to get some insight. How do ISIS and its adherents view the Islamic Republic of Iran? In the first instance, they see it as a religious competitor. Iran is dominated by [Shiite Muslims], one of the two major sects of Islam, and it's a sect that ISIS views as a rival. There is also a strategic competition, of course, with Iran for control in Iraq. ISIS has been fighting Iranian forces off and on since 2006, when it was founded. And it has long wanted to carry out an attack inside of Iran, but could not do so because al-Qaeda told it "no" in the early years. And once ISIS split from al-Qaeda, it may not have had the assets to do so. Talk a little more about the religious rivalry.
