Isn't it true that in the mindset of ISIS, the Shiite [also called Shia] Muslims are not just rivals, but also apostates? That's right. Sunni jihadists in general have a dim view of the Shia. They believe Shia leaders are apostates, there's a debate among jihadists as to whether the hoi polloi are apostates. For ISIS, they are certainly apostates — all of them. Ignorance of religion is no excuse, they would argue. Shia Muslims are all beyond the pale, and, in ISIS's view, worse than infidels like Jews and Christians, because these Shia, they argue, have made a choice to place themselves outside of true Islam, as they would see it. ISIS is losing territory in Iraq and Syria, and even in its longtime stronghold of the Syrian city Raqqa. So, why would ISIS want to attack the regional power of Iran now? On the face of it, it doesn't seem to make much sense. But there are a couple of reasons why. One is to, of course, punish Iran for its meddling in Iraq. Two, to force Iran to go all-in in Iraq, to commit even more forces and turn this into a major sectarian war. The thinking here being that, if this war is clearly divided along sectarian lines, ISIS would gain more support from the Sunni locals, who fear Iran. Another reason to carry out an attack like this would be to push the Iranian government into cracking down domestically on the Sunnis [inside Iran]. ISIS would hope in that instance that the crackdown would be wide-ranging and would create a lot of alienation among the Sunnis in Iran, and that they would then support ISIS's cause. Another way to think about this is through the lens of recruitment and competition with al-Qaeda. ISIS used to be a part of al-Qaeda. They split in recent years. And they have been competing with one another for market share among global Sunni jihadists. ISIS has had trouble attracting recruits over the last year or so, because security has tightened and it's losing a lot of territory. So, it wants to retain momentum to show that it's still in the fight, and what better way to do that in the jihadist world than to carry out a strike in the heart of Iran, at two of its major symbols of power? In the same vein, this is part of a global series of attacks that have happened over the past few days, either inspired or directed by ISIS, that are also meant to show that the organization is still in the fight, can still recruit. It's the Muslim month of fasting, Ramadan. And during the same month last year, ISIS also carried out a high-profile series of attacks, probably toward the same end — to attract more recruits. So, while on the face of it, it doesn't look like a good idea to poke a bear like Iran, there can be other reasons to do it that are smart, at least from ISIS's perspective. Is it your sense that ISIS had been trying to carry out an attack like this in Iran for some time? We know from a series of internal ISIS and al-Qaeda documents that ISIS has wanted to carry out an attack in Iran for a long time. But its parent organization al-Qaeda discouraged it. That's because al-Qaeda has a logistics hub inside of Iran, and also has a number of people being held by the Iranian government. And al-Qaeda didn't want to lose either the hub or its people, so it said, "no." Once the two organizations split, then ISIS could of course pursue its designs to carry out an attack in Iran, but then it would have to build up its capability. ISIS has had a number of people — Sunnis from Iran — go fight for its organization in Iraq. And perhaps it's one of their members who carried out today's attack. We'll see about that. So, after 2014, when ISIS split with al-Qaeda, it was probably a matter of lacking capability rather than any other reason why we haven't seen this kind of attack before. What's significant about the two sites of today's attack in Tehran — at Iran's national parliament building, and the mausoleum of the Islamic Republic’s founder, Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini? Well, the parliament is the heart, of course, of the Iranian government — its lawmaking body. And the shrine is the spiritual heart of Iran's revolutionary government. Khomeini was the founder of the republic, and some whispered that he was something even more, approaching messianic status. So this is an attack that went against both the heart of Iran's premier deliberative body, but also the spiritual heart of the revolution. Last question, Will. What comes to mind in the aftermath of this attack when you think about what's at stake for the United States? It's about demonstrating solidarity with any country that has been through an attack on innocent civilians. The US terrorist list is dominated by Sunni groups, but not exclusively. In the past, the US has designated groups [as terrorist organizations] that threatened Iran domestically that did not pose a threat to the US. I think it's important to uphold that principle. This interview was condensed and edited for clarity.

Iran was rocked by coordinated assaults on Wednesday, targeting its Parliament building and the shrine of Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini, the founder of the Islamic Republic. These attacks, carried out by armed individuals, some reportedly disguised as women, represent the most serious terrorist incident to strike the nation in years. The near-simultaneous assaults unfolded over several hours, catching Iran's security forces off guard and challenging the country's carefully cultivated image of stability in a volatile region.

The attacks resulted in at least 12 deaths and 46 injuries. All six known assailants were killed during the incidents. Five individuals were reportedly detained in connection with the attacks, though their identities remain undisclosed. Brig. Gen. Hossein Salami, deputy commander of the Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps (IRGC), vowed retaliation, stating, "We will avenge the blood of those martyred in today’s terrorism attacks." The IRGC is expected to spearhead the nation's response.

## ISIS Attacks on Iranian Ideological Symbols Escalate Geopolitical Tensions

Recent audacious attacks targeting key symbols of Iranian ideology, claimed by the Islamic State (ISIS), have significantly escalated geopolitical tensions with far-reaching global implications. The brazen strikes specifically aimed at Iran's parliament, the Majlis, and the revered tomb of Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini, the architect of the 1979 Islamic Revolution, struck at the very heart of Iran's foundational beliefs. These assaults have sent shockwaves through Tehran, deeply affecting ordinary citizens who have largely been spared such direct acts of violence. This marks a critical and concerning shift, signaling a new, more volatile phase in the complex regional power struggles.

The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) has directly and forcefully accused Saudi Arabia of orchestrating these devastating attacks. While this claim remains unproven, the assaults are undeniably poised to cause significant political fallout extending well beyond Iran's borders. They are highly likely to exacerbate existing tensions with the United States, Saudi Arabia, and the Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) monarchies. Should ISIS's claim of responsibility prove accurate, it would represent a major success for the group in bringing its terror directly to its primary Shia adversary, a long-standing and deeply held objective for ISIS.

The primary targets of these meticulously planned attacks held immense symbolic and political importance, underscoring the calculated nature of the assaults. The Iranian Parliament, known as the Majlis, is a heavily guarded government building situated in the heart of Tehran. It represents the legislative power of the Islamic Republic. The sheer audacity of breaching its security, which is designed to be exceptionally robust, highlights the boldness of the perpetrators and the perceived vulnerability of even the most protected state institutions.

Located south of Tehran, the expansive complex housing the tomb of Ayatollah Khomeini serves as a national shrine, drawing thousands of Iranian pilgrims annually. While the parliament boasts stringent security measures, the shrine's security, though substantial, will undoubtedly face intense scrutiny and likely be significantly strengthened in the aftermath of this attack. For many Iranians, an assault on Khomeini's tomb is perceived as a direct and deeply personal affront, akin to an attack on a revered national monument and the very foundations of their revolutionary identity.

If ISIS's claim of responsibility is confirmed, this would represent their first major successful strike within Iran, despite previous attempts and a prolonged conflict with Iranian forces and their allies in Syria and Iraq. The group has a history of vowing to "conquer Iran and restore it to the Sunni Muslim nation," asserting that Iran's leaders have historically persecuted Sunnis. This narrative, as highlighted by researchers at the International Centre for the Study of Radicalisation (ICSR), resonates with certain militant groups operating within Iran's Sistan-Baluchistan province, a region with a significant Sunni population. This echoes past actions attributed to groups like Jundallah, suggesting a potential resurgence of extremist ideologies within the region.

These attacks are occurring at a critical juncture as ISIS's self-declared caliphate faces imminent collapse. Iranian-backed forces and U.S.-led coalition militias are steadily advancing on its remaining strongholds in Syria and Iraq. In response to this existential threat, ISIS has intensified its calls for followers worldwide to launch attacks against perceived enemies. This directive, as noted by the Soufan Center, may offer an explanation for recent attacks in diverse cities such as Manchester, Kabul, Baghdad, Marawi, and London, demonstrating the group's continued, albeit decentralized, reach and capacity for inspiring terror globally.

Conservative Iranian leaders are expected to lend their support to the IRGC's accusations, directing blame towards Saudi Arabia and, by extension, the Trump administration. Iran harbors deep historical grievances concerning U.S. efforts to destabilize the country, including past support for groups like the Mujahedin-e-Khalq (MEK). Analysts are questioning whether President Trump's overtly strong stance against Iran is inadvertently encouraging such destabilization tactics. Trump's consistent criticism of Iran, his calls for its international isolation, and his robust support for Saudi Arabia – evidenced by a significant arms deal and backing for Qatar's ostracism – have collectively heightened regional tensions to a precarious level.

Iranian leaders have consistently rejected Trump's accusations, arguing that Saudi Arabia is, in fact, ISIS's primary state sponsor. They often point to Iran's cooperation with the U.S. against jihadist groups in Iraq, a fact frequently overlooked in the prevailing narrative. Furthermore, they contend that Iran's semi-democratic system, characterized by an elected parliament and president, fundamentally contradicts the "sponsor of terrorism" label. This reality, according to Iranian officials, seems to provoke a strong and often disproportionate reaction from President Trump, Saudi leadership, and ISIS alike. This complex exchange of accusations and counter-accusations underscores the deep-seated animosities and intricate strategic calculations that continue to define the volatile landscape of the Middle East. The targeting of these specific ideological symbols by ISIS represents a significant escalation, aiming to sow discord and undermine the very foundations of the Iranian state. The geopolitical fallout from these attacks is likely to be profound, reshaping regional alliances and further complicating the already intricate dynamics of the Middle East. The world watches closely as these escalating tensions unfold, with the potential for wider conflict looming.

The video shows a bloody body on the floor next to a desk. An Iranian member of Parliament said the images in the video were from inside his office, and he said the victims were his “colleagues.” A voice on the video is heard in Arabic saying: “Do you think we will leave? We will remain, God willing.”

Iran was struck by unprecedented attacks on Wednesday as suicide bombers and gunmen targeted the nation's parliament and the mausoleum of Ayatollah Khomeini in Tehran, resulting in at least 13 fatalities. The Islamic Revolutionary Guards immediately accused regional rival Saudi Arabia, while the Islamic State group claimed responsibility and issued further threats against Iran's Shi'ite majority, whom the hardline Sunni militants consider heretics.

Iranian Foreign Minister Javad Zarif, via Twitter, stated, "Terror-sponsoring despots threaten to bring the fight to our homeland. Proxies attack what their masters despise most: the seat of democracy." While not explicitly naming a country, his remarks appeared to allude to comments made by Saudi Arabia's deputy crown prince, Mohammed bin Salman al-Saud, in May. At that time, Prince Mohammed suggested Riyadh would bring the regional influence struggle to Iran. Saudi Arabia, a Sunni Muslim nation, denied any involvement in the Tehran attacks. However, the assault intensified existing tensions between Riyadh and Tehran, as both nations compete for dominance in the Persian Gulf and influence across the wider Islamic world. This incident occurred just days after Riyadh and other Sunni powers severed ties with Qatar, accusing it of supporting Tehran and militant organizations. These attacks marked the first time Islamic State claimed responsibility for assaults within Iran, a Shi'ite Muslim country that is a key player in the fight against IS forces in neighboring Iraq and Syria.

Reza Seifollhai, deputy head of Iran’s National Security Council, informed state television that the attackers were Iranian nationals who had joined Islamic State. Iranian police reported arresting five suspects. Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei asserted, "These fireworks have no effect on Iran. They will soon be eliminated." He further stated, according to state TV, "They are too small to affect the will of the Iranian nation and its officials." The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, a powerful entity, accused Riyadh of orchestrating the attacks and vowed retribution. A statement from the Guards declared, "This terrorist attack happened only a week after the meeting between the U.S. president (Donald Trump) and the (Saudi) backward leaders who support terrorists. The fact that Islamic State has claimed responsibility proves that they were involved in the brutal attack." U.S. President Donald Trump expressed his condolences to the victims, adding, "states that sponsor terrorism risk falling victim to the evil they promote." The U.S. State Department and U.N. Secretary-General Antonio Guterres both condemned the violence. Saudi Foreign Minister Adel Al-Jubeir stated he was unaware of who was responsible and that there was no evidence of Saudi extremist involvement.

According to Mohammad Hossein Zolfaghari, deputy interior minister, attackers disguised as women breached the main entrance of the parliament. He confirmed that one of the assailants detonated a suicide vest. A video released by the IS news agency Amaq featured a man purportedly inside the parliament saying, "Oh God, thank you. [Gunshots]. Do you think we will leave? No! We will remain, God willing." Police helicopters circled the parliament building, with snipers positioned on its roof. Iranian media reported that the incident concluded within five hours, with four attackers killed. A journalist present at the scene described the chaos: "I was inside the parliament when shooting happened. Everyone was shocked and scared. I saw two men shooting randomly." Shortly after the parliament attack commenced, a bomber detonated a suicide vest near the shrine of Ayatollah Khomeini, the revered founder of the Islamic Republic, located a few kilometers south of the city, Zolfaghari stated. A second attacker was killed by security forces. The shrine is a significant site for tourists and religious pilgrims. Mohammadali Ansari, the shrine's overseer, recounted, "The terrorists had explosives strapped to them and suddenly started to shoot around." By late evening, Deputy Interior Minister Zolfaghari confirmed a death toll of 13, with 43 individuals injured. The Intelligence Ministry announced that security forces had apprehended another "terrorist team" planning a third attack. Reza Seifollhai of the National Security Council indicated that Iran had previously thwarted 58 similar attacks, without specifying a timeframe.

These attacks followed weeks of escalating rhetorical hostility between Riyadh and Tehran. In notably direct remarks on May 2, Prince Mohammed bin Salman, who also serves as Saudi defense minister and is a son of King Salman, declared his intention to protect his country from what he termed Iranian efforts to dominate the Muslim world. He suggested that any competition for influence between the Sunni kingdom and the revolutionary Shi'ite theocracy should occur "inside Iran, not in Saudi Arabia." The following day, Iran accused Saudi Arabia of fomenting regional tension, characterizing the prince's comments as "destructive" and proof of Riyadh's support for terrorism. The attacks could also heighten internal divisions in Iran between the newly re-elected President Hassan Rouhani, who presents himself as a reformer, and his hardline clerical and Revolutionary Guard rivals. However, President Rouhani pledged that Iran would emerge more united and resolute in its fight against regional terrorism and violence. "We will prove once again that we will crush the enemies’ plots with more unity and more strength," he stated. In an appeal for solidarity, Rouhani's chief of staff, Hamid Aboutalebi, tweeted praise for the security services: "Applause to the power and firmness of our revolutionary guards, Basij (volunteer militia), police and security forces." Nevertheless, two senior government officials, who requested anonymity, suggested the attacks might trigger a blame game. One official commented, "They (hardliners) are very angry and will use every opportunity to grow in strength to isolate Rouhani." The other official predicted the attacks would push Iran toward "a harsher regional policy." Militant attacks are uncommon in Tehran and other major cities. However, two Sunni militant groups, Jaish al-Adl and Jundallah, have been engaged in a protracted and deadly insurgency, primarily in remote areas, for nearly a decade. Iran's southeastern Sistan and Baluchestan province, bordering Pakistan and Afghanistan, is home to the Balouch minority and has long been a focal point for Sunni insurgents opposing the Shi'ite-led republic. Last year, Iranian authorities reported foiling a plot by Sunni militants to bomb targets in Tehran and other cities during the Muslim holy month of Ramadan.

Saudi Arabia's Foreign Minister, Adel Al-Jubeir, has issued a strong condemnation of Iran's regional activities, advocating for punitive actions against Tehran. He specifically called for measures to address Iran's alleged interference and its support for terrorist organizations. Speaking from Paris, as reported by Al Arabiya News Channel on Wednesday, June 7, 2017, Al-Jubeir asserted that Iran provides refuge to leaders from groups such as Al-Qaeda and other terrorist entities. He characterized Iran as the world's leading state sponsor of terrorism. Al-Jubeir emphasized that for Iran to be recognized as a "normal state," it must commit to upholding international law and abandon its current "political ideas."

The Foreign Minister detailed a consistent pattern of aggression exhibited by the Iranian regime. He pointed to a history of attacks on more than a dozen embassies over the past 37 years as evidence of this behavior.

These pointed remarks were made in the broader context of Saudi Arabia's recent decision to sever diplomatic ties with Qatar. This significant move was undertaken alongside seven other nations, including Egypt, the UAE, Bahrain, Yemen, Maldives, Mauritius, and Mauritania. According to Al-Jubeir, this action was a direct consequence of Qatari policies that have had a detrimental impact on the region, particularly affecting the Gulf states. He maintained that Qatar had failed to honor its anti-terrorism agreements. Instead, he alleged, Qatar continued to offer support to terrorist groups.

The Saudi Foreign Minister outlined specific demands for Qatar to address these concerns. He urged Qatar to:

*   **Immediately cease all support for extremist groups.**
*   **Discontinue any backing for organizations like Hamas and the Muslim Brotherhood.**
*   **Undertake a fundamental "change their policies" to actively foster regional peace and stability.**

Al-Jubeir further clarified the intent behind these actions. He stated that the objective was not to inflict harm upon Qatar. Rather, it was a necessary decision made to convey a clear message. "We decided to make it clear that enough is enough," he declared. He expressed a genuine desire for Qatar to eventually become a constructive ally in the collective pursuit of regional peace and stability.

These significant statements from the Saudi Foreign Minister occurred concurrently with Kuwait's ongoing diplomatic efforts. Kuwait has been actively mediating to de-escalate the rising tensions between Qatar and its neighboring Gulf states.

On June 7, 2017, Tehran experienced a rare and devastating series of coordinated assaults. Armed militants, some disguised as women and equipped with suicide vests, launched a violent attack on the Iranian Parliament building. This initial strike was swiftly followed by another group of attackers who stormed the mausoleum of Ayatollah Khomeini, the late Iranian revolutionary founder.

The assailants engaged in a deadly rampage, firing on civilians, taking hostages, and detonating explosives. These twin terror attacks, highly unusual in Iran's tightly controlled security environment, resulted in significant casualties:

*   **12 people were killed.**
*   **More than 40 individuals were wounded.**

Iran's intelligence ministry announced the arrest of an additional "team" believed to be plotting a third attack, as reported by Reuters.

ISIS quickly claimed responsibility for the violence, releasing a graphic video purportedly filmed by one of the attackers. If confirmed, this would mark the jihadist organization's first incursion into Iran, according to The Guardian. ISIS, which adheres to a distorted interpretation of Sunni Islam, considers Shiite Muslims, the majority in Iran, to be apostates.

However, Iran's Revolutionary Guard pointed fingers elsewhere, accusing Saudi Arabia of orchestrating the attacks. A statement from the Revolutionary Guard linked the assault to a recent meeting between the U.S. president and "backward leaders who support terrorists," implicitly referencing President Trump and Saudi Arabia. The statement further suggested that ISIS's claim of responsibility was evidence of Saudi involvement with the Sunni extremist group.

This accusation comes at a particularly volatile moment in the region. Saudi Arabia and other Sunni-majority nations had recently severed diplomatic ties with Qatar, citing its alleged support for radical groups like the Muslim Brotherhood and ISIS. Qatar's engagement with Iran was also seen as a factor in this decision. President Trump, during his first foreign trip to Saudi Arabia, had emphasized a shared goal with Sunni allies to isolate Iran.

While the Saudi foreign minister denied any involvement of Saudi extremists, Wednesday's attacks, amplified by President Trump's strong anti-Iran rhetoric, intensified the existing regional rivalry. As Reuters noted, the events could also exacerbate internal tensions within Iran between moderate factions, who supported President Hassan Rouhani's recent reelection, and conservative hard-liners.

Following terrorist attacks in Iran, U.S. President Donald Trump issued a statement directly linking the nation's actions to the violence it suffered. The attacks, which struck the Iranian parliament and the Mausoleum of Ayatollah Khomeini in Tehran, killed at least 12 people. While Iran's Revolutionary Guards initially pointed fingers at regional rival Saudi Arabia, the Islamic State of Iraq and Syria (ISIS) claimed responsibility. ISIS released a video that appeared to show militants inside the parliament.

President Trump offered condolences to the victims and the Iranian people, stating, "We grieve and pray for the innocent victims of the terrorist attacks in Iran, and for the Iranian people, who are going through such challenging times." He then issued a clear warning: "We underscore that states that sponsor terrorism risk falling victim to the evil they promote." This statement follows the U.S. designation of Iran as a state sponsor of terrorism.

### Regional Tensions and ISIS

The timing of these attacks is notable. They occurred shortly after Saudi Arabia and other Sunni powers cut ties with Qatar, accusing it of supporting Tehran and militant groups. ISIS has a history of threatening Iran, a country actively involved in combating militants in Iraq and Syria.

### Iran's Role in Regional Dynamics

Several key factors shape Iran's position and the broader regional situation:

*   **Designation as a Terrorist Sponsor:** The U.S. labeling of Iran as a state sponsor of terrorism is a crucial element in the region's politics.
*   **Support for Militant Groups:** Iran is known to provide financial and military aid to various terrorist organizations, particularly the Shia group Hezbollah.
*   **Expanding Regional Influence:** Iran has been increasing its influence throughout the Middle East, including its alliance with Syrian President Bashar al-Assad, who faces accusations of war crimes.
*   **Criticism of the Nuclear Deal:** President Trump has consistently opposed the nuclear agreement negotiated with Iran during the Obama administration.

The dual attacks in Iran underscore the region's complex and unstable environment. ISIS appears to be exploiting existing conflicts and rivalries. As Reuters reported, these events highlight how state actions are interconnected with the ongoing threat from extremist groups.

Wednesday's coordinated attacks on Iran's parliament and the Imam Khomeini Mausoleum, which killed 12 people, underscore a persistent threat from the so-called Islamic State (IS). While Iran's proximity to territory once controlled by IS might suggest a higher vulnerability than Western nations, the country had, until this recent incident, successfully prevented attacks within its borders. This success was largely attributed to a robust counter-terrorism program.

Iran's security forces, recognized for their effectiveness, played a crucial role in these preventative measures. Their efforts included dismantling terrorist cells, thwarting plots, and engaging in an anti-IS messaging campaign. They also worked to contain the conflict within Syria and Iraq and gathered extensive intelligence. However, given Iran's prominent status as a target, complete invulnerability was never guaranteed.

The current situation sees IS militants facing defeats in Iraq and withdrawing in Syria, leading to a greater emphasis on external attacks. The group has recently increased its anti-Iran propaganda and attempted to recruit from the country's Sunni minority. Wednesday's attack, therefore, serves as a significant morale boost for IS, marking their first successful operation within Iran, a major Shia nation and a relatively stable one in the region.

These attacks are likely to intensify calls for retaliation, potentially hindering President Hassan Rouhani's administration's efforts to reduce Iran's involvement in Iraq and Syria. Public support for the Revolutionary Guards and Iran's anti-IS initiatives is also expected to rise. However, Iran's existing commitments in Iraq and Syria already strain its resources. Furthermore, its military involvement in Syria is not universally supported, often viewed as an expensive undertaking with limited gains and a high cost in Iranian lives. The intricate geopolitical environment and Iran's resource constraints pose ongoing challenges.

The Steubenville rape trial verdict sparked widespread online criticism, not just for the case's grim details, but also for how the media, particularly CNN, covered it. During live courtroom reporting, CNN anchor Candy Crowley and correspondent Poppy Harlow faced backlash for what many saw as inappropriate sympathy towards the convicted Trent Mays and Ma'lik Richmond. Harlow's statement, "Incredibly difficult, even for an outsider like me, to watch what happened as these two young men that had such promising futures, star football players, very good students, literally watched as they believed their lives fell apart," drew particular anger. Legal expert Paul Callan also highlighted the severe, lifelong consequences of becoming registered sex offenders.

While the facts presented were accurate, the overall tone of the CNN segment was widely perceived as insensitive. In its attempt to provide context, the report inadvertently seemed to foster sympathy for the perpetrators. This contrasted sharply with offensive online comments from genuine Steubenville rape sympathizers. For example, one sarcastic tweet read, "The Steubenville story is all too familiar. Be responsible for your actions ladies before your drunken decisions ruin innocent lives." Another, more disturbingly sincere, stated, "So you got drunk at a party and two people take advantage of you, that's not rape you're just a loose drunk slut."

The Steubenville rape trial exposed distorted perceptions of justice and societal narratives surrounding sexual assault. The blogosphere quickly critiqued CNN's reporting. Gawker's Mallory Ortberg argued against emphasizing the convicted individuals' "promising futures." Ortberg contended, "Their dreams and hopes were not crushed by an impersonal, inexorable legal system; Mays and Richardson raped a girl and have been sentenced accordingly." She noted that such reporting often frames the narrative around anonymous female victims and "dynamic, sympathetic, complicated male figures," a point amplified by the victim's testimony being heard late in the trial. This framing, she suggested, can obscure the crime's gravity and the victim's experience.

Media outlets often focused on the perceived tragedy of the convicted individuals' potentially ruined lives, even before the verdict. For instance, "Good Morning America" published a preview titled, "The Steubenville Rape Case: What You Haven't Heard." This report detailed the case, dedicating significant attention to Mays, an "honors student," and Richmond, described as coming from the "wrong side of the tracks." The report concluded sympathetically, lamenting that the teenagers "face incarceration in a detention center until their 21st birthdays and the almost-certain demise of their dreams of playing football." While empathy for the accused might be understandable before conviction, this reporting fell short of objectivity. The emphasis on their potential future losses overshadowed the profound harm inflicted upon the victim.

The Steubenville case is far from over. Ohio is convening a grand jury to investigate potential further charges, signaling another media circus. Ohio Attorney General Mike DeWine stated, "Any rape is a tragedy. But, it is even more of a tragedy when that victim is continually re-victimized in the social media." The hope is for more balanced and sensitive future reporting, avoiding harmful patterns and insensitivities. This includes a nuanced understanding of sexual assault's impact and greater respect for the victim's experience.

Later, it became clear CNN wasn't alone in its reporting missteps. As questions about the grand jury hearing arose, MSNBC and Fox also broadcast the victim's name, a significant privacy breach. This highlights a broader issue in media coverage of sensitive cases, especially sexual assault. The Steubenville reporting, with its focus on perpetrators' futures and undertones of sympathy, underscores the need for greater sensitivity, ethical consideration, and a victim-centered approach. Media's role in shaping public perception is immense, and in Steubenville, it was mishandled.

Victim vulnerability and systemic challenges are starkly illuminated by statistics, underscoring the impact of such cases and the importance of responsible reporting. According to RAINN, **1 in 6 American women has been a victim of attempted or completed rape.** This highlights the pervasiveness of sexual violence. Further, **90% of adult sexual assault victims are female.** Challenges in seeking justice are evident in reporting rates; **only 36% of rapes are reported to police.** Of those reported, **only 3% of perpetrators are convicted of rape.** Media portrayals that re-victimize survivors by focusing on perpetrators' futures or downplaying the crime are deeply damaging. The Steubenville case serves as a potent reminder of the immense responsibility media outlets bear to uphold justice, respect survivors' dignity, and contribute to a more informed and empathetic public discourse.

## Steubenville Rape Case: CNN's Coverage and Legal Ramifications

CNN provided extensive coverage of the Steubenville rape case, a high-profile legal proceeding that garnered significant public attention. The case involved the convictions of high school football stars Trent Mays and Ma’lik Richmond for the rape of a 16-year-old girl. This intense scrutiny was amplified by social media and mainstream news outlets.

### Live Broadcast and Courtroom Dynamics

During its 11 a.m. broadcast, CNN offered live reporting from Steubenville, Ohio, with correspondent Poppy Harlow on the scene. Anchor Candy Crowley, broadcasting from Washington D.C., introduced the case, emphasizing its national significance. Crowley detailed the ruling by Judge Thomas Lipps of Hamilton County Family Court. Judge Lipps committed both defendants to the Department of Youth Services, with a minimum confinement of one year and a maximum until they turn 21. The case was handled in juvenile court, meaning the judge, not a jury, determined the verdict and sentencing.

Poppy Harlow described the courtroom as "incredibly emotional" and "incredibly difficult." She highlighted the impact on the two young men, who had promising futures. Harlow recounted Ma’lik Richmond's collapse upon hearing the sentence, his despair evident as he exclaimed, "My life is over. No one is going to want me now."

### The Crime and its Consequences

Harlow underscored the gravity of the offense, which involved the sexual assault of a 16-year-old girl during alcohol-fueled parties in August. CNN legal contributor Paul Callan provided expert analysis, noting the courtroom was "drenched in tears and tragedy." Callan discussed the lasting consequences for the convicted individuals, particularly the mandatory registration as sex offenders. This requirement, under Ohio law and similar statutes nationwide, Callan stressed, would significantly impact their future employment and societal reintegration, potentially more than their juvenile detention.

### Victim's Perspective and Societal Impact

Candy Crowley then shifted the focus to the victim's experience. Harlow confirmed that the victim's life would "never be the same again" and emphasized the immense difficulty she faced in testifying. Sharing a statement from the victim's mother, Harlow conveyed her pity for the young men. The mother called compassion a "God-given gift" that the defendants lacked, citing their social media posts as crucial, self-incriminating evidence.

CNN's detailed reporting, featuring correspondents like Poppy Harlow and legal analysts like Paul Callan, underscored the crime's deep and lasting impact. It illuminated the complex legal repercussions for all involved, focusing on the young defendants and the victim.

### Additional Context and Visuals

The Associated Press, through photographer Keith Srakocic, provided poignant images of defense attorneys with their clients, adding a powerful visual dimension. The reporting also drew on insights from legal scholars and victim advocacy groups like the National Sexual Violence Resource Center (NSVRC), which offers data on the long-term effects of sexual assault. Discussions also touched upon broader societal implications, referencing research from institutions like the Vera Institute of Justice on juvenile justice reform and the challenges faced by young offenders.

The media's handling of the Steubenville, Ohio, rape case, particularly following the convictions of two teenage boys, ignited considerable criticism. Journalists, media watchdogs, and advocacy groups observed a discernible tendency among major news outlets, including CNN, Fox News, and MSNBC, to adopt a seemingly lenient stance and engage in victim-blaming. This intense scrutiny underscored how journalistic practices can inadvertently perpetuate harmful stereotypes surrounding sexual assault.

### Reframing the Narrative: Prioritizing the Assault Over Victim Blame

Prominent critics, such as Lauren Wolfe, director of the Women's Media Center's Women Under Siege project, and Xeni Jardin, highlighted a pervasive issue in reporting: the frequent redirection of focus from the assault itself to the victim's alleged actions, often characterized by phrases like "allegedly drunk." They argued that this approach risked fostering a rape culture, a phenomenon Wolfe explicitly identified within mainstream media coverage. Wolfe posed a critical question: should journalistic institutions be held to a higher ethical standard, particularly concerning their inclination to prioritize the defense of the accused over the victim's lived experience?

The controversy reached a fever pitch when Fox News, CNN, and MSNBC published the victim's name. Think Progress vehemently condemned this action as "journalistic negligence," marking a significant departure from established ethical standards for reporting on sensitive cases. This decision catalyzed a broader and more urgent discussion regarding journalistic responsibility and the profound harm that can result from sensationalizing or misrepresenting victims' accounts.

### Deconstructing Media Priorities and Framing

Wolfe also voiced her disapproval of ABC's *Nightline* for framing the case as a mere "cautionary tale for teenagers living in today’s digital world." She asserted that the fundamental issue was the rape itself, not the broader implications of online activity. This particular framing, she contended, effectively diverted attention away from the crime and its devastating impact on the victim.

Furthermore, the media's pronounced focus on the convicted boys' futures emerged as a significant point of contention. CNN's Poppy Harlow's remark that the boys "literally watched as their life fell apart" drew sharp criticism for its apparent lack of empathy towards the victim's profound trauma. This sentiment echoed problematic framing observed in *The New York Times'* 2011 reporting on a gang rape in Cleveland, Texas, suggesting a recurring pattern of prioritizing the perpetrators' perceived misfortunes over the victim's enduring suffering.

### The Undue Influence of Defendants' Backgrounds

In the initial stages of media coverage, scant information was often provided about the young woman, while substantial attention was dedicated to the male defendants. For instance, interviews with the attorney and former guardians of one convicted teen, as conducted by Matt Lauer, presented the young man in a sympathetic light. This disparity raised serious concerns about the insufficient focus on the victim's well-being and the true impact of the crime.

The defendants' identities as football players also played a considerable role in shaping the media narrative. As observed by BuzzFeed's Katie Heaney, an accuser's athletic achievements could be subtly reframed as an "alibi," thereby diminishing the offense or shifting blame. Salon's Irin Carmon critically analyzed CNN's coverage, noting that while visual documentation of the victim might have been scarce, anchors like Harlow provided extensive narrative commentary. Carmon argued that the gravity of the crimes was consequently minimized and supplanted by a "sideshow." She further suggested that such leniency was rarely extended to young offenders from less privileged backgrounds, pointing to a potential bias in how defendants are portrayed.

### Championing Responsible Reporting and Essential Context

Amidst the widespread criticism, both Wolfe and Carmon lauded Yahoo Sports' Dan Wetzel for his exceptionally insightful reporting. Wolfe specifically commended his column for "smashing victim-blaming" and for his incisive examination of the town's "culture of extreme arrogance." Wetzel posited that the local football team might have cultivated an environment of impunity, citing evidence from social media and text messages that depicted a team "spiraling out of control."

Wetzel also provided crucial context regarding rape as a crime fundamentally rooted in power and control, drawing upon expert opinions and underscoring the pervasive arrogance evident in Steubenville. While acknowledging the inherent complexities of covering rape cases, Wetzel's approach exemplified the media's profound responsibility to deliver comprehensive, context-rich reporting. His work served as a vital counterpoint to problematic coverage, offering a more responsible and insightful methodology for addressing sensitive issues. This approach moved beyond mere sensationalism, actively working to avoid the perpetuation of harmful narratives and providing a model for how journalists can navigate difficult stories with enhanced nuance and ethical consideration.

CNN's coverage of the Steubenville rape case drew criticism for its focus on the convicted boys and their families, rather than the survivor's experience. Critics argued that the network's emphasis on the defendants' parents' distress overshadowed the harm to the victim. This approach, they contended, skewed the narrative and failed to center the survivor's voice and trauma.

The case involved two Steubenville High School football players convicted of assaulting a 16-year-old girl. Evidence presented during the trial, including text messages detailing the assault, painted a stark picture of the events.

During a broadcast, CNN anchor Fredricka Whitfield announced the guilty verdict, describing the incident as occurring "after a night of heavy partying." The report heavily featured footage of the convicted boys' parents' distress in court. A substantial portion of the segment focused on emotional appeals from Ma'lik Richmond's parents. Whitfield also noted that the defendants' "family members tried their hardest to plead for some forgiveness." While reporter Poppy Harlow acknowledged the families' tears, she also underscored the crime's gravity, stating, "this is an incredibly serious crime, it's the crime of rape." Despite this, the segment continued to dwell on the defendants' emotional struggles. Harlow described the courtroom atmosphere as "incredibly emotional, it was difficult for anyone in there to watch those boys break down." CNN replayed Richmond's father's plea for forgiveness twice. He expressed regret for not being more involved in his son's life and advised parents to "get involved more in their kids' lives." The victim's mother's statement was aired, but only after extensive coverage of the defendants' families' distress, a disparity highlighted as problematic by media watchdogs.

Earlier that Sunday, anchor Candy Crowley also expressed sympathy for the convicted boys. Poppy Harlow described the courtroom scene as "incredibly difficult" to watch, focusing on the two young men who "had such promising futures, star football players, very good students -- literally watched as they believed their life fell apart." Crowley then posed the question, "What's the lasting effect though on two young men being found guilty juvenile court of rape essentially?"

Media critics dissected this framing as a journalistic choice to prioritize visible, dramatic emotions, which often enhances viewer engagement on television. This approach contrasted sharply with the victim's protected identity and the absence of her family expressing grief publicly in court. Organizations like the National Organization for Women (NOW) and RAINN condemned this coverage, arguing it minimized the crime's severity and the victim's experience. Whitfield concluded the initial segment by remarking, "a heart-breaking case to watch, no matter how you look at it."

Critics viewed CNN's approach to the trial as a significant journalistic misstep, particularly in sexual assault cases. The network's repeated focus on the perpetrators' families' distress, while marginalizing the victim's perspective, drew strong criticism from advocates for more victim-centered reporting. Experts emphasize the importance of trauma-informed reporting, which prioritizes survivor well-being and avoids sensationalism. The coverage of the Steubenville case, according to critics, fell short of these standards, raising concerns about future portrayals of sexual violence. The disparity in focus highlighted a persistent challenge in media coverage of sexual assault: balancing legal proceedings with the profound human impact on survivors.

CNN's reporting on the Steubenville rape trial has drawn criticism for its focus on the convicted rapists' "promising futures" rather than the victim's experience or the legal ramifications of the crime. While reporting on the outcome of a rape trial can include discussions of legal consequences or the impact on a victim's life, CNN correspondents Poppy Harlow and Candy Crowley, along with legal expert Paul Callan, instead emphasized the perceived downfall of the young defendants, Trent Mays and Ma'lik Richmond.

Harlow described the scene in court, noting how the two young men, identified as star football players and good students, appeared to watch their lives fall apart after the sentence was delivered. She recounted one of the defendants, Ma'lik Richmond, collapsing in his attorney's arms and stating, "My life is over. No one is going to want me now." CNN also aired footage of the convicted rapists tearfully apologizing in court and detailed an emotional exchange between Richmond and his estranged father.

During the segment, Candy Crowley prompted legal expert Paul Callan to elaborate on the future prospects of Mays and Richmond, highlighting their youth and emotional state. Callan discussed the severe impact of being labeled as registered sex offenders under Ohio law, stating, "That label is now placed on them by Ohio law... That will haunt them for the rest of their lives." He explained that employers and neighbors would be able to access this information through public registries, affecting their future employment and community integration.

The article then pivots to address the implications of sex offender registries, noting that individuals convicted of sexual violence, such as rape, are legally required to register. It suggests that avoiding such a label begins with refraining from committing rape, regardless of academic or athletic achievements. The piece emphasizes that a person's ability to excel in calculus or football is irrelevant in legal proceedings concerning sexual assault.

The author critiques CNN's journalistic approach, arguing that while discussing the sentence is appropriate, focusing on the end of the convicted rapists' athletic and academic careers as if it were an unrelated consequence is problematic. The article asserts that Mays and Richmond's futures were not derailed by an impersonal legal system, but by their decision to commit rape. Had they not committed the crime, they would not be facing juvenile detention.

While acknowledging that Crowley and Harlow may not be intentional apologists for rape, the author suggests their reporting prioritized a sensationalized, emotional angle for a high-profile trial. The piece contends that by focusing on the defendants' emotional breakdowns and perceived ruined futures, and by keeping the victim's identity protected, CNN presented a narrative that humanized the perpetrators while marginalizing the victim. The article concludes by criticizing the portrayal of Mays and Richmond as the "stars" of the trial, arguing that this framing, coupled with the disregard for the victim's experience, could lead viewers to sympathize with the convicted rapists. The reporting, according to the author, failed to adequately address the "lasting effects" of the graphic sexual assault on the victim.

The nation's eyes will be focused this week on what happens inside a tiny Steubenville, Ohio, courthouse. The juvenile trial set to begin there is every parent's nightmare and a cautionary tale for teenagers living in today's digital world. Steubenville is a town used to having media attention lavished on a much different building. In the middle of this city of 18,000 nestled on the Eastern border of Ohio stands Harding Stadium, the crown jewel of this former steel town. Nicknamed Death Valley, the 10,000-seat structure is home to the Big Red football team, one of Ohio's most storied high school programs. Steubenville is a place where football is more than just a past time; it's a religion. And residents here worship on Friday nights. Every time Big Red scores, a sculpture of a stallion named Man O' War breathes a 6-foot stream of fire into the night sky over Harding Stadium. But this past season, the team's second-round playoff defeat was overshadowed by a very different firestorm that engulfed the team and the entire town. Steubenville Launches Website on Alleged Teen Rape Just as the season was gearing up late last summer, two Big Red football players were accused of participating in the rape of a 16-year-old intoxicated girl with friends documenting the alleged crime through cellphone pictures and video. The social media frenzy took on a life of its own, with reports going as far as calling the incident a "gang-rape" of an unconscious girl. In reality, prosecutors contend that Trent Mays, 17, and Ma'lik Richmond, 16, used their hands to penetrate her while she was too drunk to consent, By Ohio law, such a crime constitutes rape, as it does in many places. At least three other Steubenville students say they witnessed the alleged encounters, and still others heard about them and posted messages, photographs and videos about the incident on social media sites. See the full story on ABC's "20/20" Friday, March 22, at 10 p.m. The news soon spread beyond Steubenville, leading both hacker-activists and women's advocacy groups to blow the lid off the story nationally, questioning why people who knew about the allegations weren't also charged under an Ohio law requiring people to report crimes of which they're aware. The uproar surrounding the case soon split the town into two furious camps; one that firmly believes there's a conspiracy to cover up a "rape culture" among the football team, and the other believing that the town's once-stellar reputation is being unfairly tarnished by outsiders who don't know all the facts. Now, documents and photographs obtained exclusively by "20/20," along with never-before-seen taped police interviews with many of the teenage party goers, are shedding light on many of the facts of the case for the first time. On the night of Aug. 11, 2012, Big Red ran a scrimmage to show off the team's newest talent. Trent Mays was a quarterback and honors student from a town 15 minutes outside of Steubenville. With a football coach for a father, Trent had the sport in his DNA. Ever since he could remember, he shared a dream that so many boys in this corner of the Ohio Valley do; to one day hear the roar of Big Red fans from the field. A favorite target for Trent that night was wide receiver Ma'lik Richmond. Ma'lik came from the rougher side of Steubenville. His earliest memories involve dodging stray bullets in his living room and watching most of his male role models being killed or incarcerated. He had turned to sports early in life as an escape from the realities around him. That night, Trent and Ma'lik helped propel Big Red to victory. For the faithful who filled the stands, it was tempting to fantasize about winning a 10th state championship. For the players, it was an excuse to party. Hours after the game, Trent, still relishing his role in Big Red's win, was receiving text messages from a girl he had been flirting with over social media, according to his lawyer. She was from just over the Ohio River in Weirton, W.Va., and, his lawyer says, persuaded him to come to a party where she was with several girlfriends. Party No. 1: When Trent and Ma'lik arrived, the narrow street outside the house of the party was crammed with cars. By some estimates, there were as many as 40 to 50 teenagers there and no adults. What was in abundance was alcohol, according to Ma'lik and several of the attendees. Witnesses said the girl who invited Trent was one of the more tipsy teens there. "She had her arm wrapped around me and one hand on my chest. It just felt like she was coming on to me," Ma'lik told ABC News' Elizabeth Vargas in an exclusive interview for "20/20." After midnight, the party was breaking up. The intoxicated girl, who would soon be at the center of a rape investigation, made it clear she wanted to leave with Trent, according to the police interviews with several of her friends. They also said she resisted their pleas for her not to leave with a car full of boys. Nevertheless, the girl got into a car with Trent, Ma'lik and two other boys and drove off. In her interview with police exclusively obtained by ABC News, the alleged victim says there is little she remembers from the time between the first party and waking up the next morning. "I remember everything that happened at the girl's house I was at but I don't remember anything past the point of me walking off the porch with him," she told them. Party No. 2: When the five teenagers arrived at the next house, the group was much smaller. There are contradictory accounts about whether the girl was able to walk into the house on her own or needed help from Ma'lik and Trent. Feeling ill, the girl was taken to the bathroom where she threw up. When she emerged, a photo of her was taken that would become a flashpoint in the case. The photo shows Trent and Ma'lik's holding the girl by her arms and legs with her head hanging back. It is unclear from the picture whether her eyes are open and witness accounts conflict on the exact context of this photo. The boy who took it, and ultimately uploaded it to his Instagram account, was another football player for Big Red and an ex-boyfriend of the intoxicated girl in the picture. "She was just like laughing, we were all talking, just clowning around and that's when her ex-boyfriend was like, 'Let me get a picture of this drunk B. And that's when we took the picture," Ma'lik told ABC News. The picture, Ma'lik maintains, was intended as a joke; he says the girl was conscious, was playing along and was not carried out of the house that way. The girl's civil attorney, Bob Fitzsimmons, calls this characterization "bizarre." "It's common sense as to what's going on in that picture," he said. Adds Fitzsimmons: "My client was unconscious that night. She doesn't have any memory of what happened." ABC News does not name the victims of alleged sexual assaults. Several witnesses said that once outside, the girl needed to stop in the street because she was sick again. "She throws up on her blouse and takes her blouse off," Ma'lik said. "And then she asked for something to drink and I gave her my jacket to cover her up." After several minutes, the girl got back into the car with those same four boys. It is during this ride that prosecutors contend Trent raped the alleged victim. One of Trent's teammates, who was seated in the backseat, told police that he used his phone to videotape Trent exposing the girl's breasts and penetrating her vaginally with his fingers. The girl was talking but he could not decipher her slurred speech, he told police. But Ma'lik, who was seated in the front passenger seat, told ABC News that she was participating. "I turned around and I can see the flash on his phone. Trent was rubbing on her breasts and she was kissing his neck. And then he was trying to unbutton her pants," Ma'lik said. Police would never see the video because, by the next morning, he had deleted it from his phone. Party No. 3 That same boy who videotaped the alleged rape in the car, and who is now a key prosecution witness, testified that when the car arrived at his home, the alleged victim was again taken to the bathroom to throw up. When the girl emerged, prosecutors say, a second alleged rape occurred. The eyewitness told police that he saw Trent trying to get the girl to perform oral sex on him while she was lying on the floor. Next, he says he saw both Trent and Ma'lik's lying beside her, sexually touching the girl's groin area with their hands. At least one other witness claims to have seen the alleged rape. "I wouldn't say she was completely passed out but she wasn't in any state to make a decision for herself," one of the eyewitnesses told police. A defense attorney for Ma'lik told Vargas of "20/20" that the alleged victim was conscious enough to provide the pass codes for her cellphone at some point after the second alleged assault. "That doesn't sound like a person that's incapacitated to the point where they cannot answer a question, let alone consent," defense attorney Walter Madison said. The girl's civil attorney challenges such an assessment, saying, "The mere fact that someone presents an argument doesn't make it true." The Steubenville rumor mill was already beginning to churn with speculation about what happened to the intoxicated girl. Naked photos of the girl that were circulated that night fueled a series of tweets and also one YouTube video of an 18-year-old former Steubenville baseball player named Michael Nodianos. In the rambling 12-minute rant, Nodianos, who wasn't present during the alleged rapes, made jokes about the incident, repeatedly referring to the victim as "dead." When the sun finally rose over Steubenville the next morning, the 16-year-old alleged victim woke up naked in a home she had never been to before. Her girlfriends, who spent much of the previous night trying to contact her and anxiously reading tweets posted about her, soon were summoned to pick her up. ABC News has learned that one of the girls who picked up the alleged victim told police, "She and Trent were just lying on the couch together as if nothing happened. She looked hung over but then she got up and was completely fine." By the next day, so much had been written and uploaded to social networking sites that the town was abuzz with rumors and innuendo. Even the girl's parents found out by word of mouth. They brought her to the hospital Aug. 13, more than 24 hours after the incident. By then, she had already showered and her clothes from that night had been washed. No physical evidence of a rape was recovered. Nevertheless, 10 days after the alleged assault, on the strength of the witness accounts, Ma'lik Richmond and Trent Mays were arrested in the middle of the night and charged with rape and kidnapping (the kidnapping charge was later dropped.) Trent was also charged with disseminating child pornography for texting naked photos of the underage alleged victim. "They sent three or four police cars," Trent's mom, Linda Mays, told ABC News. "They surrounded the house and it was surreal." By this time, many of the social media posts and pictures had been deleted. But not all were lost. ABC News has learned that, in addition to the picture of the defendants' carrying the alleged victim, they also recovered two additional photos from Trent's phone. One of the photos shows the alleged victim lying naked and face down on the floor and the other shows her naked on the couch seemingly asleep. The intersection of idolized athletes, social media over-sharing and reckless teen behavior proved an explosive combination and the story soon went national. In December, the Nodianos video was re-posted by an offshoot of the Internet hacking group Anonymous called Knight Sec. The video quickly went viral and appeared to be proof to online activist groups and even the National Organization for Women that other athletes either witnessed or knew of the alleged assault and were never charged with a crime. Such sentiments have fueled much speculation of a cover up in Steubenville. Nodianos, who until this winter was attending Ohio State University on an academic scholarship, told police he only saw the alleged victim in passing that night as she left the second location. The details he talked about in the video came from viewing one photo of the alleged victim and talking to the other boys who were with her that night, he said. His lawyer has since issued an apology on his behalf for the shameful comments he made on the video posted on YouTube. Prosecutors have not commented on the specifics of the case but at the probable cause hearing in October, prosecutor Marianne Hemmeter said, "She was a toy to them that night and the bottom line is we don't have to prove that she said no. All we have to prove is when she's being penetrated that she was unresponsive and not in a position to consent and they knew it." Attorneys for Trent and Ma'lik insist that their clients are not guilty of any crime, claiming that she was sober enough throughout the night to consent. "What we believe we will be able to support is that she voluntarily proceeded throughout the night with our client," Trent's attorney, Brian Duncan, told ABC News. "There is no indication that she was somehow so intoxicated that she could not have consented to any of the contact that occurred." Ma'lik's attorney, Walter Madison, is equally confident in his client's innocence. He questions the prosecution's dependence on testimony from the three teenage witnesses. "They all have immunity and have been granted deals not to be prosecuted for their involvement," he said. "When you give a child an option to have a seat at the trial table or tell us what we need to know and in exchange we won't prosecute you, they're probably going to tell you what you want to hear." Attorney General DeWine denied that any deals have been made and won't rule out future charges for those witnesses. The alleged victim is slated to take the stand, but because she says she has little memory of the night in question, her testimony is not expected to clarify the events of Aug. 11-12. Defense attorneys say the intense scrutiny the case has garnered is creating another challenge for them. "We have found it very difficult to find people willing to talk to us," Duncan, Trent's attorney, said. "People have either not returned calls or they have lawyers that are involved. We have material subpoenas that have been issued." A West Virginia judge Friday refused to enforce those subpoenas for three juveniles who reside just outside of Ohio. The judge cited a lack of legal precedence for compelling an underage witness to testify in a juvenile proceeding out of state. When the trial commences Wednesday, there will be no jury involved. Instead, a juvenile judge will decide the fates of Trent Mays and Ma'lik Richmond, who face incarceration in a detention center until their 21st birthdays and the almost-certain demise of their dreams of playing football. See the full story on ABC's "20/20" Friday, March 22, at 10 p.m.
